title: Helly-type problems from a topological perspective
summary: We discuss recent progress on topological Helly-type theorems and their variants. We provide an overview of two different proof techniques, one based on the nerve lemma, while the other on non-embeddability.

AI.tldr: Overview of recent progress on topological Helly-type theorems and two proof techniques: nerve lemma-based and non-embeddability-based.
AI.motivation: to survey developments in topological Helly-type theorems and their variants and to contrast proof strategies.
AI.method: provide a concise overview of two proof techniques: (i) nerve lemma approach; (ii) non-embeddability approach.
AI.result: the work synthesizes these methods and situates their applicability to various variants; no new theorems stated in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: the two techniques offer complementary tools for proving topological Helly-type statements and guide future work.

title: Low-distortion planar embedding of rod-based structures
summary: Rod-based structures are commonly used in practical applications in science and engineering. However, in many design, analysis, and manufacturing tasks, handling the rod-based structures in three dimensions directly is generally challenging. To simplify the tasks, it is usually more desirable to achieve a two-dimensional representation of the rod-based structures via some suitable geometric mappings. In this work, we develop a novel method for computing a low-distortion planar embedding of rod-based structures. Specifically, we identify geometrical constraints that aim to preserve key length and angle quantities of the 3D rod-based structures and prevent the occurrence of overlapping rods in the planar embedding. Experimental results with a variety of rod-based structures are presented to demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Moreover, our method can be naturally extended to the design and mapping of hybrid structures consisting of both rods and surface elements. Altogether, our approach paves a new way for the efficient design and fabrication of novel three-dimensional geometric structures for practical applications.

AI.tldr: Introduces a low-distortion 2D embedding method for 3D rod-based structures by enforcing length/angle constraints to prevent overlaps; extends to rod-surface hybrids; validated on diverse structures.
AI.motivation: 3D rod-based structures are challenging to handle directly in design, analysis, and fabrication. A 2D planar representation that preserves key geometric quantities enables easier manipulation while minimizing distortion and avoiding self-overlaps.
AI.method: Identify geometric constraints that preserve key length and angle quantities of the 3D structures and prevent overlapping rods in the planar embedding; develop an embedding algorithm to minimize distortion under these constraints; validate on a variety of rod-based structures; extendable to hybrids with surface elements.
AI.result: The approach yields low-distortion planar embeddings for various rod-based structures, successfully preventing overlaps in the 2D mapping; experimental results demonstrate effectiveness and robustness; the framework can be extended to hybrid rod-surface designs.
AI.conclusion: Provides a new framework for efficient design and fabrication of 3D rod-based geometries via 2D mappings, enabling easier design, analysis, and manufacturing in practical applications.

title: Attractor Patch Networks: Reducing Catastrophic Forgetting with Routed Low-Rank Patch Experts
comment: 9 pages. Code (APN implementation in nanoGPT transformer): https://github.com/shankch/nanoGPT-apn (baseline: https://github.com/karpathy/nanoGPT) Data prep: https://github.com/karpathy/nanoGPT/tree/master/data/shakespeare_char and https://github.com/karpathy/nanoGPT/tree/master/data/shakespeare
summary:
Transformers achieve strong language modeling accuracy, yet their position-wise feed-forward networks (FFNs) are dense, globally shared, and typically updated end to end. These properties create two practical tensions. First, dense FFNs spend the same compute on every token regardless of context, and they allocate capacity uniformly even when language exhibits highly clustered context structure. Second, continual learning, in the sense of updating the model while serving a data stream, often produces interference because a small update touches broadly shared weights.
  We propose Attractor Patch Networks (APN), a plug-compatible replacement for the Transformer FFN. APN is a bank of patch experts. A similarity router selects a small top-k set of patches for each token by matching the token representation to learned prototypes. Each selected patch emits a low-rank residual update conditioned on a compact code. The architecture yields conditional, context-specialized nonlinear transformations while preserving the standard Transformer interface.
  This paper focuses on APN as an architectural primitive. We formalize APN, analyze its expressivity as a piecewise low-rank residual function class, and derive simple interference and stability arguments that make APN naturally compatible with continual learning. In experiments on character-level language modeling, APN achieves competitive perplexity (4.57 vs 4.32 PPL) while enabling dramatically better continual adaptation: when adapting to a shifted domain, APN achieves 2.6 times better retention (11.1 vs 29.4 PPL on the original domain) and 2.8 times better adaptation (6.4 vs 17.8 PPL on the new domain) compared to global fine-tuning of a dense FFN baseline.

AI.tldr: APN replaces dense Transformer FFNs with a patch-based expert bank and a similarity router to produce context-specific, low-rank residual updates, preserving the Transformer interface while enabling more efficient and continual-learning-friendly adaptation.
AI.motivation: Dense, globally shared FFNs allocate uniform compute and capacity, causing inefficiency in clustered language contexts and frequent interference during continual learning. A context-aware, patch-based alternative could reduce wasted computation and improve stability and adaptability.
AI.method: APN consists of a bank of patch experts. A similarity router selects a top-k set of patches for each token by matching its representation to learned prototypes. Each chosen patch contributes a low-rank residual update conditioned on a compact code. This yields conditional, context-specific nonlinear transformations while preserving theTransformer interface. The paper formalizes APN, analyzes its expressivity as a piecewise low-rank residual function class, and derives interference and stability arguments favorable to continual learning.
AI.result: In character-level language modeling, APN achieves competitive perplexity (4.57 vs 4.32 PPL) while enabling substantially better continual adaptation: when adapting to a shifted domain, APN shows 2.6x better retention (11.1 vs 29.4 PPL on the original domain) and 2.8x better adaptation (6.4 vs 17.8 PPL on the new domain) compared to global fine-tuning of a dense FFN baseline.
AI.conclusion: APN provides context-specialized, nonlinear transformations via a patch-expert mechanism that preserves the Transformer interface and improves continual learning behavior, while maintaining competitive language modeling performance.

title: MotionCrafter: Dense Geometry and Motion Reconstruction with a 4D VAE
summary: We introduce MotionCrafter, a video diffusion-based framework that jointly reconstructs 4D geometry and estimates dense motion from a monocular video. The core of our method is a novel joint representation of dense 3D point maps and 3D scene flows in a shared coordinate system, and a novel 4D VAE to effectively learn this representation. Unlike prior work that forces the 3D value and latents to align strictly with RGB VAE latents-despite their fundamentally different distributions-we show that such alignment is unnecessary and leads to suboptimal performance. Instead, we introduce a new data normalization and VAE training strategy that better transfers diffusion priors and greatly improves reconstruction quality. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate that MotionCrafter achieves state-of-the-art performance in both geometry reconstruction and dense scene flow estimation, delivering 38.64% and 25.0% improvements in geometry and motion reconstruction, respectively, all without any post-optimization. Project page: https://ruijiezhu94.github.io/MotionCrafter_Page

AI.tldr: MotionCrafter uses a diffusion-based framework to jointly reconstruct 4D geometry and dense motion from monocular video via a novel joint 3D point/scene-flow representation learned by a 4D VAE, with data normalization that decouples RGB VAE priors to achieve state-of-the-art performance without post-optimization.
AI.motivation: Reconstructing accurate 4D geometry and dense scene flow from monocular video is challenging and existing approaches relying on RGB VAE alignment can hinderPerformance; a unified 3D+flow representation and better diffusion priors are needed.
AI.method: Introduce a joint representation of dense 3D point maps and 3D scene flows in a shared coordinate system, learned with a 4D VAE. Propose data normalization and VAE training strategy that avoids forcing alignment with RGB VAE latents, enabling effective diffusion priors and end-to-end video diffusion-based reconstruction without post-optimization.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art geometry and motion reconstruction on multiple datasets, with 38.64% improvement in geometry and 25.0% improvement in dense motion reconstruction, all without any post-optimization.
AI.conclusion: Presents a novel 4D representation and training strategy enabling diffusion priors to improve joint geometry and motion reconstruction from monocular video, demonstrating robust, post-optimization-free performance across datasets; project page provided.

title: Neural Sabermetrics with World Model: Play-by-play Predictive Modeling with Large Language Model
summary: Classical sabermetrics has profoundly shaped baseball analytics by summarizing long histories of play into compact statistics. While these metrics are invaluable for valuation and retrospective analysis, they do not define a generative model of how baseball games unfold pitch by pitch, leaving most existing approaches limited to single-step prediction or post-hoc analysis. In this work, we present Neural Sabermetrics with World Model, a Large Language Model (LLM) based play-by-play world model for baseball. We cast baseball games as long auto-regressive sequences of events and continuously pretrain a single LLM on more than ten years of Major League Baseball (MLB) tracking data, comprising over seven million pitch sequences and approximately three billion tokens. The resulting model is capable of predicting multiple aspects of game evolution within a unified framework. We evaluate our model on both in-distribution regular-season data and out-of-distribution postseason games and compare against strong neural baselines from prior work. Despite using a single backbone model, our approach outperforms the performance of existing baselines, (1) correctly predicting approximately 64% of next pitches within a plate appearance and (2) 78% of batter swing decisions, suggesting that LLMs can serve as effective world models for sports.

AI.tldr: A single LLM-based world model for baseball play-by-play pre-trained on 10+ years of MLB data, achieving state-of-the-art-like predictive performance and supporting multi-aspect game evolution within a unified framework.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap of a generative, pitch-by-pitch model in sabermetrics and enable unified, long-horizon predictions by leveraging large-scale sequential baseball data.
AI.method: Treat games as long autoregressive sequences of events and pretrain one LLM on over 10 years of MLB tracking data (≈7M pitch sequences, ≈3B tokens); evaluate on in-distribution regular season and out-of-distribution postseason; compare to neural baselines.
AI.result: Outperforms prior baselines: ~64% next-pitch prediction accuracy within a plate appearance and ~78% accuracy for batter swing decisions; demonstrates robustness to postseason data.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can serve as effective world models for sports, enabling reliable, multi-faceted predictions in a unified generative framework.

title: ST-Raptor: An Agentic System for Semi-Structured Table QA
summary: Semi-structured table question answering (QA) is a challenging task that requires (1) precise extraction of cell contents and positions and (2) accurate recovery of key implicit logical structures, hierarchical relationships, and semantic associations encoded in table layouts. In practice, such tables are often interpreted manually by human experts, which is labor-intensive and time-consuming. However, automating this process remains difficult. Existing Text-to-SQL methods typically require converting semi-structured tables into structured formats, inevitably leading to information loss, while approaches like Text-to-Code and multimodal LLM-based QA struggle with complex layouts and often yield inaccurate answers. To address these limitations, we present ST-Raptor, an agentic system for semi-structured table QA. ST-Raptor offers an interactive analysis environment that combines visual editing, tree-based structural modeling, and agent-driven query resolution to support accurate and user-friendly table understanding. Experimental results on both benchmark and real-world datasets demonstrate that ST-Raptor outperforms existing methods in both accuracy and usability. The code is available at https://github.com/weAIDB/ST-Raptor, and a demonstration video is available at https://youtu.be/9GDR-94Cau4.

AI.tldr: ST-Raptor is an agentic system for semi-structured table QA that combines interactive analysis (visual editing, tree-based structural modeling) and agent-driven query resolution to improve accuracy and usability, outperforming existing methods on benchmarks and real-world data; code and demo are available.
AI.motivation: Semi-structured tables require precise extraction of cell contents/positions and recovery of implicit layouts and semantic relationships; existing approaches (Text-to-SQL, Text-to-Code, multimodal LLM QA) either lose information or struggle with complex layouts, hindering accurate QA.
AI.method: Introduce ST-Raptor, an interactive, agentic system that fuses visual editing, tree-based structural modeling, and agent-driven query resolution to perform accurate semi-structured table QA without collapsing tables into rigid structures.
AI.result: Empirical results on benchmark and real-world datasets show that ST-Raptor achieves higher accuracy and better usability than competing methods.
AI.conclusion: ST-Raptor provides a more reliable and user-friendly solution for semi-structured table QA by preserving structural information and enabling interactive, agent-assisted query resolution; code and demonstration are publicly available.

title: MAU-GPT: Enhancing Multi-type Industrial Anomaly Understanding via Anomaly-aware and Generalist Experts Adaptation
summary: As industrial manufacturing scales, automating fine-grained product image analysis has become critical for quality control. However, existing approaches are hindered by limited dataset coverage and poor model generalization across diverse and complex anomaly patterns. To address these challenges, we introduce MAU-Set, a comprehensive dataset for Multi-type industrial Anomaly Understanding. It spans multiple industrial domains and features a hierarchical task structure, ranging from binary classification to complex reasoning. Alongside this dataset, we establish a rigorous evaluation protocol to facilitate fair and comprehensive model assessment. Building upon this foundation, we further present MAU-GPT, a domain-adapted multimodal large model specifically designed for industrial anomaly understanding. It incorporates a novel AMoE-LoRA mechanism that unifies anomaly-aware and generalist experts adaptation, enhancing both understanding and reasoning across diverse defect classes. Extensive experiments show that MAU-GPT consistently outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods across all domains, demonstrating strong potential for scalable and automated industrial inspection.

AI.tldr: Proposes MAU-Set dataset for multi-type industrial anomaly understanding and MAU-GPT with AMoE-LoRA, achieving state-of-the-art performance across diverse industrial domains.
AI.motivation: Address limited dataset coverage and poor generalization to diverse anomaly patterns in industrial anomaly detection; enable robust automated inspection.
AI.method: MAU-Set: multi-domain, hierarchical task structure from binary classification to complex reasoning; rigorous evaluation protocol. MAU-GPT: domain-adapted multimodal LLM; AMoE-LoRA mechanism combining anomaly-aware experts with generalist experts for better understanding and reasoning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show MAU-GPT consistently outperforms prior SOTA across all domains.
AI.conclusion: Dataset and protocol enable fair, scalable evaluation; MAU-GPT demonstrates strong potential for automated industrial inspection via improved generalization and reasoning.

title: A General Model for Retinal Segmentation and Quantification
summary: Retinal imaging is fast, non-invasive, and widely available, offering quantifiable structural and vascular signals for ophthalmic and systemic health assessment. This accessibility creates an opportunity to study how quantitative retinal phenotypes relate to ocular and systemic diseases. However, such analyses remain difficult at scale due to the limited availability of public multi-label datasets and the lack of a unified segmentation-to-quantification pipeline. We present RetSAM, a general retinal segmentation and quantification framework for fundus imaging. It delivers robust multi-target segmentation and standardized biomarker extraction, supporting downstream ophthalmologic studies and oculomics correlation analyses. Trained on over 200,000 fundus images, RetSAM supports three task categories and segments five anatomical structures, four retinal phenotypic patterns, and more than 20 distinct lesion types. It converts these segmentation results into over 30 standardized biomarkers that capture structural morphology, vascular geometry, and degenerative changes. Trained with a multi-stage strategy using both private and public fundus data, RetSAM achieves superior segmentation performance on 17 public datasets. It improves on prior best methods by 3.9 percentage points in DSC on average, with up to 15 percentage points on challenging multi-task benchmarks, and generalizes well across diverse populations, imaging devices, and clinical settings. The resulting biomarkers enable systematic correlation analyses across major ophthalmic diseases, including diabetic retinopathy, age-related macular degeneration, glaucoma, and pathologic myopia. Together, RetSAM transforms fundus images into standardized, interpretable quantitative phenotypes, enabling large-scale ophthalmic research and translation.

AI.tldr: A scalable retinal segmentation/quantification framework (RetSAM) for fundus images that enables multi-target segmentation and standardized biomarker extraction, trained on 200k+ images, achieving state-of-the-art segmentation across 17 public datasets and enabling oculomics analyses across major eye diseases.
AI.motivation: To overcome the lack of public multi-label datasets and a unified segmentation-to-quantification pipeline that limits large-scale studies linking retinal phenotypes to ocular and systemic diseases.
AI.method: Multi-stage training on private and public fundus data to build a general framework that performs multi-task segmentation (five anatomical structures, four phenotypic patterns, >20 lesion types) and converts results into 30+ standardized biomarkers capturing morphology, vascular geometry, and degeneration; three task categories; robust performance across diverse populations and devices.
AI.result: Outperforms prior best methods by ~3.9 percentage points DSC on average across 17 public datasets and up to ~15 percentage points on challenging benchmarks; generalizes across populations/devices/clinical settings; yields 30+ interpretable biomarkers enabling correlation studies across diabetic retinopathy, AMD, glaucoma, pathologic myopia.
AI.conclusion: RetSAM provides a unified, scalable, interpretable pipeline that transforms fundus images into standardized phenotypes, enabling large-scale ophthalmic research and oculomics translation.

title: Aster: Autonomous Scientific Discovery over 20x Faster Than Existing Methods
comment: Available at www.asterlab.ai, 25 pages, 8 figures, 4 tables
summary:
We introduce Aster, an AI agent for autonomous scientific discovery capable of operating over 20 times faster than existing frameworks. Given a task, an initial program, and a script to evaluate the performance of the program, Aster iteratively improves the program, often leading to new state-of-the-art performances. Aster's significant reduction in the number of iterations required for novel discovery expands the domain of tractable problems to include tasks with long evaluation durations, such as multi-hour machine learning training runs.
  We applied Aster to problems in mathematics, GPU kernel engineering, biology, neuroscience, and language model training. More specifically: the Erdos minimum overlap problem, optimizing the TriMul kernel, a single-cell analysis denoising problem, training a neural activity prediction model to perform well on ZAPBench, and the NanoGPT Speedrun Competition. Aster attains SOTA results in every task, except for ZAPBench, where it matches the performance of the best human solution with less than 1/190th of the compute.
  Aster is accessible via a web interface and API at asterlab.ai.

AI.tldr: Aster is an AI agent that autonomously discovers scientific solutions by iteratively improving programs, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple domains and enabling long-running evaluations with substantially fewer iterations.
AI.motivation: To accelerate autonomous scientific discovery and expand tractable problem domains by reducing the iteration cost and enabling tasks with long evaluation times.
AI.method: Given a task, an initial program, and an evaluation script, Aster autonomously iterates to improve the program. It has been applied to mathematics (Erdős minimum overlap), GPU kernel optimization (TriMul), single-cell data denoising, neural activity prediction (ZAPBench), and NanoGPT speedrunning; it achieves state-of-the-art results in most tasks and matches the best human performance on ZAPBench using far less compute.
AI.result: Aster attains SOTA on most tasks; on ZAPBench it matches the best human solution with under 1/190th of the compute; demonstrates up to 20x faster operation than existing frameworks; enables long-running evaluations like multi-hour ML training runs; accessible via web interface and API.
AI.conclusion: Aster substantially accelerates autonomous scientific discovery across domains and broadens the set of tractable problems by reducing iteration costs and permitting long-duration experiments; availability at asterlab.ai.

title: Formal Methods in Robot Policy Learning and Verification: A Survey on Current Techniques and Future Directions
comment: 19 Pages. 6 Figures. Published in Transactions on Machine Learning Research
summary: As hardware and software systems have grown in complexity, formal methods have been indispensable tools for rigorously specifying acceptable behaviors, synthesizing programs to meet these specifications, and validating the correctness of existing programs. In the field of robotics, a similar trend of rising complexity has emerged, driven in large part by the adoption of deep learning. While this shift has enabled the development of highly performant robot policies, their implementation as deep neural networks has posed challenges to traditional formal analysis, leading to models that are inflexible, fragile, and difficult to interpret. In response, the robotics community has introduced new formal and semi-formal methods to support the precise specification of complex objectives, guide the learning process to achieve them, and enable the verification of learned policies against them. In this survey, we provide a comprehensive overview of how formal methods have been used in recent robot learning research. We organize our discussion around two pillars: policy learning and policy verification. For both, we highlight representative techniques, compare their scalability and expressiveness, and summarize how they contribute to meaningfully improving realistic robot safety and correctness. We conclude with a discussion of remaining obstacles for achieving that goal and promising directions for advancing formal methods in robot learning.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey of formal methods in robot learning, organized by policy learning and policy verification, comparing techniques by scalability and expressiveness, and outlining remaining obstacles and future directions.
AI.motivation: Robotics complexity with deep learning undermines traditional formal analysis; there is a need for rigorous specifications, guided learning, and verifiable guarantees to ensure safety and correctness.
AI.method: Literature survey with a taxonomy organized around two pillars (policy learning and policy verification); evaluation of representative techniques; comparison on scalability and expressiveness; discussion of practical impact on robot safety.
AI.result: Provides a structured landscape with a taxonomy and comparative insights across methods, highlighting current capabilities, trade-offs, and gaps; summarizes how formal methods contribute to safer, more correct robot learning and guides future research.
AI.conclusion: Identifies obstacles (scalability, interpretability, integration with learning) and promising directions (scalable verification, expressive specification languages, learning-guided formal methods, runtime monitoring) to advance formal methods in robot learning.

title: Steering to Say No: Configurable Refusal via Activation Steering in Vision Language Models
summary: With the rapid advancement of Vision Language Models (VLMs), refusal mechanisms have become a critical component for ensuring responsible and safe model behavior. However, existing refusal strategies are largely \textit{one-size-fits-all} and fail to adapt to diverse user needs and contextual constraints, leading to either under-refusal or over-refusal. In this work, we firstly explore the challenges mentioned above and develop \textbf{C}onfigurable \textbf{R}efusal in \textbf{VLM}s (\textbf{CR-VLM}), a robust and efficient approach for {\em configurable} refusal based on activation steering. CR-VLM consists of three integrated components: (1) extracting a configurable refusal vector via a teacher-forced mechanism to amplify the refusal signal; (2) introducing a gating mechanism that mitigates over-refusal by preserving acceptance for in-scope queries; and (3) designing a counterfactual vision enhancement module that aligns visual representations with refusal requirements. Comprehensive experiments across multiple datasets and various VLMs demonstrate that CR-VLM achieves effective, efficient, and robust configurable refusals, offering a scalable path toward user-adaptive safety alignment in VLMs.

AI.tldr: CR-VLM: a configurable refusal framework for Vision-Language Models using activation steering, with a teacher-forced refusal vector, a gating mechanism to reduce over-refusal, and a counterfactual vision enhancement module. Evaluated across datasets and VLMs, it demonstrates effective, efficient, user-adaptive safety refusals.
AI.motivation: Addresses the limitation of one-size-fits-all refusals in VLMs, which can cause under-refusal or over-refusal. Aims to enable user-adaptive safety by configuring refusals based on context.
AI.method: Three components: (1) extract configurable refusal vector via teacher-forced mechanism to amplify refusal signal; (2) gating mechanism to preserve acceptance for in-scope queries and mitigate over-refusal; (3) counterfactual vision enhancement module that aligns visual representations with refusal requirements.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and various VLMs show CR-VLM achieves effective, efficient, and robust configurable refusals, offering a scalable path toward user-adaptive safety alignment in VLMs.
AI.conclusion: CR-VLM provides a robust, scalable framework for configurable refusals in VLMs, demonstrating effectiveness across diverse models and datasets and enabling safer, user-adaptive interactions.

title: AVERE: Improving Audiovisual Emotion Reasoning with Preference Optimization
comment: Accepted as a conference paper at ICLR 2026. Project page: https://avere-iclr.github.io
summary: Emotion understanding is essential for building socially intelligent agents. Although recent multimodal large language models have shown strong performance on this task, two key challenges remain - spurious associations between emotions and irrelevant audiovisual cues, and hallucinations of audiovisual cues driven by text priors in the language model backbone. To quantify and understand these issues, we introduce EmoReAlM, a benchmark designed to evaluate MLLMs for cue-emotion associations, hallucinations and modality agreement. We then propose AVEm-DPO, a preference optimization technique that aligns model responses with both audiovisual inputs and emotion-centric queries. Specifically, we construct preferences over responses exhibiting spurious associations or hallucinations, and audiovisual input pairs guided by textual prompts. We also include a regularization term that penalizes reliance on text priors, thereby mitigating modality-specific cue hallucinations. Experimental results on DFEW, RAVDESS and EMER demonstrate that our method significantly improves the performance of the reference baseline models with 6-19% of relative performance gains in zero-shot settings. By providing both a rigorous benchmark and a robust optimization framework, this work enables principled evaluation and improvement of MLLMs for emotion understanding and social AI. Code, models and benchmark will be released at https://avere-iclr.github.io.

AI.tldr: Proposes EmoReAlM benchmark and AVEm-DPO optimization to reduce spurious cue associations and text-prior hallucinations in multimodal LLMs for emotion understanding; reports 6-19% relative gains on DFEW, RAVDESS, EMER in zero-shot; code and benchmark to be released.
AI.motivation: Addresses core challenges in emotion understanding with multimodal LLMs: spurious emotion-cue associations and hallucinations driven by text priors. Provides a principled benchmark (EmoReAlM) and optimization approach (AVEm-DPO) to enable reliable evaluation and alignment for social AI.
AI.method: Introduce EmoReAlM as a benchmark to evaluate cue-emotion associations, hallucinations, and modality agreement. Propose AVEm-DPO, a preference optimization technique that aligns model responses with audiovisual inputs and emotion-centric queries; construct preferences over responses with spurious associations or hallucinations and over audiovisual input pairs guided by prompts; include a regularization term penalizing reliance on text priors to mitigate modality-specific cue hallucinations.
AI.result: Demonstrates 6-19% relative performance gains over baselines on DFEW, RAVDESS, and EMER in zero-shot settings.
AI.conclusion: Provides a rigorous benchmark and robust optimization framework for evaluating and improving multimodal LLMs in emotion understanding and social AI; code, models, and benchmark to be released.

title: Theory of Space: Can Foundation Models Construct Spatial Beliefs through Active Exploration?
summary: Spatial embodied intelligence requires agents to act to acquire information under partial observability. While multimodal foundation models excel at passive perception, their capacity for active, self-directed exploration remains understudied. We propose Theory of Space, defined as an agent's ability to actively acquire information through self-directed, active exploration and to construct, revise, and exploit a spatial belief from sequential, partial observations. We evaluate this through a benchmark where the goal is curiosity-driven exploration to build an accurate cognitive map. A key innovation is spatial belief probing, which prompts models to reveal their internal spatial representations at each step. Our evaluation of state-of-the-art models reveals several critical bottlenecks. First, we identify an Active-Passive Gap, where performance drops significantly when agents must autonomously gather information. Second, we find high inefficiency, as models explore unsystematically compared to program-based proxies. Through belief probing, we diagnose that while perception is an initial bottleneck, global beliefs suffer from instability that causes spatial knowledge to degrade over time. Finally, using a false belief paradigm, we uncover Belief Inertia, where agents fail to update obsolete priors with new evidence. This issue is present in text-based agents but is particularly severe in vision-based models. Our findings suggest that current foundation models struggle to maintain coherent, revisable spatial beliefs during active exploration.

AI.tldr: Active exploration requires maintaining coherent spatial beliefs, but current multimodal models exhibit gaps: they struggle to autonomously gather information (Active-Passive Gap), explore inefficiently, suffer belief instability, and show Belief Inertia, especially in vision-based systems, degrading cognitive maps over time.
AI.motivation: To investigate how agents can actively acquire information and maintain revisable spatial beliefs under partial observability, bridging passive perception with active exploration in multimodal foundation models.
AI.method: Propose Theory of Space and a benchmarking framework for curiosity-driven exploration to build accurate cognitive maps. Introduce spatial belief probing to extract internal spatial representations at each step. Evaluate state-of-the-art models, including a false belief paradigm to test belief updating, and compare text-based and vision-based agents.
AI.result: Identification of four bottlenecks: (1) Active-Passive Gap—autonomous information gathering reduces performance; (2) High exploration inefficiency—unsystematic search vs program-based proxies; (3) Global-belief instability—spatial beliefs degrade over time; (4) Belief Inertia—priors not updated with new evidence, more severe in vision-based models. Perception is the initial bottleneck, but maintaining coherent, revisable spatial beliefs is the core challenge.
AI.conclusion: Current foundation models struggle to sustain coherent, revisable spatial beliefs during active exploration; addressing belief updating, stability, and efficient active information gathering is essential for robust spatial understanding in embodied agents.

title: FeudalNav: A Simple Framework for Visual Navigation
comment: 8 Pages, 6 figures and 4 tables. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2411.09893, arXiv:2402.12498
summary: Visual navigation for robotics is inspired by the human ability to navigate environments using visual cues and memory, eliminating the need for detailed maps. In unseen, unmapped, or GPS-denied settings, traditional metric map-based methods fall short, prompting a shift toward learning-based approaches with minimal exploration. In this work, we develop a hierarchical framework that decomposes the navigation decision-making process into multiple levels. Our method learns to select subgoals through a simple, transferable waypoint selection network. A key component of the approach is a latent-space memory module organized solely by visual similarity, as a proxy for distance. This alternative to graph-based topological representations proves sufficient for navigation tasks, providing a compact, light-weight, simple-to-train navigator that can find its way to the goal in novel locations. We show competitive results with a suite of SOTA methods in Habitat AI environments without using any odometry in training or inference. An additional contribution leverages the interpretablility of the framework for interactive navigation. We consider the question: how much direction intervention/interaction is needed to achieve success in all trials? We demonstrate that even minimal human involvement can significantly enhance overall navigation performance.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical, odometry-free visual navigation framework that uses a visual-similarity latent memory and a waypoint-selection network to choose subgoals, achieving competitive results with SOTA methods in Habitat AI and enabling interpretable interactive navigation with minimal human guidance.
AI.motivation: In unseen, unmapped, or GPS-denied environments, metric map-based approaches falter. There is a need for learning-based, lightweight, transferable navigation methods that require minimal exploration and can operate without odometry.
AI.method: A multi-level hierarchical policy that selects subgoals via a transferable waypoint selection network. A latent-space memory module, organized solely by visual similarity, acts as a distance proxy and replaces graph-based topological maps. The system operates without odometry during training or inference and emphasizes interpretability for interactive navigation.
AI.result: The approach yields competitive results against state-of-the-art methods in Habitat AI with odometry disabled during training/inference. The latent visual-similarity memory supports effective navigation to goals in novel environments, offering a compact, easy-to-train navigator. Interactive navigation experiments show that minimal human intervention can substantially improve performance.
AI.conclusion: A compact, light-weight navigational framework based on visual-similarity memory and waypoint subgoal selection can perform competitively without odometry and provide interpretable interactive control, suggesting that topological graph structures may be unnecessary for robust visual navigation in unseen environments.

title: Vectra: A New Metric, Dataset, and Model for Visual Quality Assessment in E-Commerce In-Image Machine Translation
summary: In-Image Machine Translation (IIMT) powers cross-border e-commerce product listings; existing research focuses on machine translation evaluation, while visual rendering quality is critical for user engagement. When facing context-dense product imagery and multimodal defects, current reference-based methods (e.g., SSIM, FID) lack explainability, while model-as-judge approaches lack domain-grounded, fine-grained reward signals. To bridge this gap, we introduce Vectra, to the best of our knowledge, the first reference-free, MLLM-driven visual quality assessment framework for e-commerce IIMT. Vectra comprises three components: (1) Vectra Score, a multidimensional quality metric system that decomposes visual quality into 14 interpretable dimensions, with spatially-aware Defect Area Ratio (DAR) quantification to reduce annotation ambiguity; (2) Vectra Dataset, constructed from 1.1M real-world product images via diversity-aware sampling, comprising a 2K benchmark for system evaluation, 30K reasoning-based annotations for instruction tuning, and 3.5K expert-labeled preferences for alignment and evaluation; and (3) Vectra Model, a 4B-parameter MLLM that generates both quantitative scores and diagnostic reasoning. Experiments demonstrate that Vectra achieves state-of-the-art correlation with human rankings, and our model outperforms leading MLLMs, including GPT-5 and Gemini-3, in scoring performance. The dataset and model will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Introduces Vectra, a reference-free, MLLM-driven visual quality assessment framework for in-image machine translation (IIMT) in e-commerce, with a 14-dimension quality metric, a large dataset, and a 4B parameter model; achieves strong correlation with human rankings and beats leading MLLMs.
AI.motivation: Addresses lack of explainable, fine-grained, domain-grounded visual quality assessment for IIMT; existing reference-based metrics like SSIM/FID are insufficient for context-dense product imagery and multimodal defects; model-as-judge approaches lack domain signals.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Vectra Score with 14 interpretable dimensions and spatially-aware Defect Area Ratio; (2) Vectra Dataset with 1.1M images, 2K benchmark, 30K reasoning annotations, 3.5K preferences; (3) Vectra Model, a 4B-parameter MLLM that outputs scores and diagnostic reasoning; evaluation shows high correlation and outperformance of GPT-5 and Gemini-3.
AI.result: Vectra achieves state-of-the-art correlation with human rankings; outperforms leading MLLMs on scoring tasks; dataset/model to be released upon acceptance.
AI.conclusion: Vectra provides an explainable, domain-grounded framework for visual quality assessment in IIMT, enabling better evaluation and potential feedback for MT systems; release planned.

title: TACIT: Transformation-Aware Capturing of Implicit Thought
summary:
We present TACIT (Transformation-Aware Capturing of Implicit Thought), a diffusion-based transformer for interpretable visual reasoning. Unlike language-based reasoning systems, TACIT operates entirely in pixel space using rectified flow, enabling direct visualization of the reasoning process at each inference step. We demonstrate the approach on maze-solving, where the model learns to transform images of unsolved mazes into solutions. Key results on 1 million synthetic maze pairs include:
  - 192x reduction in training loss over 100 epochs
  - 22.7x improvement in L2 distance to ground truth
  - Only 10 Euler steps required (vs. 100-1000 for typical diffusion models)
  Quantitative analysis reveals a striking phase transition phenomenon: the solution remains invisible for 68% of the transformation (zero recall), then emerges abruptly at t=0.70 within just 2% of the process. Most remarkably, 100% of samples exhibit simultaneous emergence across all spatial regions, ruling out sequential path construction and providing evidence for holistic rather than algorithmic reasoning. This "eureka moment" pattern -- long incubation followed by sudden crystallization -- parallels insight phenomena in human cognition. The pixel-space design with noise-free flow matching provides a foundation for understanding how neural networks develop implicit reasoning strategies that operate below and before language.

AI.tldr: Introduces TACIT, a diffusion-based transformer that reasons in pixel space via rectified flow for interpretable visual reasoning; demonstrates maze solving with strong empirical gains and evidence for holistic, insight-like reasoning, enabling direct visualization of intermediate steps.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap for interpretable, language-free reasoning in visual tasks by operating in pixel space and providing visualizable intermediate states; challenge sequential path-planning with evidence for holistic reasoning.
AI.method: A diffusion-based transformer operating directly in pixel space using rectified flow (noise-free) to transform unsolved maze images into solutions. Trained on ~1M synthetic maze pairs. Evaluates training loss, L2 distance to ground truth, and step efficiency (Euler steps). Analyzes the temporal emergence of solutions (phase transition) and spatial coherence across regions.
AI.result: 192x reduction in training loss over 100 epochs; 22.7x improvement in L2 distance to ground truth; requires only 10 Euler steps (vs 100–1000 for typical diffusion models). Phase transition: solution invisible for ~68% of transformation (zero recall) then emerges abruptly at t=0.70 within ~2% of process. 100% samples show simultaneous emergence across all spatial regions, suggesting holistic rather than sequential reasoning. Demonstrates an “eureka moment” pattern akin to human insight and supports pixel-space interpretability of implicit reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Pixel-space diffusion with rectified flow matching enables visualization of intermediate reasoning steps and supports the hypothesis that neural networks can develop holistic, insight-like reasoning strategies prior to language; provides a framework for studying implicit reasoning in vision without language priming.

title: ANCHOR: Branch-Point Data Generation for GUI Agents
summary: End-to-end GUI agents for real desktop environments require large amounts of high-quality interaction data, yet collecting human demonstrations is expensive and existing synthetic pipelines often suffer from limited task diversity or noisy, goal-drifting trajectories. We present a trajectory expansion framework Anchor that bootstraps scalable desktop supervision from a small set of verified seed demonstrations. Starting from each seed, we identify branch points that correspond to meaningful state changes and propose new, state-grounded task variants conditioned on the current GUI context. An executing agent then follows the proposed instructions to generate new trajectories, while a verifier enforces task completion via state-aware checks and trajectory-level consistency. To improve supervision quality, we further apply task-conditioned step-level filtering to remove ungrounded actions and denoise post-branch segments to maintain coherent intent. Experiments on standard desktop benchmarks, OSWorld and WindowsAgentArena, show that models fine-tuned on our expanded corpus achieve consistent improvements over zero-shot agents and representative synthesis baselines, and generalize across applications and operating systems.

AI.tldr: Anchor is a trajectory expansion framework for end-to-end desktop GUI agents that bootstraps scalable supervision from a small set of seed demonstrations, generating state-grounded task variants and using verification and filtering to produce a higher-quality, diverse training corpus.
AI.motivation: Address the data bottleneck in end-to-end GUI agents by producing diverse, high-quality trajectories without requiring extensive human demonstrations or noisy synthetic data; ensure task relevance and coherence in desktop environments across apps and OSes.
AI.method: From seed demonstrations, identify branch points representing meaningful state changes and propose new, state-grounded task variants conditioned on the current GUI context. An executing agent follows these instructions to generate new trajectories, while a verifier enforces task completion with state-aware checks and trajectory-level consistency. Apply task-conditioned step-level filtering to remove ungrounded actions and denoise post-branch segments to maintain coherent intent.
AI.result: Experiments on standard desktop benchmarks OSWorld and WindowsAgentArena show that models fine-tuned on the expanded corpus outperform zero-shot agents and representative synthesis baselines, with better generalization across applications and operating systems.
AI.conclusion: Anchor enables scalable, higher-quality desktop supervision by expanding seed-based demonstrations into diverse, coherent trajectories through branching, verification, and denoising; it improves performance and generalization and may be applicable to other end-to-end agent domains.

title: Robust and Real-Time Bangladeshi Currency Recognition: A Dual-Stream MobileNet and EfficientNet Approach
summary: Accurate currency recognition is essential for assistive technologies, particularly for visually impaired individuals who rely on others to identify banknotes. This dependency puts them at risk of fraud and exploitation. To address these challenges, we first build a new Bangladeshi banknote dataset that includes both controlled and real-world scenarios, ensuring a more comprehensive and diverse representation. Next, to enhance the dataset's robustness, we incorporate four additional datasets, including public benchmarks, to cover various complexities and improve the model's generalization. To overcome the limitations of current recognition models, we propose a novel hybrid CNN architecture that combines MobileNetV3-Large and EfficientNetB0 for efficient feature extraction. This is followed by an effective multilayer perceptron (MLP) classifier to improve performance while keeping computational costs low, making the system suitable for resource-constrained devices. The experimental results show that the proposed model achieves 97.95% accuracy on controlled datasets, 92.84% on complex backgrounds, and 94.98% accuracy when combining all datasets. The model's performance is thoroughly evaluated using five-fold cross-validation and seven metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, Cohen's Kappa, MCC, and AUC. Additionally, explainable AI methods like LIME and SHAP are incorporated to enhance transparency and interpretability.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Bangladeshi banknote dataset augmentation with four extra datasets; a hybrid CNN (MobileNetV3-Large + EfficientNetB0) with an MLP classifier; achieves high accuracy across controlled/complex datasets and supports explainability with LIME/SHAP.
AI.motivation: Address currency recognition for visually impaired, reduce fraud risk, improve robustness and generalization across real-world conditions, and enable on-device deployment.
AI.method: Construct new Bangladeshi banknote dataset (controlled + real-world). Integrate four additional public datasets. Propose a hybrid CNN architecture combining MobileNetV3-Large and EfficientNetB0 for feature extraction, followed by an MLP classifier. Evaluate with five-fold cross-validation across seven metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, Cohen's Kappa, MCC, AUC). Apply LIME and SHAP for explainability.
AI.result: Achieves 97.95% accuracy on controlled datasets, 92.84% on complex backgrounds, and 94.98% when combining all datasets. Demonstrates good generalization and transparency via explainability methods.
AI.conclusion: Shows promise for accurate, efficient currency recognition on resource-constrained devices; enhanced robustness through diverse datasets; interpretability via XAI methods; potential for deployment in assistive tech, though practical deployment details and computational constraints require further validation.

title: Video-based Music Generation
summary: As the volume of video content on the internet grows rapidly, finding a suitable soundtrack remains a significant challenge. This thesis presents EMSYNC (EMotion and SYNChronization), a fast, free, and automatic solution that generates music tailored to the input video, enabling content creators to enhance their productions without composing or licensing music. Our model creates music that is emotionally and rhythmically synchronized with the video. A core component of EMSYNC is a novel video emotion classifier. By leveraging pretrained deep neural networks for feature extraction and keeping them frozen while training only fusion layers, we reduce computational complexity while improving accuracy. We show the generalization abilities of our method by obtaining state-of-the-art results on Ekman-6 and MovieNet. Another key contribution is a large-scale, emotion-labeled MIDI dataset for affective music generation. We then present an emotion-based MIDI generator, the first to condition on continuous emotional values rather than discrete categories, enabling nuanced music generation aligned with complex emotional content. To enhance temporal synchronization, we introduce a novel temporal boundary conditioning method, called "boundary offset encodings," aligning musical chords with scene changes. Combining video emotion classification, emotion-based music generation, and temporal boundary conditioning, EMSYNC emerges as a fully automatic video-based music generator. User studies show that it consistently outperforms existing methods in terms of music richness, emotional alignment, temporal synchronization, and overall preference, setting a new state-of-the-art in video-based music generation.

AI.tldr: A fully automatic video-based music generator (EMSYNC) that links video emotion to continuous-emotion MIDI generation with temporal boundary conditioning, achieving state-of-the-art results on Ekman-6 and MovieNet and favorable user studies.
AI.motivation: As online video content expands, creators need inexpensive, automated soundtracks that are emotionally and rhythmically aligned with footage, reducing reliance on manual composition or licensing.
AI.method: EMSYNC integrates (1) a novel video emotion classifier built on frozen pretrained feature extractors with trainable fusion layers; (2) a large-scale emotion-labeled MIDI dataset; (3) an emotion-conditioned MIDI generator that uses continuous emotion values; (4) a temporal boundary conditioning mechanism (boundary offset encodings) to align chords with scene changes; (5) an end-to-end pipeline combining these components for automatic music generation synchronized to video.
AI.result: State-of-the-art results on Ekman-6 and MovieNet; user studies report improvements in music richness, emotional alignment, temporal synchronization, and overall preference compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: EMSYNC delivers a fully automatic, emotion-aware, temporally synchronized video music generator, supported by new datasets and continuous-emotion conditioning, and sets a new benchmark in video-based music generation.

title: PreFlect: From Retrospective to Prospective Reflection in Large Language Model Agents
summary: Advanced large language model agents typically adopt self-reflection for improving performance, where agents iteratively analyze past actions to correct errors. However, existing reflective approaches are inherently retrospective: agents act, observe failure, and only then attempt to recover. In this work, we introduce PreFlect, a prospective reflection mechanism that shifts the paradigm from post hoc correction to pre-execution foresight by criticizing and refining agent plans before execution. To support grounded prospective reflection, we distill planning errors from historical agent trajectories, capturing recurring success and failure patterns observed across past executions. Furthermore, we complement prospective reflection with a dynamic re-planning mechanism that provides execution-time plan update in case the original plan encounters unexpected deviation. Evaluations on different benchmarks demonstrate that PreFlect significantly improves overall agent utility on complex real-world tasks, outperforming strong reflection-based baselines and several more complex agent architectures. Code will be updated at https://github.com/wwwhy725/PreFlect.

AI.tldr: Proposes PreFlect, a prospective reflection framework for LLM agents that critiques and refines plans before execution and dynamically re-plans during execution; it uses historical trajectories to distill planning errors and improves performance over retrospective reflection baselines.
AI.motivation: Retrospective self-reflection is inherently post hoc and may fail to prevent errors; there is a need for proactive planning and adaptability in complex tasks; grounding reflection on past planning failures can make planning more robust.
AI.method: Introduce PreFlect: prospective reflection that critiques/refines plans pre-execution; distill planning errors from historical trajectories to identify recurring patterns; add dynamic re-planning for execution-time plan updates when deviations occur; evaluate on benchmarks and compare to strong baselines and more complex architectures.
AI.result: Significant improvement in overall agent utility on complex real-world tasks; outperforms strong reflection-based baselines and several more complex agent architectures; code to be released on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Prospective reflection with dynamic re-planning effectively mitigates limitations of post hoc correction, yielding robust performance gains; broad applicability to real-world LLM agent systems.

title: LangGS-SLAM: Real-Time Language-Feature Gaussian Splatting SLAM
summary: In this paper, we propose a RGB-D SLAM system that reconstructs a language-aligned dense feature field while sustaining low-latency tracking and mapping. First, we introduce a Top-K Rendering pipeline, a high-throughput and semantic-distortion-free method for efficiently rendering high-dimensional feature maps. To address the resulting semantic-geometric discrepancy and mitigate the memory consumption, we further design a multi-criteria map management strategy that prunes redundant or inconsistent Gaussians while preserving scene integrity. Finally, a hybrid field optimization framework jointly refines the geometric and semantic fields under real-time constraints by decoupling their optimization frequencies according to field characteristics. The proposed system achieves superior geometric fidelity compared to geometric-only baselines and comparable semantic fidelity to offline approaches while operating at 15 FPS. Our results demonstrate that online SLAM with dense, uncompressed language-aligned feature fields is both feasible and effective, bridging the gap between 3D perception and language-based reasoning.

AI.tldr: RGB-D SLAM with language-aligned dense feature fields enabling real-time tracking (15 FPS) via Top-K Rendering, multi-criteria map management, and a hybrid optimization framework that decouples geometry and semantics.
AI.motivation: Bridge 3D perception and language-based reasoning by enabling online SLAM with dense language-aligned features while controlling memory and latency.
AI.method: 1) Top-K Rendering to efficiently render high-dimensional feature maps with minimal semantic distortion. 2) Multi-criteria map management pruning redundant/inconsistent Gaussians to reduce memory while preserving scene integrity. 3) Hybrid field optimization that jointly refines geometric and semantic fields but decouples their optimization frequencies according to field characteristics to meet real-time constraints.
AI.result: System achieves superior geometric fidelity over geometric-only baselines and semantic fidelity comparable to offline approaches, operating at 15 FPS, demonstrating feasibility of online SLAM with dense, uncompressed language-aligned feature fields.
AI.conclusion: Online dense language-aligned features in SLAM are feasible and effective, enabling tighter integration between 3D perception and language-based reasoning.

title: Gaussian-Constrained LeJEPA Representations for Unsupervised Scene Discovery and Pose Consistency
summary: Unsupervised 3D scene reconstruction from unstructured image collections remains a fundamental challenge in computer vision, particularly when images originate from multiple unrelated scenes and contain significant visual ambiguity. The Image Matching Challenge 2025 (IMC2025) highlights these difficulties by requiring both scene discovery and camera pose estimation under real-world conditions, including outliers and mixed content. This paper investigates the application of Gaussian-constrained representations inspired by LeJEPA (Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture) to address these challenges. We present three progressively refined pipelines, culminating in a LeJEPA-inspired approach that enforces isotropic Gaussian constraints on learned image embeddings. Rather than introducing new theoretical guarantees, our work empirically evaluates how these constraints influence clustering consistency and pose estimation robustness in practice. Experimental results on IMC2025 demonstrate that Gaussian-constrained embeddings can improve scene separation and pose plausibility compared to heuristic-driven baselines, particularly in visually ambiguous settings. These findings suggest that theoretically motivated representation constraints offer a promising direction for bridging self-supervised learning principles and practical structure-from-motion pipelines.

AI.tldr: Gaussian-constrained, LeJEPA-inspired embeddings improve unsupervised 3D scene discovery and pose estimation on IMC2025.
AI.motivation: Tackle unsupervised 3D reconstruction from unstructured, multi-scene image collections with significant visual ambiguity, where scene discovery and camera pose estimation must be robust to outliers and mixed content.
AI.method: Present three progressively refined pipelines, culminating in a LeJEPA-inspired approach that enforces isotropic Gaussian constraints on learned image embeddings to guide clustering and pose estimation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows improved scene separation and pose plausibility with Gaussian-constrained embeddings compared to heuristic baselines, particularly in visually ambiguous settings; no new theoretical guarantees but practical benefits demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: Theoretically motivated representation constraints offer a promising direction to bridge self-supervised learning principles and practical structure-from-motion pipelines, enabling more robust unsupervised 3D scene understanding in real-world, mixed-content data.

title: Hybrid Dual-Path Linear Transformations for Efficient Transformer Architectures
summary: Standard Transformer architectures rely heavily on dense linear transformations, treating feature projection as a monolithic, full-rank operation. We argue that this formulation is inefficient and lacks the structural inductive bias necessary for distinguishing between local feature preservation and global context integration. To address this, we introduce the Hybrid Dual-Path Linear (HDPL) operator, which decomposes the affine transformation into two topologically distinct pathways: a sparse block-diagonal component for high-rank local processing, and a low-rank Variational Autoencoder (VAE) bottleneck for global context regularization. By "surgically" replacing specific projections (Query, Key, Value, Gate, Up) with HDPL operators while retaining standard dense layers for aggregation (Output, Down), we achieve a superior balance of efficiency and representational power. Experiments on the FineWeb-Edu dataset demonstrate that the HDPL architecture outperforms a standard Llama-style baseline, reducing validation loss while simultaneously reducing parameter count by 6.8%. Beyond immediate performance gains, we discuss how the explicit materialization of a probabilistic latent space within the Transformer backbone serves as a vital architectural affordance, offering new pathways for inference-time or hypernetwork induced control, continual adaptation, interpretability, and cross-model or cross-modal synchronization. The code is available at https://github.com/VladimerKhasia/HDPL

AI.tldr: Hybrid Dual-Path Linear (HDPL) replaces selected Transformer projections with a dual-path operator: a sparse block-diagonal local path and a low-rank VAE bottleneck for global context, improving efficiency and performance. Applied by substituting Q/K/V/Gate/Up while keeping Output/Down dense layers.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiency and weak inductive bias in dense Transformer projections by separating local high-rank feature processing from global context regularization through structured linear operators.
AI.method: Introduce HDPL as two parallel pathways: a sparse block-diagonal local processor and a low-rank VAE bottleneck. Replace projections Q, K, V, Gate, Up with HDPL while retaining dense Output/Down layers. Train and evaluate on FineWeb-Edu, compare to a standard Llama-style baseline, report parameter-efficiency and loss improvements. Discuss probabilistic latent space as an architectural affordance.
AI.result: HDPL outperforms the standard Llama-style baseline on FineWeb-Edu, reducing validation loss while also reducing parameter count by 6.8%. The approach provides efficiency gains and a latent-space structure that enables potential inference-time control, continual adaptation, interpretability, and cross-modal synchronization.
AI.conclusion: Explicit probabilistic latent space within the Transformer backbone is a valuable architectural affordance, enabling new capabilities (control, adaptation, interpretability) and practical gains, with demonstrated performance and efficiency on FineWeb-Edu; code available at the provided GitHub link.

title: Is there "Secret Sauce'' in Large Language Model Development?
summary: Do leading LLM developers possess a proprietary ``secret sauce'', or is LLM performance driven by scaling up compute? Using training and benchmark data for 809 models released between 2022 and 2025, we estimate scaling-law regressions with release-date and developer fixed effects. We find clear evidence of developer-specific efficiency advantages, but their importance depends on where models lie in the performance distribution. At the frontier, 80-90% of performance differences are explained by higher training compute, implying that scale--not proprietary technology--drives frontier advances. Away from the frontier, however, proprietary techniques and shared algorithmic progress substantially reduce the compute required to reach fixed capability thresholds. Some companies can systematically produce smaller models more efficiently. Strikingly, we also find substantial variation of model efficiency within companies; a firm can train two models with more than 40x compute efficiency difference. We also discuss the implications for AI leadership and capability diffusion.

AI.tldr: At the frontier, model performance is mostly driven by training compute, not proprietary techniques; away from the frontier, developer-specific efficiency and shared progress reduce compute needs. There is substantial intra-company variation in efficiency.
AI.motivation: To determine whether leading LLM performance arises from scale (compute) or from proprietary technology and algorithmic advances, using a large panel of models released 2022–2025 and accounting for developer effects.
AI.method: Estimates scaling-law regressions with model performance as the outcome, including release date and developer fixed effects, across 809 models.
AI.result: Frontier models: 80–90% of performance differences explained by higher training compute, indicating scale-dominated frontier progress. Non-frontier models benefit more from proprietary techniques and shared algorithmic progress, which reduce the compute needed to reach fixed capability thresholds. Some firms consistently produce smaller (more compute-efficient) models, and there is substantial intra-firm variation (e.g., more than 40x compute efficiency difference between two models within the same firm).
AI.conclusion: Scale is a primary driver of frontier advances, while proprietary methods and shared progress enable efficiency gains for broader capability thresholds. Implications for AI leadership and the diffusion of capability are nuanced, with both scaling and efficiency improvements shaping competitive dynamics.

title: When Simultaneous Localization and Mapping Meets Wireless Communications: A Survey
summary: The availability of commercial wireless communication and sensing equipment combined with the advancements in intelligent autonomous systems paves the way towards robust joint communications and simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM). This paper surveys the state-of-the-art in the nexus of SLAM and Wireless Communications, attributing the bidirectional impact of each with a focus on visual SLAM (V-SLAM) integration. We provide an overview of key concepts related to wireless signal propagation, geometric channel modeling, and radio frequency (RF)-based localization and sensing. In addition to this, we show image processing techniques that can detect landmarks, proactively predicting optimal paths for wireless channels. Several dimensions are considered, including the prerequisites, techniques, background, and future directions and challenges of the intersection between SLAM and wireless communications. We analyze mathematical approaches such as probabilistic models, and spatial methods for signal processing, as well as key technological aspects. We expose techniques and items towards enabling a highly effective retrieval of the autonomous robot state. Among other interesting findings, we observe that monocular V-SLAM would benefit from RF relevant information, as the latter can serve as a proxy for the scale ambiguity resolution. Conversely, we find that wireless communications in the context of 5G and beyond can potentially benefit from visual odometry that is central in SLAM. Moreover, we examine other sources besides the camera for SLAM and describe the twofold relation with wireless communications. Finally, integrated solutions performing joint communications and SLAM are still in their infancy: theoretical and practical advancements are required to add higher-level localization and semantic perception capabilities to RF and multi-antenna technologies.

AI.tldr: A survey of SLAM–wireless communications integration, outlining bidirectional benefits, challenges, and key findings; highlights include RF information aiding monocular V-SLAM scale and visual odometry aiding 5G/beyond channel understanding; integrated joint-communications-and-SLAM solutions are nascent.
AI.motivation: As autonomous systems proliferate, leveraging wireless signals for localization/sensing and using SLAM to enhance communications promises robust, scalable operation; a comprehensive synthesis is needed.
AI.method: Literature survey and synthesis across SLAM, RF propagation, geometric modeling, RF localization/sensing, and image processing; analysis of probabilistic and spatial signal-processing methods; discussion of prerequisites, techniques, background, future directions, and challenges.
AI.result: Key observations include: RF information can resolve scale ambiguity in monocular V-SLAM; visual odometry can enhance wireless channels and 5G/beyond performance; consideration of SLAM sources beyond the camera; two-way relationship between SLAM and wireless communications; integrated joint-communications-and-SLAM solutions remain in early stages requiring both theoretical and practical advances.
AI.conclusion: The field is in its infancy; substantial theoretical and practical work is needed to realize higher-level localization and semantic perception through RF and multi-antenna technologies; future directions and challenges are identified.

title: XAI-CLIP: ROI-Guided Perturbation Framework for Explainable Medical Image Segmentation in Multimodal Vision-Language Models
summary: Medical image segmentation is a critical component of clinical workflows, enabling accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and disease monitoring. However, despite the superior performance of transformer-based models over convolutional architectures, their limited interpretability remains a major obstacle to clinical trust and deployment. Existing explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques, including gradient-based saliency methods and perturbation-based approaches, are often computationally expensive, require numerous forward passes, and frequently produce noisy or anatomically irrelevant explanations. To address these limitations, we propose XAI-CLIP, an ROI-guided perturbation framework that leverages multimodal vision-language model embeddings to localize clinically meaningful anatomical regions and guide the explanation process. By integrating language-informed region localization with medical image segmentation and applying targeted, region-aware perturbations, the proposed method generates clearer, boundary-aware saliency maps while substantially reducing computational overhead. Experiments conducted on the FLARE22 and CHAOS datasets demonstrate that XAI-CLIP achieves up to a 60\% reduction in runtime, a 44.6\% improvement in dice score, and a 96.7\% increase in Intersection-over-Union for occlusion-based explanations compared to conventional perturbation methods. Qualitative results further confirm cleaner and more anatomically consistent attribution maps with fewer artifacts, highlighting that the incorporation of multimodal vision-language representations into perturbation-based XAI frameworks significantly enhances both interpretability and efficiency, thereby enabling transparent and clinically deployable medical image segmentation systems.

AI.tldr: XAI-CLIP: a ROI-guided, multimodal perturbation framework for medical image segmentation that uses vision-language embeddings to localize anatomical regions and guide explanations, achieving faster and more accurate, boundary-aware saliency maps with improved dice/IoU on FLARE22 and CHAOS datasets.
AI.motivation: Transformers offer strong segmentation performance but suffer from limited interpretability in clinical contexts. Existing XAI methods are costly, produce noisy artifacts, and hamper deployment. There is a need for efficient, clinically meaningful explanations that are anatomically consistent.
AI.method: Integrate vision-language model embeddings to localize ROIs corresponding to anatomical regions and apply targeted, region-aware perturbations to medical images. This ROI-guided perturbation yields boundary-aware saliency maps while reducing computational overhead. The approach combines language-informed localization with segmentation tasks.
AI.result: On FLARE22 and CHAOS, XAI-CLIP reduces runtime by up to 60%, improves Dice score by 44.6%, and increases IoU for occlusion-based explanations by 96.7% compared to standard perturbation methods. Qualitative results show cleaner, anatomically consistent attribution maps with fewer artifacts.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating multimodal vision-language representations into perturbation-based XAI enhances interpretability and efficiency, enabling more transparent and clinically deployable medical image segmentation systems.

title: The Optimal Token Baseline: Variance Reduction for Long-Horizon LLM-RL
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) for Large Language Models (LLMs) often suffers from training collapse in long-horizon tasks due to exploding gradient variance. To mitigate this, a baseline is commonly introduced for advantage computation; however, traditional value models remain difficult to optimize, and standard group-based baselines overlook sequence heterogeneity. Although classic optimal baseline theory can achieve global variance reduction, it neglects token heterogeneity and requires prohibitive gradient-based computation. In this work, we derive the Optimal Token Baseline (OTB) from first principles, proving that gradient updates should be weighted inversely to their cumulative gradient norm. To ensure efficiency, we propose the Logit-Gradient Proxy that approximates the gradient norm using only forward-pass probabilities. Our method achieves training stability and matches the performance of large group sizes ($N=32$) with only $N=4$, reducing token consumption by over 65% across single-turn and tool-integrated reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: Proposes Optimal Token Baseline (OTB) for RL in LLMs to reduce gradient variance in long-horizon tasks; introduces Logit-Gradient Proxy for efficiency; achieves stability and strong performance with much smaller sample groups, saving tokens.
AI.motivation: RL for LLMs suffers from training collapse due to exploding gradient variance in long-horizon tasks; existing baselines and value models either fail to capture token heterogeneity or are computationally expensive.
AI.method: Derives OTB from first principles; gradient updates weighted inversely by cumulative gradient norm; introduces Logit-Gradient Proxy using forward-pass probabilities to approximate gradient norm; validates on single-turn and tool-integrated reasoning tasks.
AI.result: Training stability; matches results of larger baselines (N=32) with N=4; reduces token consumption by >65%; robust across tasks.
AI.conclusion: OTB with Logit-Gradient Proxy provides efficient, stable variance reduction for RL in LLMs, enabling effective long-horizon learning with smaller group sizes and lower token usage.

title: From Out-of-Distribution Detection to Hallucination Detection: A Geometric View
summary: Detecting hallucinations in large language models is a critical open problem with significant implications for safety and reliability. While existing hallucination detection methods achieve strong performance in question-answering tasks, they remain less effective on tasks requiring reasoning. In this work, we revisit hallucination detection through the lens of out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, a well-studied problem in areas like computer vision. Treating next-token prediction in language models as a classification task allows us to apply OOD techniques, provided appropriate modifications are made to account for the structural differences in large language models. We show that OOD-based approaches yield training-free, single-sample-based detectors, achieving strong accuracy in hallucination detection for reasoning tasks. Overall, our work suggests that reframing hallucination detection as OOD detection provides a promising and scalable pathway toward language model safety.

AI.tldr: Reframe hallucination detection as out-of-distribution (OOD) detection for next-token prediction in LLMs, enabling training-free, single-sample detectors with strong reasoning-task performance.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations undermine safety and reliability; existing detectors work poorly on reasoning tasks. OOD detection is a mature, scalable framework that could generalize to LLMs.
AI.method: Treat next-token prediction as a classification problem and apply OOD techniques with adaptations for LLM structure, enabling training-free, single-sample detectors.
AI.result: OOD-based detectors achieved strong accuracy in hallucination detection for reasoning tasks in a training-free, single-sample setting.
AI.conclusion: Framing hallucination detection as OOD detection is a promising, scalable path for improving language model safety.

title: Admittance-Based Motion Planning with Vision-Guided Initialization for Robotic Manipulators in Self-Driving Laboratories
summary: Self driving laboratories (SDLs) are highly automated research environments that leverage advanced technologies to conduct experiments and analyze data with minimal human involvement. These environments often involve delicate laboratory equipment, unpredictable environmental interactions, and occasional human intervention, making compliant and force aware control essential for ensuring safety, adaptability, and reliability. This paper introduces a motion-planning framework centered on admittance control to enable adaptive and compliant robotic manipulation. Unlike conventional schemes, the proposed approach integrates an admittance controller directly into trajectory execution, allowing the manipulator to dynamically respond to external forces during interaction. This capability enables human operators to override or redirect the robot's motion in real time. A vision algorithm based on structured planar pose estimation is employed to detect and localize textured planar objects through feature extraction, homography estimation, and depth fusion, thereby providing an initial target configuration for motion planning.
