The vision based initialization establishes the reference trajectory, while the embedded admittance controller ensures that trajectory execution remains safe, adaptive, and responsive to external forces or human intervention. The proposed strategy is validated using textured image detection as a proof of concept. Future work will extend the framework to SDL environments involving transparent laboratory objects where compliant motion planning can further enhance autonomy, safety, and human-robot collaboration.

AI.tldr: A motion-planning framework for self-driving laboratories that integrates an admittance controller into trajectory execution to enable compliant, force-aware robotic manipulation, with a vision-based planar pose estimation for initialization; validated via a textured-object proof-of-concept and aimed at safer human-robot collaboration in SDLs.
AI.motivation: Safety, adaptability, and reliability in automated laboratories (SDLs) require compliant control that responds to external forces and supports real-time human intervention.
AI.method: Embed an admittance controller directly in trajectory execution to allow dynamic response to external forces; use a structured planar pose estimation pipeline (feature extraction, homography estimation, depth fusion) to detect/locate textured planar objects and provide an initial target configuration; vision-based initialization defines the reference trajectory, which the admittance controller then executes safely with adaptability and potential for human override.
AI.result: Proof-of-concept demonstration using textured image detection; shows feasibility of real-time compliant motion and human override, but no quantitative metrics are provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields a compliant, adaptive motion-planning framework suitable for SDLs, with demonstrated feasibility and a roadmap to extend to transparent objects to further enhance autonomy and human–robot collaboration.

title: Deep Learning Based Multi-Level Classification for Aviation Safety
summary: Bird strikes pose a significant threat to aviation safety, often resulting in loss of life, severe aircraft damage, and substantial financial costs. Existing bird strike prevention strategies primarily rely on avian radar systems that detect and track birds in real time. A major limitation of these systems is their inability to identify bird species, an essential factor, as different species exhibit distinct flight behaviors, and altitudinal preference. To address this challenge, we propose an image-based bird classification framework using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), designed to work with camera systems for autonomous visual detection. The CNN is designed to identify bird species and provide critical input to species-specific predictive models for accurate flight path prediction. In addition to species identification, we implemented dedicated CNN classifiers to estimate flock formation type and flock size. These characteristics provide valuable supplementary information for aviation safety. Specifically, flock type and size offer insights into collective flight behavior, and trajectory dispersion . Flock size directly relates to the potential impact severity, as the overall damage risk increases with the combined kinetic energy of multiple birds.

AI.tldr: CNN-based image analysis for bird species, flock type, and flock size to inform species-specific flight-path models for improved bird-strike prediction; no empirical results reported.
AI.motivation: Mitigate bird-strike risk by leveraging species-specific flight behaviors; radar lacks species-level information; image-based approach aims to enable autonomous, camera-based detection and better trajectory prediction.
AI.method: Train CNN classifiers for species identification and for estimating flock formation type and flock size from camera imagery; integrate outputs into species-specific predictive flight-path models; discuss potential for real-time applicability.
AI.result: Proposed framework; no experimental results presented in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: If validated with appropriate datasets and real-time deployment, this approach could enhance detection and predictive safety; requires datasets, evaluation, and integration with existing systems.

title: Attention-Driven Framework for Non-Rigid Medical Image Registration
summary: Deformable medical image registration is a fundamental task in medical image analysis with applications in disease diagnosis, treatment planning, and image-guided interventions. Despite significant advances in deep learning based registration methods, accurately aligning images with large deformations while preserving anatomical plausibility remains a challenging task. In this paper, we propose a novel Attention-Driven Framework for Non-Rigid Medical Image Registration (AD-RegNet) that employs attention mechanisms to guide the registration process. Our approach combines a 3D UNet backbone with bidirectional cross-attention, which establishes correspondences between moving and fixed images at multiple scales. We introduce a regional adaptive attention mechanism that focuses on anatomically relevant structures, along with a multi-resolution deformation field synthesis approach for accurate alignment. The method is evaluated on two distinct datasets: DIRLab for thoracic 4D CT scans and IXI for brain MRI scans, demonstrating its versatility across different anatomical structures and imaging modalities. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves performance competitive with state-of-the-art methods on the IXI and DIRLab datasets. The proposed method maintains a favorable balance between registration accuracy and computational efficiency, making it suitable for clinical applications. A comprehensive evaluation using normalized cross-correlation (NCC), mean squared error (MSE), structural similarity (SSIM), Jacobian determinant, and target registration error (TRE) indicates that attention-guided registration improves alignment accuracy while ensuring anatomically plausible deformations.

AI.tldr: AD-RegNet: an attention-driven non-rigid registration framework using a 3D UNet backbone with bidirectional cross-attention and regional adaptive attention, achieving competitive accuracy and plausible deformations on DIRLab and IXI.
AI.motivation: Large deformations and anatomical plausibility remain challenging in deep learning-based registration; attention-guided mechanisms can improve correspondences across scales while preserving anatomical integrity.
AI.method: 3D UNet backbone; bidirectional cross-attention between moving and fixed images at multiple scales; regional adaptive attention focusing on anatomically relevant structures; multi-resolution deformation field synthesis; evaluation on DIRLab (thoracic 4D CT) and IXI (brain MRI) using NCC, MSE, SSIM, Jacobian determinant, TRE.
AI.result: Competitive with state-of-the-art methods on IXI and DIRLab; favorable accuracy-efﬁciency trade-off; attention-guided registration improves alignment accuracy while ensuring anatomically plausible deformations; comprehensive metrics reported.
AI.conclusion: Attention-guided, multi-scale registration is effective across modalities and holds clinical promise due to its accuracy and efficiency; future work could expand datasets and explore more complex deformation models.

title: Incentive-Aware AI Safety via Strategic Resource Allocation: A Stackelberg Security Games Perspective
summary: As AI systems grow more capable and autonomous, ensuring their safety and reliability requires not only model-level alignment but also strategic oversight of the humans and institutions involved in their development and deployment. Existing safety frameworks largely treat alignment as a static optimization problem (e.g., tuning models to desired behavior) while overlooking the dynamic, adversarial incentives that shape how data are collected, how models are evaluated, and how they are ultimately deployed. We propose a new perspective on AI safety grounded in Stackelberg Security Games (SSGs): a class of game-theoretic models designed for adversarial resource allocation under uncertainty. By viewing AI oversight as a strategic interaction between defenders (auditors, evaluators, and deployers) and attackers (malicious actors, misaligned contributors, or worst-case failure modes), SSGs provide a unifying framework for reasoning about incentive design, limited oversight capacity, and adversarial uncertainty across the AI lifecycle. We illustrate how this framework can inform (1) training-time auditing against data/feedback poisoning, (2) pre-deployment evaluation under constrained reviewer resources, and (3) robust multi-model deployment in adversarial environments. This synthesis bridges algorithmic alignment and institutional oversight design, highlighting how game-theoretic deterrence can make AI oversight proactive, risk-aware, and resilient to manipulation.

AI.tldr: Proposes using Stackelberg Security Games to model AI oversight, treating defenders (auditors/deployers) and attackers (malicious actors/failed modes) to address data poisoning, constrained evaluation, and robust deployment, offering a unified, proactive safety framework that links algorithmic alignment with governance.
AI.motivation: Current AI safety largely treats alignment as static optimization and ignores dynamic, adversarial incentives shaping data collection, evaluation, and deployment under limited oversight. A principled game-theoretic framework can integrate incentives, uncertainty, and oversight constraints across the AI lifecycle.
AI.method: Frame AI safety as a Stackelberg Security Game with defenders and attackers. Map oversight tasks (training-time auditing, pre-deployment evaluation with limited reviewers, robust multi-model deployment) into defender/attacker strategies and payoffs. Use Stackelberg equilibria to derive incentive-compatible policies and resource allocation under uncertainty, illustrating the approach through three application lenses.
AI.result: A unifying framework linking algorithmic alignment with institutional oversight. Provides guidance for auditing, evaluation budgeting, and deployment decisions under adversarial conditions; fosters proactive, risk-aware, and manipulation-resistant AI oversight.
AI.conclusion: Stackelberg Security Games offer a principled, proactive approach to AI safety that integrates technical alignment with governance structures, enabling deterrence-based, resilient oversight across the AI lifecycle.

title: ARGOS: Automated Functional Safety Requirement Synthesis for Embodied AI via Attribute-Guided Combinatorial Reasoning
summary: Ensuring functional safety is essential for the deployment of Embodied AI in complex open-world environments. However, traditional Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment (HARA) methods struggle to scale in this domain. While HARA relies on enumerating risks for finite and pre-defined function lists, Embodied AI operates on open-ended natural language instructions, creating a challenge of combinatorial interaction risks. Whereas Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as a promising solution to this scalability challenge, they often lack physical grounding, yielding semantically superficial and incoherent hazard descriptions. To overcome these limitations, we propose a new framework ARGOS (AttRibute-Guided cOmbinatorial reaSoning), which bridges the gap between open-ended user instructions and concrete physical attributes. By dynamically decomposing entities from instructions into these fine-grained properties, ARGOS grounds LLM reasoning in causal risk factors to generate physically plausible hazard scenarios. It then instantiates abstract safety standards, such as ISO 13482, into context-specific Functional Safety Requirements (FSRs) by integrating these scenarios with robot capabilities. Extensive experiments validate that ARGOS produces high-quality FSRs and outperforms baselines in identifying long-tail risks. Overall, this work paves the way for systematic and grounded functional safety requirement generation, a critical step toward the safe industrial deployment of Embodied AI.

AI.tldr: ARGOS grounds open-ended instructions into fine-grained physical attributes to generate context-specific functional safety requirements (FSRs) for Embodied AI, outperforming baselines in identifying long-tail hazards.
AI.motivation: HARA struggles with combinatorial interaction risks in open-ended Embodied AI and LLMs often lack physical grounding, leading to superficial hazard descriptions. A scalable, grounded approach is needed for safe deployment.
AI.method: Dynamically decompose entities from instructions into fine-grained physical attributes, ground LLM reasoning in causal risk factors, and instantiate ISO 13482 safety standards into context-specific FSRs by integrating hazard scenarios with robot capabilities.
AI.result: ARGOS produces high-quality FSRs and outperforms baselines in identifying long-tail risks across scenarios.
AI.conclusion: This framework enables systematic, grounded safety requirement generation, advancing safe industrial deployment of Embodied AI by bridging user instructions with concrete physical attributes.

title: The Geometry of Representational Failures in Vision Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) exhibit puzzling failures in multi-object visual tasks, such as hallucinating non-existent elements or failing to identify the most similar objects among distractions. While these errors mirror human cognitive constraints, such as the "Binding Problem", the internal mechanisms driving them in artificial systems remain poorly understood. Here, we propose a mechanistic insight by analyzing the representational geometry of open-weight VLMs (Qwen, InternVL, Gemma), comparing methodologies to distill "concept vectors" - latent directions encoding visual concepts. We validate our concept vectors via steering interventions that reliably manipulate model behavior in both simplified and naturalistic vision tasks (e.g., forcing the model to perceive a red flower as blue). We observe that the geometric overlap between these vectors strongly correlates with specific error patterns, offering a grounded quantitative framework to understand how internal representations shape model behavior and drive visual failures.

AI.tldr: The work investigates the representational geometry of open-weight VLMs to extract latent 'concept vectors' encoding visual concepts, then validates them by steering interventions; they find that vector overlap predicts error patterns, offering a mechanistic, geometry-based account of visual failures in VLMs.
AI.motivation: To explain puzzling multi-object failures in VLMs (hallucinations, misbinding) by grounding them in the geometry of internal representations and linking cognitive constraints (binding problem) to measurable vector directions across state-of-the-art models.
AI.method: Analyze open-weight VLMs (Qwen, InternVL, Gemma); distill concept vectors as latent directions in representation space aligned with visual concepts; apply steering interventions by perturbing inputs or prompt guidance to manipulate model perception (e.g., red flower perceived as blue); assess on simplified and naturalistic tasks; measure geometric overlap between concept vectors and correlate with error patterns.
AI.result: Concept vectors exist and their geometric overlap correlates with specific errors; steering interventions reliably shift model outputs; provides a grounded, quantitative relation between representation geometry and behavior.
AI.conclusion: Internal representations and their geometric relations significantly shape VLM behavior and failure modes; concept-vector geometry offers a mechanistic framework for diagnosing and potentially mitigating visual errors, with implications for model design and interpretability.

title: Finding Connections: Membership Inference Attacks for the Multi-Table Synthetic Data Setting
summary:
Synthetic tabular data has gained attention for enabling privacy-preserving data sharing. While substantial progress has been made in single-table synthetic generation where data are modeled at the row or item level, most real-world data exists in relational databases where a user's information spans items across multiple interconnected tables. Recent advances in synthetic relational data generation have emerged to address this complexity, yet release of these data introduce unique privacy challenges as information can be leaked not only from individual items but also through the relationships that comprise a complete user entity.
  To address this, we propose a novel Membership Inference Attack (MIA) setting to audit the empirical user-level privacy of synthetic relational data and show that single-table MIAs that audit at an item level underestimate user-level privacy leakage. We then propose Multi-Table Membership Inference Attack (MT-MIA), a novel adversarial attack under a No-Box threat model that targets learned representations of user entities via Heterogeneous Graph Neural Networks. By incorporating all connected items for a user, MT-MIA better targets user-level vulnerabilities induced by inter-tabular relationships than existing attacks. We evaluate MT-MIA on a range of real-world multi-table datasets and demonstrate that this vulnerability exists in state-of-the-art relational synthetic data generators, employing MT-MIA to additionally study where this leakage occurs.

AI.tldr: Proposes MT-MIA, a No-Box, multi-table membership inference attack against synthetic relational data using heterogeneous GNNs. Demonstrates that item-level MIAs underestimate user-level leakage and that current relational synthetic data generators leak user information via inter-table relationships.
AI.motivation: Relational synthetic data offer privacy-preserving data sharing but introduce unique privacy risks due to cross-table user relationships. Existing item-level MIAs fail to capture user-level leakage across interconnected tables.
AI.method: Introduce MT-MIA under a No-Box threat model. Build a heterogeneous graph neural network to learn representations of user entities by aggregating all connected items across tables, and perform membership inference at the user level.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on real-world multi-table datasets shows MT-MIA reveals user-level leakage in state-of-the-art relational synthetic data generators, highlighting where leakage occurs and outperforming single-table MIAs.
AI.conclusion: User-level privacy risks in relational synthetic data are real and underappreciated; MT-MIA provides a more accurate audit of privacy by considering inter-table relations, suggesting the need for stronger protections and evaluation in synthetic relational data generators.

title: BRIDGE: Predicting Human Task Completion Time From Model Performance
summary: Evaluating the real-world capabilities of AI systems requires grounding benchmark performance in human-interpretable measures of task difficulty. Existing approaches that rely on direct human task completion time annotations are costly, noisy, and difficult to scale across benchmarks. In this work, we propose BRIDGE, a unified psychometric framework that learns the latent difficulty scale from model responses and anchors it to human task completion time. Using a two-parameter logistic Item Response Theory model, we jointly estimate latent task difficulty and model capability from model performance data across multiple benchmarks. We demonstrate that latent task difficulty varies linearly with the logarithm of human completion time, allowing human task completion time to be inferred for new benchmarks from model performance alone. Leveraging this alignment, we forecast frontier model capabilities in terms of human task length and independently reproduce METR's exponential scaling results, with the 50% solvable task horizon doubling approximately every 6 months.

AI.tldr: BRIDGE uses a two-parameter logistic IRT model to align model performance with human task difficulty, grounding benchmark difficulty in human-interpretable time measures and enabling time estimates for new benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To obtain human-interpretable, scalable task difficulty measures for AI benchmarks without costly time annotations, enabling cross-benchmark comparability and forecasting frontier capabilities.
AI.method: Joint estimation of latent task difficulty and model capability across benchmarks using a 2-PL IRT model; anchor latent difficulty to human completion time via a linear relationship with log(time); infer human task times for new benchmarks from model performance.
AI.result: Demonstrates a linear relationship between latent difficulty and log human completion time; enables inferring human completion times for new benchmarks from model performance; reproduces METR's exponential scaling (50% solvable horizon doubles ~every 6 months).
AI.conclusion: BRIDGE provides a scalable, interpretable framework to quantify AI benchmark difficulty in human terms and to forecast frontier capabilities based on task length.

title: A Distributed Multi-Modal Sensing Approach for Human Activity Recognition in Real-Time Human-Robot Collaboration
summary: Human activity recognition (HAR) is fundamental in human-robot collaboration (HRC), enabling robots to respond to and dynamically adapt to human intentions. This paper introduces a HAR system combining a modular data glove equipped with Inertial Measurement Units and a vision-based tactile sensor to capture hand activities in contact with a robot. We tested our activity recognition approach under different conditions, including offline classification of segmented sequences, real-time classification under static conditions, and a realistic HRC scenario. The experimental results show a high accuracy for all the tasks, suggesting that multiple collaborative settings could benefit from this multi-modal approach.

AI.tldr: A multimodal hand HAR system using a data glove with IMUs and a vision-based tactile sensor achieves high accuracy across offline, real-time static, and realistic HRC tests.
AI.motivation: To enhance robot responsiveness and safety in HRC by robustly recognizing hand activities through integrated IMU and tactile vision sensing.
AI.method: Integrates a modular data glove with IMUs and a vision-based tactile sensor to capture hand actions; evaluates offline sequence classification, real-time static classification, and a realistic HRC scenario.
AI.result: High accuracy observed for all tasks, indicating the approach's effectiveness and potential benefits to various collaborative settings.
AI.conclusion: Multi-modal sensing improves HAR performance in HRC and supports diverse collaborative applications.

title: Modality Gap-Driven Subspace Alignment Training Paradigm For Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Despite the success of multimodal contrastive learning in aligning visual and linguistic representations, a persistent geometric anomaly, the Modality Gap, remains: embeddings of distinct modalities expressing identical semantics occupy systematically offset regions. Prior approaches to bridge this gap are largely limited by oversimplified isotropic assumptions, hindering their application in large-scale scenarios. In this paper, we address these limitations by precisely characterizing the geometric shape of the modality gap and leveraging it for efficient model scaling. First, we propose the Fixed-frame Modality Gap Theory, which decomposes the modality gap within a frozen reference frame into stable biases and anisotropic residuals. Guided by this precise modeling, we introduce ReAlign, a training-free modality alignment strategy. Utilizing statistics from massive unpaired data, ReAlign aligns text representation into the image representation distribution via a three-step process comprising Anchor, Trace, and Centroid Alignment, thereby explicitly rectifying geometric misalignment. Building on ReAlign, we propose ReVision, a scalable training paradigm for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). ReVision integrates ReAlign into the pretraining stage, enabling the model to learn the distribution of visual representations from unpaired text before visual instruction tuning, without the need for large-scale, high-quality image-text pairs. Our framework demonstrates that statistically aligned unpaired data can effectively substitute for expensive image-text pairs, offering a robust path for the efficient scaling of MLLMs.

AI.tldr: Introduces Fixed-frame Modality Gap Theory to characterize and fix the geometry of cross-modal representations; proposes ReAlign (training-free alignment using unpaired data) and ReVision (scalable pretraining for MLLMs that leverages unpaired text to learn visual representation distribution), enabling efficient scaling without expensive image-text pairs.
AI.motivation: To overcome the persistent Modality Gap in multimodal contrastive learning, which shows systematic offsets between modalities with identical semantics due to anisotropic geometry and isotropic assumptions, and to enable scalable learning using unpaired data.
AI.method: Decompose the modality gap into stable biases and anisotropic residuals within a frozen reference frame; implement ReAlign with Anchor-Trace-Centroid Alignment using statistics from large-scale unpaired data to map text into the image representation distribution; integrate ReAlign into pretraining to form ReVision, letting the model learn visual distribution from unpaired text before visual instruction tuning.
AI.result: Demonstrates that statistically aligned unpaired data can substitute for expensive image-text pairs, enabling robust scaling of Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) via the proposed training-free alignment and scalable pretraining paradigm.
AI.conclusion: The work presents a cohesive framework for efficient MMLM scaling by precisely modeling and correcting the modality gap geometry, with ReAlign enabling training-free alignment and ReVision providing a scalable pretraining strategy that leverages unpaired data.

title: TermiGen: High-Fidelity Environment and Robust Trajectory Synthesis for Terminal Agents
summary: Executing complex terminal tasks remains a significant challenge for open-weight LLMs, constrained by two fundamental limitations. First, high-fidelity, executable training environments are scarce: environments synthesized from real-world repositories are not diverse and scalable, while trajectories synthesized by LLMs suffer from hallucinations. Second, standard instruction tuning uses expert trajectories that rarely exhibit simple mistakes common to smaller models. This creates a distributional mismatch, leaving student models ill-equipped to recover from their own runtime failures. To bridge these gaps, we introduce TermiGen, an end-to-end pipeline for synthesizing verifiable environments and resilient expert trajectories. Termi-Gen first generates functionally valid tasks and Docker containers via an iterative multi-agent refinement loop. Subsequently, we employ a Generator-Critic protocol that actively injects errors during trajectory collection, synthesizing data rich in error-correction cycles. Fine-tuned on this TermiGen-generated dataset, our TermiGen-Qwen2.5-Coder-32B achieves a 31.3% pass rate on TerminalBench. This establishes a new open-weights state-of-the-art, outperforming existing baselines and notably surpassing capable proprietary models such as o4-mini. Dataset is avaiable at https://github.com/ucsb-mlsec/terminal-bench-env.

AI.tldr: TermiGen proposes an end-to-end pipeline to synthesize verifiable environments and resilient expert trajectories for open-weight LLMs to tackle complex terminal tasks; achieves 31.3% pass rate on TerminalBench and establishes open-weight SOTA.
AI.motivation: to overcome scarcity of high-fidelity executable environments and distribution mismatch in expert trajectories, enabling robust error recovery in smaller models.
AI.method: iterative multi-agent refinement to generate functionally valid tasks and Docker containers; Generator-Critic protocol to inject errors during trajectory collection to create training data with recovery cycles; fine-tuning on generated data; model TermiGen-Qwen2.5-Coder-32B.
AI.result: 31.3% pass rate on TerminalBench; open-weight SOTA, surpassing baselines and o4-mini.
AI.conclusion: TermiGen advances open-weight terminal-task capabilities and provides a reproducible dataset; potential for broader applicability and further improvement through richer environments.

title: Airspace-aware Contingency Landing Planning
summary: This paper develops a real-time, search-based aircraft contingency landing planner that minimizes traffic disruptions while accounting for ground risk. The airspace model captures dense air traffic departure and arrival flows, helicopter corridors, and prohibited zones and is demonstrated with a Washington, D.C., area case study. Historical Automatic Dependent Surveillance-Broadcast (ADS-B) data are processed to estimate air traffic density. A low-latency computational geometry algorithm generates proximity-based heatmaps around high-risk corridors and restricted regions. Airspace risk is quantified as the cumulative exposure time of a landing trajectory within congested regions, while ground risk is assessed from overflown population density to jointly guide trajectory selection. A landing site selection module further mitigates disruption to nominal air traffic operations. Benchmarking against minimum-risk Dubins solutions demonstrates that the proposed planner achieves lower joint risk and reduced airspace disruption while maintaining real-time performance. Under airspace-risk-only conditions, the planner generates trajectories within an average of 2.9 seconds on a laptop computer. Future work will incorporate dynamic air traffic updates to enable spatiotemporal contingency landing planning that minimizes the need for real-time traffic rerouting.

AI.tldr: A real-time, search-based contingency landing planner minimizes joint airspace and ground risk using ADS-B density, proximity heatmaps, and a landing-site module, outperforming minimum-risk Dubins planning on real-time performance.
AI.motivation: To reduce disruption to nominal air traffic while protecting ground populations during contingency aircraft landings by jointly accounting for airspace congestion and ground exposure.
AI.method: 1) Build an airspace model with dense departure/arrival flows, helicopter corridors, and prohibited zones (DC area case study). 2) Use historical ADS-B data to estimate air traffic density. 3) Apply a low-latency computational geometry algorithm to generate proximity-based heatmaps around high-risk corridors and restricted regions. 4) Define airspace risk as cumulative exposure time of the landing trajectory within congested regions; assess ground risk from overflown population density. 5) Use a landing-site selection module to reduce disruption to nominal operations. 6) Benchmark against minimum-risk Dubins solutions for joint risk and airspace disruption, plus real-time performance metrics.
AI.result: The planner achieves lower joint (airspace plus ground) risk and reduced airspace disruption compared with Dubins-based baselines, while maintaining real-time performance. Under airspace-risk-only conditions, trajectories are generated in an average of 2.9 seconds on a laptop, indicating strong computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Future work includes incorporating dynamic air traffic updates to enable spatiotemporal contingency landing planning and further minimize real-time traffic rerouting.

title: Fair Context Learning for Evidence-Balanced Test-Time Adaptation in Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as CLIP enable strong zero-shot recognition but suffer substantial degradation under distribution shifts. Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) aims to improve robustness using only unlabeled test samples, yet most prompt-based TTA methods rely on entropy minimization -- an approach that can amplify spurious correlations and induce overconfident errors when classes share visual features. We propose Fair Context Learning (FCL), an episodic TTA framework that avoids entropy minimization by explicitly addressing shared-evidence bias. Motivated by our additive evidence decomposition assumption, FCL decouples adaptation into (i) augmentation-based exploration to identify plausible class candidates, and (ii) fairness-driven calibration that adapts text contexts to equalize sensitivity to common visual evidence. This fairness constraint mitigates partial feature obsession and enables effective calibration of text embeddings without relying on entropy reduction. Through extensive evaluation, we empirically validate our theoretical motivation and show that FCL achieves competitive adaptation performance relative to state-of-the-art TTA methods across diverse domain-shift and fine-grained benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Fair Context Learning (FCL) is an episodic test-time adaptation method for vision-language models that avoids entropy minimization by tackling shared-evidence bias through augmentation-based exploration and fairness-driven calibration of text prompts, yielding robust performance under distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models like CLIP suffer performance degradation under distribution shifts. Prompt-based TTA often relies on entropy minimization, which can amplify spurious correlations when classes share visual features. An explicit mitigation of shared evidence is needed.
AI.method: FCL adopts an additive evidence decomposition. It decouples adaptation into (i) augmentation-based exploration to identify plausible class candidates, and (ii) fairness-driven calibration to adapt text contexts so that sensitivity to common visual evidence is equalized. This episodic approach avoids entropy reduction.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows FCL achieves competitive adaptation performance compared with state-of-the-art TTA methods across diverse domain-shift and fine-grained benchmarks, and provides empirical support for the theoretical motivation.
AI.conclusion: FCL mitigates partial feature obsession, enables effective calibration of text embeddings without entropy minimization, and validates the additive-evidence decomposition for robust TTA in VLMs.

title: Featured Reproducing Kernel Banach Spaces for Learning and Neural Networks
summary: Reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces provide a foundational framework for kernel-based learning, where regularization and interpolation problems admit finite-dimensional solutions through classical representer theorems. Many modern learning models, however -- including fixed-architecture neural networks equipped with non-quadratic norms -- naturally give rise to non-Hilbertian geometries that fall outside this setting. In Banach spaces, continuity of point-evaluation functionals alone is insufficient to guarantee feature representations or kernel-based learning formulations. In this work, we develop a functional-analytic framework for learning in Banach spaces based on the notion of featured reproducing kernel Banach spaces. We identify the precise structural conditions under which feature maps, kernel constructions, and representer-type results can be recovered beyond the Hilbertian regime. Within this framework, supervised learning is formulated as a minimal-norm interpolation or regularization problem, and existence results together with conditional representer theorems are established. We further extend the theory to vector-valued featured reproducing kernel Banach spaces and show that fixed-architecture neural networks naturally induce special instances of such spaces. This provides a unified function-space perspective on kernel methods and neural networks and clarifies when kernel-based learning principles extend beyond reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces.

AI.tldr: Extends kernel methods from reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces to Banach spaces via featured reproducing kernel Banach spaces (RKBS), establishing structural conditions for feature maps, kernels, and representer-type results; formulates learning as minimal-norm interpolation/regularization; extends to vector-valued RKBS; connects to fixed-architecture neural networks as special cases, offering a unified view beyond Hilbert spaces.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of Hilbert-space-based kernel frameworks for non-Hilbertian geometries used by modern models (e.g., fixed-architecture neural networks with non-quadratic norms). In Banach spaces, evaluation continuity alone does not guarantee kernel representations; the work develops a functional-analytic framework (featured RKBS) to recover feature maps, kernels, and representer theorems beyond Hilbert spaces.
AI.method: Introduce featured reproducing kernel Banach spaces with explicit structural conditions for the existence of feature maps and kernels. Prove existsence and conditional representer theorems for minimal-norm interpolation and regularization in Banach spaces. Extend the theory to vector-valued RKBS and show that fixed-architecture neural networks induce special RKBS instances.
AI.result: A theoretical framework that generalizes kernel learning to Banach spaces, including existence results and conditional representer theorems; structured conditions for feature maps and kernels; vector-valued extension; neural networks interpreted as particular RKBS constructions.
AI.conclusion: Provides a unified function-space perspective that clarifies when kernel-based learning extends beyond RKHS, showing that neural networks can be viewed as special cases within RKBS and establishing groundwork for kernel-method principles in non-Hilbertian settings.

title: Steer2Adapt: Dynamically Composing Steering Vectors Elicits Efficient Adaptation of LLMs
summary: Activation steering has emerged as a promising approach for efficiently adapting large language models (LLMs) to downstream behaviors. However, most existing steering methods rely on a single static direction per task or concept, making them inflexible under task variation and inadequate for complex tasks that require multiple coordinated capabilities. To address this limitation, we propose STEER2ADAPT, a lightweight framework that adapts LLMs by composing steering vectors rather than learning new ones from scratch. In many domains (e.g., reasoning or safety), tasks share a small set of underlying concept dimensions. STEER2ADAPT captures these dimensions as a reusable, low-dimensional semantic prior subspace, and adapts to new tasks by dynamically discovering a linear combination of basis vectors from only a handful of examples. Experiments across 9 tasks and 3 models in both reasoning and safety domains demonstrate the effectiveness of STEER2ADAPT, achieving an average improvement of 8.2%. Extensive analyses further show that STEER2ADAPT is a data-efficient, stable, and transparent inference-time adaptation method for LLMs.

AI.tldr: STEER2ADAPT is a subspace-based, data-efficient method that composes steering vectors from a reusable low-dimensional basis to adapt LLMs across tasks, enabling multi-capability adaptation by learning task-specific linear combinations from few examples.
AI.motivation: Single static steering directions per task limit flexibility under task variation and fail to capture multi- capability requirements; there is a need for a reusable, low-dimensional semantic prior subspace to support diverse downstream tasks.
AI.method: Construct a reusable, low-dimensional basis of steering vectors representing underlying concept dimensions; adapt to new tasks by dynamically finding a linear combination of basis vectors from a small number of examples, without learning new high-dimensional vectors from scratch.
AI.result: Across 9 tasks and 3 models in reasoning and safety domains, STEER2ADAPT yields an average improvement of 8.2% and demonstrates data-efficiency, stability, and transparency during inference-time adaptation.
AI.conclusion: STEER2ADAPT offers a flexible, efficient, and interpretable framework for LLM adaptation by leveraging a compositional, subspace-based steering approach that generalizes across tasks with shared concept dimensions.

title: A compliant ankle-actuated compass walker with triggering timing control
summary: Passive dynamic walkers are widely adopted as a mathematical model to represent biped walking. The stable locomotion of these models is limited to tilted surfaces, requiring gravitational energy. Various techniques, such as actuation through the ankle and hip joints, have been proposed to extend the applicability of these models to level ground and rough terrain with improved locomotion efficiency. However, most of these techniques rely on impulsive energy injection schemes and torsional springs, which are quite challenging to implement in a physical platform. Here, a new model is proposed, named triggering controlled ankle actuated compass gait (TC-AACG), which allows non-instantaneous compliant ankle pushoff. The proposed technique can be implemented in physical platforms via series elastic actuators (SEAs). Our systematic examination shows that the proposed approach extends the locomotion capabilities of a biped model compared to impulsive ankle pushoff approach. We provide extensive simulation analysis investigating the locomotion speed, mechanical cost of transport, and basin of attraction of the proposed model.

AI.tldr: Introduces TC-AACG, a non-instantaneous compliant ankle pushoff mechanism using series elastic actuators (SEAs), extending biped locomotion beyond impulsive pushoff, with simulations showing improved speed, lower mechanical cost of transport, and a larger basin of attraction on level and rough terrain.
AI.motivation: Passive dynamic walkers are limited to tilted surfaces and rely on gravitational energy; impulsive ankle pushoff with torsional springs is difficult to implement on real platforms. A practical, non-impulsive, compliant actuation strategy is sought to enable level-ground and rough-terrain locomotion.
AI.method: Proposes triggering controlled ankle actuated compass gait (TC-AACG) featuring non-instantaneous compliant ankle pushoff implemented via SEAs. Conducts systematic simulation studies to evaluate locomotion speed, mechanical cost of transport, and basin of attraction, and compares to impulsive ankle pushoff approaches.
AI.result: Simulation results indicate that TC-AACG extends locomotion capabilities beyond the impulsive-pushoff approach, achieving favorable speed, reduced cost of transport, and an enlarged basin of attraction.
AI.conclusion: Non-instantaneous compliant ankle actuation with SEAs can practically realize enhanced biped locomotion on level/rough terrain, broadening the applicability of compass-gait models; hardware validation and robustness analyses are needed in future work.

title: A Comparative Study of Adversarial Robustness in CNN and CNN-ANFIS Architectures
summary: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) achieve strong image classification performance but lack interpretability and are vulnerable to adversarial attacks. Neuro-fuzzy hybrids such as DCNFIS replace fully connected CNN classifiers with Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference Systems (ANFIS) to improve interpretability, yet their robustness remains underexplored. This work compares standard CNNs (ConvNet, VGG, ResNet18) with their ANFIS-augmented counterparts on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 under gradient-based (PGD) and gradient-free (Square) attacks. Results show that ANFIS integration does not consistently improve clean accuracy and has architecture-dependent effects on robustness: ResNet18-ANFIS exhibits improved adversarial robustness, while VGG-ANFIS often underperforms its baseline. These findings suggest that neuro-fuzzy augmentation can enhance robustness in specific architectures but is not universally beneficial.

AI.tldr: ANFIS-augmented CNNs show mixed robustness: some architectures gain protection against adversarial attacks while others degrade, with no universal improvement in clean accuracy or robustness.
AI.motivation: To address CNN interpretability and robustness by substituting fully connected layers with Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference Systems (ANFIS); evaluate whether neuro-fuzzy hybrids improve resilience to adversarial perturbations across common datasets.
AI.method: Evaluate standard CNNs (ConvNet, VGG, ResNet18) and their ANFIS-based counterparts on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100 under gradient-based PGD and gradient-free Square attacks; compare clean accuracy and robustness; analyze architecture-dependent effects.
AI.result: ANFIS integration yields inconsistent clean accuracy gains; robustness is architecture-dependent: ResNet18-ANFIS shows improved adversarial robustness, while VGG-ANFIS often underperforms; no universal robustness enhancement across datasets or attacks.
AI.conclusion: Neuro-fuzzy augmentation can boost robustness in certain architectures but is not universally beneficial; architecture-aware design is needed, and further research is required to map when ANFIS helps.

title: BONSAI: Bayesian Optimization with Natural Simplicity and Interpretability
summary: Bayesian optimization (BO) is a popular technique for sample-efficient optimization of black-box functions. In many applications, the parameters being tuned come with a carefully engineered default configuration, and practitioners only want to deviate from this default when necessary. Standard BO, however, does not aim to minimize deviation from the default and, in practice, often pushes weakly relevant parameters to the boundary of the search space. This makes it difficult to distinguish between important and spurious changes and increases the burden of vetting recommendations when the optimization objective omits relevant operational considerations. We introduce BONSAI, a default-aware BO policy that prunes low-impact deviations from a default configuration while explicitly controlling the loss in acquisition value. BONSAI is compatible with a variety of acquisition functions, including expected improvement and upper confidence bound (GP-UCB). We theoretically bound the regret incurred by BONSAI, showing that, under certain conditions, it enjoys the same no-regret property as vanilla GP-UCB. Across many real-world applications, we empirically find that BONSAI substantially reduces the number of non-default parameters in recommended configurations while maintaining competitive optimization performance, with little effect on wall time.

AI.tldr: BONSAI is a default-aware Bayesian optimization policy that prunes low-impact deviations from a given default configuration while bounding the loss in acquisition value, compatible with EI and GP-UCB, with no-regret guarantees under certain conditions; empirically reduces non-default parameter changes without sacrificing optimization performance or speed.
AI.motivation: Practitioners often prefer to stay close to a known default configuration; standard BO tends to push parameters to the search space boundary, making it hard to distinguish important from spurious changes and increasing vetting burden. BONSAI addresses this by prioritizing deviating from the default only when impactful, and by controlling acquisition-value loss.
AI.method: BONSAI introduces a policy that prunes low-impact deviations from the default configuration, explicitly bounding the allowed drop in acquisition value; it is compatible with multiple acquisition functions (e.g., EI, GP-UCB); theoretical analysis provides no-regret bounds under certain conditions.
AI.result: Empirically, BONSAI reduces the number of non-default parameters in recommended configurations, while maintaining competitive optimization performance and incurring little overhead in wall-clock time; it performs well across real-world applications.
AI.conclusion: BONSAI demonstrates that it is possible to retain optimization effectiveness while being default-aware, offering a principled way to limit unnecessary departures from default configurations and easing practitioner burden; it retains key theoretical guarantees and broad compatibility.

title: Adaptive Scaffolding for Cognitive Engagement in an Intelligent Tutoring System
summary: The ICAP framework defines four cognitive engagement levels: Passive, Active, Constructive, and Interactive, where increased cognitive engagement can yield improved learning. However, personalizing learning activities that elicit the optimal level of cognitive engagement remains a key challenge in intelligent tutoring systems (ITS). In this work, we develop and evaluate a system that adaptively scaffolds cognitive engagement by dynamically selecting worked examples in two different ICAP modes: (active) Guided examples and (constructive) Buggy examples. We compare Bayesian Knowledge Tracing (BKT) and Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) as adaptive methods against a non-adaptive baseline method for selecting example type in a logic ITS. Our experiment with 113 students demonstrates that both adaptive policies significantly improved student performance on test problems. BKT yielded the largest improvement in posttest scores for low prior knowledge students, helping them catch up with their high prior knowledge peers, whereas DRL yielded significantly higher posttest scores among high prior knowledge students. This paper contributes new insights into the complex interactions of cognitive engagement and adaptivity and their results on learning outcomes.

AI.tldr: Adaptive selection of ICAP-mode worked examples (guided vs buggy) in a logic ITS using BKT and DRL improves posttest scores; BKT helps low prior knowledge, DRL helps high prior knowledge; non-adaptive baseline underperforms.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of personalizing learning activities to elicit optimal cognitive engagement in ITS by dynamically selecting example types within ICAP framework; compare two adaptive methods (BKT, DRL) vs baseline.
AI.method: Logic ITS with two ICAP modes: Guided examples (active) and Buggy examples (constructive). Implement adaptive policies using Bayesian Knowledge Tracing (BKT) and Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) to select example type; non-adaptive baseline; experiment with 113 students; measure posttest performance.
AI.result: Both adaptive policies significantly improve posttest scores relative to baseline. BKT yields larger gains for low prior knowledge students; DRL yields larger gains for high prior knowledge students.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive selection of example types via BKT/DRL can modulate cognitive engagement to improve learning; BKT is advantageous for learners with low prior knowledge, DRL for high prior knowledge; findings contribute to understanding ICAP engagement and adaptive pedagogy; further work needed for generalization and deeper analysis.

title: Continuum Robot Localization using Distributed Time-of-Flight Sensors
summary: Localization and mapping of an environment are crucial tasks for any robot operating in unstructured environments. Time-of-flight (ToF) sensors (e.g.,~lidar) have proven useful in mobile robotics, where high-resolution sensors can be used for simultaneous localization and mapping. In soft and continuum robotics, however, these high-resolution sensors are too large for practical use. This, combined with the deformable nature of such robots, has resulted in continuum robot (CR) localization and mapping in unstructured environments being a largely untouched area. In this work, we present a localization technique for CRs that relies on small, low-resolution ToF sensors distributed along the length of the robot. By fusing measurement information with a robot shape prior, we show that accurate localization is possible despite each sensor experiencing frequent degenerate scenarios. We achieve an average localization error of 2.5cm in position and 7.2° in rotation across all experimental conditions with a 53cm long robot. We demonstrate that the results are repeated across multiple environments, in both simulation and real-world experiments, and study robustness in the estimation to deviations in the prior map.

AI.tldr: Localization of continuum robots using distributed, low-resolution ToF sensors fused with a shape prior; achieves 2.5 cm position and 7.2° orientation accuracy on a 53 cm robot across simulations and real-world tests.
AI.motivation: CRs are deformable and bulky high-resolution ToF sensors hinder practical localization and mapping. A localizing method using small, distributed ToF measurements combined with a prior shape model addresses this gap.
AI.method: Fuse measurements from distributed low-resolution ToF sensors along the robot with a robot shape prior to estimate pose; leverages prior to mitigate degenerate sensor readings; evaluated in both simulation and real-world experiments with a 53 cm CR.
AI.result: Average localization error: 2.5 cm in position and 7.2° in rotation across all conditions; results replicated across environments and between simulation and real-world; robustness to deviations in the prior map.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of accurate CR localization using compact sensing and prior fusion; shows robustness to prior deviations; suggests practical applicability in unstructured environments; future work may address scalability, dynamic deformations, and learning-based priors to handle larger variations.

title: UNIKIE-BENCH: Benchmarking Large Multimodal Models for Key Information Extraction in Visual Documents
summary: Key Information Extraction (KIE) from real-world documents remains challenging due to substantial variations in layout structures, visual quality, and task-specific information requirements. Recent Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have shown promising potential for performing end-to-end KIE directly from document images. To enable a comprehensive and systematic evaluation across realistic and diverse application scenarios, we introduce UNIKIE-BENCH, a unified benchmark designed to rigorously evaluate the KIE capabilities of LMMs. UNIKIE-BENCH consists of two complementary tracks: a constrained-category KIE track with scenario-predefined schemas that reflect practical application needs, and an open-category KIE track that extracts any key information that is explicitly present in the document. Experiments on 15 state-of-the-art LMMs reveal substantial performance degradation under diverse schema definitions, long-tail key fields, and complex layouts, along with pronounced performance disparities across different document types and scenarios. These findings underscore persistent challenges in grounding accuracy and layout-aware reasoning for LMM-based KIE. All codes and datasets are available at https://github.com/NEUIR/UNIKIE-BENCH.

AI.tldr: UNIKIE-BENCH introduces a two-track benchmark to rigorously evaluate LMM-based KIE on realistic documents, revealing significant gaps in grounding accuracy and layout-aware reasoning across schemas, long-tail fields, and layouts.
AI.motivation: Document KIE with real-world variability is challenging for LMMs; there is a need for a unified, systematic benchmark to assess end-to-end KIE performance under diverse schemas and layouts.
AI.method: Propose UNIKIE-BENCH with two complementary tracks: (1) constrained-category KIE with scenario-predefined schemas, and (2) open-category KIE that extracts any key information evident in the document. Evaluate 15 state-of-the-art LMMs on the benchmark to analyze grounding and layout reasoning under varied conditions.
AI.result: Experiments show substantial performance degradation when schemas vary, long-tail key fields appear, or layouts are complex; results also reveal pronounced performance disparities across document types and scenarios.
AI.conclusion: UNIKIE-BENCH highlights persistent challenges in grounding accuracy and layout-aware reasoning for LMM-based KIE and provides a unified platform for comprehensive evaluation; code and data are publicly available.

title: Convex Dominance in Deep Learning I: A Scaling Law of Loss and Learning Rate
comment: Part of a planned series to understand and leverage the convexity in deep learning. Accepted to ICLR 2026
summary: Deep learning has non-convex loss landscape and its optimization dynamics is hard to analyze or control. Nevertheless, the dynamics can be empirically convex-like across various tasks, models, optimizers, hyperparameters, etc. In this work, we examine the applicability of convexity and Lipschitz continuity in deep learning, in order to precisely control the loss dynamics via the learning rate schedules. We illustrate that deep learning quickly becomes weakly convex after a short period of training, and the loss is predicable by an upper bound on the last iterate, which further informs the scaling of optimal learning rate. Through the lens of convexity, we build scaling laws of learning rates and losses that extrapolate as much as 80X across training horizons and 70X across model sizes.

AI.tldr: Quasi-convex/weakly convex behavior emerges quickly in deep learning, enabling last-iterate loss bounds to guide learning-rate schedules; convexity/Lipschitz analysis yields scaling laws extrapolating LR and loss across training horizons (80x) and model sizes (70x).
AI.motivation: Understand and control deep-learning optimization by applying convexity and Lipschitz concepts to derive principled learning-rate schedules.
AI.method: Empirical analysis of convex-like properties in DL loss landscapes, derivation of upper bounds on the last iterate, and construction of scaling laws for learning rates and losses across training horizons and model sizes.
AI.result: Evidence that DL optimization rapidly becomes weakly convex; last-iterate bounds predict loss; proposed scaling laws allow substantial extrapolation of LR and loss.
AI.conclusion: A convexity-oriented lens may enable principled, scalable learning-rate scheduling across training regimes, suggesting a unifying view of optimization dynamics in deep learning.

title: RAPiD: Real-time Deterministic Trajectory Planning via Diffusion Behavior Priors for Safe and Efficient Autonomous Driving
summary: Diffusion-based trajectory planners have demonstrated strong capability for modeling the multimodal nature of human driving behavior, but their reliance on iterative stochastic sampling poses critical challenges for real-time, safety-critical deployment. In this work, we present RAPiD, a deterministic policy extraction framework that distills a pretrained diffusion-based planner into an efficient policy while eliminating diffusion sampling. Using score-regularized policy optimization, we leverage the score function of a pre-trained diffusion planner as a behavior prior to regularize policy learning. To promote safety and passenger comfort, the policy is optimized using a critic trained to imitate a predictive driver controller, providing dense, safety-focused supervision beyond conventional imitation learning. Evaluations demonstrate that RAPiD achieves competitive performance on closed-loop nuPlan scenarios with an 8x speedup over diffusion baselines, while achieving state-of-the-art generalization among learning-based planners on the interPlan benchmark. The official website of this work is: https://github.com/ruturajreddy/RAPiD.

AI.tldr: RAPiD distills a diffusion-based planner into a deterministic policy via score-regularized policy optimization, achieving real-time performance with strong generalization.
AI.motivation: Diffusion planners model multimodal driving but are slow due to iterative sampling, limiting real-time, safety-critical deployment.
AI.method: Leverage the diffusion planner's score function as a behavior prior and regularize policy learning with score-regularized policy optimization. Use a critic trained to imitate a predictive driver controller to provide safety-focused supervision.
AI.result: RAPiD achieves ~8x speedup over diffusion baselines in closed-loop nuPlan scenarios and attains state-of-the-art generalization among learning-based planners on interPlan.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic policy extraction from diffusion planners can deliver real-time, safe, and generalizable planning by combining score-based priors with driver-controller supervision.

title: Realistic Synthetic Household Data Generation at Scale
comment: Accepted at Agentic AI Benchmarks and Applications for Enterprise Tasks workshop at AAAI 2026
summary:
Advancements in foundation models have catalyzed research in Embodied AI to develop interactive agents capable of environmental reasoning and interaction. Developing such agents requires diverse, large-scale datasets. Prior frameworks generate synthetic data for long-term human-robot interactions but fail to model the bidirectional influence between human behavior and household environments. Our proposed generative framework creates household datasets at scale through loosely coupled generation of long-term human-robot interactions and environments. Human personas influence environment generation, while environment schematics and semantics shape human-robot interactions.
  The generated 3D data includes rich static context such as object and environment semantics, and temporal context capturing human and agent behaviors over extended periods. Our flexible tool allows users to define dataset characteristics via natural language prompts, enabling configuration of environment and human activity data through natural language specifications. The tool creates variations of user-defined configurations, enabling scalable data generation.
  We validate our framework through statistical evaluation using multi-modal embeddings and key metrics: cosine similarity, mutual information gain, intervention analysis, and iterative improvement validation. Statistical comparisons show good alignment with real-world datasets (HOMER) with cosine similarity (0.60), while synthetic datasets (Wang et al.) show moderate alignment (0.27). Intervention analysis across age, organization, and sleep pattern changes shows statistically significant effects (p < 0.001) with large effect sizes (Cohen's d = 0.51-1.12), confirming bidirectional coupling translates persona traits into measurable environmental and behavioral differences. These contributions enable development and testing of household smart devices at scale.

AI.tldr: A generative framework for scalable, long-term human–robot interaction datasets in household environments that models bidirectional coupling between human personas and environments; validates via multi-modal embeddings against real data and interventions; enables natural-language-driven configuration and data variations; shows realism improvements over prior synthetic methods.
AI.motivation: There is a need for large-scale, diverse embodied AI datasets that capture long-term interactions and environment dynamics. Existing synthetic data pipelines typically generate HRI in isolation and miss bidirectional influences between human behavior and home environments, limiting realism and transferability.
AI.method: Loosely coupled generation of long-term human–robot interactions and environments. Human personas influence environment generation, while environment schematics and semantics shape interactions. The generated 3D data includes rich static context and temporal behavior. A flexible tool accepts natural language prompts to define dataset characteristics and can produce multiple variations of user-defined configurations. Evaluation uses multi-modal embeddings and metrics such as cosine similarity, mutual information gain, intervention analysis, and iterative improvement validation.
AI.result: Cosine similarity to real-world HOMER data: 0.60 (strong alignment); synthetic Wang et al. data: 0.27 (moderate alignment). Intervention analysis shows significant effects for changes in age, organization, and sleep pattern (p < 0.001) with large effects (Cohen’s d = 0.51–1.12), indicating bidirectional coupling translates persona traits into environmental and behavioral differences. The framework enables scalable data generation to test and develop household smart devices.
AI.conclusion: The framework achieves meaningful alignment with real data and demonstrates bidirectional persona–environment coupling, enabling scalable testing of embodied AI agents in household contexts. The natural-language configurability adds practical flexibility for creating diverse datasets.

title: OMNI-Dent: Towards an Accessible and Explainable AI Framework for Automated Dental Diagnosis
summary: Accurate dental diagnosis is essential for oral healthcare, yet many individuals lack access to timely professional evaluation. Existing AI-based methods primarily treat diagnosis as a visual pattern recognition task and do not reflect the structured clinical reasoning used by dental professionals. These approaches also require large amounts of expert-annotated data and often struggle to generalize across diverse real-world imaging conditions. To address these limitations, we present OMNI-Dent, a data-efficient and explainable diagnostic framework that incorporates clinical reasoning principles into a Vision-Language Model (VLM)-based pipeline. The framework operates on multi-view smartphone photographs,embeds diagnostic heuristics from dental experts, and guides a general-purpose VLM to perform tooth-level evaluation without dental-specific fine-tuning of the VLM. By utilizing the VLM's existing visual-linguistic capabilities, OMNI-Dent aims to support diagnostic assessment in settings where curated clinical imaging is unavailable. Designed as an early-stage assistive tool, OMNI-Dent helps users identify potential abnormalities and determine when professional evaluation may be needed, offering a practical option for individuals with limited access to in-person care.

AI.tldr: OMNI-Dent proposes a data-efficient, explainable VLM-based framework for tooth-level dental diagnosis using multi-view smartphone photos, embedding expert heuristics, without dental-specific VLM fine-tuning, aimed at assistive screening in low-access settings.
AI.motivation: Current AI dental diagnosis relies on visual pattern recognition, requiring large annotated datasets and often failing to generalize across diverse imaging conditions; there is a need for data-efficient, explainable decision-support aligned with clinical reasoning.
AI.method: Integrates a Vision-Language Model into a pipeline with multi-view smartphone images, encodes clinical heuristics from experts, and guides the VLM to perform tooth-level evaluation without fine-tuning the VLM on dental data.
AI.result: Abstract describes design and intended capabilities; does not present empirical results or metrics.
AI.conclusion: OMNI-Dent could enable early-stage, explainable triage and diagnostic guidance in settings with limited access to curated clinical imaging, serving as an assistive tool for professional evaluation.

title: On Randomness in Agentic Evals
summary: Agentic systems are evaluated on benchmarks where agents interact with environments to solve tasks. Most papers report a pass@1 score computed from a single run per task, assuming this gives a reliable performance estimate. We test this assumption by collecting 60,000 agentic trajectories on SWE-Bench-Verified, spanning three models and two scaffolds. We find substantial variance: single-run pass@1 estimates vary by 2.2 to 6.0 percentage points depending on which run is selected, with standard deviations exceeding 1.5 percentage points even at temperature 0. This variance has critical implications: reported improvements of 2--3 percentage points may reflect evaluation noise rather than genuine algorithmic progress. Through token-level analysis, we show that trajectories diverge early, often within the first few percent of tokens, and that these small differences cascade into different solution strategies. To enable reliable evaluation of agentic systems, we recommend three concrete practices: (1) estimate pass@1 from multiple independent runs per task, especially when measuring small improvements, (2) use statistical power analysis to determine the number of runs needed to detect expected effect sizes, and (3) consider metrics like pass@k (optimistic bound) and pass^k (pessimistic bound) with k>1 to better characterize the full performance envelope. While these practices increase evaluation cost, they are essential for distinguishing genuine scientific progress from statistical noise.

AI.tldr: Substantial run-to-run variance in pass@1 estimates; single-run evaluations can misrepresent progress; recommends multi-run evaluation, power analysis, and alternative metrics.
AI.motivation: Current practice relies on pass@1 from one run per task; variance undermines reliability; need robust evaluation methods to distinguish true algorithmic progress from noise.
AI.method: Analyzed 60,000 agentic trajectories on SWE-Bench-Verified across 3 models and 2 scaffolds; computed pass@1; examined run-to-run and token-level divergence; assessed implications and proposed metrics.
AI.result: Single-run estimates vary by 2.2–6.0 pp depending on the run; SD >1.5 pp even at temperature 0; early token divergences cascade into different solutions; evaluation noise can obscure improvements of 2–3 pp.
AI.conclusion: Advocate three practices: multiple independent runs per task, power analysis to determine run counts, and use pass@k and pass^k with k>1; these improve reliability but increase evaluation cost.

title: SupChain-Bench: Benchmarking Large Language Models for Real-World Supply Chain Management
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in complex reasoning and tool-based decision making, motivating their application to real-world supply chain management. However, supply chain workflows require reliable long-horizon, multi-step orchestration grounded in domain-specific procedures, which remains challenging for current models. To systematically evaluate LLM performance in this setting, we introduce SupChain-Bench, a unified real-world benchmark that assesses both supply chain domain knowledge and long-horizon tool-based orchestration grounded in standard operating procedures (SOPs). Our experiments reveal substantial gaps in execution reliability across models. We further propose SupChain-ReAct, an SOP-free framework that autonomously synthesizes executable procedures for tool use, achieving the strongest and most consistent tool-calling performance. Our work establishes a principled benchmark for studying reliable long-horizon orchestration in real-world operational settings and highlights significant room for improvement in LLM-based supply chain agents.

AI.tldr: Introduces SupChain-Bench, a real-world benchmark to test LLMs on long-horizon, SOP-grounded supply chain tasks; presents SupChain-ReAct, an SOP-free method for autonomous tool-use synthesis, achieving strong tool-calling performance; identifies gaps in current models and highlights need for reliable long-horizon orchestration in supply chains.
AI.motivation: Supply chains require reliable, long-horizon decision making guided by standard operating procedures (SOPs). Current LLMs struggle with domain-specific knowledge and orchestrating multi-step tasks across tools, necessitating a benchmark and new methods.
AI.method: 1) Develop SupChain-Bench, a unified real-world benchmark assessing domain knowledge and long-horizon tool-based orchestration; 2) empirically evaluate LLMs on the benchmark; 3) Propose SupChain-ReAct, an SOP-free framework that autonomously synthesizes executable tool-use procedures.
AI.result: Experiments reveal substantial gaps in execution reliability across models when performing long-horizon orchestration; SupChain-ReAct achieves the strongest and most consistent tool-calling performance.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a principled benchmark for studying reliable long-horizon orchestration in real-world operational settings and highlights significant room for improvement in LLM-based supply chain agents.

title: aerial-autonomy-stack -- a Faster-than-real-time, Autopilot-agnostic, ROS2 Framework to Simulate and Deploy Perception-based Drones
summary: Unmanned aerial vehicles are rapidly transforming multiple applications, from agricultural and infrastructure monitoring to logistics and defense. Introducing greater autonomy to these systems can simultaneously make them more effective as well as reliable. Thus, the ability to rapidly engineer and deploy autonomous aerial systems has become of strategic importance. In the 2010s, a combination of high-performance compute, data, and open-source software led to the current deep learning and AI boom, unlocking decades of prior theoretical work. Robotics is on the cusp of a similar transformation. However, physical AI faces unique hurdles, often combined under the umbrella term "simulation-to-reality gap". These span from modeling shortcomings to the complexity of vertically integrating the highly heterogeneous hardware and software systems typically found in field robots. To address the latter, we introduce aerial-autonomy-stack, an open-source, end-to-end framework designed to streamline the pipeline from (GPU-accelerated) perception to (flight controller-based) action. Our stack allows the development of aerial autonomy using ROS2 and provides a common interface for two of the most popular autopilots: PX4 and ArduPilot. We show that it supports over 20x faster-than-real-time, end-to-end simulation of a complete development and deployment stack -- including edge compute and networking -- significantly compressing the build-test-release cycle of perception-based autonomy.

AI.tldr: Open-source aerial-autonomy-stack enabling a ROS2-based perception-to-action pipeline with PX4/ArduPilot, achieving about 20x real-time end-to-end UAV simulation to accelerate development.
AI.motivation: Address the sim-to-real gap and the integration challenges of heterogeneous hardware/software in aerial robotics, aiming to accelerate engineering and deployment of autonomous UAV systems.
AI.method: Introduce aerial-autonomy-stack, an end-to-end framework leveraging GPU-accelerated perception, ROS2 interfaces, and adapters for PX4 and ArduPilot; supports edge compute and networking; reports >20x faster-than-real-time end-to-end simulation.
AI.result: Demonstrates substantially faster end-to-end UAV simulation and a compressed build-test-release cycle for perception-based autonomy.
AI.conclusion: Provides an open-source, scalable path to streamline aerial autonomy development and deployment, bridging simulation and real-world operation across diverse hardware/software stacks.

title: COMBOOD: A Semiparametric Approach for Detecting Out-of-distribution Data for Image Classification
comment: Copyright by SIAM. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited First Published in Proceedings of the 2024 SIAM International Conference on Data Mining (SDM24), published by the Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics (SIAM)
summary: Identifying out-of-distribution (OOD) data at inference time is crucial for many machine learning applications, especially for automation. We present a novel unsupervised semi-parametric framework COMBOOD for OOD detection with respect to image recognition. Our framework combines signals from two distance metrics, nearest-neighbor and Mahalanobis, to derive a confidence score for an inference point to be out-of-distribution. The former provides a non-parametric approach to OOD detection. The latter provides a parametric, simple, yet effective method for detecting OOD data points, especially, in the far OOD scenario, where the inference point is far apart from the training data set in the embedding space. However, its performance is not satisfactory in the near OOD scenarios that arise in practical situations. Our COMBOOD framework combines the two signals in a semi-parametric setting to provide a confidence score that is accurate both for the near-OOD and far-OOD scenarios. We show experimental results with the COMBOOD framework for different types of feature extraction strategies. We demonstrate experimentally that COMBOOD outperforms state-of-the-art OOD detection methods on the OpenOOD (both version 1 and most recent version 1.5) benchmark datasets (for both far-OOD and near-OOD) as well as on the documents dataset in terms of accuracy. On a majority of the benchmark datasets, the improvements in accuracy resulting from the COMBOOD framework are statistically significant. COMBOOD scales linearly with the size of the embedding space, making it ideal for many real-life applications.

AI.tldr: A semi-parametric fusion of non-parametric nearest-neighbor and parametric Mahalanobis signals for OOD detection, yielding robust accuracy across near- and far-OOD, with strong open-domain benchmark performance and linear scalability.
AI.motivation: Detecting out-of-distribution data at inference is essential for automated systems. Existing methods struggle in near-OOD scenarios; a robust, scalable approach that leverages both non-parametric and parametric cues can improve confidence estimation and detection performance.
AI.method: Introduce COMBOOD, an unsupervised semi-parametric framework that combines nearest-neighbor (non-parametric) and Mahalanobis (parametric) signals to produce an OOD confidence score. The framework integrates two complementary cues to handle both near-OOD and far-OOD cases. Evaluation across multiple feature extraction strategies on OpenOOD benchmarks (v1 and v1.5) and a documents dataset.
AI.result: COMBOOD outperforms state-of-the-art OOD detection methods on OpenOOD v1 and v1.5, including far-OOD and near-OOD scenarios, and on a documents dataset in terms of accuracy. Improvements are statistically significant on most benchmarks. The method scales linearly with embedding space size.
AI.conclusion: COMBOOD provides accurate, scalable OOD detection across near- and far-OOD regimes, addressing practical deployment needs in real-world applications.

title: Beyond Pooling: Matching for Robust Generalization under Data Heterogeneity
summary: Pooling heterogeneous datasets across domains is a common strategy in representation learning, but naive pooling can amplify distributional asymmetries and yield biased estimators, especially in settings where zero-shot generalization is required. We propose a matching framework that selects samples relative to an adaptive centroid and iteratively refines the representation distribution. The double robustness and the propensity score matching for the inclusion of data domains make matching more robust than naive pooling and uniform subsampling by filtering out the confounding domains (the main cause of heterogeneity). Theoretical and empirical analyses show that, unlike naive pooling or uniform subsampling, matching achieves better results under asymmetric meta-distributions, which are also extended to non-Gaussian and multimodal real-world settings. Most importantly, we show that these improvements translate to zero-shot medical anomaly detection, one of the extreme forms of data heterogeneity and asymmetry. The code is available on https://github.com/AyushRoy2001/Beyond-Pooling.

AI.tldr: A robust matching-based pooling framework with adaptive centroid and propensity-score matching to filter domains, improving zero-shot generalization under heterogeneous, asymmetric distributions.
