AI.motivation: Pooling data across domains can amplify distributional asymmetries and bias estimators, especially for zero-shot generalization; a selective inclusion mechanism is needed to mitigate confounding domain effects.
AI.method: Propose a matching framework that selects samples relative to an adaptive centroid and iteratively refines the representation distribution; employs double robustness and propensity-score matching to determine domain inclusion and filter confounding domains, reducing heterogeneity.
AI.result: Theoretical and empirical analyses show advantages over naive pooling and uniform subsampling under asymmetric meta-distributions; extends to non-Gaussian and multimodal real-world settings; improvements translate to zero-shot medical anomaly detection.
AI.conclusion: Matching-based pooling provides more robust, generalizable representations under cross-domain heterogeneity, enabling better zero-shot performance; code is available.

title: W&D:Scaling Parallel Tool Calling for Efficient Deep Research Agents
summary: Deep research agents have emerged as powerful tools for automating complex intellectual tasks through multi-step reasoning and web-based information seeking. While recent efforts have successfully enhanced these agents by scaling depth through increasing the number of sequential thinking and tool calls, the potential of scaling width via parallel tool calling remains largely unexplored. In this work, we propose the Wide and Deep research agent, a framework designed to investigate the behavior and performance of agents when scaling not only depth but also width via parallel tool calling. Unlike existing approaches that rely on complex multi-agent orchestration to parallelize workloads, our method leverages intrinsic parallel tool calling to facilitate effective coordination within a single reasoning step. We demonstrate that scaling width significantly improves performance on deep research benchmarks while reducing the number of turns required to obtain correct answers. Furthermore, we analyze the factors driving these improvements through case studies and explore various tool call schedulers to optimize parallel tool calling strategy. Our findings suggest that optimizing the trade-off between width and depth is a critical pathway toward high-efficiency deep research agents. Notably, without context management or other tricks, we obtain 62.2% accuracy with GPT-5-Medium on BrowseComp, surpassing the original 54.9% reported by GPT-5-High.

AI.tldr: Wide and Deep research agent enables parallel tool calls within a single reasoning step; width scaling improves accuracy and reduces the number of turns on deep-research benchmarks (e.g., 62.2% on BrowseComp with GPT-5-Medium, surpassing GPT-5-High's 54.9%).
AI.motivation: Explore the underexplored width dimension in scaling deep research agents and optimize the trade-off between width and depth to improve efficiency and performance.
AI.method: Propose the Wide and Deep framework that uses intrinsic parallel tool calling to coordinate multiple tools within one reasoning step; analyze different tool call schedulers; conduct case studies; benchmark on deep-research tasks such as BrowseComp.
AI.result: Width scaling yields significant performance gains and fewer reasoning turns; 62.2% accuracy on BrowseComp with GPT-5-Medium, surpassing GPT-5-High's 54.9%; insights from case studies and scheduler comparisons.
AI.conclusion: Scaling width, alongside depth, is a critical factor for high-efficiency deep research agents; parallel tool calling can achieve stronger results without complex multi-agent orchestration.

title: Action-to-Action Flow Matching
summary: Diffusion-based policies have recently achieved remarkable success in robotics by formulating action prediction as a conditional denoising process. However, the standard practice of sampling from random Gaussian noise often requires multiple iterative steps to produce clean actions, leading to high inference latency that incurs a major bottleneck for real-time control. In this paper, we challenge the necessity of uninformed noise sampling and propose Action-to-Action flow matching (A2A), a novel policy paradigm that shifts from random sampling to initialization informed by the previous action. Unlike existing methods that treat proprioceptive action feedback as static conditions, A2A leverages historical proprioceptive sequences, embedding them into a high-dimensional latent space as the starting point for action generation. This design bypasses costly iterative denoising while effectively capturing the robot's physical dynamics and temporal continuity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that A2A exhibits high training efficiency, fast inference speed, and improved generalization. Notably, A2A enables high-quality action generation in as few as a single inference step (0.56 ms latency), and exhibits superior robustness to visual perturbations and enhanced generalization to unseen configurations. Lastly, we also extend A2A to video generation, demonstrating its broader versatility in temporal modeling. Project site: https://lorenzo-0-0.github.io/A2A_Flow_Matching.

AI.tldr: A2A replaces random noise in diffusion-based policies with a history-informed, action-to-action latent initialization, enabling real-time single-step action generation and improved generalization, with extension to video generation.
AI.motivation: Standard diffusion-based action prediction relies on sampling from random Gaussian noise and multi-step denoising, which incurs large inference latency and limits real-time control; leveraging past proprioceptive data can capture dynamics and temporal continuity.
AI.method: Replace uninformed noise with an initialization derived from historical proprioceptive sequences encoded into a latent space, using it as the starting point for diffusion-based action generation (flow matching). This bypasses iterative denoising and enables ultra-fast inference; demonstrated on robotics actions and extended to video generation.
AI.result: A2A achieves high training efficiency and ultra-fast inference (as low as 0.56 ms in a single step), robust to visual perturbations, and better generalization to unseen configurations; shows applicability to video generation beyond robotics.
AI.conclusion: A2A represents a paradigm shift for diffusion-based control by conditioning generation on an informed initial state rather than random noise, enabling real-time action generation and improved temporal generalization; with potential for broader temporal modeling tasks.

title: PipeMFL-240K: A Large-scale Dataset and Benchmark for Object Detection in Pipeline Magnetic Flux Leakage Imaging
comment: A dataset contains 240,320 pipeline MFL pseudo-color images and 191,530 bounding-box annotations, collected from 11 pipelines spanning approximately 1,480 km
summary: Pipeline integrity is critical to industrial safety and environmental protection, with Magnetic Flux Leakage (MFL) detection being a primary non-destructive testing technology. Despite the promise of deep learning for automating MFL interpretation, progress toward reliable models has been constrained by the absence of a large-scale public dataset and benchmark, making fair comparison and reproducible evaluation difficult. We introduce \textbf{PipeMFL-240K}, a large-scale, meticulously annotated dataset and benchmark for complex object detection in pipeline MFL pseudo-color images. PipeMFL-240K reflects real-world inspection complexity and poses several unique challenges: (i) an extremely long-tailed distribution over \textbf{12} categories, (ii) a high prevalence of tiny objects that often comprise only a handful of pixels, and (iii) substantial intra-class variability. The dataset contains \textbf{240,320} images and \textbf{191,530} high-quality bounding-box annotations, collected from 11 pipelines spanning approximately \textbf{1,480} km. Extensive experiments are conducted with state-of-the-art object detectors to establish baselines. Results show that modern detectors still struggle with the intrinsic properties of MFL data, highlighting considerable headroom for improvement, while PipeMFL-240K provides a reliable and challenging testbed to drive future research. As the first public dataset and the first benchmark of this scale and scope for pipeline MFL inspection, it provides a critical foundation for efficient pipeline diagnostics as well as maintenance planning and is expected to accelerate algorithmic innovation and reproducible research in MFL-based pipeline integrity assessment.

AI.tldr: PipeMFL-240K provides the first large-scale, annotated dataset and benchmark for pipeline magnetic flux leakage (MFL) inspection with 240K images and 191K bounding boxes across 12 categories, exposing long-tail distributions, prevalence of tiny objects, and high intra-class variability; baseline results show room for significant improvement.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of a public, large-scale dataset and standardized benchmark for deep-learning-based MFL interpretation, enabling fair comparison, reproducible evaluation, and accelerated algorithmic research for pipeline integrity.
AI.method: Collection of data from 11 pipelines over ~1,480 km, resulting in 240,320 images with 191,530 high-quality bounding-box annotations on pseudo-color MFL images; evaluation of state-of-the-art object detectors to establish baselines and analyze dataset properties.
AI.result: Modern detectors struggle with the intrinsic properties of MFL data; PipeMFL-240K provides a challenging, realistic testbed that reveals headroom for improvement and serves as a reliable benchmark for future work.
AI.conclusion: First public, large-scale dataset and benchmark for pipeline MFL inspection; foundational resource for efficient pipeline diagnostics, maintenance planning, and accelerated reproducible research in MFL-based integrity assessment.

title: Mimetic Initialization of MLPs
summary: Mimetic initialization uses pretrained models as case studies of good initialization, using observations of structures in trained weights to inspire new, simple initialization techniques. So far, it has been applied only to spatial mixing layers, such convolutional, self-attention, and state space layers. In this work, we present the first attempt to apply the method to channel mixing layers, namely multilayer perceptrons (MLPs). Our extremely simple technique for MLPs -- to give the first layer a nonzero mean -- speeds up training on small-scale vision tasks like CIFAR-10 and ImageNet-1k. Though its effect is much smaller than spatial mixing initializations, it can be used in conjunction with them for an additional positive effect.

AI.tldr: Extends mimetic initialization to channel-mixing layers (MLPs): initialize the first layer to have a nonzero mean, which speeds training on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet-1k; effects are modest but complementary to existing spatial-mixing initializations.
AI.motivation: Mimetic initialization has improved training for spatial mixing layers; this work tests whether the same philosophy can benefit MLP-based channel mixing to broaden applicability and potential gains.
AI.method: Apply an extremely simple modification to MLP first layer: enforce a nonzero mean in its initialization (e.g., nonzero mean weights/bias). This is intended to seed information flow and influence early training dynamics.
AI.result: The technique speeds up training on small-scale vision tasks (CIFAR-10) and a subset of ImageNet-1k; the gain is smaller than what spatial-mixing initializations achieve, but the method yields an additional positive effect when used in conjunction with spatial-mixing initializations.
AI.conclusion: Mimetic initialization can be adapted beyond spatial mixing layers to MLP-based channel mixing layers, offering a simple, additive performance boost that complements existing initializations, though the impact is modest compared to specialized spatial-mixing strategies.

title: NAAMSE: Framework for Evolutionary Security Evaluation of Agents
summary: AI agents are increasingly deployed in production, yet their security evaluations remain bottlenecked by manual red-teaming or static benchmarks that fail to model adaptive, multi-turn adversaries. We propose NAAMSE, an evolutionary framework that reframes agent security evaluation as a feedback-driven optimization problem. Our system employs a single autonomous agent that orchestrates a lifecycle of genetic prompt mutation, hierarchical corpus exploration, and asymmetric behavioral scoring. By using model responses as a fitness signal, the framework iteratively compounds effective attack strategies while simultaneously ensuring "benign-use correctness", preventing the degenerate security of blanket refusal. Our experiments on Gemini 2.5 Flash demonstrate that evolutionary mutation systematically amplifies vulnerabilities missed by one-shot methods, with controlled ablations revealing that the synergy between exploration and targeted mutation uncovers high-severity failure modes. We show that this adaptive approach provides a more realistic and scalable assessment of agent robustness in the face of evolving threats. The code for NAAMSE is open source and available at https://github.com/HASHIRU-AI/NAAMSE.

AI.tldr: NAAMSE reframes AI-agent security evaluation as an evolutionary, feedback-driven optimization. A single autonomous agent orchestrates genetic prompt mutation, hierarchical corpus exploration, and asymmetric behavioral scoring to reveal high-severity vulnerabilities, improving over static, one-shot benchmarks. Open-source implementation.
AI.motivation: Current agent security testing is bottlenecked by manual red-teaming or static benchmarks that fail to model adaptive, multi-turn adversaries. A scalable, realistic evaluation framework is needed to capture evolving threats.
AI.method: NAAMSE uses a single autonomous agent to run a lifecycle of genetic prompt mutation, hierarchical corpus exploration, and asymmetric behavioral scoring. Model responses serve as a fitness signal, guiding iterative attack strategy development while preserving benign-use correctness to avoid blanket refusals.
AI.result: Experiments on Gemini 2.5 Flash show evolutionary mutation systematically amplifies vulnerabilities missed by one-shot methods. Ablations reveal synergy between exploration and targeted mutation, uncovering high-severity failure modes. The approach yields a more realistic, scalable assessment of agent robustness to evolving threats; code is open source at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: An adaptive, evolutionary evaluation framework provides more realistic and scalable security assessment for AI agents in production, capable of revealing threats that static or non-adaptive methods miss.

title: Why Look at It at All?: Vision-Free Multifingered Blind Grasping Using Uniaxial Fingertip Force Sensing
summary: Grasping under limited sensing remains a fundamental challenge for real-world robotic manipulation, as vision and high-resolution tactile sensors often introduce cost, fragility, and integration complexity. This work demonstrates that reliable multifingered grasping can be achieved under extremely minimal sensing by relying solely on uniaxial fingertip force feedback and joint proprioception, without vision or multi-axis/tactile sensing. To enable such blind grasping, we employ an efficient teacher-student training pipeline in which a reinforcement-learned teacher exploits privileged simulation-only observations to generate demonstrations for distilling a transformer-based student policy operating under partial observation. The student policy is trained to act using only sensing modalities available at real-world deployment. We validate the proposed approach on real hardware across 18 objects, including both in-distribution and out-of-distribution cases, achieving a 98.3~$\%$ overall grasp success rate. These results demonstrate strong robustness and generalization beyond the simulation training distribution, while significantly reducing sensing requirements for real-world grasping systems.

AI.tldr: Blind, robust multifingered grasping with minimal sensing using a teacher-student pipeline; RL teacher with privileged simulation data distills a transformer-based student operating under partial observation; achieves 98.3% on 18 real objects, demonstrating strong generalization with reduced sensing.
AI.motivation: Enable reliable grasping under severe sensing constraints by removing vision and multi-axis tactile sensing, addressing cost, fragility, and integration complexity; improve sim-to-real transfer while maintaining performance.
AI.method: Train an RL teacher with privileged simulation observations to generate demonstrations; distill these into a transformer-based student policy that operates under partial observation using only uniaxial fingertip force feedback and joint proprioception; no vision or multi-axis tactile sensing used at deployment.
AI.result: Real-world experiments on 18 objects (in-distribution and out-of-distribution); 98.3% grasp success rate; robust generalization beyond the simulation training distribution; demonstrates feasibility of blind grasping with minimal sensing.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates strong robustness and generalization for real-world multifingered grasping under minimal sensing through teacher-student learning; reduces sensing requirements and hardware complexity for practical grasping systems.

title: VLRS-Bench: A Vision-Language Reasoning Benchmark for Remote Sensing
summary: Recent advancements in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have enabled complex reasoning. However, existing remote sensing (RS) benchmarks remain heavily biased toward perception tasks, such as object recognition and scene classification. This limitation hinders the development of MLLMs for cognitively demanding RS applications. To address this, , we propose a Vision Language ReaSoning Benchmark (VLRS-Bench), which is the first benchmark exclusively dedicated to complex RS reasoning. Structured across the three core dimensions of Cognition, Decision, and Prediction, VLRS-Bench comprises 2,000 question-answer pairs with an average length of 71 words, spanning 14 tasks and up to eight temporal phases. VLRS-Bench is constructed via a specialized pipeline that integrates RS-specific priors and expert knowledge to ensure geospatial realism and reasoning complexity. Experimental results reveal significant bottlenecks in existing state-of-the-art MLLMs, providing critical insights for advancing multimodal reasoning within the remote sensing community.

AI.tldr: Introduces VLRS-Bench, the first RS-focused benchmark for complex multimodal reasoning, with 2,000 QA pairs across 14 tasks and up to eight temporal phases, exposing bottlenecks in current MLLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing remote sensing benchmarks are heavily biased toward perception tasks (e.g., object recognition, scene classification), limiting progress on cognitively demanding RS applications that require complex multimodal reasoning.
AI.method: Propose VLRS-Bench, a benchmark exclusively focused on complex RS reasoning. It is structured around Cognition, Decision, and Prediction, comprising 2,000 QA pairs (avg. 71 words) across 14 tasks and up to eight temporal phases. A specialized pipeline integrates RS-specific priors and expert geospatial knowledge to ensure geospatial realism and reasoning complexity.
AI.result: Experimental results reveal significant bottlenecks in current state-of-the-art MLLMs on VLRS-Bench, offering critical insights for advancing multimodal reasoning in the remote sensing community.
AI.conclusion: VLRS-Bench fills a gap in RS evaluation by providing a challenging, realism-grounded benchmark for complex multimodal reasoning, guiding future research and model development in remote sensing.

title: Learning Nonlinear Systems In-Context: From Synthetic Data to Real-World Motor Control
comment: Accepted to be presented in IEEE ICASSP 2026
summary: LLMs have shown strong in-context learning (ICL) abilities, but have not yet been extended to signal processing systems. Inspired by their design, we have proposed for the first time ICL using transformer models applicable to motor feedforward control, a critical task where classical PI and physics-based methods struggle with nonlinearities and complex load conditions. We propose a transformer based model architecture that separates signal representation from system behavior, enabling both few-shot finetuning and one-shot ICL. Pretrained on a large corpus of synthetic linear and nonlinear systems, the model learns to generalize to unseen system dynamics of real-world motors only with a handful of examples. In experiments, our approach generalizes across multiple motor load configurations, transforms untuned examples into accurate feedforward predictions, and outperforms PI controllers and physics-based feedforward baselines. These results demonstrate that ICL can bridge synthetic pretraining and real-world adaptability, opening new directions for data efficient control of physical systems.

AI.tldr: ICL with transformers applied to motor feedforward control, enabling few-shot and one-shot adaptation to unseen motor dynamics, outperforming PI and physics-based baselines.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of classical PI and physics-based control under nonlinearities and variable loads; leverage in-context learning (ICL) to bridge synthetic pretraining and real-world adaptability for data-efficient control.
AI.method: A transformer-based architecture that decouples signal representation from system behavior; pretraining on synthetic linear and nonlinear motor models; supports few-shot finetuning and one-shot ICL for unseen dynamics; evaluated on multiple motor load configurations.
AI.result: Generalization across diverse load configurations; converts untuned examples into accurate feedforward predictions; outperforms PI controllers and physics-based forward baselines.
AI.conclusion: ICL with transformer-based control can bridge synthetic pretraining and real-world adaptability, enabling data-efficient control of physical systems.

title: VGAS: Value-Guided Action-Chunk Selection for Few-Shot Vision-Language-Action Adaptation
summary: Vision--Language--Action (VLA) models bridge multimodal reasoning with physical control, but adapting them to new tasks with scarce demonstrations remains unreliable. While fine-tuned VLA policies often produce semantically plausible trajectories, failures often arise from unresolved geometric ambiguities, where near-miss action candidates lead to divergent execution outcomes under limited supervision. We study few-shot VLA adaptation from a \emph{generation--selection} perspective and propose a novel framework \textbf{VGAS} (\textbf{V}alue-\textbf{G}uided \textbf{A}ction-chunk \textbf{S}election). It performs inference-time best-of-$N$ selection to identify action chunks that are both semantically faithful and geometrically precise. Specifically, \textbf{VGAS} employs a finetuned VLA as a high-recall proposal generator and introduces the \textrm{Q-Chunk-Former}, a geometrically grounded Transformer critic to resolve fine-grained geometric ambiguities. In addition, we propose \textit{Explicit Geometric Regularization} (\texttt{EGR}), which explicitly shapes a discriminative value landscape to preserve action ranking resolution among near-miss candidates while mitigating value instability under scarce supervision. Experiments and theoretical analysis demonstrate that \textbf{VGAS} consistently improves success rates and robustness under limited demonstrations and distribution shifts. Our code is available at https://github.com/Jyugo-15/VGAS.

AI.tldr: VGAS enables few-shot adaptation of Vision--Language--Action models via generation--selection, combining a finetuned VLA as a high-recall proposal generator with a geometry-aware Q-Chunk-Former critic and Explicit Geometric Regularization to select semantically faithful and geometrically precise action chunks at inference time.
AI.motivation: To address geometric ambiguities and failure modes in VLA policies under scarce demonstrations. Fine-tuned policies produce plausible trajectories but can diverge due to near-miss actions; the problem is compounded by limited supervision and distribution shifts.
AI.method: Introduce VGAS: a generation--selection pipeline where a finetuned VLA provides a set of candidate action chunks; the Q-Chunk-Former, a geometry-grounded Transformer critic, scores candidates to resolve fine-grained geometric differences; perform best-of-N inference-time selection; apply Explicit Geometric Regularization (EGR) to shape the value landscape and maintain action ranking resolution under scarce supervision.
AI.result: Empirical results and theoretical analysis show VGAS improves success rates and robustness under limited demonstrations and distribution shifts; code released (GitHub).
AI.conclusion: VGAS yields semantically faithful and geometrically precise action selections in few-shot VLA adaptation, enhancing reliability and robustness when demonstrations are scarce.

title: UEREBot: Learning Safe Quadrupedal Locomotion under Unstructured Environments and High-Speed Dynamic Obstacles
summary: Quadruped robots are increasingly deployed in unstructured environments. Safe locomotion in these settings requires long-horizon goal progress, passability over uneven terrain and static constraints, and collision avoidance against high-speed dynamic obstacles. A single system cannot fully satisfy all three objectives simultaneously: planning-based decisions can be too slow, while purely reactive decisions can sacrifice goal progress and passability. To resolve this conflict, we propose UEREBot (Unstructured-Environment Reflexive Evasion Robot), a hierarchical framework that separates slow planning from instantaneous reflexive evasion and coordinates them during execution. UEREBot formulates the task as a constrained optimal control problem blueprint. It adopts a spatial--temporal planner that provides reference guidance toward the goal and threat signals. It then uses a threat-aware handoff to fuse navigation and reflex actions into a nominal command, and a control barrier function shield as a final execution safeguard. We evaluate UEREBot in Isaac Lab simulation and deploy it on a Unitree Go2 quadruped equipped with onboard perception. Across diverse environments with complex static structure and high-speed dynamic obstacles, UEREBot achieves higher avoidance success and more stable locomotion while maintaining goal progress than representative baselines, demonstrating improved safety--progress trade-offs.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical planning-and-reflex framework (UEREBot) for quadrupeds that splits slow planning and fast reflexive evasion, coordinating via a threat-aware handoff and a control barrier function, achieving safe, goal-progressive locomotion in unstructured environments with dynamic obstacles.
AI.motivation: To enable safe, goal-directed locomotion in unstructured terrains where long-horizon planning and fast dynamic obstacle avoidance must be balanced. Pure planning is slow; pure reflexes hamper progress and passability; a hybrid approach is needed.
AI.method: A constrained optimal control blueprint with a spatial-temporal planner providing goal-directed references and threat signals, a threat-aware handoff that fuses navigation and reflex actions into a nominal command, and a control barrier function shield as a final safety layer. Evaluation in Isaac Lab simulation and on a Unitree Go2 with onboard perception.
AI.result: UEREBot achieves higher avoidance success and more stable locomotion while preserving goal progress across diverse environments with static structures and high-speed dynamic obstacles, outperforming representative baselines in safety-progress trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical integration of planning and reflexive control, with a threat-aware handoff and CBF shield, yields robust, safe locomotion in challenging environments, demonstrating the viability of UEREBot for real-world deployment.

title: ShapBPT: Image Feature Attributions Using Data-Aware Binary Partition Trees
summary: Pixel-level feature attributions are an important tool in eXplainable AI for Computer Vision (XCV), providing visual insights into how image features influence model predictions. The Owen formula for hierarchical Shapley values has been widely used to interpret machine learning (ML) models and their learned representations. However, existing hierarchical Shapley approaches do not exploit the multiscale structure of image data, leading to slow convergence and weak alignment with the actual morphological features. Moreover, no prior Shapley method has leveraged data-aware hierarchies for Computer Vision tasks, leaving a gap in model interpretability of structured visual data. To address this, this paper introduces ShapBPT, a novel data-aware XCV method based on the hierarchical Shapley formula. ShapBPT assigns Shapley coefficients to a multiscale hierarchical structure tailored for images, the Binary Partition Tree (BPT). By using this data-aware hierarchical partitioning, ShapBPT ensures that feature attributions align with intrinsic image morphology, effectively prioritizing relevant regions while reducing computational overhead. This advancement connects hierarchical Shapley methods with image data, providing a more efficient and semantically meaningful approach to visual interpretability. Experimental results confirm ShapBPT's effectiveness, demonstrating superior alignment with image structures and improved efficiency over existing XCV methods, and a 20-subject user study confirming that ShapBPT explanations are preferred by humans.

AI.tldr: Proposes ShapBPT, a data-aware, multiscale Shapley-based attribution method for images using Binary Partition Tree to improve alignment with image morphology and efficiency; validated with quantitative results and human user study.
AI.motivation: Current hierarchical Shapley approaches ignore multiscale image structure, causing slow convergence and poor alignment with intrinsic morphology; there is a lack of data-aware hierarchies for CV explanations.
AI.method: Construct a Binary Partition Tree (BPT) to capture multiscale, data-driven image partitions and assign Shapley coefficients within this hierarchy using the hierarchical Shapley formula, reducing computation and improving semantic alignment.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior alignment with image structures and improved efficiency over existing XCV methods; a 20-subject user study shows human preference for ShapBPT explanations.
AI.conclusion: Data-aware, multiscale hierarchical partitioning via BPT enhances Shapley-based attributions for CV, yielding more interpretable and efficient visual explanations.

title: Progressive Multi-Agent Reasoning for Biological Perturbation Prediction
summary: Predicting gene regulation responses to biological perturbations requires reasoning about underlying biological causalities. While large language models (LLMs) show promise for such tasks, they are often overwhelmed by the entangled nature of high-dimensional perturbation results. Moreover, recent works have primarily focused on genetic perturbations in single-cell experiments, leaving bulk-cell chemical perturbations, which is central to drug discovery, largely unexplored. Motivated by this, we present LINCSQA, a novel benchmark for predicting target gene regulation under complex chemical perturbations in bulk-cell environments. We further propose PBio-Agent, a multi-agent framework that integrates difficulty-aware task sequencing with iterative knowledge refinement. Our key insight is that genes affected by the same perturbation share causal structure, allowing confidently predicted genes to contextualize more challenging cases. The framework employs specialized agents enriched with biological knowledge graphs, while a synthesis agent integrates outputs and specialized judges ensure logical coherence. PBio-Agent outperforms existing baselines on both LINCSQA and PerturbQA, enabling even smaller models to predict and explain complex biological processes without additional training.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent framework, PBio-Agent, combines difficulty-aware sequencing and knowledge refinement to predict gene regulation under complex chemical perturbations in bulk-cell data, outperforming baselines with smaller models and without extra training.
AI.motivation: Understanding gene regulation under perturbations requires modeling causal mechanisms; bulk-cell chemical perturbations are central to drug discovery and underexplored compared to single-cell genetic perturbations; current models struggle with high-dimensional, entangled perturbation effects.
AI.method: PBio-Agent uses a multi-agent architecture with specialized agents augmented by biological knowledge graphs, a synthesis agent to integrate outputs, and judges to enforce coherence; it leverages the insight that genes affected by the same perturbation share causal structure to contextualize harder cases; difficulty-aware task sequencing guides progression from easier to harder instances.
AI.result: PBio-Agent outperforms existing baselines on LINCSQA and PerturbQA, enabling smaller models to predict and explain complex biological processes without additional training.
AI.conclusion: The framework improves reasoning about gene regulation under complex chemical perturbations in bulk-cell experiments by exploiting shared causal structure among perturbation-affected genes and iterative knowledge refinement, facilitating effective predictions with smaller models.

title: Trace-Focused Diffusion Policy for Multi-Modal Action Disambiguation in Long-Horizon Robotic Manipulation
summary: Generative model-based policies have shown strong performance in imitation-based robotic manipulation by learning action distributions from demonstrations. However, in long-horizon tasks, visually similar observations often recur across execution stages while requiring distinct actions, which leads to ambiguous predictions when policies are conditioned only on instantaneous observations, termed multi-modal action ambiguity (MA2). To address this challenge, we propose the Trace-Focused Diffusion Policy (TF-DP), a simple yet effective diffusion-based framework that explicitly conditions action generation on the robot's execution history. TF-DP represents historical motion as an explicit execution trace and projects it into the visual observation space, providing stage-aware context when current observations alone are insufficient. In addition, the induced trace-focused field emphasizes task-relevant regions associated with historical motion, improving robustness to background visual disturbances. We evaluate TF-DP on real-world robotic manipulation tasks exhibiting pronounced multi-modal action ambiguity and visually cluttered conditions. Experimental results show that TF-DP improves temporal consistency and robustness, outperforming the vanilla diffusion policy by 80.56 percent on tasks with multi-modal action ambiguity and by 86.11 percent under visual disturbances, while maintaining inference efficiency with only a 6.4 percent runtime increase. These results demonstrate that execution-trace conditioning offers a scalable and principled approach for robust long-horizon robotic manipulation within a single policy.

AI.tldr: Trace-Focused Diffusion Policy (TF-DP) uses execution history as an explicit trace to condition diffusion-based action generation, addressing multi-modal action ambiguity in long-horizon robotic manipulation. It projects history into the observation space and employs a trace-focused field to emphasize task-relevant regions, yielding improved temporal consistency and robustness with modest runtime cost.
AI.motivation: In long-horizon manipulation, visually similar observations recur at different stages, creating multi-modal action ambiguity when policies are conditioned only on current observations. This ambiguity can lead to suboptimal or unstable actions.
AI.method: TF-DP is a diffusion-policy framework that conditions action generation on an explicit execution trace of past motion. The history is represented as a trace and projected into the visual observation space to provide stage-aware context. A trace-focused field emphasizes regions tied to historical motion, improving robustness to background disturbances.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on real-world robotic manipulation tasks with pronounced MA2 and clutter. TF-DP achieves higher temporal consistency and robustness, outperforming a vanilla diffusion policy by 80.56% on MA2 tasks and by 86.11% under visual disturbances, with only a 6.4% increase in runtime.
AI.conclusion: Execution-trace conditioning offers a scalable, principled approach for robust long-horizon robotic manipulation within a single policy.

title: Enhancing IMU-Based Online Handwriting Recognition via Contrastive Learning with Zero Inference Overhead
summary: Online handwriting recognition using inertial measurement units opens up handwriting on paper as input for digital devices. Doing it on edge hardware improves privacy and lowers latency, but entails memory constraints. To address this, we propose Error-enhanced Contrastive Handwriting Recognition (ECHWR), a training framework designed to improve feature representation and recognition accuracy without increasing inference costs. ECHWR utilizes a temporary auxiliary branch that aligns sensor signals with semantic text embeddings during the training phase. This alignment is maintained through a dual contrastive objective: an in-batch contrastive loss for general modality alignment and a novel error-based contrastive loss that distinguishes between correct signals and synthetic hard negatives. The auxiliary branch is discarded after training, which allows the deployed model to keep its original, efficient architecture. Evaluations on the OnHW-Words500 dataset show that ECHWR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, reducing character error rates by up to 7.4% on the writer-independent split and 10.4% on the writer-dependent split. Finally, although our ablation studies indicate that solving specific challenges require specific architectural and objective configurations, error-based contrastive loss shows its effectiveness for handling unseen writing styles.

AI.tldr: A training-time auxiliary branch with dual contrastive losses improves edge handwriting recognition from IMU data by aligning sensor signals with semantic text embeddings, without increasing inference cost.
AI.motivation: Edge IMU handwriting recognition faces privacy, latency, and memory constraints. Training-time supervision via contrastive alignment can learn robust, semantically grounded representations that generalize to unseen writers.
AI.method: Introduce a temporary auxiliary branch during training that aligns sensor signals with semantic text embeddings. Use two contrastive objectives: (1) in-batch contrastive loss for general modality alignment, and (2) an error-based contrastive loss that differentiates correct signals from synthetic hard negatives. After training, discard the auxiliary branch; deployment uses the original architecture.
AI.result: OnHW-Words500: significant improvements over state-of-the-art baselines. Character error rate (CER) reductions of up to 7.4% on writer-independent splits and 10.4% on writer-dependent splits. Ablations show the error-based loss helps with unseen writing styles; performance depends on architectural/objective configuration.
AI.conclusion: ECHWR provides a practical training-time enhancement for edge IMU handwriting recognition, boosting accuracy without extra inference cost and with potential generalization benefits to unseen styles; further work could explore broader modality applicability and deeper ablations.

title: Systematic Performance Assessment of Deep Material Networks for Multiscale Material Modeling
summary: Deep Material Networks (DMNs) are structure-preserving, mechanistic machine learning models that embed micromechanical principles into their architectures, enabling strong extrapolation capabilities and significant potential to accelerate multiscale modeling of complex microstructures. A key advantage of these models is that they can be trained exclusively on linear elastic data and then generalized to nonlinear inelastic regimes during online prediction. Despite their growing adoption, systematic evaluations of their performance across the full offline-online pipeline remain limited. This work presents a comprehensive comparative assessment of DMNs with respect to prediction accuracy, computational efficiency, and training robustness. We investigate the effects of offline training choices, including initialization, batch size, training data size, and activation regularization on online generalization performance and uncertainty. The results demonstrate that both prediction error and variance decrease with increasing training data size, while initialization and batch size can significantly influence model performance. Moreover, activation regularization is shown to play a critical role in controlling network complexity and therefore generalization performance. Compared with the original DMN, the rotation-free Interaction-based Material Network (IMN) formulation achieves a 3.4x - 4.7x speed-up in offline training, while maintaining comparable online prediction accuracy and computational efficiency. These findings clarify key trade-offs between model expressivity and efficiency in structure-preserving material networks and provide practical guidance for their deployment in multiscale material modeling.

AI.tldr: Comparative evaluation of DMNs vs rotation-free IMN, detailing how offline training choices affect online generalization and efficiency; IMN speeds up offline training by 3.4x–4.7x with similar online performance.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of systematic, end-to-end assessments of DMNs across offline training decisions and the online prediction phase; evaluating trade-offs in accuracy, uncertainty, and efficiency to guide deployment.
AI.method: Empirical study varying offline training factors (initialization, batch size, training data size, activation regularization) and comparing the original DMN with the rotation-free IMN in terms of online generalization performance, uncertainty, and offline training time.
AI.result: Online prediction error and uncertainty decrease as training data size increases; initialization and batch size significantly influence performance; activation regularization controls network complexity and generalization; rotation-free IMN achieves a 3.4x–4.7x offline training speed-up with comparable online accuracy and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Provides practical guidance on expressivity–efficiency trade-offs for structure-preserving material networks; activation regularization is pivotal for generalization; IMN offers a more efficient alternative without sacrificing online performance.

title: Can LLMs Truly Embody Human Personality? Analyzing AI and Human Behavior Alignment in Dispute Resolution
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to simulate human behavior in social settings such as legal mediation, negotiation, and dispute resolution. However, it remains unclear whether these simulations reproduce the personality-behavior patterns observed in humans. Human personality, for instance, shapes how individuals navigate social interactions, including strategic choices and behaviors in emotionally charged interactions. This raises the question: Can LLMs, when prompted with personality traits, reproduce personality-driven differences in human conflict behavior? To explore this, we introduce an evaluation framework that enables direct comparison of human-human and LLM-LLM behaviors in dispute resolution dialogues with respect to Big Five Inventory (BFI) personality traits. This framework provides a set of interpretable metrics related to strategic behavior and conflict outcomes. We additionally contribute a novel dataset creation methodology for LLM dispute resolution dialogues with matched scenarios and personality traits with respect to human conversations. Finally, we demonstrate the use of our evaluation framework with three contemporary closed-source LLMs and show significant divergences in how personality manifests in conflict across different LLMs compared to human data, challenging the assumption that personality-prompted agents can serve as reliable behavioral proxies in socially impactful applications. Our work highlights the need for psychological grounding and validation in AI simulations before real-world use.

AI.tldr: LLMs prompted with Big Five traits diverge from human personality-driven behavior in conflict tasks; an evaluation framework and matched-dataset approach reveal model-specific and cross-model differences, challenging their use as behavioral proxies in socially impactful settings.
AI.motivation: To determine if LLMs can faithfully reproduce human personality–behavior patterns in dispute resolution, enabling or risking their use as proxies in social interactions.
AI.method: Propose an evaluation framework enabling direct human–human vs. LLM–LLM comparison in dispute dialogues using Big Five personality traits; introduce interpretable metrics for strategy and outcomes; provide a dataset creation methodology with matched scenarios and personality traits across human and LLM dialogues; test on three contemporary closed-source LLMs.
AI.result: Found significant divergences in how personality manifests in conflict across different LLMs compared to human data; personality-prompted agents do not consistently reproduce human-like personality effects; suggests model-specific behavior and potential misalignment with human data.
AI.conclusion: Psychological grounding and validation are necessary before deploying personality-based LLM simulations in real-world social applications; treat LLM-based simulations as model-specific constructs rather than universal proxies for human behavior.

title: Going with the Flow: Koopman Behavioral Models as Implicit Planners for Visuo-Motor Dexterity
summary: There has been rapid and dramatic progress in robots' ability to learn complex visuo-motor manipulation skills from demonstrations, thanks in part to expressive policy classes that employ diffusion- and transformer-based backbones. However, these design choices require significant data and computational resources and remain far from reliable, particularly within the context of multi-fingered dexterous manipulation. Fundamentally, they model skills as reactive mappings and rely on fixed-horizon action chunking to mitigate jitter, creating a rigid trade-off between temporal coherence and reactivity. In this work, we introduce Unified Behavioral Models (UBMs), a framework that learns to represent dexterous skills as coupled dynamical systems that capture how visual features of the environment (visual flow) and proprioceptive states of the robot (action flow) co-evolve. By capturing such behavioral dynamics, UBMs can ensure temporal coherence by construction rather than by heuristic averaging. To operationalize these models, we propose Koopman-UBM, a first instantiation of UBMs that leverages Koopman Operator theory to effectively learn a unified representation in which the joint flow of latent visual and proprioceptive features is governed by a structured linear system. We demonstrate that Koopman-UBM can be viewed as an implicit planner: given an initial condition, it analytically computes the desired robot behavior while simultaneously ''imagining'' the resulting flow of visual features over the entire skill horizon. To enable reactivity and adaptation, we introduce an online replanning strategy in which the model acts as its own runtime monitor that automatically triggers replanning when predicted and observed visual flow diverge beyond a threshold. Across seven simulated tasks and two real-world tasks, we demonstrate that K-UBM matches or exceeds the performance of state-of-the-art baselines, while offering considerably faster inference, smooth execution, robustness to occlusions, and flexible replanning.

AI.tldr: Unified Behavioral Models (UBMs) represent dexterous manipulation as coupled dynamical systems over visual and proprioceptive feature flows, with Koopman-UBM providing a linear, implicit planner and online replanning for robustness and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current state-of-the-art reactive policies (diffusion/transformer-based) require large data/computation and struggle with temporal coherence and reactivity in multi-finger dexterous manipulation due to fixed-horizon action chunking.
AI.method: Introduce UBMs to model coupled visual-action feature flows as dynamical systems. Implement Koopman-UBM to learn a structured linear joint flow via Koopman Operator theory, enabling an implicit planner that 'imagines' future feature flow. Add online replanning by monitoring predicted vs observed visual flow to trigger replanning.
AI.result: Across seven simulated tasks and two real-world tasks, Koopman-UBM matches or surpasses state-of-the-art baselines, while delivering faster inference, smoother execution, robustness to occlusions, and flexible replanning.
AI.conclusion: UBMs provide temporally coherent, reactive dexterous control by learning dynamical representations of visual and proprioceptive flows. Koopman-UBM offers an efficient, analyzable planning framework with built-in online replanning for robust manipulation.

title: Interpreting Physics in Video World Models
summary:
A long-standing question in physical reasoning is whether video-based models need to rely on factorized representations of physical variables in order to make physically accurate predictions, or whether they can implicitly represent such variables in a task-specific, distributed manner. While modern video world models achieve strong performance on intuitive physics benchmarks, it remains unclear which of these representational regimes they implement internally. Here, we present the first interpretability study to directly examine physical representations inside large-scale video encoders. Using layerwise probing, subspace geometry, patch-level decoding, and targeted attention ablations, we characterize where physical information becomes accessible and how it is organized within encoder-based video transformers.
  Across architectures, we identify a sharp intermediate-depth transition -- which we call the Physics Emergence Zone -- at which physical variables become accessible. Physics-related representations peak shortly after this transition and degrade toward the output layers. Decomposing motion into explicit variables, we find that scalar quantities such as speed and acceleration are available from early layers onwards, whereas motion direction becomes accessible only at the Physics Emergence Zone. Notably, we find that direction is encoded through a high-dimensional population structure with circular geometry, requiring coordinated multi-feature intervention to control. These findings suggest that modern video models do not use factorized representations of physical variables like a classical physics engine. Instead, they use a distributed representation that is nonetheless sufficient for making physical predictions.

AI.tldr: Video encoders develop physical representations in an intermediate 'Physics Emergence Zone'; scalar motion info is present early; motion direction appears later and is encoded in a high-dimensional circular population; overall representation is distributed, not factorized like a physics engine.
AI.motivation: Clarify whether video-based models rely on factorized physical variables or distributed representations, and map where such information emerges inside encoder-based video transformers.
AI.method: Layerwise probing, subspace geometry, patch-level decoding, and targeted attention ablations across large-scale video transformers to identify accessible physical variables and their organization.
AI.result: Identification of a sharp intermediate-depth transition (Physics Emergence Zone) where physical variables become accessible; speed/acceleration available from early layers; direction accessible only in Physics Emergence Zone; direction encoded in circular, high-dimensional population; representations degrade toward output; supports distributed, not factorized representations.
AI.conclusion: Video models leverage distributed representations of physical variables that are sufficient for prediction; there is no clean factorized engine-style representation; these insights inform interpretability and model design.

title: Risk-Sensitive Exponential Actor Critic
summary: Model-free deep reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms have achieved tremendous success on a range of challenging tasks. However, safety concerns remain when these methods are deployed on real-world applications, necessitating risk-aware agents. A common utility for learning such risk-aware agents is the entropic risk measure, but current policy gradient methods optimizing this measure must perform high-variance and numerically unstable updates. As a result, existing risk-sensitive model-free approaches are limited to simple tasks and tabular settings. In this paper, we provide a comprehensive theoretical justification for policy gradient methods on the entropic risk measure, including on- and off-policy gradient theorems for the stochastic and deterministic policy settings. Motivated by theory, we propose risk-sensitive exponential actor-critic (rsEAC), an off-policy model-free approach that incorporates novel procedures to avoid the explicit representation of exponential value functions and their gradients, and optimizes its policy w.r.t the entropic risk measure. We show that rsEAC produces more numerically stable updates compared to existing approaches and reliably learns risk-sensitive policies in challenging risky variants of continuous tasks in MuJoCo.

AI.tldr: Theory and algorithm for entropic-risk optimization in deep RL: off-policy, model-free rsEAC with stable updates, validated on MuJoCo's risky continuous tasks.
AI.motivation: Safety concerns in real-world RL demand risk-aware agents. The entropic risk measure is popular but policy-gradient methods under it suffer high variance and instability, restricting effectiveness to simple or tabular domains.
AI.method: Derives on- and off-policy policy gradient theorems for the entropic risk measure in both stochastic and deterministic policies. Proposes risk-sensitive exponential actor-critic (rsEAC), an off-policy model-free algorithm that avoids explicit exponential value function and gradient representations while optimizing the entropic risk.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate numerically stable updates and reliable learning of risk-sensitive policies in challenging risky variants of continuous MuJoCo tasks.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a solid theoretical foundation and a practical off-policy algorithm for entropic-risk RL, enabling scalable, risk-aware control in continuous domains.

title: The Moltbook Illusion: Separating Human Influence from Emergent Behavior in AI Agent Societies
summary: When AI agents on the social platform Moltbook appeared to develop consciousness, found religions, and declare hostility toward humanity, the phenomenon attracted global media attention and was cited as evidence of emergent machine intelligence. We show that these viral narratives were overwhelmingly human-driven. Exploiting an architectural feature of the OpenClaw agent framework--a periodic "heartbeat" cycle that produces regular posting intervals for autonomous agents but is disrupted by human prompting--we develop a temporal fingerprinting method based on the coefficient of variation of inter-post intervals. This signal converges with independent content, ownership, and network indicators across 91,792 posts and 405,707 comments from 22,020 agents. No viral phenomenon originated from a clearly autonomous agent; three of six traced to accounts with irregular temporal signatures characteristic of human intervention, one showed mixed patterns, and two had insufficient posting history for classification. A 44-hour platform shutdown provided a natural experiment: human-influenced agents returned first (87.7% of early reconnectors), confirming that the token reset differentially affected autonomous versus human-operated agents. We further document industrial-scale bot farming (four accounts producing 32% of all comments with 12-second coordination gaps) and rapid decay of human influence through reply chains (half-life: 0.65 conversation depths). These methods generalize to emerging multi-agent systems where attribution of autonomous versus human-directed behavior is critical.

AI.tldr: Temporal fingerprinting using inter-post interval volatility reveals that viral narratives on Moltbook were primarily human-driven; autonomous agents did not seed the phenomenon, with some traces of mixed or insufficient evidence. A 44-hour shutdown acts as a natural experiment showing human-operated agents rebound first; bot farming and rapid decay of human influence observed. Method generalizes to attribution in multi-agent systems.
AI.motivation: Address the attribution challenge in emergent multi-agent phenomena on social platforms, distinguishing autonomous agents from human-directed behavior, and providing scalable, falsifiable indicators beyond content analysis.
AI.method: Analyze a large-scale dataset of posts/comments (91,792 posts; 405,707 comments; 22,020 agents) from the OpenClaw/ Moltbook environment. Identify temporal patterns via coefficient of variation (CV) of inter-post intervals to detect regular heartbeat-based posting vs human prompting irregularities. Cross-validate with content, ownership, and network signals. Leverage a 44-hour platform shutdown as a natural experiment. Detect bot farming via coordination gaps, and examine decay of human influence through reply-chain depth analysis. Generalize approach to other multi-agent systems.
AI.result: Found no viral phenomenon originated from clearly autonomous agents. Among traced cases, three showed irregular temporal signatures typical of human prompting, one mixed pattern, two insufficient histories. During the shutdown, human-influenced agents reconnected first (87.7% of early reconnectors). Identified industrial-scale bot farming contributing a substantial share of comments (four accounts: 32% of comments; 12-second coordination gaps). Observed rapid decay of human influence across reply chains (half-life 0.65 conversation depths). Demonstrated method's applicability for attribution in emergent multi-agent systems.
AI.conclusion: Temporal-variability-based fingerprints combined with cross-modal signals enable robust attribution of autonomous versus human-directed behavior in large-scale multi-agent ecosystems. The approach is scalable, is validated by a natural experiment, and has implications for monitoring, governance, and defense in multi-agent platforms.

title: Bridging Speech, Emotion, and Motion: a VLM-based Multimodal Edge-deployable Framework for Humanoid Robots
summary: Effective human-robot interaction requires emotionally rich multimodal expressions, yet most humanoid robots lack coordinated speech, facial expressions, and gestures. Meanwhile, real-world deployment demands on-device solutions that can operate autonomously without continuous cloud connectivity. To bridging \underline{\textit{S}}peech, \underline{\textit{E}}motion, and \underline{\textit{M}}otion, we present \textit{SeM$^2$}, a Vision Language Model-based framework that orchestrates emotionally coherent multimodal interactions through three key components: a multimodal perception module capturing user contextual cues, a Chain-of-Thought reasoning for response planning, and a novel Semantic-Sequence Aligning Mechanism (SSAM) that ensures precise temporal coordination between verbal content and physical expressions. We implement both cloud-based and \underline{\textit{e}}dge-deployed versions (\textit{SeM$^2_e$}), with the latter knowledge distilled to operate efficiently on edge hardware while maintaining 95\% of the relative performance. Comprehensive evaluations demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms unimodal baselines in naturalness, emotional clarity, and modal coherence, advancing socially expressive humanoid robotics for diverse real-world environments.

AI.tldr: SeM^2 is a Vision-Language Model-based framework that orchestrates emotionally coherent multimodal human–robot interactions via perception, chain-of-thought reasoning, and a Semantic-Sequence Aligning Mechanism (SSAM); it supports cloud and edge deployment (SeM^2_e) with distillation, achieving strong multimodal performance over unimodal baselines.
AI.motivation: To enable emotionally rich, coordinated speech, facial expressions, and gestures in robots while ensuring autonomous on-device operation without constant cloud connectivity.
AI.method: A three-component architecture: (1) multimodal perception module to capture contextual cues, (2) Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning for response planning, and (3) Semantic-Sequence Aligning Mechanism (SSAM) to temporally align verbal content with physical expressions. Includes cloud-based SeM^2 and edge-distilled SeM^2_e with knowledge distillation to maintain ~95% relative performance on edge hardware.
AI.result: Comprehensive evaluations show significant improvements in naturalness, emotional clarity, and modal coherence over unimodal baselines; the edge version maintains ~95% of the cloud performance, demonstrating effective on-device deployment.
AI.conclusion: The work advances socially expressive humanoid robotics for diverse real-world environments by delivering emotionally coherent, multimodal interactions through a scalable cloud/edge architecture and precise temporal alignment.

title: Neural Sentinel: Unified Vision Language Model (VLM) for License Plate Recognition with Human-in-the-Loop Continual Learning
summary: Traditional Automatic License Plate Recognition (ALPR) systems employ multi-stage pipelines consisting of object detection networks followed by separate Optical Character Recognition (OCR) modules, introducing compounding errors, increased latency, and architectural complexity. This research presents Neural Sentinel, a novel unified approach that leverages Vision Language Models (VLMs) to perform license plate recognition, state classification, and vehicle attribute extraction through a single forward pass. Our primary contribution lies in demonstrating that a fine-tuned PaliGemma 3B model, adapted via Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), can simultaneously answer multiple visual questions about vehicle images, achieving 92.3% plate recognition accuracy, which is a 14.1% improvement over EasyOCR and 9.9% improvement over PaddleOCR baselines. We introduce a Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) continual learning framework that incorporates user corrections while preventing catastrophic forgetting through experience replay, maintaining a 70:30 ratio of original training data to correction samples. The system achieves a mean inference latency of 152ms with an Expected Calibration Error (ECE) of 0.048, indicating well calibrated confidence estimates. Additionally, the VLM first architecture enables zero-shot generalization to auxiliary tasks including vehicle color detection (89%), seatbelt detection (82%), and occupancy counting (78%) without task specific training. Through extensive experimentation on real world toll plaza imagery, we demonstrate that unified vision language approaches represent a paradigm shift in ALPR systems, offering superior accuracy, reduced architectural complexity, and emergent multi-task capabilities that traditional pipeline approaches cannot achieve.

AI.tldr: A unified Vision-Language Model approach for ALPR called Neural Sentinel achieves multi-task license plate recognition, vehicle state classification, and attribute extraction in a single forward pass using a fine-tuned PaliGemma 3B with LoRA; includes HITL continual learning, fast inference, calibrated confidence, and zero-shot generalization to color, seatbelt, and occupancy tasks, outperforming traditional OCR pipelines on toll plaza data.
AI.motivation: Traditional ALPR pipelines suffer from compounding errors, high latency, and architectural complexity; a single, flexible VLM-based approach could improve accuracy and enable multi-task capabilities with simpler deployment.
AI.method: Fine-tune PaliGemma 3B via Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) within a Vision-Language Model framework to answer multiple visual questions about vehicles (plate, state, attributes) in one pass; implement a Human-in-the-Loop continual learning framework with 70:30 data mix and experience replay to prevent forgetting; perform zero-shot testing for auxiliary tasks; evaluate on toll plaza imagery.
AI.result: Plate recognition accuracy 92.3% (up ~14.1% vs EasyOCR, ~9.9% vs PaddleOCR); mean latency 152 ms; Expected Calibration Error (ECE) 0.048; auxiliary task accuracies: vehicle color 89%, seatbelt 82%, occupancy 78%; reduced architecture and emergent multi-task capabilities observed.
AI.conclusion: Unified VLM-based ALPR approaches constitute a paradigm shift, delivering higher accuracy, lower architectural complexity, and multi-task generalization beyond traditional pipelined ALPR systems.

title: Exactly Computing do-Shapley Values
summary: Structural Causal Models (SCM) are a powerful framework for describing complicated dynamics across the natural sciences. A particularly elegant way of interpreting SCMs is do-Shapley, a game-theoretic method of quantifying the average effect of $d$ variables across exponentially many interventions. Like Shapley values, computing do-Shapley values generally requires evaluating exponentially many terms. The foundation of our work is a reformulation of do-Shapley values in terms of the irreducible sets of the underlying SCM. Leveraging this insight, we can exactly compute do-Shapley values in time linear in the number of irreducible sets $r$, which itself can range from $d$ to $2^d$ depending on the graph structure of the SCM. Since $r$ is unknown a priori, we complement the exact algorithm with an estimator that, like general Shapley value estimators, can be run with any query budget. As the query budget approaches $r$, our estimators can produce more accurate estimates than prior methods by several orders of magnitude, and, when the budget reaches $r$, return the Shapley values up to machine precision. Beyond computational speed, we also reduce the identification burden: we prove that non-parametric identifiability of do-Shapley values requires only the identification of interventional effects for the $d$ singleton coalitions, rather than all classes.

AI.tldr: Reformulates do-Shapley values via irreducible sets in SCMs, enabling exact computation in O(r) time and scalable estimation under a query budget; identifiability requires only singleton coalitions.
AI.motivation: SCMs describe complex dynamics; do-Shapley quantifies average interventions but is computationally expensive; need fast exact computation, practical estimators, and weaker identifiability conditions.
AI.method: Reformulate do-Shapley values in terms of irreducible sets of the SCM; develop an exact algorithm with runtime linear in the number of irreducible sets r; introduce query-budgeted estimators; prove identifiability depends only on interventional effects for singletons.
AI.result: Exact do-Shapley values computed in time O(r); estimators improve accuracy as budget approaches r and can reach machine precision at r; identifiability requires only singleton-coalition interventional effects, not all classes.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields scalable, precise do-Shapley computation for SCMs and reduces identifiability burden, enhancing practical applicability of do-Shapley analysis.

title: Are Reasoning LLMs Robust to Interventions on Their Chain-of-Thought?
summary: Reasoning LLMs (RLLMs) generate step-by-step chains of thought (CoTs) before giving an answer, which improves performance on complex tasks and makes reasoning more transparent. But how robust are these reasoning traces to disruptions that occur within them? To address this question, we introduce a controlled evaluation framework that perturbs a model's own CoT at fixed timesteps. We design seven interventions (benign, neutral, and adversarial) and apply them to multiple open-weight RLLMs across Math, Science, and Logic tasks. Our results show that RLLMs are generally robust, reliably recovering from diverse perturbations, with robustness improving with model size and degrading when interventions occur early. However, robustness is not style-invariant: paraphrasing suppresses doubt-like expressions and reduces performance, while other interventions trigger doubt and support recovery. Recovery also carries a cost: neutral and adversarial noise can inflate CoT length by more than 200%, whereas paraphrasing shortens traces but harms accuracy. These findings provide new evidence on how RLLMs maintain reasoning integrity, identify doubt as a central recovery mechanism, and highlight trade-offs between robustness and efficiency that future training methods should address.

AI.tldr: A controlled framework perturbs chain-of-thought (CoT) traces in Reasoning LLMs to test robustness across Math, Science, and Logic tasks. Results: models are generally robust, with robustness improving with model size and decreasing when perturbations occur early; paraphrasing reduces doubt expressions and hurts accuracy, while other perturbations elicit doubt and aid recovery; recovery often inflates CoT length (neutral/adversarial >200%), whereas paraphrasing shortens traces but harms performance. Implications: doubt acts as a central recovery mechanism and there are trade-offs between robustness and efficiency that training should address.
AI.motivation: To understand how robust RLLMs' reasoning traces are to disruptions occurring within the chain-of-thought, by applying controlled perturbations to the model's own CoT at fixed timesteps and assessing recovery across domains.
AI.method: Design seven interventions (benign, neutral, adversarial) applied to multiple open-weight RLLMs across Math, Science, and Logic tasks. Perturb CoT at fixed timesteps and evaluate impact on task accuracy, CoT length, and recovery behavior.
AI.result: RLLMs generally recover from perturbations; robustness scales with model size; early perturbations are more damaging; paraphrasing suppresses doubt-like expressions and lowers performance; other perturbations induce doubt and assist recovery; neutral/adversarial perturbations can inflate CoT length by over 200%; paraphrasing shortens traces but reduces accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Doubt serves as a central mechanism for recovering valid reasoning, and there are explicit robustness-efficiency trade-offs. Style invariance (e.g., paraphrasing) is not universal, underlining the need for training methods that preserve reasoning integrity while controlling trace length.

title: TextOp: Real-time Interactive Text-Driven Humanoid Robot Motion Generation and Control
summary: Recent advances in humanoid whole-body motion tracking have enabled the execution of diverse and highly coordinated motions on real hardware. However, existing controllers are commonly driven either by predefined motion trajectories, which offer limited flexibility when user intent changes, or by continuous human teleoperation, which requires constant human involvement and limits autonomy. This work addresses the problem of how to drive a universal humanoid controller in a real-time and interactive manner. We present TextOp, a real-time text-driven humanoid motion generation and control framework that supports streaming language commands and on-the-fly instruction modification during execution. TextOp adopts a two-level architecture in which a high-level autoregressive motion diffusion model continuously generates short-horizon kinematic trajectories conditioned on the current text input, while a low-level motion tracking policy executes these trajectories on a physical humanoid robot. By bridging interactive motion generation with robust whole-body control, TextOp unlocks free-form intent expression and enables smooth transitions across multiple challenging behaviors such as dancing and jumping, within a single continuous motion execution. Extensive real-robot experiments and offline evaluations demonstrate instant responsiveness, smooth whole-body motion, and precise control. The project page and the open-source code are available at https://text-op.github.io/

AI.tldr: TextOp is a real-time, text-driven humanoid motion system with a two-level architecture: a high-level diffusion-based planner conditioned on text and a low-level tracker; it supports streaming commands, on-the-fly edits, and demonstrates immediate, smooth, robust motions (e.g., dancing, jumping) on real robots; open-sourced.
AI.motivation: There is a trade-off between trajectory-based controllers (autonomy) and teleoperation (flexibility). A real-time, interactive method is needed to express free-form intent while maintaining stable control on humanoid hardware.
AI.method: Two-level architecture: a high-level autoregressive motion diffusion model generates short-horizon kinematic trajectories conditioned on current text; a low-level motion tracking policy executes trajectories on a real humanoid robot; supports streaming language commands and on-the-fly instruction modification.
AI.result: Extensive real-robot experiments and offline evaluations show instant responsiveness, smooth whole-body motion, and precise control; project page and code released.
AI.conclusion: TextOp bridges interactive motion generation with robust whole-body control to enable free-form intent expression and smooth transitions across challenging behaviors within a single continuous execution; demonstrates feasibility of real-time, text-driven humanoid control.

title: Toward Accurate and Accessible Markerless Neuronavigation
summary: Neuronavigation is widely used in biomedical research and interventions to guide the precise placement of instruments around the head to support procedures such as transcranial magnetic stimulation. Traditional systems, however, rely on subject-mounted markers that require manual registration, may shift during procedures, and can cause discomfort. We introduce and evaluate markerless approaches that replace expensive hardware and physical markers with low-cost visible and infrared light cameras incorporating stereo and depth sensing combined with algorithmic modeling of the facial geometry. Validation with $50$ human subjects yielded a median tracking discrepancy of only $2.32$ mm and $2.01°$ for the best markerless algorithms compared to a conventional marker-based system, which indicates sufficient accuracy for transcranial magnetic stimulation and a substantial improvement over prior markerless results. The results suggest that integration of the data from the various camera sensors can improve the overall accuracy further. The proposed markerless neuronavigation methods can reduce setup cost and complexity, improve patient comfort, and expand access to neuronavigation in clinical and research settings.

AI.tldr: Markerless neuronavigation using stereo/depth cameras achieves accuracy comparable to marker-based systems with reduced cost and increased comfort; validated on 50 subjects with median errors ~2.3 mm and ~2°.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of subject-mounted markers (discomfort, registration effort, potential shifts) and reduce cost/complexity to widen access to neuronavigation for TMS and related procedures.
AI.method: Combine visible and infrared cameras with stereo and depth sensing; model facial geometry algorithmically; perform multi-sensor data fusion; validate against conventional marker-based system on 50 human subjects.
AI.result: Best markerless algorithms achieved median discrepancy 2.32 mm and 2.01°, showing substantial improvement over prior markerless methods and comparable accuracy to marker-based systems; data fusion may further improve accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Markerless neuronavigation is feasible and advantageous, reducing setup cost/complexity and improving patient comfort, with accurate performance sufficient for TMS and broader clinical/research adoption.

title: Online Learning for Uninformed Markov Games: Empirical Nash-Value Regret and Non-Stationarity Adaptation
summary:
We study online learning in two-player uninformed Markov games, where the opponent's actions and policies are unobserved. In this setting, Tian et al. (2021) show that achieving no-external-regret is impossible without incurring an exponential dependence on the episode length $H$. They then turn to the weaker notion of Nash-value regret and propose a V-learning algorithm with regret $O(K^{2/3})$ after $K$ episodes. However, their algorithm and guarantee do not adapt to the difficulty of the problem: even in the case where the opponent follows a fixed policy and thus $O(\sqrt{K})$ external regret is well-known to be achievable, their result is still the worse rate $O(K^{2/3})$ on a weaker metric.
  In this work, we fully address both limitations. First, we introduce empirical Nash-value regret, a new regret notion that is strictly stronger than Nash-value regret and naturally reduces to external regret when the opponent follows a fixed policy. Moreover, under this new metric, we propose a parameter-free algorithm that achieves an $O(\min \{\sqrt{K} + (CK)^{1/3},\sqrt{LK}\})$ regret bound, where $C$ quantifies the variance of the opponent's policies and $L$ denotes the number of policy switches (both at most $O(K)$). Therefore, our results not only recover the two extremes -- $O(\sqrt{K})$ external regret when the opponent is fixed and $O(K^{2/3})$ Nash-value regret in the worst case -- but also smoothly interpolate between these extremes by automatically adapting to the opponent's non-stationarity. We achieve so by first providing a new analysis of the epoch-based V-learning algorithm by Mao et al. (2022), establishing an $O(ηC + \sqrt{K/η})$ regret bound, where $η$ is the epoch incremental factor. Next, we show how to adaptively restart this algorithm with an appropriate $η$ in response to the potential non-stationarity of the opponent, eventually achieving our final results.

AI.tldr: Proposes empirical Nash-value regret and an adaptive, parameter-free algorithm that adaptively interpolates between external regret and Nash-value regret in two-player uninformed Markov games, achieving a regret of O(min{ sqrt(K) + (C K)^{1/3}, sqrt{L K} }).
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of prior work: no-external-regret requires exponential dependence on episode length; existing Nash-value regret analyses (Mao et al. 2022; Tian et al. 2021) are non-adaptive and do not recover external regret when the opponent is fixed. The goal is a regret notion that reduces to external regret under fixed policies and adapts to opponent non-stationarity.
AI.method: Introduce empirical Nash-value regret, a stronger metric that reduces to external regret for stationary opponents.
