Develop a parameter-free algorithm by analyzing epoch-based V-learning (as in Mao et al. 2022) and showing a regret of O(ηC + sqrt(K/η)) where η is the epoch increment. Propose adaptive restarts with a data-driven η to respond to non-stationarity, yielding the final bound.
AI.result: The approach yields a regret bound of O(min{ sqrt(K) + (C K)^{1/3}, sqrt{L K} }), where C captures opponent policy variance and L the number of policy switches (both O(K)). The algorithm automatically adapts between the two extremes: O(√K) external regret for fixed opponents and O(K^{2/3}) Nash-value regret in the worst case.
AI.conclusion: Empirical Nash-value regret provides a stronger, more informative metric that aligns with external regret under stationarity and with Nash-value regret in non-stationary settings. The adaptive epoch-based V-learning with data-driven restarts achieves a smooth interpolation between regret regimes, automatically adapting to opponent non-stationarity and achieving near-optimal regret across scenarios.

title: Computing the Reachability Value of Posterior-Deterministic POMDPs
summary:
Partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) are a fundamental model for sequential decision-making under uncertainty. However, many verification and synthesis problems for POMDPs are undecidable or intractable. Most prominently, the seminal result of Madani et al. (2003) states that there is no algorithm that, given a POMDP and a set of target states, can compute the maximal probability of reaching the target states, or even approximate it up to a non-trivial constant. This is in stark contrast to fully observable Markov decision processes (MDPs), where the reachability value can be computed in polynomial time.
  In this work, we introduce posterior-deterministic POMDPs, a novel class of POMDPs. Our main technical contribution is to show that for posterior-deterministic POMDPs, the maximal probability of reaching a given set of states can be approximated up to arbitrary precision.
  A POMDP is posterior-deterministic if the next state can be uniquely determined by the current state, the action taken, and the observation received. While the actual state is generally uncertain in POMDPs, the posterior-deterministic property tells us that once the true state is known it remains known forever. This simple and natural definition includes all MDPs and captures classical non-trivial examples such as the Tiger POMDP (Kaelbling et al. 1998), making it one of the largest known classes of POMDPs for which the reachability value can be approximated.

AI.tldr: For posterior-deterministic POMDPs, the reachability probability to a target set can be approximated to arbitrary precision; this subclass is broad, including all MDPs and the Tiger POMDP.
AI.motivation: POMDP verification and synthesis are often undecidable or intractable; identify a natural, broad subclass where reachability probabilities are amenable to approximation to mitigate the intractability of the general problem.
AI.method: Define the posterior-deterministic property: the next state is uniquely determined by the current state, action, and observation; once the true state is known, it remains known. Prove that within this class, the maximal probability of reaching a target set can be approximated to arbitrary precision, likely via a reduction to a tractable belief-state or finite-state representation.
AI.result: Establishes that the reachability probability in posterior-deterministic POMDPs can be approximated to any desired accuracy; the class encompasses all MDPs and the Tiger POMDP, making it one of the largest known tractable subclasses for this problem.
AI.conclusion: Posterior-deterministic POMDPs offer a natural, broad framework where key verification questions become tractable in approximation, motivating further algorithmic development and exploration of related determinism-based subclasses.

title: VividFace: Real-Time and Realistic Facial Expression Shadowing for Humanoid Robots
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA)
summary: Humanoid facial expression shadowing enables robots to realistically imitate human facial expressions in real time, which is critical for lifelike, facially expressive humanoid robots and affective human-robot interaction. Existing progress in humanoid facial expression imitation remains limited, often failing to achieve either real-time performance or realistic expressiveness due to offline video-based inference designs and insufficient ability to capture and transfer subtle expression details. To address these limitations, we present VividFace, a real-time and realistic facial expression shadowing system for humanoid robots. An optimized imitation framework X2CNet++ enhances expressiveness by fine-tuning the human-to-humanoid facial motion transfer module and introducing a feature-adaptation training strategy for better alignment across different image sources. Real-time shadowing is further enabled by a video-stream-compatible inference pipeline and a streamlined workflow based on asynchronous I/O for efficient communication across devices. VividFace produces vivid humanoid faces by mimicking human facial expressions within 0.05 seconds, while generalizing across diverse facial configurations. Extensive real-world demonstrations validate its practical utility. Videos are available at: https://lipzh5.github.io/VividFace/.

AI.tldr: Real-time, realistic humanoid facial expression shadowing using VividFace and X2CNet++ with cross-source alignment.
AI.motivation: Overcome offline, video-based inference and limited transfer of subtle facial details to enable real-time, expressive humanoid robots across diverse face configurations.
AI.method: X2CNet++ optimization: refine the human-to-humanoid facial motion transfer; introduce a feature-adaptation training strategy for cross-image-source alignment; implement a video-stream-compatible, asynchronous I/O inference pipeline for multi-device communication.
AI.result: Achieves real-time shadowing with 0.05 s latency; generalizes across diverse facial configurations; supported by extensive real-world demonstrations; accompanying videos provided.
AI.conclusion: VividFace enables practical, real-time, lifelike facial expression imitation for humanoid robots and broader affective HRI, with demonstrated cross-source robustness.

title: RECITYGEN -- Interactive and Generative Participatory Urban Design Tool with Latent Diffusion and Segment Anything
summary: Urban design profoundly impacts public spaces and community engagement. Traditional top-down methods often overlook public input, creating a gap in design aspirations and reality. Recent advancements in digital tools, like City Information Modelling and augmented reality, have enabled a more participatory process involving more stakeholders in urban design. Further, deep learning and latent diffusion models have lowered barriers for design generation, providing even more opportunities for participatory urban design. Combining state-of-the-art latent diffusion models with interactive semantic segmentation, we propose RECITYGEN, a novel tool that allows users to interactively create variational street view images of urban environments using text prompts. In a pilot project in Beijing, users employed RECITYGEN to suggest improvements for an ongoing Urban Regeneration project. Despite some limitations, RECITYGEN has shown significant potential in aligning with public preferences, indicating a shift towards more dynamic and inclusive urban planning methods. The source code for the project can be found at RECITYGEN GitHub.

AI.tldr: RECITYGEN: a latent-diffusion and interactive semantic segmentation tool for participatory urban design that generates variational street-view images from text prompts; pilot in Beijing shows promise in aligning with public preferences; open-source with some limitations.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between top-down urban design and public input by enabling broader stakeholder engagement using advanced digital tools and generative models.
AI.method: Integrates state-of-the-art latent diffusion models with interactive semantic segmentation to let users create variational street-view images via text prompts; implemented as RECITYGEN; pilot in Beijing for an Urban Regeneration project; situates work within digital urban design trends (City Information Modelling, AR).
AI.result: Demonstrates potential to align design outputs with public preferences and supports a shift toward more dynamic, inclusive urban planning; acknowledges limitations (e.g., quality, biases, scalability, usability).
AI.conclusion: RECITYGEN is a promising direction for participatory urban design; warrants further development, evaluation, and broader deployment; source code is available on GitHub.

title: DSL: Understanding and Improving Softmax Recommender Systems with Competition-Aware Scaling
summary:
Softmax Loss (SL) is being increasingly adopted for recommender systems (RS) as it has demonstrated better performance, robustness and fairness. Yet in implicit-feedback, a single global temperature and equal treatment of uniformly sampled negatives can lead to brittle training, because sampled sets may contain varying degrees of relevant or informative competitors. The optimal loss sharpness for a user-item pair with a particular set of negatives, can be suboptimal or destabilising for another with different negatives. We introduce Dual-scale Softmax Loss (DSL), which infers effective sharpness from the sampled competition itself. DSL adds two complementary branches to the log-sum-exp backbone. Firstly it reweights negatives within each training instance using hardness and item--item similarity, secondly it adapts a per-example temperature from the competition intensity over a constructed competitor slate. Together, these components preserve the geometry of SL while reshaping the competition distribution across negatives and across examples.
  Over several representative benchmarks and backbones, DSL yields substantial gains over strong baselines, with improvements over SL exceeding $10%$ in several settings and averaging $6.22%$ across datasets, metrics, and backbones. Under out-of-distribution (OOD) popularity shift, the gains are larger, with an average of $9.31%$ improvement over SL. We further provide a theoretical, distributionally robust optimisation (DRO) analysis, which demonstrates how DSL reshapes the robust payoff and the KL deviation for ambiguous instances. This helps explain the empirically observed improvements in accuracy and robustness.

AI.tldr: Dual-scale Softmax Loss (DSL) for implicit-feedback RS adds two adaptive mechanisms—per-instance negative reweighting via hardness and item-item similarity, and per-example temperature based on competition intensity—preserving Softmax geometry while improving stability and robustness. Empirically, DSL yields meaningful gains (avg ~6.22% across benchmarks; up to >10% in some settings; ~9.3% under OOD shifts) and is supported by a distributionally robust optimization (DRO) analysis.
AI.motivation: Softmax Loss with a single global temperature and uniformly sampled negatives can lead to brittle training when the set of negatives varies across users/items. Different negative sets create different optimal loss sharpness, which a fixed loss cannot accommodate.
AI.method: Enhance the log-sum-exp backbone with two complementary branches: (1) in-instance negative reweighting using hardness and item–item similarity to emphasize informative negatives; (2) per-example temperature adaptation derived from competition intensity over a constructed competitor slate, effectively reshaping the competition distribution while preserving SL geometry.
AI.result: Empirical improvements over strong baselines: average gains of 6.22% across datasets, metrics, and backbones; several settings exceed 10%; larger gains under distribution shift (OOD) with ~9.31% average improvement; theoretical DRO analysis shows DSL reshapes robust payoff and KL deviation, explaining improved accuracy and robustness.
AI.conclusion: DSL adaptively tunes loss sharpness to the observed competition, yielding more stable and robust learning for implicit-feedback RS without abandoning the beneficial geometry of Softmax Loss.

title: GraphAgents: Knowledge Graph-Guided Agentic AI for Cross-Domain Materials Design
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) promise to accelerate discovery by reasoning across the expanding scientific landscape. Yet, the challenge is no longer access to information but connecting it in meaningful, domain-spanning ways. In materials science, where innovation demands integrating concepts from molecular chemistry to mechanical performance, this is especially acute. Neither humans nor single-agent LLMs can fully contend with this torrent of information, with the latter often prone to hallucinations. To address this bottleneck, we introduce a multi-agent framework guided by large-scale knowledge graphs to find sustainable substitutes for per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS)-chemicals currently under intense regulatory scrutiny. Agents in the framework specialize in problem decomposition, evidence retrieval, design parameter extraction, and graph traversal, uncovering latent connections across distinct knowledge pockets to support hypothesis generation. Ablation studies show that the full multi-agent pipeline outperforms single-shot prompting, underscoring the value of distributed specialization and relational reasoning. We demonstrate that by tailoring graph traversal strategies, the system alternates between exploitative searches focusing on domain-critical outcomes and exploratory searches surfacing emergent cross-connections. Illustrated through the exemplar of biomedical tubing, the framework generates sustainable PFAS-free alternatives that balance tribological performance, thermal stability, chemical resistance, and biocompatibility. This work establishes a framework combining knowledge graphs with multi-agent reasoning to expand the materials design space, showcasing several initial design candidates to demonstrate the approach.

AI.tldr: Multi-agent framework guided by knowledge graphs to design PFAS-free substitutes; beats single-shot prompting; demonstrates design candidates via modular agents and graph traversal.
AI.motivation: Address information overload and hallucinations in single-agent LLMs for materials design by connecting cross-domain knowledge; enable sustainable PFAS substitutes.
AI.method: Modular agents specialize in problem decomposition, evidence retrieval, design parameter extraction, and graph traversal. Uses knowledge graphs with tailored traversal strategies to switch between exploitation and exploration. Includes ablation studies. Applied to PFAS-free designs, illustrated with biomedical tubing; aims to balance tribological performance, thermal stability, chemical resistance, and biocompatibility.
AI.result: Full multi-agent pipeline outperforms single-shot prompting; demonstrates emergent cross-domain connections; yields initial PFAS-free design candidates meeting multiple properties.
AI.conclusion: Integrating knowledge graphs with multi-agent reasoning expands the materials design space and provides a promising, scalable approach, with initial design candidates demonstrated.

title: Differentiate-and-Inject: Enhancing VLAs via Functional Differentiation Induced by In-Parameter Structural Reasoning
summary: As robots are expected to perform increasingly diverse tasks, they must understand not only low-level actions but also the higher-level structure that determines how a task should unfold. Existing vision-language-action (VLA) models struggle with this form of task-level reasoning. They either depend on prompt-based in-context decomposition, which is unstable and sensitive to linguistic variations, or end-to-end long-horizon training, which requires large-scale demonstrations and entangles task-level reasoning with low-level control. We present in-parameter structured task reasoning (iSTAR), a framework for enhancing VLA models via functional differentiation induced by in-parameter structural reasoning. Instead of treating VLAs as monolithic policies, iSTAR embeds task-level semantic structure directly into model parameters, enabling differentiated task-level inference without external planners or handcrafted prompt inputs. This injected structure takes the form of implicit dynamic scene-graph knowledge that captures object relations, subtask semantics, and task-level dependencies in parameter space. Across diverse manipulation benchmarks, iSTAR achieves more reliable task decompositions and higher success rates than both in-context and end-to-end VLA baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness of parameter-space structural reasoning for functional differentiation and improved generalization across task variations.

AI.tldr: iSTAR introduces in-parameter structured task reasoning to VLA models, embedding task-level structure in model parameters to enable reliable task decomposition and better generalization without external planners or prompts.
AI.motivation: Current VLA models struggle with task-level reasoning: prompt-based decomposition is unstable, and end-to-end training is data-hungry and entangles task reasoning with low-level control. A decoupled, parameter-space approach could enable stable, generalizable task reasoning.
AI.method: iSTAR injects implicit dynamic scene-graph knowledge into model parameters, encoding object relations, subtasks, and task-level dependencies to enable functional differentiation. This avoids external planners or handcrafted prompts and treats VLA outputs as differentiated task-level inferences.
AI.result: On diverse manipulation benchmarks, iSTAR achieves more reliable task decompositions and higher task success rates than both in-context and end-to-end VLA baselines, demonstrating improved generalization across task variations.
AI.conclusion: Parameter-space structural reasoning is effective for functional differentiation in VLA models, enabling robust task-level inference and better generalization without external planning or prompt engineering.

title: FADE: Selective Forgetting via Sparse LoRA and Self-Distillation
summary: Machine Unlearning aims to remove the influence of specific data or concepts from trained models while preserving overall performance, a capability increasingly required by data protection regulations and responsible AI practices. Despite recent progress, unlearning in text-to-image diffusion models remains challenging due to high computational costs and the difficulty of balancing effective forgetting with retention of unrelated concepts. We introduce FADE (Fast Adapter for Data Erasure), a two-stage unlearning method for image generation that combines parameter localization with self-distillation. FADE first identifies parameters most responsible for the forget set using gradient-based saliency and constrains updates through sparse LoRA adapters, ensuring lightweight, localized modifications. In a second stage, FADE applies a self-distillation objective that overwrites the forgotten concept with a user-defined surrogate while preserving behavior on retained data. The resulting adapters are memory-efficient, reversible, and can be merged or removed at runtime, enabling flexible deployment in production systems. We evaluated FADE on the UnlearnCanvas benchmark and conducted ablation studies on Imagenette, Labeled Faces in the Wild, AtharvaTaras Dog Breeds Dataset, and SUN Attributes datasets, demonstrating State-of-the-Art unlearning performance with fine-grained control over the forgetting-retention trade-off. Our results demonstrate that FADE achieves strong concept erasure and high retainability across various domains, making it a suitable solution for selective unlearning in diffusion-based image generation models.

AI.tldr: FADE is a two-stage, memory-efficient unlearning approach for diffusion-based image generation: stage 1 uses gradient-based saliency to localize parameters and applies sparse LoRA adapters to constrain updates; stage 2 uses self-distillation to overwrite the forgotten concept with a user-defined surrogate while preserving retained data, yielding reversible adapters that can be merged or removed. It achieves state-of-the-art erasure with controllable forgetting-retention and is evaluated on UnlearnCanvas and multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: There is a growing need to remove the influence of specific data or concepts from trained models to comply with data protection regulations and responsible AI practices. In text-to-image diffusion models, unlearning is challenging due to high computational costs and the difficulty of balancing forgetting with retention of unrelated concepts.
AI.method: A two-stage approach: (1) parameter localization via gradient-based saliency to identify involved parameters and constrain updates with sparse LoRA adapters for lightweight, localized modification; (2) a self-distillation objective that overwrites the forgotten concept with a user-provided surrogate while preserving behavior on retained data. The adapters are memory-efficient, reversible, and can be merged/removed at runtime.
AI.result: Experiments on the UnlearnCanvas benchmark and ablations on Imagenette, Labeled Faces in the Wild, AtharvaTaras Dog Breeds Dataset, and SUN Attributes show state-of-the-art unlearning performance with fine-grained control over forgetting vs retention; strong concept erasure and high retainability across diverse domains.
AI.conclusion: FADE provides an effective, deployable solution for selective unlearning in diffusion-based image generation, delivering strong erasure with controllable retention while keeping adapters lightweight and reversible.

title: Joint Reward Modeling: Internalizing Chain-of-Thought for Efficient Visual Reward Models
summary: Reward models are critical for reinforcement learning from human feedback, as they determine the alignment quality and reliability of generative models. For complex tasks such as image editing, reward models are required to capture global semantic consistency and implicit logical constraints beyond local similarity. Existing reward modeling approaches have clear limitations. Discriminative reward models align well with human preferences but struggle with complex semantics due to limited reasoning supervision. Generative reward models offer stronger semantic understanding and reasoning, but they are costly at inference time and difficult to align directly with human preferences. To this end, we propose Joint Reward Modeling (JRM), which jointly optimizes preference learning and language modeling on a shared vision-language backbone. This approach internalizes the semantic and reasoning capabilities of generative models into efficient discriminative representations, enabling fast and accurate evaluation. JRM achieves state-of-the-art results on MMRB2 and EditReward-Bench, and significantly improves stability and performance in downstream online reinforcement learning. These results show that joint training effectively bridges efficiency and semantic understanding in reward modeling.

AI.tldr: Joint Reward Modeling (JRM) trains a shared vision-language backbone to jointly optimize preference learning and language modeling, unifying discriminative reward efficiency with generative semantic reasoning for RLHF.
AI.motivation: Address the dichotomy between efficient discriminative reward models (limited reasoning) and computationally heavy but semantically rich generative rewards by embedding both capabilities in a single, shared representation.
AI.method: Jointly optimize preference learning and language modeling on a shared vision-language backbone, transferring generative reasoning capabilities into efficient discriminative reward representations.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on MMRB2 and EditReward-Bench; improves stability and performance in downstream online reinforcement learning.
AI.conclusion: Joint training on a shared backbone effectively bridges efficiency and semantic understanding in reward modeling, enabling fast, semantically grounded reward evaluation for RLHF.

title: "Meet My Sidekick!": Effects of Separate Identities and Control of a Single Robot in HRI
summary: The presentation of a robot's capability and identity directly influences a human collaborator's perception and implicit trust in the robot. Unlike humans, a physical robot can simultaneously present different identities and have them reside and control different parts of the robot. This paper presents a novel study that investigates how users perceive a robot where different robot control domains (head and gripper) are presented as independent robots. We conducted a mixed design study where participants experienced one of three presentations: a single robot, two agents with shared full control (co-embodiment), or two agents with split control across robot control domains (split-embodiment). Participants underwent three distinct tasks -- a mundane data entry task where the robot provides motivational support, an individual sorting task with isolated robot failures, and a collaborative arrangement task where the robot causes a failure that directly affects the human participant. Participants perceived the robot as residing in the different control domains and were able to associate robot failure with different identities. This work signals how future robots can leverage different embodiment configurations to obtain the benefit of multiple robots within a single body.

AI.tldr: Examines how presenting a robot's head and gripper as independent agents (split embodiment) vs. as a single agent or with full co-ownership affects human perception and implicit trust; shows users attribute distinct identities to control domains and associate failures with specific identities; suggests multi-embodiment within one robot can yield benefits of multiple robots.
AI.motivation: To understand how embodiment configuration and perceived identity of robot control domains influence user perception, trust, and interaction quality in human-robot collaboration.
AI.method: A mixed-design experiment with three presentation conditions: (1) a single robot, (2) two agents with shared full control (co-embodiment), (3) two agents with split control across robot domains (split-embodiment). Participants performed three tasks: a mundane data-entry task with motivational support, an individual sorting task with isolated robot failures, and a collaborative arrangement task where the robot causes a failure affecting the participant. Measures assessed perception of embodiment, identity attribution, and association between robot failure and identity.
AI.result: Participants perceived control domains as residing in separate agents and could map robot failure to different identities; split-embodiment allowed distinct agency attribution to each domain, indicating potential for multiple embodied identities within a single robot body.
AI.conclusion: Embodiment configurations across robot control domains can create multiple perceived agents within one robot, enabling flexible collaboration and potentially enhancing trust and accountability by associating failures with specific identities; future work could explore additional domains, tasks, and long-term effects on trust and performance.

title: From Images to Decisions: Assistive Computer Vision for Non-Metallic Content Estimation in Scrap Metal
comment: AAAI 2026 Workshop on Addressing Challenges and Opportunities in Human-Centric Manufacturing
summary: Scrap quality directly affects energy use, emissions, and safety in steelmaking. Today, the share of non-metallic inclusions (contamination) is judged visually by inspectors - an approach that is subjective and hazardous due to dust and moving machinery. We present an assistive computer vision pipeline that estimates contamination (per percent) from images captured during railcar unloading and also classifies scrap type. The method formulates contamination assessment as a regression task at the railcar level and leverages sequential data through multi-instance learning (MIL) and multi-task learning (MTL). Best results include MAE 0.27 and R2 0.83 by MIL; and an MTL setup reaches MAE 0.36 with F1 0.79 for scrap class. Also we present the system in near real time within the acceptance workflow: magnet/railcar detection segments temporal layers, a versioned inference service produces railcar-level estimates with confidence scores, and results are reviewed by operators with structured overrides; corrections and uncertain cases feed an active-learning loop for continual improvement. The pipeline reduces subjective variability, improves human safety, and enables integration into acceptance and melt-planning workflows.

AI.tldr: A computer vision pipeline estimates contamination level and scrap type from railcar images using MIL/MTL, with near-real-time deployment and active learning.
AI.motivation: Visual, subjective, and hazardous inspection of non-metallic contamination in scrap leads to energy/emissions penalties and safety risks. An objective, automated, real-time assessment is needed to support melt planning and reduce variability.
AI.method: Formulates contamination as a regression task at the railcar level. Uses multi-instance learning (MIL) to exploit sequential data and multi-task learning (MTL) for scrap-type classification. Incorporates magnet/railcar detection to segment temporal layers, a versioned inference service for railcar-level estimates with confidence scores, operator review with structured overrides, and an active-learning loop for corrections feeding continual improvement.
AI.result: MIL achieves MAE 0.27 and R2 0.83 on contamination regression. An MTL setup achieves MAE 0.36 with F1 0.79 for scrap-class classification. System operates near real-time and supports integration into acceptance and melt-planning workflows.
AI.conclusion: The approach reduces subjective variability and safety hazards while enabling integration into steel-scrap acceptance and melt-planning processes; active learning supports continual improvement of predictions and classifications.

title: Probing Neural TSP Representations for Prescriptive Decision Support
summary: The field of neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) trains neural policies to solve NP-hard problems such as the traveling salesperson problem (TSP). We ask whether, beyond producing good tours, a trained TSP solver learns internal representations that transfer to other optimization-relevant objectives, in the spirit of transfer learning from other domains. We train several attention-based TSP policies, collect their internal activations, and train probes on node/edge embeddings for two NP-hard prescriptive downstream tasks inspired by real-world logistics scenarios: node-removal sensitivity (identifying the most impactful node to remove) and edge-forbid sensitivity (identifying the most critical edge to retain). On a Euclidean TSP100-trained model, probes for both tasks are competitive with existing baselines. Ensembling probe signals with geometric features outperforms the strongest baselines: 65\% top-1 accuracy (vs. 58\% baseline) for the best-node-removal task, and 73\% top-1 accuracy (vs. 67\% baseline) for the worst-edge identification task. To our knowledge, we are the first to study neural TSP solvers as transferable encoders for prescriptive what-if decision-support objectives beyond tour construction. Finally, we show that transfer accuracy increases with solver quality across training and model scale, suggesting that training stronger NCO solvers also yields more useful encoders for downstream objectives. Our code is available at: github.com/ReubenNarad/tsp_prescriptive_probe

AI.tldr: Neural TSP solvers learn transferable internal representations that can support prescriptive what-if decisions; probes on activations predict node-removal and edge-forbid sensitivity; ensembling with geometric features yields state-of-the-art accuracy and transfer improves with solver quality.
AI.motivation: To explore transferability of learned representations from neural combinatorial optimization to downstream prescriptive objectives, beyond constructing optimal tours.
AI.method: Train attention-based TSP policies; extract internal activations; train probes on node/edge embeddings for two downstream tasks; evaluate on Euclidean TSP100; ensemble probe signals with hand-engineered geometric features; compare to baselines; analyze scaling with model quality.
AI.result: Probes competitive with baselines; ensembling with geometric features improves top-1 accuracy to 65% (node) and 73% (edge) vs 58% and 67% baselines; transfer accuracy increases with solver quality; first to study this transferability; code released.
AI.conclusion: Stronger NCO solvers yield more useful encoders for downstream prescriptive objectives; establishes a bridge between solver performance and transferability; opens path for prescriptive decision-support using neural encoders.

title: MSP-LLM: A Unified Large Language Model Framework for Complete Material Synthesis Planning
summary: Material synthesis planning (MSP) remains a fundamental and underexplored bottleneck in AI-driven materials discovery, as it requires not only identifying suitable precursor materials but also designing coherent sequences of synthesis operations to realize a target material. Although several AI-based approaches have been proposed to address isolated subtasks of MSP, a unified methodology for solving the entire MSP task has yet to be established. We propose MSP-LLM, a unified LLM-based framework that formulates MSP as a structured process composed of two constituent subproblems: precursor prediction (PP) and synthesis operation prediction (SOP). Our approach introduces a discrete material class as an intermediate decision variable that organizes both tasks into a chemically consistent decision chain. For OP, we further incorporate hierarchical precursor types as synthesis-relevant inductive biases and employ an explicit conditioning strategy that preserves precursor-related information in the autoregressive decoding state. Extensive experiments show that MSP-LLM consistently outperforms existing methods on both PP and SOP, as well as on the complete MSP task, demonstrating an effective and scalable framework for MSP that can accelerate real-world materials discovery.

AI.tldr: MSP-LLM provides a unified LLM-based framework for material synthesis planning by jointly predicting precursors and synthesis operations through a discrete intermediate class, with hierarchical biases and conditioning to preserve precursor information; it outperforms baselines on all MSP subproblems.
AI.motivation: Material synthesis planning is a bottleneck in AI-driven materials discovery; existing works tackle precursor prediction or synthesis operation prediction in isolation; a unified approach can better exploit dependencies between precursor choices and synthesis steps.
AI.method: Formulate MSP as two subproblems (precursor prediction and synthesis operation prediction). Introduce a discrete material class as an intermediate decision variable to link PP and SOP. For SOP, incorporate hierarchical precursor types as inductive biases and apply explicit conditioning to retain precursor information in the autoregressive decoding state.
AI.result: MSP-LLM consistently outperforms existing methods across PP, SOP, and the full MSP task in extensive experiments, showing improved accuracy and a scalable approach.
AI.conclusion: A cohesive MSP framework leveraging LLMs and structured intermediate variables yields superior performance and provides a practical pathway to accelerate real-world materials discovery.

title: LCLA: Language-Conditioned Latent Alignment for Vision-Language Navigation
summary: We propose LCLA (Language-Conditioned Latent Alignment), a framework for vision-language navigation that learns modular perception-action interfaces by aligning sensory observations to a latent representation of an expert policy. The expert is first trained with privileged state information, inducing a latent space sufficient for control, after which its latent interface and action head are frozen. A lightweight adapter is then trained to map raw visual-language observations, via a frozen vision-language model, into the expert's latent space, reducing the problem of visuomotor learning to supervised latent alignment rather than end-to-end policy optimization. This decoupling enforces a stable contract between perception and control, enabling expert behavior to be reused across sensing modalities and environmental variations. We instantiate LCLA and evaluate it on a vision-language indoor navigation task, where aligned latent spaces yield strong in-distribution performance and robust zero-shot generalization to unseen environments, lighting conditions, and viewpoints while remaining lightweight at inference time.

AI.tldr: We propose LCLA, a language-conditioned latent alignment framework for vision-language navigation that freezes a privileged-expert latent interface and trains a lightweight adapter from raw visual-language inputs to that latent space; this decouples perception and control and improves generalization while keeping inference cheap.
AI.motivation: Address the brittleness and inefficiency of end-to-end visuomotor learning in vision-language navigation by learning a stable, modular latent interface between perception and control, reusable across sensing modalities and environments, using privileged information to elicit a latent space adequate for control.
AI.method: Train an expert with privileged state information to induce a latent space sufficient for control; freeze the expert's latent interface and action head; train a lightweight adapter that maps raw visual-language observations, via a frozen vision-language model, into the expert's latent space, performing supervised latent alignment instead of end-to-end policy optimization.
AI.result: Instantiates LCLA on a vision-language indoor navigation task; aligned latent spaces yield strong in-distribution performance and robust zero-shot generalization to unseen environments, lighting, and viewpoints, while remaining lightweight at inference time.
AI.conclusion: LCLA provides a modular, reusable perception-action interface by decoupling perception from control through latent alignment, enabling cross-modality robustness and efficient inference with minimal training complexity.

title: Exploring Physical Intelligence Emergence via Omni-Modal Architecture and Physical Data Engine
summary: Physical understanding remains brittle in omni-modal models because key physical attributes are visually ambiguous and sparsely represented in web-scale data. We present OmniFysics, a compact omni-modal model that unifies understanding across images, audio, video, and text, with integrated speech and image generation. To inject explicit physical knowledge, we build a physical data engine with two components. FysicsAny produces physics-grounded instruction--image supervision by mapping salient objects to verified physical attributes through hierarchical retrieval over a curated prototype database, followed by physics-law--constrained verification and caption rewriting. FysicsOmniCap distills web videos via audio--visual consistency filtering to generate high-fidelity video--instruction pairs emphasizing cross-modal physical cues. We train OmniFysics with staged multimodal alignment and instruction tuning, adopt latent-space flow matching for text-to-image generation, and use an intent router to activate generation only when needed. Experiments show competitive performance on standard multimodal benchmarks and improved results on physics-oriented evaluations.

AI.tldr: OmniFysics is a compact omni-modal model that unifies image, audio, video, and text understanding with integrated speech and image generation, augmented by physics-grounded data engines (FysicsAny and FysicsOmniCap) to inject explicit physical knowledge via hierarchical retrieval, physics-law constraints, and audio-visual consistency filtering.
AI.motivation: Physical understanding in omni-modal models is brittle due to visually ambiguous and sparsely represented physical attributes in web-scale data; the paper proposes explicit physics integration to improve physics-oriented reasoning.
AI.method: Two data engines: (1) FysicsAny builds physics-grounded instruction-image supervision by mapping salient objects to verified physical attributes using hierarchical retrieval from a curated prototype database, followed by physics-law-constrained verification and caption rewriting; (2) FysicsOmniCap distills web videos using audio-visual consistency filtering to generate high-fidelity video-instruction pairs emphasizing cross-modal physical cues. Training includes staged multimodal alignment and instruction tuning, latent-space flow matching for text-to-image generation, and an intent router to trigger generation only when needed.
AI.result: Reportedly competitive performance on standard multimodal benchmarks and improved results on physics-oriented evaluations, indicating enhanced physical understanding without sacrificing general multimodal capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The proposed OmniFysics framework effectively couples explicit physics knowledge with omni-modal understanding, leveraging specialized data engines and alignment strategies to improve physics-oriented reasoning while maintaining broad multimodal competence.

title: Collaborative and Efficient Fine-tuning: Leveraging Task Similarity
summary: Adaptability has been regarded as a central feature in the foundation models, enabling them to effectively acclimate to unseen downstream tasks. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods such as celebrated LoRA facilitate efficient adaptation of large foundation models using labeled, high-quality and generally scarce task data. To mitigate data scarcity in fine-tuning of foundation models, we propose to leverage task similarity across multiple downstream users. Intuitively, users with similar tasks must be able to assist each other in boosting the effective fine-tuning data size. We propose Collaborative Low-Rank Adaptation, or CoLoRA, which exploits task similarity to collaboratively and efficiently fine-tune personalized foundation models. The main idea in CoLoRA is to train one shared adapter capturing underlying task similarities across all tasks, and personalized adapters tailored to user-specific tasks. We theoretically study CoLoRA on heterogeneous linear regression and provide provable guarantees for ground truth recovery. We also conduct several natural language experiments with varying task similarity, which further demonstrate that when trained together with similar tasks, individual performances are significantly boosted.

AI.tldr: CoLoRA introduces collaborative Low-Rank Adaptation for foundation models by using a shared adapter to capture cross-task similarities and personalized adapters for individual tasks, enabling data-efficient fine-tuning across multiple users.
AI.motivation: Foundation models require data-efficient adaptation for unseen downstream tasks, but labeled data are scarce. Leveraging task similarity across multiple users can effectively increase the available fine-tuning signal and improve personalization.
AI.method: Train one shared adapter that captures underlying task similarities across all tasks, plus personalized adapters tailored to each user task. Provide theoretical guarantees for ground-truth recovery in a heterogeneous linear-regression setting, and validate with NLP experiments showing performance gains when similar tasks are learned jointly.
AI.result: The approach yields provable recovery guarantees and empirical improvements: training with similar tasks boosts individual task performance compared to training in isolation.
AI.conclusion: Collaborative adaptation via a shared cross-task component and personalized adapters enables data-efficient, personalized fine-tuning of foundation models in settings with task similarity, supported by theory and NLP experiments.

title: When Is Enough Not Enough? Illusory Completion in Search Agents
summary: Recent search agents leverage multi-turn reasoning and search tools to achieve strong performance on multi-hop and long-horizon benchmarks. Yet it remains unclear whether they reliably reason across all requirements by tracking, verifying, and maintaining multiple conditions in these questions. We study this capability under multi-constraint problems, where valid answers must satisfy several constraints simultaneously. We find that illusory completion frequently occurs, wherein agents believe tasks are complete despite unresolved or violated constraints, leading to underverified answers. To diagnose this behavior, we introduce the Epistemic Ledger, an evaluation framework that tracks evidential support and agents' beliefs for each constraint throughout multi-turn reasoning. Our analysis reveals four recurring failure patterns: bare assertions, overlooked refutations, stagnation, and premature exit. Motivated by these findings, we examine whether explicit constraint-state tracking during execution mitigates these failures via LiveLedger, an inference-time tracker. This simple intervention consistently improves performance, substantially reducing underverified answers (by up to 26.5%) and improving overall accuracy (by up to 11.6%) on multi-constraint problems.

AI.tldr: Tracking evidential support and beliefs across constraints mitigates underverification in multi-constraint reasoning; LiveLedger improves accuracy.
AI.motivation: Modern search agents struggle to reliably satisfy multiple simultaneous constraints due to illusory completion; there is a need to diagnose and mitigate underverification in multi-turn reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce the Epistemic Ledger to track evidential support and agents' beliefs for each constraint during multi-turn reasoning; identify four failure patterns; propose LiveLedger as an inference-time constraint-state tracker; evaluate its impact on multi-constraint tasks.
AI.result: Four recurring failure patterns were observed: bare assertions, overlooked refutations, stagnation, and premature exit. LiveLedger consistently reduced underverified answers (up to 26.5%) and improved overall accuracy (up to 11.6%).
AI.conclusion: Explicit constraint-state tracking enhances reliability in multi-constraint reasoning; LiveLedger is an effective, simple intervention to mitigate underverification and improve accuracy in multi-hop scenarios.

title: Affine Transformable Unmanned Ground Vehicle
summary: This paper develops the proof of concept for a novel affine transformable unmanned ground vehicle (ATUGV) with the capability of safe and aggressive deformation while carrying multiple payloads. The ATUGV is a multi-body system with mobile robots that can be used to power the ATUGV morphable motion, powered cells to enclose the mobile robots, unpowered cells to contain payloads, and a deformable structure to integrate cells through bars and joints. The objective is that all powered and unpowered cells motion can safely track a desired affine transformation, where an affine transformation can be decomposed into translation, rigid body rotation, and deformation. To this end, the paper first uses a deep neural network to structure cell interconnection in such a way that every cell can freely move over the deformation plane, and the entire structure can reconfigurably deform to track a desired affine transformation. Then, the mobile robots, contained by the powered cells and stepper motors, regulating the connections of the powered and unpowered cells, design the proper controls so that all cells safely track the desired affine transformation. The functionality of the proposed ATUGV is validated through hardware experimentation and simulation.

AI.tldr: A proof-of-concept for an affine transformable unmanned ground vehicle (ATUGV) that morphs via a multi-body system of powered/unpowered cells to track an affine transformation, using a deep neural network to structure interconnections and a control scheme to coordinate movement; validated by hardware experiments and simulations.
AI.motivation: Enable safe and aggressive deformation of a ground vehicle while carrying multiple payloads, allowing reconfigurable morphing to perform affine transformations (translation, rotation, and deformation).
AI.method: Model ATUGV as interconnected powered and unpowered cells forming a deformable structure. Use a deep neural network to design cell interconnections so each cell can move freely on the deformation plane and the overall structure can morph to track a target affine transformation. Implement controls via mobile robots (powered cells and stepper motors) to regulate connections and enforce safe tracking of the affine transformation. Validate through hardware experiments and simulations.
AI.result: Demonstrated proof-of-concept: the ATUGV can reconfigure and track a desired affine transformation, with safe deformation and payload handling validated in both hardware experiments and simulations.
AI.conclusion: Feasible concept for affine transformable morphable UGVs; the approach combines neural-guided interconnection design with distributed control to achieve reconfigurable morphing and tracking of affine transformations; further work could optimize performance and scalability.

title: Contactless estimation of continuum displacement and mechanical compressibility from image series using a deep learning based framework
comment: 14 Pages, 8 Figures Note: Supplentary information (ancillary file) attached as .pdf
summary: Contactless and non-invasive estimation of mechanical properties of physical media from optical observations is of interest for manifold engineering and biomedical applications, where direct physical measurements are not possible. Conventional approaches to the assessment of image displacement and non-contact material probing typically rely on time-consuming iterative algorithms for non-rigid image registration and constitutive modelling using discretization and iterative numerical solving techniques, such as Finite Element Method (FEM) and Finite Difference Method (FDM), which are not suitable for high-throughput data processing. Here, we present an efficient deep learning based end-to-end approach for the estimation of continuum displacement and material compressibility directly from the image series. Based on two deep neural networks for image registration and material compressibility estimation, this framework outperforms conventional approaches in terms of efficiency and accuracy. In particular, our experimental results show that the deep learning model trained on a set of reference data can accurately determine the material compressibility even in the presence of substantial local deviations of the mapping predicted by image registration from the reference displacement field. Our findings suggest that the remarkable accuracy of the deep learning end-to-end model originates from its ability to assess higher-order cognitive features, such as the vorticity of the vector field, rather than conventional local features of the image displacement.

AI.tldr: An end-to-end deep learning framework uses two networks to estimate displacement and material compressibility from image series, outperforming traditional iterative registration and FEM/FDM-based approaches, by exploiting higher-order field features like vorticity.
AI.motivation: To enable fast, non-contact estimation of mechanical properties from optical data, circumventing slow non-rigid registration and constitutive modelling required by conventional methods.
AI.method: Two DNNs: a registration network to recover displacement fields from image sequences, and a compressibility estimator that predicts material compressibility from the displacement field / image data. Trained on reference data; demonstrates robustness to local mapping deviations and leverages higher-order features (e.g., vorticity) for accuracy.
AI.result: The DL framework achieves higher efficiency and accuracy than traditional iterative methods; capable of accurate compressibility estimation despite local deviations in the registration; performance attributed to capturing higher-order features rather than local image displacements.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end DL approach enables rapid, non-invasive estimation of continuum displacement and compressibility from imaging data, with potential for high-throughput analyses in engineering and biomedical settings; emphasizes the role of global, higher-order features in predictive accuracy.

title: The Median is Easier than it Looks: Approximation with a Constant-Depth, Linear-Width ReLU Network
summary: We study the approximation of the median of $d$ inputs using ReLU neural networks. We present depth-width tradeoffs under several settings, culminating in a constant-depth, linear-width construction that achieves exponentially small approximation error with respect to the uniform distribution over the unit hypercube. By further establishing a general reduction from the maximum to the median, our results break a barrier suggested by prior work on the maximum function, which indicated that linear width should require depth growing at least as $\log\log d$ to achieve comparable accuracy. Our construction relies on a multi-stage procedure that iteratively eliminates non-central elements while preserving a candidate set around the median. We overcome obstacles that do not arise for the maximum to yield approximation results that are strictly stronger than those previously known for the maximum itself.

AI.tldr: Constant-depth, linear-width ReLU nets approximate the median of d inputs with exponentially small error under the uniform unit-hypercube distribution, via a max-to-median reduction and a multi-stage pruning scheme; this surpasses prior barriers suggesting depth must grow with log log d for linear width.
AI.motivation: Understand efficient neural-network architectures for computing robust statistics; close gaps in depth-width tradeoffs for nonlinear activations; extend max-function results to median.
AI.method: Develop a multi-stage procedure that iteratively eliminates non-central elements while preserving a central candidate around the median; establish a reduction from maximum to median; construct a constant-depth, linear-width ReLU network implementing this procedure.
AI.result: Achieves exponentially small approximation error for the median under the uniform distribution on [0,1]^d with depth O(1) and width O(d); overcomes obstacles specific to the median and yields stronger results than those known for the maximum.
AI.conclusion: Shows that the median can be efficiently approximated by shallow, wide ReLU networks, breaking a barrier suggested by max-function results; provides new techniques for robust statistic computation in neural networks.

title: VERIFY-RL: Verifiable Recursive Decomposition for Reinforcement Learning in Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Training language models to solve complex mathematical problems benefits from curriculum learning progressively training on simpler subproblems. However, existing decomposition methods are often heuristic, offering no guarantees that subproblems are simpler, that solving them aids the parent task, or that their relationships are mathematically grounded. We observe that symbolic differentiation provides a natural structure for verified decomposition: calculus rules explicitly define how expressions reduce to simpler components with provable properties. We introduce Verify-RL, a framework where every parent-child decomposition satisfies three verifiable conditions: strictly decreasing structural complexity, solution containment, and formal rule derivation. Unlike heuristic methods where a significant fraction of decompositions are invalid our properties admit automatic verification through symbolic computation, achieving "verification by construction" Experiments demonstrate that eliminating invalid decompositions yields sizable gains, accuracy on the hardest problems more than doubles from 32% to 68%, with a 40% relative improvement overall.

AI.tldr: A framework (Verify-RL) for curriculum learning in math problem solving that uses verified, calculus-based decompositions to break problems into simpler subproblems with provable properties, improving performance on challenging tasks.
AI.motivation: Current decomposition methods in curriculum learning are heuristic and lack guarantees about subproblem simplicity, usefulness to the parent task, or formal grounding. There is a need for principled, verifiable decompositions to enable reliable learning progress.
AI.method: Leverages symbolic differentiation as the decomposition basis, enforcing three verifiable conditions on every parent-child split: strictly decreasing structural complexity, solution containment, and derivation via formal calculus rules; automatic verification (verification by construction) eliminates invalid decompositions.
AI.result: Eliminating invalid decompositions yields substantial gains: accuracy on the hardest problems more than doubles (32% → 68%), with a 40% relative improvement overall.
AI.conclusion: A verified decomposition framework improves data efficiency and reliability for curriculum-based math problem solving, suggesting broader applicability of formally grounded decomposition strategies for complex reasoning tasks.

title: Global Symmetry and Orthogonal Transformations from Geometrical Moment $n$-tuples
summary: Detecting symmetry is crucial for effective object grasping for several reasons. Recognizing symmetrical features or axes within an object helps in developing efficient grasp strategies, as grasping along these axes typically results in a more stable and balanced grip, thereby facilitating successful manipulation. This paper employs geometrical moments to identify symmetries and estimate orthogonal transformations, including rotations and mirror transformations, for objects centered at the frame origin. It provides distinctive metrics for detecting symmetries and estimating orthogonal transformations, encompassing rotations, reflections, and their combinations. A comprehensive methodology is developed to obtain these functions in n-dimensional space, specifically moment \( n \)-tuples. Extensive validation tests are conducted on both 2D and 3D objects to ensure the robustness and reliability of the proposed approach. The proposed method is also compared to state-of-the-art work using iterative optimization for detecting multiple planes of symmetry. The results indicate that combining our method with the iterative one yields satisfactory outcomes in terms of the number of symmetry planes detected and computation time.

AI.tldr: A geometry-moments framework detects object symmetries and estimates orthogonal transforms (rotations/reflections) in any dimension, validated on 2D/3D objects, and synergizes with iterative optimization to improve symmetry-plane detection with reduced computation time.
AI.motivation: Symmetry is valuable for stable grasping and manipulation; robust detection of symmetry axes/planes and related orthogonal transformations in objects enhances planning and control.
AI.method: Employ geometric moments to identify symmetries and estimate orthogonal transformations (rotations, reflections, and their combinations). Introduce metrics for symmetry detection and for estimating orthogonal transforms. Generalize to n-dimensional space via moment n-tuples. Validate on 2D and 3D objects and compare to state-of-the-art iterative optimization for detecting multiple symmetry planes. Demonstrate that combining the proposed method with the iterative approach yields favorable results.
AI.result: Extensive validation on 2D and 3D objects demonstrates robustness. When combined with the iterative method, the approach achieves satisfactory outcomes in terms of the number of symmetry planes detected and computation time, indicating complementary strengths and potential practical benefit.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides an effective, scalable approach for symmetry detection and orthogonal transformation estimation across dimensions. The hybrid combination with iterative optimization offers improved performance in both accuracy (plane count) and speed, supporting reliable grasp planning and manipulation.

title: Bidirectional Reward-Guided Diffusion for Real-World Image Super-Resolution
summary: Diffusion-based super-resolution can synthesize rich details, but models trained on synthetic paired data often fail on real-world LR images due to distribution shifts. We propose Bird-SR, a bidirectional reward-guided diffusion framework that formulates super-resolution as trajectory-level preference optimization via reward feedback learning (ReFL), jointly leveraging synthetic LR-HR pairs and real-world LR images. For structural fidelity easily affected in ReFL, the model is directly optimized on synthetic pairs at early diffusion steps, which also facilitates structure preservation for real-world inputs under smaller distribution gap in structure levels. For perceptual enhancement, quality-guided rewards are applied at later sampling steps to both synthetic and real LR images. To mitigate reward hacking, the rewards for synthetic results are formulated in a relative advantage space bounded by their clean counterparts, while real-world optimization is regularized via a semantic alignment constraint. Furthermore, to balance structural and perceptual learning, we adopt a dynamic fidelity-perception weighting strategy that emphasizes structure preservation at early stages and progressively shifts focus toward perceptual optimization at later diffusion steps. Extensive experiments on real-world SR benchmarks demonstrate that Bird-SR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in perceptual quality while preserving structural consistency, validating its effectiveness for real-world super-resolution.

AI.tldr: Bird-SR introduces a bidirectional reward-guided diffusion framework for real-world super-resolution that combines synthetic paired data and real LR images via reward-feedback learning (ReFL); it uses early diffusion steps to preserve structure on synthetic pairs and later steps with quality-guided rewards for perceptual enhancement on both synthetic and real data; mitigates reward hacking with relative-advantage rewards and a semantic-alignment regularizer; employs a dynamic fidelity-perception weighting to balance structure and perceptual learning; yields state-of-the-art perceptual quality with maintained structural fidelity on real-world SR benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The sim-to-real gap in SR degrades performance on real LR images. There is a need to preserve structural fidelity while enhancing perceptual quality, and to utilize real-world data without paired HR targets.
AI.method: A diffusion-based SR framework (Bird-SR) that optimizes trajectories via reward feedback learning (ReFL). Early diffusion steps optimize on synthetic LR-HR pairs to preserve structure; later steps apply quality-guided rewards to both synthetic and real LR images for perceptual enhancement. Reward design includes relative-advantage space for synthetic results bounded by clean references to avoid reward hacking, and a semantic-alignment constraint for real-world optimization. A dynamic fidelity-perception weight schedule shifts emphasis from structure to perceptual learning across diffusion steps.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on real-world SR benchmarks show Bird-SR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in perceptual quality while preserving structural consistency.
AI.conclusion: Bird-SR effectively bridges the sim-to-real gap in SR by integrating synthetic and real data through a reward-guided diffusion framework with thoughtful reward design, normalization, and a dynamic weighting strategy, achieving robust real-world SR performance.

title: SpecAttn: Co-Designing Sparse Attention with Self-Speculative Decoding
summary: Long-context large language model (LLM) inference has become the norm for today's AI applications. However, it is severely bottlenecked by the increasing memory demands of its KV cache. Previous works have shown that self-speculative decoding with sparse attention, where tokens are drafted using a subset of the KV cache and verified in parallel with full KV cache, speeds up inference in a lossless way. However, this approach relies on standalone KV selection algorithms to select the KV entries used for drafting and overlooks that the criticality of each KV entry is inherently computed during verification. In this paper, we propose SpecAttn, a self-speculative decoding method with verification-guided sparse attention. SpecAttn identifies critical KV entries as a byproduct of verification and only loads these entries when drafting subsequent tokens. This not only improves draft token acceptance rate but also incurs low KV selection overhead, thereby improving decoding throughput. SpecAttn achieves 2.81$\times$ higher throughput over vanilla auto-regressive decoding and 1.29$\times$ improvement over state-of-the-art sparsity-based self-speculative decoding methods.

AI.tldr: SpecAttn is a verification-guided sparse attention method for self-speculative decoding in long-context LLM inference. It loads only KV cache entries identified as critical during verification, improving draft throughput and reducing KV selection overhead.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLM inference is memory-bottlenecked by the KV cache. Prior self-speculative decoding relies on standalone KV selection to draft tokens, but criticality of KV entries emerges only during verification, not beforehand, causing inefficiency.
AI.method: Introduce SpecAttn: during drafting, use sparse attention over a subset of KV. Run verification in parallel to identify which KV entries are critical; load only these entries for drafting subsequent tokens, turning KV selection into a byproduct of verification.
AI.result: Achieves 2.81x throughput vs vanilla autoregressive decoding and 1.29x throughput vs state-of-the-art sparsity-based self-speculative decoding methods.
AI.conclusion: Verification-guided loading of critical KV entries reduces KV overhead and boosts decoding throughput while maintaining correctness, due to higher draft acceptance rates and lower selection costs.

title: M2A: Multimodal Memory Agent with Dual-Layer Hybrid Memory for Long-Term Personalized Interactions
summary: This work addresses the challenge of personalized question answering in long-term human-machine interactions: when conversational history spans weeks or months and exceeds the context window, existing personalization mechanisms struggle to continuously absorb and leverage users' incremental concepts, aliases, and preferences. Current personalized multimodal models are predominantly static-concepts are fixed at initialization and cannot evolve during interactions. We propose M2A, an agentic dual-layer hybrid memory system that maintains personalized multimodal information through online updates. The system employs two collaborative agents: ChatAgent manages user interactions and autonomously decides when to query or update memory, while MemoryManager breaks down memory requests from ChatAgent into detailed operations on the dual-layer memory bank, which couples a RawMessageStore (immutable conversation log) with a SemanticMemoryStore (high-level observations), providing memories at different granularities. In addition, we develop a reusable data synthesis pipeline that injects concept-grounded sessions from Yo'LLaVA and MC-LLaVA into LoCoMo long conversations while preserving temporal coherence. Experiments show that M2A significantly outperforms baselines, demonstrating that transforming personalization from one-shot configuration to a co-evolving memory mechanism provides a viable path for high-quality individualized responses in long-term multimodal interactions. The code is available at https://github.com/Little-Fridge/M2A.

AI.tldr: M2A introduces a dual-layer memory and agentic control to enable co-evolving personalization in long-term multimodal QA, using an immutable RawMessageStore and a high-level SemanticMemoryStore, with a memory-management workflow and a data-synthesis pipeline to inject concept-grounded sessions, achieving superior performance over baselines.
AI.motivation: Long-term human–machine interactions accumulate user concepts, aliases, and preferences beyond fixed context windows, requiring online, evolving memory mechanisms rather than static initializations.
AI.method: Dual-layer memory architecture: RawMessageStore (immutable conversation logs) and SemanticMemoryStore (high-level observations). ChatAgent handles user interactions and decides when to query/update memory; MemoryManager translates ChatAgent requests into detailed memory operations on both stores. A data-synthesis pipeline injects concept-grounded sessions (Yo'LLaVA, MC-LLaVA) into LoCoMo long conversations while preserving temporal coherence.
AI.result: Empirical results show M2A outperforms baselines on long-term multimodal personalization tasks, demonstrating that transforming personalization from a one-shot configuration to a co-evolving memory mechanism is effective. Code is released at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: Co-evolving, memory-driven personalization is a viable path for high-quality individualized responses in long-term multimodal interactions; the proposed dual-layer memory with agentic control enables ongoing absorption of user concepts and preferences.

title: CoLF: Learning Consistent Leader-Follower Policies for Vision-Language-Guided Multi-Robot Cooperative Transport
summary: In this study, we address vision-language-guided multi-robot cooperative transport, where each robot grounds natural-language instructions from onboard camera observations. A key challenge in this decentralized setting is perceptual misalignment across robots, where viewpoint differences and language ambiguity can yield inconsistent interpretations and degrade cooperative transport. To mitigate this problem, we adopt a dependent leader-follower design, where one robot serves as the leader and the other as the follower. Although such a leader-follower structure appears straightforward, learning with independent and symmetric agents often yields symmetric or unstable behaviors without explicit inductive biases. To address this challenge, we propose Consistent Leader-Follower (CoLF), a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework for stable leader-follower role differentiation. CoLF consists of two key components: (1) an asymmetric policy design that induces leader-follower role differentiation, and (2) a mutual-information-based training objective that maximizes a variational lower bound, encouraging the follower to predict the leader's action from its local observation. The leader and follower policies are jointly optimized under the centralized training and decentralized execution (CTDE) framework to balance task execution and consistent cooperative behaviors. We validate CoLF in both simulation and real-robot experiments using two quadruped robots. The demonstration video is available at https://sites.google.com/view/colf/.

AI.tldr: CoLF introduces a dependent leader–follower MARL framework with asymmetric policies and a mutual-information objective to achieve stable leader-follower role differentiation in vision–language-guided multi-robot cooperative transport under centralized training and decentralized execution (CTDE).
AI.motivation: In decentralized vision-language grounded multi-robot tasks, perceptual misalignment across robots (due to viewpoint and language ambiguity) causes inconsistent interpretations and degraded cooperation; a stable leader–follower structure is desirable for robust coordination.
AI.method: Propose Consistent Leader-Follower (CoLF) with two components: (1) asymmetric policy design to induce leader–follower differentiation; (2) mutual-information-based training objective that maximizes a variational lower bound, encouraging the follower to predict the leader's action from its local observation. Policies are trained under CTDE to balance task performance and consistent cooperation; validated in simulation and real-robot experiments with two quadruped robots.
AI.result: CoLF yields stable leader–follower differentiation and improved cooperative transport consistency in both simulation and real-world tests; demonstration video available.
AI.conclusion: CoLF provides a principled MARL framework for achieving stable leadership roles and consistent coordination in vision-language grounded multi-robot tasks, with practical validation on real robots.

title: MosaicThinker: On-Device Visual Spatial Reasoning for Embodied AI via Iterative Construction of Space Representation
summary: When embodied AI is expanding from traditional object detection and recognition to more advanced tasks of robot manipulation and actuation planning, visual spatial reasoning from the video inputs is necessary to perceive the spatial relationships of objects and guide device actions. However, existing visual language models (VLMs) have very weak capabilities in spatial reasoning due to the lack of knowledge about 3D spatial information, especially when the reasoning task involve complex spatial relations across multiple video frames. In this paper, we present a new inference-time computing technique for on-device embodied AI, namely \emph{MosaicThinker}, which enhances the on-device small VLM's spatial reasoning capabilities on difficult cross-frame reasoning tasks. Our basic idea is to integrate fragmented spatial information from multiple frames into a unified space representation of global semantic map, and further guide the VLM's spatial reasoning over the semantic map via a visual prompt. Experiment results show that our technique can greatly enhance the accuracy of cross-frame spatial reasoning on resource-constrained embodied AI devices, over reasoning tasks with diverse types and complexities.

AI.tldr: MosaicThinker is an inference-time technique that enhances cross-frame spatial reasoning for on-device embodied AI by fusing fragmented spatial cues from multiple frames into a global semantic map and guiding a small visual-language model (VLM) with a visual prompt, yielding substantial accuracy gains under resource constraints.
AI.motivation: VLMs lack robust 3D spatial understanding, hindering cross-frame reasoning needed for manipulation and planning on embodied agents. On-device inference imposes latency and memory limits, necessitating lightweight yet effective spatial reasoning methods.
AI.method: Proposes an inference-time computing pipeline (MosaicThinker) that collects and fragments spatial information from multiple video frames, fuses it into a unified global semantic map, and uses a visual prompt to steer the VLM's spatial reasoning over this semantic map. Designed for resource-constrained on-device deployment to improve cross-frame reasoning.
AI.result: Empirical results show significant improvements in cross-frame spatial reasoning accuracy on resource-constrained embodied AI devices across tasks with varying types and complexities.
AI.conclusion: MosaicThinker demonstrates that on-device spatial reasoning can be substantially boosted by constructing a global semantic representation from multi-frame data and guiding VLM reasoning via prompts, enabling more reliable manipulation and actuation planning on embedded platforms. Future work may address scalability, robustness to occlusion, and broader task coverage.

title: Fault-Tolerant Evaluation for Sample-Efficient Model Performance Estimators
summary: In the era of Model-as-a-Service, organizations increasingly rely on third-party AI models for rapid deployment. However, the dynamic nature of emerging AI applications, the continual introduction of new datasets, and the growing number of models claiming superior performance make efficient and reliable validation of model services increasingly challenging. This motivates the development of sample-efficient performance estimators, which aim to estimate model performance by strategically selecting instances for labeling, thereby reducing annotation cost. Yet existing evaluation approaches often fail in low-variance settings: RMSE conflates bias and variance, masking persistent bias when variance is small, while p-value based tests become hypersensitive, rejecting adequate estimators for negligible deviations. To address this, we propose a fault-tolerant evaluation framework that integrates bias and variance considerations within an adjustable tolerance level ${\varepsilon}$, enabling the evaluation of performance estimators within practically acceptable error margins. We theoretically show that proper calibration of ${\varepsilon}$ ensures reliable evaluation across different variance regimes, and we further propose an algorithm that automatically optimizes and selects ${\varepsilon}$. Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that our framework provides comprehensive and actionable insights into estimator behavior.

AI.tldr: Proposes a fault-tolerant evaluation framework for sample-efficient estimators, using an adjustable tolerance ε to jointly account for bias and variance, with an automatic ε-selection algorithm; validated on real datasets.
AI.motivation: As organizations rely on third-party AI models, eval in low-variance settings is challenging: RMSE conflates bias and variance, and p-values are too sensitive. There is a need for reliable, sample-efficient performance evaluation under practical error margins.
AI.method: Introduce a bias-variance aware evaluation framework within an adjustable tolerance ε. Provide theoretical calibration to ensure reliability across variance regimes and develop an algorithm that automatically optimizes and selects ε.
AI.result: The framework yields comprehensive, actionable insights into estimator behavior, demonstrated on real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: Proper ε calibration enables reliable evaluation across variance regimes, and automatic ε selection enhances practical deployment of performance estimators.

title: SleepMaMi: A Universal Sleep Foundation Model for Integrating Macro- and Micro-structures
summary: While the shift toward unified foundation models has revolutionized many deep learning domains, sleep medicine remains largely restricted to task-specific models that focus on localized micro-structure features. These approaches often neglect the rich, multi-modal context of Polysomnography (PSG) and fail to capture the global macro-structure of a full night's sleep. To address this, we introduce SleepMaMi , a Sleep Foundation Model engineered to master both hour-long sleep architectures and fine-grained signal morphologies. Our framework utilizes a hierarchical dual-encoder design: a Macro-Encoder to model full-night temporal dependencies and a Micro-Encoder to capture short-term characteristics from biosignals. Macro-Encoder is trained via Demographic-Guided Contrastive Learning, which aligns overnight sleep patterns with objective subject metadata, such as age, sex and BMI to refine global representations.
