Micro-Encoder is optimized via a hybrid Masked Autoencoder (MAE) and multi-modal contrastive objective. Pre-trained on a massive corpus of $>$20,000 PSG recordings (158K hours),SleepMaMi outperforms existing foundation models across a diverse suite of downstream tasks, demonstrating superior generalizability and label-efficient adaptation for clinical sleep analysis.

AI.tldr: SleepMaMi is a hierarchical sleep foundation model that combines a Macro-Encoder for full-night sleep architecture with a Micro-Encoder for short-term biosignal morphology. It is pre-trained on >20,000 PSG recordings (~158k hours) using Demographic-Guided Contrastive Learning for macro representations and a MAE-plus-multi-modal contrastive objective for micro representations, achieving superior performance and label-efficient adaptation across diverse sleep-analysis tasks.
AI.motivation: Current sleep models are largely task-specific and focused on local features, neglecting global sleep architecture and multi-modal context. There is a need for a unified foundation model that captures both macro (overnight) and micro (signal-level) structure and leverages subject metadata.
AI.method: A hierarchical dual-encoder architecture with a Macro-Encoder modeling full-night temporal dependencies trained via Demographic-Guided Contrastive Learning to align representations with metadata (age, sex, BMI). A Micro-Encoder capturing short-term signal morphology trained with a hybrid Masked Autoencoder (MAE) objective and multi-modal contrastive loss. Pre-training uses a large PSG corpus (>20k recordings, ~158k hours).
AI.result: SleepMaMi outperforms existing foundation models on a diverse set of downstream tasks, demonstrating strong generalizability and label-efficient adaptation for clinical sleep analysis.
AI.conclusion: The approach advances sleep AI by delivering a scalable, multi-level foundation model that integrates macro- and micro-structural knowledge, enabling robust, data-efficient clinical sleep analysis.

title: RLinf-USER: A Unified and Extensible System for Real-World Online Policy Learning in Embodied AI
summary: Online policy learning directly in the physical world is a promising yet challenging direction for embodied intelligence. Unlike simulation, real-world systems cannot be arbitrarily accelerated, cheaply reset, or massively replicated, which makes scalable data collection, heterogeneous deployment, and long-horizon effective training difficult. These challenges suggest that real-world policy learning is not only an algorithmic issue but fundamentally a systems problem. We present USER, a Unified and extensible SystEm for Real-world online policy learning. USER treats physical robots as first-class hardware resources alongside GPUs through a unified hardware abstraction layer, enabling automatic discovery, management, and scheduling of heterogeneous robots. To address cloud-edge communication, USER introduces an adaptive communication plane with tunneling-based networking, distributed data channels for traffic localization, and streaming-multiprocessor-aware weight synchronization to regulate GPU-side overhead. On top of this infrastructure, USER organizes learning as a fully asynchronous framework with a persistent, cache-aware buffer, enabling efficient long-horizon experiments with robust crash recovery and reuse of historical data. In addition, USER provides extensible abstractions for rewards, algorithms, and policies, supporting online imitation or reinforcement learning of CNN/MLP, generative policies, and large vision-language-action (VLA) models within a unified pipeline. Results in both simulation and the real world show that USER enables multi-robot coordination, heterogeneous manipulators, edge-cloud collaboration with large models, and long-running asynchronous training, offering a unified and extensible systems foundation for real-world online policy learning.

AI.tldr: A unified, extensible platform (USER) for real-world online policy learning that treats robots as first-class hardware resources, with adaptive networking, streaming weight synchronization, and asynchronous long-horizon RL/IL supporting CNN/MLP, generative policies, and large VLA models; demonstrated on multi-robot and edge/cloud setups.
AI.motivation: Real-world policy learning is constrained by non-simulated environments: no fast-forward, few resets, limited replication. A systems-level solution is needed to manage hardware heterogeneity, communication bottlenecks, data reuse, and long-horizon training.
AI.method: Introduce USER with (1) a hardware abstraction layer for automatic discovery and management of heterogeneous robots; (2) an adaptive communication plane featuring tunneling-based networking, traffic-localized channels, and streaming-multiprocessor-aware weight synchronization; (3) a fully asynchronous learning framework with a persistent, cache-aware buffer, robust crash recovery, and data reuse; (4) extensible abstractions for rewards, algorithms, and policies enabling online imitation or reinforcement learning of CNN/MLP, generative policies, and large VLA models in a unified pipeline.
AI.result: Simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate multi-robot coordination, heterogeneous manipulators, edge-cloud collaboration with large models, and long-running asynchronous training, validating USER as a unified, extensible foundation for real-world online policy learning.
AI.conclusion: USER provides a cohesive systems solution that addresses hardware heterogeneity, networking, data management, and long-horizon training in real-world online policy learning, enabling scalable multi-robot deployments and large-model workflows.

title: WorldEdit: Towards Open-World Image Editing with a Knowledge-Informed Benchmark
summary: Recent advances in image editing models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in executing explicit instructions, such as attribute manipulation, style transfer, and pose synthesis. However, these models often face challenges when dealing with implicit editing instructions, which describe the cause of a visual change without explicitly detailing the resulting outcome. These limitations arise because existing models rely on uniform editing strategies that are not equipped to handle the complex world knowledge and reasoning required for implicit instructions. To address this gap, we introduce \textbf{WorldEdit}, a dataset specifically designed to enable world-driven image editing. WorldEdit consists of high-quality editing samples, guided by paraphrased instructions that align with real-world causal logic. Furthermore, we provide \textbf{WorldEdit-Test} for evaluating the existing model's performance on causal editing scenarios. With WorldEdit, we use a two-stage training framework for fine-tuning models like Bagel, integrating with a causal verification reward. Our results show that the proposed dataset and methods significantly narrow the gap with GPT-4o and Nano-Banana, demonstrating competitive performance not only in instruction following but also in knowledge plausibility, where many open-source systems typically struggle.

AI.tldr: WorldEdit is a dataset and evaluation suite for world-driven image editing that handles implicit, causally-grounded instructions; paired with a two-stage Bagel fine-tuning method and a causal verification reward to bridge performance toward GPT-4o and Nano-Banana, including knowledge plausibility.
AI.motivation: Current image-editing models struggle with implicit instructions requiring real-world causal reasoning. A dataset and evaluation framework that anchors edits to causal knowledge can improve instruction comprehension and knowledge plausibility, especially for open-source models.
AI.method: Construct WorldEdit with high-quality editing samples guided by paraphrased, causally aligned instructions. Create WorldEdit-Test for causal editing evaluation. Fine-tune models (e.g., Bagel) in two stages and integrate a causal verification reward to encourage world-consistent edits.
AI.result: The approach narrows the gap with GPT-4o and Nano-Banana and achieves competitive performance in instruction following and knowledge plausibility, addressing a common weakness of open-source systems.
AI.conclusion: WorldEdit enables world-driven image editing by leveraging causal-aligned data and a two-stage fine-tuning framework with a causal verification objective, providing a principled benchmark for evaluating implicit-edit capabilities.

title: Cerebellar-Inspired Residual Control for Fault Recovery: From Inference-Time Adaptation to Structural Consolidation
summary: Robotic policies deployed in real-world environments often encounter post-training faults, where retraining, exploration, or system identification are impractical. We introduce an inference-time, cerebellar-inspired residual control framework that augments a frozen reinforcement learning policy with online corrective actions, enabling fault recovery without modifying base policy parameters. The framework instantiates core cerebellar principles, including high-dimensional pattern separation via fixed feature expansion, parallel microzone-style residual pathways, and local error-driven plasticity with excitatory and inhibitory eligibility traces operating at distinct time scales. These mechanisms enable fast, localized correction under post-training disturbances while avoiding destabilizing global policy updates. A conservative, performance-driven meta-adaptation regulates residual authority and plasticity, preserving nominal behavior and suppressing unnecessary intervention. Experiments on MuJoCo benchmarks under actuator, dynamic, and environmental perturbations show improvements of up to $+66\%$ on \texttt{HalfCheetah-v5} and $+53\%$ on \texttt{Humanoid-v5} under moderate faults, with graceful degradation under severe shifts and complementary robustness from consolidating persistent residual corrections into policy parameters.

AI.tldr: A cerebellar-inspired residual controller augments a frozen RL policy at inference to recover from post-training faults, using fixed feature expansion, parallel microzone residuals, and local error-driven plasticity with excitatory/inhibitory traces; a meta-adaptation gates residual influence. Demonstrates substantial gains under moderate faults on MuJoCo benchmarks, with graceful degradation under severe shifts and potential to consolidate residuals into the base policy.
AI.motivation: Robots deployed with pretrained policies often encounter faults not solvable by retraining; need an inference-time mechanism to correct behavior without altering the original policy parameters.
AI.method: Introduce a modular residual-control framework inspired by cerebellar circuitry: fixed feature expansion for high-dimensional separation, parallel microzone-like residual pathways, local, time-scale-heterogeneous error-driven plasticity (excitatory/inhibitory eligibility traces), and a conservative meta-adaptation to regulate residual authority; integrate with frozen RL policy without modifying base parameters; optionally consolidate residuals into the policy parameters for sustained robustness.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on MuJoCo under actuator, dynamic, and environmental perturbations shows up to +66% improvement on HalfCheetah-v5 and +53% on Humanoid-v5 under moderate faults; graceful degradation under severe shifts; robustness enhanced by consolidating persistent residuals into policy parameters.
AI.conclusion: Inference-time, cerebellar-inspired residual control can provide fast, localized fault correction without destabilizing the base policy, improving fault-tolerance; persistent corrections can be amalgamated into the base policy to strengthen long-term robustness.

title: Efficient Table Retrieval and Understanding with Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Tabular data is frequently captured in image form across a wide range of real-world scenarios such as financial reports, handwritten records, and document scans. These visual representations pose unique challenges for machine understanding, as they combine both structural and visual complexities. While recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) show promising results in table understanding, they typically assume the relevant table is readily available. However, a more practical scenario involves identifying and reasoning over relevant tables from large-scale collections to answer user queries. To address this gap, we propose TabRAG, a framework that enables MLLMs to answer queries over large collections of table images. Our approach first retrieves candidate tables using jointly trained visual-text foundation models, then leverages MLLMs to perform fine-grained reranking of these candidates, and finally employs MLLMs to reason over the selected tables for answer generation. Through extensive experiments on a newly constructed dataset comprising 88,161 training and 9,819 testing samples across 8 benchmarks with 48,504 unique tables, we demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms existing methods by 7.0% in retrieval recall and 6.1% in answer accuracy, offering a practical solution for real-world table understanding tasks.

AI.tldr: TabRAG enables retrieval, reranking, and reasoning over large collections of table images for multimodal LLMs, using a three-stage pipeline that improves retrieval recall and answer accuracy over baselines on a large-scale dataset.
AI.motivation: To address the practical need of identifying and reasoning about relevant tables from large image-based collections, rather than assuming the table is pre-available to the model.
AI.method: A three-stage pipeline: (1) retrieve candidate tables via jointly trained visual-text foundation models; (2) fine-grained reranking of candidates using MLLMs; (3) reasoning over the selected tables with MLLMs to generate answers. Evaluation on a large constructed dataset (88,161 train, 9,819 test; 48,504 unique tables) across 8 benchmarks.
AI.result: Significant improvements over baselines: 7.0% higher retrieval recall and 6.1% higher answer accuracy.
AI.conclusion: TabRAG provides a practical, scalable solution for real-world table understanding tasks and demonstrates the viability of combining retrieval, reranking, and reasoning stages for multimodal table QA.

title: Recurrent-Depth VLA: Implicit Test-Time Compute Scaling of Vision-Language-Action Models via Latent Iterative Reasoning
summary: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models rely on fixed computational depth, expending the same amount of compute on simple adjustments and complex multi-step manipulation. While Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting enables variable computation, it scales memory linearly and is ill-suited for continuous action spaces. We introduce Recurrent-Depth VLA (RD-VLA), an architecture that achieves computational adaptivity via latent iterative refinement rather than explicit token generation. RD-VLA employs a recurrent, weight-tied action head that supports arbitrary inference depth with a constant memory footprint. The model is trained using truncated backpropagation through time (TBPTT) to efficiently supervise the refinement process. At inference, RD-VLA dynamically allocates compute using an adaptive stopping criterion based on latent convergence. Experiments on challenging manipulation tasks show that recurrent depth is critical: tasks that fail entirely (0 percent success) with single-iteration inference exceed 90 percent success with four iterations, while simpler tasks saturate rapidly. RD-VLA provides a scalable path to test-time compute in robotics, replacing token-based reasoning with latent reasoning to achieve constant memory usage and up to 80x inference speedup over prior reasoning-based VLA models. Project page: https://rd-vla.github.io/

AI.tldr: RD-VLA introduces latent-iterative refinement with a recurrent, weight-tied action head to enable adaptive compute depth in vision-language-action models, maintaining constant memory and enabling large speedups.
AI.motivation: Current VLA models use fixed compute depth; CoT prompts scale memory linearly and are ill-suited for continuous action spaces. There is a need for variable compute with constant memory, especially for robotics and continuous actions.
AI.method: A recurrent-depth VLA architecture performs latent iterative refinement with a weight-tied action head. Training uses truncated backpropagation through time (TBPTT). Inference uses an adaptive stopping criterion based on latent convergence to allocate compute dynamically, yielding a constant memory footprint regardless of iterations.
AI.result: Experiments show that enabling multiple iterations dramatically improves success on difficult tasks (from 0% with one iteration to >90% with four iterations). Simpler tasks converge quickly. RD-VLA achieves up to 80x inference speedup and maintains constant memory usage, outperforming prior reasoning-based VLA models.
AI.conclusion: RD-VLA provides a scalable, compute-adaptive approach for robotics, replacing token-based reasoning with latent reasoning to obtain constant memory and significant speedups, enabling more flexible deployment in real-time manipulation tasks.

title: TLC-Plan: A Two-Level Codebook Based Network for End-to-End Vector Floorplan Generation
summary: Automated floorplan generation aims to improve design quality, architectural efficiency, and sustainability by jointly modeling global spatial organization and precise geometric detail. However, existing approaches operate in raster space and rely on post hoc vectorization, which introduces structural inconsistencies and hinders end-to-end learning. Motivated by compositional spatial reasoning, we propose TLC-Plan, a hierarchical generative model that directly synthesizes vector floorplans from input boundaries, aligning with human architectural workflows based on modular and reusable patterns. TLC-Plan employs a two-level VQ-VAE to encode global layouts as semantically labeled room bounding boxes and to refine local geometries using polygon-level codes. This hierarchy is unified in a CodeTree representation, while an autoregressive transformer samples codes conditioned on the boundary to generate diverse and topologically valid designs, without requiring explicit room topology or dimensional priors. Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance on RPLAN dataset (FID = 1.84, MSE = 2.06) and leading results on LIFULL dataset. The proposed framework advances constraint-aware and scalable vector floorplan generation for real-world architectural applications. Source code and trained models are released at https://github.com/rosolose/TLC-PLAN.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical two-level VQ-VAE with CodeTree and autoregressive transformer to generate vector floorplans directly from input boundaries, improving end-to-end vector generation and consistency over raster-to-vector approaches.
AI.motivation: To bridge raster-based floorplan generation and vector-level architectural workflows by enabling end-to-end, constraint-aware, vectorized design generation that aligns with modular, reusable patterns in architecture.
AI.method: A two-level VQ-VAE encodes global layouts as semantically labeled room bounding boxes and refines local geometries with polygon-level codes. These are unified in a CodeTree representation and sampled by an autoregressive transformer conditioned on input boundaries to produce diverse, topologically valid vector floorplans without requiring explicit topology or dimensional priors.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on the RPLAN dataset with FID = 1.84 and MSE = 2.06; leading results on the LIFULL dataset, demonstrating improved vector floorplan generation and constraint awareness.
AI.conclusion: TLC-Plan advances end-to-end, constraint-aware vector floorplan generation, aligning with architectural workflows; code and models are released for reproducibility.

title: ArcMark: Multi-bit LLM Watermark via Optimal Transport
summary: Watermarking is an important tool for promoting the responsible use of language models (LMs). Existing watermarks insert a signal into generated tokens that either flags LM-generated text (zero-bit watermarking) or encodes more complex messages (multi-bit watermarking). Though a number of recent multi-bit watermarks insert several bits into text without perturbing average next-token predictions, they largely extend design principles from the zero-bit setting, such as encoding a single bit per token. Notably, the information-theoretic capacity of multi-bit watermarking -- the maximum number of bits per token that can be inserted and detected without changing average next-token predictions -- has remained unknown. We address this gap by deriving the first capacity characterization of multi-bit watermarks. Our results inform the design of ArcMark: a new watermark construction based on coding-theoretic principles that, under certain assumptions, achieves the capacity of the multi-bit watermark channel. In practice, ArcMark outperforms competing multi-bit watermarks in terms of bit rate per token and detection accuracy. Our work demonstrates that LM watermarking is fundamentally a channel coding problem, paving the way for principled coding-theoretic approaches to watermark design.

AI.tldr: First capacity characterization of multi-bit LM watermarking; ArcMark, a coding-theoretic watermarking scheme, reaches capacity under certain assumptions and outperforms existing multi-bit watermarks in bit rate and detection; frames LM watermarking as a channel coding problem.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental limits of multi-bit LM watermarking (beyond the zero-bit setting) and to enable principled, high-rate watermark designs.
AI.method: Derive the capacity of the multi-bit watermark channel; formulate ArcMark based on coding theory principles; analyze under specific assumptions; compare to existing watermark schemes in rate and detection accuracy.
AI.result: Provided the first capacity characterization for multi-bit watermarking; ArcMark achieves (under assumptions) the channel capacity and outperforms competing schemes in rate and detection performance.
AI.conclusion: LM watermarking is a channel coding problem; coding-theoretic designs like ArcMark can achieve capacity and improve practical performance, suggesting a principled design paradigm for watermarking.

title: ONTrust: A Reference Ontology of Trust
summary: Trust has stood out more than ever in the light of recent innovations. Some examples are advances in artificial intelligence that make machines more and more humanlike, and the introduction of decentralized technologies (e.g. blockchains), which creates new forms of (decentralized) trust. These new developments have the potential to improve the provision of products and services, as well as to contribute to individual and collective well-being. However, their adoption depends largely on trust. In order to build trustworthy systems, along with defining laws, regulations and proper governance models for new forms of trust, it is necessary to properly conceptualize trust, so that it can be understood both by humans and machines. This paper is the culmination of a long-term research program of providing a solid ontological foundation on trust, by creating reference conceptual models to support information modeling, automated reasoning, information integration and semantic interoperability tasks. To address this, a Reference Ontology of Trust (ONTrust) was developed, grounded on the Unified Foundational Ontology and specified in OntoUML, which has been applied in several initiatives, to demonstrate, for example, how it can be used for conceptual modeling and enterprise architecture design, for language evaluation and (re)design, for trust management, for requirements engineering, and for trustworthy artificial intelligence (AI) in the context of affective Human-AI teaming. ONTrust formally characterizes the concept of trust and its different types, describes the different factors that can influence trust, as well as explains how risk emerges from trust relations. To illustrate the working of ONTrust, the ontology is applied to model two case studies extracted from the literature.

AI.tldr: ONTrust is a formal reference ontology for trust, grounded in the Unified Foundational Ontology and specified in OntoUML, with applications across modeling, governance, and trustworthy AI; validated via two case studies.
AI.motivation: To enable consistent conceptualization of trust for humans and machines, supporting information modeling, reasoning, interoperability, and governance in AI and decentralized technologies.
AI.method: Developed ONTrust grounded on the Unified Foundational Ontology; specified in OntoUML; applied to multiple initiatives including enterprise architecture design, language evaluation/re-design, trust management, requirements engineering, and trustworthy AI; illustrated with two literature-derived case studies.
AI.result: ONTrust formally characterizes trust, its types, factors influencing trust, and the emergence of risk from trust relations; demonstrates applicability through two case studies.
AI.conclusion: ONTrust provides a solid ontological foundation for trust, enabling modeling, automated reasoning, risk assessment, and semantic interoperability, with broad applicability to trustworthy AI and governance contexts.

title: System-Level Error Propagation and Tail-Risk Amplification in Reference-Based Robotic Navigation
summary: Image guided robotic navigation systems often rely on reference based geometric perception pipelines, where accurate spatial mapping is established through multi stage estimation processes. In biplanar X ray guided navigation, such pipelines are widely used due to their real time capability and geometric interpretability. However, navigation reliability can be constrained by an overlooked system level failure mechanism in which installation induced structural perturbations introduced at the perception stage are progressively amplified along the perception reconstruction execution chain and dominate execution level error and tail risk behavior. This paper investigates this mechanism from a system level perspective and presents a unified error propagation modeling framework that characterizes how installation induced structural perturbations propagate and couple with pixel level observation noise through biplanar imaging, projection matrix estimation, triangulation, and coordinate mapping. Using first order analytic uncertainty propagation and Monte Carlo simulations, we analyze dominant sensitivity channels and quantify worst case error behavior beyond mean accuracy metrics. The results show that rotational installation error is a primary driver of system level error amplification, while translational misalignment of comparable magnitude plays a secondary role under typical biplanar geometries. Real biplanar X ray bench top experiments further confirm that the predicted amplification trends persist under realistic imaging conditions. These findings reveal a broader structural limitation of reference based multi stage geometric perception pipelines and provide a framework for system level reliability analysis and risk aware design in safety critical robotic navigation systems.

AI.tldr: A unified framework to model how installation-induced perturbations propagate through biplanar X-ray perception pipelines, showing rotational errors dominate system-level amplification and tail risk, validated by simulations and bench-top experiments.
AI.motivation: Address an overlooked system-level failure mechanism in multi-stage geometric perception pipelines: installation perturbations at perception stage can be amplified along the perception–execution chain, reducing reliability in safety-critical robotic navigation.
AI.method: Combine first-order analytic uncertainty propagation with Monte Carlo simulations to trace how installation perturbations couple with pixel-noise across biplanar imaging, projection matrix estimation, triangulation, and coordinate mapping; supported by bench-top experiments under realistic imaging.
AI.result: Rotational installation error is the primary driver of system-level error amplification; translational misalignment of comparable magnitude plays a secondary role under typical biplanar geometries; amplification trends persist in realistic imaging conditions as shown by bench-top experiments; provides a framework for system-level reliability analysis and risk-aware design.
AI.conclusion: Reveals a structural limitation of reference-based multi-stage geometric perception pipelines and offers a framework for system-level reliability analysis and risk-aware design in safety-critical robotic navigation systems.

title: Zero-Shot UAV Navigation in Forests via Relightable 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: UAV navigation in unstructured outdoor environments using passive monocular vision is hindered by the substantial visual domain gap between simulation and reality. While 3D Gaussian Splatting enables photorealistic scene reconstruction from real-world data, existing methods inherently couple static lighting with geometry, severely limiting policy generalization to dynamic real-world illumination. In this paper, we propose a novel end-to-end reinforcement learning framework designed for effective zero-shot transfer to unstructured outdoors. Within a high-fidelity simulation grounded in real-world data, our policy is trained to map raw monocular RGB observations directly to continuous control commands. To overcome photometric limitations, we introduce Relightable 3D Gaussian Splatting, which decomposes scene components to enable explicit, physically grounded editing of environmental lighting within the neural representation. By augmenting training with diverse synthesized lighting conditions ranging from strong directional sunlight to diffuse overcast skies, we compel the policy to learn robust, illumination-invariant visual features. Extensive real-world experiments demonstrate that a lightweight quadrotor achieves robust, collision-free navigation in complex forest environments at speeds up to 10 m/s, exhibiting significant resilience to drastic lighting variations without fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: End-to-end RL for UAV navigation in unstructured outdoors using Relightable 3D Gaussian Splatting to decouple lighting from geometry, trained in photorealistic sim with diverse lighting to achieve zero-shot transfer and robust real-world flight up to 10 m/s without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Close the sim-to-real gap in monocular-vision UAV control by addressing photometric variability; existing methods couple lighting with geometry, hindering generalization under real-world illumination changes.
AI.method: Train an end-to-end RL policy mapping raw monocular RGB to continuous control in a high-fidelity, real-data-grounded sim. Introduce Relightable 3D Gaussian Splatting to decompose scene into components and enable physically grounded lighting edits within the neural representation; augment training with diverse synthetic lighting conditions to learn illumination-invariant features.
AI.result: Real-world experiments show a lightweight quadrotor achieving robust, collision-free navigation in complex forest environments at up to 10 m/s with strong resilience to lighting changes, without any fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Relightable 3D Gaussian Splatting plus lighting-augmented training enables robust zero-shot UAV navigation in unstructured outdoors by disentangling lighting from geometry and promoting illumination-invariant visual representations.

title: Graph homophily booster: Reimagining the role of discrete features in heterophilic graph learning
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) have emerged as a powerful tool for modeling graph-structured data. However, existing GNNs often struggle with heterophilic graphs, where connected nodes tend to have dissimilar features or labels. While numerous methods have been proposed to address this challenge, they primarily focus on architectural designs without directly targeting the root cause of the heterophily problem. These approaches still perform even worse than the simplest MLPs on challenging heterophilic datasets. For instance, our experiments show that 21 latest GNNs still fall behind the MLP on the Actor dataset. This critical challenge calls for an innovative approach to addressing graph heterophily beyond architectural designs. To bridge this gap, we propose and study a new and unexplored paradigm: directly increasing the graph homophily via a carefully designed graph transformation. In this work, we present a simple yet effective framework called GRAPHITE to address graph heterophily. To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first method that explicitly transforms the graph to directly improve the graph homophily. Stemmed from the exact definition of homophily, our proposed GRAPHITE creates feature nodes to facilitate homophilic message passing between nodes that share similar features. Furthermore, we both theoretically and empirically show that our proposed GRAPHITE significantly increases the homophily of originally heterophilic graphs, with only a slight increase in the graph size. Extensive experiments on challenging datasets demonstrate that our proposed GRAPHITE significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on heterophilic graphs while achieving comparable accuracy with state-of-the-art methods on homophilic graphs.

AI.tldr: GRAPHITE introduces a graph transformation that creates feature nodes to convert heterophilic graphs into more homophilic ones, enabling more effective message passing. The method is claimed to theoretically and empirically raise homophily with a small graph-size overhead, achieving strong gains on heterophilic datasets while remaining competitive on homophilic ones.
AI.motivation: Address the root cause of graph heterophily rather than relying solely on architectural tweaks, by explicitly increasing graph homophily through a transformation grounded in the formal definition of homophily.
AI.method: Propose a simple transformation that adds feature nodes to the graph, facilitating homophilic message passing between nodes sharing similar features. The approach is framed with theoretical analysis showing increased homophily and empirical experiments across challenging datasets.
AI.result: Significant improvement over state-of-the-art on heterophilic graphs; comparable accuracy to SOTA on homophilic graphs, with only a slight increase in graph size.
AI.conclusion: Transforming the graph to increase homophily is a promising and effective paradigm for graph learning on heterophilic data, offering gains where architectural methods struggle while preserving performance on homophilic data.

title: EventCast: Hybrid Demand Forecasting in E-Commerce with LLM-Based Event Knowledge
summary: Demand forecasting is a cornerstone of e-commerce operations, directly impacting inventory planning and fulfillment scheduling. However, existing forecasting systems often fail during high-impact periods such as flash sales, holiday campaigns, and sudden policy interventions, where demand patterns shift abruptly and unpredictably. In this paper, we introduce EventCast, a modular forecasting framework that integrates future event knowledge into time-series prediction. Unlike prior approaches that ignore future interventions or directly use large language models (LLMs) for numerical forecasting, EventCast leverages LLMs solely for event-driven reasoning. Unstructured business data, which covers campaigns, holiday schedules, and seller incentives, from existing operational databases, is processed by an LLM that converts it into interpretable textual summaries leveraging world knowledge for cultural nuances and novel event combinations. These summaries are fused with historical demand features within a dual-tower architecture, enabling accurate, explainable, and scalable forecasts. Deployed on real-world e-commerce scenarios spanning 4 countries of 160 regions over 10 months, EventCast achieves up to 86.9% and 97.7% improvement on MAE and MSE compared to the variant without event knowledge, and reduces MAE by up to 57.0% and MSE by 83.3% versus the best industrial baseline during event-driven periods. EventCast has deployed into real-world industrial pipelines since March 2025, offering a practical solution for improving operational decision-making in dynamic e-commerce environments.

AI.tldr: EventCast: a modular forecasting framework that uses LLM-driven event reasoning to incorporate future event knowledge into time-series demand forecasting, improving accuracy during event-driven periods in real-world e-commerce deployments.
AI.motivation: Forecasting during high-impact periods is brittle due to abrupt demand shifts and omission of future interventions; there is a need to leverage unstructured event data to improve predictions with interpretability.
AI.method: A dual-tower architecture where one tower handles historical demand features and an LLM processes unstructured business data (campaigns, holidays, incentives) into interpretable text summaries that encode event knowledge; summaries are fused with historical features for forecasting; LLM is used for event-driven reasoning, not end-to-end numerical forecasting; deployed across 4 countries, 160 regions, over 10 months.
AI.result: Substantial improvements: MAE up to 86.9% and MSE up to 97.7% over a variant without event knowledge; MAE reductions up to 57.0% and MSE reductions up to 83.3% against top industrial baseline during event periods; deployed in real-world industrial pipelines since March 2025.
AI.conclusion: EventCast demonstrates that incorporating future event knowledge via text-based summaries generated by LLMs can yield accurate, explainable, and scalable demand forecasts in dynamic e-commerce environments, with practical deployment and significant performance gains.

title: Research on a Camera Position Measurement Method based on a Parallel Perspective Error Transfer Model
summary: Camera pose estimation from sparse correspondences is a fundamental problem in geometric computer vision and remains particularly challenging in near-field scenarios, where strong perspective effects and heterogeneous measurement noise can significantly degrade the stability of analytic PnP solutions. In this paper, we present a geometric error propagation framework for camera pose estimation based on a parallel perspective approximation. By explicitly modeling how image measurement errors propagate through perspective geometry, we derive an error transfer model that characterizes the relationship between feature point distribution, camera depth, and pose estimation uncertainty. Building on this analysis, we develop a pose estimation method that leverages parallel perspective initialization and error-aware weighting within a Gauss-Newton optimization scheme, leading to improved robustness in proximity operations. Extensive experiments on both synthetic data and real-world images, covering diverse conditions such as strong illumination, surgical lighting, and underwater low-light environments, demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves accuracy and robustness comparable to state-of-the-art analytic and iterative PnP methods, while maintaining high computational efficiency. These results highlight the importance of explicit geometric error modeling for reliable camera pose estimation in challenging near-field settings.

AI.tldr: Proposes a geometric error propagation framework for near-field camera pose estimation using a parallel perspective approximation; introduces error-aware weighting in Gauss-Newton with parallel-perspective initialization; demonstrates robustness and efficiency across challenging conditions.
AI.motivation: Near-field pose estimation is highly sensitive to perspective effects and heterogeneous noise, causing instability in analytic PnP solutions; explicit error modeling is needed for robust pose estimation.
AI.method: Model image measurement error propagation through perspective geometry via a parallel-perspective approximation; derive an error transfer model linking feature distribution, depth, and pose uncertainty; use parallel-perspective initialization and error-aware weighting in Gauss-Newton optimization.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real data, under diverse conditions (strong illumination, surgical lighting, underwater low-light) show accuracy and robustness comparable to state-of-the-art analytic and iterative PnP methods with high computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Explicit geometric error modeling is important for reliable pose estimation in challenging near-field settings; the proposed framework improves robustness and efficiency through error-aware analysis and optimization.

title: Extended to Reality: Prompt Injection in 3D Environments
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have advanced the capabilities to interpret and act on visual input in 3D environments, empowering diverse applications such as robotics and situated conversational agents. When MLLMs reason over camera-captured views of the physical world, a new attack surface emerges: an attacker can place text-bearing physical objects in the environment to override MLLMs' intended task. While prior work has studied prompt injection in the text domain and through digitally edited 2D images, it remains unclear how these attacks function in 3D physical environments. To bridge the gap, we introduce PI3D, a prompt injection attack against MLLMs in 3D environments, realized through text-bearing physical object placement rather than digital image edits. We formulate and solve the problem of identifying an effective 3D object pose (position and orientation) with injected text, where the attacker's goal is to induce the MLLM to perform the injected task while ensuring that the object placement remains physically plausible. Experiments demonstrate that PI3D is an effective attack against multiple MLLMs under diverse camera trajectories. We further evaluate existing defenses and show that they are insufficient to defend against PI3D.

AI.tldr: PI3D introduces a physical-world prompt injection attack on multimodal LLMs by placing text-bearing objects in the 3D environment, optimizing their pose to steer the model into executing injected tasks, with defenses shown inadequate.
AI.motivation: To understand and quantify the vulnerability of MLLMs to in-situ, 3D prompt injections that cannot be addressed by 2D image edits or text-only prompts.
AI.method: Formulate: an optimization problem to identify a 3D object pose (position and orientation) with injected text that reliably triggers the injected task. Realize the attack with text-bearing physical objects, and evaluate across multiple MLLMs and camera trajectories. Assess existing defenses.
AI.result: PI3D achieves effective attack performance across several MLLMs and diverse camera paths; current defenses fail to robustly defend against such 3D prompt injections.
AI.conclusion: Physical, text-bearing objects in 3D spaces can surreptitiously influence MLLMs; robust defenses require addressing 3D space semantics, object plausibility, and cross-modal alignment under dynamic views.

title: Robust Ultra-High-Dimensional Variable Selection With Correlated Structure Using Group Testing
summary:
Background: High-dimensional genomic data exhibit strong group correlation structures that challenge conventional feature selection methods, which often assume feature independence or rely on pre-defined pathways and are sensitive to outliers and model misspecification.
  Methods: We propose the Dorfman screening framework, a multi-stage procedure that forms data-driven variable groups via hierarchical clustering, performs group and within-group hypothesis testing, and refines selection using elastic net or adaptive elastic net. Robust variants incorporate OGK-based covariance estimation, rank-based correlation, and Huber-weighted regression to handle contaminated and non-normal data.
  Results: In simulations, Dorfman-Sparse-Adaptive-EN performed best under normal conditions, while Robust-OGK-Dorfman-Adaptive-EN showed clear advantages under data contamination, outperforming classical Dorfman and competing methods. Applied to NSCLC gene expression data for trametinib response, robust Dorfman methods achieved the lowest prediction errors and enriched recovery of clinically relevant genes.
  Conclusions: The Dorfman framework provides an efficient and robust approach to genomic feature selection. Robust-OGK-Dorfman-Adaptive-EN offers strong performance under both ideal and contaminated conditions and scales to ultra-high-dimensional settings, making it well suited for modern genomic biomarker discovery.

AI.tldr: The Dorfman screening framework provides a data-driven, robust multi-stage feature selection for ultra-high-dimensional genomic data by forming feature groups via hierarchical clustering, testing at group and within-group levels, and refining with elastic-net penalties; robust variants using OGK covariance and Huber regression improve performance under contamination.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional genomic data exhibit strong group correlation structures; traditional feature selection methods assuming independence or relying on pre-defined pathways are sensitive to outliers and model misspecification, motivating a robust, group-aware, scalable approach.
AI.method: Form data-driven groups via hierarchical clustering; perform group-level then within-group hypothesis tests; refine selection with elastic net or adaptive elastic net. Robust variants incorporate OGK-based covariance estimation, rank-based correlations, and Huber-weighted regression to handle contaminated/non-normal data.
AI.result: In simulations, Dorfman-Sparse-Adaptive-EN excels under normal conditions; Robust-OGK-Dorfman-Adaptive-EN dominates under contamination, outperforming classical Dorfman and competing methods. Applied to NSCLC trametinib response data, robust Dorfman methods achieve lower prediction errors and better recovery of clinically relevant genes.
AI.conclusion: The Dorfman framework is an efficient, robust approach for genomic feature selection, with Robust-OGK-Dorfman-Adaptive-EN delivering strong performance in ideal and contaminated settings and scalable to ultra-high-dimensional data for biomarker discovery.

title: Geo-Code: A Code Framework for Reverse Code Generation from Geometric Images Based on Two-Stage Multi-Agent Evolution
summary: Program code serves as a bridge linking vision and logic, providing a feasible supervisory approach for enhancing the multimodal reasoning capability of large models through geometric operations such as auxiliary line construction and perspective transformation. Nevertheless, current inverse graphics methods face tremendous challenges in accurately reconstructing complex geometric details, which often results in the loss of key geometric constraints or structural distortion. To address this bottleneck, we propose Geo-coder -- the first inverse programming framework for geometric images based on a multi-agent system. Our method innovatively decouples the process into geometric modeling via pixel-wise anchoring and metric-driven code evolution: Stage 1 leverages the complementary advantages of visual operators and large models to achieve precise capture of pixel coordinates and visual attributes; Stage 2 introduces a synthesis-rendering-validation closed loop, where bidirectional visual feedback drives the self-correction of code. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Geo-coder achieves a substantial lead in both geometric reconstruction accuracy and visual consistency. Notably, by effectively preserving the core geometric semantics, the images reconstructed with our method exhibit equivalent performance to the original ones in multimodal reasoning tasks, which fully validates the robustness of the framework. Finally, to further reduce research costs, we have open-sourced the Geo-coder dataset constructed on the GeoCode framework, which contains more than 1,500 samples. On this basis, we have also open-sourced the GeocodeLM model, laying a solid data and model foundation for subsequent research in this field.

AI.tldr: Geo-coder is the first inverse-programming framework for geometric images using a multi-agent system to separate geometric modeling and code evolution, featuring a bidirectional synthesis-rendering-validation loop; it achieves superior geometric reconstruction and preserves geometric semantics for multimodal reasoning, and releases both data and model.
AI.motivation: Current inverse graphics methods struggle to reconstruct complex geometric details and preserve core geometric constraints, which undermines downstream multimodal reasoning tasks.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: Stage 1 pixel-wise anchoring combines visual operators and large models for precise coordinate capture and attributes; Stage 2 a closed loop of synthesis, rendering, and validation with bidirectional visual feedback that enables self-correction of code. The framework also includes a GeoCode dataset (~1,500 samples) and the GeocodeLM model.
AI.result: Geo-coder achieves superior geometric reconstruction accuracy and visual consistency; reconstructed images preserve core geometric semantics, yielding equivalent multimodal-reasoning performance to the originals; the GeoCode dataset and GeocodeLM model are open-sourced.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a robust, cost-efficient path for inverse programming in geometric imagery, enabling reliable geometric reasoning in multimodal tasks and accelerating future research by providing open data and model resources; potential limitations and future work are not detailed in the abstract.

title: Incremental Mapping with Measurement Synchronization & Compression
summary: Modern autonomous vehicles and robots utilize versatile sensors for localization and mapping. The fidelity of these maps is paramount, as an accurate environmental representation is a prerequisite for stable and precise localization. Factor graphs provide a powerful approach for sensor fusion, enabling the estimation of the maximum a posteriori solution. However, the discrete nature of graph-based representations, combined with asynchronous sensor measurements, complicates consistent state estimation. The design of an optimal factor graph topology remains an open challenge, especially in multi-sensor systems with asynchronous data. Conventional approaches rely on a rigid graph structure, which becomes inefficient with sensors of disparate rates. Although preintegration techniques can mitigate this for high-rate sensors, their applicability is limited. To address this problem, this work introduces a novel approach that incrementally constructs connected factor graphs, ensuring the incorporation of all available sensor data by choosing the optimal graph topology based on the external evaluation criteria. The proposed methodology facilitates graph compression, reducing the number of nodes (optimized variables) by ~30% on average while maintaining map quality at a level comparable to conventional approaches.

AI.tldr: Incrementally builds connected factor graphs for multi-sensor, asynchronous data, enabling graph compression (~30% fewer nodes) while preserving map accuracy.
AI.motivation: To efficiently fuse heterogeneous sensors with different sampling rates in SLAM/localization. Fixed graph structures become inefficient when data arrive asynchronously; existing preintegration helps high-rate sensors but has limitations. An adaptive topology approach aims to maintain accuracy with reduced computation.
AI.method: Propose an incremental graph-construction algorithm that selects an optimal factor-graph topology based on external evaluation criteria, ensuring all sensor data are incorporated. The method yields compressed graphs with fewer optimized variables without degrading map quality.
AI.result: Graph compression of about 30% fewer nodes on average, with map quality comparable to conventional fixed-topology approaches.
AI.conclusion: Incremental, topology-optimized factor graphs enable efficient multi-sensor fusion under asynchronous data, reducing computational burden while maintaining estimation accuracy.

title: Ex-Omni: Enabling 3D Facial Animation Generation for Omni-modal Large Language Models
summary: Omni-modal large language models (OLLMs) aim to unify multimodal understanding and generation, yet incorporating speech with 3D facial animation remains largely unexplored despite its importance for natural interaction. A key challenge arises from the representation mismatch between discrete, token-level semantic reasoning in LLMs and the dense, fine-grained temporal dynamics required for 3D facial motion, which makes direct modeling difficult to optimize under limited data. We propose Expressive Omni (Ex-Omni), an open-source omni-modal framework that augments OLLMs with speech-accompanied 3D facial animation. Ex-Omni reduces learning difficulty by decoupling semantic reasoning from temporal generation, leveraging speech units as temporal scaffolding and a unified token-as-query gated fusion (TQGF) mechanism for controlled semantic injection. We further introduce InstructEx, a dataset aims to facilitate augment OLLMs with speech-accompanied 3D facial animation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Ex-Omni performs competitively against existing open-source OLLMs while enabling stable aligned speech and facial animation generation.

AI.tldr: Proposes Ex-Omni, an open-source omni-modal framework that adds speech-accompanied 3D facial animation to omni-modal LLMs by decoupling semantic reasoning from temporal generation, using speech units as temporal scaffolding and a token-as-query gated fusion (TQGF). Introduces InstructEx to facilitate data for this task; experiments show competitive performance and stable alignment.
AI.motivation: To address the representation mismatch between token-level semantic reasoning in LLMs and the dense, fine-grained temporal dynamics required for 3D facial motion, particularly when incorporating speech for natural interaction, and to enable effective learning with limited data.
AI.method: 1) Ex-Omni decouples semantic reasoning from temporal generation. 2) Uses speech units as temporal scaffolding to guide dynamics. 3) Introduces a novel token-as-query gated fusion (TQGF) mechanism for controlled semantic injection. 4) Presents InstructEx, a dataset to augment OLLMs with speech-accompanied 3D facial animation.
AI.result: Experiments show Ex-Omni is competitive with existing open-source OLLMs and enables stable, aligned speech and 3D facial animation generation.
AI.conclusion: Ex-Omni provides a viable approach to augment OLLMs with speech-accompanied 3D facial animation, demonstrating competitive performance and data-efficient learning via temporal scaffolding and TQGF; InstructEx supports broader evaluation and development.

title: tLoRA: Efficient Multi-LoRA Training with Elastic Shared Super-Models
summary: As Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) becomes the standard approach for efficiently fine-tuning large language models (LLMs), shared clusters increasingly execute many concurrent LoRA training jobs over the same frozen backbone. While recent advances enable batching (co-locating) multiple adapters during serving, efficient training-time co-location of heterogeneous LoRA adapters presents unique challenges. Jobs often differ in adapter rank, batch size, and resource allocation, and naïve batching can introduce synchronization stalls, communication overheads, and per-job slowdowns that are worse than executing independently. We introduce tLoRA, a framework that enables efficient batch training of multiple LoRA jobs. tLoRA fuses adapters that share the same base model into an elastic shared super-model, exploiting existing distributed training frameworks to derive parallelism plans that share resources effectively. At the kernel level, tLoRA employs a fused LoRA kernel that adaptively reconstructs low-rank computation tiles and schedules rank-aware nano-batches to maximize overlap between computation and communication across adapters. At the scheduling layer, tLoRA incorporates an online, residual-capacity-aware scheduler that adaptively groups jobs to maximize collective throughput. Evaluations using real-world cluster traces demonstrate that tLoRA improves training throughput by 1.2--1.8x, job training completion time by 2.3--5.4x, and GPU utilization by 37%.

AI.tldr: tLoRA enables efficient batch training of multiple LoRA jobs by fusing adapters into a shared super-model with a fused kernel and a residual-capacity scheduler, delivering substantial gains in throughput and completion time.
AI.motivation: With LoRA becoming the standard for efficient fine-tuning, clusters increasingly run many concurrent LoRA training jobs on the same frozen backbone. Naïve training-time co-location of heterogeneous adapters introduces synchronization stalls, communication overheads, and per-job slowdowns, especially when adapter rank, batch size, and resource allocation vary. There is a need for mechanisms to co-schedule and fuse adapters to maximize parallelism and resource utilization.
AI.method: Proposes tLoRA: (1) an elastic shared super-model that fuses adapters referencing the same base model to share computation; (2) a fused LoRA kernel that adaptively reconstructs low-rank tiles and schedules rank-aware nano-batches to maximize overlap between computation and communication across adapters; (3) an online, residual-capacity-aware scheduler that adaptively groups jobs to maximize collective throughput; leverages existing distributed training frameworks to derive parallelism plans.
AI.result: Evaluations on real-world cluster traces show improvements of training throughput by 1.2–1.8x, job training completion time by 2.3–5.4x, and GPU utilization by 37%.
AI.conclusion: tLoRA provides substantial gains for multi-adapter training on shared backbones by combining model fusion, kernel fusion, and adaptive scheduling; the approach appears practical and scalable, though further work could explore broader heterogeneity and dynamic workloads.

title: Humanizing AI Grading: Student-Centered Insights on Fairness, Trust, Consistency and Transparency
summary: This study investigates students' perceptions of Artificial Intelligence (AI) grading systems in an undergraduate computer science course (n = 27), focusing on a block-based programming final project. Guided by the ethical principles framework articulated by Jobin (2019), our study examines fairness, trust, consistency, and transparency in AI grading by comparing AI-generated feedback with original human-graded feedback. Findings reveal concerns about AI's lack of contextual understanding and personalization. We recommend that equitable and trustworthy AI systems reflect human judgment, flexibility, and empathy, serving as supplementary tools under human oversight. This work contributes to ethics-centered assessment practices by amplifying student voices and offering design principles for humanizing AI in designed learning environments.

AI.tldr: Assesses undergraduate CS students' perceptions of AI-based grading; compares AI-generated feedback with human feedback; finds AI lacks context and personalization; recommends human-in-the-loop, ethics-centered design; contributes principles for humanizing AI in assessment.
AI.motivation: Address ethical concerns (fairness, trust, consistency, transparency) in AI grading within education, guided by Jobin 2019 framework, and examine whether AI feedback can complement human judgment.
AI.method: Quasi-experimental, qualitative study (n=27) in an undergraduate CS course using a block-based programming final project; compares AI-generated feedback with original human-graded feedback; analyzes student perceptions and implications for design.
AI.result: Students express concerns about AI's lack of contextual understanding and personalization; AI should be a supplementary tool under human oversight; design principles should reflect human judgment, flexibility, and empathy.
AI.conclusion: Ethics-centered assessment practice: amplify student voices and provide design principles for making AI grading more humane and trustworthy, suggesting AI systems that align with human judgment and empathy.

title: Multi-Agent Route Planning as a QUBO Problem
summary: Multi-Agent Route Planning considers selecting vehicles, each associated with a single predefined route, such that the spatial coverage of a road network is increased while redundant overlaps are limited. This paper gives a formal problem definition, proves NP-hardness by reduction from the Weighted Set Packing problem, and derives a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization formulation whose coefficients directly encode unique coverage rewards and pairwise overlap penalties. A single penalty parameter controls the coverage-overlap trade-off. We distinguish between a soft regime, which supports multi-objective exploration, and a hard regime, in which the penalty is strong enough to effectively enforce near-disjoint routes. We describe a practical pipeline for generating city instances, constructing candidate routes, building the QUBO matrix, and solving it with an exact mixed-integer solver (Gurobi), simulated annealing, and D-Wave hybrid quantum annealing. Experiments on Barcelona instances with up to 10 000 vehicles reveal a clear coverage-overlap knee and show that Pareto-optimal solutions are mainly obtained under the hard-penalty regime, while D-Wave hybrid solvers and Gurobi achieve essentially identical objective values with only minor differences in runtime as problem size grows.

AI.tldr: Formulates a Multi-Agent Route Planning problem to maximize spatial coverage with limited overlap, proving NP-hard via Weighted Set Packing; derives a QUBO with a single trade-off penalty between coverage and overlap; distinguishes soft (multi-objective exploration) and hard (near-disjoint routes) regimes; provides a practical pipeline and benchmarks on Barcelona data with up to 10k vehicles, showing a coverage-overlap knee and similar objective values for D-Wave hybrid and Gurobi.
AI.motivation: Increase road-network coverage while minimizing redundant overlaps; address scalability and solver comparisons for large urban instances; examine the efficacy of QUBO formulations and quantum-classical solvers for practical MARP tasks.
AI.method: Formal problem definition; NP-hardness proof via reduction from Weighted Set Packing; derive Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) encoding with unique-coverage rewards and pairwise-overlap penalties; single penalty parameter controls trade-off; define soft vs hard regimes; develop a pipeline to generate city instances, construct candidate routes, build the QUBO matrix, and solve using exact mixed-integer programming (Gurobi), simulated annealing, and D-Wave hybrid quantum annealing.
AI.result: Identify a clear knee in the coverage-overlap trade-off; Pareto-optimal solutions predominantly arise under the hard-penalty regime; D-Wave hybrid solvers and Gurobi achieve essentially identical objective values with only minor runtime differences as problem size increases.
AI.conclusion: A tunable QUBO formulation effectively balances coverage and overlap, with the hard regime yielding near-disjoint routes that capture the Pareto frontier; quantum-classical hybrid methods are competitive with exact solvers for large instances, validating the proposed pipeline and its scalability for City-scale MARP problems.

title: Privacy in Image Datasets: A Case Study on Pregnancy Ultrasounds
summary: The rise of generative models has led to increased use of large-scale datasets collected from the internet, often with minimal or no data curation. This raises concerns about the inclusion of sensitive or private information. In this work, we explore the presence of pregnancy ultrasound images, which contain sensitive personal information and are often shared online. Through a systematic examination of LAION-400M dataset using CLIP embedding similarity, we retrieve images containing pregnancy ultrasound and detect thousands of entities of private information such as names and locations. Our findings reveal that multiple images have high-risk information that could enable re-identification or impersonation. We conclude with recommended practices for dataset curation, data privacy, and ethical use of public image datasets.

AI.tldr: The abstract investigates privacy risks in large-scale image datasets by identifying pregnancy ultrasound images and sensitive identifiers using CLIP embeddings, highlighting privacy concerns and proposing curation guidelines.
AI.motivation: With growing use of web-scraped image datasets, there is a risk of leaking sensitive information. Pregnancy ultrasound images are highly personal medical data, making privacy preservation critical.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of the LAION-400M dataset via CLIP embedding similarity to retrieve pregnancy ultrasound images and detect private information (e.g., names, locations) present in the images or associated metadata.
AI.result: Thousands of private information entities (e.g., names, locations) are detected; several images contain high-risk information that could enable re-identification or impersonation.
AI.conclusion: Advocate improved dataset curation, privacy-preserving data handling, and ethical use of public image datasets; emphasize need for guidelines to mitigate medical data leakage in large corpora.

title: XShare: Collaborative in-Batch Expert Sharing for Faster MoE Inference
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures are increasingly used to efficiently scale large language models. However, in production inference, request batching and speculative decoding significantly amplify expert activation, eroding these efficiency benefits. We address this issue by modeling batch-aware expert selection as a modular optimization problem and designing efficient greedy algorithms for different deployment settings. The proposed method, namely XShare, requires no retraining and dynamically adapts to each batch by maximizing the total gating score of selected experts. It reduces expert activation by up to 30% under standard batching, cuts peak GPU load by up to 3x in expert-parallel deployments, and achieves up to 14% throughput gains in speculative decoding via hierarchical, correlation-aware expert selection even if requests in a batch drawn from heterogeneous datasets.

AI.tldr: A modular, greedy optimization for batch-aware expert selection in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models, named XShare, achieving non-retraining efficiency gains by maximizing the total gating score per batch.
AI.motivation: In production inference, request batching and speculative decoding inflate expert activation in MoE models, eroding scalability gains. A dynamic, retraining-free method is needed to reduce activation and resource usage.
AI.method: Formulate batch-aware expert selection as a modular optimization problem and derive efficient greedy algorithms for different deployment settings. XShare selects a subset of experts per batch to maximize the total gating score, adapts to batch composition without retraining, and uses hierarchical, correlation-aware selection to handle heterogeneous request batches.
AI.result: Under standard batching, expert activation is reduced by up to 30%. In expert-parallel deployments, peak GPU load is reduced up to 3x. In speculative decoding, throughput improves up to 14%, even with heterogeneous batch sources.
AI.conclusion: XShare offers a practical, deployment-friendly approach to mitigating batch-induced inefficiencies in MoE models, delivering meaningful throughput and resource gains without retraining.

title: Learning to Continually Learn via Meta-learning Agentic Memory Designs
summary: The statelessness of foundation models bottlenecks agentic systems' ability to continually learn, a core capability for long-horizon reasoning and adaptation. To address this limitation, agentic systems commonly incorporate memory modules to retain and reuse past experience, aiming for continual learning during test time. However, most existing memory designs are human-crafted and fixed, which limits their ability to adapt to the diversity and non-stationarity of real-world tasks. In this paper, we introduce ALMA (Automated meta-Learning of Memory designs for Agentic systems), a framework that meta-learns memory designs to replace hand-engineered memory designs, therefore minimizing human effort and enabling agentic systems to be continual learners across diverse domains. Our approach employs a Meta Agent that searches over memory designs expressed as executable code in an open-ended manner, theoretically allowing the discovery of arbitrary memory designs, including database schemas as well as their retrieval and update mechanisms. Extensive experiments across four sequential decision-making domains demonstrate that the learned memory designs enable more effective and efficient learning from experience than state-of-the-art human-crafted memory designs on all benchmarks. When developed and deployed safely, ALMA represents a step toward self-improving AI systems that learn to be adaptive, continual learners.

AI.tldr: ALMA meta-learns memory designs for agentic systems by evolving executable memory components via a Meta Agent, achieving superior continual learning across four domains versus hand-crafted memories; suggests potential for self-improving, adaptable AI with safety considerations.
AI.motivation: Stateless foundation models hinder continual learning in long-horizon tasks. Memory modules are needed to retain and reuse past experiences, but fixed, human-crafted designs struggle with task diversity and non-stationarity. ALMA aims to automate memory design to reduce human effort and improve adaptability.
AI.method: A Meta Agent searches over executable-code representations of memory designs (including storage schemas and retrieval/update mechanisms) to discover diverse, potentially arbitrary memory architectures. The process is open-ended, enabling discovery beyond hand-crafted designs.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across four sequential decision-making domains shows the learned memory designs outperform state-of-the-art human-crafted memories in both effectiveness and efficiency of learning from experience.
AI.conclusion: ALMA advances toward self-improving, continual-learning AI systems by automating memory design. With safe deployment in mind, it enables adaptive agentic behavior without extensive human engineering.

title: Optimized Human-Robot Co-Dispatch Planning for Petro-Site Surveillance under Varying Criticalities
summary: Securing petroleum infrastructure requires balancing autonomous system efficiency with human judgment for threat escalation, a challenge unaddressed by classical facility location models assuming homogeneous resources. This paper formulates the Human-Robot Co-Dispatch Facility Location Problem (HRCD-FLP), a capacitated facility location variant incorporating tiered infrastructure criticality, human-robot supervision ratio constraints, and minimum utilization requirements. We evaluate command center selection across three technology maturity scenarios. Results show transitioning from conservative (1:3 human-robot supervision) to future autonomous operations (1:10) yields significant cost reduction while maintaining complete critical infrastructure coverage. For small problems, exact methods dominate in both cost and computation time; for larger problems, the proposed heuristic achieves feasible solutions in under 3 minutes with approximately 14% optimality gap where comparison is possible. From systems perspective, our work demonstrate that optimized planning for human-robot teaming is key to achieve both cost-effective and mission-reliable deployments.

AI.tldr: HRCD-FLP: a capacitated facility location variant that integrates tiered critical infrastructure, human-robot supervision ratios, and minimum utilization to optimize command center placement under different technology maturity scenarios; smaller instances favor exact methods, while larger instances are effectively solved by a heuristic with modest optimality gaps and fast runtimes.
AI.motivation: Traditional facility location models assume homogeneous resources; securing critical petroleum infrastructure requires balancing autonomous system efficiency with human judgment for threat escalation, necessitating a co-dispatch framework that accounts for supervision constraints and infrastructure criticality.
AI.method: Formulate the Human-Robot Co-Dispatch Facility Location Problem (HRCD-FLP) as a capacitated facility location variant with tiered infrastructure criticality, supervision ratio constraints, and minimum utilization requirements; evaluate command center (facility) selection under three technology maturity scenarios.
AI.result: Exact methods yield cost and time advantages on small problems; on larger problems, the proposed heuristic finds feasible solutions in under 3 minutes with about a 14% optimality gap (where comparison is possible). Transitioning from conservative (1:3) to autonomous (1:10) supervision yields significant cost reductions while preserving full coverage.
AI.conclusion: Optimized planning for human-robot teaming is essential for cost-effective and mission-reliable deployments in critical infrastructure protection; higher autonomy can reduce costs substantially without compromising coverage, given effective co-dispatch optimization.

title: DuMeta++: Spatiotemporal Dual Meta-Learning for Generalizable Few-Shot Brain Tissue Segmentation Across Diverse Ages
summary: Accurate segmentation of brain tissues from MRI scans is critical for neuroscience and clinical applications, but achieving consistent performance across the human lifespan remains challenging due to dynamic, age-related changes in brain appearance and morphology. While prior work has sought to mitigate these shifts by using self-supervised regularization with paired longitudinal data, such data are often unavailable in practice. To address this, we propose \emph{DuMeta++}, a dual meta-learning framework that operates without paired longitudinal data. Our approach integrates: (1) meta-feature learning to extract age-agnostic semantic representations of spatiotemporally evolving brain structures, and (2) meta-initialization learning to enable data-efficient adaptation of the segmentation model. Furthermore, we propose a memory-bank-based class-aware regularization strategy to enforce longitudinal consistency without explicit longitudinal supervision. We theoretically prove the convergence of our DuMeta++, ensuring stability. Experiments on diverse datasets (iSeg-2019, IBIS, OASIS, ADNI) under few-shot settings demonstrate that DuMeta++ outperforms existing methods in cross-age generalization. Code will be available at https://github.com/ladderlab-xjtu/DuMeta++.

AI.tldr: DuMeta++ offers a two-pronged meta-learning approach for robust brain MRI tissue segmentation across the lifespan without paired longitudinal data, achieving improved cross-age generalization in few-shot regimes and with a convergent training framework.
AI.motivation: Tackle age-related shifts in brain appearance and morphology that hinder consistent segmentation, while avoiding the practical barrier of acquiring paired longitudinal MRI data.
AI.method: 1) Meta-feature learning to extract age-agnostic, spatiotemporal semantic representations. 2) Meta-initialization learning to enable data-efficient model adaptation. 3) Memory-bank-based, class-aware regularization to enforce longitudinal consistency without supervision. 4) Theoretical convergence proof guaranteeing stability.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on iSeg-2019, IBIS, OASIS, and ADNI under few-shot settings shows DuMeta++ surpasses existing methods in cross-age generalization. Code will be released at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: DuMeta++ effectively mitigates lifespan-related appearance/morphology shifts in MRI segmentation without paired longitudinal data, delivering stable convergence and improved cross-age performance, with practical implications for longitudinal neuroimaging studies.

title: Hybrid Feedback-Guided Optimal Learning for Wireless Interactive Panoramic Scene Delivery
summary: Immersive applications such as virtual and augmented reality impose stringent requirements on frame rate, latency, and synchronization between physical and virtual environments. To meet these requirements, an edge server must render panoramic content, predict user head motion, and transmit a portion of the scene that is large enough to cover the user viewport while remaining within wireless bandwidth constraints. Each portion produces two feedback signals: prediction feedback, indicating whether the selected portion covers the actual viewport, and transmission feedback, indicating whether the corresponding packets are successfully delivered. Prior work models this problem as a multi-armed bandit with two-level bandit feedback, but fails to exploit the fact that prediction feedback can be retrospectively computed for all candidate portions once the user head pose is observed. As a result, prediction feedback constitutes full-information feedback rather than bandit feedback. Motivated by this observation, we introduce a two-level hybrid feedback model that combines full-information and bandit feedback, and formulate the portion selection problem as an online learning task under this setting. We derive an instance-dependent regret lower bound for the hybrid feedback model and propose AdaPort, a hybrid learning algorithm that leverages both feedback types to improve learning efficiency. We further establish an instance-dependent regret upper bound that matches the lower bound asymptotically, and demonstrate through real-world trace driven simulations that AdaPort consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baseline methods.

AI.tldr: AdaPort: a hybrid feedback online learning approach for viewport portion selection in immersive VR/AR edge rendering, leveraging both full-information prediction feedback and bandit transmission feedback. It introduces a two-level hybrid model, proves instance-dependent regret bounds (lower and upper) that match asymptotically, and demonstrates empirical gains over baselines using real-world traces.
AI.motivation: Immersive XR demands high frame rates, low latency, and bandwidth-efficient viewport rendering. Existing models treat prediction feedback as bandit feedback, ignoring that it can be computed fully after observing head pose, leading to suboptimal learning. A hybrid model can exploit both feedback types to accelerate learning.
AI.method: Formulate viewport portion selection as online learning under a two-level hybrid feedback model. Derive a lower bound on instance-dependent regret for this model. Propose AdaPort, an algorithm that utilizes both full-information prediction feedback and bandit transmission feedback. Prove an instance-dependent regret upper bound for AdaPort that matches the lower bound asymptotically. Validate via real-world trace-driven simulations against state-of-the-art baselines.
