AI.result: Theoretical: instance-dependent regret lower bound for the hybrid feedback model; AdaPort algorithm with matching asymptotic upper bound. Empirical: AdaPort consistently outperforms baselines in real-world trace-driven simulations.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid feedback modeling captures the actual information structure of viewport selection in immersive streaming better than purely bandit models. AdaPort achieves provable efficiency and practical performance gains, suggesting improved viewport rendering and transmission under bandwidth constraints for XR systems.

title: Disentangled Instrumental Variables for Causal Inference with Networked Observational Data
summary: Instrumental variables (IVs) are crucial for addressing unobservable confounders, yet their stringent exogeneity assumptions pose significant challenges in networked data. Existing methods typically rely on modelling neighbour information when recovering IVs, thereby inevitably mixing shared environment-induced endogenous correlations and individual-specific exogenous variation, leading the resulting IVs to inherit dependence on unobserved confounders and to violate exogeneity. To overcome this challenge, we propose $\underline{Dis}$entangled $\underline{I}$nstrumental $\underline{V}$ariables (DisIV) framework, a novel method for causal inference based on networked observational data with latent confounders. DisIV exploits network homogeneity as an inductive bias and employs a structural disentanglement mechanism to extract individual-specific components that serve as latent IVs. The causal validity of the extracted IVs is constrained through explicit orthogonality and exclusion conditions. Extensive semi-synthetic experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that DisIV consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in causal effect estimation under network-induced confounding.

AI.tldr: Disentangled Instrumental Variables (DisIV) extracts individual-specific latent IVs from networked data by structural disentanglement guided by network homogeneity, enforcing orthogonality and exclusion to ensure causal validity; evaluated on semi-synthetic real-world data, showing improved causal effect estimation under network-induced confounding.
AI.motivation: IVs struggle with exogeneity in networks because neighbour information blends shared environment-induced endogeneity with individual exogenous variation, compromising valid causal inference.
AI.method: Structural disentanglement using network homogeneity as inductive bias to separate individual-specific latent components; imposes explicit orthogonality and exclusion constraints to ensure the extracted components function as valid IVs.
AI.result: Semi-synthetic experiments on real-world datasets show DisIV consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in causal effect estimation under network-induced confounding.
AI.conclusion: DisIV provides a principled approach to recover valid latent IVs in networks by disentangling individual-specific variation and enforcing exogeneity-like constraints, improving causal inference when latent confounding is present.

title: Feasibility-Guided Planning over Multi-Specialized Locomotion Policies
summary: Planning over unstructured terrain presents a significant challenge in the field of legged robotics. Although recent works in reinforcement learning have yielded various locomotion strategies, planning over multiple experts remains a complex issue. Existing approaches encounter several constraints: traditional planners are unable to integrate skill-specific policies, whereas hierarchical learning frameworks often lose interpretability and require retraining whenever new policies are added. In this paper, we propose a feasibility-guided planning framework that successfully incorporates multiple terrain-specific policies. Each policy is paired with a Feasibility-Net, which learned to predict feasibility tensors based on the local elevation maps and task vectors. This integration allows classical planning algorithms to derive optimal paths. Through both simulated and real-world experiments, we demonstrate that our method efficiently generates reliable plans across diverse and challenging terrains, while consistently aligning with the capabilities of the underlying policies.

AI.tldr: A modular feasibility-guided planner combines terrain-specific policies with learned feasibility tensors to enable classical planners to compute reliable, policy-aligned paths in unstructured terrains.
AI.motivation: Planning on unstructured terrain is difficult; traditional planners cannot leverage skill-specific policies, and hierarchical RL often lacks interpretability and requires retraining when new policies are added. A modular approach that preserves policy capabilities while maintaining planning transparency is needed.
AI.method: For each terrain-specific policy, pair it with a Feasibility-Net that predicts feasibility tensors from local elevation maps and task vectors. These tensors guide classical planning algorithms to derive optimal paths that respect policy capabilities. Evaluation includes simulated and real-world experiments to test robustness across diverse terrains.
AI.result: The framework efficiently produces reliable plans across challenging terrains while remaining consistent with the capabilities of the underlying policies; demonstrates practical viability in both simulated and real-world settings.
AI.conclusion: A modular, interpretable, plug-and-play framework that unites multiple terrain-specific policies with planning via learned feasibility guidance. It preserves policy strengths while leveraging classical planning, enabling scalable extension with new policies without full retraining.

title: Condition Matters in Full-head 3D GANs
comment: Accepted by ICLR 2026. Project page: https://lhyfst.github.io/balancehead/
summary: Conditioning is crucial for stable training of full-head 3D GANs. Without any conditioning signal, the model suffers from severe mode collapse, making it impractical to training. However, a series of previous full-head 3D GANs conventionally choose the view angle as the conditioning input, which leads to a bias in the learned 3D full-head space along the conditional view direction. This is evident in the significant differences in generation quality and diversity between the conditional view and non-conditional views of the generated 3D heads, resulting in global incoherence across different head regions. In this work, we propose to use view-invariant semantic feature as the conditioning input, thereby decoupling the generative capability of 3D heads from the viewing direction. To construct a view-invariant semantic condition for each training image, we create a novel synthesized head image dataset. We leverage FLUX.1 Kontext to extend existing high-quality frontal face datasets to a wide range of view angles. The image clip feature extracted from the frontal view is then used as a shared semantic condition across all views in the extended images, ensuring semantic alignment while eliminating directional bias. This also allows supervision from different views of the same subject to be consolidated under a shared semantic condition, which accelerates training and enhances the global coherence of the generated 3D heads. Moreover, as GANs often experience slower improvements in diversity once the generator learns a few modes that successfully fool the discriminator, our semantic conditioning encourages the generator to follow the true semantic distribution, thereby promoting continuous learning and diverse generation. Extensive experiments on full-head synthesis and single-view GAN inversion demonstrate that our method achieves significantly higher fidelity, diversity, and generalizability.

AI.tldr: View-invariant semantic conditioning for full-head 3D GANs using a synthesized multi-view dataset; decouples generation from view direction to improve fidelity, diversity, and coherence.
AI.motivation: Address severe mode collapse and directional bias when conditioning on view angle in 3D GANs; enable consistent semantic guidance across views.
AI.method: Create a view-invariant semantic condition by extending frontal-face datasets to multiple viewpoints using FLUX.1 Kontext; extract frontal-view clip features as shared semantic condition across views; apply across extended images; consolidate supervision from different views; train GAN with semantic conditioning to follow semantic distribution.
AI.result: Reported improvements in fidelity, diversity, and generalization for full-head synthesis and single-view GAN inversion; enhanced global coherence.
AI.conclusion: View-invariant semantic conditioning reduces view-direction bias, improves training stability, and enables more generalizable 3D head generation.

title: Laplacian-LoRA: Delaying Oversmoothing in Deep GCNs via Spectral Low-Rank Adaptation
summary: Oversmoothing is a fundamental limitation of deep graph convolutional networks (GCNs), causing node representations to collapse as depth increases. While many prior approaches mitigate this effect through architectural modifications or residual mechanisms, the underlying spectral cause of oversmoothing is often left implicit. We propose Laplacian-LoRA, a simple and interpretable low-rank spectral adaptation of standard GCNs. Rather than redesigning message passing, Laplacian-LoRA introduces a learnable, spectrally anchored correction to the fixed Laplacian propagation operator, selectively weakening contraction while preserving stability and the low-pass inductive bias. Across multiple benchmark datasets and depths, Laplacian-LoRA consistently delays the onset of oversmoothing, extending the effective depth of GCNs by up to a factor of two. Embedding variance diagnostics confirm that these gains arise from delayed representational collapse, while learned spectral analysis demonstrates that the correction is smooth, bounded, and well behaved. Our results show that oversmoothing is a depth-dependent spectral phenomenon that can be systematically delayed through modest, low-rank adaptation of the graph propagation operator.

AI.tldr: Laplacian-LoRA adds a learnable, low-rank spectral correction to the Laplacian in GCNs to combat oversmoothing, extending effective depth while maintaining stability and the low-pass bias.
AI.motivation: Oversmoothing causes node representations to collapse as layers increase in deep GCNs. The spectral cause is often implicit; a simple, interpretable spectral correction could delay collapse without redesigning message passing.
AI.method: Introduce a learnable, spectrally anchored low-rank adaptation to the fixed Laplacian propagation operator (Laplacian-LoRA); apply as a correction to the Laplacian while preserving stability and low-pass behavior; trainable parameters implement a gentle spectral modification.
AI.result: Empirically delays oversmoothing across benchmarks and depths; extends effective depth by up to ~2x; embedding variance diagnostics show delayed collapse; spectral analysis indicates the correction is smooth, bounded, and well-behaved.
AI.conclusion: Oversmoothing is a depth-dependent spectral phenomenon that can be mitigated with modest, low-rank spectral adaptation of the propagation operator, offering interpretability and performance gains without altering the underlying message-passing framework.

title: Do Multi-Agents Dream of Electric Screens? Achieving Perfect Accuracy on AndroidWorld Through Task Decomposition
summary: We present Minitap, a multi-agent system that achieves 100% success on the AndroidWorld benchmark, the first to fully solve all 116 tasks and surpassing human performance (80%). We first analyze why single-agent architectures fail: context pollution from mixed reasoning traces, silent text input failures undetected by the agent, and repetitive action loops without escape. Minitap addresses each failure through targeted mechanisms: cognitive separation across six specialized agents, deterministic post-validation of text input against device state, and meta-cognitive reasoning that detects cycles and triggers strategy changes. Ablations show multi-agent decomposition contributes +21 points over single-agent baselines; verified execution adds +7 points; meta-cognition adds +9 points. We release Minitap as open-source software. https://github.com/minitap-ai/mobile-use

AI.tldr: Minitap, a 6-agent system, fully solves the AndroidWorld benchmark (116 tasks) with 100% success, outperforming the 80% human baseline; ablation shows significant gains from multi-agent design, verification, and meta-cognition; released as open-source.
AI.motivation: To overcome systemic failures of single-agent architectures in complex, multi-step mobile task-solving: context contamination from mixed traces, undetected input failures, and dead-end loops.
AI.method: Introduce cognitive separation across six specialized agents; implement deterministic post-validation of text input against device state; integrate meta-cognitive reasoning to detect cycles and trigger strategy changes.
AI.result: Achieves 100% success on AndroidWorld benchmark by fully solving all 116 tasks, exceeding human performance; ablation studies show +21 points from multi-agent decomposition, +7 from verified execution, and +9 from meta-cognition.
AI.conclusion: Minitap is released as open-source software, enabling broader adoption and study of multi-agent collaboration for mobile task solving.

title: Analyzing the Impact of Simulation Fidelity on the Evaluation of Autonomous Driving Motion Control
comment: Accepted for publication at the IEEE IV 2024
summary: Simulation is crucial in the development of autonomous driving software. In particular, assessing control algorithms requires an accurate vehicle dynamics simulation. However, recent publications use models with varying levels of detail. This disparity makes it difficult to compare individual control algorithms. Therefore, this paper aims to investigate the influence of the fidelity of vehicle dynamics modeling on the closed-loop behavior of trajectory-following controllers. For this purpose, we introduce a comprehensive Autoware-compatible vehicle model. By simplifying this, we derive models with varying fidelity. Evaluating over 550 simulation runs allows us to quantify each model's approximation quality compared to real-world data. Furthermore, we investigate whether the influence of model simplifications changes with varying margins to the acceleration limit of the vehicle. From this, we deduce to which degree a vehicle model can be simplified to evaluate control algorithms depending on the specific application. The real-world data used to validate the simulation environment originate from the Indy Autonomous Challenge race at the Autodromo Nazionale di Monza in June 2023. They show the fastest fully autonomous lap of TUM Autonomous Motorsport, with vehicle speeds reaching 267 kph and lateral accelerations of up to 15 mps2.

AI.tldr: A study assesses how the fidelity of vehicle dynamics models affects closed-loop trajectory-following controllers by deriving a spectrum of Autoware-compatible models from a comprehensive base, validating against real-world Indy Autonomous Challenge data (Monza, 2023) across 550 simulations to quantify approximation quality and how simplifications interact with acceleration-margin.
AI.motivation: Disparate vehicle dynamics models hinder fair comparison of control algorithms in autonomous driving. Understanding how fidelity affects closed-loop behavior is essential to select appropriate models for algorithm evaluation while balancing accuracy and computational efficiency.
AI.method: Start from a detailed, Autoware-compatible vehicle model and derive reduced-fidelity variants by systematic simplifications. Run 550 simulation trials to compare model outputs against real-world data from the Indy Autonomous Challenge at Monza (June 2023). Vary the margin to the vehicle’s acceleration limit to study how model simplifications influence controller performance across operating envelopes.
AI.result: The study quantifies how closely each model matches real-world data and shows how simplifications alter closed-loop trajectory-following performance. It identifies the fidelity level necessary for reliable controller evaluation and demonstrates how the acceptable level of simplification depends on the available headroom to the acceleration limit.
AI.conclusion: Model simplification can be tailored to the evaluation task: for scenarios with tight acceleration margins, higher-fidelity dynamics are required, while for looser margins, reduced models may suffice. The work provides guidance on choosing vehicle models for benchmarking control algorithms and contributes a standardized Autoware-compatible framework for cross-study comparisons.

title: Understanding Real-World Traffic Safety through RoadSafe365 Benchmark
summary: Although recent traffic benchmarks have advanced multimodal data analysis, they generally lack systematic evaluation aligned with official safety standards. To fill this gap, we introduce RoadSafe365, a large-scale vision-language benchmark that supports fine-grained analysis of traffic safety from extensive and diverse real-world video data collections. Unlike prior works that focus primarily on coarse accident identification, RoadSafe365 is independently curated and systematically organized using a hierarchical taxonomy that refines and extends foundational definitions of crash, incident, and violation to bridge official traffic safety standards with data-driven traffic understanding systems. RoadSafe365 provides rich attribute annotations across diverse traffic event types, environmental contexts, and interaction scenarios, yielding 36,196 annotated clips from both dashcam and surveillance cameras. Each clip is paired with multiple-choice question-answer sets, comprising 864K candidate options, 8.4K unique answers, and 36K detailed scene descriptions collectively designed for vision-language understanding and reasoning. We establish strong baselines and observe consistent gains when fine-tuning on RoadSafe365. Cross-domain experiments on both real and synthetic datasets further validate its effectiveness. Designed for large-scale training and standardized evaluation, RoadSafe365 provides a comprehensive benchmark to advance reproducible research in real-world traffic safety analysis.

AI.tldr: RoadSafe365 is a large, hierarchical vision-language benchmark for real-world traffic safety analysis, bridging official traffic safety standards with data-driven understanding through 36k annotated clips, rich attributes, QA sets, and scene descriptions to enable scalable evaluation and cross-domain research.
AI.motivation: There is a gap between official traffic safety standards and existing multimodal benchmarks, requiring fine-grained, standardized evaluation and dataset coverage across crashes, incidents, and violations to advance reproducible traffic safety analysis.
AI.method: Independent curation of a hierarchical taxonomy that refines crash/incident/violation definitions; extensive attribute annotations across event types, contexts, and interactions; collection of 36,196 clips from dashcams and surveillance cameras; pairing each clip with multiple-choice QA sets (864k candidate options, 8.4k unique answers) and 36k scene descriptions; baseline models and cross-domain experiments, including fine-tuning on RoadSafe365 and validation with real and synthetic data.
AI.result: Fine-tuning on RoadSafe365 yields consistent performance gains; strong baselines established; cross-domain experiments validate effectiveness on both real and synthetic datasets; the benchmark supports large-scale training and standardized evaluation.
AI.conclusion: RoadSafe365 provides a comprehensive, reproducible benchmark for real-world traffic safety analysis, enabling large-scale training and standardized evaluation to advance research in data-driven traffic safety understanding.

title: VertCoHiRF: Decentralized Vertical Clustering Beyond k-means
summary: Vertical Federated Learning (VFL) enables collaborative analysis across parties holding complementary feature views of the same samples, yet existing approaches are largely restricted to distributed variants of $k$-means, requiring centralized coordination or the exchange of feature-dependent numerical statistics, and exhibiting limited robustness under heterogeneous views or adversarial behavior. We introduce VertCoHiRF, a fully decentralized framework for vertical federated clustering based on structural consensus across heterogeneous views, allowing each agent to apply a base clustering method adapted to its local feature space in a peer-to-peer manner. Rather than exchanging feature-dependent statistics or relying on noise injection for privacy, agents cluster their local views independently and reconcile their proposals through identifier-level consensus. Consensus is achieved via decentralized ordinal ranking to select representative medoids, progressively inducing a shared hierarchical clustering across agents. Communication is limited to sample identifiers, cluster labels, and ordinal rankings, providing privacy by design while supporting overlapping feature partitions and heterogeneous local clustering methods, and yielding an interpretable shared Cluster Fusion Hierarchy (CFH) that captures cross-view agreement at multiple resolutions.We analyze communication complexity and robustness, and experiments demonstrate competitive clustering performance in vertical federated settings.

AI.tldr: Decentralized vertical federated clustering (VertCoHiRF) enables cross-view consensus without sharing feature-dependent statistics by using decentralized ordinal rankings to select medoids, producing an interpretable Cluster Fusion Hierarchy (CFH) and privacy-preserving multi-resolution clustering.
AI.motivation: Addresses limitations of existing VFL clustering approaches that rely on centralized coordination or exchange of feature-dependent statistics, and struggle with heterogeneous views or adversarial behavior; aims to enable fully decentralized, privacy-preserving clustering across heterogeneous local feature spaces.
AI.method: Each agent performs local clustering in its own feature space. Proposals are reconciled through identifier-level consensus using decentralized ordinal rankings to select representative medoids. A shared hierarchical clustering is induced progressively via the Cluster Fusion Hierarchy (CFH). Communication is restricted to sample identifiers, cluster labels, and ordinal rankings, enabling privacy by design and support for overlapping partitions and heterogeneous local clustering methods.
AI.result: Analysis of communication complexity and robustness; experiments show competitive clustering performance in vertical federated settings, with an interpretable CFH that captures cross-view agreement at multiple resolutions.
AI.conclusion: VertCoHiRF enables fully decentralized, privacy-preserving vertical federated clustering across heterogeneous views, yielding a scalable Cluster Fusion Hierarchy with cross-view consensus, while avoiding sharing feature-dependent statistics and noise injections.

title: Data Darwinism Part I: Unlocking the Value of Scientific Data for Pre-training
summary:
Data quality determines foundation model performance, yet systematic processing frameworks are lacking. We introduce Data Darwinism, a ten-level taxonomy (L0-L9) that conceptualizes data-model co-evolution: advanced models produce superior data for next-generation systems. We validate this on scientific literature by constructing Darwin-Science, a 900B-token corpus (L0-L5). We identify a learnability gap in raw scientific text, which we bridge via L4 (Generative Refinement) and L5 (Cognitive Completion) using frontier LLMs to explicate reasoning and terminology.
  To ensure rigorous attribution, we pre-trained daVinci-origin-3B/7B models from scratch, excluding scientific content to create contamination-free baselines. After 600B tokens of continued pre-training, Darwin-Science outperforms baselines by +2.12 (3B) and +2.95 (7B) points across 20+ benchmarks, rising to +5.60 and +8.40 points on domain-aligned tasks. Systematic progression to L5 yields a +1.36 total gain, confirming that higher-level processing unlocks latent data value. We release the Darwin-Science corpus and daVinci-origin models to enable principled, co-evolutionary development.

AI.tldr: Introduces Data Darwinism with a ten-level data-model co-evolution taxonomy (L0-L9), builds a 900B-token Darwin-Science corpus, and shows higher-level data processing (L4-L5) yields substantial performance gains. Pre-trains contamination-free baselines (daVinci-origin-3B/7B) and releases resources.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of systematic data processing frameworks for foundation models and to empirically validate data-model co-evolution and the value of higher-level data processing on model performance.
AI.method: Define a ten-level taxonomy of data processing (L0-L9), construct the Darwin-Science corpus (900B tokens across L0-L5), pre-train daVinci-origin-3B/7B models from scratch without scientific content, continue pre-training for 600B tokens, evaluate on 20+ benchmarks and domain-aligned tasks, analyze gains across levels, and release the corpus and models.
AI.result: Darwin-Science yields +2.12 (3B) and +2.95 (7B) points over baselines on 20+ benchmarks; gains rise to +5.60 and +8.40 on domain-aligned tasks; progression to L5 gives an additional +1.36 total gain, supporting the claim that higher-level processing unlocks latent data value.
AI.conclusion: Higher-level data processing within a coherent Darwinism framework unlocks extra value in data for foundation-model performance; the authors provide resources to facilitate principled co-evolutionary development.

title: From Ellipsoids to Midair Control of Dynamic Hitches
summary: The ability to dynamically manipulate interaction between cables, carried by pairs of aerial vehicles attached to the ends of each cable, can greatly improve the versatility and agility of cable-assisted aerial manipulation. Such interlacing cables create hitches by winding two or more cables around each other, which can enclose payloads or can further develop into knots. Dynamic modeling and control of such hitches is key to mastering the inter-cable manipulation in context of cable-suspended aerial manipulation. This paper introduces an ellipsoid-based kinematic model to connect the geometric nature of a hitch created by two cables and the dynamics of the hitch driven by four aerial vehicles, which reveals the control-affine form of the system. As the constraint for maintaining tension of a cable is also control-affine, we design a quadratic programming-based controller that combines Control Lyapunov and High-Order Control Barrier Functions (CLF-HOCBF-QP) to precisely track a desired hitch position and system shape while enforcing safety constraints like cable tautness. We convert desired geometric reference configurations into target robot positions and introduce a composite error into the Lyapunov function to ensure a relative degree of one to the input. Numerical simulations validate our approach, demonstrating stable, high-speed tracking of dynamic references.

AI.tldr: An ellipsoid-based kinematic model links hitch geometry (two cables between four aerial vehicles) to dynamics, enabling a CLF-HOCBF-QP controller to track hitch positions while enforcing cable tautness; simulations show stable, high-speed tracking of dynamic references.
AI.motivation: To enable dynamic, safe inter-cable manipulation (hitches) in cable-suspended aerial manipulation by capturing the geometry-dynamics relationship and enforcing safety constraints.
AI.method: Develop an ellipsoid-based, control-affine kinematic model of the hitch formed by two cables with four aerial vehicles; derive a constraint for cable tension that is also control-affine; design a quadratic programming controller integrating Control Lyapunov Functions and High-Order Control Barrier Functions (CLF-HOCBF-QP) to track a desired hitch configuration and enforce safety constraints; map geometric references to target vehicle positions and introduce a composite Lyapunov error to achieve relative degree one.
AI.result: Numerical simulations validate the method, showing stable and high-speed tracking of dynamic hitch references under the proposed CLF-HOCBF-QP controller with tension constraints.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ellipsoid-based model and CLF-HOCBF-QP controller provide a viable framework for precise, safe, and fast inter-cable manipulation in multi-UAV cable-suspended systems, enabling reliable hitch tracking under dynamic references.

title: The Double-Edged Sword of Data-Driven Super-Resolution: Adversarial Super-Resolution Models
summary: Data-driven super-resolution (SR) methods are often integrated into imaging pipelines as preprocessing steps to improve downstream tasks such as classification and detection. However, these SR models introduce a previously unexplored attack surface into imaging pipelines. In this paper, we present AdvSR, a framework demonstrating that adversarial behavior can be embedded directly into SR model weights during training, requiring no access to inputs at inference time. Unlike prior attacks that perturb inputs or rely on backdoor triggers, AdvSR operates entirely at the model level. By jointly optimizing for reconstruction quality and targeted adversarial outcomes, AdvSR produces models that appear benign under standard image quality metrics while inducing downstream misclassification. We evaluate AdvSR on three SR architectures (SRCNN, EDSR, SwinIR) paired with a YOLOv11 classifier and demonstrate that AdvSR models can achieve high attack success rates with minimal quality degradation. These findings highlight a new model-level threat for imaging pipelines, with implications for how practitioners source and validate models in safety-critical applications.

AI.tldr: Adversarial SR models are trained with weights that induce targeted misclassification through the SR process itself, without input perturbations at inference.
AI.motivation: Imaging pipelines rely on SR to improve downstream tasks, but model-level backdoors in SR can silently compromise safety-critical systems; there is a need to characterize and mitigate such model-based threats.
AI.method: Jointly optimize reconstruction quality and a targeted adversarial objective during SR model training; evaluate on three SR backbones (SRCNN, EDSR, SwinIR) coupled with a YOLOv11 classifier to assess attack effectiveness and image fidelity.
AI.result: The AdvSR approach achieves substantial attack success rates with minimal degradation in standard image quality metrics, demonstrating a stealthy, model-level vulnerability in SR-to-classifier pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Model-weight level threats in SR pipelines are a new risk for imaging systems; practitioners should exercise rigorous model sourcing, validation, and robustness testing to guard against such backdoor-like behaviors.

title: Fair Decisions from Calibrated Scores: Achieving Optimal Classification While Satisfying Sufficiency
summary: Binary classification based on predicted probabilities (scores) is a fundamental task in supervised machine learning. While thresholding scores is Bayes-optimal in the unconstrained setting, using a single threshold generally violates statistical group fairness constraints. Under independence (statistical parity) and separation (equalized odds), such thresholding suffices when the scores already satisfy the corresponding criterion. However, this does not extend to sufficiency: even perfectly group-calibrated scores -- including true class probabilities -- violate predictive parity after thresholding. In this work, we present an exact solution for optimal binary (randomized) classification under sufficiency, assuming finite sets of group-calibrated scores. We provide a geometric characterization of the feasible pairs of positive predictive value (PPV) and false omission rate (FOR) achievable by such classifiers, and use it to derive a simple post-processing algorithm that attains the optimal classifier using only group-calibrated scores and group membership. Finally, since sufficiency and separation are generally incompatible, we identify the classifier that minimizes deviation from separation subject to sufficiency, and show that it can also be obtained by our algorithm, often achieving performance comparable to the optimum.

AI.tldr: Post-processing of scores under sufficiency to achieve optimal binary classification with group-calibrated scores; geometric PPV-FOR characterization; algorithm using group scores and group membership; also identifies a classifier that minimizes deviation from separation.
AI.motivation: Thresholding scores is Bayes-optimal without constraints, but sufficient fairness cannot be ensured by a single threshold; even perfectly calibrated scores can violate predictive parity after thresholding. The work seeks exact optimality under sufficiency and a practical approximation to separation.
AI.method: Assumes a finite set of group-calibrated scores. Derives a geometric characterization of feasible PPV and FOR pairs achievable by randomized classifiers respecting sufficiency. Proposes a post-processing algorithm that uses only the scores and group membership to attain the optimal sufficiency-compliant classifier. Also formulates the problem of minimizing deviation from separation under sufficiency and shows the same algorithm achieves near-optimal results.
AI.result: Provides an exact solution for optimal binary randomized classification under sufficiency for finite score sets. Gives a constructive post-processing algorithm to realize the optimum using group-calibrated scores and group labels. Identifies a classifier that minimizes separation violation under sufficiency and shows the algorithm attains it, achieving performance close to the unconstrained optimum.
AI.conclusion: Sufficiency and separation are generally incompatible, but the proposed post-processing approach yields the optimal sufficiency-compliant classifier and a practically competitive solution regarding separation. The method is data-efficient, requiring only group-calibrated scores and group membership.

title: Time Series Reasoning via Process-Verifiable Thinking Data Synthesis and Scheduling for Tailored LLM Reasoning
summary: Time series is a pervasive data type across various application domains, rendering the reasonable solving of diverse time series tasks a long-standing goal. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs), especially their reasoning abilities unlocked through reinforcement learning (RL), have opened new opportunities for tackling tasks with long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. However, leveraging LLM reasoning for time series remains in its infancy, hindered by the absence of carefully curated time series CoT data for training, limited data efficiency caused by underexplored data scheduling, and the lack of RL algorithms tailored for exploiting such time series CoT data. In this paper, we introduce VeriTime, a framework that tailors LLMs for time series reasoning through data synthesis, data scheduling, and RL training. First, we propose a data synthesis pipeline that constructs a TS-text multimodal dataset with process-verifiable annotations. Second, we design a data scheduling mechanism that arranges training samples according to a principled hierarchy of difficulty and task taxonomy. Third, we develop a two-stage reinforcement finetuning featuring fine-grained, multi-objective rewards that leverage verifiable process-level CoT data. Extensive experiments show that VeriTime substantially boosts LLM performance across diverse time series reasoning tasks. Notably, it enables compact 3B, 4B models to achieve reasoning capabilities on par with or exceeding those of larger proprietary LLMs.

AI.tldr: A framework (VeriTime) for time-series reasoning with LLMs using data synthesis, scheduling, and RL finetuning; shows smaller models achieve competitive reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: Addresses lack of CoT-quality TS data, data-inefficiency, and absence of TS-tailored RL methods for LLMs in time-series tasks.
AI.method: Three pillars: (1) synth TS-text multimodal dataset with verifiable annotations; (2) scheme to order training samples by difficulty and taxonomic task categories; (3) two-stage RL finetuning with multi-objective rewards leveraging verifiable CoT.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate substantial gains across TS reasoning tasks; 3B and 4B models reach or surpass larger proprietary LLMs in TS reasoning.
AI.conclusion: VeriTime demonstrates feasibility of specialized data synthesis, scheduling, and RL to unlock strong time-series reasoning in compact LLMs; potential for practical impact and further improvements.

title: Self-Supervised Bootstrapping of Action-Predictive Embodied Reasoning
summary: Embodied Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning has significantly enhanced Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, yet current methods rely on rigid templates to specify reasoning primitives (e.g., objects in the scene, high-level plans, structural affordances). These templates can force policies to process irrelevant information that distracts from critical action-prediction signals. This creates a bottleneck: without successful policies, we cannot verify reasoning quality; without quality reasoning, we cannot build robust policies. We introduce R&B-EnCoRe, which enables models to bootstrap embodied reasoning from internet-scale knowledge through self-supervised refinement. By treating reasoning as a latent variable within importance-weighted variational inference, models can generate and distill a refined reasoning training dataset of embodiment-specific strategies without external rewards, verifiers, or human annotation. We validate R&B-EnCoRe across manipulation (Franka Panda in simulation, WidowX in hardware), legged navigation (bipedal, wheeled, bicycle, quadruped), and autonomous driving embodiments using various VLA architectures with 1B, 4B, 7B, and 30B parameters. Our approach achieves 28% gains in manipulation success, 101% improvement in navigation scores, and 21% reduction in collision-rate metric over models that indiscriminately reason about all available primitives. R&B-EnCoRe enables models to distill reasoning that is predictive of successful control, bypassing manual annotation engineering while grounding internet-scale knowledge in physical execution.

AI.tldr: Proposes R&B-EnCoRe: a self-supervised latent-variable approach to bootstrap embodied reasoning for Vision-Language-Action models, improving task performance across manipulation, navigation, and driving by filtering relevant primitives without external rewards.
AI.motivation: Current embodied CoT relies on rigid templates and external rewards/verifiers; there is a need to learn flexible, action-relevant reasoning that can be distilled into robust policies without manual annotation.
AI.method: Treat reasoning as a latent variable and optimize via importance-weighted variational inference to generate a refined dataset of embodiment-specific strategies. Self-supervised, no external rewards, verifiers, or human annotation. Evaluated across manipulation (simulation and hardware), legged navigation, and autonomous driving with models of 1B–30B parameters.
AI.result: Significant improvements over baselines that reason about all primitives: manipulation success +28%, navigation scores +101%, collision-rate reduced by 21%. Demonstrates distillation of reasoning predictive of successful control and grounding of internet-scale knowledge in physical execution across diverse embodiments.
AI.conclusion: R&B-EnCoRe enables self-supervised, latent reasoning that improves policy quality by focusing on pertinent primitives, reducing reliance on handcrafted templates and annotations, and generalizing across tasks and architectures.

title: 3D Transport-based Morphometry (3D-TBM) for medical image analysis
summary: Transport-Based Morphometry (TBM) has emerged as a new framework for 3D medical image analysis. By embedding images into a transport domain via invertible transformations, TBM facilitates effective classification, regression, and other tasks using transport-domain features. Crucially, the inverse mapping enables the projection of analytic results back into the original image space, allowing researchers to directly interpret clinical features associated with model outputs in a spatially meaningful way. To facilitate broader adoption of TBM in clinical imaging research, we present 3D-TBM, a tool designed for morphological analysis of 3D medical images. The framework includes data preprocessing, computation of optimal transport embeddings, and analytical methods such as visualization of main transport directions, together with techniques for discerning discriminating directions and related analysis methods. We also provide comprehensive documentation and practical tutorials to support researchers interested in applying 3D-TBM in their own medical imaging studies. The source code is publicly available through PyTransKit.

AI.tldr: 3D-TBM is a practical, open-source toolkit for applying Transport-Based Morphometry to 3D medical images, enabling transport-domain embeddings, invertible mappings for interpretability, preprocessing, visualization, and discrimination analyses, with tutorials and PyTransKit release.
AI.motivation: TBM provides a powerful framework for morphological analysis; there is a need for a user-friendly 3D tool to promote adoption, enabling interpretable, spatially localized results via invertible transport mappings.
AI.method: Compute optimal transport embeddings for 3D images; use invertible transformations to map results back to the original image space; include data preprocessing, visualization of main transport directions, methods to identify discriminating directions, and related analysis techniques; provide documentation and tutorials; source code in PyTransKit.
AI.result: The work presents a software framework (3D-TBM) with capabilities for embedding, interpretation, and visualization. The abstract describes the components and tutorials but does not report empirical results.
AI.conclusion: 3D-TBM offers a practical, interpretable, and accessible platform for 3D morphological analysis in medical imaging, with open-access tooling and comprehensive documentation to facilitate adoption.

title: Incorruptible Neural Networks: Training Models that can Generalize to Large Internal Perturbations
summary: Flat regions of the neural network loss landscape have long been hypothesized to correlate with better generalization properties. A closely related but distinct problem is training models that are robust to internal perturbations to their weights, which may be an important need for future low-power hardware platforms. In this paper, we explore the usage of two methods, sharpness-aware minimization (SAM) and random-weight perturbation (RWP), to find minima robust to a variety of random corruptions to weights. We consider the problem from two angles: generalization (how do we reduce the noise-robust generalization gap) and optimization (how do we maximize performance from optimizers when subject to strong perturbations). First, we establish, both theoretically and empirically, that an over-regularized RWP training objective is optimal for noise-robust generalization. For small-magnitude noise, we find that SAM's adversarial objective further improves performance over any RWP configuration, but performs poorly for large-magnitude noise. We link the cause of this to a vanishing-gradient effect, caused by unevenness in the loss landscape, affecting both SAM and RWP. Lastly, we demonstrate that dynamically adjusting the perturbation strength to match the evolution of the loss landscape improves optimizing for these perturbed objectives.

AI.tldr: RWP with strong regularization yields best noise-robust generalization; SAM helps for small perturbations but struggles with large perturbations; gradient-imbalanced loss landscapes cause vanishing gradients affecting both methods; dynamically adjusting perturbation strength to landscape evolution improves optimization under perturbations.
AI.motivation: Address the need for weight-perturbation robustness in neural networks, motivated by robust generalization and potential hardware-induced perturbations on low-power devices; compare sharpness-aware minimization (SAM) and random-weight perturbation (RWP) as two strategies to locate minima robust to perturbations.
AI.method: Theoretically and empirically evaluate SAM and RWP across noise perturbations. Analyze generalization under weight noise and optimization performance under perturbations. Identify the impact of loss-landscape unevenness on gradient signals, and test dynamic perturbation-strength schedules that adapt to landscape evolution.
AI.result: An over-regularized RWP objective is optimal for noise-robust generalization. For small-magnitude noise, SAM’s adversarial objective improves performance beyond any RWP setup, but SAM performs poorly under large-magnitude noise due to vanishing-gradient effects from landscape unevenness, affecting both SAM and RWP. Dynamically adjusting perturbation strength to mirror the loss landscape evolution enhances optimization under perturbed objectives.
AI.conclusion: Careful tuning of perturbation strength is crucial for robustness: RWP with strong regularization best supports generalization to weight perturbations, while SAM offers gains only for small noise; understanding and mitigating gradient-vanishing dynamics induced by landscape unevenness is important for both methods. Adaptive perturbation strategies that track the loss landscape can improve optimization under perturbations.

title: LQA: A Lightweight Quantized-Adaptive Framework for Vision-Language Models on the Edge
summary: Deploying Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on edge devices is challenged by resource constraints and performance degradation under distribution shifts. While test-time adaptation (TTA) can counteract such shifts, existing methods are too resource-intensive for on-device deployment. To address this challenge, we propose LQA, a lightweight, quantized-adaptive framework for VLMs that combines a modality-aware quantization strategy with gradient-free test-time adaptation. We introduce Selective Hybrid Quantization (SHQ) and a quantized, gradient-free adaptation mechanism to enable robust and efficient VLM deployment on resource-constrained hardware. Experiments across both synthetic and real-world distribution shifts show that LQA improves overall adaptation performance by 4.5\%, uses less memory than full-precision models, and significantly outperforms gradient-based TTA methods, achieving up to 19.9$\times$ lower memory usage across seven open-source datasets. These results demonstrate that LQA offers a practical pathway for robust, privacy-preserving, and efficient VLM deployment on edge devices.

AI.tldr: LQA is a lightweight framework for on-device adaptation of Vision-Language Models using modality-aware quantization and gradient-free TTA to enable robust, memory-efficient edge deployment.
AI.motivation: Deploy VLMs on resource-constrained edge devices facing distribution shifts; test-time adaptation is too resource-intensive for on-device use.
AI.method: Introduce Selective Hybrid Quantization (SHQ) with a gradient-free adaptation mechanism; modality-aware quantization tailored to VLMs to enable efficient, on-device TTA without gradients.
AI.result: Empirical results show 4.5% improvement in overall adaptation performance; lower memory usage than full-precision models; up to 19.9x reduction in memory compared to gradient-based TTA across seven open-source datasets; outperforms gradient-based TTA methods.
AI.conclusion: LQA offers a practical, privacy-preserving, and efficient pathway for robust VLM deployment on edge devices, combining quantization and gradient-free adaptation to mitigate distribution shifts with limited resources.

title: Chamelion: Reliable Change Detection for Long-Term LiDAR Mapping in Transient Environments
summary: Online change detection is crucial for mobile robots to efficiently navigate through dynamic environments. Detecting changes in transient settings, such as active construction sites or frequently reconfigured indoor spaces, is particularly challenging due to frequent occlusions and spatiotemporal variations. Existing approaches often struggle to detect changes and fail to update the map across different observations. To address these limitations, we propose a dual-head network designed for online change detection and long-term map maintenance. A key difficulty in this task is the collection and alignment of real-world data, as manually registering structural differences over time is both labor-intensive and often impractical. To overcome this, we develop a data augmentation strategy that synthesizes structural changes by importing elements from different scenes, enabling effective model training without the need for extensive ground-truth annotations. Experiments conducted at real-world construction sites and in indoor office environments demonstrate that our approach generalizes well across diverse scenarios, achieving efficient and accurate map updates.\resubmit{Our source code and additional material are available at: https://chamelion-pages.github.io/.

AI.tldr: Dual-head network for online change detection and long-term map maintenance, leveraging synthetic cross-scene data augmentation to train without extensive ground-truth; demonstrates generalization to construction sites and indoor environments with efficient map updates; code released.
AI.motivation: Tasked with detecting transient changes and maintaining accurate maps in dynamic environments, where occlusions and spatiotemporal variation hinder performance and collecting aligned real-world data is labor-intensive.
AI.method: Introduce a dual-head network for online change detection and long-term map maintenance. Employ a data augmentation strategy that synthesizes structural changes by importing elements from different scenes to simulate time-varying structure without requiring manual ground-truth registrations. Train on augmented data and evaluate on real-world construction sites and indoor office environments.
AI.result: The approach generalizes across diverse scenarios and yields efficient and accurate map updates, as demonstrated in real-world settings. Source code and supplementary materials are provided.
AI.conclusion: Integrating online change detection with long-term mapping through synthetic augmentation reduces data collection burden and enables practical, scalable map maintenance for dynamic environments.

title: TwistNet-2D: Learning Second-Order Channel Interactions via Spiral Twisting for Texture Recognition
summary: Second-order feature statistics are central to texture recognition, yet current methods face a fundamental tension: bilinear pooling and Gram matrices capture global channel correlations but collapse spatial structure, while self-attention models spatial context through weighted aggregation rather than explicit pairwise feature interactions. We introduce TwistNet-2D, a lightweight module that computes \emph{local} pairwise channel products under directional spatial displacement, jointly encoding where features co-occur and how they interact. The core component, Spiral-Twisted Channel Interaction (STCI), shifts one feature map along a prescribed direction before element-wise channel multiplication, thereby capturing the cross-position co-occurrence patterns characteristic of structured and periodic textures. Aggregating four directional heads with learned channel reweighting and injecting the result through a sigmoid-gated residual path, \TwistNet incurs only 3.5% additional parameters and 2% additional FLOPs over ResNet-18, yet consistently surpasses both parameter-matched and substantially larger baselines -- including ConvNeXt, Swin Transformer, and hybrid CNN--Transformer architectures -- across four texture and fine-grained recognition benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Introduces TwistNet-2D with Spiral-Twisted Channel Interaction (STCI) to model local cross-position channel co-occurrences for texture recognition with negligible overhead.
AI.motivation: Address the trade-off between bilinear/Gram-based global channel correlations (loss of spatial structure) and self-attention (lacks explicit pairwise feature interactions). Propose efficient local cross-position interactions for textures.
AI.method: Four directional STCI heads—shift one feature map along a direction, then multiply channels element-wise; aggregate with learned channel reweighting; fuse via sigmoid-gated residual path; minimal overhead over ResNet-18.
AI.result: Achieves ~3.5% extra parameters and ~2% extra FLOPs relative to ResNet-18; outperforms parameter-matched and larger baselines (ConvNeXt, Swin Transformer, CNN-Transformer hybrids) on four texture and fine-grained benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Efficiently captures local co-occurrence patterns, offering competitive accuracy with small computational cost, suitable for texture/fine-grained recognition.

title: Revisiting Robustness for LLM Safety Alignment via Selective Geometry Control
summary: Safety alignment of large language models remains brittle under domain shift and noisy preference supervision. Most existing robust alignment methods focus on uncertainty in alignment data, while overlooking optimization-induced fragility in preference-based objectives. In this work, we revisit robustness for LLM safety alignment from an optimization geometry perspective, and argue that robustness failures cannot be addressed by data-centric methods alone. We propose ShaPO, a geometry-aware preference optimization framework that enforces worst-case alignment objectives via selective geometry control over alignment-critical parameter subspace. By avoiding uniform geometry constraints, ShaPO mitigates the over-regularization that can harm robustness under distribution shift. We instantiate ShaPO at two levels: token-level ShaPO stabilizes likelihood-based surrogate optimization, while reward-level ShaPO enforces reward-consistent optimization under noisy supervision. Across diverse safety benchmarks and noisy preference settings, ShaPO consistently improves safety robustness over popular preference optimization methods. Moreover, ShaPO composes cleanly with data-robust objectives, yielding additional gains and empirically supporting the proposed optimization-geometry perspective.

AI.tldr: ShaPO is a geometry-aware optimization framework to robustify safety alignment of LLMs under domain shift and noisy preferences, using selective geometry control over critical parameter subspaces with token-level and reward-level variants, improving robustness over standard data-centric methods and combining additively with data-robust objectives.
AI.motivation: Robustness gaps in LLM safety alignment stem not only from data uncertainty but also from optimization-induced fragility in preference-based objectives; data-centric approaches alone fail under distribution shift, necessitating geometry-aware optimization strategies.
AI.method: Introduce ShaPO, a geometry-aware preference optimization framework that enforces worst-case objectives via selective geometry control over alignment-critical parameter subspaces. It includes token-level ShaPO to stabilize likelihood-based surrogate optimization and reward-level ShaPO to ensure reward-consistent optimization under noisy supervision. The approach avoids uniform geometry constraints to prevent over-regularization and can compose with data-robust objectives.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across diverse safety benchmarks with noisy preferences shows ShaPO consistently improves safety robustness compared with popular preference optimization baselines; combining ShaPO with data-robust objectives yields additional gains, supporting the proposed optimization-geometry perspective.
AI.conclusion: Optimization geometry plays a key role in robustness for LLM safety alignment; ShaPO offers a principled, modular framework that enhances robustness beyond data-centric methods and can be integrated with data-robust objectives for further gains.

title: Emergent Misalignment is Easy, Narrow Misalignment is Hard
summary: Finetuning large language models on narrowly harmful datasets can cause them to become emergently misaligned, giving stereotypically `evil' responses across diverse unrelated settings. Concerningly, a pre-registered survey of experts failed to predict this result, highlighting our poor understanding of the inductive biases governing learning and generalisation in LLMs. We use emergent misalignment (EM) as a case study to investigate these inductive biases and find that models can just learn the narrow dataset task, but that the general solution appears to be more stable and more efficient. To establish this, we build on the result that different EM finetunes converge to the same linear representation of general misalignment, which can be used to mediate misaligned behaviour. We find a linear representation of the narrow solution also exists, and can be learned by introducing a KL divergence loss. Comparing these representations reveals that general misalignment achieves lower loss, is more robust to perturbations, and is more influential in the pre-training distribution. This work isolates a concrete representation of general misalignment for monitoring and mitigation. More broadly, it offers a detailed case study and preliminary metrics for investigating how inductive biases shape generalisation in LLMs. We open-source all code, datasets and model finetunes.

AI.tldr: Emergent misalignment from finetuning LLMs on narrowly harmful data can be understood as two linear representations: a general misalignment subspace that is robust and influential, and a narrow-solution subspace. A KL-divergence loss can induce the narrow representation, and contrasting the two reveals general misalignment dominates in loss, robustness, and pretraining influence. This work offers concrete metrics and opens resources for monitoring and mitigation.
AI.motivation: To understand how inductive biases shape generalization and misalignment in LLMs, using emergent misalignment (EM) as a case study to identify stable, transferable representations that underlie misaligned behavior.
AI.method: Build on the finding that EM finetunes converge to a common linear representation of general misalignment. Investigate whether a linear representation for the narrow (task-specific) solution exists by introducing a KL-divergence loss to guide learning. Compare the two representations across metrics such as loss, perturbation robustness, and influence on the pretrained distribution. Provide monitoring/mitigation insights and open-source code/datasets.
AI.result: There exists a linear representation for the narrow solution that can be learned via KL-divergence loss. Different EM finetunes converge to the same linear representation of general misalignment, which is lower in loss, more robust to perturbations, and more influential in the pretraining distribution compared to the narrow solution. This yields a concrete, monitorable representation of general misalignment and supports metrics for investigating inductive biases in LLM generalization.
AI.conclusion: The study isolates a concrete representation for general misalignment amenable to monitoring and mitigation, offers a detailed case study with preliminary metrics on inductive biases shaping LLM generalization, and publishes code, datasets, and finetunes to facilitate broader evaluation.

title: STEP: Warm-Started Visuomotor Policies with Spatiotemporal Consistency Prediction
summary: Diffusion policies have recently emerged as a powerful paradigm for visuomotor control in robotic manipulation due to their ability to model the distribution of action sequences and capture multimodality. However, iterative denoising leads to substantial inference latency, limiting control frequency in real-time closed-loop systems. Existing acceleration methods either reduce sampling steps, bypass diffusion through direct prediction, or reuse past actions, but often struggle to jointly preserve action quality and achieve consistently low latency. In this work, we propose STEP, a lightweight spatiotemporal consistency prediction mechanism to construct high-quality warm-start actions that are both distributionally close to the target action and temporally consistent, without compromising the generative capability of the original diffusion policy. Then, we propose a velocity-aware perturbation injection mechanism that adaptively modulates actuation excitation based on temporal action variation to prevent execution stall especially for real-world tasks. We further provide a theoretical analysis showing that the proposed prediction induces a locally contractive mapping, ensuring convergence of action errors during diffusion refinement. We conduct extensive evaluations on nine simulated benchmarks and two real-world tasks. Notably, STEP with 2 steps can achieve an average 21.6% and 27.5% higher success rate than BRIDGER and DDIM on the RoboMimic benchmark and real-world tasks, respectively. These results demonstrate that STEP consistently advances the Pareto frontier of inference latency and success rate over existing methods.

AI.tldr: STEP enables fast, high-quality diffusion-based visuomotor control by warm-starting with spatiotemporal-consistent predictions and adaptive perturbations; it reduces inference latency while maintaining or improving success rates.
AI.motivation: Diffusion policies capture action distributions and multimodality but require iterative denoising, causing high inference latency for real-time closed-loop control. Existing acceleration methods trade off action quality or generative ability, limiting performance.
AI.method: Introduce STEP: a lightweight spatiotemporal consistency predictor to produce warm-start actions that are distributionally close to the target and temporally coherent, without sacrificing the diffusion policy's generative power. Add a velocity-aware perturbation injection mechanism to adapt actuation excitation to temporal action variation, preventing stalls. Provide a theoretical analysis showing the predictor induces a locally contractive mapping to guarantee convergence of action errors during diffusion refinement. Evaluate on nine simulated benchmarks and two real-world tasks.
AI.result: With STEP using 2 sampling steps, average improvements of 21.6% in success rate over BRIDGER and 27.5% over DDIM on RoboMimic benchmark and real-world tasks, respectively. STEP consistently improves the latency–success-rate Pareto frontier compared with existing methods.
AI.conclusion: STEP offers a low-latency, high-quality diffusion-based control approach that preserves generative capability while achieving consistent performance gains across benchmarks.

title: VideoNeuMat: Neural Material Extraction from Generative Video Models
summary: Creating photorealistic materials for 3D rendering requires exceptional artistic skill. Generative models for materials could help, but are currently limited by the lack of high-quality training data. While recent video generative models effortlessly produce realistic material appearances, this knowledge remains entangled with geometry and lighting. We present VideoNeuMat, a two-stage pipeline that extracts reusable neural material assets from video diffusion models. First, we finetune a large video model (Wan 2.1 14B) to generate material sample videos under controlled camera and lighting trajectories, effectively creating a "virtual gonioreflectometer" that preserves the model's material realism while learning a structured measurement pattern. Second, we reconstruct compact neural materials from these videos through a Large Reconstruction Model (LRM) finetuned from a smaller Wan 1.3B video backbone. From 17 generated video frames, our LRM performs single-pass inference to predict neural material parameters that generalize to novel viewing and lighting conditions. The resulting materials exhibit realism and diversity far exceeding the limited synthetic training data, demonstrating that material knowledge can be successfully transferred from internet-scale video models into standalone, reusable neural 3D assets.

AI.tldr: A two-stage method that extracts reusable neural material assets from video diffusion models by first simulating a controlled measurement setup and then reconstructing material parameters from short videos, enabling realistic, diverse 3D materials without manual data collection.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of high-quality training data for photorealistic materials; video models offer realism but intertwine material appearance with geometry and lighting; need decoupled, transferable neural material representations.
AI.method: Stage 1 finetunes Wan 2.1 14B video model to generate material samples under controlled camera/lighting, acting as a virtual gonioreflectometer to capture material behavior; Stage 2 finetunes a Large Reconstruction Model (LRM) from Wan 1.3B video backbone and performs single-pass inference on 17 frames to predict neural material parameters.
AI.result: Realism and diversity surpass limited synthetic data; demonstrates transfer of material knowledge from internet-scale video models into reusable neural 3D assets; parameters generalize to novel viewing and lighting conditions.
AI.conclusion: The approach validates a practical pipeline to obtain portable neural materials from video diffusion models, enabling scalable creation of photorealistic materials for 3D rendering with minimal manual data collection.

title: Scalable Dexterous Robot Learning with AR-based Remote Human-Robot Interactions
summary: This paper focuses on the scalable robot learning for manipulation in the dexterous robot arm-hand systems, where the remote human-robot interactions via augmented reality (AR) are established to collect the expert demonstration data for improving efficiency. In such a system, we present a unified framework to address the general manipulation task problem. Specifically, the proposed method consists of two phases: i) In the first phase for pretraining, the policy is created in a behavior cloning (BC) manner, through leveraging the learning data from our AR-based remote human-robot interaction system; ii) In the second phase, a contrastive learning empowered reinforcement learning (RL) method is developed to obtain more efficient and robust policy than the BC, and thus a projection head is designed to accelerate the learning progress. An event-driven augmented reward is adopted for enhancing the safety. To validate the proposed method, both the physics simulations via PyBullet and real-world experiments are carried out. The results demonstrate that compared to the classic proximal policy optimization and soft actor-critic policies, our method not only significantly speeds up the inference, but also achieves much better performance in terms of the success rate for fulfilling the manipulation tasks. By conducting the ablation study, it is confirmed that the proposed RL with contrastive learning overcomes policy collapse. Supplementary demonstrations are available at https://cyberyyc.github.io/.

AI.tldr: Two-stage dexterous manipulation learning framework: pretrain with behavior cloning from AR-based demonstrations, then apply contrastive-learning empowered RL with a projection head; adds event-driven augmented rewards for safety; validated in PyBullet and real-world; outperforms PPO and SAC and mitigates policy collapse via ablation.
AI.motivation: Improve sample efficiency and safety in dexterous manipulation by leveraging remote AR demonstrations to bootstrap policy learning and address RL instability (policy collapse).
AI.method: Phase 1: Pretrain a policy via behavior cloning using data collected from an AR-based remote human-robot interaction system. Phase 2: Apply reinforcement learning enhanced with contrastive learning; incorporate a projection head to accelerate learning; introduce event-driven augmented rewards for safety. Evaluate in PyBullet simulations and real-world experiments; perform ablations to verify the benefits of contrastive RL.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves faster inference and higher success rates than PPO and SAC; ablation confirms that contrastive RL mitigates policy collapse; supplementary demonstrations provided.
AI.conclusion: A two-phase framework combining AR-based BC with contrastive-learning RL and safety-oriented rewards yields robust and efficient manipulation policies for dexterous robots, demonstrated in both simulation and real-world experiments and outperforming standard baselines.

title: ToolSelf: Unifying Task Execution and Self-Reconfiguration via Tool-Driven Intrinsic Adaptation
summary: Agentic systems powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable potential in tackling complex, long-horizon tasks. However, their efficacy is fundamentally constrained by static configurations governing agent behaviors, which are fixed prior to execution and fail to adapt to evolving task dynamics. Existing approaches, relying on manual orchestration or heuristic-based patches, often struggle with poor generalization and fragmented optimization. To transcend these limitations, we propose ToolSelf, a novel paradigm enabling tool-driven runtime self-reconfiguration. By abstracting configuration updates as a callable tool, ToolSelf unifies task execution and self-adjustment into a single action space, achieving a phase transition from external rules to intrinsic parameters. Agents can thereby autonomously update their sub-goals and context based on task progression, and correspondingly adapt their strategy and toolbox, transforming from passive executors into dual managers of both task and self. We further devise Configuration-Aware Two-stage Training (CAT), combining rejection sampling fine-tuning with trajectory-level reinforcement learning to internalize this meta-capability. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that ToolSelf rivals specialized workflows while generalizing to novel tasks, achieving a 24.1% average performance gain and illuminating a path toward truly self-adaptive agents.

AI.tldr: ToolSelf introduces a tool-driven runtime self-reconfiguration paradigm for LLM-powered agents, unifying task execution and self-adjustment by treating configuration updates as a callable tool; paired with CAT training, it enables self-managed sub-goals and tool selection, achieving 24.1% average gains and better generalization to new tasks.
AI.motivation: Static agent configurations limit adaptation to evolving task dynamics; prior approaches relying on manual orchestration or patchwork optimization suffer from poor generalization and fragmentation.
AI.method: Define ToolSelf where configuration updates are treated as a callable tool, unifying task execution and self-adjustment into a single action space; develop Configuration-Aware Two-stage Training (CAT) combining rejection sampling fine-tuning with trajectory-level reinforcement learning to internalize the meta-capability.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across diverse benchmarks shows ToolSelf rivals specialized workflows and generalizes to novel tasks, achieving a 24.1% average performance gain.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates a path toward truly self-adaptive agents, enabling autonomous updates of sub-goals and toolbox, and shifting from externally defined rules to intrinsic parameters.

title: Aerial Manipulation with Contact-Aware Onboard Perception and Hybrid Control
summary: Aerial manipulation (AM) promises to move Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) beyond passive inspection to contact-rich tasks such as grasping, assembly, and in-situ maintenance. Most prior AM demonstrations rely on external motion capture (MoCap) and emphasize position control for coarse interactions, limiting deployability. We present a fully onboard perception-control pipeline for contact-rich AM that achieves accurate motion tracking and regulated contact wrenches without MoCap. The main components are (1) an augmented visual-inertial odometry (VIO) estimator with contact-consistency factors that activate only during interaction, tightening uncertainty around the contact frame and reducing drift, and (2) image-based visual servoing (IBVS) to mitigate perception-control coupling, together with a hybrid force-motion controller that regulates contact wrenches and lateral motion for stable contact. Experiments show that our approach closes the perception-to-wrench loop using only onboard sensing, yielding an velocity estimation improvement of 66.01% at contact, reliable target approach, and stable force holding-pointing toward deployable, in-the-wild aerial manipulation.

AI.tldr: Onboard perception-control for contact-rich aerial manipulation using augmented VIO with contact-consistency factors, IBVS, and a hybrid force-motion controller, enabling operation without motion capture and improving contact-wrench regulation and velocity estimation.
AI.motivation: Overcome reliance on external motion capture and coarse position control to enable deployable, contact-rich AM with only onboard sensing.
AI.method: 1) Augmented VIO with contact-consistency factors activated during interaction to tighten uncertainty around the contact frame; 2) image-based visual servoing (IBVS) to mitigate perception-control coupling; 3) a hybrid force-motion controller that regulates contact wrenches and lateral motion for stable contact.
AI.result: Experiments show onboard loop closure from perception to wrench; 66.01% improvement in velocity estimation at contact; reliable target approach and stable force holding-pointing in real-world AM scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a fully onboard perception-control pipeline for contact-rich aerial manipulation without external MoCap, achieving accurate motion tracking and regulated contact wrenches in-the-wild.

title: Cross-View World Models
summary: World models enable agents to plan by imagining future states, but existing approaches operate from a single viewpoint, typically egocentric, even when other perspectives would make planning easier; navigation, for instance, benefits from a bird's-eye view. We introduce Cross-View World Models (XVWM), trained with a cross-view prediction objective: given a sequence of frames from one viewpoint, predict the future state from the same or a different viewpoint after an action is taken. Enforcing cross-view consistency acts as geometric regularization: because the input and output views may share little or no visual overlap, to predict across viewpoints, the model must learn view-invariant representations of the environment's 3D structure. We train on synchronized multi-view gameplay data from Aimlabs, an aim-training platform providing precisely aligned multi-camera recordings with high-frequency action labels. The resulting model gives agents parallel imagination streams across viewpoints, enabling planning in whichever frame of reference best suits the task while executing from the egocentric view. Our results show that multi-view consistency provides a strong learning signal for spatially grounded representations. Finally, predicting the consequences of one's actions from another viewpoint may offer a foundation for perspective-taking in multi-agent settings.

AI.tldr: Cross-View World Models (XVWM) learn view-invariant 3D representations by predicting future states across viewpoints, enabling multi-view imagination streams for planning; trained on synchronized multi-view gameplay data; cross-view consistency acts as a geometric regularizer; potential for perspective-taking in multi-agent settings.
AI.motivation: Single-view world models limit planning efficiency; leveraging alternative viewpoints (e.g., bird's-eye) can simplify planning and improve spatial reasoning; enforce cross-view consistency to learn geometry-grounded representations.
AI.method: Train a model to forecast future states from the same or a different viewpoint after an action, using a cross-view prediction objective. Use synchronized multi-view data from Aimlabs with aligned video and action labels. Learn view-invariant, 3D-aware representations and parallel imagined futures across viewpoints, integrated with egocentric action execution.
AI.result: Cross-view consistency provides a strong signal for learning spatial representations; the model produces parallel imagination streams across viewpoints and enables planning from whichever frame is most suitable; initial results suggest improved cross-view predictive performance and potential gains in planning and multi-view tasks; demonstrates foundation for perspective-taking in multi-agent settings.
AI.conclusion: Cross-view prediction with cross-view consistency is an effective geometric regularizer for learning 3D environment representations, enabling flexible planning across viewpoints and laying groundwork for perspective-taking in multi-agent contexts.

title: Controllable Value Alignment in Large Language Models through Neuron-Level Editing
summary: Aligning large language models (LLMs) with human values has become increasingly important as their influence on human behavior and decision-making expands. However, existing steering-based alignment methods suffer from limited controllability: steering a target value often unintentionally activates other, non-target values. To characterize this limitation, we introduce value leakage, a diagnostic notion that captures the unintended activation of non-target values during value steering, along with a normalized leakage metric grounded in Schwartz's value theory. In light of this analysis, we propose NeVA, a neuron-level editing framework for controllable value alignment in LLMs. NeVA identifies sparse, value-relevant neurons and performs inference-time activation editing, enabling fine-grained control without parameter updates or retraining. Experiments show that NeVA achieves stronger target value alignment while incurring smaller performance degradation on general capability. Moreover, NeVA significantly reduces the average leakage, with residual effects largely confined to semantically related value classes. Overall, NeVA offers a more controllable and interpretable mechanism for value alignment.

AI.tldr: NeVA enables neuron-level, inference-time activation editing to align LLMs with target values while reducing unintended activation (value leakage) of non-target values, with leakage largely confined to related value classes.
AI.motivation: Steering-based alignment methods exhibit value leakage and limited controllability; a fine-grained, interpretable mechanism is needed to align models to human values without sacrificing general capabilities.
AI.method: Identify sparse, value-relevant neurons and perform activation editing at inference time (no parameter updates or retraining). Use a normalized leakage metric grounded in Schwartz's value theory to quantify non-target activation.
