AI.result: Empirical results show stronger target-value alignment and smaller degradation to general capabilities; leakage is significantly reduced and mainly confined to semantically related value classes.
AI.conclusion: NeVA provides a controllable, interpretable value-alignment mechanism by editing neuron activations rather than model parameters, achieving tighter control with reduced leakage.

title: MemFly: On-the-Fly Memory Optimization via Information Bottleneck
summary: Long-term memory enables large language model agents to tackle complex tasks through historical interactions. However, existing frameworks encounter a fundamental dilemma between compressing redundant information efficiently and maintaining precise retrieval for downstream tasks. To bridge this gap, we propose MemFly, a framework grounded in information bottleneck principles that facilitates on-the-fly memory evolution for LLMs. Our approach minimizes compression entropy while maximizing relevance entropy via a gradient-free optimizer, constructing a stratified memory structure for efficient storage. To fully leverage MemFly, we develop a hybrid retrieval mechanism that seamlessly integrates semantic, symbolic, and topological pathways, incorporating iterative refinement to handle complex multi-hop queries. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that MemFly substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in memory coherence, response fidelity, and accuracy.

AI.tldr: MemFly: an LLM memory framework using information-bottleneck principles to evolve memory on-the-fly, with stratified memory and a hybrid retrieval system, achieving better memory coherence, fidelity, and accuracy.
AI.motivation: Addresses the trade-off between compressing historical interactions and maintaining precise retrieval in long-term memory for LLM agents.
AI.method: Use information bottleneck to minimize compression entropy and maximize relevance entropy via a gradient-free optimizer; build a stratified memory structure; develop a hybrid retrieval mechanism combining semantic, symbolic, and topological pathways with iterative refinement for complex multi-hop queries.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments show MemFly substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in memory coherence, response fidelity, and accuracy.
AI.conclusion: MemFly effectively bridges memory compression and retrieval precision, enabling efficient, accurate long-term memory for LLMs with on-the-fly memory evolution and multi-path retrieval.

title: Informative Object-centric Next Best View for Object-aware 3D Gaussian Splatting in Cluttered Scenes
comment: 9 pages, 8 figures, 4 tables, accepted to ICRA 2026
summary: In cluttered scenes with inevitable occlusions and incomplete observations, selecting informative viewpoints is essential for building a reliable representation. In this context, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) offers a distinct advantage, as it can explicitly guide the selection of subsequent viewpoints and then refine the representation with new observations. However, existing approaches rely solely on geometric cues, neglect manipulation-relevant semantics, and tend to prioritize exploitation over exploration. To tackle these limitations, we introduce an instance-aware Next Best View (NBV) policy that prioritizes underexplored regions by leveraging object features. Specifically, our object-aware 3DGS distills instancelevel information into one-hot object vectors, which are used to compute confidence-weighted information gain that guides the identification of regions associated with erroneous and uncertain Gaussians. Furthermore, our method can be easily adapted to an object-centric NBV, which focuses view selection on a target object, thereby improving reconstruction robustness to object placement. Experiments demonstrate that our NBV policy reduces depth error by up to 77.14% on the synthetic dataset and 34.10% on the real-world GraspNet dataset compared to baselines. Moreover, compared to targeting the entire scene, performing NBV on a specific object yields an additional reduction of 25.60% in depth error for that object. We further validate the effectiveness of our approach through real-world robotic manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: An instance-aware NBV policy for 3D Gaussian Splatting uses object-level features to steer exploration, improving depth reconstruction and robustness, including an object-centric variant.
AI.motivation: Address occlusions and incomplete observations in cluttered scenes by moving beyond geometry-only NBV to leverage manipulation-relevant semantics, balancing exploration and exploitation.
AI.method: Distill instance-level information into one-hot object vectors and compute confidence-weighted information gain to guide viewpoints toward underexplored regions with erroneous/uncertain Gaussians; supports an object-centric NBV focusing on a target object; integrates with 3D Gaussian Splatting for iterative refinement.
AI.result: Depth error reduced by up to 77.14% on synthetic data and 34.10% on GraspNet real data vs baselines; object-centric NBV yields an additional 25.60% depth error reduction for the target object; validated in real-world robotic manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: An effective, semantics-informed NBV policy that enhances 3DGS reconstructions in clutter and occlusion through instance-aware viewpoint selection, with configurable object-centric operation and demonstrated robustness in robotics tasks.

title: Diabetic Retinopathy Lesion Segmentation through Attention Mechanisms
summary: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) is an eye disease which arises due to diabetes mellitus. It might cause vision loss and blindness. To prevent irreversible vision loss, early detection through systematic screening is crucial. Although researchers have developed numerous automated deep learning-based algorithms for DR screening, their clinical applicability remains limited, particularly in lesion segmentation. Our method provides pixel-level annotations for lesions, which practically supports Ophthalmologist to screen DR from fundus images. In this work, we segmented four types of DR-related lesions: microaneurysms, soft exudates, hard exudates, and hemorrhages on 757 images from DDR dataset. To enhance lesion segmentation, an attention mechanism was integrated with DeepLab-V3+. Compared to the baseline model, the Attention-DeepLab model increases mean average precision (mAP) from 0.3010 to 0.3326 and the mean Intersection over Union (IoU) from 0.1791 to 0.1928. The model also increased microaneurysm detection from 0.0205 to 0.0763, a clinically significant improvement. The detection of microaneurysms is the earliest visible symptom of DR.

AI.tldr: Attention-DeepLab-V3+ for pixel-level DR lesion segmentation on 757 DDR images; four lesion types segmented; improved mAP (0.3326) and IoU (0.1928); notable rise in microaneurysm detection from 0.0205 to 0.0763, indicating clinical relevance.
AI.motivation: DR screening requires accurate, pixel-level lesion delineation to support ophthalmologists and enable early intervention. Existing DL approaches lack robust lesion segmentation; this work emphasizes clinically useful lesion masks.
AI.method: Integrates an attention mechanism with DeepLab-V3+ to improve segmentation of four DR-related lesions (microaneurysms, soft/hard exudates, hemorrhages) on fundus images from the DDR dataset; evaluates with mAP and IoU, with emphasis on microaneurysm detection.
AI.result: Attention-DeepLab improves segmentation metrics over baseline: mAP from 0.3010 to 0.3326; IoU from 0.1791 to 0.1928. Microaneurysm detection increases from 0.0205 to 0.0763, a clinically meaningful gain.
AI.conclusion: The attention-enhanced DeepLab-V3+ model yields improved lesion segmentation performance and notably better microaneurysm detection, supporting its potential utility in clinical DR screening workflows. Further validation on diverse datasets and ablation studies could solidify generalizability.

title: UTOPIA: Unlearnable Tabular Data via Decoupled Shortcut Embedding
summary: Unlearnable examples (UE) have emerged as a practical mechanism to prevent unauthorized model training on private vision data, while extending this protection to tabular data is nontrivial. Tabular data in finance and healthcare is highly sensitive, yet existing UE methods transfer poorly because tabular features mix numerical and categorical constraints and exhibit saliency sparsity, with learning dominated by a few dimensions. Under a Spectral Dominance condition, we show certified unlearnability is feasible when the poison spectrum overwhelms the clean semantic spectrum. Guided by this, we propose Unlearnable Tabular Data via DecOuPled Shortcut EmbeddIng (UTOPIA), which exploits feature redundancy to decouple optimization into two channels: high saliency features for semantic obfuscation and low saliency redundant features for embedding a hyper correlated shortcut, yielding constraint-aware dominant shortcuts while preserving tabular validity. Extensive experiments across tabular datasets and models show UTOPIA drives unauthorized training toward near random performance, outperforming strong UE baselines and transferring well across architectures.

AI.tldr: Proposes UTOPIA, a two-channel, constraint-aware method for tabular unlearnable data that uses spectral dominance to certify unlearnability; achieves near-random unauthorized training and cross-architecture transfer.
AI.motivation: Protect privacy-sensitive tabular data (finance/healthcare) from unauthorized model training. Existing unlearnable-example methods struggle with tabular data due to mixed numerical/categorical constraints and saliency sparsity; seek certified unlearnability under a Spectral Dominance regime.
AI.method: UTOPIA decouples optimization into two channels: (1) high-saliency features for semantic obfuscation, (2) low-saliency redundant features to embed a hyper-correlated shortcut. This yields constraint-aware dominant shortcuts while preserving tabular validity. The approach relies on a Spectral Dominance condition where the poison spectrum overwhelms the clean semantic spectrum to certify unlearnability.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on tabular datasets and models show UTOPIA drives unauthorized training toward near-random performance, outperforms strong UE baselines, and transfers well across architectures.
AI.conclusion: UTOPIA provides a practical, transferable mechanism for certifiable unlearning in tabular data by exploiting feature redundancy to create dominant, constrained shortcuts without compromising data validity.

title: GCN-MPPR: Enhancing the Propagation of Message Passing Neural Networks via Motif-Based Personalized PageRank
summary: The algorithms based on message passing neural networks (MPNNs) on graphs have recently achieved great success for various graph applications. However, studies find that these methods always propagate the information to very limited neighborhoods with shallow depth, particularly due to over-smoothing. That means most of the existing MPNNs fail to be so `deep'. Although some previous work tended to handle this challenge via optimization- or structure-level remedies, the overall performance of GCNs still suffers from limited accuracy, poor stability, and unaffordable computational cost. Moreover, neglect of higher-order relationships during the propagation of MPNNs has further limited the performance of them. To overcome these challenges, a novel variant of PageRank named motif-based personalized PageRank (MPPR) is proposed to measure the influence of one node to another on the basis of considering higher-order motif relationships. Secondly, the MPPR is utilized to the message passing process of GCNs, thereby guiding the message passing process at a relatively `high' level. The experimental results show that the proposed method outperforms almost all of the baselines on accuracy, stability, and time consumption. Additionally, the proposed method can be considered as a component that can underpin almost all GCN tasks, with DGCRL being demonstrated in the experiment. The anonymous code repository is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/GCN-MPPR-AFD6/.

AI.tldr: MPNNs on graphs suffer from over-smoothing and limited depth, hindering accuracy, stability, and efficiency. The paper proposes motif-based personalized PageRank (MPPR) to capture higher-order motif relationships and guide GCN message passing at a higher level. Empirical results show improved accuracy, stability, and runtime; MPPR can serve as a general component for GCNs, with DGCRL demonstrated and code released anonymously.
AI.motivation: To overcome over-smoothing, limited effective depth, and neglect of higher-order relationships in graph neural networks, which degrade performance and computational costs.
AI.method: Introduce motif-based personalized PageRank (MPPR) to quantify node influence based on higher-order motif structures; integrate MPPR into the GCN message passing process to guide information propagation at a higher level, addressing depth and stability issues.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate superior accuracy, stability, and time efficiency compared with baselines; MPPR component can underpin most GCN tasks, with DGCRL showcased as an example; code repository provided (anonymous).
AI.conclusion: MPPR provides a principled way to incorporate higher-order motif information into GCNs, enhancing propagation depth, stability, and efficiency, and serving as a general augmentation for diverse GCN applications.

title: DexFormer: Cross-Embodied Dexterous Manipulation via History-Conditioned Transformer
summary: Dexterous manipulation remains one of the most challenging problems in robotics, requiring coherent control of high-DoF hands and arms under complex, contact-rich dynamics. A major barrier is embodiment variability: different dexterous hands exhibit distinct kinematics and dynamics, forcing prior methods to train separate policies or rely on shared action spaces with per-embodiment decoder heads. We present DexFormer, an end-to-end, dynamics-aware cross-embodiment policy built on a modified transformer backbone that conditions on historical observations. By using temporal context to infer morphology and dynamics on the fly, DexFormer adapts to diverse hand configurations and produces embodiment-appropriate control actions. Trained over a variety of procedurally generated dexterous-hand assets, DexFormer acquires a generalizable manipulation prior and exhibits strong zero-shot transfer to Leap Hand, Allegro Hand, and Rapid Hand. Our results show that a single policy can generalize across heterogeneous hand embodiments, establishing a scalable foundation for cross-embodiment dexterous manipulation. Project website: https://davidlxu.github.io/DexFormer-web/.

AI.tldr: DexFormer is a dynamics-aware transformer-based policy trained on diverse dexterous-hand assets that adapts to different hand morphologies via temporal observations, enabling zero-shot cross-embodiment manipulation across Leap, Allegro, and Rapid Hands with a single policy.
AI.motivation: Embodiment variability across dexterous hands makes existing policies brittle, requiring separate policies for each hand or per-embodiment decoders. A unified, generalizable controller that infers morphology from history could enable scalable cross-embodiment manipulation.
AI.method: An end-to-end policy built on a modified transformer backbone that uses historical observations to infer current morphology and dynamics. The policy is conditioned on temporal context to adapt actions to the current hand, trained on procedurally generated dexterous-hand assets, and designed to produce embodiment-appropriate control without per-embodiment decoders.
AI.result: DexFormer demonstrates generalization across heterogeneous hand embodiments with zero-shot transfer to Leap Hand, Allegro Hand, and Rapid Hand, indicating a generalizable manipulation prior and a scalable path toward cross-embodiment dexterous manipulation.
AI.conclusion: A single, dynamics-aware transformer policy can handle multiple dexterous hand embodiments, establishing a scalable foundation for cross-embodiment manipulation with potential applicability to unseen devices and tasks.

title: Optimization of Precipitate Segmentation Through Linear Genetic Programming of Image Processing
summary: Current analysis of additive manufactured niobium-based copper alloys relies on hand annotation due to varying contrast, noise, and image artifacts present in micrographs, slowing iteration speed in alloy development. We present a filtering and segmentation algorithm for detecting precipitates in FIB cross-section micrographs, optimized using linear genetic programming (LGP), which accounts for the various artifacts. To this end, the optimization environment uses a domain-specific language for image processing to iterate on solutions. Programs in this language are a list of image-filtering blocks with tunable parameters that sequentially process an input image, allowing for reliable generation and mutation by a genetic algorithm. Our environment produces optimized human-interpretable MATLAB code representing an image filtering pipeline. Under ideal conditions--a population size of 60 and a maximum program length of 5 blocks--our system was able to find a near-human accuracy solution with an average evaluation error of 1.8% when comparing segmentations pixel-by-pixel to a human baseline using an XOR error evaluation. Our automation work enabled faster iteration cycles and furthered exploration of the material composition and processing space: our optimized pipeline algorithm processes a 3.6 megapixel image in about 2 seconds on average. This ultimately enables convergence on strong, low-activation, precipitation hardened copper alloys for additive manufactured fusion reactor parts.

AI.tldr: An image-processing pipeline optimized via linear genetic programming (LGP) automates precipitate segmentation in FIB micrographs, reducing manual annotation and speeding alloy development.
AI.motivation: To overcome reliance on hand-annotated micrographs caused by varying contrast, noise, and artifacts; accelerate exploration of niobium-based copper alloy precipitation during additive manufacturing.
AI.method: Use a domain-specific language for image processing to define pipelines as sequences of tunable image-filtering blocks. Optimize these pipelines with linear genetic programming and a genetic algorithm, producing human-interpretable MATLAB code.
AI.result: Achieved near-human segmentation accuracy with 1.8% average XOR error, using population 60 and program length up to 5 blocks; processes a 3.6 MP image in ~2 seconds.
AI.conclusion: Automation speeds iteration and enables comprehensive exploration of composition/processing space, supporting convergence on strong, low-activation, precipitation-hardened copper alloys for AM fusion reactor parts.

title: FEM-Informed Hypergraph Neural Networks for Efficient Elastoplasticity
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) naturally align with sparse operators and unstructured discretizations, making them a promising paradigm for physics-informed machine learning in computational mechanics. Motivated by discrete physics losses and Hierarchical Deep Learning Neural Network (HiDeNN) constructions, we embed finite-element (FEM) computations at nodes and Gauss points directly into message-passing layers and propose a numerically consistent FEM-Informed Hypergraph Neural Networks (FHGNN). Similar to conventional physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), training is purely physics-driven and requires no labeled data: the input is a node element hypergraph whose edges encode mesh connectivity. Guided by empirical results and condition-number analysis, we adopt an efficient variational loss. Validated on 3D benchmarks, including cyclic loading with isotropic/kinematic hardening, the proposed method delivers substantially improved accuracy and efficiency over recent, competitive PINN variants. By leveraging GPU-parallel tensor operations and the discrete representation, it scales effectively to large elastoplastic problems and can be competitive with, or faster than, multi-core FEM implementations at comparable accuracy. This work establishes a foundation for scalable, physics-embedded learning in nonlinear solid mechanics.

AI.tldr: Proposes FEM-Informed Hypergraph Neural Networks (FHGNN) that embed FEM computations within GNN layers for physics-informed learning in nonlinear elastoplastic solid mechanics, achieving higher accuracy and efficiency than PINN variants and competitive with conventional FEM; scalable to large 3D problems.
AI.motivation: GNNs align with sparse operators and unstructured discretizations, enabling physics-informed learning. Embedding finite-element computations at nodes and Gauss points into message passing aims to create numerically consistent, data-free training (PINN-like) on a node-element hypergraph. This addresses efficiency, scalability, and accuracy gaps in existing PINN approaches for solid mechanics.
AI.method: Construct a node-element hypergraph where FEM computations at nodes and Gauss points are embedded directly into message-passing layers. Edges encode mesh connectivity. Train with a variational physics-driven loss guided by condition-number analysis. Leverage GPU-accelerated tensor ops and discrete representation to scale to large elastoplastic problems.
AI.result: Empirically and theoretically more accurate and efficient than recent PINN variants. Demonstrates scalability to large 3D elastoplastic problems and can match or exceed multi-core FEM at comparable accuracy, based on cyclic loading tests with isotropic/kinematic hardening.
AI.conclusion: FHGNN establishes a solid foundation for scalable, physics-embedded learning in nonlinear solid mechanics, combining FEM fidelity with graph-based neural scalability.

title: MedCoG: Maximizing LLM Inference Density in Medical Reasoning via Meta-Cognitive Regulation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown strong potential in complex medical reasoning yet face diminishing gains under inference scaling laws. While existing studies augment LLMs with various knowledge types, it remains unclear how effectively the additional costs translate into accuracy. In this paper, we explore how meta-cognition of LLMs, i.e., their self-awareness of their own knowledge states, can regulate the reasoning process. Specifically, we propose MedCoG, a Medical Meta-Cognition Agent with Knowledge Graph, where the meta-cognitive assessments of task complexity, familiarity, and knowledge density dynamically regulate utilization of procedural, episodic, and factual knowledge. The LLM-centric on-demand reasoning aims to mitigate scaling laws by (1) reducing costs via avoiding indiscriminate scaling, (2) improving accuracy via filtering out distractive knowledge. To validate this, we empirically characterize the scaling curve and introduce inference density to quantify inference efficiency, defined as the ratio of theoretically effective cost to actual cost. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of MedCoG on five hard sets of medical benchmarks, yielding 5.5x inference density. Furthermore, the Oracle study highlights the significant potential of meta-cognitive regulation.

AI.tldr: MedCoG introduces a meta-cognitive, knowledge-graph–based agent to regulate LLM reasoning for medical tasks, aiming to improve efficiency and accuracy under inference scaling; shows 5.5x higher inference density on hard medical benchmarks and potential via an Oracle study.
AI.motivation: Scaling laws limit gains from simply increasing model size and computation in medical reasoning. There is a need to manage diverse knowledge types (procedural, episodic, factual) efficiently with self-awareness of knowledge states to improve cost-effectiveness and accuracy.
AI.method: Propose MedCoG, a Medical Meta-Cognition Agent with Knowledge Graph. The system uses meta-cognitive assessments—task complexity, familiarity, and knowledge density—to dynamically regulate which knowledge sources and reasoning strategies (procedural, episodic, factual) are invoked. This on-demand reasoning aims to reduce unnecessary scaling and filter distractive knowledge.
AI.result: Empirical characterization of scaling curves and introduction of inference density (effective cost vs actual cost). Experiments on five hard medical benchmarks show 5.5x inference density. An Oracle study demonstrates substantial potential of meta-cognitive regulation.
AI.conclusion: Meta-cognitive regulation with a knowledge-graph backbone can substantially improve inference efficiency and accuracy in medical reasoning, offering a viable path to mitigate diminishing returns from scaling and guiding future research on on-demand, source-aware reasoning.

title: ReefFlex: A Generative Design Framework for Soft Robotic Grasping of Organic and Fragile objects
summary: Climate change, invasive species and human activities are currently damaging the world's coral reefs at unprecedented rates, threatening their vast biodiversity and fisheries, and reducing coastal protection. Solving this vast challenge requires scalable coral regeneration technologies that can breed climate-resilient species and accelerate the natural regrowth processes; actions that are impeded by the absence of safe and robust tools to handle the fragile coral. We investigate ReefFlex, a generative soft finger design methodology that explores a diverse space of soft fingers to produce a set of candidates capable of safely grasping fragile and geometrically heterogeneous coral in a cluttered environment. Our key insight is encoding heterogeneous grasping into a reduced set of motion primitives, creating a simplified, tractable multi-objective optimisation problem. To evaluate the method, we design a soft robot for reef rehabilitation, which grows and manipulates coral in onshore aquaculture facilities for future reef out-planting. We demonstrate ReefFlex increases both grasp success and grasp quality (disturbance resistance, positioning accuracy) and reduces in adverse events encountered during coral manipulation compared to reference designs. ReefFlex, offers a generalisable method to design soft end-effectors for complex handling and paves a pathway towards automation in previously unachievable domains like coral handling for restoration.

AI.tldr: ReefFlex offers a generative design framework for soft robotic fingers to safely grasp and manipulate fragile corals, enabling scalable reef rehabilitation.
AI.motivation: Urgent need to protect and restore coral reefs amid climate change, invasive species, and human impacts, and to develop safe, robust tools to handle fragile coral cycles at scale.
AI.method: A generative, soft-finger design methodology (ReefFlex) that explores a diverse space of soft finger geometries and encodes heterogeneous grasping into a reduced set of motion primitives, forming a tractable multi-objective optimization. Demonstrated by designing a soft robot for reef rehabilitation that grows and manipulates coral in onshore facilities.
AI.result: ReefFlex increases grasp success and grasp quality (disturbance resistance, positioning accuracy) and reduces adverse events during coral manipulation compared to reference designs.
AI.conclusion: The ReefFlex approach is generalizable for designing soft end-effectors for complex handling tasks and advances automation in coral handling for restoration.

title: LUCID-SAE: Learning Unified Vision-Language Sparse Codes for Interpretable Concept Discovery
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) offer a natural path toward comparable explanations across different representation spaces. However, current SAEs are trained per modality, producing dictionaries whose features are not directly understandable and whose explanations do not transfer across domains. In this study, we introduce LUCID (Learning Unified vision-language sparse Codes for Interpretable concept Discovery), a unified vision-language sparse autoencoder that learns a shared latent dictionary for image patch and text token representations, while reserving private capacity for modality-specific details. We achieve feature alignment by coupling the shared codes with a learned optimal transport matching objective without the need of labeling. LUCID yields interpretable shared features that support patch-level grounding, establish cross-modal neuron correspondence, and enhance robustness against the concept clustering problem in similarity-based evaluation. Leveraging the alignment properties, we develop an automated dictionary interpretation pipeline based on term clustering without manual observations. Our analysis reveals that LUCID's shared features capture diverse semantic categories beyond objects, including actions, attributes, and abstract concepts, demonstrating a comprehensive approach to interpretable multimodal representations.

AI.tldr: Unified vision-language sparse autoencoder (LUCID) learns a shared multimodal dictionary for image patches and text tokens, with modality-specific private capacity; alignment via optimal transport enables unsupervised cross-modal grounding and transferable, interpretable features.
AI.motivation: Current SAEs trained per modality yield hard-to-interpret features that do not transfer across domains; a unified, interpretable cross-modal representation is desirable for consistent explanations and grounding across modalities.
AI.method: Train a shared latent dictionary for image patch and text token representations, with a private capacity per modality; align shared codes using a learned optimal transport matching objective without supervision; develop an automated dictionary interpretation pipeline based on term clustering.
AI.result: Interpretable shared features enable patch-level grounding and cross-modal neuron correspondence; improve robustness against concept clustering in similarity-based evaluations; shared features cover objects, actions, attributes, and abstract concepts; automatically interprets dictionaries without manual observations.
AI.conclusion: LUCID offers a novel unsupervised framework for unified multimodal interpretable representations with cross-domain transferability and robust evaluation, expanding semantic coverage beyond conventional object-centric concepts.

title: Privately Learning Decision Lists and a Differentially Private Winnow
comment: 27 pages, The 37th International Conference on Algorithmic Learning Theory
summary: We give new differentially private algorithms for the classic problems of learning decision lists and large-margin halfspaces in the PAC and online models. In the PAC model, we give a computationally efficient algorithm for learning decision lists with minimal sample overhead over the best non-private algorithms. In the online model, we give a private analog of the influential Winnow algorithm for learning halfspaces with mistake bound polylogarithmic in the dimension and inverse polynomial in the margin. As an application, we describe how to privately learn decision lists in the online model, qualitatively matching state-of-the art non-private guarantees.

AI.tldr: Introduces differentially private algorithms for learning decision lists and large-margin halfspaces in PAC and online models; achieves competitive performance with privacy guarantees.
AI.motivation: To enable private learning for classic problems (decision lists, large-margin halfspaces) with minimal sample overhead and efficient computation, in both PAC and online settings.
AI.method: In PAC, a computationally efficient DP algorithm for learning decision lists with minimal sample overhead over the best non-private baselines. In online learning, a private analog of Winnow for halfspaces with mistake bounds polylogarithmic in dimension and inverse polynomial in the margin. Application includes privately learning decision lists in the online model.
AI.result: PAC: DP learner achieves near non-private sample complexity and efficiency. Online: Winnow-like DP algorithm yields favorable mistake bounds (polylog in dimension, polynomial in 1/margin). Decision lists can be privately learned online, qualitatively matching state-of-the-art non-private guarantees.
AI.conclusion: Differential privacy can be integrated into learning decision lists and large-margin halfspaces with performance close to non-private baselines in both PAC and online models, indicating practical viability for private learning in these problem classes.

title: Selective Fine-Tuning for Targeted and Robust Concept Unlearning
comment: Given the brittle nature of existing methods in unlearning harmful content in diffusion models, we propose TRuST, a novel approach for dynamically estimating target concept neurons and unlearning them by selectively fine-tuning
summary: Text guided diffusion models are used by millions of users, but can be easily exploited to produce harmful content. Concept unlearning methods aim at reducing the models' likelihood of generating harmful content. Traditionally, this has been tackled at an individual concept level, with only a handful of recent works considering more realistic concept combinations. However, state of the art methods depend on full finetuning, which is computationally expensive. Concept localisation methods can facilitate selective finetuning, but existing techniques are static, resulting in suboptimal utility. In order to tackle these challenges, we propose TRUST (Targeted Robust Selective fine Tuning), a novel approach for dynamically estimating target concept neurons and unlearning them through selective finetuning, empowered by a Hessian based regularization. We show experimentally, against a number of SOTA baselines, that TRUST is robust against adversarial prompts, preserves generation quality to a significant degree, and is also significantly faster than the SOTA. Our method achieves unlearning of not only individual concepts but also combinations of concepts and conditional concepts, without any specific regularization.

AI.tldr: TRUST provides a dynamic, Hessian-regularized selective fine-tuning strategy to unlearn targeted concepts in text-guided diffusion models, enabling robust unlearning of individual, combined, and conditional concepts with reduced computation and preserved generation quality.
AI.motivation: Address the computational burden of full fine-tuning for concept unlearning and the limitations of static concept localisation, by enabling dynamic estimation of target concept neurons and selective, robust unlearning.
AI.method: Introduce TRUST (Targeted Robust Selective fine Tuning): dynamically estimate target concept neurons and apply selective finetuning guided by a Hessian-based regularization to unlearn target concepts (including combinations and conditional concepts) without requiring bespoke regularizers; aims to be faster and more robust than baselines.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation against SOTA baselines shows TRUST is robust to adversarial prompts, preserves generation quality substantially, and is significantly faster than state-of-the-art methods; achieves unlearning of individual concepts, concept combinations, and conditional concepts without explicit extra regularization.
AI.conclusion: TRUST offers a dynamic, robust, and efficient pathway for concept unlearning in diffusion models, enabling selective unlearning of single, combined, and conditional concepts with reduced computational cost while maintaining output quality.

title: Benchmarking Autonomous Vehicles: A Driver Foundation Model Framework
summary: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) are poised to revolutionize global transportation systems. However, its widespread acceptance and market penetration remain significantly below expectations. This gap is primarily driven by persistent challenges in safety, comfort, commuting efficiency and energy economy when compared to the performance of experienced human drivers. We hypothesize that these challenges can be addressed through the development of a driver foundation model (DFM). Accordingly, we propose a framework for establishing DFMs to comprehensively benchmark AVs. Specifically, we describe a large-scale dataset collection strategy for training a DFM, discuss the core functionalities such a model should possess, and explore potential technical solutions to realize these functionalities. We further present the utility of the DFM across the operational spectrum, from defining human-centric safety envelopes to establishing benchmarks for energy economy. Overall, We aim to formalize the DFM concept and introduce a new paradigm for the systematic specification, verification and validation of AVs.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Driver Foundation Model (DFM) to benchmark autonomous vehicles, detailing a large-scale data-collection strategy, required functionalities, and technical approaches, with a framework spanning safety to energy benchmarks and a paradigm for AV specification, verification, and validation.
AI.motivation: AV performance (safety, comfort, efficiency, energy economy) lags human drivers; a unifying, scalable foundation model is proposed to standardize benchmarking and evaluation.
AI.method: Conceptual framework: define Driver Foundation Model (DFM); outline data collection strategy to train DFMs; enumerate core functionalities; discuss potential technical solutions; describe the utility of DFM across operational spectrum (safety envelopes to energy benchmarks).
AI.result: A formalized concept and blueprint for DFMs, including a dataset strategy, core functionalities, and applications for benchmarking and validating AVs across safety, comfort, efficiency, and energy.
AI.conclusion: Formalizes the DFM concept and introduces a new paradigm for the systematic specification, verification, and validation of autonomous vehicles.

title: Seeing Roads Through Words: A Language-Guided Framework for RGB-T Driving Scene Segmentation
summary: Robust semantic segmentation of road scenes under adverse illumination, lighting, and shadow conditions remain a core challenge for autonomous driving applications. RGB-Thermal fusion is a standard approach, yet existing methods apply static fusion strategies uniformly across all conditions, allowing modality-specific noise to propagate throughout the network. Hence, we propose CLARITY that dynamically adapts its fusion strategy to the detected scene condition. Guided by vision-language model (VLM) priors, the network learns to modulate each modality's contribution based on the illumination state while leveraging object embeddings for segmentation, rather than applying a fixed fusion policy. We further introduce two mechanisms, i.e., one which preserves valid dark-object semantics that prior noise-suppression methods incorrectly discard, and a hierarchical decoder that enforces structural consistency across scales to sharpen boundaries on thin objects. Experiments on the MFNet dataset demonstrate that CLARITY establishes a new state-of-the-art (SOTA), achieving 62.3% mIoU and 77.5% mAcc.

AI.tldr: Proposes CLARITY, a dynamic RGB-Thermal fusion network that adapts modality contributions using vision-language priors and scene condition, with a dark-object preservation mechanism and hierarchical decoder, achieving new SOTA on MFNet (62.3% mIoU, 77.5% mAcc).
AI.motivation: Robust semantic segmentation in road scenes under adverse illumination, lighting, and shadows is hard; static RGB-T fusion causes modality-specific noise to propagate through the network.
AI.method: CLARITY dynamically adjusts fusion weights guided by vision-language model priors and object embeddings for segmentation, includes a dark-object preservation mechanism to retain valid dark semantics, and uses a hierarchical decoder to enforce cross-scale structural consistency.
AI.result: On MFNet, CLARITY achieves state-of-the-art performance with 62.3% mIoU and 77.5% mAcc.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, condition-aware fusion with VLM priors and structural decoding improves robustness and boundary delineation in adverse road conditions, establishing a new SOTA on MFNet.

title: Dichotomy of Feature Learning and Unlearning: Fast-Slow Analysis on Neural Networks with Stochastic Gradient Descent
summary: The dynamics of gradient-based training in neural networks often exhibit nontrivial structures; hence, understanding them remains a central challenge in theoretical machine learning. In particular, a concept of feature unlearning, in which a neural network progressively loses previously learned features over long training, has gained attention. In this study, we consider the infinite-width limit of a two-layer neural network updated with a large-batch stochastic gradient, then derive differential equations with different time scales, revealing the mechanism and conditions for feature unlearning to occur. Specifically, we utilize the fast-slow dynamics: while an alignment of first-layer weights develops rapidly, the second-layer weights develop slowly. The direction of a flow on a critical manifold, determined by the slow dynamics, decides whether feature unlearning occurs. We give numerical validation of the result, and derive theoretical grounding and scaling laws of the feature unlearning. Our results yield the following insights: (i) the strength of the primary nonlinear term in data induces the feature unlearning, and (ii) an initial scale of the second-layer weights mitigates the feature unlearning. Technically, our analysis utilizes Tensor Programs and the singular perturbation theory.

AI.tldr: In the infinite-width, large-batch SGD setting for a two-layer network, fast-slow gradient dynamics produce feature unlearning along a slow manifold; unlearning occurs when data nonlinear terms are strong, while larger initial second-layer weights mitigate it; the paper derives scaling laws and validates them numerically using Tensor Programs and singular perturbation theory.
AI.motivation: To understand the mechanisms and conditions of feature unlearning in neural networks, particularly under long training and in the infinite-width regime, and to connect observed phenomena to a rigorous slow-fast dynamical framework.
AI.method: Derive differential equations with multiple time scales in the infinite-width limit for a two-layer network trained with large-batch SGD. Use Tensor Programs to formalize the dynamics and apply singular perturbation theory to separate fast (first-layer weight alignment) and slow (second-layer weight) dynamics. Analyze the resulting slow manifold flow to determine when feature unlearning occurs; supplement with numerical validation and derive scaling laws.
AI.result: Identification of conditions under which feature unlearning occurs, showing that the direction of slow dynamics on the critical manifold governs unlearning. Key factors include the strength of the primary nonlinear term in the data (driving unlearning) and the initial scale of second-layer weights (mitigating unlearning). Empirical validation corroborates the theoretical predictions and scaling laws.
AI.conclusion: Feature unlearning can arise from slow dynamics along a critical manifold in the infinite-width, large-batch regime. The work provides a theoretical grounding via Tensor Programs and singular perturbation theory, revealing that data nonlinearity and initialization scale critically influence unlearning, and offering scaling laws for practical regimes.

title: MePo: Meta Post-Refinement for Rehearsal-Free General Continual Learnin
summary: To cope with uncertain changes of the external world, intelligent systems must continually learn from complex, evolving environments and respond in real time. This ability, collectively known as general continual learning (GCL), encapsulates practical challenges such as online datastreams and blurry task boundaries. Although leveraging pretrained models (PTMs) has greatly advanced conventional continual learning (CL), these methods remain limited in reconciling the diverse and temporally mixed information along a single pass, resulting in sub-optimal GCL performance. Inspired by meta-plasticity and reconstructive memory in neuroscience, we introduce here an innovative approach named Meta Post-Refinement (MePo) for PTMs-based GCL. This approach constructs pseudo task sequences from pretraining data and develops a bi-level meta-learning paradigm to refine the pretrained backbone, which serves as a prolonged pretraining phase but greatly facilitates rapid adaptation of representation learning to downstream GCL tasks. MePo further initializes a meta covariance matrix as the reference geometry of pretrained representation space, enabling GCL to exploit second-order statistics for robust output alignment. MePo serves as a plug-in strategy that achieves significant performance gains across a variety of GCL benchmarks and pretrained checkpoints in a rehearsal-free manner (e.g., 15.10\%, 13.36\%, and 12.56\% on CIFAR-100, ImageNet-R, and CUB-200 under Sup-21/1K). Our source code is available at \href{https://github.com/SunGL001/MePo}{MePo}

AI.tldr: Meta Post-Refinement (MePo) is a plug-in meta-learning approach for pretrained-model–based general continual learning (GCL). By constructing pseudo task sequences from pretraining data, applying a bi-level meta-learning refinement to the backbone, and initializing a meta-covariance to capture the geometry of representation space, MePo enables rehearsal-free, rapid adaptation with notable gains across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: GCL requires models to learn continuously from online, temporally-mixed data while adapting quickly. While pretrained models bolster continual learning, they struggle to reconcile diverse, evolving information in a single-pass setting. Incorporating neuroscience-inspired mechanisms (meta-plasticity, reconstructive memory) and second-order statistics can improve robust output alignment and adaptability.
AI.method: 1) Build pseudo task sequences from pretraining data. 2) Apply a bi-level meta-learning framework to refine the pretrained backbone, effectively performing a prolonged pretraining that facilitates downstream GCL adaptation. 3) Initialize a meta covariance matrix representing the geometry of the pretrained representation space to exploit second-order statistics for robust alignment. 4) Treat MePo as a plug-in that operates without rehearsal (rehearsal-free).
AI.result: MePo yields significant performance gains across multiple GCL benchmarks and pretrained checkpoints in rehearsal-free settings. Reported improvements include 15.10% on CIFAR-100, 13.36% on ImageNet-R, and 12.56% on CUB-200 under the Sup-21/1K setup.
AI.conclusion: MePo provides a practical, plug-in enhancement for PTM-based GCL by leveraging meta-refinement and second-order geometry of representations, achieving robust, rapid adaptation without replay and broad applicability across datasets and checkpoints.

title: Personalized Autonomous Driving via Optimal Control with Clearance Constraints from Questionnaires
summary: Driving without considering the preferred separation distance from surrounding vehicles may cause discomfort for users. To address this limitation, we propose a planning framework that explicitly incorporates user preferences regarding the desired level of safe clearance from surrounding vehicles. We design a questionnaire purposefully tailored to capture user preferences relevant to our framework, while minimizing unnecessary questions. Specifically, the questionnaire considers various interaction-relevant factors, including the surrounding vehicle's size, speed, position, and maneuvers of surrounding vehicles, as well as the maneuvers of the ego vehicle. The response indicates the user-preferred clearance for the scenario defined by the question and is incorporated as constraints in the optimal control problem. However, it is impractical to account for all possible scenarios that may arise in a driving environment within a single optimal control problem, as the resulting computational complexity renders real-time implementation infeasible. To overcome this limitation, we approximate the original problem by decomposing it into multiple subproblems, each dealing with one fixed scenario. We then solve these subproblems in parallel and select one using the cost function from the original problem. To validate our work, we conduct simulations using different user responses to the questionnaire. We assess how effectively our planner reflects user preferences compared to preference-agnostic baseline planners by measuring preference alignment.

AI.tldr: A planning framework that integrates user preferences for safe clearance from surrounding vehicles via a questionnaire-derived constraint in an optimal control problem, approximated by decomposing into fixed-scenario subproblems solved in parallel and selected by the original cost; validated with simulations measuring alignment to user preferences.
AI.motivation: Users may experience discomfort when AV planning ignores their preferred separation distances; embedding explicit, interpretable preferences aims to improve perceived safety and acceptance.
AI.method: Design a tailored questionnaire capturing interaction-relevant factors (surrounding vehicle size, speed, position, maneuvers; ego vehicle maneuvers). Use responses to constrain an optimal control problem. To keep real-time feasibility, decompose the problem into multiple fixed-scenario subproblems, solve in parallel, and select the solution using the original objective as the criterion.
AI.result: Simulations with varied user responses show how well the planner reflects user preferences, quantified by a preference-alignment metric against preference-agnostic baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates feasibility of incorporating user preferences into planning and offers a scalable approach via scenario decomposition; future work may extend scenario coverage and explore online/adaptive preference learning.

title: Optimizing Few-Step Generation with Adaptive Matching Distillation
summary: Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) is a powerful acceleration paradigm, yet its stability is often compromised in Forbidden Zone, regions where the real teacher provides unreliable guidance while the fake teacher exerts insufficient repulsive force. In this work, we propose a unified optimization framework that reinterprets prior art as implicit strategies to avoid these corrupted regions. Based on this insight, we introduce Adaptive Matching Distillation (AMD), a self-correcting mechanism that utilizes reward proxies to explicitly detect and escape Forbidden Zones. AMD dynamically prioritizes corrective gradients via structural signal decomposition and introduces Repulsive Landscape Sharpening to enforce steep energy barriers against failure mode collapse. Extensive experiments across image and video generation tasks (e.g., SDXL, Wan2.1) and rigorous benchmarks (e.g., VBench, GenEval) demonstrate that AMD significantly enhances sample fidelity and training robustness. For instance, AMD improves the HPSv2 score on SDXL from 30.64 to 31.25, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. These findings validate that explicitly rectifying optimization trajectories within Forbidden Zones is essential for pushing the performance ceiling of few-step generative models.

AI.tldr: AMD is a self-correcting distillation framework that detects and escapes Forbidden Zones in Distribution Matching Distillation to improve stability and sample fidelity, achieving state-of-the-art gains on SDXL and other benchmarks.
AI.motivation: DMD stability is compromised in Forbidden Zone where the real teacher provides unreliable guidance and the fake teacher lacks repulsion; a unified optimization framework is needed to reinterpret prior art and actively avoid corrupted regions.
AI.method: Proposes Adaptive Matching Distillation (AMD): uses reward proxies to detect Forbidden Zones; structural signal decomposition to prioritize corrective gradients; Repulsive Landscape Sharpening to create steeper barriers; a self-correcting mechanism dynamically adjusts gradients during training; integrates with existing DMD pipelines.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on image and video generation (SDXL, Wan2.1) and benchmarks (VBench, GenEval) show improved sample fidelity and robustness; example: HPSv2 on SDXL increases from 30.64 to 31.25; AMD outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly rectifying optimization trajectories within Forbidden Zones is crucial to enhancing performance and robustness of few-step generative models.

title: Scout Before You Attend: Sketch-and-Walk Sparse Attention for Efficient LLM Inference
summary: Self-attention dominates the computational and memory cost of long-context LLM inference across both prefill and decode phases. To address this challenge, we introduce Sketch&Walk Attention, a training-free sparse attention method that determines sparsity with lightweight sketches and deterministic walk. Sketch&Walk applies Hadamard sketching to get inexpensive approximations of attention scores, then aggregates these estimates across layers via a walk mechanism that captures attention influence beyond direct interactions between tokens. The accumulated walk scores are used to select top-k attention blocks, enabling dynamic sparsity with a single training-free algorithm that applies uniformly to both the prefill and decode phases, together with custom sparse attention kernels. Across a wide range of models and tasks, Sketch&Walk maintains near-lossless accuracy at 20% attention density and can slightly outperform dense attention in some settings, while achieving up to 6x inference speedup.

AI.tldr: A training-free sparse attention method (Sketch&Walk) that uses Hadamard sketches and a walk-based aggregation to select top-k attention blocks, enabling dynamic sparsity for prefill and decode with near-lossless accuracy at ~20% density and up to 6x speedup.
AI.motivation: Reduce the heavy compute/memory cost of self-attention in long-context LLM inference, addressing both prefill and decode phases without retraining.
AI.method: Apply Hadamard sketching to approximate attention scores; propagate influence across layers via a deterministic walk mechanism; accumulate scores to select top-k attention blocks; implement with custom sparse attention kernels; training-free and uniform across phases.
AI.result: Maintains near-lossless accuracy at 20% attention density; can exceed dense accuracy in some cases; delivers up to 6x inference speedup across models/tasks.
AI.conclusion: Sketch&Walk offers a practical, training-free dynamic sparse attention approach that reduces the cost of long-context inference while preserving accuracy and generalizes across prefill and decode phases.

title: IV Co-Scientist: Multi-Agent LLM Framework for Causal Instrumental Variable Discovery
summary: In the presence of confounding between an endogenous variable and the outcome, instrumental variables (IVs) are used to isolate the causal effect of the endogenous variable. Identifying valid instruments requires interdisciplinary knowledge, creativity, and contextual understanding, making it a non-trivial task. In this paper, we investigate whether large language models (LLMs) can aid in this task. We perform a two-stage evaluation framework. First, we test whether LLMs can recover well-established instruments from the literature, assessing their ability to replicate standard reasoning. Second, we evaluate whether LLMs can identify and avoid instruments that have been empirically or theoretically discredited. Building on these results, we introduce IV Co-Scientist, a multi-agent system that proposes, critiques, and refines IVs for a given treatment-outcome pair. We also introduce a statistical test to contextualize consistency in the absence of ground truth. Our results show the potential of LLMs to discover valid instrumental variables from a large observational database.

AI.tldr: LLMs can assist in discovering and vetting instrumental variables for causal inference; introduces IV Co-Scientist, a multi-agent system that proposes, critiques, and refines IVs for a treatment-outcome pair, plus a statistical test to contextualize consistency without ground truth; preliminary results show potential to recover known instruments and avoid discredited ones from large observational data.
AI.motivation: To alleviate the knowledge-intensive, interdisciplinary task of instrument selection by leveraging LLMs to identify plausible instruments and to critique them, reducing reliance on only expert manual selection.
AI.method: Two-stage evaluation: (1) recover established instruments from literature to test replication of standard reasoning; (2) assess ability to avoid empirically/theoretically discredited instruments; then develop IV Co-Scientist as a multi-agent loop to propose, critique, and refine IVs; introduce a contextual statistical test for consistency when ground truth is absent.
AI.result: Preliminary results indicate LLMs can recover known instruments and avoid discredited ones, suggesting potential to discover valid IVs from large observational databases.
AI.conclusion: LLMs offer a promising aid for IV identification and vetting; the multi-agent framework and the contextual test enhance robustness; further validation, diverse data, and scrutiny of biases and validity are needed before deployment.

title: Controlled Flight of an Insect-Scale Flapping-Wing Robot via Integrated Onboard Sensing and Computation
summary: Aerial insects can effortlessly navigate dense vegetation, whereas similarly sized aerial robots typically depend on offboard sensors and computation to maintain stable flight. This disparity restricts insect-scale robots to operation within motion capture environments, substantially limiting their applicability to tasks such as search-and-rescue and precision agriculture. In this work, we present a 1.29-gram aerial robot capable of hovering and tracking trajectories with solely onboard sensing and computation. The combination of a sensor suite, estimators, and a low-level controller achieved centimeter-scale positional flight accuracy. Additionally, we developed a hierarchical controller in which a human operator provides high-level commands to direct the robot's motion. In a 30-second flight experiment conducted outside a motion capture system, the robot avoided obstacles and ultimately landed on a sunflower. This level of sensing and computational autonomy represents a significant advancement for the aerial microrobotics community, further opening opportunities to explore onboard planning and power autonomy.

AI.tldr: A 1.29 g aerial robot achieves fully onboard sensing, control, and autonomous flight with centimeter-level precision, including outdoor obstacle avoidance and landing on a sunflower, enabled by a hierarchical controller and onboard planning.
AI.motivation: Overcome reliance on external motion capture and offboard computation for insect-scale aerial robots to operate in real-world environments (e.g., search-and-rescue, precision agriculture) and enable onboard planning and power autonomy.
AI.method: Developed a 1.29 g UAV with onboard sensor suite, estimators, low-level controller, and a hierarchical controller allowing high-level commands from a human operator; demonstrated outdoor 30 s flight with obstacle avoidance and a landing on a sunflower.
AI.result: Centimeter-scale positional accuracy; successful obstacle avoidance; outdoor operation without motion capture; feasible onboard autonomy at microrobot scale.
AI.conclusion: Represents a substantial step toward fully onboard autonomous microrobotics with potential for onboard planning and power-efficient operation in real-world tasks.

title: Row-Column Separated Attention Based Low-Light Image/Video Enhancement
summary: U-Net structure is widely used for low-light image/video enhancement. The enhanced images result in areas with large local noise and loss of more details without proper guidance for global information. Attention mechanisms can better focus on and use global information. However, attention to images could significantly increase the number of parameters and computations. We propose a Row-Column Separated Attention module (RCSA) inserted after an improved U-Net. The RCSA module's input is the mean and maximum of the row and column of the feature map, which utilizes global information to guide local information with fewer parameters. We propose two temporal loss functions to apply the method to low-light video enhancement and maintain temporal consistency. Extensive experiments on the LOL, MIT Adobe FiveK image, and SDSD video datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/cq-dong/URCSA.

AI.tldr: A lightweight global-attention mechanism (RCSA) for U-Net-based low-light image/video enhancement. It uses row/column statistics (mean and max) to capture global information with fewer parameters, and includes temporal losses for video consistency. Validated on LOL, MIT Adobe FiveK, and SDSD datasets with publicly released code.
AI.motivation: Low-light images/videos suffer from high local noise and loss of details; leveraging global information improves enhancement, but standard attention greatly increases parameters and computation. A parameter-efficient, global-aware attention module is needed.
AI.method: Integrate a Row-Column Separated Attention (RCSA) module after an improved U-Net. RCSA uses the mean and maximum values along rows and columns of the feature map to form a global context that guides local processing with reduced parameter count. For video, two temporal loss functions are proposed to enforce temporal consistency. Trained/evaluated on LOL (images), MIT Adobe FiveK (images), and SDSD (videos).
AI.result: Experiments show the proposed approach is effective for both image and video low-light enhancement, outperforming or matching baselines while using fewer parameters due to the efficient RCSA design. Temporal losses help maintain temporal coherence in videos.
AI.conclusion: RCSA provides an efficient means of injecting global context into U-Net for low-light enhancement, enabling improved image quality and temporal consistency in video with reduced computational burden. The method is publicly available (code link).

title: BitLogic: Training Framework for Gradient-Based FPGA-Native Neural Networks
summary: The energy and latency costs of deep neural network inference are increasingly driven by deployment rather than training, motivating hardware-specialized alternatives to arithmetic-heavy models. Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) provide an attractive substrate for such specialization, yet existing FPGA-based neural approaches are fragmented and difficult to compare. We present BitLogic, a fully gradient-based, end-to-end trainable framework for FPGA-native neural networks built around Lookup Table (LUT) computation. BitLogic replaces multiply-accumulate operations with differentiable LUT nodes that map directly to FPGA primitives, enabling native binary computation, sparse connectivity, and efficient hardware realization. The framework offers a modular functional API supporting diverse architectures, along with learned encoders, hardware-aware heads, and multiple boundary-consistent LUT relaxations. An automated Register Transfer Level (RTL) export pipeline translates trained PyTorch models into synthesizable HDL, ensuring equivalence between software and hardware inference. Experiments across standard vision benchmarks and heterogeneous hardware platforms demonstrate competitive accuracy and substantial gains in FPGA efficiency, including 72.3% test accuracy on CIFAR-10 achieved with fewer than 0.3M logic gates, while attaining sub-20 ns single-sample inference using only LUT resources.

AI.tldr: BitLogic is a gradient-based, end-to-end trainable framework for FPGA-native neural networks that replaces MACs with differentiable LUT nodes, exports trained PyTorch models to RTL, and demonstrates FPGA-efficient inference (e.g., CIFAR-10 72.3% with <0.3M logic gates and sub-20 ns per-sample via LUT resources).
AI.motivation: Energy and latency in DNN inference increasingly stem from deployment; FPGA approaches are fragmented and hard to compare. A unified, LUT-based, trainable, RTL-exportable framework can enable hardware-efficient, comparable neural networks on FPGAs.
AI.method: Differentiable LUT nodes replace multiply-accumulate operations; supports native binary computation and sparse connectivity; modular API with learned encoders, hardware-aware heads, and boundary-consistent LUT relaxations; automated RTL export translates PyTorch models into synthesizable HDL and ensures software–hardware inference equivalence.
AI.result: Experiments on standard vision benchmarks across heterogeneous hardware show competitive accuracy and substantial FPGA efficiency gains, notably 72.3% accuracy on CIFAR-10 with under 0.3M logic gates and sub-20 ns single-sample inference using only LUT resources.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of end-to-end trainable, FPGA-native neural networks based on LUT computation with a full training-to-hardware flow; provides a practical pathway to hardware-aware, efficient FPGA deployment and cross-platform comparability.

title: LOCA-bench: Benchmarking Language Agents Under Controllable and Extreme Context Growth
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly capable of carrying out long-running, real-world tasks. However, as the amount of context grows, their reliability often deteriorates, a phenomenon known as "context rot". Existing long-context benchmarks primarily focus on single-step settings that evaluate a model's ability to retrieve information from a long snippet. In realistic scenarios, however, LLMs often need to act as agents that explore environments, follow instructions and plans, extract useful information, and predict correct actions under a dynamically growing context. To assess language agents in such settings, we introduce LOCA-bench (a benchmark for LOng-Context Agents). Given a task prompt, LOCA-bench leverages automated and scalable control of environment states to regulate the agent's context length. This design enables LOCA-bench to extend the context length potentially to infinity in a controlled way while keeping the underlying task semantics fixed. LOCA-bench evaluates language agents as a combination of models and scaffolds, including various context management strategies. While agent performance generally degrades as the environment states grow more complex, advanced context management techniques can substantially improve the overall success rate. We open-source LOCA-bench to provide a platform for evaluating models and scaffolds in long-context, agentic scenarios: https://github.com/hkust-nlp/LOCA-bench

AI.tldr: LOCA-bench is a scalable benchmark for long-context agents that controls environment state to vary an agent’s context length, enabling evaluation of models and scaffolds as context grows, with degradation against longer contexts but notable gains from context-management strategies; openly released.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLMs suffer context rot as context length grows; existing benchmarks focus on single-step retrieval and fail to assess agents operating in dynamic, expanding contexts. LOCA-bench provides a scalable, controlled platform to evaluate long-context, agentic performance.
AI.method: Automated, scalable control of environment states to regulate the agent’s context length while keeping the task semantics fixed. Evaluates a hybrid of model and scaffolds (context-management strategies) under increasing context demand, potentially extending to near-infinite context.
AI.result: Agent performance generally degrades as context grows, but advanced context-management techniques substantially improve success rates, demonstrating the utility of scaffolds in long-context settings.
AI.conclusion: LOCA-bench offers a platform and open-source resource to evaluate long-context agents (models plus scaffolds) in scalable, agentic tasks, enabling systematic study of context management under expanding contexts and supporting future research.

title: Vec-QMDP: Vectorized POMDP Planning on CPUs for Real-Time Autonomous Driving
summary: Planning under uncertainty for real-world robotics tasks, such as autonomous driving, requires reasoning in enormous high-dimensional belief spaces, rendering the problem computationally intensive. While parallelization offers scalability, existing hybrid CPU-GPU solvers face critical bottlenecks due to host-device synchronization latency and branch divergence on SIMT architectures, limiting their utility for real-time planning and hindering real-robot deployment. We present Vec-QMDP, a CPU-native parallel planner that aligns POMDP search with modern CPUs' SIMD architecture, achieving $227\times$--$1073\times$ speedup over state-of-the-art serial planners. Vec-QMDP adopts a Data-Oriented Design (DOD), refactoring scattered, pointer-based data structures into contiguous, cache-efficient memory layouts. We further introduce a hierarchical parallelism scheme: distributing sub-trees across independent CPU cores and SIMD lanes, enabling fully vectorized tree expansion and collision checking. Efficiency is maximized with the help of UCB load balancing across trees and a vectorized STR-tree for coarse-level collision checking. Evaluated on large-scale autonomous driving benchmarks, Vec-QMDP achieves state-of-the-art planning performance with millisecond-level latency, establishing CPUs as a high-performance computing platform for large-scale planning under uncertainty.

AI.tldr: Vec-QMDP is a CPU-native, SIMD-optimized POMDP planner that delivers massive speedups and millisecond latency for large-scale autonomous driving tasks, surpassing CPU-GPU hybrids.
AI.motivation: Planning under uncertainty in high-dimensional belief spaces for real-world robotics is computationally prohibitive. Hybrid CPU-GPU solvers incur host-device synchronization latency and branch divergence on SIMT architectures, limiting real-time planning and deployment.
AI.method: Employs Data-Oriented Design to convert scattered, pointer-based data into contiguous, cache-friendly layouts. Introduces hierarchical parallelism by distributing sub-trees across CPU cores and SIMD lanes, achieving fully vectorized tree expansion and collision checking. Uses UCB-based load balancing across trees and a vectorized STR-tree for coarse collision checking.
AI.result: Achieves 227x–1073x speedup over state-of-the-art serial planners with millisecond-level latency on large-scale autonomous driving benchmarks, setting a new performance standard for planning under uncertainty on CPUs.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that CPU-native, vectorized planning can match or exceed hybrid approaches, establishing CPUs as a high-performance platform for large-scale, real-time planning under uncertainty in robotics.

title: Perspective-aware fusion of incomplete depth maps and surface normals for accurate 3D reconstruction
summary: We address the problem of reconstructing 3D surfaces from depth and surface normal maps acquired by a sensor system based on a single perspective camera. Depth and normal maps can be obtained through techniques such as structured-light scanning and photometric stereo, respectively. We propose a perspective-aware log-depth fusion approach that extends existing orthographic gradient-based depth-normals fusion methods by explicitly accounting for perspective projection, leading to metrically accurate 3D reconstructions. Additionally, the method handles missing depth measurements by leveraging available surface normal information to inpaint gaps. Experiments on the DiLiGenT-MV data set demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach and highlight the importance of perspective-aware depth-normals fusion.

AI.tldr: Perspective-aware log-depth fusion for 3D reconstruction from depth and normals captured by a single camera, enabling metric accuracy and missing-depth inpainting.
AI.motivation: Orthographic assumptions in depth-normals fusion fail under perspective projection; need metrically correct reconstructions and gap filling using normals.
AI.method: Extend orthographic gradient-based fusion to a perspective camera model using log-depth; incorporate depth inpainting guided by normals; handle missing depth by inpainting.
AI.result: Empirically validated on DiLiGenT-MV dataset; demonstrates the importance of perspective-aware fusion and effectiveness in reconstructing 3D surfaces with partial data.
AI.conclusion: Perspective-aware depth-normals fusion yields metrically accurate surfaces and robustly handles missing depth; improves over prior orthographic approaches.

title: Nonparametric Bayesian Optimization for General Rewards
summary: This work focuses on Bayesian optimization (BO) under reward model uncertainty. We propose the first BO algorithm that achieves no-regret guarantee in a general reward setting, requiring only Lipschitz continuity of the objective function and accommodating a broad class of measurement noise. The core of our approach is a novel surrogate model, termed as infinite Gaussian process ($\infty$-GP). It is a Bayesian nonparametric model that places a prior on the space of reward distributions, enabling it to represent a substantially broader class of reward models than classical Gaussian process (GP). The $\infty$-GP is used in combination with Thompson Sampling (TS) to enable effective exploration and exploitation. Correspondingly, we develop a new TS regret analysis framework for general rewards, which relates the regret to the total variation distance between the surrogate model and the true reward distribution. Furthermore, with a truncated Gibbs sampling procedure, our method is computationally scalable, incurring minimal additional memory and computational complexities compared to classical GP. Empirical results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, particularly in settings with non-stationary, heavy-tailed, or other ill-conditioned rewards.

AI.tldr: A no-regret Bayesian optimization method under reward-model uncertainty using an infinite Gaussian process surrogate with Thompson Sampling, plus a TV-distance regret analysis and a scalable truncated Gibbs sampler, achieving state-of-the-art results on challenging reward distributions.
AI.motivation: Handle reward-model uncertainty in Bayesian optimization by allowing a broad class of reward distributions while guaranteeing no-regret performance under minimal assumptions (Lipschitz objective, general noise).
AI.method: Introduce an infinite Gaussian process (∞-GP) prior over reward distributions; couple with Thompson Sampling for exploration-exploitation; develop a regret analysis framework that bounds regret by the total variation distance between the surrogate ∞-GP and the true reward distribution; implement scalable inference via truncated Gibbs sampling to keep memory and computation close to standard GP-based BO.
AI.result: Establishes no-regret guarantees in general reward settings; demonstrates computational scalability; empirical results show state-of-the-art performance, particularly with non-stationary, heavy-tailed, or ill-conditioned rewards.
AI.conclusion: Presents a principled, scalable Bayesian optimization framework robust to reward-model uncertainty, backed by a general regret bound and strong empirical performance, enabling robust optimization across diverse reward structures.

title: Accelerating Social Science Research via Agentic Hypothesization and Experimentation
summary: Data-driven social science research is inherently slow, relying on iterative cycles of observation, hypothesis generation, and experimental validation. While recent data-driven methods promise to accelerate parts of this process, they largely fail to support end-to-end scientific discovery. To address this gap, we introduce EXPERIGEN, an agentic framework that operationalizes end-to-end discovery through a Bayesian optimization inspired two-phase search, in which a Generator proposes candidate hypotheses and an Experimenter evaluates them empirically. Across multiple domains, EXPERIGEN consistently discovers 2-4x more statistically significant hypotheses that are 7-17 percent more predictive than prior approaches, and naturally extends to complex data regimes including multimodal and relational datasets. Beyond statistical performance, hypotheses must be novel, empirically grounded, and actionable to drive real scientific progress. To evaluate these qualities, we conduct an expert review of machine-generated hypotheses, collecting feedback from senior faculty. Among 25 reviewed hypotheses, 88 percent were rated moderately or strongly novel, 70 percent were deemed impactful and worth pursuing, and most demonstrated rigor comparable to senior graduate-level research. Finally, recognizing that ultimate validation requires real-world evidence, we conduct the first A/B test of LLM-generated hypotheses, observing statistically significant results with p less than 1e-6 and a large effect size of 344 percent.

AI.tldr: EXPERIGEN is an agentic, Bayesian-optimization-inspired framework that enables end-to-end discovery by having a Generator propose hypotheses and an Experimenter test them, yielding significantly improved discovery efficiency and quality across data-rich domains.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in data-driven science: existing methods accelerate parts of the process but do not support end-to-end discovery from hypothesis generation to empirical validation.
AI.method: A two-phase search where a Generator proposes candidate hypotheses and an Experimenter evaluates them empirically, guided by Bayesian-optimization principles. The framework handles multimodal and relational data and demonstrates generality across domains.
AI.result: The approach discovers 2-4x more statistically significant hypotheses and yields 7-17% more predictive hypotheses than prior methods. Expert review of 25 hypotheses shows 88% novel, 70% impactful, with rigor comparable to senior grad-level work. First A/B test of LLM-generated hypotheses yields p<1e-6 and a large effect size of 344%.
AI.conclusion: END-TO-END discovery in data-driven social science can be substantially accelerated and improved through agentic, experiment-guided hypothesis generation, with strong empirical and expert validation and applicability to complex data regimes.

title: Learning Human-Like Badminton Skills for Humanoid Robots
summary: Realizing versatile and human-like performance in high-demand sports like badminton remains a formidable challenge for humanoid robotics. Unlike standard locomotion or static manipulation, this task demands a seamless integration of explosive whole-body coordination and precise, timing-critical interception. While recent advances have achieved lifelike motion mimicry, bridging the gap between kinematic imitation and functional, physics-aware striking without compromising stylistic naturalness is non-trivial. To address this, we propose Imitation-to-Interaction, a progressive reinforcement learning framework designed to evolve a robot from a "mimic" to a capable "striker." Our approach establishes a robust motor prior from human data, distills it into a compact, model-based state representation, and stabilizes dynamics via adversarial priors.
