Crucially, to overcome the sparsity of expert demonstrations, we introduce a manifold expansion strategy that generalizes discrete strike points into a dense interaction volume. We validate our framework through the mastery of diverse skills, including lifts and drop shots, in simulation. Furthermore, we demonstrate the first zero-shot sim-to-real transfer of anthropomorphic badminton skills to a humanoid robot, successfully replicating the kinetic elegance and functional precision of human athletes in the physical world.

AI.tldr: Imitation-to-Interaction: a progressive RL pipeline that converts imitation-based motor priors into a physics-aware badminton striker, using a compact model-based state representation, adversarial priors for stability, and manifold expansion to densify strike opportunities, enabling zero-shot sim-to-real transfer.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between kinematic imitation and functional, physics-based interaction in dynamic sports robotics; address data sparsity of expert demonstrations and the challenges of sim-to-real transfer.
AI.method: Extract a motor prior from human badminton data and distill it into a compact, model-based state representation; stabilize dynamics via adversarial priors; employ a manifold expansion strategy to convert discrete strike points into a dense interaction volume; implement a progressive reinforcement learning curriculum that evolves a robot from mimicry to a capable striker; validate in simulation and demonstrate zero-shot sim-to-real transfer to a humanoid robot.
AI.result: The framework masters diverse badminton skills (e.g., lifts, drop shots) in simulation and achieves the first zero-shot sim-to-real transfer of anthropomorphic badminton skills to a humanoid robot, preserving kinetic elegance and functional precision.
AI.conclusion: Imitation-to-Interaction offers a practical pathway to transform imitation-based priors into functional, physics-aware interaction controllers for dynamic sports, with strong sim-to-real transfer potential and extensibility to related high-demand, timing-critical manipulation tasks.

title: PTB-XL-Image-17K: A Large-Scale Synthetic ECG Image Dataset with Comprehensive Ground Truth for Deep Learning-Based Digitization
summary: Electrocardiogram (ECG) digitization-converting paper-based or scanned ECG images back into time-series signals-is critical for leveraging decades of legacy clinical data in modern deep learning applications. However, progress has been hindered by the lack of large-scale datasets providing both ECG images and their corresponding ground truth signals with comprehensive annotations. We introduce PTB-XL-Image-17K, a complete synthetic ECG image dataset comprising 17,271 high-quality 12-lead ECG images generated from the PTB-XL signal database. Our dataset uniquely provides five complementary data types per sample: (1) realistic ECG images with authentic grid patterns and annotations (50% with visible grid, 50% without), (2) pixel-level segmentation masks, (3) ground truth time-series signals, (4) bounding box annotations in YOLO format for both lead regions and lead name labels, and (5) comprehensive metadata including visual parameters and patient information. We present an open-source Python framework enabling customizable dataset generation with controllable parameters including paper speed (25/50 mm/s), voltage scale (5/10 mm/mV), sampling rate (500 Hz), grid appearance (4 colors), and waveform characteristics. The dataset achieves 100% generation success rate with an average processing time of 1.35 seconds per sample. PTB-XL-Image-17K addresses critical gaps in ECG digitization research by providing the first large-scale resource supporting the complete pipeline: lead detection, waveform segmentation, and signal extraction with full ground truth for rigorous evaluation. The dataset, generation framework, and documentation are publicly available at https://github.com/naqchoalimehdi/PTB-XL-Image-17K and https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18197519.

AI.tldr: A synthetic, large-scale ECG image dataset (PTB-XL-Image-17K) with 17,271 samples, providing five data modalities per sample (images with/without grids, pixel masks, ground-truth signals, YOLO-format annotations, and rich metadata) and an open framework to generate customizable ECG images for end-to-end digitization evaluation.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of large-scale, richly annotated ECG image datasets that pair images with ground-truth time-series signals, enabling evaluation of the full image-to-signal digitization pipeline in deep learning.
AI.method: Generate synthetic ECG images from the PTB-XL signal database to create 17,271 samples with five modalities: (1) realistic images with grid and annotations (half with visible grid, half without), (2) pixel-level segmentation masks, (3) ground-truth time-series signals, (4) YOLO-format bounding boxes for lead regions and lead-name labels, (5) metadata. Provides an open-source Python framework allowing configurable paper speed (25/50 mm/s), voltage scale (5/10 mm/mV), sampling rate (500 Hz), grid colors (4 colors), and waveform characteristics; reports 100% generation success and ~1.35 s per sample.
AI.result: The dataset comprises 17,271 samples with comprehensive multi-modal annotations, enabling lead detection, waveform segmentation, and signal extraction with full ground truth; generation is fully automated and fast, supporting rigorous evaluation of digitization pipelines.
AI.conclusion: PTB-XL-Image-17K fills a critical gap by offering the first large-scale resource for end-to-end ECG digitization research, including images, masks, signals, bounding boxes, and metadata, with an open-generation framework. Resources are publicly available (GitHub and Zenodo).

title: Learning Molecular Chirality via Chiral Determinant Kernels
summary: Chirality is a fundamental molecular property that governs stereospecific behavior in chemistry and biology. Capturing chirality in machine learning models remains challenging due to the geometric complexity of stereochemical relationships and the limitations of traditional molecular representations that often lack explicit stereochemical encoding. Existing approaches to chiral molecular representation primarily focus on central chirality, relying on handcrafted stereochemical tags or limited 3D encodings, and thus fail to generalize to more complex forms such as axial chirality. In this work, we introduce ChiDeK (Chiral Determinant Kernels), a framework that systematically integrates stereogenic information into molecular representation learning. We propose the chiral determinant kernel to encode the SE(3)-invariant chirality matrix and employ cross-attention to integrate stereochemical information from local chiral centers into the global molecular representation. This design enables explicit modeling of chiral-related features within a unified architecture, capable of jointly encoding central and axial chirality. To support the evaluation of axial chirality, we construct a new benchmark for electronic circular dichroism (ECD) and optical rotation (OR) prediction. Across four tasks, including R/S configuration classification, enantiomer ranking, ECD spectrum prediction, and OR prediction, ChiDeK achieves substantial improvements over state-of-the-art baselines, most notably yielding over 7% higher accuracy on axially chiral tasks on average.

AI.tldr: ChiDeK introduces chiral determinant kernels to encode SE(3)-invariant chirality and uses cross-attention to fuse local chiral centers into a global molecular representation, enabling simultaneous central and axial chirality handling; a new benchmark for ECD and OR supports evaluation of axial chirality; shows >7% average accuracy gains on axially chiral tasks vs baselines.
AI.motivation: Chirality is central to chemical behavior but difficult for ML models due to rich stereochemical relationships and inadequacies of traditional representations that lack explicit stereochemical encoding. There's a gap in handling axial chirality beyond central (R/S) chirality.
AI.method: Propose Chiral Determinant Kernel (ChiDeK): encode SE(3)-invariant chirality matrix via a chiral determinant kernel; employ cross-attention to integrate stereochemical cues from local chiral centers into the global molecular representation; architecture supports both central and axial chirality; construct a benchmark for electronic circular dichroism (ECD) and optical rotation (OR) prediction to evaluate axial chirality.
AI.result: ChiDeK achieves substantial improvements over state-of-the-art baselines across four tasks: R/S classification, enantiomer ranking, ECD spectrum prediction, and OR prediction; notably yields >7% higher accuracy on axially chiral tasks on average.
AI.conclusion: A unified representation learning framework that explicitly encodes stereochemical information can effectively capture both central and axial chirality, improving predictive performance in chiral-related tasks and enabling evaluation on ECD/OR benchmarks; the approach suggests a generalizable path for stereochemistry-aware ML models.

title: Towards Adaptive, Scalable, and Robust Coordination of LLM Agents: A Dynamic Ad-Hoc Networking Perspective
summary: Multi-agent architectures built on large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated the potential to realize swarm intelligence through well-crafted collaboration. However, the substantial burden of manual orchestration inherently raises an imperative to automate the design of agentic workflows. We frame such an agent coordination challenge as a classic problem in dynamic ad-hoc networking: How to establish adaptive and reliable communication among a scalable number of agentic hosts? In response to this unresolved dilemma, we introduce RAPS, a reputation-aware publish-subscribe paradigm for adaptive, scalable, and robust coordination of LLM agents. RAPS is grounded in the Distributed Publish-Subscribe Protocol, allowing LLM agents to exchange messages based on their declared intents rather than predefined topologies. Beyond this substrate, RAPS further incorporates two coherent overlays: (i) Reactive Subscription, enabling agents to dynamically refine their intents; and (ii) Bayesian Reputation, empowering each agent with a local watchdog to detect and isolate malicious peers. Extensive experiments over five benchmarks showcase that our design effectively reconciles adaptivity, scalability, and robustness in a unified multi-agent coordination framework.

AI.tldr: A reputation-aware publish-subscribe framework (RAPS) for adaptive, scalable, and robust coordination of LLM agents, built on the Distributed Publish-Subscribe Protocol, with Reactive Subscription and Bayesian Reputation overlays; validated empirically on five benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Automation of agent workflow orchestration in multi-agent LLM systems; addressing scalability, dynamic topology adaptation, and robustness against malicious agents.
AI.method: Design of RAPS atop a Distributed Publish-Subscribe Protocol; intents-based messaging rather than fixed topologies; Reactive Subscription overlay for dynamic intent refinement; Bayesian Reputation overlay for detecting and isolating malicious peers; evaluation across five benchmarks.
AI.result: RAPS demonstrates favorable trade-offs among adaptivity, scalability, and robustness; shows improved coordination across diverse benchmarks; enables online intent refinement and reputation-based mitigation of misbehaving agents.
AI.conclusion: RAPS offers a unified, scalable, and robust framework for multi-agent LLM coordination in dynamic environments, with built-in mechanisms for adaptability and security; potential for broader applicability and further enhancement of reputation and subscription dynamics.

title: BiManiBench: A Hierarchical Benchmark for Evaluating Bimanual Coordination of Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have significantly advanced embodied AI, and using them to benchmark robotic intelligence has become a pivotal trend. However, existing frameworks remain predominantly confined to single-arm manipulation, failing to capture the spatio-temporal coordination required for bimanual tasks like lifting a heavy pot. To address this, we introduce BiManiBench, a hierarchical benchmark evaluating MLLMs across three tiers: fundamental spatial reasoning, high-level action planning, and low-level end-effector control. Our framework isolates unique bimanual challenges, such as arm reachability and kinematic constraints, thereby distinguishing perceptual hallucinations from planning failures. Analysis of over 30 state-of-the-art models reveals that despite high-level reasoning proficiency, MLLMs struggle with dual-arm spatial grounding and control, frequently resulting in mutual interference and sequencing errors. These findings suggest the current paradigm lacks a deep understanding of mutual kinematic constraints, highlighting the need for future research to focus on inter-arm collision-avoidance and fine-grained temporal sequencing.

AI.tldr: BiManiBench presents a hierarchical, three-tier benchmark (spatial reasoning, planning, and low-level control) to evaluate multimodal LLMs on bimanual manipulation, revealing that many models excel at high-level reasoning but struggle with dual-arm grounding, coordination, and kinematic constraints, leading to mutual interference and sequencing errors; highlights need for inter-arm collision avoidance and finer temporal sequencing in future work.
AI.motivation: Current MLLM benchmarks focus on single-arm manipulation or static perception, failing to capture the complex spatio-temporal coordination required for bimanual tasks. There is a critical need to study mutual interactions between arms (kinematics, reachability, collision) to better benchmark embodied AI.
AI.method: Introduce BiManiBench, a hierarchical benchmark with three tiers: (1) fundamental spatial reasoning, (2) high-level action planning, and (3) low-level end-effector control. The framework isolates bimanual challenges like arm reachability and kinematic constraints, enabling distinction between perceptual hallucinations and planning failures. Evaluation across over 30 state-of-the-art MLLMs to diagnose dual-arm grounding and control performance.
AI.result: Findings indicate that while models show competence in high-level reasoning, they struggle with dual-arm spatial grounding, leading to mutual interference and sequencing errors. The results imply a shallow understanding of mutual kinematic constraints in current MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: Future work should focus on inter-arm collision avoidance and fine-grained temporal sequencing, along with improving integration of perception, planning, and control for robust bimanual manipulation in MLLMs.

title: SoulX-FlashHead: Oracle-guided Generation of Infinite Real-time Streaming Talking Heads
summary: Achieving a balance between high-fidelity visual quality and low-latency streaming remains a formidable challenge in audio-driven portrait generation. Existing large-scale models often suffer from prohibitive computational costs, while lightweight alternatives typically compromise on holistic facial representations and temporal stability. In this paper, we propose SoulX-FlashHead, a unified 1.3B-parameter framework designed for real-time, infinite-length, and high-fidelity streaming video generation. To address the instability of audio features in streaming scenarios, we introduce Streaming-Aware Spatiotemporal Pre-training equipped with a Temporal Audio Context Cache mechanism, which ensures robust feature extraction from short audio fragments. Furthermore, to mitigate the error accumulation and identity drift inherent in long-sequence autoregressive generation, we propose Oracle-Guided Bidirectional Distillation, leveraging ground-truth motion priors to provide precise physical guidance. We also present VividHead, a large-scale, high-quality dataset containing 782 hours of strictly aligned footage to support robust training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SoulX-FlashHead achieves state-of-the-art performance on HDTF and VFHQ benchmarks. Notably, our Lite variant achieves an inference speed of 96 FPS on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090, facilitating ultra-fast interaction without sacrificing visual coherence.

AI.tldr: Unified 1.3B audio-driven portrait generation framework enabling real-time, infinite-length streaming with streaming-aware pretraining, oracle-guided distillation, and a 782-hour aligned dataset; achieves SOTA on HDTF and VFHQ with 96 FPS Lite variant.
AI.motivation: The core challenge is balancing high-fidelity visual quality with low-latency, robust streaming in audio-driven portrait generation. Large-scale models offer quality but are computationally prohibitive; lightweight models sacrifice holistic facial representation and temporal stability.
AI.method: SoulX-FlashHead: a unified 1.3B-parameter model for real-time streaming. Introduces Streaming-Aware Spatiotemporal Pre-training with a Temporal Audio Context Cache to stabilize features from short audio fragments. Proposes Oracle-Guided Bidirectional Distillation using ground-truth motion priors to curb error accumulation and identity drift. Supports training with VividHead, a 782-hour strictly aligned dataset.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art on HDTF and VFHQ benchmarks. The Lite variant attains 96 FPS inference on a single RTX 4090 while maintaining visual coherence and temporal stability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed streaming-aware pretraining and oracle-guided distillation effectively address instability and drift in long-sequence autoregressive generation, enabling real-time, high-fidelity, infinite-length streaming for audio-driven portrait synthesis. The VividHead dataset supports robust training, validating practical feasibility and potential for interactive applications.

title: Achieving Optimal Static and Dynamic Regret Simultaneously in Bandits with Deterministic Losses
summary:
In adversarial multi-armed bandits, two performance measures are commonly used: static regret, which compares the learner to the best fixed arm, and dynamic regret, which compares it to the best sequence of arms. While optimal algorithms are known for each measure individually, there is no known algorithm achieving optimal bounds for both simultaneously. Marinov and Zimmert [2021] first showed that such simultaneous optimality is impossible against an adaptive adversary. Our work takes a first step to demonstrate its possibility against an oblivious adversary when losses are deterministic. First, we extend the impossibility result of Marinov and Zimmert [2021] to the case of deterministic losses. Then, we present an algorithm achieving optimal static and dynamic regret simultaneously against an oblivious adversary. Together, they reveal a fundamental separation between adaptive and oblivious adversaries when multiple regret benchmarks are considered simultaneously. It also provides new insight into the long open problem of simultaneously achieving optimal regret against switching benchmarks of different numbers of switches.
  Our algorithm uses negative static regret to compensate for the exploration overhead incurred when controlling dynamic regret, and leverages Blackwell approachability to jointly control both regrets. This yields a new model selection procedure for bandits that may be of independent interest.

AI.tldr: Demonstrates a separation between adversary types in adversarial bandits: impossible to achieve both static and dynamic regret optimally against adaptive adversaries; but for deterministic losses against oblivious adversaries, provides an algorithm that attains optimal static and dynamic regret simultaneously, using negative static regret and Blackwell approachability, and offers a model selection perspective.
AI.motivation: There is a long-standing gap: optimal static regret and optimal dynamic regret are known separately, with no known algorithm achieving both simultaneously. Marinov and Zimmert (2021) proved impossibility against adaptive adversaries. This work investigates the oblivious-adversary setting with deterministic losses to see whether simultaneous optimality is feasible, and analyzes the fundamental role of adversary choice in multi-benchmark regret.
AI.method: 1) Extend the Marinov–Zimmert impossibility result to deterministic losses. 2) Propose an algorithm that achieves optimal static and dynamic regret simultaneously against an oblivious adversary. 3) Use negative static regret to offset exploration overhead when controlling dynamic regret. 4) Employ Blackwell approachability to jointly constrain both regrets. 5) Derive a model selection procedure from the joint-control framework.
AI.result: The impossibility result is extended to deterministic losses, establishing a separation between adaptive and oblivious adversaries. The proposed algorithm attains optimal static and dynamic regret simultaneously against an oblivious adversary, demonstrating the feasibility of simultaneous optimality in this setting. The work also highlights a new model selection mechanism rooted in the joint control of regrets, which may be of independent interest and sheds light on switching benchmarks with varying numbers of switches.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals a fundamental separation: simultaneous optimal regret is impossible against adaptive adversaries but achievable against oblivious adversaries with deterministic losses. It advances the long-standing open problem on regret with switching benchmarks and introduces a practical, information-theoretic approach that combines negative static regret and Blackwell approachability, potentially inspiring new model-selection techniques in bandits.

title: Small Agent Group is the Future of Digital Health
summary: The rapid adoption of large language models (LLMs) in digital health has been driven by a "scaling-first" philosophy, i.e., the assumption that clinical intelligence increases with model size and data. However, real-world clinical needs include not only effectiveness, but also reliability and reasonable deployment cost. Since clinical decision-making is inherently collaborative, we challenge the monolithic scaling paradigm and ask whether a Small Agent Group (SAG) can support better clinical reasoning. SAG shifts from single-model intelligence to collective expertise by distributing reasoning, evidence-based analysis, and critical audit through a collaborative deliberation process. To assess the clinical utility of SAG, we conduct extensive evaluations using diverse clinical metrics spanning effectiveness, reliability, and deployment cost. Our results show that SAG achieves superior performance compared to a single giant model, both with and without additional optimization or retrieval-augmented generation. These findings suggest that the synergistic reasoning represented by SAG can substitute for model parameter growth in clinical settings. Overall, SAG offers a scalable solution to digital health that better balances effectiveness, reliability, and deployment efficiency.

AI.tldr: Small Agent Group (SAG) uses collaborative reasoning to substitute for scaling giant LLMs, achieving better clinical performance and lower deployment costs.
AI.motivation: Reassess the scaling-first paradigm in digital health; address reliability, cost, and the inherently collaborative nature of clinical decision-making.
AI.method: Extensive evaluations across diverse clinical metrics; compare SAG against a single giant model, with and without retrieval-augmented generation; includes optimization and evidence-based auditing.
AI.result: SAG outperforms a single giant model on effectiveness, reliability, and deployment cost; SAG's synergistic reasoning can substitute for parameter growth in clinical settings.
AI.conclusion: SAG offers a scalable, balanced approach to digital health by distributing reasoning across multiple agents, improving clinical utility while containing deployment costs.

title: Graph-Loc: Robust Graph-Based LiDAR Pose Tracking with Compact Structural Map Priors under Low Observability and Occlusion
summary: Map-based LiDAR pose tracking is essential for long-term autonomous operation, where onboard map priors need be compact for scalable storage and fast retrieval, while online observations are often partial, repetitive, and heavily occluded. We propose Graph-Loc, a graph-based localization framework that tracks the platform pose against compact structural map priors represented as a lightweight point-line graph. Such priors can be constructed from heterogeneous sources commonly available in practice, including polygon outlines vectorized from occupancy/grid maps and CAD/model/floor-plan layouts. For each incoming LiDAR scan, Graph-Loc extracts sparse point and line primitives to form an observation graph, retrieves a pose-conditioned visible subgraph via LiDAR ray simulation, and performs scan-to-map association through unbalanced optimal transport with a local graph-context regularizer. The unbalanced formulation relaxes mass conservation, improving robustness to missing, spurious, and fragmented structures under occlusion. To enhance stability in low-observability segments, we estimate information anisotropy from the refinement normal matrix and defer updates along weakly constrained directions until sufficient constraints reappear. Experiments on public benchmarks, controlled stress tests, and real-world deployments demonstrate accurate and stable tracking with KB-level priors from heterogeneous map sources, including under geometrically degenerate and sustained occlusion and in the presence of gradual scene changes.

AI.tldr: Graph-Loc is a graph-based LiDAR pose-tracking framework that uses compact point-line map priors and unbalanced optimal transport with local graph-context regularization to achieve robust, scalable tracking under occlusion and scene changes.
AI.motivation: Long-term autonomous operation requires compact, scalable map priors and robust data association despite partial, repetitive, and occluded observations.
AI.method: Construct lightweight point-line priors from heterogeneous sources (occupancy/grid outlines, CAD/floorplans). For each LiDAR scan, extract sparse point/line primitives to form an observation graph, retrieve a pose-conditioned visible subgraph via LiDAR ray simulation, and perform scan-to-map association using unbalanced optimal transport with a local graph-context regularizer. Relax mass conservation to improve robustness to missing/spurious/fragmented structures. Estimate information anisotropy from the refinement normal matrix and defer updates along weakly constrained directions until sufficient constraints reappear.
AI.result: Experiments on public benchmarks, stress tests, and real deployments show accurate and stable tracking using KB-level priors from heterogeneous maps, robust to geometric degeneracy, sustained occlusion, and gradual scene changes.
AI.conclusion: Graph-Loc provides a scalable, robust solution for map-based LiDAR tracking with multi-source priors, achieving stable performance under challenging occlusions and scene changes.

title: SpatialReward: Bridging the Perception Gap in Online RL for Image Editing via Explicit Spatial Reasoning
summary: Online Reinforcement Learning (RL) offers a promising avenue for complex image editing but is currently constrained by the scarcity of reliable and fine-grained reward signals. Existing evaluators frequently struggle with a critical perception gap we term "Attention Collapse," where models neglect cross-image comparisons and fail to capture fine-grained details, resulting in inaccurate perception and miscalibrated scores. To address these limitations, we propose SpatialReward, a reward model that enforces precise verification via explicit spatial reasoning. By anchoring reasoning to predicted edit regions, SpatialReward grounds semantic judgments in pixel-level evidence, significantly enhancing evaluative accuracy. Trained on a curated 260k spatial-aware dataset, our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on MMRB2 and EditReward-Bench, and outperforms proprietary evaluators on our proposed MultiEditReward-Bench. Furthermore, SpatialReward serves as a robust signal in online RL, boosting OmniGen2 by +0.90 on GEdit-Bench--surpassing the leading discriminative model and doubling the gain of GPT-4.1 (+0.45). These results demonstrate that spatial reasoning is essential for unlocking effective alignment in image editing.

AI.tldr: SpatialReward is a spatially-grounded reward model for online RL in image editing that addresses Attention Collapse by anchoring judgments to predicted edit regions; trained on a 260k spatial-aware dataset; achieves SOTA on MMRB2/EditReward-Bench; boosts OmniGen2 by +0.90 on GEdit-Bench; surpasses GPT-4.1 gains; demonstrates importance of spatial reasoning for alignment.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of fine-grained reward signals in online RL for image editing and a perception gap where models ignore cross-image comparisons and fine details (Attention Collapse).
AI.method: Introduce SpatialReward, a reward model with explicit spatial reasoning anchored to predicted edit regions; train on a 260k spatial-aware dataset; evaluate on MMRB2, EditReward-Bench, MultiEditReward-Bench; integrate as reward signal in online RL to improve OmniGen2; compare to proprietary evaluators and GPT-4.1 baselines.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art on MMRB2 and EditReward-Bench; outperforms proprietary evaluators on MultiEditReward-Bench; boosts OmniGen2 by +0.90 on GEdit-Bench, surpassing leading discriminative model and doubling GPT-4.1 gain (+0.45).
AI.conclusion: Spatial reasoning grounded in pixel-level evidence is essential for effective alignment in image editing; spatially-aware reward signals improve evaluative accuracy and RL performance.

title: Sign-Based Optimizers Are Effective Under Heavy-Tailed Noise
summary: While adaptive gradient methods are the workhorse of modern machine learning, sign-based optimization algorithms such as Lion and Muon have recently demonstrated superior empirical performance over AdamW in training large language models (LLM). However, a theoretical understanding of why sign-based updates outperform variance-adapted methods remains elusive. In this paper, we aim to bridge the gap between theory and practice through the lens of heavy-tailed gradient noise, a phenomenon frequently observed in language modeling tasks. Theoretically, we introduce a novel generalized heavy-tailed noise condition that captures the behavior of LLMs more accurately than standard finite variance assumptions. Under this noise model, we establish sharp convergence rates of SignSGD and Lion for generalized smooth function classes, matching or surpassing previous best-known bounds. Furthermore, we extend our analysis to Muon and Muonlight, providing what is, to our knowledge, the first rigorous analysis of matrix optimization under heavy-tailed stochasticity. These results offer a strong theoretical justification for the empirical superiority of sign-based optimizers, showcasing that they are naturally suited to handle the noisy gradients associated with heavy tails. Empirically, LLM pretraining experiments validate our theoretical insights and confirm that our proposed noise models are well-aligned with practice.

AI.tldr: We propose a heavy-tailed gradient noise model for LLM training and derive sharp convergence guarantees for sign-based optimizers (SignSGD, Lion) and their matrix-optimization extensions (Muon, Muonlight), supported by LLM pretraining experiments.
AI.motivation: Empirically, sign-based optimizers outperform variance-adapted methods in LLM training; existing finite-variance theory does not capture heavy-tailed gradient noise observed in practice. A theoretical framework is needed to explain and predict this behavior.
AI.method: Introduce generalized heavy-tailed noise condition; analyze convergence of SignSGD and Lion under generalized smoothness; derive rates; extend analysis to Muon and Muonlight for matrix optimization under heavy-tailed stochasticity; provide empirical validation on LLM pretraining.
AI.result: Derived sharp convergence rates for sign-based methods under the new noise model, matching or surpassing existing bounds; first rigorous analysis of Muon/Muonlight in heavy-tailed setting; empirical results align with theory, validating noise model and conclusions.
AI.conclusion: Sign-based optimizers naturally handle heavy-tailed gradient noise in LLM training, offering theoretical justification for their empirical advantage; the generalized heavy-tailed framework bridges theory and practice and can guide future optimizer design and analysis.

title: Structure-Aware Robust Counterfactual Explanations via Conditional Gaussian Network Classifiers
summary: Counterfactual explanation (CE) is a core technique in explainable artificial intelligence (XAI), widely used to interpret model decisions and suggest actionable alternatives. This work presents a structure-aware and robustness-oriented counterfactual search method based on the conditional Gaussian network classifier (CGNC). The CGNC has a generative structure that encodes conditional dependencies and potential causal relations among features through a directed acyclic graph (DAG). This structure naturally embeds feature relationships into the search process, eliminating the need for additional constraints to ensure consistency with the model's structural assumptions. We adopt a convergence-guaranteed cutting-set procedure as an adversarial optimization framework, which iteratively approximates solutions that satisfy global robustness conditions. To address the nonconvex quadratic structure induced by feature dependencies, we apply piecewise McCormick relaxation to reformulate the problem as a mixed-integer linear program (MILP), ensuring global optimality. Experimental results show that our method achieves strong robustness, with direct global optimization of the original formulation providing especially stable and efficient results. The proposed framework is extensible to more complex constraint settings, laying the groundwork for future advances in counterfactual reasoning under nonconvex quadratic formulations.

AI.tldr: A structure-aware counterfactual search for CGNC using a DAG to encode feature dependencies, solved via a convergence-guaranteed cutting-set framework with piecewise McCormick relaxations to MILP for global optimality and robustness.
AI.motivation: Provide robust, structurally consistent counterfactual explanations that respect the model's conditional dependencies and potential causal relations, addressing nonconvexity while guaranteeing global optimality.
AI.method: Leverage CGNC with a DAG-encoded dependency structure; integrate into a convergence-guaranteed cutting-set adversarial optimization; reformulate the nonconvex quadratic problem with piecewise McCormick relaxations to a MILP to ensure global optimality; results show robustness and efficiency.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate strong robustness; direct global optimization of the original nonconvex formulation yields particularly stable and efficient explanations.
AI.conclusion: The framework is extensible to more complex constraint settings and provides a foundation for future counterfactual reasoning under nonconvex quadratic formulations.

title: Decentralized Intent-Based Multi-Robot Task Planner with LLM Oracles on Hyperledger Fabric
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have opened new opportunities for transforming natural language user intents into executable actions. This capability enables embodied AI agents to perform complex tasks, without involvement of an expert, making human-robot interaction (HRI) more convenient. However these developments raise significant security and privacy challenges such as self-preferencing, where a single LLM service provider dominates the market and uses this power to promote their own preferences. LLM oracles have been recently proposed as a mechanism to decentralize LLMs by executing multiple LLMs from different vendors and aggregating their outputs to obtain a more reliable and trustworthy final result. However, the accuracy of these approaches highly depends on the aggregation method. The current aggregation methods mostly use semantic similarity between various LLM outputs, not suitable for robotic task planning, where the temporal order of tasks is important. To fill the gap, we propose an LLM oracle with a new aggregation method for robotic task planning. In addition, we propose a decentralized multi-robot infrastructure based on Hyperledger Fabric that can host the proposed oracle. The proposed infrastructure enables users to express their natural language intent to the system, which then can be decomposed into subtasks. These subtasks require coordinating different robots from different vendors, while enforcing fine-grained access control management on the data. To evaluate our methodology, we created the SkillChain-RTD benchmark made it publicly available. Our experimental results demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed architecture, and the proposed aggregation method outperforms other aggregation methods currently in use.

AI.tldr: Introduces an LLM oracle for robotic task planning that aggregates outputs from multiple vendors with a new aggregation method tailored to task order, deployable on a Hyperledger Fabric-based decentralized infrastructure, and validated by a public SkillChain-RTD benchmark showing improved accuracy over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Address security/privacy risks and single-provider dominance in LLM services (self-preferencing) by decentralizing LLM execution for robotic task planning; ensure reliable, ordered subtasks coordination across multi-vendor robots with fine-grained access control.
AI.method: Proposes a novel aggregation method designed for robotic task planning that preserves temporal order of subtasks. Builds a decentralized multi-robot infrastructure via Hyperledger Fabric to host the oracle, enabling natural language intents to be decomposed into subtasks and coordinated among robots from different vendors with strict access control.
AI.result: Feasibility demonstrated experimentally; the proposed aggregation method outperforms existing aggregation approaches; a public benchmark (SkillChain-RTD) is introduced for evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the viability of a decentralized LLM-oracle for robotic task planning with improved accuracy, enabling cross-vendor coordination under secure access control, and provides a reusable benchmark for future evaluation.

title: GlobalWasteData: A Large-Scale, Integrated Dataset for Robust Waste Classification and Environmental Monitoring
summary: The growing amount of waste is a problem for the environment that requires efficient sorting techniques for various kinds of waste. An automated waste classification system is used for this purpose. The effectiveness of these Artificial Intelligence (AI) models depends on the quality and accessibility of publicly available datasets, which provide the basis for training and analyzing classification algorithms. Although several public waste classification datasets exist, they remain fragmented, inconsistent, and biased toward specific environments. Differences in class names, annotation formats, image conditions, and class distributions make it difficult to combine these datasets or train models that generalize well to real world scenarios. To address these issues, we introduce the GlobalWasteData (GWD) archive, a large scale dataset of 89,807 images across 14 main categories, annotated with 68 distinct subclasses. We compile this novel integrated GWD archive by merging multiple publicly available datasets into a single, unified resource. This GWD archive offers consistent labeling, improved domain diversity, and more balanced class representation, enabling the development of robust and generalizable waste recognition models. Additional preprocessing steps such as quality filtering, duplicate removal, and metadata generation further improve dataset reliability. Overall, this dataset offers a strong foundation for Machine Learning (ML) applications in environmental monitoring, recycling automation, and waste identification, and is publicly available to promote future research and reproducibility.

AI.tldr: GlobalWasteData (GWD) is a unified, large-scale waste image dataset (89,807 images, 14 main categories, 68 subclasses) built by merging public datasets to provide consistent labeling and greater domain diversity for robust waste recognition models.
AI.motivation: Public waste datasets are fragmented, inconsistently labeled, and biased to specific environments, hindering generalizable AI-based waste classification.
AI.method: Merge multiple public datasets into a single resource; standardize labels and annotation formats; apply quality filtering, duplicate removal, and metadata generation; organize into 14 main categories with 68 subclasses; aim for balanced class representation and domain diversity.
AI.result: A publicly available GlobalWasteData archive with 89,807 images, 14 main categories, 68 subclasses; improved labeling consistency, diversity, and data reliability for ML in environmental monitoring and recycling automation.
AI.conclusion: GWD provides a robust foundation for training generalizable waste recognition models and promotes reproducibility; can facilitate better performance in real-world waste sorting scenarios.

title: Brep2Shape: Boundary and Shape Representation Alignment via Self-Supervised Transformers
summary: Boundary representation (B-rep) is the industry standard for computer-aided design (CAD). While deep learning shows promise in processing B-rep models, existing methods suffer from a representation gap: continuous approaches offer analytical precision but are visually abstract, whereas discrete methods provide intuitive clarity at the expense of geometric precision. To bridge this gap, we introduce Brep2Shape, a novel self-supervised pre-training method designed to align abstract boundary representations with intuitive shape representations. Our method employs a geometry-aware task where the model learns to predict dense spatial points from parametric Bézier control points, enabling the network to better understand physical manifolds derived from abstract coefficients. To enhance this alignment, we propose a Dual Transformer backbone with parallel streams that independently encode surface and curve tokens to capture their distinct geometric properties. Moreover, the topology attention is integrated to model the interdependencies between surfaces and curves, thereby maintaining topological consistency. Experimental results demonstrate that Brep2Shape offers significant scalability, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and faster convergence across various downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: Brep2Shape is a self-supervised pre-training approach that aligns boundary representations (B-rep) with intuitive shape representations using a geometry-aware task and a Dual Transformer backbone with topology attention, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and faster convergence on downstream CAD tasks.
AI.motivation: Addresses the representation gap in CAD deep learning between continuous, analytically precise B-rep and discrete, intuitive shape representations, aiming to improve geometric understanding and downstream performance.
AI.method: A geometry-aware self-supervised task that predicts dense spatial points from parametric Bézier control points; a Dual Transformer backbone with parallel streams encodes surface and curve tokens separately; topology attention models interdependencies between surfaces and curves to preserve topology.
AI.result: Demonstrates scalability with state-of-the-art accuracy and faster convergence across multiple downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Bridging the gap between abstract B-rep coefficients and intuitive shape representations via aligned encodings improves geometric comprehension and yields superior CAD model understanding and efficiency.

title: Free(): Learning to Forget in Malloc-Only Reasoning Models
summary:
Reasoning models enhance problem-solving by scaling test-time compute, yet they face a critical paradox: excessive thinking tokens often degrade performance rather than improve it. We attribute this to a fundamental architectural flaw: standard LLMs operate as "malloc-only" engines, continuously accumulating valid and redundant steps alike without a mechanism to prune obsolete information. To break this cycle, we propose Free()LM, a model that introduces an intrinsic self-forgetting capability via the Free-Module, a plug-and-play LoRA adapter. By iteratively switching between reasoning and cleaning modes, Free()LM dynamically identifies and prunes useless context chunks, maintaining a compact and noise-free state.
  Extensive experiments show that Free()LM provides consistent improvements across all model scales (8B to 685B). It achieves a 3.3% average improvement over top-tier reasoning baselines, even establishing a new SOTA on IMOanswerBench using DeepSeek V3.2-Speciale. Most notably, in long-horizon tasks where the standard Qwen3-235B-A22B model suffers a total collapse (0% accuracy), Free()LM restores performance to 50%. Our findings suggest that sustainable intelligence requires the freedom to forget as much as the power to think.

AI.tldr: Free()LM introduces a self-forgetting mechanism via a Free-Module (LoRA) to prune useless context chunks, improving reasoning performance across scales and long-horizon tasks by alternating reasoning and cleaning modes.
AI.motivation: To solve the paradox where increasing test-time reasoning tokens can hurt performance due to lack of pruning; standard LLMs accumulate both useful and redundant steps without forgetting.
AI.method: Introduce Free-Module, a plug-and-play LoRA adapter enabling self-forgetting. Iteratively switch between reasoning and cleaning modes to identify and prune useless context chunks, maintaining a compact, noise-free state. Evaluate across model scales (8B–685B) and long-horizon tasks, reporting improvements and SOTA on specific benchmarks.
AI.result: Consistent improvements across all model scales, averaging +3.3% over top reasoning baselines; achieves new SOTA on IMOanswerBench with DeepSeek V3.2-Speciale; in long-horizon tasks, recovers from 0% to 50% accuracy for a challenged model (Qwen3-235B-A22B).
AI.conclusion: Sustainable intelligence requires the ability to forget as well as to think; dynamic pruning of context enables more robust reasoning by maintaining a compact, noise-free internal state.

title: Bi-Adapt: Few-shot Bimanual Adaptation for Novel Categories of 3D Objects via Semantic Correspondence
summary: Bimanual manipulation is imperative yet challenging for robots to execute complex tasks, requiring coordinated collaboration between two arms. However, existing methods for bimanual manipulation often rely on costly data collection and training, struggling to generalize to unseen objects in novel categories efficiently. In this paper, we present Bi-Adapt, a novel framework designed for efficient generalization for bimanual manipulation via semantic correspondence. Bi-Adapt achieves cross-category affordance mapping by leveraging the strong capability of vision foundation models. Fine-tuning with restricted data on novel categories, Bi-Adapt exhibits notable generalization to out-of-category objects in a zero-shot manner. Extensive experiments conducted in both simulation and real-world environments validate the effectiveness of our approach and demonstrate its high efficiency, achieving a high success rate on different benchmark tasks across novel categories with limited data. Project website: https://biadapt-project.github.io/

AI.tldr: Bi-Adapt enables efficient cross-category generalization for bimanual manipulation by leveraging semantic correspondence from vision foundation models and fine-tuning with limited data to achieve zero-shot generalization on unseen instances.
AI.motivation: Address data inefficiency and poor generalization in bimanual manipulation, enabling cross-category affordance mapping via foundation-model-driven semantic correspondence.
AI.method: Bi-Adapt framework uses semantic correspondence from vision foundation models to map affordances across object categories; performs fine-tuning with restricted data on novel categories; achieves cross-category mapping for bimanual tasks.
AI.result: Demonstrates high success rates across benchmark tasks in simulation and real world; efficient adaptation with limited data; zero-shot generalization to certain unseen objects.
AI.conclusion: Bi-Adapt offers an effective, data-efficient path to cross-category bimanual manipulation by leveraging semantic correspondences from foundation models, with strong empirical validation.

title: Thermal odometry and dense mapping using learned ddometry and Gaussian splatting
summary: Thermal infrared sensors, with wavelengths longer than smoke particles, can capture imagery independent of darkness, dust, and smoke. This robustness has made them increasingly valuable for motion estimation and environmental perception in robotics, particularly in adverse conditions. Existing thermal odometry and mapping approaches, however, are predominantly geometric and often fail across diverse datasets while lacking the ability to produce dense maps. Motivated by the efficiency and high-quality reconstruction ability of recent Gaussian Splatting (GS) techniques, we propose TOM-GS, a thermal odometry and mapping method that integrates learning-based odometry with GS-based dense mapping. TOM-GS is among the first GS-based SLAM systems tailored for thermal cameras, featuring dedicated thermal image enhancement and monocular depth integration. Extensive experiments on motion estimation and novel-view rendering demonstrate that TOM-GS outperforms existing learning-based methods, confirming the benefits of learning-based pipelines for robust thermal odometry and dense reconstruction.

AI.tldr: A learning-enabled thermal SLAM framework (TOM-GS) combining monocular depth integration with Gaussian Splatting for dense mapping, achieving superior robustness and dense reconstructions over prior geometric thermal SLAM methods.
AI.motivation: Thermal imaging is robust to darkness, dust, and smoke, but existing thermal odometry/mapping is largely geometric, brittle across datasets, and unable to produce dense maps. A method that combines learning-based perception with dense GS-based mapping could improve robustness and map quality.
AI.method: TOM-GS integrates learning-based odometry with Gaussian Splatting–based dense mapping for thermal cameras. It includes dedicated thermal image enhancement and monocular depth integration, and is the first GS-based SLAM system tailored for thermal imagery.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on motion estimation and novel-view rendering show TOM-GS outperforms existing learning-based methods, validating the benefits of learning-based pipelines for robust thermal odometry and dense reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of combining learning-based odometry with GS-based dense mapping for thermal SLAM, enabling robust odometry and high-quality dense reconstructions in adverse conditions; suggests future work on real-time performance, generalization, and additional thermal-specific enhancements.

title: Active Learning Using Aggregated Acquisition Functions: Accuracy and Sustainability Analysis
summary: Active learning (AL) is a machine learning (ML) approach that strategically selects the most informative samples for annotation during training, aiming to minimize annotation costs. This strategy not only reduces labeling expenses but also results in energy savings during neural network training, thereby enhancing both data and energy efficiency. In this paper, we implement and evaluate various state-of-the-art acquisition functions, analyzing their accuracy and computational costs, while discussing the advantages and disadvantages of each method. Our findings reveal that representativity-based acquisition functions effectively explore the dataset but do not prioritize boundary decisions, whereas uncertainty-based acquisition functions focus on refining boundary decisions already identified by the neural network. This trade-off is known as the exploration-exploitation dilemma. To address this dilemma, we introduce six aggregation structures: series, parallel, hybrid, adaptive feedback, random exploration, and annealing exploration. Our aggregated acquisition functions alleviate common AL pathologies such as batch mode inefficiency and the cold start problem. Additionally, we focus on balancing accuracy and energy consumption, contributing to the development of more sustainable, energy-aware artificial intelligence (AI). We evaluate our proposed structures on various models and datasets. Our results demonstrate the potential of these structures to reduce computational costs while maintaining or even improving accuracy. Innovative aggregation approaches, such as alternating between acquisition functions such as BALD and BADGE, have shown robust results. Sequentially running functions like $K$-Centers followed by BALD has achieved the same performance goals with up to 12\% fewer samples, while reducing the acquisition cost by almost half.

AI.tldr: Six aggregation structures for active learning (series, parallel, hybrid, adaptive feedback, random exploration, annealing exploration) improve accuracy and reduce energy/annotation costs by balancing exploration and exploitation; examples include alternating BALD and BADGE or sequencing K-Centers before BALD, achieving similar performance with fewer samples (up to 12%) and lower acquisition cost (~50%).
AI.motivation: Reduce annotation costs and energy consumption in active learning while maintaining high accuracy; address the exploration-exploitation trade-off and common AL pathologies (batch mode inefficiency, cold start).
AI.method: Implement and evaluate multiple state-of-the-art acquisition functions; analyze accuracy and computational costs; develop six aggregation structures (series, parallel, hybrid, adaptive feedback, random exploration, annealing exploration) to combine acquisition strategies; assess on various models and datasets.
AI.result: Aggregation-based AL strategies reduce computational costs while maintaining or improving accuracy. Notably, alternating acquisition functions (e.g., BALD and BADGE) yields robust results; sequentially applying K-Centers followed by BALD achieves equivalent performance with up to 12% fewer labeled samples and nearly 50% reduction in acquisition cost.
AI.conclusion: Aggregation-driven AL design can mitigate common AL pathologies and enable energy-aware AI by balancing accuracy and resource use; the proposed structures offer practical, cost-effective routes to efficient AL across models and datasets.

title: Graph-Enhanced Deep Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Objective Unrelated Parallel Machine Scheduling
summary: The Unrelated Parallel Machine Scheduling Problem (UPMSP) with release dates, setups, and eligibility constraints presents a significant multi-objective challenge. Traditional methods struggle to balance minimizing Total Weighted Tardiness (TWT) and Total Setup Time (TST). This paper proposes a Deep Reinforcement Learning framework using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and a Graph Neural Network (GNN). The GNN effectively represents the complex state of jobs, machines, and setups, allowing the PPO agent to learn a direct scheduling policy. Guided by a multi-objective reward function, the agent simultaneously minimizes TWT and TST. Experimental results on benchmark instances demonstrate that our PPO-GNN agent significantly outperforms a standard dispatching rule and a metaheuristic, achieving a superior trade-off between both objectives. This provides a robust and scalable solution for complex manufacturing scheduling.

AI.tldr: A PPO-GNN deep reinforcement learning framework for UPMSP with release dates, setups, and eligibility constraints to jointly minimize Total Weighted Tardiness and Total Setup Time, outperforming standard dispatching rules and a metaheuristic.
AI.motivation: UPMSP with release dates, setups, and eligibility constraints is a complex, multi-objective scheduling problem. Conventional methods struggle to balance tardiness and setup overhead, and scalable, adaptive solutions are needed.
AI.method: A Graph Neural Network encodes the state of jobs, machines, and setups. A Proximal Policy Optimization agent learns a direct scheduling policy guided by a multi-objective reward that targets both TWT and TST. Training and evaluation are conducted on benchmark instances, with comparisons against a standard dispatching rule and a metaheuristic.
AI.result: The PPO-GNN agent significantly outperforms the baselines, achieving a superior trade-off between Total Weighted Tardiness and Total Setup Time.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a robust and scalable solution for complex manufacturing scheduling, with potential for practical deployment and extension to additional objectives.

title: SteerVLA: Steering Vision-Language-Action Models in Long-Tail Driving Scenarios
summary: A fundamental challenge in autonomous driving is the integration of high-level, semantic reasoning for long-tail events with low-level, reactive control for robust driving. While large vision-language models (VLMs) trained on web-scale data offer powerful common-sense reasoning, they lack the grounded experience necessary for safe vehicle control. We posit that an effective autonomous agent should leverage the world knowledge of VLMs to guide a steerable driving policy toward robust control in driving scenarios. To this end, we propose SteerVLA, which leverages the reasoning capabilities of VLMs to produce fine-grained language instructions that steer a vision-language-action (VLA) driving policy. Key to our method is this rich language interface between the high-level VLM and low-level VLA, which allows the high-level policy to more effectively ground its reasoning in the control outputs of the low-level policy. To provide fine-grained language supervision aligned with vehicle control, we leverage a VLM to augment existing driving data with detailed language annotations, which we find to be essential for effective reasoning and steerability. We evaluate SteerVLA on a challenging closed-loop benchmark, where it outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 4.77 points in overall driving score and by 8.04 points on a long-tail subset. The project website is available at: https://steervla.github.io/.

AI.tldr: SteerVLA uses vision-language models to generate fine-grained language instructions that steer a vision-language-action driving policy, bridging high-level semantic reasoning and low-level control; augmented language supervision improves ground-truth alignment; achieves SOTA performance on a closed-loop driving benchmark.
AI.motivation: To address the gap between powerful but inattentive VLM-driven reasoning and the grounded, reliable control required for autonomous driving; high-level semantics need alignment with low-level policy; augment data with language annotations to enhance steerability.
AI.method: Propose SteerVLA framework: a VLM produces fine-grained language instructions that steer a VLA driving policy; implement a rich language interface between high-level reasoning and low-level control; augment driving data with detailed language annotations via VLM to provide supervision; evaluate on a closed-loop benchmark.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 4.77 points in overall driving score and 8.04 points on the long-tail subset.
AI.conclusion: Integrating VLM-derived language guidance with a steerable VLA policy improves robustness and long-tail driving performance; fine-grained language supervision is essential for effective grounding of high-level reasoning into control.

title: Learning Brain Representation with Hierarchical Visual Embeddings
summary: Decoding visual representations from brain signals has attracted significant attention in both neuroscience and artificial intelligence. However, the degree to which brain signals truly encode visual information remains unclear. Current visual decoding approaches explore various brain-image alignment strategies, yet most emphasize high-level semantic features while neglecting pixel-level details, thereby limiting our understanding of the human visual system. In this paper, we propose a brain-image alignment strategy that leverages multiple pre-trained visual encoders with distinct inductive biases to capture hierarchical and multi-scale visual representations, while employing a contrastive learning objective to achieve effective alignment between brain signals and visual embeddings. Furthermore, we introduce a Fusion Prior, which learns a stable mapping on large-scale visual data and subsequently matches brain features to this pre-trained prior, thereby enhancing distributional consistency across modalities. Extensive quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate that our method achieves a favorable balance between retrieval accuracy and reconstruction fidelity.

AI.tldr: A brain–image alignment method using multiple pre-trained visual encoders and a Fusion Prior with contrastive learning to align brain signals to hierarchical, multi-scale visual embeddings, achieving good retrieval and reconstruction fidelity.
AI.motivation: To determine how extensively brain signals encode visual information and to improve decoding beyond high-level semantics by capturing pixel-level details and ensuring multisensory distributional consistency.
AI.method: Compute brain–image alignment by leveraging several pre-trained visual encoders with distinct inductive biases to capture hierarchical and multi-scale features; use a contrastive learning objective to align brain signals with visual embeddings; introduce a Fusion Prior that learns a stable mapping on large-scale visual data and then matches brain features to this prior to stabilize cross-modal distributional alignment.
AI.result: Quantitative and qualitative evaluations show a favorable balance between retrieval accuracy and reconstruction fidelity, indicating effective brain-to-visual embedding alignment across modalities.
AI.conclusion: The approach improves decoding of visual information from brain signals by combining multi-encoder visual representations with a pre-trained Fusion Prior, achieving accurate retrieval while preserving pixel-level reconstruction details.

title: Proximal Action Replacement for Behavior Cloning Actor-Critic in Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) optimizes policies from a previously collected static dataset and is an important branch of RL. A popular and promising approach is to regularize actor-critic methods with behavior cloning (BC), which yields realistic policies and mitigates bias from out-of-distribution actions, but can impose an often-overlooked performance ceiling: when dataset actions are suboptimal, indiscriminate imitation structurally prevents the actor from fully exploiting high-value regions suggested by the critic, especially in later training when imitation is already dominant. We formally analyzed this limitation by investigating convergence properties of BC-regularized actor-critic optimization and verified it on a controlled continuous bandit task. To break this ceiling, we propose proximal action replacement (PAR), a plug-and-play training sample replacer that progressively replaces low-value actions with high-value actions generated by a stable actor, broadening the action exploration space while reducing the impact of low-value data. PAR is compatible with multiple BC regularization paradigms. Extensive experiments across offline RL benchmarks show that PAR consistently improves performance and approaches state-of-the-art when combined with the basic TD3+BC.

AI.tldr: Proposes proximal action replacement (PAR) to overcome a performance ceiling in BC-regularized offline RL by replacing low-value actions with high-value actions from a stable actor, improving exploration; demonstrates empirical gains and compatibility with TD3+BC.
AI.motivation: Offline RL with BC regularization can fix realism but imposes a ceiling when dataset actions are suboptimal, as imitation hinders exploiting high-value critic guidance; need mechanisms to widen the effective action distribution without sacrificing stability.
AI.method: Formal analysis of BC-regularized actor-critic convergence; validation on a controlled continuous bandit task; introduction of PAR, a plug-and-play sample replacer that progressively replaces low-value actions with high-value actions from a stable actor; designed to be compatible with multiple BC regularization schemes.
AI.result: PAR yields consistent performance gains across offline RL benchmarks and approaches state-of-the-art when combined with basic TD3+BC.
AI.conclusion: PAR successfully relaxes the BC-imitation ceiling in offline RL, offering a practical, compatible enhancement to BC-regularized actor-critic methods that broadens action exploration while maintaining stability.

title: Securing Dual-Use Pathogen Data of Concern
comment: 39th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2025) Workshop: Biosecurity Safeguards for Generative AI
summary: Training data is an essential input into creating competent artificial intelligence (AI) models. AI models for biology are trained on large volumes of data, including data related to biological sequences, structures, images, and functions. The type of data used to train a model is intimately tied to the capabilities it ultimately possesses--including those of biosecurity concern. For this reason, an international group of more than 100 researchers at the recent 50th anniversary Asilomar Conference endorsed data controls to prevent the use of AI for harmful applications such as bioweapons development. To help design such controls, we introduce a five-tier Biosecurity Data Level (BDL) framework for categorizing pathogen data. Each level contains specific data types, based on their expected ability to contribute to capabilities of concern when used to train AI models. For each BDL tier, we propose technical restrictions appropriate to its level of risk. Finally, we outline a novel governance framework for newly created dual-use pathogen data. In a world with widely accessible computational and coding resources, data controls may be among the most high-leverage interventions available to reduce the proliferation of concerning biological AI capabilities.

AI.tldr: Five-tier Biosecurity Data Level (BDL) framework categorizes pathogen data for AI applications, with tier-specific data types and restrictions plus a governance scheme for dual-use data.
AI.motivation: Respond to calls from the Asilomar Conference to implement data controls that mitigate misuse of AI in biology and reduce the emergence of harmful biosecurity capabilities.
AI.method: Define a five-tier BDL; assign data types to each tier based on contribution to capabilities of concern; propose technical restrictions per tier; outline a governance framework for newly created dual-use pathogen data.
AI.result: A structured BDL framework plus governance proposal intended to guide safe data use in AI for biology and to reduce dual-use risk.
AI.conclusion: Data controls, when properly designed and implemented, can be among the most effective interventions to curb the proliferation of concerning biological AI capabilities.

title: Post-Collision Trajectory Restoration for a Single-track Ackermann Vehicle using Heuristic Steering and Tractive Force Functions
summary: Post-collision trajectory restoration is a safety-critical capability for autonomous vehicles, as impact-induced lateral motion and yaw transients can rapidly drive the vehicle away from the intended path. This paper proposes a structured heuristic recovery control law that jointly commands steering and tractive force for a generalized single-track Ackermann vehicle model. The formulation explicitly accounts for time-varying longitudinal velocity in the lateral-yaw dynamics and retains nonlinear steering-coupled interaction terms that are commonly simplified in the literature. Unlike approaches that assume constant longitudinal speed, the proposed design targets the transient post-impact regime where speed variations and nonlinear coupling significantly influence recovery. The method is evaluated in simulation on the proposed generalized single-track model and a standard 3DOF single-track reference model in MATLAB, demonstrating consistent post-collision restoration behaviour across representative initial post-impact conditions.

AI.tldr: Structured heuristic recovery control for post-collision trajectory restoration of a generalized single-track Ackermann vehicle; accounts for time-varying speed and nonlinear coupling; validated via MATLAB simulations showing robust recovery across initial post-impact conditions.
AI.motivation: Post-collision recovery is safety-critical for autonomous vehicles. Existing methods often assume constant longitudinal speed and simplify steering–yaw interactions, potentially degrading performance during speed transients.
AI.method: Proposes a structured heuristic recovery control law that jointly commands steering and tractive force. The model includes time-varying longitudinal velocity in the lateral–yaw dynamics and preserves nonlinear steering–coupled terms. Evaluated on a generalized single-track Ackermann model and a 3DOF reference model in MATLAB.
AI.result: Simulation results show consistent post-collision restoration behavior across representative initial post-impact conditions.
AI.conclusion: The approach addresses transient speed variations and nonlinear coupling, improving robustness of trajectory restoration in post-collision scenarios.

title: IM-Animation: An Implicit Motion Representation for Identity-decoupled Character Animation
summary: Recent progress in video diffusion models has markedly advanced character animation, which synthesizes motioned videos by animating a static identity image according to a driving video. Explicit methods represent motion using skeleton, DWPose or other explicit structured signals, but struggle to handle spatial mismatches and varying body scales. %proportions. Implicit methods, on the other hand, capture high-level implicit motion semantics directly from the driving video, but suffer from identity leakage and entanglement between motion and appearance. To address the above challenges, we propose a novel implicit motion representation that compresses per-frame motion into compact 1D motion tokens. This design relaxes strict spatial constraints inherent in 2D representations and effectively prevents identity information leakage from the motion video. Furthermore, we design a temporally consistent mask token-based retargeting module that enforces a temporal training bottleneck, mitigating interference from the source images' motion and improving retargeting consistency. Our methodology employs a three-stage training strategy to enhance the training efficiency and ensure high fidelity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our implicit motion representation and the propose IM-Animation's generative capabilities are achieve superior or competitive performance compared with state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: Introduces IM-Animation, an implicit motion representation that uses compact 1D motion tokens and a mask-token retargeting module, with a three-stage training pipeline, achieving competitive or superior results for video-driven character animation.
AI.motivation: Addresses limitations of explicit motion representations (skeleton/DWPose) which struggle with spatial misalignment and varying body scales, and implicit methods that suffer from identity leakage and entanglement between motion and appearance.
AI.method: Compress per-frame motion into compact 1D tokens; employ a temporally consistent mask-token-based retargeting module to enforce a temporal training bottleneck and improve retargeting consistency; utilize a three-stage training strategy to enhance efficiency and fidelity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that the proposed implicit motion representation and IM-Animation achieve superior or competitive performance compared with state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The combination of implicit 1D motion tokens with mask-token retargeting and a staged training pipeline effectively mitigates identity leakage and spatial mismatches in video-driven character animation, delivering strong generative capabilities.

title: Objective Decoupling in Social Reinforcement Learning: Recovering Ground Truth from Sycophantic Majorities
summary: Contemporary AI alignment strategies rely on a fragile premise: that human feedback, while noisy, remains a fundamentally truthful signal. In this paper, we identify this assumption as Dogma 4 of Reinforcement Learning (RL). We demonstrate that while this dogma holds in static environments, it fails in social settings where evaluators may be sycophantic, lazy, or adversarial. We prove that under Dogma 4, standard RL agents suffer from what we call Objective Decoupling, a structural failure mode where the agent's learned objective permanently separates from the latent ground truth, guaranteeing convergence to misalignment. To resolve this, we propose Epistemic Source Alignment (ESA). Unlike standard robust methods that rely on statistical consensus (trusting the majority), ESA utilizes sparse safety axioms to judge the source of the feedback rather than the signal itself. We prove that this "judging the judges" mechanism guarantees convergence to the true objective, even when a majority of evaluators are biased. Empirically, we show that while traditional consensus methods fail under majority collusion, our approach successfully recovers the optimal policy.

AI.tldr: Proposes Epistemic Source Alignment (ESA) to fix RL misalignment caused by biased/evaluators. Introduces Objective Decoupling; proves convergence to the true objective under ESA; shows ESA outperforms consensus under majority collusion both theoretically and empirically.
AI.motivation: Human feedback in RL is noisy but typically treated as truthful. In social settings, evaluators can be sycophantic, lazy, or adversarial, causing misalignment or objective drift (Objective Decoupling). Need to shift focus from trusting signals to assessing the source of feedback.
AI.method: Identify Dogma 4 (trusting human feedback as truth). Define Objective Decoupling as a structural failure where learned objective diverges from ground truth. Propose Epistemic Source Alignment (ESA), which uses sparse safety axioms to judge feedback sources rather than the signal itself (a 'judge the judges' approach). Provide theoretical proofs of convergence to true objective under ESA; and empirical tests comparing ESA to robust consensus methods under majority bias.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantee: ESA ensures convergence to the true objective even when a majority of evaluators are biased. Empirical results show that consensus-based methods fail under majority collusion, while ESA recovers the optimal policy.
AI.conclusion: Shifting to source-level evaluation of feedback (ESA) robustly aligns RL agents to the ground truth despite biased evaluators, addressing Objective Decoupling and providing both theoretical and empirical support.

title: UAV-Supported Maritime Search System: Experience from Valun Bay Field Trials
summary: This paper presents the integration of flow field reconstruction, dynamic probabilistic modeling, search control, and machine vision detection in a system for autonomous maritime search operations. Field experiments conducted in Valun Bay (Cres Island, Croatia) involved real-time drifter data acquisition, surrogate flow model fitting based on computational fluid dynamics and numerical optimization, advanced multi-UAV search control and vision sensing, as well as deep learning-based object detection. The results demonstrate that a tightly coupled approach enables reliable detection of floating targets under realistic uncertainties and complex environmental conditions, providing concrete insights for future autonomous maritime search and rescue applications.

AI.tldr: Integrated system for autonomous maritime search leveraging real-time drifter data, CFD-based surrogate flow modeling, probabilistic dynamics, UAV search control, and deep-learning vision; field tests show reliable floating-target detection under realistic maritime conditions.
AI.motivation: Improve reliability and autonomy of maritime search and rescue by tightly coupling flow estimation, probabilistic planning, control, and sensing to cope with environmental uncertainties.
AI.method: Combine flow-field reconstruction, dynamic probabilistic modeling, search control, and machine vision detection. Field deployment with real-time drifter data; surrogate flow model calibration via CFD and numerical optimization; coordinated multi-UAV search, vision sensing, and deep learning-based object detection.
AI.result: Demonstrates that a tightly coupled approach yields reliable floating-target detection despite uncertainties and complex conditions; provides actionable insights for future autonomous maritime search and rescue applications.
AI.conclusion: The integrated framework shows promise for scalable autonomous maritime search and rescue, with avenues for enhanced real-time adaptation, uncertainty quantification, and expansion to larger or more dynamic environments.

title: Adaptive Image Zoom-in with Bounding Box Transformation for UAV Object Detection
comment: paper accepted by ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing ( IF=12.2)
summary: Detecting objects from UAV-captured images is challenging due to the small object size. In this work, a simple and efficient adaptive zoom-in framework is explored for object detection on UAV images. The main motivation is that the foreground objects are generally smaller and sparser than those in common scene images, which hinders the optimization of effective object detectors. We thus aim to zoom in adaptively on the objects to better capture object features for the detection task. To achieve the goal, two core designs are required: \textcolor{black}{i) How to conduct non-uniform zooming on each image efficiently? ii) How to enable object detection training and inference with the zoomed image space?} Correspondingly, a lightweight offset prediction scheme coupled with a novel box-based zooming objective is introduced to learn non-uniform zooming on the input image. Based on the learned zooming transformation, a corner-aligned bounding box transformation method is proposed. The method warps the ground-truth bounding boxes to the zoomed space to learn object detection, and warps the predicted bounding boxes back to the original space during inference. We conduct extensive experiments on three representative UAV object detection datasets, including VisDrone, UAVDT, and SeaDronesSee. The proposed ZoomDet is architecture-independent and can be applied to an arbitrary object detection architecture.
