Remarkably, on the SeaDronesSee dataset, ZoomDet offers more than 8.4 absolute gain of mAP with a Faster R-CNN model, with only about 3 ms additional latency. The code is available at https://github.com/twangnh/zoomdet_code.

AI.tldr: An architecture-agnostic adaptive zoom-in framework (ZoomDet) for UAV object detection, using a lightweight offset predictor and a box-based zoom objective to learn non-uniform zooms and warp boxes between original and zoomed spaces, yielding substantial mAP gains with minimal latency.
AI.motivation: UAV images feature small, sparse foreground objects, which impair detector optimization. Adaptive, non-uniform zooming aims to magnify object details efficiently and improve detection performance, while maintaining compatibility with any detector and keeping latency low.
AI.method: Introduce a lightweight offset-prediction module to determine non-uniform zooms per image. Use a box-based zooming objective to learn the zooming transformation. Apply corner-aligned bounding box transformation by warping ground-truth boxes to zoomed space during training and back to original space during inference. The ZoomDet framework is architecture-independent and can be plugged into any detector.
AI.result: Evaluations on VisDrone, UAVDT, and SeaDronesSee show that ZoomDet enhances performance; on SeaDronesSee with Faster R-CNN, it achieves >8.4 absolute mAP gain with only ~3 ms additional latency. Code is available online.
AI.conclusion: ZoomDet is an effective, efficient, and architecture-agnostic solution for UAV object detection with small objects, enabling improved feature learning through adaptive zooms without significant computational overhead.

title: On the Importance of a Multi-Scale Calibration for Quantization
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) is a cornerstone for efficiently deploying large language models (LLMs), where a small calibration set critically affects quantization performance. However, conventional practices rely on random sequences of fixed length, overlooking the variable-length nature of LLM inputs. Input length directly influences the activation distribution and, consequently, the weight importance captured by the Hessian, which in turn affects quantization outcomes. As a result, Hessian estimates derived from fixed-length calibration may fail to represent the true importance of weights across diverse input scenarios. We propose MaCa (Matryoshka Calibration), a simple yet effective method for length-aware Hessian construction. MaCa (i) incorporates multi-scale sequence length information into Hessian estimation and (ii) regularizes each sequence as an independent sample, yielding a more stable and fruitful Hessian for accurate quantization. Experiments on state-of-the-art LLMs (e.g., Qwen3, Gemma3, LLaMA3) demonstrate that MaCa consistently improves accuracy under low bit quantization, offering a lightweight enhancement compatible with existing PTQ frameworks. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to systematically highlight the role of multi-scale calibration in LLM quantization.

AI.tldr: MaCa proposes length-aware Hessian construction for post-training quantization (PTQ) of LLMs, using multi-scale sequence lengths and treating each input sequence as an independent sample to stabilize Hessian estimates, leading to improved low-bit quantization accuracy across models like Qwen3, Gemma3, and LLaMA3.
AI.motivation: Calibrating LLMs for PTQ relies on fixed-length random sequences, but input lengths vary greatly in practice. Activation distributions and weight Hessians depend on input length, so length-invariant calibration yields misestimated weight importance and poorer quantization.
AI.method: MaCa (Matryoshka Calibration) integrates multi-scale sequence length information into Hessian estimation and regularizes each sequence as an independent sample. This yields a more robust, scale-aware Hessian that better reflects weight importance across diverse inputs, improving quantization outcomes.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on state-of-the-art LLMs (Qwen3, Gemma3, LLaMA3) shows consistent accuracy gains under low-bit quantization, with MaCa serving as a lightweight enhancement compatible with existing PTQ pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Length-aware calibration is crucial for effective LLM quantization. MaCa provides a simple, effective approach to incorporate multi-scale input lengths, yielding more reliable Hessian-based weight importance and improved quantization performance.

title: Interpretable Failure Analysis in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Systems
summary: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) is increasingly deployed in safety-critical domains, yet methods for interpretable failure detection and attribution remain underdeveloped. We introduce a two-stage gradient-based framework that provides interpretable diagnostics for three critical failure analysis tasks: (1) detecting the true initial failure source (Patient-0); (2) validating why non-attacked agents may be flagged first due to domino effects; and (3) tracing how failures propagate through learned coordination pathways. Stage 1 performs interpretable per-agent failure detection via Taylor-remainder analysis of policy-gradient costs, declaring an initial Patient-0 candidate at the first threshold crossing. Stage 2 provides validation through geometric analysis of critic derivatives-first-order sensitivity and directional second-order curvature aggregated over causal windows to construct interpretable contagion graphs. This approach explains "downstream-first" detection anomalies by revealing pathways that amplify upstream deviations. Evaluated across 500 episodes in Simple Spread (3 and 5 agents) and 100 episodes in StarCraft II using MADDPG and HATRPO, our method achieves 88.2-99.4% Patient-0 detection accuracy while providing interpretable geometric evidence for detection decisions. By moving beyond black-box detection to interpretable gradient-level forensics, this framework offers practical tools for diagnosing cascading failures in safety-critical MARL systems.

AI.tldr: Two-stage gradient-based framework for interpretable failure diagnostics in MARL. Stage 1 uses Taylor-remainder analysis on policy-gradient costs to detect an initial Patient-0; Stage 2 uses critic-derivative first-order sensitivity and directional second-order curvature over causal windows to build contagion graphs, explaining downstream failures. Demonstrated on Simple Spread (3/5 agents) and StarCraft II with MADDPG and HATRPO, achieving 88.2-99.4% Patient-0 detection accuracy.
AI.motivation: Interpretable failure detection and attribution in safety-critical MARL, where black-box diagnostics are insufficient for diagnosing cascading failures and tracing propagation through coordination pathways.
AI.method: Stage 1: interpretable per-agent failure detection via Taylor-remainder analysis of policy-gradient costs, declaring a Patient-0 candidate at the first threshold crossing. Stage 2: validation through geometric analysis of critic derivatives-first-order sensitivity and directional second-order curvature aggregated over causal windows to construct interpretable contagion graphs. This yields explanations for downstream-first detections by revealing amplification pathways.
AI.result: Evaluated on 500 episodes in Simple Spread (3 and 5 agents) and 100 episodes in StarCraft II using MADDPG and HATRPO. Reported Patient-0 detection accuracy of 88.2-99.4%, with interpretable geometric evidence supporting decisions.
AI.conclusion: Moves beyond black-box detection to gradient-level forensics, offering practical tools for diagnosing cascading failures in safety-critical MARL systems.

title: Reliability-aware Execution Gating for Near-field and Off-axis Vision-guided Robotic Alignment
summary: Vision-guided robotic systems are increasingly deployed in precision alignment tasks that require reliable execution under near-field and off-axis configurations. While recent advances in pose estimation have significantly improved numerical accuracy, practical robotic systems still suffer from frequent execution failures even when pose estimates appear accurate. This gap suggests that pose accuracy alone is insufficient to guarantee execution-level reliability. In this paper, we reveal that such failures arise from a deterministic geometric error amplification mechanism, in which small pose estimation errors are magnified through system structure and motion execution, leading to unstable or failed alignment. Rather than modifying pose estimation algorithms, we propose a Reliability-aware Execution Gating mechanism that operates at the execution level. The proposed approach evaluates geometric consistency and configuration risk before execution, and selectively rejects or scales high-risk pose updates. We validate the proposed method on a real UR5 robotic platform performing single-step visual alignment tasks under varying camera-target distances and off-axis configurations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed execution gating significantly improves task success rates, reduces execution variance, and suppresses tail-risk behavior, while leaving average pose accuracy largely unchanged. Importantly, the proposed mechanism is estimator-agnostic and can be readily integrated with both classical geometry-based and learning-based pose estimation pipelines. These results highlight the importance of execution-level reliability modeling and provide a practical solution for improving robustness in near-field vision-guided robotic systems.

AI.tldr: Reliability-aware execution gating to mitigate error amplification in near-field vision-guided robotic alignment, improving execution reliability without degrading pose accuracy.
AI.motivation: Pose accuracy does not guarantee execution success in near-field/ off-axis setups; small pose errors can be deterministically amplified by system geometry during motion, causing failures.
AI.method: Propose an execution-level gating mechanism that evaluates geometric consistency and configuration risk before execution. It is estimator-agnostic and can reject or scale high-risk pose updates. Validated on a UR5 platform across varying camera-target distances and off-axis configurations using single-step visual alignment tasks.
AI.result: Execution gating significantly improves task success rates, reduces execution variance and tail-risk behavior, while leaving average pose accuracy largely unchanged; compatible with classical geometry-based and learning-based pose estimation pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Execution-level reliability modeling is essential for robustness in near-field vision-guided robotics; the gating mechanism offers a practical, estimator-agnostic solution to improve robustness without altering pose estimation methods.

title: CA-YOLO: Cross Attention Empowered YOLO for Biomimetic Localization
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication.Please note that once the article has been published by IEEE, preprints on locations not specified above should be removed if possible
summary: In modern complex environments, achieving accurate and efficient target localization is essential in numerous fields. However, existing systems often face limitations in both accuracy and the ability to recognize small targets. In this study, we propose a bionic stabilized localization system based on CA-YOLO, designed to enhance both target localization accuracy and small target recognition capabilities. Acting as the "brain" of the system, the target detection algorithm emulates the visual focusing mechanism of animals by integrating bionic modules into the YOLO backbone network. These modules include the introduction of a small target detection head and the development of a Characteristic Fusion Attention Mechanism (CFAM). Furthermore, drawing inspiration from the human Vestibulo-Ocular Reflex (VOR), a bionic pan-tilt tracking control strategy is developed, which incorporates central positioning, stability optimization, adaptive control coefficient adjustment, and an intelligent recapture function. The experimental results show that CA-YOLO outperforms the original model on standard datasets (COCO and VisDrone), with average accuracy metrics improved by 3.94%and 4.90%, respectively.Further time-sensitive target localization experiments validate the effectiveness and practicality of this bionic stabilized localization system.

AI.tldr: CA-YOLO integrates bionic modules into YOLO to boost small-target detection and localization in complex environments, using a small-target head and CFAM, plus a VOR-inspired pan-tilt control. It surpasses COCO and VisDrone baselines by 3.94% and 4.90% in average precision, with time-sensitive validation supporting practicality.
AI.motivation: Limitations in current target localization systems include insufficient accuracy and poor recognition of small targets in complex scenes, hindering reliable operation in real-world applications.
AI.method: Enhance YOLO backbone with (1) a small-target detection head; (2) a Characteristic Fusion Attention Mechanism (CFAM); (3) vicarious biologically inspired components, including a bionic VOR-based pan-tilt tracking control with central positioning, stability optimization, adaptive coefficient tuning, and an intelligent recapture function.
AI.result: CA-YOLO outperforms the baseline YOLO on COCO and VisDrone, with average precision improvements of 3.94% and 4.90%, respectively. Time-sensitive localization experiments validate practicality.
AI.conclusion: A biologically inspired augmentation to YOLO (CFAM+small-target head and VOR-based pan-tilt control) improves target localization accuracy and small-target recognition, showing effectiveness and practicality in dynamic scenarios.

title: Bandit Allocational Instability
summary: When multi-armed bandit (MAB) algorithms allocate pulls among competing arms, the resulting allocation can exhibit huge variation. This is particularly harmful in modern applications such as learning-enhanced platform operations and post-bandit statistical inference. Thus motivated, we introduce a new performance metric of MAB algorithms termed allocation variability, which is the largest (over arms) standard deviation of an arm's number of pulls. We establish a fundamental trade-off between allocation variability and regret, the canonical performance metric of reward maximization. In particular, for any algorithm, the worst-case regret $R_T$ and worst-case allocation variability $S_T$ must satisfy $R_T \cdot S_T=Ω(T^{\frac{3}{2}})$ as $T\rightarrow\infty$, as long as $R_T=o(T)$. This indicates that any minimax regret-optimal algorithm must incur worst-case allocation variability $Θ(T)$, the largest possible scale; while any algorithm with sublinear worst-case regret must necessarily incur ${S}_T= ω(\sqrt{T})$. We further show that this lower bound is essentially tight, and that any point on the Pareto frontier $R_T \cdot S_T=\tildeΘ(T^{3/2})$ can be achieved by a simple tunable algorithm UCB-f, a generalization of the classic UCB1. Finally, we discuss implications for platform operations and for statistical inference, when bandit algorithms are used. As a byproduct of our result, we resolve an open question of Praharaj and Khamaru (2025).

AI.tldr: Introduces allocation variability as the max-arm standard deviation of pull counts, and establishes a fundamental minimax trade-off with regret: R_T * S_T = Ω(T^{3/2}) for sublinear regret. Shows tightness via UCB-f achieving Pareto frontier R_T * S_T = ~Θ(T^{3/2}); resolves an open question by Praharaj and Khamaru (2025).
AI.motivation: Allocation variability during MAB experiments is harmful for platform operations and post-bandit statistical inference; quantifying the trade-off with regret helps understand fundamental limits and design tunable algorithms.
AI.method: Minimax analysis over time horizon T; derive lower bound on the product of worst-case regret and worst-case allocation variability under R_T = o(T); propose a tunable algorithm UCB-f (generalizing UCB1) and show it achieves the Pareto frontier; analyze asymptotics with tilde-Θ to indicate polylog factors.
AI.result: Proves R_T * S_T = Ω(T^{3/2}) for any algorithm with sublinear regret; shows S_T must be Θ(T) for regret-optimal algorithms; shows S_T = ω(√T) for any sublinear-regret algorithm; presents UCB-f achieving R_T * S_T = tildeΘ(T^{3/2}); resolves an open question.
AI.conclusion: Allocation variability and regret are fundamentally linked; one cannot minimize both beyond a Pareto frontier. The results guide design of bandit algorithms for practical deployments and have implications for platform operations and inference; provides a tunable framework (UCB-f) to navigate the trade-off and tightens theoretical understanding.

title: Characteristics, Management, and Utilization of Muscles in Musculoskeletal Humanoids: Empirical Study on Kengoro and Musashi
summary: Various musculoskeletal humanoids have been developed so far, and numerous studies on control mechanisms have been conducted to leverage the advantages of their biomimetic bodies. However, there has not been sufficient and unified discussion on the diverse properties inherent in these musculoskeletal structures, nor on how to manage and utilize them. Therefore, this study categorizes and analyzes the characteristics of muscles, as well as their management and utilization methods, based on the various research conducted on the musculoskeletal humanoids we have developed, Kengoro and Musashi. We classify the features of the musculoskeletal structure into five properties: Redundancy, Independency, Anisotropy, Variable Moment Arm, and Nonlinear Elasticity. We then organize the diverse advantages and disadvantages of musculoskeletal humanoids that arise from the combination of these properties. In particular, we discuss body schema learning and reflex control, along with muscle grouping and body schema adaptation. Also, we describe the implementation of movements through an integrated system and discuss future challenges and prospects.

AI.tldr: Proposes a five-property framework for musculoskeletal humanoids and analyzes how redundancy, independency, anisotropy, variable moment arm, and nonlinear elasticity interact to shape control strategies, with discussion on body schema learning, reflex control, muscle grouping, and integrated movement implementation.
AI.motivation: There is no unified discussion on the diverse properties of musculoskeletal structures or on how to manage and utilize them; aim to categorize features and analyze implications for control and system integration using Kengoro and Musashi as case studies.
AI.method: Literature-driven categorization of musculoskeletal properties from existing humanoid platforms; classify features into five properties; examine trade-offs and synergy; discuss control approaches (body schema learning, reflex control, muscle grouping, adaptation); outline integrated system implementation and future challenges.
AI.result: A five-property framework (Redundancy, Independency, Anisotropy, Variable Moment Arm, Nonlinear Elasticity) with an organized map of advantages and disadvantages arising from their combinations; guidelines for control strategies and adaptation; identification of future research directions.
AI.conclusion: Provides a unified lens to analyze and utilize musculoskeletal structures in humanoids, highlighting key challenges and prospects in body schema adaptation, reflex control, and integrated motion systems for Kengoro and Musashi.

title: Evaluating Object-Centric Models beyond Object Discovery
summary: Object-centric learning (OCL) aims to learn structured scene representations that support compositional generalization and robustness to out-of-distribution (OOD) data. However, OCL models are often not evaluated regarding these goals. Instead, most prior work focuses on evaluating OCL models solely through object discovery and simple reasoning tasks, such as probing the representation via image classification. We identify two limitations in existing benchmarks: (1) They provide limited insights on the representation usefulness of OCL models, and (2) localization and representation usefulness are assessed using disjoint metrics. To address (1), we use instruction-tuned VLMs as evaluators, enabling scalable benchmarking across diverse VQA datasets to measure how well VLMs leverage OCL representations for complex reasoning tasks. To address (2), we introduce a unified evaluation task and metric that jointly assess localization (where) and representation usefulness (what), thereby eliminating inconsistencies introduced by disjoint evaluation. Finally, we include a simple multi-feature reconstruction baseline as a reference point.

AI.tldr: Unified evaluation framework for Object-Centric Learning (OCL) using instruction-tuned Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to assess how representations support complex reasoning, with a joint localization-representation metric and a simple reconstruction baseline.
AI.motivation: Current OCL benchmarks separate localization and representation assessment and focus on discovery/classification, providing limited insights into usefulness and robustness. A scalable, joint evaluation framework is needed.
AI.method: - Use instruction-tuned VLMs as evaluators on diverse VQA datasets to test how well OCL representations support reasoning; - Propose a unified task and metric that jointly measure localization (where) and representation usefulness (what); - Include a simple multi-feature reconstruction baseline as a reference point.
AI.result: Proposes and outlines an evaluation framework; no empirical results are reported in the abstract, but the approach enables scalable benchmarking across VQA datasets.
AI.conclusion: The framework links localization with representation usefulness, enabling more informative OCL benchmarking and assessment of generalization and robustness.

title: Bipartite Graph Attention-based Clustering for Large-scale scRNA-seq Data
summary: scRNA-seq clustering is a critical task for analyzing single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data, as it groups cells with similar gene expression profiles. Transformers, as powerful foundational models, have been applied to scRNA-seq clustering. Their self-attention mechanism automatically assigns higher attention weights to cells within the same cluster, enhancing the distinction between clusters. Existing methods for scRNA-seq clustering, such as graph transformer-based models, treat each cell as a token in a sequence. Their computational and space complexities are $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ with respect to the number of cells, limiting their applicability to large-scale scRNA-seq datasets.To address this challenge, we propose a Bipartite Graph Transformer-based clustering model (BGFormer) for scRNA-seq data. We introduce a set of learnable anchor tokens as shared reference points to represent the entire dataset. A bipartite graph attention mechanism is introduced to learn the similarity between cells and anchor tokens, bringing cells of the same class closer together in the embedding space. BGFormer achieves linear computational complexity with respect to the number of cells, making it scalable to large datasets. Experimental results on multiple large-scale scRNA-seq datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and scalability of BGFormer.

AI.tldr: BGFormer uses learnable anchor tokens and bipartite graph attention to perform scRNA-seq clustering with linear time in the number of cells, enabling scalable analysis of large datasets.
AI.motivation: Traditional graph-transformer scRNA-seq clustering methods have O(n^2) computational and space complexity with respect to the number of cells, hindering scalability to large datasets.
AI.method: Introduce a set of learnable anchor tokens as shared references to represent the dataset. Employ a bipartite graph attention mechanism to learn cell–anchor similarities, pulling same-cluster cells closer in embedding space and achieving linear complexity in n.
AI.result: Empirically demonstrates scalability and effectiveness on multiple large-scale scRNA-seq datasets, validating the approach."
AI.conclusion: BGFormer offers a scalable, effective clustering framework for scRNA-seq data by leveraging anchor-based representations and bipartite attention to achieve linear complexity and improved cluster separation.

title: RECUR: Resource Exhaustion Attack via Recursive-Entropy Guided Counterfactual Utilization and Reflection
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) employ reasoning to address complex tasks. Such explicit reasoning requires extended context lengths, resulting in substantially higher resource consumption. Prior work has shown that adversarially crafted inputs can trigger redundant reasoning processes, exposing LRMs to resource-exhaustion vulnerabilities. However, the reasoning process itself, especially its reflective component, has received limited attention, even though it can lead to over-reflection and consume excessive computing power. In this paper, we introduce Recursive Entropy to quantify the risk of resource consumption in reflection, thereby revealing the safety issues inherent in inference itself. Based on Recursive Entropy, we introduce RECUR, a resource exhaustion attack via Recursive Entropy guided Counterfactual Utilization and Reflection. It constructs counterfactual questions to verify the inherent flaws and risks of LRMs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that, under benign inference, recursive entropy exhibits a pronounced decreasing trend. RECUR disrupts this trend, increasing the output length by up to 11x and decreasing throughput by 90%. Our work provides a new perspective on robust reasoning.

AI.tldr: Introduces Recursive Entropy and RECUR to quantify and exploit resource risk in LRM reflection, showing substantial degradation in efficiency under attack.
AI.motivation: Address resource-exhaustion vulnerabilities in Large Reasoning Models due to extended reasoning and reflective processes; address safety of inference beyond accuracy.
AI.method: Define Recursive Entropy as a metric; design RECUR attack using Recursive Entropy-guided counterfactual utilization and reflection; construct counterfactual questions to reveal inherent flaws; validate with extensive experiments comparing benign vs. adversarial inference.
AI.result: Recursive Entropy declines under benign inference; RECUR disrupts this trend, increasing output length up to 11x and reducing throughput by 90%; evidences resource-exhaustion risk in reflective reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a new metric and attack to illuminate safety concerns in inference and reflection; calls for defenses and robust reasoning strategies in LRMs.

title: UniPlan: Vision-Language Task Planning for Mobile Manipulation with Unified PDDL Formulation
summary: Integration of VLM reasoning with symbolic planning has proven to be a promising approach to real-world robot task planning. Existing work like UniDomain effectively learns symbolic manipulation domains from real-world demonstrations, described in Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL), and has successfully applied them to real-world tasks. These domains, however, are restricted to tabletop manipulation. We propose UniPlan, a vision-language task planning system for long-horizon mobile-manipulation in large-scale indoor environments, that unifies scene topology, visuals, and robot capabilities into a holistic PDDL representation. UniPlan programmatically extends learned tabletop domains from UniDomain to support navigation, door traversal, and bimanual coordination. It operates on a visual-topological map, comprising navigation landmarks anchored with scene images. Given a language instruction, UniPlan retrieves task-relevant nodes from the map and uses a VLM to ground the anchored image into task-relevant objects and their PDDL states; next, it reconnects these nodes to a compressed, densely-connected topological map, also represented in PDDL, with connectivity and costs derived from the original map; Finally, a mobile-manipulation plan is generated using off-the-shelf PDDL solvers. Evaluated on human-raised tasks in a large-scale map with real-world imagery, UniPlan significantly outperforms VLM and LLM+PDDL planning in success rate, plan quality, and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: UniPlan extends UniDomain to long-horizon mobile manipulation by grounding a visual-topological map in PDDL and solving with standard planners, enabling navigation, door traversal, and bimanual tasks in large indoor environments.
AI.motivation: Overcome UniDomain's tabletop-only limitation by integrating scene topology, visuals, and robot capabilities into a unified PDDL framework for scalable, real-world tasks.
AI.method: Extend learned tabletop domains to support mobile manipulation; build a visual-topological map with navigation landmarks and scene images; use a vision-language model to ground anchored images into task-relevant objects and PDDL states; reconnect nodes into a densely-connected topological map represented in PDDL with connectivity/costs; generate plans with off-the-shelf PDDL solvers; evaluate on large-scale maps with real-world imagery.
AI.result: UniPlan significantly outperforms baselines (VLM alone and LLM+PDDL) in success rate, plan quality, and computational efficiency on long-horizon, real-world tasks.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of unifying scene topology, visuals, and robotic capabilities into a PDDL representation for scalable, long-horizon mobile manipulation in large indoor environments, with practical planning efficiency gains.

title: Fine-Grained Cat Breed Recognition with Global Context Vision Transformer
comment: 4 pages, accepted at International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT) 2025
summary: Accurate identification of cat breeds from images is a challenging task due to subtle differences in fur patterns, facial structure, and color. In this paper, we present a deep learning-based approach for classifying cat breeds using a subset of the Oxford-IIIT Pet Dataset, which contains high-resolution images of various domestic breeds. We employed the Global Context Vision Transformer (GCViT) architecture-tiny for cat breed recognition. To improve model generalization, we used extensive data augmentation, including rotation, horizontal flipping, and brightness adjustment. Experimental results show that the GCViT-Tiny model achieved a test accuracy of 92.00% and validation accuracy of 94.54%. These findings highlight the effectiveness of transformer-based architectures for fine-grained image classification tasks. Potential applications include veterinary diagnostics, animal shelter management, and mobile-based breed recognition systems. We also provide a hugging face demo at https://huggingface.co/spaces/bfarhad/cat-breed-classifier.

AI.tldr: Transformer-based GCViT-tiny achieves high accuracy (test 92%, validation 94.5%) for fine-grained cat-breed classification on a subset of the Oxford-IIIT Pet Dataset with data augmentation; reproducibility and split details need clarification.
AI.motivation: Fine-grained breed recognition is challenging due to subtle visual cues. Leveraging global-context transformers like GCViT can capture discriminative features across the image.
AI.method: Use GCViT-tiny architecture on a subset of the Oxford-IIIT Pet Dataset focusing on cats; apply extensive data augmentation (rotation, horizontal flip, brightness adjustment); report test and validation accuracies.
AI.result: GCViT-Tiny achieved 92.00% test accuracy and 94.54% validation accuracy on the proposed subset, indicating strong performance for fine-grained cat-breed classification.
AI.conclusion: Transformer-based architectures are effective for fine-grained image classification tasks such as cat-breed recognition. A HuggingFace demo is provided for reproducibility and practical deployment.

title: AI-Driven Predictive Modelling for Groundwater Salinization in Israel
comment: 60 pages, 9 figures in main text and 6 figures in appendix, 2 tables, 3 Appendix
summary: Increasing salinity and contamination of groundwater is a serious issue in many parts of the world, causing degradation of water resources. The aim of this work is to form a comprehensive understanding of groundwater salinization underlying causal factors and identify important meteorological, geological and anthropogenic drivers of salinity. We have integrated different datasets of potential covariates, to create a robust framework for machine learning based predictive models including Random Forest (RF), XGBoost, Neural network, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), convolution neural network (CNN) and linear regression (LR), of groundwater salinity. Additionally, Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) followed by Global sensitivity analysis (GSA) and Explainable AI (XAI) based SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) were used to estimate the importance scores and find insights into the drivers of salinization. We also did causality analysis via Double machine learning using various predictive models. From these analyses, key meteorological (Precipitation, Temperature), geological (Distance from river, Distance to saline body, TWI, Shoreline distance), and anthropogenic (Area of agriculture field, Treated Wastewater) covariates are identified to be influential drivers of groundwater salinity across Israel. XAI analysis also identified Treated Wastewater (TWW) as an essential anthropogenic driver of salinity, its significance being context-dependent but critical in vulnerable hydro-climatic environment. Our approach provides deeper insight into global salinization mechanisms at country scale, reducing AI model uncertainty and highlighting the need for tailored strategies to address salinity.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive ML-based framework to predict groundwater salinity using integrated covariates and multiple models, with feature selection, sensitivity analysis, SHAP-based explanations, and double ML causality; identifies meteorological, geological, and anthropogenic drivers, notably treated wastewater, at country scale.
AI.motivation: Salinization due to combined meteorological, geological, and human factors; need for robust predictive models and actionable insights to guide policy and resource management.
AI.method: Integrates diverse covariates (meteorological, geological, anthropogenic). Employs multiple ML models (RF, XGBoost, NN, LSTM, CNN, LR). Uses Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE), Global Sensitivity Analysis (GSA), and SHAP for feature importance and interpretation. Applies Double Machine Learning for causality. Identifies key drivers across Israel.
AI.result: Key drivers include precipitation, temperature, distance from river/saline body, TWI, shoreline distance, agricultural area, treated wastewater. SHAP highlights treated wastewater as a crucial anthropogenic driver in vulnerable hydro-climatic settings. Framework yields deeper bilateral understanding of salinization mechanisms and reduces AI model uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers country-scale mechanistic insight into salinization, enabling tailored strategies to mitigate salinity; emphasizes the role of treated wastewater and the need for policy-informed management to address salinity drivers.

title: Weak-Driven Learning: How Weak Agents make Strong Agents Stronger
summary: As post-training optimization becomes central to improving large language models, we observe a persistent saturation bottleneck: once models grow highly confident, further training yields diminishing returns. While existing methods continue to reinforce target predictions, we find that informative supervision signals remain latent in models' own historical weak states. Motivated by this observation, we propose WMSS (Weak Agents Can Make Strong Agents Stronger), a post-training paradigm that leverages weak checkpoints to guide continued optimization. By identifying recoverable learning gaps via entropy dynamics and reinforcing them through compensatory learning, WMSS enables strong agents to improve beyond conventional post-training saturation. Experiments on mathematical reasoning and code generation datasets show that agents trained with our approach achieve effective performance improvements, while incurring zero additional inference cost.

AI.tldr: Post-training saturation in large language models can be mitigated by using weak checkpoints to guide compensatory learning; WMSS leverages entropy dynamics to reveal recoverable gaps, yielding performance gains without extra inference cost on math reasoning and code generation tasks.
AI.motivation: Combine the observation that rich supervision signals remain latent in historical weak states with the need to surpass diminishing returns in post-training of highly confident models.
AI.method: Identify recoverable learning gaps by analyzing entropy dynamics across weak checkpoints and reinforce them through compensatory learning, using weak agents to supervise strong agent optimization without increasing inference cost.
AI.result: Achieves performance improvements on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks when trained with WMSS, with zero additional inference cost.
AI.conclusion: Weak agents can strengthen strong agents in post-training; WMSS provides a practical approach to surpass saturation by exploiting information in weaker historical states.

title: Constrained Sampling to Guide Universal Manipulation RL
summary: We consider how model-based solvers can be leveraged to guide training of a universal policy to control from any feasible start state to any feasible goal in a contact-rich manipulation setting. While Reinforcement Learning (RL) has demonstrated its strength in such settings, it may struggle to sufficiently explore and discover complex manipulation strategies, especially in sparse-reward settings. Our approach is based on the idea of a lower-dimensional manifold of feasible, likely-visited states during such manipulation and to guide RL with a sampler from this manifold. We propose Sample-Guided RL, which uses model-based constraint solvers to efficiently sample feasible configurations (satisfying differentiable collision, contact, and force constraints) and leverage them to guide RL for universal (goal-conditioned) manipulation policies. We study using this data directly to bias state visitation, as well as using black-box optimization of open-loop trajectories between random configurations to impose a state bias and optionally add a behavior cloning loss. In a minimalistic double sphere manipulation setting, Sample-Guided RL discovers complex manipulation strategies and achieves high success rates in reaching any statically stable state. In a more challenging panda arm setting, our approach achieves a significant success rate over a near-zero baseline, and demonstrates a breadth of complex whole-body-contact manipulation strategies.

AI.tldr: Introduces Sample-Guided RL: a model-based sampling strategy to bias training of universal, goal-conditioned policies in contact-rich manipulation by guiding exploration with feasible state configurations.
AI.motivation: RL struggles with exploration in sparse-reward, contact-rich manipulation. Leveraging a lower-dimensional manifold of feasible states can provide informative priors to guide policy learning.
AI.method: Train universal goal-conditioned policies with Sample-Guided RL. Use model-based constraint solvers to sample feasible configurations satisfying differentiable collision, contact, and force constraints; use these samples to bias state visitation during RL. Additionally, perform black-box optimization of open-loop trajectories between random configurations to impose a state bias, optionally with a behavior cloning loss.
AI.result: In a minimal double-sphere manipulation task, the method yields complex manipulation strategies and high success rates to reach statically stable states. In a Panda arm task, it achieves a significant improvement over a near-zero baseline and demonstrates a broad repertoire of whole-body contact strategies.
AI.conclusion: Sampling-guided, model-based augmentation effectively guides exploration and accelerates learning of universal, goal-conditioned policies in contact-rich manipulation; the approach shows promise across simple and more challenging robots, potentially enabling robust generalization to varied starts and goals.

title: Beyond Core and Penumbra: Bi-Temporal Image-Driven Stroke Evolution Analysis
summary: Computed tomography perfusion (CTP) at admission is routinely used to estimate the ischemic core and penumbra, while follow-up diffusion-weighted MRI (DWI) provides the definitive infarct outcome. However, single time-point segmentations fail to capture the biological heterogeneity and temporal evolution of stroke. We propose a bi-temporal analysis framework that characterizes ischemic tissue using statistical descriptors, radiomic texture features, and deep feature embeddings from two architectures (mJ-Net and nnU-Net). Bi-temporal refers to admission (T1) and post-treatment follow-up (T2). All features are extracted at T1 from CTP, with follow-up DWI aligned to ensure spatial correspondence. Manually delineated masks at T1 and T2 are intersected to construct six regions of interest (ROIs) encoding both initial tissue state and final outcome. Features were aggregated per region and analyzed in feature space. Evaluation on 18 patients with successful reperfusion demonstrated meaningful clustering of region-level representations. Regions classified as penumbra or healthy at T1 that ultimately recovered exhibited feature similarity to preserved brain tissue, whereas infarct-bound regions formed distinct groupings. Both baseline GLCM and deep embeddings showed a similar trend: penumbra regions exhibit features that are significantly different depending on final state, whereas this difference is not significant for core regions. Deep feature spaces, particularly mJ-Net, showed strong separation between salvageable and non-salvageable tissue, with a penumbra separation index that differed significantly from zero (Wilcoxon signed-rank test). These findings suggest that encoder-derived feature manifolds reflect underlying tissue phenotypes and state transitions, providing insight into imaging-based quantification of stroke evolution.

AI.tldr: Bi-temporal analysis of stroke using admission CTP features and follow-up DWI outcomes to characterize ischemic tissue; deep features from mJ-Net and nnU-Net reveal state transitions and separations between salvageable and non-salvageable tissue.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of single time-point segmentations by capturing temporal evolution and heterogeneity of ischemic tissue, linking initial tissue state to final infarct outcome.
AI.method: Extract six feature types (statistical descriptors, radiomic texture, and deep embeddings) at admission (T1) from CTP. Use follow-up DWI (T2) aligned to T1 to define ROIs by intersecting manual masks at T1 and T2. Regions encode initial state and final outcome. Features are aggregated per ROI and analyzed in feature space. Deep features come from two architectures: mJ-Net and nnU-Net. Evaluation on 18 patients with successful reperfusion.
AI.result: Region-level representations exhibit meaningful clustering. Penumbra or healthy regions at T1 that recover cluster with preserved tissue; infarct-bound regions form distinct groups. Baseline GLCM and deep embeddings show penumbra features differ by final state; core features show no significant difference. mJ-Net shows strong separation between salvageable and non-salvageable tissue; penumbra separation index significantly different from zero (Wilcoxon signed-rank test).
AI.conclusion: Encoder-derived feature manifolds reflect underlying tissue phenotypes and state transitions, offering promising imaging-based quantification of stroke evolution and potential to predict salvageability from early CTP data.

title: ODELoRA: Training Low-Rank Adaptation by Solving Ordinary Differential Equations
summary: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) has emerged as a widely adopted parameter-efficient fine-tuning method in deep transfer learning, due to its reduced number of trainable parameters and lower memory requirements enabled by Burer-Monteiro factorization on adaptation matrices. However, classical LoRA training methods treat the low-rank factor matrices individually and optimize them using standard gradient-based algorithms. Such decoupled optimization schemes are theoretically and empirically suboptimal, as they fail to fully exploit the intrinsic structure of the LoRA parameterization. In this work, we propose a novel continuous-time optimization dynamic for LoRA factor matrices in the form of an ordinary differential equation (ODE) that emulates the gradient flow of full fine-tuning on the balanced manifold. We term this approach ODELoRA. To faithfully track the trajectories of ODELoRA, we adopt well-established and theoretically grounded time-discretization schemes, including Euler and Runge--Kutta methods. Our framework provides a unified ODE-based perspective for understanding and designing LoRA training algorithms. We establish linear convergence of the proposed method under strongly convex objectives for certain discretization schemes under mild conditions, and further extend our analysis to the matrix sensing setting. Moreover, we show that ODELoRA achieves stable feature learning, a property that is crucial for training deep neural networks at different scales of problem dimensionality. Empirical results on matrix sensing tasks confirm the derived linear convergence behavior, and experiments on training physics-informed neural networks further demonstrate the superiority of ODELoRA over existing baselines, especially in the training stability.

AI.tldr: An ODE-based, continuous-time framework (ODELoRA) for LoRA factor optimization, using discretizations (Euler/RK) to emulate full fine-tuning gradient flow on a balanced manifold; provides convergence guarantees and stability with empirical validation in matrix sensing and physics-informed neural networks.
AI.motivation: To overcome suboptimal decoupled optimization of LoRA low-rank factors by leveraging the intrinsic structure of LoRA parametrization and ensuring stable, convergent training.
AI.method: Model LoRA factors as an ordinary differential equation on the balanced manifold to mimic full fine-tuning gradient flow; discretize via Euler and Runge–Kutta schemes for practical optimization; analyze convergence under strongly convex objectives and extend to matrix sensing; empirically validate on matrix sensing and physics-informed neural networks.
AI.result: The approach achieves linear convergence for certain discretizations under mild conditions; extends to matrix sensing; demonstrates stable feature learning; empirical results confirm convergence behavior and superior training stability of ODELoRA over baselines, especially in challenging training regimes.
AI.conclusion: ODELoRA provides a unified, theoretically grounded framework for LoRA training that delivers convergence guarantees and improved stability, with empirical success in matrix sensing and physics-informed neural networks.

title: InfiCoEvalChain: A Blockchain-Based Decentralized Framework for Collaborative LLM Evaluation
summary: The rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) demands increasingly reliable evaluation, yet current centralized evaluation suffers from opacity, overfitting, and hardware-induced variance. Our empirical analysis reveals an alarming inconsistency in existing evaluations: the standard deviation across ten repeated runs of a single model on HumanEval (1.67) actually exceeds the performance gap among the top-10 models on the official leaderboard (0.91), rendering current rankings statistically precarious. To mitigate these instabilities, we propose a decentralized evaluation framework that enables hardware and parameter diversity through large-scale benchmarking across heterogeneous compute nodes. By leveraging the blockchain-based protocol, the framework incentivizes global contributors to act as independent validators, using a robust reward system to ensure evaluation integrity and discourage dishonest participation. This collective verification transforms evaluation from a "centralized black box" into a "decentralized endorsement" where multi-party consensus and diverse inference environments yield a more stable, representative metric. Experimental results demonstrate that the decentralized evaluation framework reduces the standard deviation across ten runs on the same model to 0.28. This significant improvement over conventional frameworks ensures higher statistical confidence in model rankings. We have completely implemented this platform and will soon release it to the community.

AI.tldr: Decentralized, blockchain-based evaluation framework for LLMs reduces performance variability and increases reliability compared to centralized benchmarks; initial results show SD reduced from 1.67 to 0.28 on repeated HumanEval runs, with a plan to release platform.
AI.motivation: Address opacity, overfitting, and hardware-induced variance in centralized evaluations; current rankings are barely stable due to large evaluation noise.
AI.method: Implement a blockchain-based protocol enabling heterogeneous compute nodes to serve as validators, with a reward system to ensure integrity and discourage dishonest participation; decentralized endorsement through multi-party consensus.
AI.result: Significant reduction in evaluation variance: SD across ten runs on the same model drops from 1.67 to 0.28; framework reportedly fully implemented and ready for release.
AI.conclusion: Decentralized evaluation can yield more stable, representative model rankings and transform evaluation practice, pending broader adoption and governance.

title: Head-to-Head autonomous racing at the limits of handling in the A2RL challenge
comment: Submitted to Science Robotics for possible publication
summary: Autonomous racing presents a complex challenge involving multi-agent interactions between vehicles operating at the limit of performance and dynamics. As such, it provides a valuable research and testing environment for advancing autonomous driving technology and improving road safety. This article presents the algorithms and deployment strategies developed by the TUM Autonomous Motorsport team for the inaugural Abu Dhabi Autonomous Racing League (A2RL). We showcase how our software emulates human driving behavior, pushing the limits of vehicle handling and multi-vehicle interactions to win the A2RL. Finally, we highlight the key enablers of our success and share our most significant learnings.

AI.tldr: Autonomous racing study detailing TUM Autonomous Motorsport's algorithms and deployment strategies for the Abu Dhabi Autonomous Racing League (A2RL), showing software that emulates human driving, pushes vehicle handling, and secures a competitive win, with a focus on enablers and learnings.
AI.motivation: Autonomous racing provides a rigorous, multi-agent testbed at performance limits, advancing autonomous driving technology and road safety; A2RL offers a practical platform for development and evaluation.
AI.method: Development and deployment of race-specific algorithms and software strategies that emulate human driving behavior, manage multi-vehicle interactions, and optimize vehicle dynamics and decision-making for competitive performance.
AI.result: The approach achieved competitive success in the A2RL, demonstrating effective high-performance driving and multi-vehicle coordination, and identifying key enablers and learnings that contributed to success.
AI.conclusion: The work offers transferable insights for high-performance autonomous driving and road-safety applications, outlining essential components, enablers, and a roadmap for future improvements in autonomous racing systems.

title: LLM-Guided Diagnostic Evidence Alignment for Medical Vision-Language Pretraining under Limited Pairing
summary: Most existing CLIP-style medical vision--language pretraining methods rely on global or local alignment with substantial paired data. However, global alignment is easily dominated by non-diagnostic information, while local alignment fails to integrate key diagnostic evidence. As a result, learning reliable diagnostic representations becomes difficult, which limits their applicability in medical scenarios with limited paired data. To address this issue, we propose an LLM-Guided Diagnostic Evidence Alignment method (LGDEA), which shifts the pretraining objective toward evidence-level alignment that is more consistent with the medical diagnostic process. Specifically, we leverage LLMs to extract key diagnostic evidence from radiology reports and construct a shared diagnostic evidence space, enabling evidence-aware cross-modal alignment and allowing LGDEA to effectively exploit abundant unpaired medical images and reports, thereby substantially alleviating the reliance on paired data. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves consistent and significant improvements on phrase grounding, image--text retrieval, and zero-shot classification, and even rivals pretraining methods that rely on substantial paired data.

AI.tldr: LGDEA shifts medical V-L pretraining from global/local alignment to evidence-level alignment by using LLMs to extract diagnostic evidence from radiology reports and build a shared evidence space, enabling effective use of unpaired data.
AI.motivation: Global alignment can be dominated by non-diagnostic information and local alignment may miss key diagnostic cues, making reliable diagnostic representations hard to learn with limited paired data.
AI.method: Leverage LLMs to extract key diagnostic evidence from radiology reports and construct a shared diagnostic evidence space for evidence-aware cross-modal alignment, enabling learning from abundant unpaired images and reports.
AI.result: LGDEA yields consistent, significant improvements in phrase grounding, image-text retrieval, and zero-shot classification and rivals pretraining methods that rely on substantial paired data.
AI.conclusion: Evidence-level, LLM-guided alignment aligns with the diagnostic process, improving medical V-L pretraining while reducing reliance on paired data.

title: Deriving Neural Scaling Laws from the statistics of natural language
summary: Despite the fact that experimental neural scaling laws have substantially guided empirical progress in large-scale machine learning, no existing theory can quantitatively predict the exponents of these important laws for any modern LLM trained on any natural language dataset. We provide the first such theory in the case of data-limited scaling laws. We isolate two key statistical properties of language that alone can predict neural scaling exponents: (i) the decay of pairwise token correlations with time separation between token pairs, and (ii) the decay of the next-token conditional entropy with the length of the conditioning context. We further derive a simple formula in terms of these statistics that predicts data-limited neural scaling exponents from first principles without any free parameters or synthetic data models. Our theory exhibits a remarkable match with experimentally measured neural scaling laws obtained from training GPT-2 and LLaMA style models from scratch on two qualitatively different benchmarks, TinyStories and WikiText.

AI.tldr: First quantitative theory for data-limited neural scaling exponents in LLMs, derived from two language statistics; shows strong quantitative match with GPT-2 and LLaMA models on TinyStories and WikiText.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of a quantitative theory predicting neural scaling exponents in the data-limited regime for modern LLMs trained on natural language.
AI.method: Isolate two linguistic statistics: (i) decay of pairwise token correlations with time separation, and (ii) decay of next-token conditional entropy with conditioning context length; derive a closed-form formula predicting scaling exponents from these statistics without free parameters or synthetic data models.
AI.result: The derived formula matches experimentally measured neural scaling laws for GPT-2 and LLaMA-style models trained from scratch on TinyStories and WikiText across two benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Two simple, data-derived statistics suffice to predict data-limited neural scaling exponents from first principles, providing a quantitative theory for a major empirical phenomenon.

title: PTS-SNN: A Prompt-Tuned Temporal Shift Spiking Neural Networks for Efficient Speech Emotion Recognition
summary: Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) is widely deployed in Human-Computer Interaction, yet the high computational cost of conventional models hinders their implementation on resource-constrained edge devices. Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) offer an energy-efficient alternative due to their event-driven nature; however, their integration with continuous Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) representations is fundamentally challenged by distribution mismatch, where high-dynamic-range embeddings degrade the information coding capacity of threshold-based neurons. To resolve this, we propose Prompt-Tuned Spiking Neural Networks (PTS-SNN), a parameter-efficient neuromorphic adaptation framework that aligns frozen SSL backbones with spiking dynamics. Specifically, we introduce a Temporal Shift Spiking Encoder to capture local temporal dependencies via parameter-free channel shifts, establishing a stable feature basis. To bridge the domain gap, we devise a Context-Aware Membrane Potential Calibration strategy. This mechanism leverages a Spiking Sparse Linear Attention module to aggregate global semantic context into learnable soft prompts, which dynamically regulate the bias voltages of Parametric Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (PLIF) neurons. This regulation effectively centers the heterogeneous input distribution within the responsive firing range, mitigating functional silence or saturation. Extensive experiments on five multilingual datasets (e.g., IEMOCAP, CASIA, EMODB) demonstrate that PTS-SNN achieves 73.34\% accuracy on IEMOCAP, comparable to competitive Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), while requiring only 1.19M trainable parameters and 0.35 mJ inference energy per sample.

AI.tldr: PTS-SNN introduces a neuromorphic framework that aligns frozen SSL representations with spiking dynamics for energy-efficient speech emotion recognition. It uses a Temporal Shift Spiking Encoder for local temporal features and a Context-Aware Membrane Potential Calibration with a Spiking Sparse Linear Attention module to generate soft prompts that regulate PLIF neurons. This centers input distributions within firing ranges, mitigating saturation. It achieves 73.34% on IEMOCAP with 1.19M trainable params and 0.35 mJ per sample, comparable to ANNs, validated on five multilingual datasets (IEMOCAP, CASIA, EMODB, etc.).
AI.motivation: To enable accurate Speech Emotion Recognition on resource-constrained edge devices by reducing energy consumption while maintaining performance. The challenge is the distribution mismatch between high-dynamic-range SSL embeddings and the threshold-based spiking neurons, which hampers information coding capacity.
AI.method: Introduce Temporal Shift Spiking Encoder (parameter-free channel shifts) to capture local temporal dependencies. Propose Context-Aware Membrane Potential Calibration using a Spiking Sparse Linear Attention module to produce learnable soft prompts that dynamically modulate bias voltages of Parametric LIF neurons, aligning input distribution with firing range. This creates a stable feature basis and mitigates functional silence or saturation.
AI.result: Experimental results on five multilingual SER datasets show 73.34% accuracy on IEMOCAP, with 1.19M trainable parameters and 0.35 mJ per sample inference energy, achieving parity with competitive ANNs.
AI.conclusion: PTS-SNN provides an energy-efficient neuromorphic approach to SER by effectively aligning SSL backbones with spiking dynamics, achieving competitive accuracy with a compact model and low inference energy across multiple datasets.

title: MOSAIC: Bridging the Sim-to-Real Gap in Generalist Humanoid Motion Tracking and Teleoperation with Rapid Residual Adaptation
summary: Generalist humanoid motion trackers have recently achieved strong simulation metrics by scaling data and training, yet often remain brittle on hardware during sustained teleoperation due to interface- and dynamics-induced errors. We present MOSAIC, an open-source, full-stack system for humanoid motion tracking and whole-body teleoperation across multiple interfaces. MOSAIC first learns a teleoperation-oriented general motion tracker via RL on a multi-source motion bank with adaptive resampling and rewards that emphasize world-frame motion consistency, which is critical for mobile teleoperation. To bridge the sim-to-real interface gap without sacrificing generality, MOSAIC then performs rapid residual adaptation: an interface-specific policy is trained using minimal interface-specific data, and then distilled into the general tracker through an additive residual module, outperforming naive fine-tuning or continual learning. We validate MOSAIC with systematic ablations, out-of-distribution benchmarking, and real-robot experiments demonstrating robust offline motion replay and online long-horizon teleoperation under realistic latency and noise.

AI.tldr: MOSAIC is an open-source full-stack humanoid motion tracking and teleoperation system. It uses a teleoperation-oriented general motion tracker trained by reinforcement learning on a multi-source motion bank with adaptive resampling and world-frame motion-consistency rewards. To bridge sim-to-real gaps without losing generality, it applies rapid residual adaptation: an interface-specific policy trained with minimal data and distilled into the general tracker via an additive residual module. Evaluations include ablations, out-of-distribution benchmarking, and real-robot experiments showing robust offline replay and online long-horizon teleoperation under latency and noise.
AI.motivation: Generalist humanoid trackers perform well in simulation but remain brittle on hardware during sustained teleoperation due to interface and dynamics-induced errors. The work aims to achieve robust, cross-interface teleoperation by preserving generality while enabling rapid adaptation to different interfaces.
AI.method: 1) Train a teleoperation-oriented general motion tracker with reinforcement learning on a multi-source motion bank, employing adaptive resampling and rewards that emphasize world-frame motion consistency. 2) Perform rapid residual adaptation by training an interface-specific policy with minimal data. 3) Distill the interface-specific policy into the general tracker through an additive residual module, avoiding naive fine-tuning or continual learning. 4) Validate with systematic ablations, out-of-distribution benchmarking, and real-robot experiments.
AI.result: The approach yields robust offline motion replay and online long-horizon teleoperation under realistic latency and noise, outperforming naive fine-tuning or continual learning.
AI.conclusion: MOSAIC provides a scalable, generalizable, open-source solution for humanoid motion tracking and multi-interface teleoperation by decoupling a general motion tracker from interface-specific adjustments via additive residuals, enabling robust performance across hardware and interfaces.

title: MUFASA: A Multi-Layer Framework for Slot Attention
comment: Authors Sebastian Bock and Leonie Schüßler contributed equally. Project page: https://leonieschuessler.github.io/mufasa/
summary: Unsupervised object-centric learning (OCL) decomposes visual scenes into distinct entities. Slot attention is a popular approach that represents individual objects as latent vectors, called slots. Current methods obtain these slot representations solely from the last layer of a pre-trained vision transformer (ViT), ignoring valuable, semantically rich information encoded across the other layers. To better utilize this latent semantic information, we introduce MUFASA, a lightweight plug-and-play framework for slot attention-based approaches to unsupervised object segmentation. Our model computes slot attention across multiple feature layers of the ViT encoder, fully leveraging their semantic richness. We propose a fusion strategy to aggregate slots obtained on multiple layers into a unified object-centric representation. Integrating MUFASA into existing OCL methods improves their segmentation results across multiple datasets, setting a new state of the art while simultaneously improving training convergence with only minor inference overhead.

AI.tldr: MUFASA: a plug-and-play framework that fuses multi-layer ViT features for slot-attention-based unsupervised object-centric learning, yielding improved segmentation with state-of-the-art results and minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Current slot attention methods rely solely on the last ViT layer, potentially missing rich semantic cues encoded across earlier layers. Exploiting multi-layer representations can enhance object-centric slot representations and segmentation quality.
AI.method: Compute slot attention across multiple ViT encoder layers, then fuse the resulting slots with a proposed aggregation strategy into a single unified object-centric representation. The approach is lightweight and compatible with existing OCL methods, enabling easy plug-and-play integration.
AI.result: Integrating MUFASA improves segmentation performance across several datasets, achieving new state-of-the-art results while also accelerating or stabilizing training convergence and incurring only minor inference overhead.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging semantic information across ViT layers via multi-layer slot attention and a fusion mechanism yields superior, more robust object-centric representations for unsupervised segmentation with minimal additional cost.

title: Hyperparameter Transfer Laws for Non-Recurrent Multi-Path Neural Networks
summary: Deeper modern architectures are costly to train, making hyperparameter transfer preferable to expensive repeated tuning. Maximal Update Parametrization ($μ$P) helps explain why many hyperparameters transfer across width. Yet depth scaling is less understood for modern architectures, whose computation graphs contain multiple parallel paths and residual aggregation. To unify various non-recurrent multi-path neural networks such as CNNs, ResNets, and Transformers, we introduce a graph-based notion of effective depth. Under stabilizing initializations and a maximal-update criterion, we show that the optimal learning rate decays with effective depth following a universal -3/2 power law. Here, the maximal-update criterion maximizes the typical one-step representation change at initialization without causing instability, and effective depth is the minimal path length from input to output, counting layers and residual additions. Experiments across diverse architectures confirm the predicted slope and enable reliable zero-shot transfer of learning rates across depths and widths, turning depth scaling into a predictable hyperparameter-transfer problem.

AI.tldr: Introduces a graph-based notion of effective depth for multi-path networks and shows the optimal learning rate decays as depth^{-3/2} under stabilizing initialization and a maximal-update criterion, enabling zero-shot hyperparameter transfer across depths and widths.
AI.motivation: Hyperparameter tuning across depth for modern multi-path architectures (CNNs, ResNets, Transformers) is costly and poorly understood; a unified depth notion and transfer rule would simplify scaling.
AI.method: Define effective depth as the minimal path length from input to output, counting layers and residual additions in a graph of the network. Under stabilizing initialization and a maximal-update criterion (maximizing typical one-step representation change without instability), derive a universal depth-dependent learning-rate decay exponent (-3/2). Validate the theory across diverse architectures with empirical experiments.
AI.result: The predicted -3/2 slope for learning-rate scaling with effective depth is observed across CNNs, ResNets, and Transformers, enabling reliable zero-shot transfer of learning rates across depths and widths.
AI.conclusion: Depth scaling in modern multi-path networks can be understood via a graph-based effective depth, making hyperparameter transfer across depths predictable and extending μP principles to non-recurrent, multi-path architectures.

title: Do MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs
summary: While chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning has substantially improved multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on complex reasoning tasks, existing approaches largely rely on long textual reasoning trajectories and provide limited mechanisms for learning stable visual attention policies. Our analysis shows that current MLLMs exhibit weak visual focus: early-stage visual misalignment is rarely corrected during subsequent reasoning, leading to error propagation and failed inferences. We argue that this limitation stems from inadequate credit assignment for visual attention during training. To address this issue, we propose SAYO, a visual reasoning model trained with a reinforcement learning (RL) framework that introduces a region-level visual attention-based reward. This reward explicitly aligns optimization signals with visually grounded reasoning steps, enabling the model to learn more reliable attention behaviors. Extensive experiments across multiple multimodal benchmarks demonstrate that SAYO consistently improves performance on diverse reasoning and perception tasks.

AI.tldr: SAYO fixes weak visual focus in multimodal LLMs by using reinforcement learning with a region-level visual attention reward, enabling stable, visually grounded reasoning and consistent performance gains across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current MLLMs struggle with credit assignment for visual attention during training, leading to early misalignment and error propagation; improving attention policies is critical for reliable multimodal reasoning.
AI.method: A reinforcement learning framework that introduces a region-level visual attention-based reward, aligning optimization signals with visually grounded reasoning steps to train more reliable attention policies.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multimodal benchmarks show that SAYO consistently improves performance across diverse reasoning and perception tasks.
AI.conclusion: A region-level, RL-based attention reward effectively guides visual focus in MLLMs, yielding more robust, visually grounded reasoning and general performance gains across tasks.

title: A Precise Real-Time Force-Aware Grasping System for Robust Aerial Manipulation
summary: Aerial manipulation requires force-aware capabilities to enable safe and effective grasping and physical interaction. Previous works often rely on heavy, expensive force sensors unsuitable for typical quadrotor platforms, or perform grasping without force feedback, risking damage to fragile objects. To address these limitations, we propose a novel force-aware grasping framework incorporating six low-cost, sensitive skin-like tactile sensors. We introduce a magnetic-based tactile sensing module that provides high-precision three-dimensional force measurements. We eliminate geomagnetic interference through a reference Hall sensor and simplify the calibration process compared to previous work. The proposed framework enables precise force-aware grasping control, allowing safe manipulation of fragile objects and real-time weight measurement of grasped items. The system is validated through comprehensive real-world experiments, including balloon grasping, dynamic load variation tests, and ablation studies, demonstrating its effectiveness in various aerial manipulation scenarios. Our approach achieves fully onboard operation without external motion capture systems, significantly enhancing the practicality of force-sensitive aerial manipulation. The supplementary video is available at: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mbcZkrJEf1I.

AI.tldr: A force-aware grasping framework for aerial manipulation using six low-cost skin-like tactile sensors with a magnetic-based 3D force sensing module, reference Hall sensor to suppress interference, enabling onboard, fully self-contained operation and real-time weight measurement.
AI.motivation: To achieve safe and effective aerial grasping of fragile objects without heavy force sensors or lack of feedback, enabling real-time force sensing and weight estimation on lightweight quadrotor platforms.
AI.method: Develop six tactile sensors integrated into gripper, implement a magnetic-based tactile sensing module for 3D force measurement, use a reference Hall sensor to eliminate geomagnetic interference, simplify calibration, and enable fully onboard control without external motion capture.
AI.result: Accurate 3D force measurements, safe handling of fragile objects, real-time weight estimation of grasped items, validation through balloon grasping, dynamic load variation tests, and ablation studies; demonstrates practicality across aerial manipulation scenarios with onboard operation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed force-aware framework with low-cost tactile skins and magnetic sensing provides accurate force feedback for aerial manipulation, enhances safety and practicality by eliminating external tracking needs, and supports real-time manipulation and measurement of grasped payloads.

title: Revealing the Semantic Selection Gap in DINOv3 through Training-Free Few-Shot Segmentation
summary: Recent self-supervised Vision Transformers (ViTs), such as DINOv3, provide rich feature representations for dense vision tasks. This study investigates the intrinsic few-shot semantic segmentation (FSS) capabilities of frozen DINOv3 features through a training-free baseline, FSSDINO, utilizing class-specific prototypes and Gram-matrix refinement. Our results across binary, multi-class, and cross-domain (CDFSS) benchmarks demonstrate that this minimal approach, applied to the final backbone layer, is highly competitive with specialized methods involving complex decoders or test-time adaptation. Crucially, we conduct an Oracle-guided layer analysis, identifying a significant performance gap between the standard last-layer features and globally optimal intermediate representations. We reveal a "Safest vs. Optimal" dilemma: while the Oracle proves higher performance is attainable, matching the results of compute-intensive adaptation methods, current unsupervised and support-guided selection metrics consistently yield lower performance than the last-layer baseline. This characterizes a "Semantic Selection Gap" in Foundation Models, a disconnect where traditional heuristics fail to reliably identify high-fidelity features. Our work establishes the "Last-Layer" as a deceptively strong baseline and provides a rigorous diagnostic of the latent semantic potentials in DINOv3.The code is publicly available at https://github.com/hussni0997/fssdino.

AI.tldr: A training-free few-shot semantic segmentation baseline (FSSDINO) using frozen DINOv3 features with prototypes and Gram refinement; finds last-layer features are a strong baseline and reveals a Semantic Selection Gap between last-layer and optimal intermediate representations, with Oracle showing potential but current metrics underperform.
AI.motivation: Investigate intrinsic FSS capabilities of frozen ViT features and diagnose why unsupervised/support-based feature selection struggles in foundation models.
AI.method: FSSDINO: training-free baseline using final backbone layer with class-specific prototypes and Gram-matrix refinement; Oracle-guided layer analysis across layers; evaluation on binary, multi-class, and cross-domain FSS; code released.
AI.result: Competitive with decoder-based, test-time adaptation methods; significant gap between last-layer and globally optimal intermediate representations; Safest vs Optimal dilemma; conventional selection metrics underperform relative to last-layer baseline.
AI.conclusion: Last-layer representations are a robust, deceptively strong baseline for FSS in ViTs; analysis reveals a latent Semantic Selection Gap in Foundation Models; provides diagnostic insights and a publicly available implementation.

title: CoMI-IRL: Contrastive Multi-Intention Inverse Reinforcement Learning
summary: Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) seeks to infer reward functions from expert demonstrations. When demonstrations originate from multiple experts with different intentions, the problem is known as Multi-Intention IRL (MI-IRL). Recent deep generative MI-IRL approaches couple behavior clustering and reward learning, but typically require prior knowledge of the number of true behavioral modes $K^*$. This reliance on expert knowledge limits their adaptability to new behaviors, and only enables analysis related to the learned rewards, and not across the behavior modes used to train them. We propose Contrastive Multi-Intention IRL (CoMI-IRL), a transformer-based unsupervised framework that decouples behavior representation and clustering from downstream reward learning. Our experiments show that CoMI-IRL outperforms existing approaches without a priori knowledge of $K^*$ or labels, while allowing for visual interpretation of behavior relationships and adaptation to unseen behavior without full retraining.

AI.tldr: CoMI-IRL is a transformer-based unsupervised framework for Multi-Intention IRL that decouples behavior representation/clustering from reward learning, removing the need for prior knowledge of the number of behavioral modes and enabling interpretation and generalization to unseen behaviors.
AI.motivation: Previous MI-IRL methods rely on knowing the exact number of behavioral modes (K*), which limits adaptability and cross-mode analysis. There is a need for a label-free, interpretable approach that can learn rewards without fixed mode counts.
AI.method: A transformer-based, unsupervised framework (CoMI-IRL) that separates (i) behavior representation, (ii) clustering, and (iii) downstream reward learning. It uses contrastive objectives to learn meaningful behavior representations and to cluster without supervision, allowing reward learning to operate on these representations without being tied to a predefined K*.
AI.result: CoMI-IRL outperforms existing MI-IRL methods without requiring K* or labels. It enables visual interpretation of relationships between behaviors and adapts to unseen behaviors without full retraining.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling representation/clustering from reward learning in MI-IRL with a transformer and contrastive learning yields better performance, interpretability, and adaptability, addressing the main limitation of prior approaches that require a priori mode knowledge.

title: G-LNS: Generative Large Neighborhood Search for LLM-Based Automatic Heuristic Design
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently shown promise in Automated Heuristic Design (AHD), existing approaches typically formulate AHD around constructive priority rules or parameterized local search guidance, thereby restricting the search space to fixed heuristic forms.
