Ablations confirm that each component contributes to more interpretable and human-aligned visual reasoning.

AI.tldr: CoTZero is an annotation-free vision-language reasoning framework that combines dual-stage data synthesis with cognition-aligned training to enforce hierarchical, coherent reasoning, achieving strong semantic inconsistency detection and robust generalization.
AI.motivation: Current vision-language models rely on surface correlations rather than structured, verifiable reasoning, limiting compositionality and explainability.
AI.method: 1) Dual-stage data synthesis: bottom-up extraction of atomic visual primitives and their composition into diverse, structured questions and reasoning forms; top-down global structure guides interpretation of local details and causal relations. 2) Cognitively Coherent Verifiable Rewards (CCVR) in Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT) to provide stepwise feedback on reasoning coherence and factual correctness.
AI.result: Achieves 83.33% F1 on a multi-level semantic inconsistency benchmark with lexical-perturbation negatives, across in-domain and out-of-domain settings; ablations confirm the contributions of both components to improved interpretability and human-aligned visual reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Annotation-free CoTZero improves hierarchical, verifiable visual reasoning in VLMs, offering interpretable reasoning and strong generalization via cognitive-aligned training.

title: Reduced-order Control and Geometric Structure of Learned Lagrangian Latent Dynamics
summary: Model-based controllers can offer strong guarantees on stability and convergence by relying on physically accurate dynamic models. However, these are rarely available for high-dimensional mechanical systems such as deformable objects or soft robots. While neural architectures can learn to approximate complex dynamics, they are either limited to low-dimensional systems or provide only limited formal control guarantees due to a lack of embedded physical structure. This paper introduces a latent control framework based on learned structure-preserving reduced-order dynamics for high-dimensional Lagrangian systems. We derive a reduced tracking law for fully actuated systems and adopt a Riemannian perspective on projection-based model-order reduction to study the resulting latent and projected closed-loop dynamics. By quantifying the sources of modeling error, we derive interpretable conditions for stability and convergence. We extend the proposed controller and analysis to underactuated systems by introducing learned actuation patterns. Experimental results on simulated and real-world systems validate our theoretical investigation and the accuracy of our controllers.

AI.tldr: A latent, structure-preserving reduced-order control framework for high-dimensional Lagrangian systems enables stable tracking with both fully actuated and underactuated cases, validated in simulation and real experiments.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional mechanical systems (e.g., deformable objects, soft robots) lack accurate full-order models. Purely data-driven approaches offer limited guarantees; physics-informed, structure-preserving reduced-order models can provide stability and convergence guarantees while remaining expressive.
AI.method: Develop a latent control framework using learned, structure-preserving reduced-order dynamics. Derive a reduced tracking law for fully actuated systems. Apply a Riemannian perspective on projection-based model-order reduction to analyze latent and projected closed-loop dynamics. Quantify sources of modeling error and derive interpretable stability/convergence conditions. Extend to underactuated systems by learning actuation patterns.
AI.result: The approach yields theoretical stability and convergence conditions and demonstrates improved stability/accuracy in the closed-loop, validated by experiments on simulated and real-world systems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework marries learned dynamics with structure-preserving reduced-order models to provide interpretable stability guarantees for high-dimensional Lagrangian systems, effective for both fully actuated and underactuated cases, with empirical validation.

title: Automated rock joint trace mapping using a supervised learning model trained on synthetic data generated by parametric modelling
summary: This paper presents a geology-driven machine learning method for automated rock joint trace mapping from images. The approach combines geological modelling, synthetic data generation, and supervised image segmentation to address limited real data and class imbalance. First, discrete fracture network models are used to generate synthetic jointed rock images at field-relevant scales via parametric modelling, preserving joint persistence, connectivity, and node-type distributions. Second, segmentation models are trained using mixed training and pretraining followed by fine-tuning on real images. The method is tested in box and slope domains using several real datasets. The results show that synthetic data can support supervised joint trace detection when real data are scarce. Mixed training performs well when real labels are consistent (e.g. box-domain), while fine-tuning is more robust when labels are noisy (e.g. slope-domain where labels can be biased, incomplete, and inconsistent). Fully zero-shot prediction from synthetic model remains limited, but useful generalisation is achieved by fine-tuning with a small number of real data. Qualitative analysis shows clearer and more geologically meaningful joint traces than indicated by quantitative metrics alone. The proposed method supports reliable joint mapping and provides a basis for further work on domain adaptation and evaluation.

AI.tldr: Geology-driven ML with synthetic data for automated rock joint trace mapping; addresses data scarcity and class imbalance via synthetic data and mixed/fine-tuning strategies.
AI.motivation: To enable accurate joint trace segmentation when real labeled data are scarce and biased, by integrating geological models and synthetic data generation to augment training.
AI.method: Use discrete fracture network models to generate synthetic jointed rock images, preserving fracture statistics; train segmentation models with mixed training, pretraining, and fine-tuning on real images; validate in box and slope domains; analyze performance and qualitative outputs.
AI.result: Synthetic data can support joint trace detection under data scarcity; mixed training effective with consistent labels; fine-tuning robust to label noise; zero-shot from synthetic models limited; small real data fine-tuning yields generalization; qualitative results show clearer, geologically meaningful traces; method enables domain adaptation.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides reliable joint mapping and a platform for further domain adaptation and evaluation in geological image segmentation.

title: Beyond Arrow: From Impossibility to Possibilities in Multi-Criteria Benchmarking
summary: Modern benchmarks such as HELM MMLU account for multiple metrics like accuracy, robustness and efficiency. When trying to turn these metrics into a single ranking, natural aggregation procedures can become incoherent or unstable to changes in the model set. We formalize this aggregation as a social choice problem where each metric induces a preference ranking over models on each dataset, and a benchmark operator aggregates these votes across metrics. While prior work has focused on Arrow's impossibility result, we argue that the impossibility often originates from pathological examples and identify sufficient conditions under which these disappear, and meaningful multi-criteria benchmarking becomes possible. In particular, we deal with three restrictions on the combinations of rankings and prove that on single-peaked, group-separable and distance-restricted preferences, the benchmark operator allows for the construction of well-behaved rankings of the involved models. Empirically, we investigate several modern benchmark suites like HELM MMLU and verify which structural conditions are fulfilled on which benchmark problems.

AI.tldr: Proposes a social-choice-based framework for multi-criteria benchmarking, identifying conditions under which coherent rankings can be obtained and validating them on HELM MMLU.
AI.motivation: Benchmarking models on multiple metrics often yields incoherent or unstable aggregate rankings. A formal aggregation framework is needed to produce stable, interpretable model rankings across datasets.
AI.method: Model each metric as a voter that induces a ranking over models on each dataset. Treat the benchmark operator as a social-choice rule aggregating these votes. Identify sufficient conditions—single-peaked, group-separable, and distance-restricted preferences—under which the aggregation yields well-behaved model rankings. Prove results under these constraints and empirically assess HELM MMLU to check which conditions hold.
AI.result: Under the three structural restrictions, the benchmark operator can produce coherent, well-behaved rankings of models. Empirical analysis on HELM MMLU shows varying adherence to these conditions across benchmark problems, supporting the feasibility of stable multi-criteria benchmarking in some settings and highlighting when it may fail.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides principled conditions for stable multi-criteria benchmarking and guides benchmark design. It suggests that choosing problems that satisfy the stated preference structures enables meaningful aggregation; future work could relax assumptions and broaden applicability, and offer practical guidelines for practitioners.

title: Effect-Level Validation for Causal Discovery
summary: Causal discovery is increasingly applied to large-scale telemetry data to estimate the effects of user-facing interventions, yet its reliability for decision-making in feedback-driven systems with strong self-selection remains unclear. In this paper, we propose an effect-centric, admissibility-first framework that treats discovered graphs as structural hypotheses and evaluates them by identifiability, stability, and falsification rather than by graph recovery accuracy alone. Empirically, we study the effect of early exposure to competitive gameplay on short-term retention using real-world game telemetry. We find that many statistically plausible discovery outputs do not admit point-identified causal queries once minimal temporal and semantic constraints are enforced, highlighting identifiability as a critical bottleneck for decision support. When identification is possible, several algorithm families converge to similar, decision-consistent effect estimates despite producing substantially different graph structures, including cases where the direct treatment-outcome edge is absent and the effect is preserved through indirect causal pathways. These converging estimates survive placebo, subsampling, and sensitivity refutation. In contrast, other methods exhibit sporadic admissibility and threshold-sensitive or attenuated effects due to endpoint ambiguity. These results suggest that graph-level metrics alone are inadequate proxies for causal reliability for a given target query. Therefore, trustworthy causal conclusions in telemetry-driven systems require prioritizing admissibility and effect-level validation over causal structural recovery alone.

AI.tldr: Effect-centric, admissibility-first causal discovery; identifiability bottleneck; convergence of effect estimates across diverse graphs; graph metrics insufficient for decision support; emphasis on effect-level validation.
AI.motivation: Reliability of causal discovery for decision-making in feedback-driven systems with self-selection; need to assess identifiability and robustness of causal queries rather than graph-recovery accuracy.
AI.method: Propose an admissibility-first framework: treat discovered graphs as structural hypotheses and evaluate them via identifiability, stability, and falsification. Empirically study early exposure to competitive gameplay on short-term retention using real-world game telemetry. Compare multiple algorithm families and validate via placebo, subsampling, and sensitivity refutation.
AI.result: Many statistically plausible discovery outputs do not admit point-identified causal queries once minimal temporal and semantic constraints are enforced. When identification is possible, several algorithm families converge to similar, decision-consistent effect estimates despite producing substantially different graph structures, including indirect pathways. These estimates survive placebo, subsampling, and sensitivity refutation. Other methods show sporadic admissibility and threshold-sensitive or attenuated effects due to endpoint ambiguity.
AI.conclusion: Graph-level metrics alone are inadequate proxies for causal reliability for a given target query. Trustworthy causal conclusions in telemetry-driven systems require prioritizing admissibility and effect-level validation over causal structural recovery.

title: CLUE: Crossmodal disambiguation via Language-vision Understanding with attEntion
summary: With the increasing integration of robots into daily life, human-robot interaction has become more complex and multifaceted. A critical component of this interaction is Interactive Visual Grounding (IVG), through which robots must interpret human intentions and resolve ambiguity. Existing IVG models generally lack a mechanism to determine when to ask clarification questions, as they implicitly rely on their learned representations. CLUE addresses this gap by converting the VLM's cross-modal attention into an explicit, spatially grounded signal for deciding when to ask. We extract text to image attention maps and pass them to a lightweight CNN to detect referential ambiguity, while a LoRA fine-tuned decoder conducts the dialog and emits grounding location tokens. We train on a real-world interactive dataset for IVG, and a mixed ambiguity set for the detector. With InViG-only supervision, our model surpasses a state-of-the-art method while using parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Similarly, the ambiguity detector outperforms prior baselines. Overall, CLUE turns the internal cross-modal attention of a VLM into an explicit, spatially grounded signal for deciding when to ask. The data and code are publicly available at: mouadabrini.github.io/clue

AI.tldr: CLUE makes cross-modal attention explicit for interactive visual grounding by turning VLM attention into a spatial signal to decide when to ask clarifications; it uses a CNN on text-to-image attention maps for ambiguity detection and a LoRA-tuned decoder for dialog and grounding tokens, achieving SOTA with efficient fine-tuning on real IVG data; code/data released.
AI.motivation: In IVG, robots must interpret human intent and handle ambiguity. Existing models rely on learned representations and lack a mechanism to decide when to ask clarifying questions. An explicit, spatially grounded signal indicating REFERENTIAL ambiguity can improve when to query the user.
AI.method: Extract text-to-image attention maps from a vision-language model and feed them into a lightweight CNN to detect referential ambiguity. A LoRA-fine-tuned decoder handles dialog and emits grounding location tokens. Train on a real-world IVG dataset and a mixed-ambiguity detector dataset for supervision; use InViG-only supervision for grounding and a parameter-efficient fine-tuning regime.
AI.result: The model surpasses a state-of-the-art method in IVG with parameter-efficient fine-tuning. The ambiguity detector also outperforms prior baselines. CLUE converts cross-modal attention into a spatially grounded signal that dictates when to ask for clarification.
AI.conclusion: CLUE provides an explicit, spatially grounded mechanism to decide when to ask clarifying questions in IVG by leveraging cross-modal attention, achieving improved performance with efficient training and making the data/code publicly available.

title: TeleBoost: A Systematic Alignment Framework for High-Fidelity, Controllable, and Robust Video Generation
summary: Post-training is the decisive step for converting a pretrained video generator into a production-oriented model that is instruction-following, controllable, and robust over long temporal horizons. This report presents a systematical post-training framework that organizes supervised policy shaping, reward-driven reinforcement learning, and preference-based refinement into a single stability-constrained optimization stack. The framework is designed around practical video-generation constraints, including high rollout cost, temporally compounding failure modes, and feedback that is heterogeneous, uncertain, and often weakly discriminative. By treating optimization as a staged, diagnostic-driven process rather than a collection of isolated tricks, the report summarizes a cohesive recipe for improving perceptual fidelity, temporal coherence, and prompt adherence while preserving the controllability established at initialization. The resulting framework provides a clear blueprint for building scalable post-training pipelines that remain stable, extensible, and effective in real-world deployment settings.

AI.tldr: Introduces a systematic post-training framework for pretrained video generators that unifies supervised policy shaping, RL with rewards, and preference-based refinement into a stability-constrained optimization stack, aiming to improve fidelity, coherence, and instruction adherence while preserving initialization-time controllability.
AI.motivation: To transform pretrained video generators into production-ready, instruction-following, controllable models that remain robust over long temporal horizons. Addresses challenges such as high rollout cost, temporally compounding failure modes, and feedback that is heterogeneous, uncertain, and often weakly discriminative. Seeks a cohesive optimization recipe rather than ad hoc tricks.
AI.method: A staged optimization stack that integrates supervised policy shaping, reward-driven reinforcement learning, and preference-based refinement, governed by stability constraints and a diagnostic-driven process. Emphasizes a pipeline that accounts for practical video-generation constraints and heterogeneous feedback, aiming for a scalable, extensible post-training workflow.
AI.result: Provides a cohesive blueprint and recipe for building scalable post-training pipelines that remain stable, extensible, and effective in real-world deployment. Claims improvements in perceptual fidelity, temporal coherence, and prompt adherence while preserving the controllability established at initialization (no isolated tricks).
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a unified, repeatable approach to post-training for production-ready video generators, enabling stable, extensible, and robust pipelines through staged, diagnostics-driven optimization.

title: Astro: Activation-guided Structured Regularization for Outlier-Robust LLM Post-Training Quantization
summary: Weight-only post-training quantization (PTQ) is crucial for efficient Large Language Model (LLM) deployment but suffers from accuracy degradation caused by weight and activation outliers. Existing mitigation strategies often face critical limitations: they either yield insufficient outlier suppression or incur significant deployment inefficiencies, such as inference latency, heavy preprocessing, or reliance on complex operator fusion. To resolve these limitations, we leverage a key insight: over-parameterized LLMs often converge to Flat Minima, implying a vast equivalent solution space where weights can be adjusted without compromising accuracy. Building on this, we propose Astro, an Activation-guided Structured Regularization framework designed to suppress the negative effects of outliers in a hardware-friendly and efficient manner. Leveraging the activation-guided regularization objective, Astro actively reconstructs intrinsically robust weights, aggressively suppressing weight outliers corresponding to high-magnitude activations without sacrificing model accuracy. Crucially, Astro introduces zero inference latency and is orthogonal to mainstream quantization methods like GPTQ. Extensive experiments show that Astro achieves highly competitive performance; notably, on LLaMA-2-7B, it achieves better performance than complex learning-based rotation methods with almost 1/3 of the quantization time.

AI.tldr: Activation-guided structured regularization (Astro) suppresses weight outliers in weight-only PTQ for LLMs by leveraging flat minima, achieving zero inference latency and competitive accuracy with ~1/3 quantization time on LLaMA-2-7B.
AI.motivation: Weight and activation outliers degrade accuracy in weight-only post-training quantization (PTQ) for large language models. Existing mitigation strategies either underperform in outlier suppression or induce significant deployment overhead (latency, preprocessing, operator fusion).
AI.method: Introduce Astro, an activation-guided structured regularization framework. It actively reconstructs intrinsically robust weights by aggressively suppressing weight outliers associated with high-magnitude activations, guided by an activation-aware regularization objective. Astro is hardware-friendly, yields zero inference latency, and is orthogonal to mainstream PTQ methods such as GPTQ.
AI.result: Astro achieves competitive performance, outperforming complex learning-based rotation methods on LLaMA-2-7B while requiring about one-third of the quantization time.
AI.conclusion: Astro provides a practical, efficient approach to weight-only PTQ for LLMs by exploiting flat minima and activation-guided regularization, delivering zero latency and competitive accuracy without sacrificing deployment efficiency.

title: OPE: Overcoming Information Saturation in Parallel Thinking via Outline-Guided Path Exploration
summary: Parallel thinking has emerged as a new paradigm for large reasoning models (LRMs) in tackling complex problems. Recent methods leverage Reinforcement Learning (RL) to enhance parallel thinking, aiming to address the limitations in computational resources and effectiveness encountered with supervised fine-tuning. However, most existing studies primarily focus on optimizing the aggregation phase, with limited attention to the path exploration stage. In this paper, we theoretically analyze the optimization of parallel thinking under the Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) setting, and identify that the mutual information bottleneck among exploration paths fundamentally restricts overall performance. To address this, we propose Outline-Guided Path Exploration (OPE), which explicitly partitions the solution space by generating diverse reasoning outlines prior to parallel path reasoning, thereby reducing information redundancy and improving the diversity of information captured across exploration paths. We implement OPE with an iterative RL strategy that optimizes outline planning and outline-guided reasoning independently. Extensive experiments across multiple challenging mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that OPE effectively improves reasoning performance in different aggregation strategies, enabling LRMs to more reliably discover correct solutions.

AI.tldr: RLVR-based Outline-Guided Path Exploration (OPE) partitions the solution space with diverse outlines to reduce redundancy in parallel reasoning paths, improving mathematical reasoning performance across aggregation strategies.
AI.motivation: Current RL-based parallel thinking emphasizes aggregation; a mutual information bottleneck among exploration paths limits performance. Addressing path exploration with diverse outlines can enhance information diversity and reliability.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis identifies the mutual information bottleneck in RLVR. Proposes OPE that generates diverse outlines before path reasoning to partition the solution space. Implements OPE via an iterative RL loop that independently optimizes outline planning and outline-guided reasoning.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on challenging mathematical benchmarks shows OPE improves reasoning performance across various aggregation strategies, enabling LRMs to more reliably discover correct solutions.
AI.conclusion: Outline-guided exploration effectively reduces information redundancy in exploration paths, improving robustness and reliability of LRMs in parallel thinking under the RLVR framework; compatible with multiple aggregation strategies.

title: From Obstacles to Etiquette: Robot Social Navigation with VLM-Informed Path Selection
comment: Accepted to IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L)
summary: Navigating socially in human environments requires more than satisfying geometric constraints, as collision-free paths may still interfere with ongoing activities or conflict with social norms. Addressing this challenge calls for analyzing interactions between agents and incorporating common-sense reasoning into planning. This paper presents a social robot navigation framework that integrates geometric planning with contextual social reasoning. The system first extracts obstacles and human dynamics to generate geometrically feasible candidate paths, then leverages a fine-tuned vision-language model (VLM) to evaluate these paths, informed by contextually grounded social expectations, selecting a socially optimized path for the controller. This task-specific VLM distills social reasoning from large foundation models into a smaller and efficient model, allowing the framework to perform real-time adaptation in diverse human-robot interaction contexts. Experiments in four social navigation contexts demonstrate that our method achieves the best overall performance with the lowest personal space violation duration, the minimal pedestrian-facing time, and no social zone intrusions. Project page: https://path-etiquette.github.io

AI.tldr: A framework that fuses geometric path planning with context-aware social reasoning for robot navigation, using a task-tuned vision-language model to select socially compliant paths.
AI.motivation: To enable robots to navigate human environments not only collision-free but also compliant with social norms and ongoing activities.
AI.method: 1) extract obstacles and human dynamics to generate geometrically feasible candidate paths; 2) evaluate these paths with a fine-tuned vision-language model informed by contextual social expectations; 3) select a socially optimized path for real-time control.
AI.result: In four social navigation contexts, the method achieves best overall performance, with the lowest personal-space violation duration, minimal pedestrian-facing time, and no social-zone intrusions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating geometric planning with context-driven social reasoning via a compact VLM enables real-time, socially aware robot navigation.

title: Fine-R1: Make Multi-modal LLMs Excel in Fine-Grained Visual Recognition by Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026. The models are available at https://huggingface.co/collections/StevenHH2000/fine-r1
summary: Any entity in the visual world can be hierarchically grouped based on shared characteristics and mapped to fine-grained sub-categories. While Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) achieve strong performance on coarse-grained visual tasks, they often struggle with Fine-Grained Visual Recognition (FGVR). Adapting general-purpose MLLMs to FGVR typically requires large amounts of annotated data, which is costly to obtain, leaving a substantial performance gap compared to contrastive CLIP models dedicated for discriminative tasks. Moreover, MLLMs tend to overfit to seen sub-categories and generalize poorly to unseen ones. To address these challenges, we propose Fine-R1, an MLLM tailored for FGVR through an R1-style training framework: (1) Chain-of-Thought Supervised Fine-tuning, where we construct a high-quality FGVR CoT dataset with rationales of "visual analysis, candidate sub-categories, comparison, and prediction", transition the model into a strong open-world classifier; and (2) Triplet Augmented Policy Optimization, where Intra-class Augmentation mixes trajectories from anchor and positive images within the same category to improve robustness to intra-class variance, while Inter-class Augmentation maximizes the response distinction conditioned on images across sub-categories to enhance discriminative ability. With only 4-shot training, Fine-R1 outperforms existing general MLLMs, reasoning MLLMs, and even contrastive CLIP models in identifying both seen and unseen sub-categories, showing promise in working in knowledge-intensive domains where gathering expert annotations for all sub-categories is arduous. Code is available at https://github.com/PKU-ICST-MIPL/FineR1_ICLR2026.

AI.tldr: Fine-R1 tailors multimodal LLMs for fine-grained visual recognition via a two-stage training framework: Chain-of-Thought supervised fine-tuning with a CoT rationale dataset, and Triplet Augmented Policy Optimization to boost intra-class robustness and inter-class discrimination, achieving strong 4-shot performance on seen and unseen sub-categories, outperforming general MLLMs and CLIP.
AI.motivation: FGVR tasks suffer from data scarcity and poor generalization when adapting general-purpose MLLMs. The aim is to close the FGVR gap by injecting reasoning structure and robust, triplet-based augmentation, enabling open-world classification with limited annotations.
AI.method: (1) Chain-of-Thought Supervised Fine-tuning: construct a high-quality FGVR CoT dataset with rationales for visual analysis, candidate sub-categories, comparison, and prediction to train an open-world classifier. (2) Triplet Augmented Policy Optimization: intra-class augmentation mixes trajectories from anchor and positive images within the same class to reduce intra-class variance; inter-class augmentation maximizes response distinction across sub-categories conditioned on images to enhance discriminability.
AI.result: With only 4-shot training, Fine-R1 outperforms existing general MLLMs, reasoning MLLMs, and even CLIP models in identifying both seen and unseen sub-categories.
AI.conclusion: Fine-R1 shows that incorporating CoT reasoning and triplet-based policy optimization into FGVR-oriented MLLMs can substantially improve low-shot, open-world discrimination across fine-grained sub-categories, with potential applicability to knowledge-intensive domains where expert annotations are scarce.

title: Rational Transductors
summary:
Standard Transformers excel at semantic modeling but struggle with
  rigid sequential logic and state tracking. Theoretical work
  establishes that self-attention is limited to $\AC^0$ (under hard
  attention) or $\TC^0$ (under soft attention), complexity classes
  that often fail to support robust length generalization on
  sequential problems without intermediate chain-of-thought. In this
  work, we introduce \emph{Rational Transductors}, a dual-stream
  architecture that augments the Transformer with a matrix-valued
  recurrence derived from Weighted Finite Automata (WFA). By
  injecting rational state information into the attention mechanism
  via a \emph{Deep Rational Injection} scheme, our framework strictly
  generalizes the expressive power of Transformers to capture all
  Regular Languages, $\NC^1$-complete problems (such as Boolean
  Formula Evaluation), and fundamental separations like Parity and
  Modular Counting, while preserving $O(L + \log T)$ parallel time
  complexity. We ground the architecture in a rigorous learning
  theory: we prove that \emph{Random Rational Features} act as a
  universal basis for sequential dependencies, justifying our
  initialization strategy, while establishing that the
  \emph{Differentiable Rational Feature} regime is necessary to close
  the representational compactness gap. Theoretical analysis and
  empirical results demonstrate that Rational Transductors solve the
  "Regular Gap," enabling robust length generalization on algorithmic
  tasks where standard Transformers fail, without the sequential
  computational bottlenecks of traditional RNNs.

AI.tldr: Rational Transductors extend Transformers with a matrix-valued recurrence from Weighted Finite Automata and a Deep Rational Injection into attention, achieving regular-language expressivity and NC1-complete capabilities while preserving parallel efficiency.
AI.motivation: Standard Transformers struggle with rigid sequential logic and state tracking; theoretical bounds place self-attention in AC0/TC0 under hard/soft attention, limiting length generalization. The work aims to bridge neural sequence modeling with automata-theoretic expressivity, enabling robust length generalization and core algorithmic separations.
AI.method: Introduce a dual-stream Transformer augmented by a matrix-valued recurrence derived from Weighted Finite Automata (WFA). Inject rational state into attention via Deep Rational Injection. Use Random Rational Features as a universal basis for sequential dependencies; identify Differentiable Rational Feature regime to close the representational gap. The design preserves O(L + log T) parallel time.
AI.result: Theory shows the framework can represent Regular Languages, NC1-complete problems (e.g., Boolean formula evaluation), and separations like Parity and Modular Counting. Empirical results demonstrate solving the Regular Gap and robust length generalization on algorithmic tasks where standard Transformers fail, without the sequential bottlenecks of RNNs.
AI.conclusion: Rational Transductors extend Transformer expressivity beyond standard self-attention to regular languages and core computational tasks, while maintaining parallel efficiency. They provide a principled learning-theory foundation (universal basis via Random Rational Features and necessity of differentiable features) and show practical gains on algorithmic sequence tasks.

title: Towards Better Evolution Modeling for Temporal Knowledge Graphs
summary: Temporal knowledge graphs (TKGs) structurally preserve evolving human knowledge. Recent research has focused on designing models to learn the evolutionary nature of TKGs to predict future facts, achieving impressive results. For instance, Hits@10 scores over 0.9 on YAGO dataset. However, we find that existing benchmarks inadvertently introduce a shortcut. Near state-of-the-art performance can be simply achieved by counting co-occurrences, without using any temporal information. In this work, we examine the root cause of this issue, identifying inherent biases in current datasets and over simplified form of evaluation task that can be exploited by these biases. Through this analysis, we further uncover additional limitations of existing benchmarks, including unreasonable formatting of time-interval knowledge, ignorance of learning knowledge obsolescence, and insufficient information for precise evolution understanding, all of which can amplify the shortcut and hinder a fair assessment. Therefore, we introduce the TKG evolution benchmark. It includes four bias-corrected datasets and two novel tasks closely aligned with the evolution process, promoting a more accurate understanding of the challenges in TKG evolution modeling. Benchmark is available at: https://github.com/zjs123/TKG-Benchmark.

AI.tldr: Bias-aware TKG evolution benchmark to fix shortcuts in evaluation; introduces four bias-corrected datasets and two evolution-aligned tasks; code at GitHub.
AI.motivation: Existing TKG benchmarks allow high performance by exploiting dataset biases and simplistic evaluation, obscuring true temporal understanding.
AI.method: Analyze root causes of biases (dataset biases, time-interval formatting, obsolescence, and insufficient evolution information), design four bias-corrected datasets, and define two tasks that require true temporal evolution understanding; release as a benchmark.
AI.result: Four bias-corrected datasets and two novel tasks are provided, with public availability (GitHub). These facilitate fairer evaluation of TKG evolution models beyond co-occurrence shortcuts.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark addresses identified biases, enabling more reliable assessment of TKG evolution models and guiding future research toward authentic temporal reasoning.

title: Dexterous Manipulation Policies from RGB Human Videos via 4D Hand-Object Trajectory Reconstruction
summary: Multi-finger robotic hand manipulation and grasping are challenging due to the high-dimensional action space and the difficulty of acquiring large-scale training data. Existing approaches largely rely on human teleoperation with wearable devices or specialized sensing equipment to capture hand-object interactions, which limits scalability. In this work, we propose VIDEOMANIP, a device-free framework that learns dexterous manipulation directly from RGB human videos. Leveraging recent advances in computer vision, VIDEOMANIP reconstructs explicit 4D robot-object trajectories from monocular videos by estimating human hand poses, object meshes, and retargets the reconstructed human motions to robotic hands for manipulation learning. To make the reconstructed robot data suitable for dexterous manipulation training, we introduce hand-object contact optimization with interaction-centric grasp modeling, as well as a demonstration synthesis strategy that generates diverse training trajectories from a single video, enabling generalizable policy learning without additional robot demonstrations. In simulation, the learned grasping model achieves a 70.25% success rate across 20 diverse objects using the Inspire Hand. In the real world, manipulation policies trained from RGB videos achieve an average 62.86% success rate across seven tasks using the LEAP Hand, outperforming retargeting-based methods by 15.87%. Project videos are available at videomanip.github.io.

AI.tldr: Device-free dexterous manipulation from RGB videos via 4D robot-object trajectory reconstruction, contact-optimized grasping, and demo-synthesis for policy learning; achieves notable simulation and real-world success on Inspire and LEAP Hands.
AI.motivation: Overcoming the high-dimensional action space and data bottlenecks of multi-finger manipulation by avoiding wearable sensing and large-scale robot demonstrations; leveraging monocular videos to bootstrap dexterous control.
AI.method: Estimate hand poses, reconstruct object meshes, and retarget movements from monocular RGB videos to robotic hands. Introduce hand–object contact optimization with interaction-centric grasp modeling and synthesize diverse training trajectories from a single video to enable generalizable learning without extra robot demonstrations.
AI.result: In simulation, 70.25% success across 20 objects with the Inspire Hand. In real world, average 62.86% success across seven tasks with the LEAP Hand, outperforming retargeting-based methods by 15.87%.
AI.conclusion: RGB-video–driven, device-free dexterous manipulation learning is viable across multiple hand platforms, with strong gains over retargeting baselines; demonstrates scalable data-efficient policy learning from a single video.

title: HistoMet: A Pan-Cancer Deep Learning Framework for Prognostic Prediction of Metastatic Progression and Site Tropism from Primary Tumor Histopathology
summary: Metastatic Progression remains the leading cause of cancer-related mortality, yet predicting whether a primary tumor will metastasize and where it will disseminate directly from histopathology remains a fundamental challenge. Although whole-slide images (WSIs) provide rich morphological information, prior computational pathology approaches typically address metastatic status or site prediction as isolated tasks, and do not explicitly model the clinically sequential decision process of metastatic risk assessment followed by downstream site-specific evaluation. To address this research gap, we present a decision-aware, concept-aligned MIL framework, HistoMet, for prognostic metastatic outcome prediction from primary tumor WSIs. Our proposed framework adopts a two-module prediction pipeline in which the likelihood of metastatic progression from the primary tumor is first estimated, followed by conditional prediction of metastatic site for high-risk cases. To guide representation learning and improve clinical interpretability, our framework integrates linguistically defined and data-adaptive metastatic concepts through a pretrained pathology vision-language model. We evaluate HistoMet on a multi-institutional pan-cancer cohort of 6504 patients with metastasis follow-up and site annotations. Under clinically relevant high-sensitivity screening settings (95 percent sensitivity), HistoMet significantly reduces downstream workload while maintaining high metastatic risk recall. Conditional on metastatic cases, HistoMet achieves a macro F1 of 74.6 with a standard deviation of 1.3 and a macro one-vs-rest AUC of 92.1. These results demonstrate that explicitly modeling clinical decision structure enables robust and deployable prognostic prediction of metastatic progression and site tropism directly from primary tumor histopathology.

AI.tldr: Two-stage MIL framework (HistoMet) for metastatic progression and site prediction from primary tumor WSIs; uses a vision-language-guided, concept-aligned approach; evaluated on 6,504-patient, multi-institution pan-cancer cohort; at 95% sensitivity, achieves high recall with macro F1 74.6 and macro OvA AUC 92.1.
AI.motivation: Metastasis prediction from histopathology remains challenging, and existing methods treat metastasis risk and site prediction as separate tasks. There is a need to model the clinical decision pipeline (risk assessment followed by site-specific evaluation) and improve interpretability.
AI.method: Proposes a two-module prediction pipeline: (1) estimate metastatic progression likelihood from primary tumor; (2) conditionally predict metastatic site for high-risk cases. Integrates linguistically defined and data-adaptive metastatic concepts via a pretrained pathology vision-language model. Uses multiple-instance learning (MIL) on WSIs; pan-cancer, multi-institution dataset.
AI.result: Under a high-sensitivity setting (95%), HistoMet reduces downstream workload while maintaining high metastatic risk recall. Among metastatic cases, macro F1 = 74.6 (std 1.3) and macro one-vs-rest AUC = 92.1.
AI.conclusion: Modeling explicit clinical decision structure with concept-aligned representations enables robust, deployable prognostic prediction of metastatic progression and site tropism directly from primary tumor histopathology.

title: Object-Oriented Transition Modeling with Inductive Logic Programming
summary: Building models of the world from observation, i.e., induction, is one of the major challenges in machine learning. In order to be useful, models need to maintain accuracy when used in novel situations, i.e., generalize. In addition, they should be easy to interpret and efficient to train. Prior work has investigated these concepts in the context of object-oriented representations inspired by human cognition. In this paper, we develop a novel learning algorithm that is substantially more powerful than these previous methods. Our thorough experiments, including ablation tests and comparison with neural baselines, demonstrate a significant improvement over the state-of-the-art.

AI.tldr: A novel, more powerful learning algorithm for world-model induction that outperforms prior object-oriented approaches and neural baselines, with strong generalization and interpretability claims supported by thorough experiments.
AI.motivation: To advance world-model induction by addressing generalization, interpretability, and training efficiency beyond object-oriented representations inspired by human cognition.
AI.method: Proposes a new learning algorithm that surpasses previous methods. Validates via extensive experiments including ablation studies and comparisons against neural baselines.
AI.result: The algorithm shows a significant improvement over the state-of-the-art across the evaluated metrics and baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method substantially improves over prior object-oriented methods in accuracy/generalization and offers interpretability and training efficiency advantages, as supported by comprehensive experiments.

title: Does Your Reasoning Model Implicitly Know When to Stop Thinking?
summary: Recent advancements in large reasoning models (LRMs) have greatly improved their capabilities on complex reasoning tasks through Long Chains of Thought (CoTs). However, this approach often results in substantial redundancy, impairing computational efficiency and causing significant delays in real-time applications. Recent studies show that longer reasoning chains are frequently uncorrelated with correctness and can even be detrimental to accuracy. In a further in-depth analysis of this phenomenon, we surprisingly uncover and empirically verify that LRMs implicitly know the appropriate time to stop thinking, while this capability is obscured by current sampling paradigms. Motivated by this, we introduce SAGE (Self-Aware Guided Efficient Reasoning), a novel sampling paradigm that unleashes this efficient reasoning potential. Furthermore, integrating SAGE as mixed sampling into group-based reinforcement learning (SAGE-RL) enables SAGE-RL to effectively incorporate SAGE-discovered efficient reasoning patterns into standard pass@1 inference, markedly enhancing both the reasoning accuracy and efficiency of LRMs across multiple challenging mathematical benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Introduces SAGE: a self-aware sampling paradigm that exploits LRMs' latent ability to know when to stop reasoning, reducing redundancy of long CoTs. When combined with group-based RL (SAGE-RL), it improves pass@1 reasoning accuracy and efficiency on challenging math benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Long Chains of Thought (CoTs) in large reasoning models often add substantial computation and latency, with longer, more verbose reasoning not reliably correlated with correctness. There is a latent, implicit stopping capability in LRMs that current sampling hides, leading to inefficient inference.
AI.method: Proposes SAGE (Self-Aware Guided Efficient Reasoning) as a sampling paradigm to reveal and utilize the model's stopping awareness. Integrates SAGE into group-based reinforcement learning to form SAGE-RL, enabling mixed sampling that captures efficient reasoning patterns during training and improves standard inference.
AI.result: Empirically verified that LRMs have an implicit optimal stopping point; SAGE-RL achieves higher pass@1 accuracy and greater efficiency across multiple challenging mathematical benchmarks compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: LRMs possess latent self-awareness of when to stop reasoning. SAGE unlocks this capability, and SAGE-RL provides a practical framework to enhance both accuracy and efficiency of reasoning in large models.

title: Contact-Anchored Policies: Contact Conditioning Creates Strong Robot Utility Models
summary: The prevalent paradigm in robot learning attempts to generalize across environments, embodiments, and tasks with language prompts at runtime. A fundamental tension limits this approach: language is often too abstract to guide the concrete physical understanding required for robust manipulation. In this work, we introduce Contact-Anchored Policies (CAP), which replace language conditioning with points of physical contact in space. Simultaneously, we structure CAP as a library of modular utility models rather than a monolithic generalist policy. This factorization allows us to implement a real-to-sim iteration cycle: we build EgoGym, a lightweight simulation benchmark, to rapidly identify failure modes and refine our models and datasets prior to real-world deployment. We show that by conditioning on contact and iterating via simulation, CAP generalizes to novel environments and embodiments out of the box on three fundamental manipulation skills while using only 23 hours of demonstration data, and outperforms large, state-of-the-art VLAs in zero-shot evaluations by 56%. All model checkpoints, codebase, hardware, simulation, and datasets will be open-sourced. Project page: https://cap-policy.github.io/

AI.tldr: CAP uses contact anchors as conditioning instead of language, structured as modular utility models, enabling rapid real-to-sim iteration with EgoGym; achieves out-of-the-box generalization on three manipulation skills with 23 hours of demos and 56% improvement over VLAs in zero-shot.
AI.motivation: Language prompts are often too abstract to guide concrete physical manipulation. Grounding policies on contact information and factorizing into modular utilities enables robust, data-efficient generalization across environments and embodiments.
AI.method: Introduce Contact-Anchored Policies (CAP) that condition on points of physical contact in space, implemented as a library of modular utility models rather than a monolithic policy. Develop EgoGym, a lightweight simulation benchmark, to facilitate rapid real-to-sim iteration and failure analysis. Train with ~23 hours of demonstrations and evaluate zero-shot generalization to novel environments and embodiments.
AI.result: CAP generalizes to novel environments and embodiments out of the box on three fundamental manipulation skills. In zero-shot evaluations, CAP outperforms large, state-of-the-art VLAs by 56%. All resources (checkpoints, code, hardware, simulation, datasets) will be open-sourced.
AI.conclusion: Grounding manipulation policies in contact information and modularized utilities, coupled with a real-to-sim iteration loop via EgoGym, yields data-efficient generalization across environments and embodiments without relying on language prompts.

title: AD-MIR: Bridging the Gap from Perception to Persuasion in Advertising Video Understanding via Structured Reasoning
summary: Multimodal understanding of advertising videos is essential for interpreting the intricate relationship between visual storytelling and abstract persuasion strategies. However, despite excelling at general search, existing agents often struggle to bridge the cognitive gap between pixel-level perception and high-level marketing logic. To address this challenge, we introduce AD-MIR, a framework designed to decode advertising intent via a two-stage architecture. First, in the Structure-Aware Memory Construction phase, the system converts raw video into a structured database by integrating semantic retrieval with exact keyword matching. This approach prioritizes fine-grained brand details (e.g., logos, on-screen text) while dynamically filtering out irrelevant background noise to isolate key protagonists. Second, the Structured Reasoning Agent mimics a marketing expert through an iterative inquiry loop, decomposing the narrative to deduce implicit persuasion tactics. Crucially, it employs an evidence-based self-correction mechanism that rigorously validates these insights against specific video frames, automatically backtracking when visual support is lacking. Evaluation on the AdsQA benchmark demonstrates that AD-MIR achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing the strongest general-purpose agent, DVD, by 1.8% in strict and 9.5% in relaxed accuracy. These results underscore that effective advertising understanding demands explicitly grounding abstract marketing strategies in pixel-level evidence. The code is available at https://github.com/Little-Fridge/AD-MIR.

AI.tldr: AD-MIR is a two-stage framework that decodes advertising intent by (1) constructing a structure-aware memory of video content focusing on branding details, and (2) iteratively reasoning with an evidence-based self-correction loop to reveal implicit persuasion tactics, achieving state-of-the-art on AdsQA.
AI.motivation: To address the gap between low-level pixel perception and high-level marketing reasoning, which existing general agents struggle to bridge, particularly in understanding persuasive strategies in ads.
AI.method: Stage 1 - Structure-Aware Memory Construction: convert raw video into a structured database via semantic retrieval plus exact keyword matching, prioritizing fine-grained brand cues (logos, on-screen text) and filtering background noise; Stage 2 - Structured Reasoning Agent: an iterative inquiry loop that decomposes the narrative to infer persuasion tactics, with an evidence-based self-correction mechanism that validates insights against video frames and backtracks when visual support is missing.
AI.result: On AdsQA, AD-MIR achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing the strongest general-purpose agent (DVD) by 1.8% in strict accuracy and 9.5% in relaxed accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Explicit grounding of abstract marketing strategies in pixel-level evidence is essential for effective advertising understanding; code is available at the provided GitHub URL.

title: Escaping Spectral Bias without Backpropagation: Fast Implicit Neural Representations with Extreme Learning Machines
summary: Training implicit neural representations (INRs) to capture fine-scale details typically relies on iterative backpropagation and is often hindered by spectral bias when the target exhibits highly non-uniform frequency content. We propose ELM-INR, a backpropagation-free INR that decomposes the domain into overlapping subdomains and fits each local problem using an Extreme Learning Machine (ELM) in closed form, replacing iterative optimization with stable linear least-squares solutions. This design yields fast and numerically robust reconstruction by combining local predictors through a partition of unity. To understand where approximation becomes difficult under fixed local capacity, we analyze the method from a spectral Barron norm perspective, which reveals that global reconstruction error is dominated by regions with high spectral complexity. Building on this insight, we introduce BEAM, an adaptive mesh refinement strategy that balances spectral complexity across subdomains to improve reconstruction quality in capacity-constrained regimes.

AI.tldr: ELM-INR uses overlapping subdomains with Extreme Learning Machines to realize backpropagation-free implicit neural representations, fused by a partition of unity; BEAM adaptively refines the mesh to balance spectral complexity and improve capacity-limited reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Address spectral bias and slow/unstable iterative optimization in training implicit neural representations, especially for targets with non-uniform frequency content; enable fast, robust reconstructions via closed-form solutions.
AI.method: 1) Decompose the domain into overlapping subdomains. 2) For each subdomain, fit an Extreme Learning Machine in closed form (randomized hidden layer + linear least-squares readout). 3) Combine local predictors using a partition of unity to obtain a global INR. 4) Analyze the global error via the spectral Barron norm to identify regions of high spectral complexity. 5) Introduce BEAM, an adaptive mesh refinement strategy that balances spectral complexity across subdomains to improve reconstruction in capacity-constrained regimes.
AI.result: The approach yields fast, numerically robust reconstructions by avoiding backpropagation; global error is dominated by regions with high spectral complexity; BEAM mitigates this by adaptively refining subdomains to balance complexity and improve performance in constrained settings.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a practical, backprop-free INR framework with spectral-structure-aware refinement. BEAM provides a mechanism to allocate capacity where needed, reducing global error in non-uniform frequency content scenarios.

title: Circuit Representations of Random Forests with Applications to XAI
summary: We make three contributions in this paper. First, we present an approach for compiling a random forest classifier into a set of circuits, where each circuit directly encodes the instances in some class of the classifier. We show empirically that our proposed approach is significantly more efficient than existing similar approaches. Next, we utilize this approach to further obtain circuits that are tractable for computing the complete and general reasons of a decision, which are instance abstractions that play a fundamental role in computing explanations. Finally, we propose algorithms for computing the robustness of a decision and all shortest ways to flip it. We illustrate the utility of our contributions by using them to enumerate all sufficient reasons, necessary reasons and contrastive explanations of decisions; to compute the robustness of decisions; and to identify all shortest ways to flip the decisions made by random forest classifiers learned from a wide range of datasets.

AI.tldr: We compile random forest classifiers into class-specific circuits to enable efficient, exact reasoning about decisions, including complete/general reasons, robustness, and shortest flip paths, with empirical efficiency gains over prior methods and applicability to enumerating sufficient/necessary/contrastive explanations across diverse datasets.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable, exact explanations and robustness analysis for RF decisions by transforming the model into a circuit representation that supports tractable inference.
AI.method: Construct a set of circuits, each encoding the instances of a classifier class, and develop algorithms to derive complete/general reasons, robustness, and all shortest flip paths; apply the approach to enumerate sufficient/necessary/contrastive explanations and robustness across multiple datasets.
AI.result: Empirical evidence that the circuit-based approach is more efficient than existing methods; the circuits enable tractable computation of explanations and robustness; demonstrated ability to enumerate diverse explanations and identify shortest flip paths across datasets.
AI.conclusion: Circuit-based compilation of random forests provides efficient, exact explanatory and robustness analysis capabilities, improving interpretability and actionable insight for RF models.

title: Robustness Is a Function, Not a Number: A Factorized Comprehensive Study of OOD Robustness in Vision-Based Driving
summary: Out of distribution (OOD) robustness in autonomous driving is often reduced to a single number, hiding what breaks a policy. We decompose environments along five axes: scene (rural/urban), season, weather, time (day/night), and agent mix; and measure performance under controlled $k$-factor perturbations ($k \in \{0,1,2,3\}$). Using closed loop control in VISTA, we benchmark FC, CNN, and ViT policies, train compact ViT heads on frozen foundation-model (FM) features, and vary ID support in scale, diversity, and temporal context. (1) ViT policies are markedly more OOD-robust than comparably sized CNN/FC, and FM features yield state-of-the-art success at a latency cost. (2) Naive temporal inputs (multi-frame) do not beat the best single-frame baseline. (3) The largest single factor drops are rural $\rightarrow$ urban and day $\rightarrow$ night ($\sim 31\%$ each); actor swaps $\sim 10\%$, moderate rain $\sim 7\%$; season shifts can be drastic, and combining a time flip with other changes further degrades performance. (4) FM-feature policies stay above $85\%$ under three simultaneous changes; non-FM single-frame policies take a large first-shift hit, and all no-FM models fall below $50\%$ by three changes. (5) Interactions are non-additive: some pairings partially offset, whereas season-time combinations are especially harmful. (6) Training on winter/snow is most robust to single-factor shifts, while a rural+summer baseline gives the best overall OOD performance. (7) Scaling traces/views improves robustness ($+11.8$ points from $5$ to $14$ traces), yet targeted exposure to hard conditions can substitute for scale. (8) Using multiple ID environments broadens coverage and strengthens weak cases (urban OOD $60.6\% \rightarrow 70.1\%$) with a small ID drop; single-ID preserves peak performance but in a narrow domain. These results yield actionable design rules for OOD-robust driving policies.

AI.tldr: Decomposes OOD robustness into five environment factors and shows ViT with frozen foundation-model features yields strongest OOD robustness across multi-factor perturbations; temporal inputs are not beneficial; multi-ID environments and scaling traces provide additional gains; provides actionable design rules.
AI.motivation: Single-number OOD robustness conceals distinct failure modes. A factorized, multi-axis analysis reveals which factors break policies and guides robust design for autonomous driving.
AI.method: Evaluate closed-loop driving in VISTA across five axes (scene, season, weather, time, agent mix) with k-factor perturbations (k=0..3). Compare FC, CNN, ViT policies; train compact ViT heads on frozen foundation-model features; vary in-domain (ID) support in scale, diversity, and temporal context.
AI.result: (1) ViT policies outperform CNN/FC at similar size; FM features yield state-of-the-art performance but with latency cost. (2) Multi-frame inputs do not beat best single-frame. (3) Largest single-factor drops: rural->urban and day->night (~31%); actor swaps (~10%); rainy (~7%); seasons; time flips plus other changes degrade. (4) FM-feature policies stay >85% under three simultaneous changes; non-FM single-frame show large first-shift hits; no-FM fall below 50% by three changes. (5) Interactions are non-additive; some pairings offset; season-time particularly harmful. (6) Winter/snow training yields robustness to single-factor shifts; rural+summer baseline yields best overall OOD. (7) Scaling traces/views improves robustness (+11.8 points from 5 to 14 traces); targeted hard-condition exposure can substitute for scale. (8) Multi-ID environments expand coverage and strengthen weak cases (urban OOD 60.6% → 70.1%) with small ID cost; single-ID preserves peak but narrow. Overall: actionable design rules for OOD-robust driving.
AI.conclusion: Found results translate into concrete design rules for constructing OOD-robust autonomous driving policies, emphasizing ViT with FM features, multi-ID environmental exposure, and targeted hardening of sensitive factor interactions.

title: Uncovering Modality Discrepancy and Generalization Illusion for General-Purpose 3D Medical Segmentation
summary: While emerging 3D medical foundation models are envisioned as versatile tools with offer general-purpose capabilities, their validation remains largely confined to regional and structural imaging, leaving a significant modality discrepancy unexplored. To provide a rigorous and objective assessment, we curate the UMD dataset comprising 490 whole-body PET/CT and 464 whole-body PET/MRI scans ($\sim$675k 2D images, $\sim$12k 3D organ annotations) and conduct a thorough and comprehensive evaluation of representative 3D segmentation foundation models. Through intra-subject controlled comparisons of paired scans, we isolate imaging modality as the primary independent variable to evaluate model robustness in real-world applications. Our evaluation reveals a stark discrepancy between literature-reported benchmarks and real-world efficacy, particularly when transitioning from structural to functional domains. Such systemic failures underscore that current 3D foundation models are far from achieving truly general-purpose status, necessitating a paradigm shift toward multi-modal training and evaluation to bridge the gap between idealized benchmarking and comprehensive clinical utility. This dataset and analysis establish a foundational cornerstone for future research to develop truly modality-agnostic medical foundation models.

AI.tldr: A dataset+evaluation study reveals that current 3D medical foundation models struggle to generalize across imaging modalities (structural vs functional), undermining their claimed versatility.
AI.motivation: To move beyond region/structure-focused benchmarks and assess true generalization across imaging modalities (e.g., PET/CT vs PET/MRI) using a controlled, real-world setting.
AI.method: Create the UMD dataset with 490 whole-body PET/CT and 464 whole-body PET/MRI scans (≈675k 2D images, ~12k 3D organ annotations). Use intra-subject paired scans to isolate modality as the main variable and evaluate representative 3D segmentation foundation models.
AI.result: There is a stark gap between literature benchmarks and real-world performance, especially when switching from structural to functional imaging; existing models fail to generalize across modalities.
AI.conclusion: Current 3D medical foundation models are not truly modality-agnostic; progress requires multi-modal training/evaluation and modality-aware benchmarking to achieve real clinical utility.

title: SERE: Similarity-based Expert Re-routing for Efficient Batch Decoding in MoE Models
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures employ sparse activation to deliver faster training and inference with higher accuracy than dense LLMs. However, in production serving, MoE models require batch inference to optimize hardware efficiency, which may cause excessive expert activation and thus slow the memory-bound decoding stage. To address the fundamental tension between batch decoding and expert sparsity, we present SERE, a Similarity-based Expert Re-routing method for Efficient batch decoding in MoE models. SERE dynamically reduces the number of active experts in an input-aware manner by re-routing tokens from secondary experts to their most similar primary counterparts. It also leverages similarity patterns to identify and preserve critical experts, thereby preventing capability loss. Notably, SERE avoids static expert pruning or merging, instead enabling dynamic expert skipping based on batch-level expert redundancy. Additionally, we provide an efficient custom CUDA kernel for SERE, enabling plug-and-play use in vLLM with only a single-line code change. Extensive experiments on various complex reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that SERE achieves up to 2.0x speedup with minimal quality loss, providing a practical solution for cost-efficient and latency-sensitive large-scale MoE deployment. Code implementation of SERE can be found in https://github.com/JL-Cheng/SERE.

AI.tldr: SERE proposes a similarity-based re-routing mechanism to reduce active experts during batch decoding in MoE models, achieving up to 2x speedups with minimal quality loss, without static pruning, via a CUDA kernel and plug-in for vLLM.
AI.motivation: To resolve the trade-off between batch decoding efficiency and expert sparsity in mixture-of-experts models; batch inference increases efficiency but can trigger excessive expert activation, slowing decoding.
AI.method: Dynamic, input-aware reduction of active experts by re-routing tokens from secondary to most similar primary experts; use similarity patterns to preserve critical experts; no static pruning or merging; dynamic skipping based on batch-level redundancy; implement via custom CUDA kernel; plug-and-play in vLLM with one-line change.
AI.result: Experimental results show up to 2.0x speedup with minimal quality loss across complex reasoning benchmarks; demonstrates practicality for cost-efficient, latency-sensitive MoE deployment; code available.
AI.conclusion: SERE offers a practical, dynamic alternative to static pruning for balancing batch decoding and expert sparsity in MoE models, enabling faster inference while preserving capability.

title: MemAdapter: Fast Alignment across Agent Memory Paradigms via Generative Subgraph Retrieval
summary: Memory mechanism is a core component of LLM-based agents, enabling reasoning and knowledge discovery over long-horizon contexts. Existing agent memory systems are typically designed within isolated paradigms (e.g., explicit, parametric, or latent memory) with tightly coupled retrieval methods that hinder cross-paradigm generalization and fusion. In this work, we take a first step toward unifying heterogeneous memory paradigms within a single memory system. We propose MemAdapter, a memory retrieval framework that enables fast alignment across agent memory paradigms. MemAdapter adopts a two-stage training strategy: (1) training a generative subgraph retriever from the unified memory space, and (2) adapting the retriever to unseen memory paradigms by training a lightweight alignment module through contrastive learning. This design improves the flexibility for memory retrieval and substantially reduces alignment cost across paradigms. Comprehensive experiments on three public evaluation benchmarks demonstrate that the generative subgraph retriever consistently outperforms five strong agent memory systems across three memory paradigms and agent model scales. Notably, MemAdapter completes cross-paradigm alignment within 13 minutes on a single GPU, achieving superior performance over original memory retrievers with less than 5% of training compute. Furthermore, MemAdapter enables effective zero-shot fusion across memory paradigms, highlighting its potential as a plug-and-play solution for agent memory systems.

AI.tldr: MemAdapter unifies heterogeneous agent memory paradigms with a two-stage training approach, enabling fast cross-paradigm alignment and superior performance with low compute, and acts as a plug-and-play solution for memory retrieval.
AI.motivation: Current agent memory designs are siloed into explicit, parametric, or latent paradigms with tightly coupled retrieval, hindering cross-paradigm generalization and fusion; a unified, efficient memory retrieval framework is needed.
AI.method: Two-stage training: (1) train a generative subgraph retriever from a unified memory space; (2) adapt the retriever to unseen memory paradigms by training a lightweight alignment module via contrastive learning. This enables fast cross-paradigm alignment and allows cross-paradigm fusion with low training cost.
AI.result: On three public benchmarks, the generative subgraph retriever outperforms five strong agent memory systems across three memory paradigms and model scales. Cross-paradigm alignment finishes in ~13 minutes on a single GPU, using <5% of training compute, and zero-shot fusion across memory paradigms is effective.
AI.conclusion: MemAdapter provides a flexible, plug-and-play memory retrieval solution that unifies heterogeneous memory paradigms, enabling efficient cross-paradigm alignment and fusion for LLM-based agents.

title: $χ_{0}$: Resource-Aware Robust Manipulation via Taming Distributional Inconsistencies
summary: High-reliability long-horizon robotic manipulation has traditionally relied on large-scale data and compute to understand complex real-world dynamics. However, we identify that the primary bottleneck to real-world robustness is not resource scale alone, but the distributional shift among the human demonstration distribution, the inductive bias learned by the policy, and the test-time execution distribution -- a systematic inconsistency that causes compounding errors in multi-stage tasks. To mitigate these inconsistencies, we propose $χ_{0}$, a resource-efficient framework with effective modules designated to achieve production-level robustness in robotic manipulation. Our approach builds off three technical pillars: (i) Model Arithmetic, a weight-space merging strategy that efficiently soaks up diverse distributions of different demonstrations, varying from object appearance to state variations; (ii) Stage Advantage, a stage-aware advantage estimator that provides stable, dense progress signals, overcoming the numerical instability of prior non-stage approaches; and (iii) Train-Deploy Alignment, which bridges the distribution gap via spatio-temporal augmentation, heuristic DAgger corrections, and temporal chunk-wise smoothing. $χ_{0}$ enables two sets of dual-arm robots to collaboratively orchestrate long-horizon garment manipulation, spanning tasks from flattening, folding, to hanging different clothes. Our method exhibits high-reliability autonomy; we are able to run the system from arbitrary initial state for consecutive 24 hours non-stop. Experiments validate that $χ_{0}$ surpasses the state-of-the-art $π_{0.5}$ in success rate by nearly 250%, with only 20-hour data and 8 A100 GPUs. Code, data and models will be released to facilitate the community.

AI.tldr: χ0 is a resource-efficient framework for robust long-horizon robotic manipulation that tackles distributional shift with three pillars—Model Arithmetic, Stage Advantage, and Train-Deploy Alignment—demonstrated on dual-arm garment tasks achieving sustained autonomy with modest data and compute.
AI.motivation: The principal barrier to real-world robustness is distributional mismatch among human demonstrations, the inductive biases of the learned policy, and test-time execution, not merely raw resource scale.
AI.method: Three technical pillars: (i) Model Arithmetic—weight-space merging across diverse demonstrations; (ii) Stage Advantage—stage-aware, stable progress estimation; (iii) Train-Deploy Alignment—spatio-temporal augmentation, heuristic DAgger corrections, and temporal chunk-wise smoothing for distribution alignment.
AI.result: χ0 surpasses the state-of-the-art π0.5 by about 250% in success rate using only ~20 hours of data and 8 A100 GPUs; demonstrates two dual-arm robots performing long-horizon garment manipulation (flattening, folding, hanging) with production-level robustness including 24-hour continuous operation; code, data, and models will be released.
AI.conclusion: χ0 offers a practical, resource-efficient path to production-level robustness in long-horizon robotic manipulation by effectively bridging the demonstration, learning, and deployment distributions.

title: Dense Neural Networks are not Universal Approximators
summary: We investigate the approximation capabilities of dense neural networks. While universal approximation theorems establish that sufficiently large architectures can approximate arbitrary continuous functions if there are no restrictions on the weight values, we show that dense neural networks do not possess this universality. Our argument is based on a model compression approach, combining the weak regularity lemma with an interpretation of feedforward networks as message passing graph neural networks. We consider ReLU neural networks subject to natural constraints on weights and input and output dimensions, which model a notion of dense connectivity. Within this setting, we demonstrate the existence of Lipschitz continuous functions that cannot be approximated by such networks. This highlights intrinsic limitations of neural networks with dense layers and motivates the use of sparse connectivity as a necessary ingredient for achieving true universality.

AI.tldr: Dense networks with dense connectivity are not universal function approximators under natural weight and dimension constraints; sparsity is necessary for universality.
AI.motivation: Classic universal approximation theorems assume unrestricted weights and sufficient capacity. This work questions universality under dense connectivity and realistic constraints, revealing intrinsic limitations.
AI.method: Model compression framework combining the weak regularity lemma from graph theory with an interpretation of feedforward nets as message-passing graph neural networks; analysis restricted to ReLU networks with natural weight and dimension constraints.
AI.result: There exist Lipschitz continuous functions that cannot be approximated by dense ReLU networks under the stated constraints, demonstrating a fundamental limitation of dense architectures.
AI.conclusion: Dense connectivity alone does not guarantee universality; to achieve true universal approximation, sparse (or structured) connectivity is necessary, motivating sparsity in network design.

title: Grounding Generative Planners in Verifiable Logic: A Hybrid Architecture for Trustworthy Embodied AI
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise as planners for embodied AI, but their stochastic nature lacks formal reasoning, preventing strict safety guarantees for physical deployment. Current approaches often rely on unreliable LLMs for safety checks or simply reject unsafe plans without offering repairs. We introduce the Verifiable Iterative Refinement Framework (VIRF), a neuro-symbolic architecture that shifts the paradigm from passive safety gatekeeping to active collaboration. Our core contribution is a tutor-apprentice dialogue where a deterministic Logic Tutor, grounded in a formal safety ontology, provides causal and pedagogical feedback to an LLM planner. This enables intelligent plan repairs rather than mere avoidance. We also introduce a scalable knowledge acquisition pipeline that synthesizes safety knowledge bases from real-world documents, correcting blind spots in existing benchmarks. In challenging home safety tasks, VIRF achieves a perfect 0 percent Hazardous Action Rate (HAR) and a 77.3 percent Goal-Condition Rate (GCR), which is the highest among all baselines. It is highly efficient, requiring only 1.1 correction iterations on average. VIRF demonstrates a principled pathway toward building fundamentally trustworthy and verifiably safe embodied agents.

AI.tldr: Verifiable Iterative Refinement Framework (VIRF) couples a deterministic Logic Tutor with an LLM planner in a neuro-symbolic loop to enable safe, repairable planning for embodied AI, aided by a scalable safety knowledge-base pipeline; achieves zero hazardous actions and high goal-condition success with few corrections.
AI.motivation: LLMs lack formal, verifiable safety reasoning for embodied tasks; existing safety gates are brittle or restrictive and do not repair unsafe plans.
AI.method: A tutor-apprentice dialogue where a Logic Tutor, grounded in a formal safety ontology, provides causal and pedagogical feedback to an LLM planner, enabling plan repairs; plus a pipeline to synthesize safety knowledge bases from real-world documents; evaluated on home-safety tasks.
AI.result: HAR = 0% (no hazardous actions); GCR = 77.3% (highest among baselines); average correction iterations = 1.1; strong improvement over baselines.
AI.conclusion: VIRF demonstrates a principled approach for trustworthy, verifiably safe embodied agents by shifting safety from passive gating to active, knowledge-guided plan refinement.

title: TwinRL-VLA: Digital Twin-Driven Reinforcement Learning for Real-World Robotic Manipulation
summary: Despite strong generalization capabilities, Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models remain constrained by the high cost of expert demonstrations and insufficient real-world interaction. While online reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promise in improving general foundation models, applying RL to VLA manipulation in real-world settings is still hindered by low exploration efficiency and a restricted exploration space. Through systematic real-world experiments, we observe that the effective exploration space of online RL is closely tied to the data distribution of supervised fine-tuning (SFT). Motivated by this observation, we propose TwinRL, a digital twin-real-world collaborative RL framework designed to scale and guide exploration for VLA models. First, a high-fidelity digital twin is efficiently reconstructed from smartphone-captured scenes, enabling realistic bidirectional transfer between real and simulated environments.
