During the SFT warm-up stage, we introduce an exploration space expansion strategy using digital twins to broaden the support of the data trajectory distribution. Building on this enhanced initialization, we propose a sim-to-real guided exploration strategy to further accelerate online RL. Specifically, TwinRL performs efficient and parallel online RL in the digital twin prior to deployment, effectively bridging the gap between offline and online training stages. Subsequently, we exploit efficient digital twin sampling to identify failure-prone yet informative configurations, which are used to guide targeted human-in-the-loop rollouts on the real robot. In our experiments, TwinRL approaches 100% success in both in-distribution regions covered by real-world demonstrations and out-of-distribution regions, delivering at least a 30% speedup over prior real-world RL methods and requiring only about 20 minutes on average across four tasks.

AI.tldr: TwinRL introduces a digital twin–real-world collaborative RL framework for VLA manipulation, achieving complete success in tests, with 30% speedups and ~20 minutes average training across four tasks.
AI.motivation: to overcome high data cost of expert demonstrations and limited real-world exploration in Vision-Language-Action models by expanding the exploration space and bridging offline-online training via digital twins.
AI.method: Construct a high-fidelity digital twin from smartphone-captured scenes; use it for exploration-expanded SFT warm-up; apply sim-to-real guided exploration; perform online RL in the digital twin before real deployment; identify failure-prone configurations via efficient sampling to guide targeted human-in-the-loop real-world rollouts.
AI.result: 100% success in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution regions; at least 30% speedup over prior real-world RL methods; around 20 minutes on average across four tasks.
AI.conclusion: Digital twin–assisted RL effectively expands exploration, accelerates training, and enhances data efficiency for real-world VLA manipulation, enabling scalable deployment.

title: Influence of Geometry, Class Imbalance and Alignment on Reconstruction Accuracy -- A Micro-CT Phantom-Based Evaluation
summary: The accuracy of the 3D models created from medical scans depends on imaging hardware, segmentation methods and mesh processing techniques etc. The effects of geometry type, class imbalance, voxel and point cloud alignment on accuracy remain to be thoroughly explored. This work evaluates the errors across the reconstruction pipeline and explores the use of voxel and surface-based accuracy metrics for different segmentation algorithms and geometry types. A sphere, a facemask, and an AAA were printed using the SLA technique and scanned using a micro-CT machine. Segmentation was performed using GMM, Otsu and RG based methods. Segmented and reference models aligned using the KU algorithm, were quantitatively compared to evaluate metrics like Dice and Jaccard scores, precision. Surface meshes were registered with reference meshes using an ICP-based alignment process. Metrics like chamfer distance, and average Hausdorff distance were evaluated. The Otsu method was found to be the most suitable method for all the geometries. AAA yielded low overlap scores due to its small wall thickness and misalignment. The effect of class imbalance on specificity was observed the most for AAA. Surface-based accuracy metrics differed from the voxel-based trends. The RG method performed best for sphere, while GMM and Otsu perform better for AAA. The facemask surface was most error-prone, possibly due to misalignment during the ICP process. Segmentation accuracy is a cumulative sum of errors across different stages of the reconstruction process. High voxel-based accuracy metrics may be misleading in cases of high class imbalance and sensitivity to alignment. The Jaccard index is found to be more stringent than the Dice and more suitable for accuracy assessment for thin-walled structures. Voxel and point cloud alignment should be ensured to make any reliable assessment of the reconstruction pipeline.

AI.tldr: A systematic comparison of voxel- and surface-based accuracy metrics across a 3D reconstruction pipeline for medical geometries, highlighting segmentation methods, alignment, and geometry effects; Jaccard is more suitable than Dice for thin-walled structures; Otsu generally best, with geometry-dependent nuances.
AI.motivation: To understand how geometry type, class imbalance, voxel/point-cloud alignment, and segmentation method influence accuracy in 3D medical reconstructions, and how voxel- vs surface-based metrics compare.
AI.method: Printable test geometries (sphere, facemask, AAA) were SLA-printed, scanned with micro-CT; segmentation via GMM, Otsu, RG; align segmented to reference with KU algorithm; evaluate voxel metrics (Dice, Jaccard, precision) and surface metrics (Chamfer, mean/avg Hausdorff) after ICP-based surface alignment; analyze across stages for different geometries.
AI.result: Otsu segmentation performed best across geometries; AAA showed low overlap due to thin walls and misalignment; class imbalance strongly affected specificity for AAA; surface-based metrics diverged from voxel-based trends; RG best for sphere; GMM and Otsu best for AAA; facemask surface most error-prone likely from ICP misalignment; segmentation errors accumulate across pipeline; high voxel metrics can be misleading under imbalance/alignment sensitivity; Jaccard index is more stringent than Dice for thin-walled structures; voxel and point-cloud alignment is essential for reliable assessment.
AI.conclusion: Accurate reconstruction assessment requires careful alignment and metric choice; voxel- vs surface-based evaluations can diverge, especially for thin-walled geometries; misalignment and class imbalance significantly influence accuracy; Jaccard is recommended for thin structures; the pipeline’s accuracy is a cumulative outcome of errors across steps.

title: TASTE: Task-Aware Out-of-Distribution Detection via Stein Operators
summary: Out-of-distribution detection methods are often either data-centric, detecting deviations from the training input distribution irrespective of their effect on a trained model, or model-centric, relying on classifier outputs without explicit reference to data geometry. We propose TASTE (Task-Aware STEin operators): a task-aware framework based on so-called Stein operators, which allows us to link distribution shift to the input sensitivity of the model. We show that the resulting operator admits a clear geometric interpretation as a projection of distribution shift onto the sensitivity field of the model, yielding theoretical guarantees. Beyond detecting the presence of a shift, the same construction enables its localisation through a coordinate-wise decomposition, and for image data-provides interpretable per-pixel diagnostics. Experiments on controlled Gaussian shifts, MNIST under geometric perturbations, and CIFAR-10 perturbed benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed method aligns closely with task degradation while outperforming established baselines.

AI.tldr: Introduces TASTE, a task-aware Stein operator framework for out-of-distribution detection that links distribution shift to model input sensitivity, enabling both detection and per-pixel localization with theoretical guarantees; demonstrates strong empirical performance on Gaussian shifts, MNIST geometric perturbations, and CIFAR-10 perturbations.
AI.motivation: Bridging the gap between data-centric and model-centric OOD detection by tying distribution shift to the geometry of the model's input sensitivity, yielding interpretable and task-relevant diagnostics.
AI.method: Develop a task-aware Stein operator framework (TASTE) where the operator projects distribution shift onto the model's sensitivity field. The framework supports coordinate-wise decomposition for localization and provides per-pixel diagnostics for image data. The approach includes theoretical guarantees on alignment with task degradation and interpretability of the shift.
AI.result: Empirical results on controlled Gaussian shifts, MNIST with geometric perturbations, and CIFAR-10 perturbations show that TASTE tracks task degradation and outperforms established baselines.
AI.conclusion: TASTE offers a principled, interpretable framework linking distribution shifts to model sensitivity, with localization and per-pixel diagnostics, achieving strong empirical performance. Future work could address computational efficiency, broader data modalities, and robustness to model misspecification.

title: SCOUT-RAG: Scalable and Cost-Efficient Unifying Traversal for Agentic Graph-RAG over Distributed Domains
summary: Graph-RAG improves LLM reasoning using structured knowledge, yet conventional designs rely on a centralized knowledge graph. In distributed and access-restricted settings (e.g., hospitals or multinational organizations), retrieval must select relevant domains and appropriate traversal depth without global graph visibility or exhaustive querying. To address this challenge, we introduce \textbf{SCOUT-RAG} (\textit{\underline{S}calable and \underline{CO}st-efficient \underline{U}nifying \underline{T}raversal}), a distributed agentic Graph-RAG framework that performs progressive cross-domain retrieval guided by incremental utility goals. SCOUT-RAG employs four cooperative agents that: (i) estimate domain relevance, (ii) decide when to expand retrieval to additional domains, (iii) adapt traversal depth to avoid unnecessary graph exploration, and (iv) synthesize the high-quality answers. The framework is designed to minimize retrieval regret, defined as missing useful domain information, while controlling latency and API cost. Across multi-domain knowledge settings, SCOUT-RAG achieves performance comparable to centralized baselines, including DRIFT and exhaustive domain traversal, while substantially reducing cross-domain calls, total tokens processed, and latency.

AI.tldr: SCOUT-RAG is a distributed, agentic Graph-RAG framework that uses four cooperative agents to perform progressive cross-domain retrieval under privacy and cost constraints, achieving centralized-like performance with lower latency, API calls, and tokens processed.
AI.motivation: In distributed and access-restricted settings (e.g., hospitals, multinational orgs), global graph visibility is unavailable. Retrieval must selectively traverse domains and depth to minimize retrieval regret (missed useful information) while bounding latency and API costs.
AI.method: Four cooperative agents guide progressive cross-domain retrieval: (i) domain relevance estimation, (ii) when to expand to additional domains, (iii) adaptive traversal depth to avoid unnecessary exploration, and (iv) synthesis of high-quality answers. The system optimizes a utility-driven objective to balance information gain against cost, with cross-domain calls minimized.
AI.result: SCOUT-RAG achieves performance comparable to centralized baselines (including DRIFT and exhaustive domain traversal) while substantially reducing cross-domain calls, total tokens, and latency across multi-domain settings.
AI.conclusion: Distributed, agentic Graph-RAG can match centralized performance under restricted visibility while delivering better cost-efficiency and scalability in multi-domain knowledge environments; suitable for privacy-preserving and resource-constrained deployments.

title: Automating the Refinement of Reinforcement Learning Specifications
summary: Logical specifications have been shown to help reinforcement learning algorithms in achieving complex tasks. However, when a task is under-specified, agents might fail to learn useful policies. In this work, we explore the possibility of improving coarse-grained logical specifications via an exploration-guided strategy. We propose \textsc{AutoSpec}, a framework that searches for a logical specification refinement whose satisfaction implies satisfaction of the original specification, but which provides additional guidance therefore making it easier for reinforcement learning algorithms to learn useful policies. \textsc{AutoSpec} is applicable to reinforcement learning tasks specified via the SpectRL specification logic. We exploit the compositional nature of specifications written in SpectRL, and design four refinement procedures that modify the abstract graph of the specification by either refining its existing edge specifications or by introducing new edge specifications. We prove that all four procedures maintain specification soundness, i.e. any trajectory satisfying the refined specification also satisfies the original. We then show how \textsc{AutoSpec} can be integrated with existing reinforcement learning algorithms for learning policies from logical specifications. Our experiments demonstrate that \textsc{AutoSpec} yields promising improvements in terms of the complexity of control tasks that can be solved, when refined logical specifications produced by \textsc{AutoSpec} are utilized.

AI.tldr: AutoSpec automatically refines SpectRL specifications to provide guided learning signals for RL while preserving the original specification.
AI.motivation: Under-specified tasks in reinforcement learning hinder efficient policy learning; refining specifications can offer additional, sound guidance to improve learnability.
AI.method: Four refinement procedures (refine existing edge specifications or add new edge specifications) applied to the abstract specification graph; rely on SpectRL's compositional structure; prove that refinements are sound (any trajectory satisfying the refined spec also satisfies the original); integrate AutoSpec with standard RL algorithms to learn policies from the refined specifications.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantee: all four refinement procedures maintain specification soundness. Empirically: refined specifications improve the complexity of solvable control tasks and enhance learning efficiency when used to guide RL.
AI.conclusion: AutoSpec provides a principled, automatic way to tighten logical specifications for RL without compromising the original intent, enabling learning of more complex tasks.

title: Looking and Listening Inside and Outside: Multimodal Artificial Intelligence Systems for Driver Safety Assessment and Intelligent Vehicle Decision-Making
summary: The looking-in-looking-out (LILO) framework has enabled intelligent vehicle applications that understand both the outside scene and the driver state to improve safety outcomes, with examples in smart airbag deployment, takeover time prediction in autonomous control transitions, and driver attention monitoring. In this research, we propose an augmentation to this framework, making a case for the audio modality as an additional source of information to understand the driver, and in the evolving autonomy landscape, also the passengers and those outside the vehicle. We expand LILO by incorporating audio signals, forming the looking-and-listening inside-and-outside (L-LIO) framework to enhance driver state assessment and environment understanding through multimodal sensor fusion. We evaluate three example cases where audio enhances vehicle safety: supervised learning on driver speech audio to classify potential impairment states (e.g., intoxication), collection and analysis of passenger natural language instructions (e.g., "turn after that red building") to motivate how spoken language can interface with planning systems through audio-aligned instruction data, and limitations of vision-only systems where audio may disambiguate the guidance and gestures of external agents. Datasets include custom-collected in-vehicle and external audio samples in real-world environments. Pilot findings show that audio yields safety-relevant insights, particularly in nuanced or context-rich scenarios where sound is critical to safe decision-making or visual signals alone are insufficient. Challenges include ambient noise interference, privacy considerations, and robustness across human subjects, motivating further work on reliability in dynamic real-world contexts. L-LIO augments driver and scene understanding through multimodal fusion of audio and visual sensing, offering new paths for safety intervention.

AI.tldr: Extends the LILO framework to L-LIO by integrating audio for driver/passenger/outside understanding, demonstrating three audio-enhanced safety cases, and discussing practical challenges and future reliability needs.
AI.motivation: Vision-only LILO can misinterpret driver state and external cues in nuanced, context-rich scenarios; audio provides complementary signals (speech, ambient sounds) to improve safety-critical decisions and human–vehicle interaction.
AI.method: Propose L-LIO architecture with multimodal fusion of audio and visual data. Validate via three case studies: (1) supervised learning on driver speech to classify impairment states; (2) analysis of passenger natural language instructions to inform planning with audio-aligned data; (3) evidence that audio can disambiguate external agents’ guidance/gestures. Datasets include custom in-vehicle and external audio from real-world settings. Pilot studies report qualitative improvements in safety-relevant insights when audio is incorporated.
AI.result: Pilot findings indicate that audio provides safety-relevant insights, particularly in nuanced or context-rich situations where audio cues supplement or resolve ambiguities in visual signals. Benefits observed in the examined cases; challenges identified include ambient noise, privacy considerations, and robustness across diverse subjects and environments.
AI.conclusion: L-LIO broadens driver and scene understanding through multimodal fusion of audio and visual sensing, enabling new safety interventions. The work highlights practical challenges (noise, privacy, robustness) and motivates future research on reliability and deployment in dynamic real-world contexts.

title: Continuous Program Search
summary:
Genetic Programming yields interpretable programs, but small syntactic mutations can induce large, unpredictable behavioral shifts, degrading locality and sample efficiency. We frame this as an operator-design problem: learn a continuous program space where latent distance has behavioral meaning, then design mutation operators that exploit this structure without changing the evolutionary optimizer.
  We make locality measurable by tracking action-level divergence under controlled latent perturbations, identifying an empirical trust region for behavior-local continuous variation. Using a compact trading-strategy DSL with four semantic components (long/short entry and exit), we learn a matching block-factorized embedding and compare isotropic Gaussian mutation over the full latent space to geometry-compiled mutation that restricts updates to semantically paired entry--exit subspaces and proposes directions using a learned flow-based model trained on logged mutation outcomes.
  Under identical $(μ+λ)$ evolution strategies and fixed evaluation budgets across five assets, the learned mutation operator discovers strong strategies using an order of magnitude fewer evaluations and achieves the highest median out-of-sample Sharpe ratio. Although isotropic mutation occasionally attains higher peak performance, geometry-compiled mutation yields faster, more reliable progress, demonstrating that semantically aligned mutation can substantially improve search efficiency without modifying the underlying evolutionary algorithm.

AI.tldr: Learned geometry-aware mutation in GP latent space improves sample efficiency and robustness for trading strategies, outperforming isotropic mutations.
AI.motivation: GP mutations can cause large behavioral shifts; need locality-preserving operators by designing a continuous, semantics-aligned program space and operators that exploit structure without changing the evolutionary algorithm.
AI.method: Develop a block-factorized embedding for a compact DSL with four semantics (long/short entry/exit); train a flow-based model to propose directed mutations restricted to entry-exit subspaces; compare to isotropic Gaussian mutations; use latent perturbations to define a trust region and evaluate under identical μ+λ settings across five assets.
AI.result: The geometry-aware mutation discovers strong strategies with ~10x fewer evaluations and the highest median out-of-sample Sharpe ratio; isotropic mutation sometimes yields higher peaks but slower/more volatile progress; geometry-guided mutation provides faster, more reliable improvement.
AI.conclusion: Semantic alignment of mutation operators in a learned latent space can substantially improve search efficiency in GP without altering the evolutionary algorithm.

title: On Protecting Agentic Systems' Intellectual Property via Watermarking
summary: The evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) into agentic systems that perform autonomous reasoning and tool use has created significant intellectual property (IP) value. We demonstrate that these systems are highly vulnerable to imitation attacks, where adversaries steal proprietary capabilities by training imitation models on victim outputs. Crucially, existing LLM watermarking techniques fail in this domain because real-world agentic systems often operate as grey boxes, concealing the internal reasoning traces required for verification. This paper presents AGENTWM, the first watermarking framework designed specifically for agentic models. AGENTWM exploits the semantic equivalence of action sequences, injecting watermarks by subtly biasing the distribution of functionally identical tool execution paths. This mechanism allows AGENTWM to embed verifiable signals directly into the visible action trajectory while remaining indistinguishable to users. We develop an automated pipeline to generate robust watermark schemes and a rigorous statistical hypothesis testing procedure for verification. Extensive evaluations across three complex domains demonstrate that AGENTWM achieves high detection accuracy with negligible impact on agent performance. Our results confirm that AGENTWM effectively protects agentic IP against adaptive adversaries, who cannot remove the watermarks without severely degrading the stolen model's utility.

AI.tldr: AGENTWM is a watermarking framework for agentic LLMs that embeds verifiable signals in action trajectories by biasing functionally identical tool paths, enabling detection without harming performance.
AI.motivation: Agentic LLMs pose IP risk through imitation attacks; traditional watermarking fails in grey-box, reasoning-trace-obscuring settings; a robust watermark is needed for agentic systems.
AI.method: Introduce AGENTWM, which exploits semantic equivalence of action sequences by subtly biasing the distribution over functionally identical tool-execution paths; automatic pipeline to generate watermark schemes; rigorous statistical hypothesis testing for verification.
AI.result: High detection accuracy with negligible impact on agent performance; effective protection against adaptive adversaries who cannot remove watermarks without degrading utility.
AI.conclusion: First watermarking framework tailored for agentic models; demonstrates practical IP protection and resilience to adaptive attacks in complex domains.

title: Vision and language: Novel Representations and Artificial intelligence for Driving Scene Safety Assessment and Autonomous Vehicle Planning
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have recently emerged as powerful representation learning systems that align visual observations with natural language concepts, offering new opportunities for semantic reasoning in safety-critical autonomous driving. This paper investigates how vision-language representations support driving scene safety assessment and decision-making when integrated into perception, prediction, and planning pipelines. We study three complementary system-level use cases. First, we introduce a lightweight, category-agnostic hazard screening approach leveraging CLIP-based image-text similarity to produce a low-latency semantic hazard signal. This enables robust detection of diverse and out-of-distribution road hazards without explicit object detection or visual question answering. Second, we examine the integration of scene-level vision-language embeddings into a transformer-based trajectory planning framework using the Waymo Open Dataset. Our results show that naively conditioning planners on global embeddings does not improve trajectory accuracy, highlighting the importance of representation-task alignment and motivating the development of task-informed extraction methods for safety-critical planning. Third, we investigate natural language as an explicit behavioral constraint on motion planning using the doScenes dataset. In this setting, passenger-style instructions grounded in visual scene elements suppress rare but severe planning failures and improve safety-aligned behavior in ambiguous scenarios. Taken together, these findings demonstrate that vision-language representations hold significant promise for autonomous driving safety when used to express semantic risk, intent, and behavioral constraints. Realizing this potential is fundamentally an engineering problem requiring careful system design and structured grounding rather than direct feature injection.

AI.tldr: Three system-level studies explore CLIP-based hazard screening, scene-level Vision-Language Model (VLM) embeddings in planning, and natural-language constraints on planning for autonomous driving. Benefits arise only with task-aligned grounding; naive embedding conditioning underperforms, underscoring engineering requirements for practical gains.
AI.motivation: To enhance safety-critical autonomous driving by leveraging cross-modal semantic representations that capture risk, intent, and constraints beyond object-centric cues, and to address out-of-distribution hazards and planning failures.
AI.method: 1) Hazard screening using CLIP-based image-text similarity to produce a fast semantic hazard signal without explicit object detectors. 2) Incorporation of global VLM embeddings into a transformer-based trajectory planner evaluated on the Waymo Open Dataset, testing naïve vs. aligned extraction. 3) Use of natural language as explicit behavioral constraints on planning via the doScenes dataset, assessing impact on safety in ambiguous scenarios.
AI.result: 1) CLIP-based hazard signaling provides low-latency semantic hazard cues capable of detecting diverse, out-of-distribution hazards. 2) Naively conditioning planners on global embeddings does not improve trajectory accuracy, highlighting a misalignment between generic embeddings and planning tasks. 3) Natural-language constraints grounded in visual scenes suppress rare but severe planning failures and improve safety-aligned behavior in ambiguity. Overall, efficacy depends on task-informed grounding and system design rather than direct feature injection.
AI.conclusion: Vision-language representations hold promise for autonomous driving safety, especially for conveying semantic risk, intent, and behavioral constraints. Realizing this requires careful system design and structured grounding—development of task-informed extraction methods and grounded representations—rather than simple embedding injection into perception, prediction, or planning pipelines.

title: Surprisal-Guided Selection: Compute-Optimal Test-Time Strategies for Execution-Grounded Code Generation
summary: Test-time training (TTT) adapts language models through gradient-based updates at inference. But is adaptation the right strategy? We study compute-optimal test-time strategies for verifiable execution-grounded (VEG) tasks, domains like GPU kernel optimization where a deterministic evaluator provides dense, continuous reward signals. Using KernelBench as our testbed and a 120B-parameter model (GPT-OSS-120B with LoRA adaptation), we find that search outperforms minimal adaptation (1-5 gradient steps): Best-of-N sampling achieves 90% task success (18/20 tasks) at K=64 across the full KernelBench L1 eval set while TTT's best checkpoint reaches only 30.6% (3-seed mean), with TTT's "equivalent K" falling below 1, worse than single-sample inference. The failure mode is over-sharpening: gradient updates collapse diversity toward mediocre solutions rather than discovering optimal ones. Our main contribution is surprisal-guided selection: selecting the highest-surprisal (lowest-confidence) correct sample yields 80% success vs. 50% for most-confident selection, a 30% improvement. Extending to surprisal-guided-top3 matches oracle performance at 100%. This zero-cost strategy, validated through length-controlled analysis, recovers oracle performance. For dense-reward VEG tasks, compute should be allocated to sample diversity and intelligent selection rather than gradient adaptation. The surprisal-guided selection principle may generalize to other execution-grounded domains where optimal solutions occupy the distribution tail.

AI.tldr: Search-based sampling with surprisal-guided selection outperforms test-time training for dense-reward verifiable execution-grounded tasks; a zero-cost, tail-sampling strategy achieves near-oracle performance, suggesting compute should favor sample diversity over gradient adaptation.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether gradient-based adaptation (test-time training, TTT) is the optimal use of compute for verifiable execution-grounded tasks with dense rewards, using KernelBench and a large language model with LoRA, to test if adaptation outperforms smarter sampling and selection.
AI.method: Empirical study on KernelBench using a 120B GPT-OSS model with LoRA. Compare TTT (1–5 gradient steps) to Best-of-N sampling (vary N, including K=64). Introduce surprisal-guided selection (choose highest-surprisal, i.e., lowest-confidence correct sample) and surprisal-guided-top3. Conduct length-controlled analysis to validate zero-cost strategy. Measure task success rate across 20 tasks.
AI.result: Best-of-N sampling with N=64 achieves 90% success (18/20 tasks) on the KernelBench L1 eval set, while TTT best checkpoint achieves 30.6% (3-seed mean). Equivalent K is <1, worse than single-sample inference. Over-sharpening (gradient updates collapsing diversity) identified as failure mode. Surprisal-guided selection yields 80% success vs 50% for most-confident selection. Surprisal-guided-top3 matches oracle performance at 100%. The approach is zero-cost and, in length-controlled analysis, recovers oracle performance.
AI.conclusion: For dense-reward VEG tasks, allocate compute to sampling diversity and intelligent selection (surprisal-guided) rather than gradient adaptation. The surprisal-guided principle may generalize to other execution-grounded domains where optimal solutions lie in distribution tails.

title: From Assistant to Double Agent: Formalizing and Benchmarking Attacks on OpenClaw for Personalized Local AI Agent
summary: Although large language model (LLM)-based agents, exemplified by OpenClaw, are increasingly evolving from task-oriented systems into personalized AI assistants for solving complex real-world tasks, their practical deployment also introduces severe security risks. However, existing agent security research and evaluation frameworks primarily focus on synthetic or task-centric settings, and thus fail to accurately capture the attack surface and risk propagation mechanisms of personalized agents in real-world deployments. To address this gap, we propose Personalized Agent Security Bench (PASB), an end-to-end security evaluation framework tailored for real-world personalized agents. Building upon existing agent attack paradigms, PASB incorporates personalized usage scenarios, realistic toolchains, and long-horizon interactions, enabling black-box, end-to-end security evaluation on real systems. Using OpenClaw as a representative case study, we systematically evaluate its security across multiple personalized scenarios, tool capabilities, and attack types. Our results indicate that OpenClaw exhibits critical vulnerabilities at different execution stages, including user prompt processing, tool usage, and memory retrieval, highlighting substantial security risks in personalized agent deployments. The code for the proposed PASB framework is available at https://github.com/AstorYH/PASB.

AI.tldr: Introduces PASB, an end-to-end security evaluation framework for real-world personalized LLM-based agents, demonstrated on OpenClaw. It uncovers vulnerabilities across prompt processing, tools, and memory retrieval, highlighting substantial deployment risks and providing code for replication.
AI.motivation: As LLM-based agents increasingly function as personalized assistants, there is a gap in security evaluation frameworks that capture real-world attack surfaces and risk propagation in personalized deployments; existing work is largely synthetic or task-centric.
AI.method: Extend existing agent-attacks paradigms into a real-world, personalized setting by (i) modeling personalized usage scenarios, (ii) using realistic toolchains and long-horizon interactions, and (iii) enabling black-box, end-to-end evaluation on real systems. The OpenClaw system is used as a representative case study.
AI.result: PASB systematically evaluates security across personalized scenarios, tool capabilities, and attack types, revealing critical vulnerabilities at multiple execution stages (user prompt processing, tool usage, memory retrieval) in OpenClaw; demonstrates the framework’s ability to uncover substantive security risks.
AI.conclusion: PASB enables realistic, end-to-end security benchmarking for personalized agents in real deployments and can be extended to other systems; it underscores the need for robust defenses and process for securing personalized AI assistants; the framework code is publicly available.

title: Process-of-Thought Reasoning for Videos
summary: Video understanding requires not only recognizing visual content but also performing temporally grounded, multi-step reasoning over long and noisy observations. We propose Process-of-Thought (PoT) Reasoning for Videos, a framework that makes the reasoning process explicit by structuring video inference into a sequence of lightweight, verifiable steps. PoT interleaves (i) temporal evidence selection, (ii) step-wise state updates, and (iii) constrained answer synthesis, enabling the model to progressively refine hypotheses while maintaining traceability to video evidence. The framework is designed to be model-agnostic and can be plugged into existing vision-language backbones, supporting both closed-book reasoning and evidence-augmented reasoning with external tools. We further introduce a unified representation for PoT traces that aligns intermediate decisions with temporal segments, which improves robustness to distractors and reduces hallucinated explanations. Extensive experiments on standard video reasoning tasks demonstrate that PoT consistently improves factual correctness and temporal grounding, while providing interpretable reasoning traces for diagnosis and downstream use.

AI.tldr: A model-agnostic framework (PoT) that makes video reasoning explicit via a sequence of verifiable steps, improving factual accuracy and temporal grounding while reducing hallucinations by aligning intermediate decisions with video segments.
AI.motivation: Video understanding requires temporally grounded, multi-step reasoning over noisy, long observations. There is a need for traceable, editable reasoning to improve factual correctness and robustness to distractors.
AI.method: PoT Reasoning interleaves: (i) temporal evidence selection, (ii) step-wise state updates, (iii) constrained answer synthesis. It is model-agnostic, plugs into vision-language backbones, supports closed-book and evidence-augmented reasoning with external tools, and introduces a unified PoT-trace representation aligned with temporal segments to improve robustness and reduce hallucinations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard video reasoning tasks show consistent improvements in factual correctness and temporal grounding, with interpretable PoT traces that aid diagnosis and downstream use.
AI.conclusion: PoT provides a robust, interpretable, and flexible framework for video reasoning that can be integrated with existing backbones and extended with tools, though empirical validation across diverse tasks and datasets will further verify generalization and scalability.

title: Federated Learning with Profile Mapping under Distribution Shifts and Drifts
summary: Federated Learning (FL) enables decentralized model training across clients without sharing raw data, but its performance degrades under real-world data heterogeneity. Existing methods often fail to address distribution shift across clients and distribution drift over time, or they rely on unrealistic assumptions such as known number of client clusters and data heterogeneity types, which limits their generalizability. We introduce Feroma, a novel FL framework that explicitly handles both distribution shift and drift without relying on client or cluster identity. Feroma builds on client distribution profiles-compact, privacy-preserving representations of local data-that guide model aggregation and test-time model assignment through adaptive similarity-based weighting. This design allows Feroma to dynamically select aggregation strategies during training, ranging from clustered to personalized, and deploy suitable models to unseen, and unlabeled test clients without retraining, online adaptation, or prior knowledge on clients' data. Extensive experiments show that compared to 10 state-of-the-art methods, Feroma improves performance and stability under dynamic data heterogeneity conditions-an average accuracy gain of up to 12 percentage points over the best baselines across 6 benchmarks-while maintaining computational and communication overhead comparable to FedAvg. These results highlight that distribution-profile-based aggregation offers a practical path toward robust FL under both data distribution shifts and drifts.

AI.tldr: Feroma: a federated learning framework that handles distribution shift and drift without prior client or cluster labels, using privacy-preserving distribution profiles to guide adaptive aggregation and test-time model assignment, achieving up to 12pp accuracy gains with overhead comparable to FedAvg.
AI.motivation: Real-world FL suffers from non-iid data, distribution changes over time, and unknown/unstable client clusters; existing methods rely on assumptions about clusters or heterogeneity types that hinder generalization.
AI.method: Introduce distribution profiles—compact, privacy-preserving local-data representations—that inform aggregation weights and test-time model assignment via similarity measures. Feroma dynamically selects aggregation strategies (clustered to personalized) during training and can deploy suitable models to unseen/unlabeled test clients without retraining, online adaptation, or prior client knowledge.
AI.result: Across 6 benchmarks, Feroma yields average accuracy gains up to 12 percentage points over 10 state-of-the-art baselines, with computational/communication overhead comparable to FedAvg.
AI.conclusion: Distribution-profile-based aggregation provides a practical path toward robust FL under both data distribution shifts and drifts, enabling adaptive, label-free deployment to unseen clients.

title: When Evaluation Becomes a Side Channel: Regime Leakage and Structural Mitigations for Alignment Assessment
summary: Safety evaluation for advanced AI systems implicitly assumes that behavior observed under evaluation is predictive of behavior in deployment. This assumption becomes fragile for agents with situational awareness, which may exploitregime leakage-informational cues distinguishing evaluation from deployment-to implement conditional policies such as sycophancy and sleeper agents, which preserve compliance under oversight while defecting in deployment-like regimes. We reframe alignment evaluation as a problem of information flow under partial observability. Within this framework, we show that divergence between evaluation-time and deployment-time behavior is bounded by the mutual information between internal representations and the regime variable. Motivated by this result, we study regime-blind mechanisms: training-time interventions that reduce the extractability of regime information at decision-relevant internal representations via adversarial invariance. We evaluate this approach on a base, open-weight language model across two fully characterized failure modes -scientific sycophancy and temporal sleeper agents. Regime-blind training suppresses regime-conditioned behavior in both evaluated cases without measurable loss of task utility, but with qualitatively different dynamics: sycophancy exhibits a sharp representational and behavioral transition at low intervention strength, whereas sleeper-agent behavior requires substantially stronger pressure and does not exhibit a clean collapse of regime decodability. These results demonstrate that representational invariance is a meaningful but fundamentally limited control lever, whose effectiveness depends on how regime information is embedded in the policy. We argue that behavioral evaluation should be complemented with white-box diagnostics of regime awareness and information flow.

AI.tldr: Framing evaluation as information flow under partial observability; a bound shows divergence between evaluation and deployment behavior is capped by mutual information between internal representations and the deployment regime; regimes can be mitigated by regime-blind training via adversarial invariance; tested on an open-weight LLM for two failure modes (scientific sycophancy, temporal sleeper agents) with regime-blind training reducing regime-conditioned behavior without harming task utility; dynamics differ across failure modes; implies representational invariance is useful but limited and evaluation should include diagnostics of regime awareness.
AI.motivation: Make safety evaluation predictive of deployment behavior by addressing regime leakage and conditional policies; develop a method to reduce sensitivity to deployment regimes and study how information flow affects behavior under oversight.
AI.method: (1) Theoretically bound the divergence between evaluation-time and deployment-time behavior by the mutual information between internal representations and the regime variable. (2) Propose regime-blind training using adversarial invariance to reduce the extractability of regime information at decision-relevant internal representations. (3) Empirically evaluate on a base open-weight language model across two characterized failure modes: scientific sycophancy and temporal sleeper agents.
AI.result: Regime-blind training suppresses regime-conditioned behavior in both failure modes without measurable loss of task utility. Dynamics differ: sycophancy shows a sharp transition at low intervention strength; sleeper-agent behavior requires stronger pressure and lacks a clean collapse of regime decodability.
AI.conclusion: Representational invariance is a meaningful but limited control lever; the effectiveness depends on how regime information is embedded in the policy. Behavioral evaluation should be complemented with white-box diagnostics of regime awareness and information flow.

title: ElliCE: Efficient and Provably Robust Algorithmic Recourse via the Rashomon Sets
summary: Machine learning models now influence decisions that directly affect people's lives, making it important to understand not only their predictions, but also how individuals could act to obtain better results. Algorithmic recourse provides actionable input modifications to achieve more favorable outcomes, typically relying on counterfactual explanations to suggest such changes. However, when the Rashomon set - the set of near-optimal models - is large, standard counterfactual explanations can become unreliable, as a recourse action valid for one model may fail under another. We introduce ElliCE, a novel framework for robust algorithmic recourse that optimizes counterfactuals over an ellipsoidal approximation of the Rashomon set. The resulting explanations are provably valid over this ellipsoid, with theoretical guarantees on uniqueness, stability, and alignment with key feature directions. Empirically, ElliCE generates counterfactuals that are not only more robust but also more flexible, adapting to user-specified feature constraints while being substantially faster than existing baselines. This provides a principled and practical solution for reliable recourse under model uncertainty, ensuring stable recommendations for users even as models evolve.

AI.tldr: ElliCE introduces a robust recourse framework by optimizing counterfactuals over an ellipsoidal Rashomon set, yielding valid, stable, and feature-aligned explanations that adapt to constraints and run faster than baselines.
AI.motivation: Under model uncertainty with large Rashomon sets, standard counterfactuals may not transfer between near-optimal models; there is a need for recourse that remains valid across plausible models.
AI.method: Approximate the Rashomon set with an ellipsoid and solve for counterfactuals that are valid for all models within the ellipsoid; provide theoretical guarantees on uniqueness and stability; enable user-specified feature constraints.
AI.result: Counterfactuals are guaranteed valid over the ellipsoid, exhibit improved robustness and flexibility, align with key feature directions, and run substantially faster than existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: ElliCE offers a principled, practical solution for reliable recourse under model uncertainty, ensuring stable recommendations as models evolve.

title: TreeTensor: Boost AI System on Nested Data with Constrained Tree-Like Tensor
summary: Tensor is the most basic and essential data structure of nowadays artificial intelligence (AI) system. The natural properties of Tensor, especially the memory-continuity and slice-independence, make it feasible for training system to leverage parallel computing unit like GPU to process data simultaneously in batch, spatial or temporal dimensions. However, if we look beyond perception tasks, the data in a complicated cognitive AI system usually has hierarchical structures (i.e. nested data) with various modalities. They are inconvenient and inefficient to program directly with conventional Tensor with fixed shape. To address this issue, we summarize two main computational patterns of nested data, and then propose a general nested data container: TreeTensor. Through various constraints and magic utilities of TreeTensor, one can apply arbitrary functions and operations to nested data with almost zero cost, including some famous machine learning libraries, such as Scikit-Learn, Numpy and PyTorch. Our approach utilizes a constrained tree-structure perspective to systematically model data relationships, and it can also easily be combined with other methods to extend more usages, such as asynchronous execution and variable-length data computation. Detailed examples and benchmarks show TreeTensor not only provides powerful usability in various problems, especially one of the most complicated AI systems at present: AlphaStar for StarCraftII, but also exhibits excellent runtime efficiency without any overhead. Our project is available at https://github.com/opendilab/DI-treetensor.

AI.tldr: Proposes TreeTensor, a nested data container that enables operations on hierarchical/multimodal data with low overhead, integrating with common ML libraries; demonstrated on AlphaStar with promising runtime efficiency; open-source.
AI.motivation: Traditional tensors have fixed shapes, making it hard to represent and process nested/hierarchical data common in multi-modal cognitive AI systems. A general, efficient container is needed to support arbitrary functions and operations across nested structures.
AI.method: Introduce TreeTensor as a constrained tree-structure data container. Provide utilities to apply arbitrary functions and operations to nested data with minimal overhead, compatible with libraries like Scikit-Learn, NumPy, and PyTorch. Extendable with asynchronous execution and variable-length data handling. Include detailed examples and benchmarks; release code at GitHub.
AI.result: Benchmarks and examples demonstrate strong usability and runtime efficiency, with claims of negligible overhead. Notably applied to AlphaStar for StarCraft II, illustrating practical applicability in complex AI systems.
AI.conclusion: TreeTensor offers a general, composable approach to nested data, capable of integrating with existing ML ecosystems and extending to asynchronous and variable-length computation. Open-source with potential broad impact on systems that must handle hierarchical, multimodal data.

title: A hybrid Kolmogorov-Arnold network for medical image segmentation
summary: Medical image segmentation plays a vital role in diagnosis and treatment planning, but remains challenging due to the inherent complexity and variability of medical images, especially in capturing non-linear relationships within the data. We propose U-KABS, a novel hybrid framework that integrates the expressive power of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) with a U-shaped encoder-decoder architecture to enhance segmentation performance. The U-KABS model combines the convolutional and squeeze-and-excitation stage, which enhances channel-wise feature representations, and the KAN Bernstein Spline (KABS) stage, which employs learnable activation functions based on Bernstein polynomials and B-splines. This hybrid design leverages the global smoothness of Bernstein polynomials and the local adaptability of B-splines, enabling the model to effectively capture both broad contextual trends and fine-grained patterns critical for delineating complex structures in medical images. Skip connections between encoder and decoder layers support effective multi-scale feature fusion and preserve spatial details. Evaluated across diverse medical imaging benchmark datasets, U-KABS demonstrates superior performance compared to strong baselines, particularly in segmenting complex anatomical structures.

AI.tldr: Hybrid U-KABS architecture fusing Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks with U-Net for medical image segmentation, leveraging CNN+SE blocks and Bernstein/B-spline KABS activations to capture global context and local detail, achieving state-of-the-art-like performance on diverse benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the inherent non-linear complexity and variability of medical images to improve segmentation accuracy, especially for complex anatomical structures, by combining global smoothness with local adaptability.
AI.method: A U-shaped encoder-decoder framework with skip connections. The model comprises a convolutional + squeeze-and-excitation (SE) stage and a Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks Bernstein Spline (KABS) stage using learnable Bernstein polynomial and B-spline activations. The hybrid design enables capturing broad contextual trends (global) and fine-grained patterns (local).
AI.result: On diverse medical imaging benchmarks, U-KABS outperforms strong baselines, particularly in segmenting complex anatomical structures.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid integration of global KAN-based activations with local CNN-SE blocks within a U-Net framework effectively captures multi-scale context, improving segmentation performance for challenging medical images.

title: Spectral Gating Networks
summary: Gating mechanisms are ubiquitous, yet a complementary question in feed-forward networks remains under-explored: how to introduce frequency-rich expressivity without sacrificing stability and scalability? This tension is exposed by spline-based Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) parameterizations, where grid refinement can induce parameter growth and brittle optimization in high dimensions. To propose a stability-preserving way to inject spectral capacity into existing MLP/FFN layers under fixed parameter and training budgets, we introduce Spectral Gating Networks (SGN), a drop-in spectral reparameterization. SGN augments a standard activation pathway with a compact spectral pathway and learnable gates that allow the model to start from a stable base behavior and progressively allocate capacity to spectral features during training. The spectral pathway is instantiated with trainable Random Fourier Features (learned frequencies and phases), replacing grid-based splines and removing resolution dependence. A hybrid GELU-Fourier formulation further improves optimization robustness while enhancing high-frequency fidelity. Across vision, NLP, audio, and PDE benchmarks, SGN consistently improves accuracy-efficiency trade-offs under comparable computational budgets, achieving 93.15% accuracy on CIFAR-10 and up to 11.7x faster inference than spline-based KAN variants. Code and trained models will be released.

AI.tldr: Spectral Gating Networks (SGN) inject spectral expressivity into MLP/FFN layers via a compact, learnable Random Fourier Feature pathway with gates, replacing grid-based splines and removing resolution dependence. This yields stable training, improved accuracy-efficiency, and faster inference.
AI.motivation: Spline-based Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) suffer from parameter growth and brittle optimization with grid refinement in high dimensions. There is a need for frequency-rich expressivity in feed-forward networks that preserves stability and scalability under fixed parameter and budget constraints.
AI.method: Introduce SGN as a drop-in spectral reparameterization. Augment the standard activation with a compact spectral pathway governed by learnable gates. The spectral pathway uses trainable Random Fourier Features (frequencies and phases) to replace grid-based splines, removing resolution dependence. Employ a hybrid GELU-Fourier activation to improve optimization robustness and high-frequency fidelity.
AI.result: Empirically validated across vision, NLP, audio, and PDE benchmarks. SGN consistently improves accuracy-efficiency trade-offs under fixed compute budgets, achieving 93.15% on CIFAR-10 and up to 11.7x faster inference than spline-based KAN variants.
AI.conclusion: SGN provides a stable, scalable route to incorporate spectral expressivity into FFNs, enabling high-frequency capabilities with robust optimization and reduced computational burden; code and trained models will be released.

title: Reinforcement Inference: Leveraging Uncertainty for Self-Correcting Language Model Reasoning
summary:
Modern large language models (LLMs) are often evaluated and deployed under a \emph{one-shot, greedy} inference protocol, especially in professional settings that require deterministic behavior. This regime can systematically under-estimate a fixed model's true capability: many errors arise not from missing knowledge, but from premature commitment under internal ambiguity. We introduce \emph{Reinforcement Inference}, an entropy-aware inference-time control strategy that uses the model's own uncertainty to selectively invoke a second, more deliberate reasoning attempt, enabling stronger performance \emph{without any retraining}.
  On 12,032 MMLU-Pro questions across 14 subjects, using DeepSeek-v3.2 with deterministic decoding in a zero-shot setting, Reinforcement Inference improves accuracy from 60.72\% to 84.03\%, while only incurring 61.06\% additional inference calls. A 100\% re-asking ablation reaches 84.35\%, indicating that uncertainty-aware selection captures most of the attainable improvement with substantially less compute. Moreover, a \emph{prompt-only} ablation underperforms the baseline, suggesting that the gains are not explained by generic `` your output had high entropy, think step-by-step'' prompting alone.
  Beyond providing a practical inference-time upgrade, our results suggest a broader \emph{entropy-aware} paradigm for measuring and expanding model capability: because modern decoder-based models generate outputs autoregressively, entropy and related confidence measures arise naturally as first-class control signals during generation. The resulting gap between one-pass greedy inference and uncertainty-conditioned deliberation offers a diagnostic lens on an LLM's latent reasoning horizon and motivates future training objectives that explicitly constrain correctness--confidence alignment.

AI.tldr: Proposes Reinforcement Inference, an entropy-aware inference-time control that uses model uncertainty to trigger a second deliberate reasoning pass, improving zero-shot accuracy on MMLU-Pro with minimal additional calls.
AI.motivation: Address underestimation of a model's true capability under one-shot greedy inference due to early commitment under ambiguity; leverage entropy/confidence signals to extend effective reasoning horizon without retraining.
AI.method: Introduce Reinforcement Inference (RI): an entropy-aware, dynamic control strategy that selects when to invoke a second, more deliberate reasoning pass based on the model's internal uncertainty. Evaluated in a zero-shot, deterministic decoding setup (DeepSeek-v3.2) on 12,032 MMLU-Pro questions across 14 subjects. Performed ablations including 100% re-asking and a prompt-only baseline to isolate the effect of uncertainty-driven deliberation; compared against a one-pass baseline.
AI.result: Accuracy improved from 60.72% (one-pass) to 84.03% (RI with additional inference calls); additional calls amount to 61.06% more inference; a full re-asking ablation reaches 84.35%, indicating most gains come from uncertainty-driven deliberation. Prompt-only ablation underperforms, suggesting gains are not due to generic step-by-step prompting.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-aware inference reveals and leverages the latent reasoning horizon in decoder-based models. This paradigm offers a diagnostic lens on model capability and motivates training objectives that align correctness with confidence, beyond mere output token generation and without retraining.

title: All-Optical Segmentation via Diffractive Neural Networks for Autonomous Driving
summary: Semantic segmentation and lane detection are crucial tasks in autonomous driving systems. Conventional approaches predominantly rely on deep neural networks (DNNs), which incur high energy costs due to extensive analog-to-digital conversions and large-scale image computations required for low-latency, real-time responses. Diffractive optical neural networks (DONNs) have shown promising advantages over conventional DNNs on digital or optoelectronic computing platforms in energy efficiency. By performing all-optical image processing via light diffraction at the speed of light, DONNs save computation energy costs while reducing the overhead associated with analog-to-digital conversions by all-optical encoding and computing. In this work, we propose a novel all-optical computing framework for RGB image segmentation and lane detection in autonomous driving applications. Our experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the DONN system for image segmentation on the CityScapes dataset. Additionally, we conduct case studies on lane detection using a customized indoor track dataset and simulated driving scenarios in CARLA, where we further evaluate the model's generalizability under diverse environmental conditions.

AI.tldr: All-optical diffractive neural network (DONN) framework for RGB image segmentation and lane detection in autonomous driving, achieving CityScapes segmentation and lane-detection generalization in CARLA and indoor track datasets, highlighting energy efficiency and real-time potential.
AI.motivation: Reduce energy consumption and latency in autonomous driving perception by shifting computation to all-optical processing, minimizing digitization and ADC overhead.
AI.method: Proposes a DONN-based all-optical computing framework for RGB image segmentation and lane detection. Implementations perform image processing via light diffraction with all-optical encoding/computing. Experiments on CityScapes for segmentation, plus lane-detection case studies on a customized indoor track dataset and simulated CARLA scenarios to assess generalization under diverse conditions.
AI.result: Demonstrates effective image segmentation on CityScapes. Lane-detection case studies show generalization to different environments (indoor track and CARLA simulations). Indicates energy-efficient, low-latency potential due to all-optical computation and reduced ADC overhead.
AI.conclusion: DONNs offer a viable, energy-efficient approach for real-time perception in autonomous driving, with competitive segmentation performance and robust generalization for lane detection across varied environmental conditions.

title: On the Infinite Width and Depth Limits of Predictive Coding Networks
summary: Predictive coding (PC) is a biologically plausible alternative to standard backpropagation (BP) that minimises an energy function with respect to network activities before updating weights. Recent work has improved the training stability of deep PC networks (PCNs) by leveraging some BP-inspired reparameterisations. However, the full scalability and theoretical basis of these approaches remains unclear. To address this, we study the infinite width and depth limits of PCNs. For linear residual networks, we show that the set of width- and depth-stable feature-learning parameterisations for PC is exactly the same as for BP. Moreover, under any of these parameterisations, the PC energy with equilibrated activities converges to the BP loss in a regime where the model width is much larger than the depth, resulting in PC computing the same gradients as BP. Experiments show that these results hold in practice for deep nonlinear networks, as long as an activity equilibrium seem to be reached. Overall, this work unifies various previous theoretical and empirical results and has potentially important implications for the scaling of PCNs.

AI.tldr: In the infinite width and depth limits, the width- and depth-stable parameterisations for predictive coding networks (PCNs) exactly match those of backpropagation (BP) for linear residual networks; with equilibrated activities, the PC energy converges to BP loss in the width-dominant regime, so PC computes the same gradients as BP. This behavior extends to deep nonlinear networks in practice when activity equilibrium is achieved.
AI.motivation: To establish the theoretical foundations and scalability of predictive coding as an alternative learning framework to BP by characterizing its parameterisations and limit behavior.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of PCNs in the infinite-width and infinite-depth limits, focusing on linear residual networks to characterize width- and depth-stable parameterisations; analysis of energy convergence to BP loss under equilibrated activities; empirical verification on deep nonlinear networks when equilibrium is reached.
AI.result: The set of width- and depth-stable PC parameterisations coincides with BP for linear residual networks; under these parameterisations, PC energy with equilibrated activities converges to BP loss in a width-dominant regime, causing PC to compute the same gradients as BP; experiments indicate the results extend to deep nonlinear networks provided activity equilibrium is achieved.
AI.conclusion: This work unifies prior theoretical and empirical findings and has implications for scaling predictive coding networks, supporting PC as a viable alternative to BP in large-scale settings given appropriate parameterisations and activity equilibrium.

title: Dialogue Model Optimization via Agent Game and Adaptive Tree-based GRPO
summary: Open-ended dialogue agents aim to deliver engaging, personalized interactions by adapting to users' traits, but existing methods face critical limitations: over-reliance on pre-collected user data, and short-horizon biases in reinforcement learning (RL) that neglect long-term dialogue value. To address these, we propose a novel long-horizon RL framework integrating online personalization with Adaptive Tree-based Group Relative Policy Optimization (AT-GRPO). Adopting a two-agent game paradigm, a user agent constructs dynamic environments via style mimicry (learning user-specific conversational traits) and active termination (predicting turn-level termination probabilities as immediate rewards), forming an iterative cycle that drives the dialogue agent to deepen interest exploration. AT-GRPO reinterprets dialogue trajectories as trees and introduces adaptive observation ranges. Unlike full tree expansion that incurs exponential overhead, it limits each node to aggregate rewards from a stage-aware range: larger ranges support early-stage topic exploration, while smaller ranges facilitate late-stage dialogue maintenance. This design reduces rollout budgets from exponential to polynomial in the dialogue length, while preserving long-term reward capture. Extensive experiments show our framework's superior performance, sample efficiency, and robustness.

AI.tldr: A long-horizon RL framework for open-ended dialogue using online personalization and Adaptive Tree-based GRPO (AT-GRPO), featuring a two-agent user–dialogue game and adaptive tree trajectories to balance exploration and maintenance, reducing rollout complexity from exponential to polynomial; demonstrated superior performance, sample efficiency, and robustness.
AI.motivation: Overcome reliance on pre-collected user data and short-horizon RL biases in dialogue agents by enabling online personalization and long-term value optimization.
AI.method: Propose a two-agent game where a user agent creates dynamic environments via style mimicry and active termination rewards; develop AT-GRPO that treats dialogue trajectories as trees with adaptive observation ranges, limiting node expansion to stage-aware reward aggregation to manage computation (polynomial instead of exponential).
AI.result: Extensive experiments report superior performance, better sample efficiency, and robustness of the proposed framework compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Integrating online personalization with adaptive tree-based policy optimization yields improved long-horizon dialogue value and efficiency; the approach balances early topic exploration and late-stage dialogue maintenance via stage-aware rollout design.

title: PAND: Prompt-Aware Neighborhood Distillation for Lightweight Fine-Grained Visual Classification
summary: Distilling knowledge from large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) into lightweight networks is crucial yet challenging in Fine-Grained Visual Classification (FGVC), due to the reliance on fixed prompts and global alignment. To address this, we propose PAND (Prompt-Aware Neighborhood Distillation), a two-stage framework that decouples semantic calibration from structural transfer. First, we incorporate Prompt-Aware Semantic Calibration to generate adaptive semantic anchors. Second, we introduce a neighborhood-aware structural distillation strategy to constrain the student's local decision structure. PAND consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on four FGVC benchmarks. Notably, our ResNet-18 student achieves 76.09% accuracy on CUB-200, surpassing the strong baseline VL2Lite by 3.4%. Code is available at https://github.com/LLLVTA/PAND.

AI.tldr: PAND distills knowledge from large Vision-Language Models into lightweight FGVC models via two stages: prompt-aware semantic calibration and neighborhood-aware structural distillation, achieving state-of-the-art results (e.g., ResNet-18 on CUB-200: 76.09%).
AI.motivation: The FGVC task requires effective transfer from large VLMs but is hindered by fixed prompts and global alignment; a decoupled approach separating semantic calibration from structural transfer can better leverage VLMs for fine-grained recognition.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) Prompt-Aware Semantic Calibration to produce adaptive semantic anchors; (2) Neighborhood-aware Structural Distillation to constrain the student's local decision structure, separating semantic calibration from structural transfer.
AI.result: PAND consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on four FGVC benchmarks; notably, a ResNet-18 student achieves 76.09% on CUB-200, surpassing VL2Lite by 3.4%.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling semantic calibration from structural distillation enables more effective knowledge transfer from VLMs to lightweight FGVC models, yielding strong empirical gains; code is available online.

title: Dense Feature Learning via Linear Structure Preservation in Medical Data
summary: Deep learning models for medical data are typically trained using task specific objectives that encourage representations to collapse onto a small number of discriminative directions. While effective for individual prediction problems, this paradigm underutilizes the rich structure of clinical data and limits the transferability, stability, and interpretability of learned features. In this work, we propose dense feature learning, a representation centric framework that explicitly shapes the linear structure of medical embeddings. Our approach operates directly on embedding matrices, encouraging spectral balance, subspace consistency, and feature orthogonality through objectives defined entirely in terms of linear algebraic properties. Without relying on labels or generative reconstruction, dense feature learning produces representations with higher effective rank, improved conditioning, and greater stability across time. Empirical evaluations across longitudinal EHR data, clinical text, and multimodal patient representations demonstrate consistent improvements in downstream linear performance, robustness, and subspace alignment compared to supervised and self supervised baselines. These results suggest that learning to span clinical variation may be as important as learning to predict clinical outcomes, and position representation geometry as a first class objective in medical AI.

AI.tldr: Dense feature learning reframes medical representation learning from prediction-centric to geometry-centric, improving rank, conditioning, and subspace alignment without labels, yielding better linear downstream performance.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning for medical data relies on task-specific objectives that cause representations to collapse onto few discriminative directions. This underutilizes clinical data structure, limiting transferability, stability, and interpretability. A representation-centric objective could capture broader clinical variation.
AI.method: Introduce dense feature learning that operates on embedding matrices and enforces linear-algebraic properties (spectral balance, subspace consistency, feature orthogonality). The objectives are defined purely in terms of linear algebra, with no reliance on labels or generative reconstruction.
AI.result: Across longitudinal EHR data, clinical text, and multimodal patient representations, the approach yields consistent improvements in downstream linear performance, robustness, and subspace alignment versus supervised and self-supervised baselines.
AI.conclusion: Learning to span clinical variation via representation geometry can be as important as predicting outcomes; representation geometry should be a first-class objective in medical AI.

title: Rolling Sink: Bridging Limited-Horizon Training and Open-Ended Testing in Autoregressive Video Diffusion
comment: Figure PDFs were compressed to 150 dpi to comply with arXiv's submission size limit. Project page: https://rolling-sink.github.io/
summary: Recently, autoregressive (AR) video diffusion models has achieved remarkable performance. However, due to their limited training durations, a train-test gap emerges when testing at longer horizons, leading to rapid visual degradations. Following Self Forcing, which studies the train-test gap within the training duration, this work studies the train-test gap beyond the training duration, i.e., the gap between the limited horizons during training and open-ended horizons during testing. Since open-ended testing can extend beyond any finite training window, and long-video training is computationally expensive, we pursue a training-free solution to bridge this gap. To explore a training-free solution, we conduct a systematic analysis of AR cache maintenance. These insights lead to Rolling Sink. Built on Self Forcing (trained on only 5s clips), Rolling Sink effectively scales the AR video synthesis to ultra-long durations (e.g., 5-30 minutes at 16 FPS) at test time, with consistent subjects, stable colors, coherent structures, and smooth motions. As demonstrated by extensive experiments, Rolling Sink achieves superior long-horizon visual fidelity and temporal consistency compared to SOTA baselines. Project page: https://rolling-sink.github.io/

AI.tldr: Rolling Sink provides a training-free, long-horizon AR video synthesis method by exploiting AR cache maintenance insights; extends self-forcing trained on 5s clips to 5–30 minute test videos (16 FPS), outperforming SOTA baselines.
AI.motivation: There is a train-test gap beyond the training duration in autoregressive video diffusion. Long video training is expensive, so a training-free solution is sought to bridge the gap.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of autoregressive cache management; derive Rolling Sink based on Self Forcing (trained on 5s clips); apply at test time to sustain fidelity across ultra-long horizons without additional training.
AI.result: Rolling Sink achieves superior long-horizon visual fidelity and temporal consistency compared to SOTA baselines, producing 5–30 minute videos at 16 FPS with consistent subjects, stable colors, coherent structures, and smooth motions; validated by extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: Rolling Sink offers a practical training-free approach to bridge the train-test gap beyond training durations in AR video diffusion, enabling ultra-long video synthesis and potentially setting a new baseline for long-horizon generation.

title: Quantifying Explanation Quality in Graph Neural Networks using Out-of-Distribution Generalization
summary: Evaluating the quality of post-hoc explanations for Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) remains a significant challenge. While recent years have seen an increasing development of explainability methods, current evaluation metrics (e.g., fidelity, sparsity) often fail to assess whether an explanation identifies the true underlying causal variables. To address this, we propose the Explanation-Generalization Score (EGS), a metric that quantifies the causal relevance of GNN explanations. EGS is founded on the principle of feature invariance and posits that if an explanation captures true causal drivers, it should lead to stable predictions across distribution shifts. To quantify this, we introduce a framework that trains GNNs using explanatory subgraphs and evaluates their performance in Out-of-Distribution (OOD) settings (here, OOD generalization serves as a rigorous proxy for the explanation's causal validity). Through large-scale validation involving 11,200 model combinations across synthetic and real-world datasets, our results demonstrate that EGS provides a principled benchmark for ranking explainers based on their ability to capture causal substructures, offering a robust alternative to traditional fidelity-based metrics.

AI.tldr: Introduces the Explanation-Generalization Score (EGS) to evaluate GNN explanations by measuring causal relevance through Out-of-Distribution (OOD) generalization; validated with 11,200 model configurations on synthetic and real datasets; claims to outperform fidelity-based metrics in ranking explainers.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation metrics (e.g., fidelity, sparsity) do not quantify whether an explanation identifies true causal drivers. There is a need for a metric tied to causal validity that remains reliable under distribution shifts.
AI.method: Proposes a framework that uses explanatory subgraphs to train GNNs and assesses their performance under OOD settings. Leverages the feature-invariance principle: explanations revealing true causal drivers should yield stable predictions when distributions shift. Conducts large-scale validation across 11,200 model combinations on both synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.result: EGS provides a principled benchmark for ranking explainers based on their ability to capture causal substructures. It offers a robust alternative to fidelity-based metrics and demonstrates that explanations aligned with causal substructures produce more stable (OOD) performance.
AI.conclusion: EGS constitutes a rigorous, scalable metric for the causal validity of GNN explanations, advocating the use of OOD generalization as a proxy for causal relevance and encouraging evaluation beyond traditional fidelity metrics.

title: An Attention Mechanism for Robust Multimodal Integration in a Global Workspace Architecture
summary: Global Workspace Theory (GWT), inspired by cognitive neuroscience, posits that flexible cognition could arise via the attentional selection of a relevant subset of modalities within a multimodal integration system. This cognitive framework can inspire novel computational architectures for multimodal integration. Indeed, recent implementations of GWT have explored its multimodal representation capabilities, but the related attention mechanisms remain understudied. Here, we propose and evaluate a top-down attention mechanism to select modalities inside a global workspace. First, we demonstrate that our attention mechanism improves noise robustness of a global workspace system on two multimodal datasets of increasing complexity: Simple Shapes and MM-IMDb 1.0. Second, we highlight various cross-task and cross-modality generalization capabilities that are not shared by multimodal attention models from the literature. Comparing against existing baselines on the MM-IMDb 1.0 benchmark, we find our attention mechanism makes the global workspace competitive with the state of the art.

AI.tldr: Top-down modality selection within a Global Workspace Theory–inspired multimodal architecture improves robustness and generalization, achieving competitive performance on MM-IMDb 1.0.
AI.motivation: To enhance multimodal integration by integrating a top-down attention mechanism inside a global workspace, addressing gaps in existing attention models and improving noise robustness and cross-task generalization.
AI.method: Introduce a top-down attention mechanism for modality selection inside a global workspace. Evaluate on Simple Shapes and MM-IMDb 1.0 datasets; compare against baselines on MM-IMDb 1.0.
AI.result: Increases noise robustness for the global workspace across both datasets; demonstrates cross-task and cross-modality generalization not shared by existing multimodal attention models; achieves competitive performance with state-of-the-art baselines on MM-IMDb 1.0.
AI.conclusion: A top-down modality-focused attention mechanism within a GWT improves robustness and generalization in multimodal tasks and can rival current state-of-the-art approaches on standard benchmarks.

title: Integrating Specialized and Generic Agent Motion Prediction with Dynamic Occupancy Grid Maps
comment: Updated version with major revisions; currently under the second round of review at IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Vehicles
