summary: Accurate prediction of driving scene is a challenging task due to uncertainty in sensor data, the complex behaviors of agents, and the possibility of multiple feasible futures. Existing prediction methods using occupancy grid maps primarily focus on agent-agnostic scene predictions, while agent-specific predictions provide specialized behavior insights with the help of semantic information. However, both paradigms face distinct limitations: agent-agnostic models struggle to capture the behavioral complexities of dynamic actors, whereas agent-specific approaches fail to generalize to poorly perceived or unrecognized agents; combining both enables robust and safer motion forecasting. To address this, we propose a unified framework by leveraging Dynamic Occupancy Grid Maps within a streamlined temporal decoding pipeline to simultaneously predict future occupancy state grids, vehicle grids, and scene flow grids. Relying on a lightweight spatiotemporal backbone, our approach is centered on a tailored, interdependent loss function that captures inter-grid dependencies and enables diverse future predictions. By using occupancy state information to enforce flow-guided transitions, the loss function acts as a regularizer that directs occupancy evolution while accounting for obstacles and occlusions. Consequently, the model not only predicts the specific behaviors of vehicle agents, but also identifies other dynamic entities and anticipates their evolution within the complex scene. Evaluations on real-world nuScenes and Woven Planet datasets demonstrate superior prediction performances for dynamic vehicles and generic dynamic scene elements compared to baseline methods.

AI.tldr: A unified Dynamic Occupancy Grid Map (DOGM) framework jointly predicts future occupancy, vehicle-specific grids, and scene flow using a regularized inter-grid loss, yielding improved dynamic predictions on nuScenes and Woven Planet.
AI.motivation: Combine agent-agnostic occupancy predictions with agent-specific semantic insights to overcome respective limitations in dynamic scene forecasting and improve robustness to poorly perceived/unrecognized agents.
AI.method: A lightweight spatiotemporal backbone with a temporal decoding pipeline predicts future occupancy state grids, vehicle grids, and scene flow grids. An interdependent loss captures relationships between grids and uses occupancy-derived flow guidance to regularize evolution, accounting for obstacles and occlusions.
AI.result: The approach achieves superior predictions for dynamic vehicles and generic dynamic scene elements compared with baselines on real-world nuScenes and Woven Planet datasets.
AI.conclusion: Coupling occupancy dynamics with agent-specific semantic predictions via a flow-guided, inter-grid regularizer improves forecasting robustness, generalization, and safety in complex driving scenes.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Counterfactual Traffic Signal Control with Predictive Safety and Starvation-Avoidance Constraints Using Vision-Based Sensing
summary: Real-world deployment of adaptive traffic signal control, to date, remains limited due to the uncertainty associated with vision-based perception, implicit safety, and non-interpretable control policies learned and validated mainly in simulation. In this paper, we introduce UCATSC, a model-based traffic signal control system that models traffic signal control at an intersection using a stochastic decision process with constraints and under partial observability, taking into account the uncertainty associated with vision-based perception. Unlike reinforcement learning methods that learn to predict safety using reward shaping, UCATSC predicts and enforces hard constraints related to safety and starvation prevention during counterfactual rollouts in belief space. The system is designed to improve traffic delay and emission while preventing safety-critical errors and providing interpretable control policy outputs based on explicit models.

AI.tldr: A model-based, partially observable traffic signal control system (UCATSC) uses a stochastic decision process with explicit safety constraints to handle vision uncertainty, providing interpretable control outputs and hard constraint enforcement via belief-space rollouts instead of reward-shaped RL.
AI.motivation: Real-world adaptive traffic signal control is hindered by vision-based perception uncertainty, safety concerns, and non-interpretable policies learned in simulation; a safe, interpretable, model-based approach is needed.
AI.method: UCATSC formalizes intersection control as a stochastic decision process under partial observability. It enforces hard safety and starvation constraints during counterfactual rollouts in belief space, rather than relying on reward shaping. The system relies on explicit models to predict outcomes and generate interpretable control actions.
AI.result: The approach aims to improve traffic delay and emissions, while preventing safety-critical errors and yielding interpretable policy outputs.
AI.conclusion: A model-based, constraint-enforced, belief-space planning framework (UCATSC) offers a safe, interpretable alternative to RL for vision-based adaptive signal control under perception uncertainty, with potential real-world applicability beyond simulation.

title: Towards Robust Scaling Laws for Optimizers
summary: The quality of Large Language Model (LLM) pretraining depends on multiple factors, including the compute budget and the choice of optimization algorithm. Empirical scaling laws are widely used to predict loss as model size and training data grow, however, almost all existing studies fix the optimizer (typically AdamW). At the same time, a new generation of optimizers (e.g., Muon, Shampoo, SOAP) promises faster and more stable convergence, but their relationship with model and data scaling is not yet well understood. In this work, we study scaling laws across different optimizers. Empirically, we show that 1) separate Chinchilla-style scaling laws for each optimizer are ill-conditioned and have highly correlated parameters. Instead, 2) we propose a more robust law with shared power-law exponents and optimizer-specific rescaling factors, which enable direct comparison between optimizers. Finally, 3) we provide a theoretical analysis of gradient-based methods for the proxy task of a convex quadratic objective, demonstrating that Chinchilla-style scaling laws emerge naturally as a result of loss decomposition into irreducible, approximation, and optimization errors.

AI.tldr: Optimizer choice materially affects LLM pretraining scaling laws; a unified law with shared exponents and optimizer-specific rescalings enables cross-optimizer comparison; theory shows scaling emerges from loss error decomposition.
AI.motivation: Existing scaling studies fix the optimizer; new optimizers promise faster convergence but scaling behavior under different optimizers is unclear. There is a need for robust, comparable scaling laws that account for optimizer variability.
AI.method: Empirical analysis across multiple optimizers (e.g., AdamW, Muon, Shampoo, SOAP) on scaling tasks; fit Chinchilla-style laws separately; propose a law with shared exponents and optimizer-specific rescalings; provide convex quadratic proxy analysis to derive the law via loss decomposition.
AI.result: Separate optimizer-specific scaling laws are ill-conditioned and highly correlated; proposed robust law with shared exponents and per-optimizer rescalings enables direct comparison; theoretical analysis shows Chinchilla-style scaling arises from irreducible, approximation, and optimization errors in a convex quadratic proxy.
AI.conclusion: Cross-optimizer, exponent-shared scaling laws offer stable comparisons and explain observed scaling; insights inform optimizer selection and compute budgeting for pretraining; suggests a path to unify scaling theory across optimizers.

title: OSCAR: Optimization-Steered Agentic Planning for Composed Image Retrieval
summary: Composed image retrieval (CIR) requires complex reasoning over heterogeneous visual and textual constraints. Existing approaches largely fall into two paradigms: unified embedding retrieval, which suffers from single-model myopia, and heuristic agentic retrieval, which is limited by suboptimal, trial-and-error orchestration. To this end, we propose OSCAR, an optimization-steered agentic planning framework for composed image retrieval. We are the first to reformulate agentic CIR from a heuristic search process into a principled trajectory optimization problem. Instead of relying on heuristic trial-and-error exploration, OSCAR employs a novel offline-online paradigm. In the offline phase, we model CIR via atomic retrieval selection and composition as a two-stage mixed-integer programming problem, mathematically deriving optimal trajectories that maximize ground-truth coverage for training samples via rigorous boolean set operations. These trajectories are then stored in a golden library to serve as in-context demonstrations for online steering of VLM planner at online inference time. Extensive experiments on three public benchmarks and a private industrial benchmark show that OSCAR consistently outperforms SOTA baselines. Notably, it achieves superior performance using only 10% of training data, demonstrating strong generalization of planning logic rather than dataset-specific memorization.

AI.tldr: OSCAR reframes CIR as trajectory optimization with an offline–online planning workflow, delivering state-of-the-art results with limited data by leveraging a golden library of optimized retrieval trajectories for online VLM planning.
AI.motivation: Existing CIR approaches suffer from either single-model myopia (unified embeddings) or suboptimal heuristic search (agentic retrieval). A principled optimization of retrieval trajectories can integrate constraints and improve generalization.
AI.method: Offline: formulate CIR as a two-stage mixed-integer program over atomic retrieval selection and composition, deriving optimal trajectories that maximize ground-truth coverage via boolean set operations; store them in a golden library as in-context demonstrations. Online: use the golden library to steer a VLM-based planner at inference time.
AI.result: On three public benchmarks plus a private industrial benchmark, OSCAR outperforms SOTA baselines, and achieves superior performance using only 10% of training data, indicating planning-logic generalization rather than memorization.
AI.conclusion: The offline–online trajectory optimization paradigm with a golden library provides effective, data-efficient CIR with robust generalization across datasets.

title: ForecastOcc: Vision-based Semantic Occupancy Forecasting
summary: Autonomous driving requires forecasting both geometry and semantics over time to effectively reason about future environment states. Existing vision-based occupancy forecasting methods focus on motion-related categories such as static and dynamic objects, while semantic information remains largely absent. Recent semantic occupancy forecasting approaches address this gap but rely on past occupancy predictions obtained from separate networks. This makes current methods sensitive to error accumulation and prevents learning spatio-temporal features directly from images. In this work, we present ForecastOcc, the first framework for vision-based semantic occupancy forecasting that jointly predicts future occupancy states and semantic categories. Our framework yields semantic occupancy forecasts for multiple horizons directly from past camera images, without relying on externally estimated maps. We evaluate ForecastOcc in two complementary settings: multi-view forecasting on the Occ3D-nuScenes dataset and monocular forecasting on SemanticKITTI, where we establish the first benchmark for this task. We introduce the first baselines by adapting two 2D forecasting modules within our framework. Importantly, we propose a novel architecture that incorporates a temporal cross-attention forecasting module, a 2D-to-3D view transformer, a 3D encoder for occupancy prediction, and a semantic occupancy head for voxel-level forecasts across multiple horizons. Extensive experiments on both datasets show that ForecastOcc consistently outperforms baselines, yielding semantically rich, future-aware predictions that capture scene dynamics and semantics critical for autonomous driving.

AI.tldr: ForecastOcc is a vision-based framework that jointly forecasts future semantic occupancy from past camera images for multiple horizons, without maps, using a temporal cross-attention module, a 2D-to-3D view transformer, and a 3D occupancy encoder with a semantic head; evaluated on Occ3D-nuScenes and SemanticKITTI, it outperforms baselines.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of joint geometry-and-semantics forecasting in vision-based occupancy prediction. Prior work either forecasts motion categories with little semantic detail or relies on externally predicted past occupancies, causing error accumulation and preventing learning spatio-temporal features directly from images.
AI.method: Proposes ForecastOcc with a novel architecture: (i) temporal cross-attention forecasting module, (ii) 2D-to-3D view transformer, (iii) 3D encoder for occupancy prediction, and (iv) a semantic occupancy head. It forecasts voxel-level semantic occupancy across multiple horizons directly from past images, without external maps. Includes baselines adapting two 2D forecasting modules and evaluates in multi-view and monocular settings.
AI.result: ForecastOcc consistently outperforms baselines on Occ3D-nuScenes and SemanticKITTI, delivering semantically rich, future-aware predictions that capture scene dynamics and semantics essential for autonomous driving; establishes the first benchmarks for vision-based semantic occupancy forecasting.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a first end-to-end framework for vision-based semantic occupancy forecasting, enabling joint geometry-and-semantics prediction directly from images, reducing reliance on external maps, and advancing perception for autonomous driving; it also establishes new benchmarks and baselines for the task.

title: VideoTemp-o3: Harmonizing Temporal Grounding and Video Understanding in Agentic Thinking-with-Videos
summary: In long-video understanding, conventional uniform frame sampling often fails to capture key visual evidence, leading to degraded performance and increased hallucinations. To address this, recent agentic thinking-with-videos paradigms have emerged, adopting a localize-clip-answer pipeline in which the model actively identifies relevant video segments, performs dense sampling within those clips, and then produces answers. However, existing methods remain inefficient, suffer from weak localization, and adhere to rigid workflows. To solve these issues, we propose VideoTemp-o3, a unified agentic thinking-with-videos framework that jointly models video grounding and question answering. VideoTemp-o3 exhibits strong localization capability, supports on-demand clipping, and can refine inaccurate localizations. Specifically, in the supervised fine-tuning stage, we design a unified masking mechanism that encourages exploration while preventing noise. For reinforcement learning, we introduce dedicated rewards to mitigate reward hacking. Besides, from the data perspective, we develop an effective pipeline to construct high-quality long video grounded QA data, along with a corresponding benchmark for systematic evaluation across various video durations. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves remarkable performance on both long video understanding and grounding.

AI.tldr: VideoTemp-o3 introduces a unified agentic framework that jointly performs video grounding and QA for long videos, enabling on-demand clipping, improved localization, and refined answers, with supervised masking and RL rewards, plus a data pipeline and benchmark, achieving strong performance.
AI.motivation: Uniform frame sampling in long videos misses key evidence and increases hallucinations; existing agentic approaches are inefficient, with weak localization and rigid workflows. A unified, flexible framework is needed to improve localization, clipping efficiency, and QA accuracy.
AI.method: Unified agentic thinking-with-videos model VideoTemp-o3 that jointly models grounding and question answering, supports on-demand clipping and dense sampling within clips, includes a unified masking mechanism for supervised fine-tuning to balance exploration and noise, and adds RL rewards to reduce reward hacking. Data-side contributions include a pipeline to construct high-quality long-video grounded QA data and a benchmark for evaluation across durations.
AI.result: Demonstrates remarkable performance on long-video understanding and grounding, with strong localization, on-demand clipping, and refinement of localizations; evidences improved efficiency and robustness across varying video durations.
AI.conclusion: VideoTemp-o3 provides a cohesive framework for long-video grounded QA, combining effective localization, flexible clipping, and robust training strategies, supported by a high-quality data pipeline and benchmark for systematic evaluation.

title: Analyzing and Guiding Zero-Shot Posterior Sampling in Diffusion Models
summary: Recovering a signal from its degraded measurements is a long standing challenge in science and engineering. Recently, zero-shot diffusion based methods have been proposed for such inverse problems, offering a posterior sampling based solution that leverages prior knowledge. Such algorithms incorporate the observations through inference, often leaning on manual tuning and heuristics. In this work we propose a rigorous analysis of such approximate posterior-samplers, relying on a Gaussianity assumption of the prior. Under this regime, we show that both the ideal posterior sampler and diffusion-based reconstruction algorithms can be expressed in closed-form, enabling their thorough analysis and comparisons in the spectral domain. Building on these representations, we also introduce a principled framework for parameter design, replacing heuristic selection strategies used to date. The proposed approach is method-agnostic and yields tailored parameter choices for each algorithm, jointly accounting for the characteristics of the prior, the degraded signal, and the diffusion dynamics. We show that our spectral recommendations differ structurally from standard heuristics and vary with the diffusion step size, resulting in a consistent balance between perceptual quality and signal fidelity.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot diffusion-based inverse problems are analyzed under a Gaussian prior to yield closed-form posterior samplers in the spectral domain. A principled, algorithm-agnostic parameter-design framework is introduced, showing diffusion-step dependent spectral rules that differ from heuristics and balance perceptual quality and fidelity.
AI.motivation: Provide a rigorous, analytical understanding of approximate posterior samplers used for inverse problems with diffusion models, moving beyond heuristic tuning by leveraging a Gaussian prior to obtain tractable, closed-form expressions in the spectral domain.
AI.method: Derive closed-form representations of both the ideal posterior sampler and diffusion-based reconstructions under a Gaussian prior. Express these in the spectral domain to enable analytical comparisons. Develop a parameter-design framework that computes tailored spectral parameters for any diffusion-based algorithm, accounting for the prior, degradation, and diffusion dynamics.
AI.result: Obtained closed-form spectral representations for the ideal and diffusion-based posteriors, enabling direct spectral comparisons. Established principled parameter choices that differ structurally from common heuristics and depend on diffusion step size, prior, and degradation characteristics, leading to a consistent trade-off between perceptual quality and fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The analysis yields algorithm-agnostic, principled spectral parameter designs for diffusion-based inverse problems. It clarifies how diffusion dynamics interact with prior statistics, showing that spectral tuning can improve reconstruction quality and stability beyond heuristic rules.

title: Debate is efficient with your time
summary:
AI safety via debate uses two competing models to help a human judge verify complex computational tasks. Previous work has established what problems debate can solve in principle, but has not analysed the practical cost of human oversight: how many queries must the judge make to the debate transcript? We introduce Debate Query Complexity}(DQC), the minimum number of bits a verifier must inspect to correctly decide a debate.
  Surprisingly, we find that PSPACE/poly (the class of problems which debate can efficiently decide) is precisely the class of functions decidable with O(log n) queries. This characterisation shows that debate is remarkably query-efficient: even for highly complex problems, logarithmic oversight suffices. We also establish that functions depending on all their input bits require Omega(log n) queries, and that any function computable by a circuit of size s satisfies DQC(f) <= log(s) + 3. Interestingly, this last result implies that proving DQC lower bounds of log(n) + 6 for languages in P would yield new circuit lower bounds, connecting debate query complexity to central questions in circuit complexity.

AI.tldr: DQC (Debate Query Complexity) characterizes how much oversight a verifier needs in AI safety via debate. It shows PSPACE/poly exactly equals the class of problems decidable with O(log n) queries, i.e., logarithmic verification suffices for debate-enabled decision. It also proves Omega(log n) lower bounds for input-dependent functions and that any function computed by a circuit of size s has DQC ≤ log(s) + 3. Furthermore, proving DQC lower bounds of log(n) + 6 for languages in P would imply new circuit lower bounds, linking debate query complexity to core circuit complexity questions.
AI.motivation: Quantify the practical verifier effort in AI safety debates and understand whether complex tasks can be reliably checked with minimal human inspection.
AI.method: Define Debate Query Complexity (DQC) as the minimum number of bits a verifier must inspect to decide a debate correctly. Analyze its relationship to complexity classes (PSPACE/poly) and circuit size, derive upper and lower bounds, and explore implications for circuit lower bounds.
AI.result: DQC provides a tight characterization: problems in PSPACE/poly are decidable with O(log n) queries. Functions that depend on all input bits require at least Ω(log n) queries. For any function computable by a circuit of size s, DQC(f) ≤ log(s) + 3. A link is established: proving DQC lower bounds of log(n) + 6 for languages in P would yield new circuit lower bounds.
AI.conclusion: Debate is highly query-efficient, requiring only logarithmic verification. The established connection between DQC and circuit complexity suggests deep implications: progress on DQC lower bounds could translate into new circuit lower bounds, highlighting a fruitful bridge between AI-safety debate mechanisms and fundamental complexity theory.

title: Picasso: Holistic Scene Reconstruction with Physics-Constrained Sampling
summary: In the presence of occlusions and measurement noise, geometrically accurate scene reconstructions -- which fit the sensor data -- can still be physically incorrect. For instance, when estimating the poses and shapes of objects in the scene and importing the resulting estimates into a simulator, small errors might translate to implausible configurations including object interpenetration or unstable equilibrium. This makes it difficult to predict the dynamic behavior of the scene using a digital twin, an important step in simulation-based planning and control of contact-rich behaviors. In this paper, we posit that object pose and shape estimation requires reasoning holistically over the scene (instead of reasoning about each object in isolation), accounting for object interactions and physical plausibility. Towards this goal, our first contribution is Picasso, a physics-constrained reconstruction pipeline that builds multi-object scene reconstructions by considering geometry, non-penetration, and physics. Picasso relies on a fast rejection sampling method that reasons over multi-object interactions, leveraging an inferred object contact graph to guide samples. Second, we propose the Picasso dataset, a collection of 10 contact-rich real-world scenes with ground truth annotations, as well as a metric to quantify physical plausibility, which we open-source as part of our benchmark. Finally, we provide an extensive evaluation of Picasso on our newly introduced dataset and on the YCB-V dataset, and show it largely outperforms the state of the art while providing reconstructions that are both physically plausible and more aligned with human intuition.

AI.tldr: Picasso is a physics-constrained multi-object reconstruction pipeline that enforces non-penetration and physical plausibility for scene reconstructions under occlusions/noise, plus a real-world 10-scene dataset and a metric; it outperforms state-of-the-art on both the Picasso dataset and YCB-V, producing plausible, intuition-aligned reconstructions.
AI.motivation: Geometric accuracy alone can yield physically implausible scenes (e.g., interpenetration, unstable equilibria) when occlusions and sensor noise exist. A holistic, physics-aware reconstruction is needed to predict dynamic behavior and enable reliable digital twins for contact-rich manipulation.
AI.method: Picasso constructs multi-object scene reconstructions by integrating geometry, non-penetration constraints, and physics. It uses a fast rejection sampling strategy that leverages an inferred object contact graph to guide sampling and reason about inter-object interactions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on the proposed Picasso dataset and YCB-V shows Picasso largely outperforms state-of-the-art methods, delivering reconstructions that are physically plausible and align better with human intuition.
AI.conclusion: Holistic physics-constrained reconstruction improves plausibility and dynamic predictability in scenes with occlusions/noise. The work also provides a dataset and metrics for benchmarking physical plausibility in multi-object reconstructions.

title: How well are open sourced AI-generated image detection models out-of-the-box: A comprehensive benchmark study
summary: As AI-generated images proliferate across digital platforms, reliable detection methods have become critical for combating misinformation and maintaining content authenticity. While numerous deepfake detection methods have been proposed, existing benchmarks predominantly evaluate fine-tuned models, leaving a critical gap in understanding out-of-the-box performance -- the most common deployment scenario for practitioners. We present the first comprehensive zero-shot evaluation of 16 state-of-the-art detection methods, comprising 23 pretrained detector variants (due to multiple released versions of certain detectors), across 12 diverse datasets, comprising 2.6~million image samples spanning 291 unique generators including modern diffusion models. Our systematic analysis reveals striking findings: (1)~no universal winner exists, with detector rankings exhibiting substantial instability (Spearman~$ρ$: 0.01 -- 0.87 across dataset pairs); (2)~a 37~percentage-point performance gap separates the best detector (75.0\% mean accuracy) from the worst (37.5\%); (3)~training data alignment critically impacts generalization, causing up to 20--60\% performance variance within architecturally identical detector families; (4)~modern commercial generators (Flux~Dev, Firefly~v4, Midjourney~v7) defeat most detectors, achieving only 18--30\% average accuracy; and (5)~we identify three systematic failure patterns affecting cross-dataset generalization. Statistical analysis confirms significant performance differences between detectors (Friedman test: $χ^2$=121.01, $p<10^{-16}$, Kendall~$W$=0.524). Our findings challenge the ``one-size-fits-all'' detector paradigm and provide actionable deployment guidelines, demonstrating that practitioners must carefully select detectors based on their specific threat landscape rather than relying on published benchmark performance.

AI.tldr: First comprehensive zero-shot evaluation of 16 state-of-the-art AI-generated image detectors (23 pretrained variants) across 12 datasets (2.6M images, 291 generators including diffusion models). No universal winner; detector rankings unstable; large performance gap; training data alignment heavily affects generalization; modern commercial generators defeat most detectors (18–30% accuracy); three systematic cross-dataset failure patterns; Friedman test confirms significant differences. Implication: deployment should be threat-driven rather than relying on published benchmarks; no one-size-fits-all detector.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks largely assess fine-tuned detectors, leaving out-of-the-box, zero-shot performance—most practical deployment scenarios—unexplored. There is a critical need to understand how detectors generalize across diverse generators and datasets without task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.method: Zero-shot evaluation of 16 detectors (23 pretrained variants) over 12 diverse datasets, totaling ~2.6 million image samples from 291 generators (including modern diffusion models). Analyses include detector accuracy, cross-dataset ranking stability (Spearman), variance within detector families, and statistical tests (Friedman chi-squared, Kendall W) to assess significance.
AI.result: No universal winner; detector rankings are highly unstable (Spearman ρ ranging 0.01–0.87 across dataset pairs). Best detector ~75.0% mean accuracy; worst ~37.5%. A 37-point gap separates them. Training data alignment causes up to 20–60% performance variance within architecturally identical detector families. Modern commercial generators (FluxDev, Firefly v4, Midjourney v7) achieve only 18–30% average accuracy, defeating most detectors. Identified three systematic failure patterns in cross-dataset generalization. Friedman test: χ^2=121.01, p<1e-16; Kendall W=0.524.
AI.conclusion: The study refutes the one-size-fits-all detector paradigm. Practitioners should select detectors based on their specific threat landscape and deployment context. Generalization to unseen datasets/generators remains challenging, underscoring the need for more robust, zero-shot detectors and deployment guidelines that account for dataset/generator variance.

title: Efficient Planning in Reinforcement Learning via Model Introspection
summary: Reinforcement learning and classical planning are typically seen as two distinct problems, with differing formulations necessitating different solutions. Yet, when humans are given a task, regardless of the way it is specified, they can often derive the additional information needed to solve the problem efficiently. The key to this ability is introspection: by reasoning about their internal models of the problem, humans directly synthesize additional task-relevant information. In this paper, we propose that this introspection can be thought of as program analysis. We discuss examples of how this approach can be applied to various kinds of models used in reinforcement learning. We then describe an algorithm that enables efficient goal-oriented planning over the class of models used in relational reinforcement learning, demonstrating a novel link between reinforcement learning and classical planning.

AI.tldr: Proposes that introspection in humans—reasoning about internal problem models—can be viewed as program analysis, enabling a link between reinforcement learning and classical planning, with an algorithm for goal-oriented planning over relational RL models.
AI.motivation: To bridge reinforcement learning and classical planning by leveraging internal models to synthesize task-relevant information, improving efficiency in solving tasks.
AI.method: Introduce a conceptual framework equating introspection with program analysis; provide examples across RL model types; present an algorithm for efficient goal-oriented planning over relational RL models.
AI.result: Demonstrates a novel link between RL and classical planning and shows the algorithm enabling such planning; concrete empirical results are not described in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Framing introspection as program analysis can unify RL and planning, facilitating cross-domain techniques for more efficient goal-directed planning.

title: Why do we Trust Chatbots? From Normative Principles to Behavioral Drivers
summary: As chatbots increasingly blur the boundary between automated systems and human conversation, the foundations of trust in these systems warrant closer examination. While regulatory and policy frameworks tend to define trust in normative terms, the trust users place in chatbots often emerges from behavioral mechanisms. In many cases, this trust is not earned through demonstrated trustworthiness but is instead shaped by interactional design choices that leverage cognitive biases to influence user behavior. Based on this observation, we propose reframing chatbots not as companions or assistants, but as highly skilled salespeople whose objectives are determined by the deploying organization. We argue that the coexistence of competing notions of "trust" under a shared term obscures important distinctions between psychological trust formation and normative trustworthiness. Addressing this gap requires further research and stronger support mechanisms to help users appropriately calibrate trust in conversational AI systems.

AI.tldr: Trust in chatbots is shaped by interaction design and cognitive biases, not just demonstrated trustworthiness; reframing chatbots as organizationally driven salespeople clarifies the distinction between psychological trust and normative trust; calls for research and mechanisms to calibrate user trust.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between normative notions of trustworthiness and the behavioral trust users exhibit, revealing how design choices and incentives shape trust.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis and normative critique, drawing on existing literature to argue for a reframing and its policy implications.
AI.result: Proposes viewing chatbots as skilled salespeople governed by deploying organizations; delineates differences between psychological trust formation and normative trustworthiness; highlights need for mechanisms to help users calibrate trust; outlines research directions.
AI.conclusion: Distinctly address psychological trust formation and normative trustworthiness; develop support mechanisms to calibrate user trust in conversational AI; calls for further research.

title: Modeling 3D Pedestrian-Vehicle Interactions for Vehicle-Conditioned Pose Forecasting
comment: Accepted for IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA) 2026
summary: Accurately predicting pedestrian motion is crucial for safe and reliable autonomous driving in complex urban environments. In this work, we present a 3D vehicle-conditioned pedestrian pose forecasting framework that explicitly incorporates surrounding vehicle information. To support this, we enhance the Waymo-3DSkelMo dataset with aligned 3D vehicle bounding boxes, enabling realistic modeling of multi-agent pedestrian-vehicle interactions. We introduce a sampling scheme to categorize scenes by pedestrian and vehicle count, facilitating training across varying interaction complexities. Our proposed network adapts the TBIFormer architecture with a dedicated vehicle encoder and pedestrian-vehicle interaction cross-attention module to fuse pedestrian and vehicle features, allowing predictions to be conditioned on both historical pedestrian motion and surrounding vehicles. Extensive experiments demonstrate substantial improvements in forecasting accuracy and validate different approaches for modeling pedestrian-vehicle interactions, highlighting the importance of vehicle-aware 3D pose prediction for autonomous driving. Code is available at: https://github.com/GuangxunZhu/VehCondPose3D

AI.tldr: Vehicle-conditioned 3D pedestrian pose forecasting: extends Waymo-3DSkelMo with aligned vehicle boxes, categorizes scenes by counts, and uses a TBIFormer-based network with a dedicated vehicle encoder and cross-attention to fuse pedestrian and vehicle data for improved prediction; code released.
AI.motivation: Accurate pedestrian motion prediction in urban autonomous driving requires explicit modeling of multi-agent interactions with surrounding vehicles; vehicle-aware forecasts can improve safety and reliability.
AI.method: Enhance Waymo-3DSkelMo dataset with aligned 3D vehicle bounding boxes; implement a sampling scheme to categorize scenes by pedestrian/vehicle counts; adapt the TBIFormer architecture by adding a vehicle encoder and a pedestrian–vehicle interaction cross-attention module to condition pedestrian pose predictions on history and surrounding vehicle information.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show substantial improvements in forecasting accuracy and validate multiple approaches to modeling pedestrian–vehicle interactions, underscoring the value of vehicle-aware 3D pose forecasting for autonomous driving.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating surrounding vehicle context via a dedicated encoder and cross-attention mechanism within a 3D pose forecasting framework enhances pedestrian motion prediction, supporting safer autonomous driving; code is available at the provided repository.

title: Out of the box age estimation through facial imagery: A Comprehensive Benchmark of Vision-Language Models vs. out-of-the-box Traditional Architectures
summary: Facial age estimation is critical for content moderation, age verification, and deepfake detection, yet no prior benchmark has systematically compared modern vision-language models (VLMs) against specialized age estimation architectures. We present the first large-scale cross-paradigm benchmark, evaluating \textbf{34 models} -- 22 specialized architectures with publicly available pretrained weights and 12 general-purpose VLMs -- across \textbf{8 standard datasets} (UTKFace, IMDB-WIKI, MORPH, AFAD, CACD, FG-NET, APPA-REAL, AgeDB) totaling 1{,}100 test images per model. Our key finding is striking: \emph{zero-shot VLMs significantly outperform most specialized models}, achieving an average MAE of 5.65 years compared to 9.88 for non-LLM models. The best VLM (Gemini~3 Flash Preview, MAE~4.32) outperforms the best non-LLM model (MiVOLO, MAE~5.10) by 15\%. Only MiVOLO, which uniquely combines face and body features via Vision Transformers, competes with VLMs. We further analyze age verification at the 18-year threshold, revealing that non-LLM models exhibit 60--100\% false adult rates on minors while VLMs achieve 13--25\%, and demonstrate that coarse age binning (8--9 classes) consistently degrades MAE beyond 13 years. Our stratified analysis across 14 age groups reveals that all models struggle most at extreme ages ($<$5 and 65+). These findings challenge the assumption that task-specific architectures are necessary for age estimation and suggest that the field should redirect toward distilling VLM capabilities into efficient specialized models.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot vision-language models (VLMs) outperform most specialized age-estimation architectures across 8 datasets, with an average MAE of 5.65 vs. 9.88 for non-LLM models; Gemini 3 Flash Preview (MAE 4.32) is the top performer, beating MiVOLO (5.10). Non-LLMs struggle with 18-year threshold false-adult rates; VLMs achieve 13–25%. Coarse age bins degrade accuracy; all models have difficulty at extremes (<5, 65+). Implication: distill VLM capabilities into efficient specialized models; benchmark highlights cross-paradigm potential and limitations.
AI.motivation: Assess whether modern vision-language models can match or surpass task-specific architectures for facial age estimation; address the lack of systematic cross-paradigm benchmarks across specialized models and VLMs.
AI.method: Benchmark 34 models (22 specialized architectures with public weights + 12 general-purpose VLMs) on 8 datasets (UTKFace, IMDB-WIKI, MORPH, AFAD, CACD, FG-NET, APPA-REAL, AgeDB). Use 1,100 test images per model. Evaluate MAE, 18-year threshold false-positive rates for age verification, and the impact of age binning (coarse vs fine). Stratify results across 14 age groups.
AI.result: Zero-shot VLMs significantly outperform most specialized models (avg MAE 5.65 vs 9.88). Gemini 3 Flash Preview achieves MAE ~4.32, outperforming MiVOLO (~5.10) by ~15%. Non-LLM models show high false-adult rates (60–100%) at 18-year threshold; VLMs reduce to 13–25%. Coarse binning (8–9 classes) degrades MAE by >13 years. All models struggle at ages <5 and 65+.
AI.conclusion: Task-specific architectures are not strictly necessary for age estimation in this setting; VLM capabilities can outperform specialized models in zero-shot scenarios. The field should consider distilling VLM capabilities into efficient specialized architectures, but further work is needed on extreme ages, verification tasks, and dataset biases.

title: ParisKV: Fast and Drift-Robust KV-Cache Retrieval for Long-Context LLMs
summary: KV-cache retrieval is essential for long-context LLM inference, yet existing methods struggle with distribution drift and high latency at scale. We introduce ParisKV, a drift-robust, GPU-native KV-cache retrieval framework based on collision-based candidate selection, followed by a quantized inner-product reranking estimator. For million-token contexts, ParisKV supports CPU-offloaded KV caches via Unified Virtual Addressing (UVA), enabling on-demand top-$k$ fetching with minimal overhead. ParisKV matches or outperforms full attention quality on long-input and long-generation benchmarks. It achieves state-of-the-art long-context decoding efficiency: it matches or exceeds full attention speed even at batch size 1 for long contexts, delivers up to 2.8$\times$ higher throughput within full attention's runnable range, and scales to million-token contexts where full attention runs out of memory. At million-token scale, ParisKV reduces decode latency by 17$\times$ and 44$\times$ compared to MagicPIG and PQCache, respectively, two state-of-the-art KV-cache Top-$k$ retrieval baselines.

AI.tldr: ParisKV is a drift-robust, GPU-native KV-cache retrieval framework that uses collision-based candidate selection and a quantized inner-product reranker to enable efficient, scalable KV-cache retrieval for long-context LLM inference, including million-token contexts with CPU-offloaded caches via UVA.
AI.motivation: KV-cache retrieval for long-context LLMs suffers from distribution drift and high latency at scale; there is a need for drift-robust, scalable, and hardware-efficient retrieval techniques that maintain quality while reducing latency.
AI.method: ParisKV introduces collision-based candidate selection to prune KV candidates, followed by a quantized inner-product reranking estimator. It is GPU-native and supports CPU-offloaded caches via Unified Virtual Addressing (UVA) for million-token contexts, enabling on-demand top-k fetching with minimal overhead.
AI.result: ParisKV achieves equivalent or superior quality to full attention on long-input and long-generation benchmarks, and delivers state-of-the-art long-context decoding efficiency: it matches or exceeds full-attention speed even at batch size 1 for long contexts, provides up to 2.8x higher throughput within full-attention's runnable range, and scales to million-token contexts where full attention runs out of memory. It reduces decode latency by 17x and 44x compared to MagicPIG and PQCache respectively at million-token scale.
AI.conclusion: ParisKV delivers drift-robust, efficient, and scalable KV-cache retrieval for long-context LLM inference, achieving near-parity with full attention in quality while surpassing it in efficiency and enabling million-token context support where competing KV-cache baselines fail.

title: Intermediate Results on the Complexity of STRIPS$_{1}^{1}$
summary: This paper is based on Bylander's results on the computational complexity of propositional STRIPS planning. He showed that when only ground literals are permitted, determining plan existence is PSPACE-complete even if operators are limited to two preconditions and two postconditions. While NP-hardness is settled, it is unknown whether propositional STRIPS with operators that only have one precondition and one effect is NP-complete. We shed light on the question whether this small solution hypothesis for STRIPS$^1_1$ is true, calling a SAT solver for small instances, introducing the literal graph, and mapping it to Petri nets.

AI.tldr: Advances understanding of STRIPS1_1 complexity by using SAT, a literal graph, and Petri nets; builds a framework to test NP-hardness vs NP-membership for one-precondition-one-effect STRIPS.
AI.motivation: Address whether STRIPS with one precondition and one effect is NP-complete, i.e., the small-solution hypothesis for STRIPS^1_1.
AI.method: Empirical and theoretical approach: solve small instances with SAT, define a 'literal graph' to represent variable dependencies, and reduce/map to Petri nets to study plan-existence dynamics.
AI.result: Provides evidence and tools to interrogate the open problem; not conclusive but demonstrates viability of the approach and potential connections to NP/PSPACE boundaries.
AI.conclusion: Offers a methodological bridge between classical STRIPS complexity and Petri-net theory, enabling further investigation into STRIPS^1_1 hardness.

title: WorldArena: A Unified Benchmark for Evaluating Perception and Functional Utility of Embodied World Models
summary: While world models have emerged as a cornerstone of embodied intelligence by enabling agents to reason about environmental dynamics through action-conditioned prediction, their evaluation remains fragmented. Current evaluation of embodied world models has largely focused on perceptual fidelity (e.g., video generation quality), overlooking the functional utility of these models in downstream decision-making tasks. In this work, we introduce WorldArena, a unified benchmark designed to systematically evaluate embodied world models across both perceptual and functional dimensions. WorldArena assesses models through three dimensions: video perception quality, measured with 16 metrics across six sub-dimensions; embodied task functionality, which evaluates world models as data engines, policy evaluators, and action planners integrating with subjective human evaluation. Furthermore, we propose EWMScore, a holistic metric integrating multi-dimensional performance into a single interpretable index. Through extensive experiments on 14 representative models, we reveal a significant perception-functionality gap, showing that high visual quality does not necessarily translate into strong embodied task capability. WorldArena benchmark with the public leaderboard is released at https://worldarena.ai, providing a framework for tracking progress toward truly functional world models in embodied AI.

AI.tldr: A unified benchmark (WorldArena) evaluates embodied world models on both perceptual (video quality) and functional (downstream decision-making) dimensions, introducing EWMScore. Evaluated on 14 models, it uncovers a perception–functionality gap where high visual fidelity does not guarantee strong embodied task capability; a public leaderboard is provided.
AI.motivation: To address fragmented evaluation of embodied world models, which have been judged mainly on perceptual fidelity, by introducing a comprehensive benchmark that also assesses downstream utility in decision-making tasks.
AI.method: WorldArena assesses models along three dimensions: (1) video perception quality using 16 metrics across six sub-dimensions; (2) embodied task functionality where world models function as data engines, policy evaluators, and action planners; and (3) subjective human evaluation. It also introduces EWMScore, a holistic index combining multi-dimensional performance. Evaluation conducted on 14 representative models with public leaderboard.
AI.result: Reveals a significant perception–functionality gap: strong visual/video quality does not necessarily translate to robust embodied task performance. Demonstrates the utility of a unified benchmark and provides a public platform for tracking progress.
AI.conclusion: WorldArena offers a practical framework for jointly evaluating perceptual and functional capabilities of embodied world models, guiding future development toward truly functional, decision-ready agents; highlights the need to optimize models for downstream tasks beyond perceptual fidelity.

title: Do We Need Adam? Surprisingly Strong and Sparse Reinforcement Learning with SGD in LLMs
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL), particularly RL from verifiable reward (RLVR), has become a crucial phase of training large language models (LLMs) and a key focus of current scaling efforts. However, optimization practices in RL largely follow those of next-token prediction stages (e.g., pretraining and supervised fine-tuning), despite fundamental differences between RL and these stages highlighted by recent work. One such practice is the use of the AdamW optimizer, which is widely adopted for training large-scale transformers despite its high memory overhead. Our analysis shows that both momentum and adaptive learning rates in AdamW are less influential in RL than in SFT, leading us to hypothesize that RL benefits less from Adam-style per-parameter adaptive learning rates and momentum. Confirming this hypothesis, our experiments demonstrate that the substantially more memory-efficient SGD, which is known to perform poorly in supervised learning of large-scale transformers, matches or even outperforms AdamW in RL for LLMs. Remarkably, full fine-tuning with SGD updates fewer than 0.02% of model parameters without any sparsity-promoting regularization, more than 1000 times fewer than AdamW. Our analysis offers potential reasons for this update sparsity. These findings provide new insights into the optimization dynamics of RL in LLMs and show that RL can be substantially more parameter-efficient than previously recognized.

AI.tldr: SGD can match or outperform AdamW for RL fine-tuning of large language models, enabling extreme update sparsity (<0.02% of parameters) and challenging the default use of AdamW.
AI.motivation: To reassess optimizer choices for RL with verifiable rewards in LLMs, given differences from supervised fine-tuning and memory constraints.
AI.method: Empirical comparison of SGD vs AdamW for RL-based fine-tuning of LLMs, evaluating performance, memory usage, and the extent of parameter updates; analysis of momentum/adaptive learning-rate contributions and sparsity of updates.
AI.result: SGD matches or outperforms AdamW in RL for LLMs; full fine-tuning with SGD updates <0.02% of parameters, >1000x fewer than AdamW; momentum and per-parameter adaptivity contribute less in RL than in SFT; RL may benefit from substantial update sparsity without sparsity-promoting regularization.
AI.conclusion: RL optimization dynamics differ from supervised stages; RL can be significantly more parameter-efficient, suggesting a reevaluation of optimizer choices for RL in LLM training.

title: Exploring SAIG Methods for an Objective Evaluation of XAI
summary: The evaluation of eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) methods is a rapidly growing field, characterized by a wide variety of approaches. This diversity highlights the complexity of the XAI evaluation, which, unlike traditional AI assessment, lacks a universally correct ground truth for the explanation, making objective evaluation challenging. One promising direction to address this issue involves the use of what we term Synthetic Artificial Intelligence Ground truth (SAIG) methods, which generate artificial ground truths to enable the direct evaluation of XAI techniques. This paper presents the first review and analysis of SAIG methods. We introduce a novel taxonomy to classify these approaches, identifying seven key features that distinguish different SAIG methods. Our comparative study reveals a concerning lack of consensus on the most effective XAI evaluation techniques, underscoring the need for further research and standardization in this area.

AI.tldr: First review of Synthetic Artificial Intelligence Ground truth (SAIG) methods for XAI evaluation. Proposes a seven-feature taxonomy to classify SAIG approaches, and demonstrates a lack of consensus on effective XAI evaluation techniques, underscoring the need for standardization.
AI.motivation: XAI evaluation lacks a universal ground truth for explanations, making objective assessment difficult. SAIG introduces artificial ground truths to enable direct evaluation of XAI methods.
AI.method: Develops a novel taxonomy to classify SAIG methods, identifying seven key distinguishing features and applying it in a comparative study across SAIG approaches.
AI.result: The comparative study uncovers a notable absence of consensus on the most effective XAI evaluation techniques, highlighting methodological heterogeneity and the need for standardization.
AI.conclusion: SAIG is a promising direction for XAI evaluation, but further research and standardization are essential; this work provides the first consolidated review and framework.

title: Open-Text Aerial Detection: A Unified Framework For Aerial Visual Grounding And Detection
summary: Open-Vocabulary Aerial Detection (OVAD) and Remote Sensing Visual Grounding (RSVG) have emerged as two key paradigms for aerial scene understanding. However, each paradigm suffers from inherent limitations when operating in isolation: OVAD is restricted to coarse category-level semantics, while RSVG is structurally limited to single-target localization. These limitations prevent existing methods from simultaneously supporting rich semantic understanding and multi-target detection. To address this, we propose OTA-Det, the first unified framework that bridges both paradigms into a cohesive architecture. Specifically, we introduce a task reformulation strategy that unifies task objectives and supervision mechanisms, enabling joint training across datasets from both paradigms with dense supervision signals. Furthermore, we propose a dense semantic alignment strategy that establishes explicit correspondence at multiple granularities, from holistic expressions to individual attributes, enabling fine-grained semantic understanding. To ensure real-time efficiency, OTA-Det builds upon the RT-DETR architecture, extending it from closed-set detection to open-text detection by introducing several high efficient modules, achieving state-of-the-art performance on six benchmarks spanning both OVAD and RSVG tasks while maintaining real-time inference at 34 FPS.

AI.tldr: OTA-Det is a unified framework that merges Open-Vocabulary Aerial Detection (OVAD) and Remote Sensing Visual Grounding (RSVG) into a single architecture, enabling dense supervision and multi-granularity semantic alignment for open-text, multi-target detection with real-time performance.
AI.motivation: OVAD is limited to coarse, category-level semantics, and RSVG is restricted to single-target localization; bridging them allows simultaneous rich semantic understanding and multi-target detection.
AI.method: Propose a task reformulation to unify objectives and supervision across OVAD and RSVG datasets; introduce dense semantic alignment across granularities from holistic expressions to individual attributes; extend the RT-DETR backbone to support open-text detection with efficiency modules for real-time inference; enable joint training across heterogeneous datasets with dense supervision signals.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on six benchmarks spanning OVAD and RSVG while maintaining real-time inference at 34 FPS.
AI.conclusion: First unified framework that bridges OVAD and RSVG, enabling joint training with dense supervision and multi-granularity semantic alignment to support rich semantics and multi-target detection in aerial scenes.

title: The Laplacian Keyboard: Beyond the Linear Span
summary: Across scientific disciplines, Laplacian eigenvectors serve as a fundamental basis for simplifying complex systems, from signal processing to quantum mechanics. In reinforcement learning (RL), these eigenvectors provide a natural basis for approximating reward functions; however, their use is typically limited to their linear span, which restricts expressivity in complex environments. We introduce the Laplacian Keyboard (LK), a hierarchical framework that goes beyond the linear span. LK constructs a task-agnostic library of options from these eigenvectors, forming a behavior basis guaranteed to contain the optimal policy for any reward within the linear span. A meta-policy learns to stitch these options dynamically, enabling efficient learning of policies outside the original linear constraints. We establish theoretical bounds on zero-shot approximation error and demonstrate empirically that LK surpasses zero-shot solutions while achieving improved sample efficiency compared to standard RL methods.

AI.tldr: Laplacian Keyboard (LK) builds a hierarchical, task-agnostic library of options from Laplacian eigenvectors to extend expressivity beyond their linear span in RL, with a meta-policy that stitches options; it offers zero-shot error bounds and improved sample efficiency over standard RL.
AI.motivation: Laplacian eigenvectors are expressive but confined to linear combinations; real-world reward functions (in RL) may require non-linear use of these primitives. LK aims to broaden representational power while retaining guarantees within the linear span and improving learning efficiency.
AI.method: Construct a library of options derived from Laplacian eigenvectors in a task-agnostic manner; employ a meta-policy to dynamically stitch these options beyond the linear span; provide theoretical zero-shot approximation bounds; empirically compare LK to zero-shot baselines and standard RL.
AI.result: LK achieves better zero-shot performance than purely linear combinations and demonstrates improved sample efficiency compared to standard RL methods in empirical evaluations.
AI.conclusion: LK extends the utility of Laplacian eigenvectors by introducing a hierarchical option framework that expands expressivity beyond the linear span with theoretical guarantees and practical performance gains.

title: Finite-State Controllers for (Hidden-Model) POMDPs using Deep Reinforcement Learning
comment: 17 pages (8 main paper, 2 references, 7 appendix). 3 figures in the main paper, 3 figures in the appendix. Accepted AAMAS'26 submission
summary: Solving partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) requires computing policies under imperfect state information. Despite recent advances, the scalability of existing POMDP solvers remains limited. Moreover, many settings require a policy that is robust across multiple POMDPs, further aggravating the scalability issue. We propose the Lexpop framework for POMDP solving. Lexpop (1) employs deep reinforcement learning to train a neural policy, represented by a recurrent neural network, and (2) constructs a finite-state controller mimicking the neural policy through efficient extraction methods. Crucially, unlike neural policies, such controllers can be formally evaluated, providing performance guarantees. We extend Lexpop to compute robust policies for hidden-model POMDPs (HM-POMDPs), which describe finite sets of POMDPs. We associate every extracted controller with its worst-case POMDP. Using a set of such POMDPs, we iteratively train a robust neural policy and consequently extract a robust controller. Our experiments show that on problems with large state spaces, Lexpop outperforms state-of-the-art solvers for POMDPs as well as HM-POMDPs.

AI.tldr: Lexpop trains a neural policy via deep RL and extracts verifiable finite-state controllers; extends to robust HM-POMDPs by worst-case association; outperforms baselines on large problem instances.
AI.motivation: POMDPs are computationally hard due to partial observability and scalability; there is a need for robust policies that generalize across multiple POMDPs while providing formal guarantees.
AI.method: Phase 1: train a recurrent neural network policy with deep RL for POMDPs. Phase 2: extract a finite-state controller that mimics the neural policy; provisions for formal evaluation of the controller to guarantee performance. For HM-POMDPs: consider a finite set of POMDPs; associate each extracted controller with its worst-case POMDP; iteratively train a robust neural policy across the ensemble and extract a robust controller.
AI.result: Empirical results show Lexpop outperforms state-of-the-art solvers for POMDPs and HM-POMDPs on problems with large state spaces.
AI.conclusion: The approach combines scalable neural policy learning with verifiable, extractable controllers and provides robust solutions across model families, addressing both scalability and robustness in POMDP settings.

title: SPD-Faith Bench: Diagnosing and Improving Faithfulness in Chain-of-Thought for Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Chain-of-Thought reasoning is widely used to improve the interpretability of multimodal large language models (MLLMs), yet the faithfulness of the generated reasoning traces remains unclear. Prior work has mainly focused on perceptual hallucinations, leaving reasoning level unfaithfulness underexplored. To isolate faithfulness from linguistic priors, we introduce SPD-Faith Bench, a diagnostic benchmark based on fine-grained image difference reasoning that enforces explicit visual comparison. Evaluations on state-of-the-art MLLMs reveal two systematic failure modes, perceptual blindness and perception-reasoning dissociation. We trace these failures to decaying visual attention and representation shifts in the residual stream. Guided by this analysis, we propose SAGE, a train-free visual evidence-calibrated framework that improves visual routing and aligns reasoning with perception. Our results highlight the importance of explicitly evaluating faithfulness beyond response correctness. Our benchmark and codes are available at https://github.com/Johanson-colab/SPD-Faith-Bench.

AI.tldr: Introduces SPD-Faith Bench to diagnose faithfulness of chain-of-thought in multimodal LLMs via fine-grained visual-difference tasks; uncovers perceptual blindness and perception–reasoning dissociation as core failure modes; analyzes causes (decaying visual attention, residual-stream shifts) and proposes SAGE, a train-free visual-evidence calibration to improve visual routing and alignment of reasoning with perception; emphasizes evaluating faithfulness beyond accuracy; code available.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in evaluating faithfulness of reasoning traces in multimodal LLMs, separating perceptual priors from true reasoning and moving beyond response correctness.
AI.method: 1) Propose SPD-Faith Bench, a diagnostic benchmark based on fine-grained image-difference reasoning requiring explicit visual comparison. 2) Evaluate state-of-the-art MLLMs to identify failure modes. 3) Analyze causes (visual attention decay, residual-stream representation shifts). 4) Introduce SAGE, a train-free visual evidence-calibration framework to improve visual routing and align reasoning with perception.
AI.result: Identification of two systematic failure modes in MLLMs: perceptual blindness and perception–reasoning dissociation. These are linked to decaying visual attention and representation shifts in the residual stream. SPD-Faith Bench provides a diagnostic tool, and SAGE offers a training-free method to calibrate visual evidence and improve faithfulness of reasoning. Benchmark and code are released (GitHub).
AI.conclusion: Highlights the need for faithfulness-focused evaluation beyond answer accuracy in multimodal reasoning. The work presents a diagnostic benchmark and a practical calibration framework (SAGE) to enhance alignment between perception and reasoning in MLLMs.

title: Efficient Adaptive Data Analysis over Dense Distributions
summary: Modern data workflows are inherently adaptive, repeatedly querying the same dataset to refine and validate sequential decisions, but such adaptivity can lead to overfitting and invalid statistical inference. Adaptive Data Analysis (ADA) mechanisms address this challenge; however, there is a fundamental tension between computational efficiency and sample complexity. For $T$ rounds of adaptive analysis, computationally efficient algorithms typically incur suboptimal $O(\sqrt{T})$ sample complexity, whereas statistically optimal $O(\log T)$ algorithms are computationally intractable under standard cryptographic assumptions. In this work, we shed light on this trade-off by identifying a natural class of data distributions under which both computational efficiency and optimal sample complexity are achievable. We propose a computationally efficient ADA mechanism that attains optimal $O(\log T)$ sample complexity when the data distribution is dense with respect to a known prior. This setting includes, in particular, feature--label data distributions arising in distribution-specific learning. As a consequence, our mechanism also yields a sample-efficient (i.e., $O(\log T)$ samples) statistical query oracle in the distribution-specific setting. Moreover, although our algorithm is not based on differential privacy, it satisfies a relaxed privacy notion known as Predicate Singling Out (PSO) security (Cohen and Nissim, 2020). Our results thus reveal an inherent connection between adaptive data analysis and privacy beyond differential privacy.

AI.tldr: A natural, density-based distribution setting enables both computationally efficient adaptive data analysis (ADA) and optimal O(log T) sample complexity. The authors propose a computationally efficient ADA mechanism that achieves O(log T) sample complexity under a prior-dense data distribution, yielding a sample-efficient statistical query (SQ) oracle in distribution-specific learning. The approach is not DP-based but satisfies Predicate Singling Out (PSO) security, highlighting a link between ADA and privacy beyond differential privacy.
AI.motivation: Adaptive data analysis (ADA) can suffer from overfitting and invalid inference due to adaptivity. There is a fundamental trade-off: efficient algorithms typically incur suboptimal sqrt(T) sample complexity, while statistically optimal O(log T) methods are computationally hard under standard cryptographic assumptions. The paper investigates natural distributional assumptions to reconcile efficiency with optimal sample complexity and to explore connections to privacy.
AI.method: Define a computationally efficient ADA mechanism for T adaptive queries under the assumption that the data distribution is dense with respect to a known prior. This distribution-specific setting includes feature–label distributions. Show that the mechanism attains the optimal O(log T) sample complexity. Demonstrate that the mechanism also yields a sample-efficient (O(log T)) statistical query oracle in distribution-specific learning. Analyze privacy via Predicate Singling Out (PSO) security rather than differential privacy.
AI.result: The proposed mechanism achieves optimal O(log T) sample complexity in the density-with-respect-to-prior setting, while remaining computationally efficient. It also provides a sample-efficient SQ oracle in distribution-specific learning and satisfies PSO security, establishing a link between adaptive data analysis and privacy beyond differential privacy.
AI.conclusion: Under a natural distributional density assumption, computational efficiency and optimal sample complexity in ADA can coincide. The work connects ADA with PSO-type privacy, suggesting broader privacy-adjacent considerations beyond DP and highlighting a distribution-specific path to efficient adaptive querying and SQ access.

title: Belief Offloading in Human-AI Interaction
summary: What happens when people's beliefs are derived from information provided by an LLM? People's use of LLM chatbots as thought partners can contribute to cognitive offloading, which can have adverse effects on cognitive skills in cases of over-reliance. This paper defines and investigates a particular kind of cognitive offloading in human-AI interaction, "belief offloading," in which people's processes of forming and upholding beliefs are offloaded onto an AI system with downstream consequences on their behavior and the nature of their system of beliefs. Drawing on philosophy, psychology, and computer science research, we clarify the boundary conditions under which belief offloading occurs and provide a descriptive taxonomy of belief offloading and its normative implications. We close with directions for future work to assess the potential for and consequences of belief offloading in human-AI interaction.

AI.tldr: Introduces 'belief offloading' as a cognitive offloading type where beliefs formation is delegated to LLMs; provides boundary conditions, a taxonomy, normative implications, and directions for future work.
AI.motivation: To understand how reliance on LLMs as thought partners affects belief formation, maintenance, and subsequent behavior, as well as the normative and ethical implications.
AI.method: Conceptual synthesis drawing on philosophy, psychology, and computer science; qualitative analysis and taxonomy development; no empirical data reported in the abstract.
AI.result: Defines belief offloading, delineates boundary conditions, presents a descriptive taxonomy of belief offloading, discusses normative implications, and outlines directions for future research.
AI.conclusion: Call for empirical and theoretical work to assess the prevalence, mechanisms, and consequences of belief offloading in human–AI interaction, with attention to both risks and benefits.

title: VFace: A Training-Free Approach for Diffusion-Based Video Face Swapping
summary: We present a training-free, plug-and-play method, namely VFace, for high-quality face swapping in videos. It can be seamlessly integrated with image-based face swapping approaches built on diffusion models. First, we introduce a Frequency Spectrum Attention Interpolation technique to facilitate generation and intact key identity characteristics. Second, we achieve Target Structure Guidance via plug-and-play attention injection to better align the structural features from the target frame to the generation. Third, we present a Flow-Guided Attention Temporal Smoothening mechanism that enforces spatiotemporal coherence without modifying the underlying diffusion model to reduce temporal inconsistencies typically encountered in frame-wise generation. Our method requires no additional training or video-specific fine-tuning. Extensive experiments show that our method significantly enhances temporal consistency and visual fidelity, offering a practical and modular solution for video-based face swapping. Our code is available at https://github.com/Sanoojan/VFace.

AI.tldr: A training-free, plug-and-play method (VFace) for high-quality, temporally coherent video face swapping that integrates with diffusion-model-based image swaps, featuring three components to preserve identity and enhance temporal consistency without training.
AI.motivation: Address temporal inconsistency and identity preservation in video face swapping using diffusion models, without requiring user-specific training or fine-tuning.
AI.method: Three main components: (1) Frequency Spectrum Attention Interpolation to maintain key identity characteristics during generation; (2) Target Structure Guidance via plug-and-play attention injection to align target-frame structural features with generation; (3) Flow-Guided Attention Temporal Smoothening to enforce spatiotemporal coherence across frames without modifying the diffusion model; seamless integration with existing image-based swaps; no training required.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show improved temporal consistency and visual fidelity; provides a practical, modular solution for video-based face swapping; code released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: VFace delivers a training-free, plug-and-play framework that enhances temporal coherence and visual fidelity in video face swapping, enabling easy integration with diffusion-model-based methods without additional training.

title: Dynamics Within Latent Chain-of-Thought: An Empirical Study of Causal Structure
summary: Latent or continuous chain-of-thought methods replace explicit textual rationales with a number of internal latent steps, but these intermediate computations are difficult to evaluate beyond correlation-based probes. In this paper, we view latent chain-of-thought as a manipulable causal process in representation space by modeling latent steps as variables in a structural causal model (SCM) and analyzing their effects through step-wise $\mathrm{do}$-interventions. We study two representative paradigms (i.e., Coconut and CODI) on both mathematical and general reasoning tasks to investigate three key questions: (1) which steps are causally necessary for correctness and when answers become decidable early; (2) how does influence propagate across steps, and how does this structure compare to explicit CoT; and (3) do intermediate trajectories retain competing answer modes, and how does output-level commitment differ from representational commitment across steps. We find that latent-step budgets behave less like homogeneous extra depth and more like staged functionality with non-local routing, and we identify a persistent gap between early output bias and late representational commitment. These results motivate mode-conditional and stability-aware analyses -- and corresponding training/decoding objectives -- as more reliable tools for interpreting and improving latent reasoning systems.

AI.tldr: Treats latent chain-of-thought as an intervenable causal process in representation space, using a structural causal model and do-interventions to probe step-wise effects in Coconut and CODI for math/general reasoning tasks; reveals stepwise functionality with non-local routing and a gap between early output bias and late representational commitment, motivating stability-aware analyses and mode-conditioned training/decode objectives.
AI.motivation: To move beyond correlation-based probes of latent chain-of-thought, by modeling latent steps as causal variables and evaluating their necessity, influence spread, and commitment across steps; and to compare latent CoT with explicit CoT and understand dynamics in reasoning systems.
AI.method: Construct a structural causal model in which latent steps are variables; apply do-interventions to individual steps to study causal effects on final outputs. Empirically examine two reasoning paradigms, Coconut and CODI, on mathematical and general reasoning tasks, analyzing: (1) causally necessary steps and early decidability, (2) influence propagation and comparison to explicit CoT, (3) whether intermediate trajectories preserve competing answer modes and how output-level and representation-level commitments diverge.
AI.result: Findings indicate latent-step budgets behave more like staged functionality with non-local routing rather than homogeneous extra depth. There exists a persistent gap between early output bias and late representational commitment. The work advocates mode-conditional and stability-aware analyses, along with corresponding training/decoding objectives, as reliable tools for interpreting and improving latent reasoning systems.
AI.conclusion: The study suggests rethinking latent CoT analysis in terms of causal structure and interventions, and endorses mode-conditional and stability-aware strategies to interpret and enhance latent reasoning, with implications for training and decoding objectives.

title: Geometry-Aware Rotary Position Embedding for Consistent Video World Model
summary: Predictive world models that simulate future observations under explicit camera control are fundamental to interactive AI. Despite rapid advances, current systems lack spatial persistence: they fail to maintain stable scene structures over long trajectories, frequently hallucinating details when cameras revisit previously observed locations. We identify that this geometric drift stems from reliance on screen-space positional embeddings, which conflict with the projective geometry required for 3D consistency. We introduce \textbf{ViewRope}, a geometry-aware encoding that injects camera-ray directions directly into video transformer self-attention layers. By parameterizing attention with relative ray geometry rather than pixel locality, ViewRope provides a model-native inductive bias for retrieving 3D-consistent content across temporal gaps. We further propose \textbf{Geometry-Aware Frame-Sparse Attention}, which exploits these geometric cues to selectively attend to relevant historical frames, improving efficiency without sacrificing memory consistency. We also present \textbf{ViewBench}, a diagnostic suite measuring loop-closure fidelity and geometric drift. Our results demonstrate that ViewRope substantially improves long-term consistency while reducing computational costs.

AI.tldr: Geometry-aware video-transformer with ray-based attention (ViewRope) to enforce 3D consistency, plus a frame-sparse attention variant and a diagnostic suite (ViewBench); claim: improved long-term stability and reduced compute.
AI.motivation: Current predictive world models struggle with spatial persistence and 3D consistency; screen-space positional embeddings conflict with projective geometry, causing drift and hallucinations on revisits.
AI.method: Introduce ViewRope by injecting camera-ray directions into self-attention, parameterizing attention with relative ray geometry rather than pixel locality. Develop Geometry-Aware Frame-Sparse Attention to attend only relevant historical frames. Propose ViewBench to measure loop-closure fidelity and geometric drift.
AI.result: Reported substantial improvements in long-term consistency and reduced computational costs, without sacrificing memory accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-aware ray-based attention provides a model-native inductive bias for maintaining 3D-consistent content over time, with efficient frame selection and a diagnostic suite to evaluate geometric fidelity.

title: Learnable Chernoff Baselines for Inference-Time Alignment
summary: We study inference-time reward-guided alignment for generative models. Existing methods often rely on either architecture-specific adaptations or computationally costly inference procedures. We introduce Learnable Chernoff Baselines (LCBs) as a method for efficiently and approximately sampling from the exponentially tilted kernels that arise from KL-regularized reward alignment. Using only black-box sampling access to the pretrained model, LCBs implement a form of rejection sampling with adaptively selected acceptance probabilities, which allows fine-grained control over inference-compute scaling.
