We establish total-variation guarantees to the ideal aligned model, and demonstrate in both continuous and discrete diffusion settings that LCB sampling closely matches ideal rejection sampling while using substantially fewer queries to the pretrained model.

AI.tldr: Learnable Chernoff Baselines (LCBs) offer an efficient, black-box approach to sampling from KL-regularized, reward-guided alignment kernels for generative models, using adaptive rejection sampling to trade inference compute for accuracy while achieving near-ideal sampling.
AI.motivation: Existing reward-guided alignment methods are either architecture-specific or computationally expensive. There is a need for a model-agnostic, inference-time method that yields good alignment with fewer model queries and predictable compute.
AI.method: Introduce Learnable Chernoff Baselines (LCBs) that perform adaptive rejection sampling to sample from exponentially tilted kernels derived from KL-regularized reward alignment. The approach uses only black-box access to the pretrained model to control acceptance probabilities, enabling scalable computation across tasks.
AI.result: LCBs provide total-variation guarantees to the ideal aligned model. Empirically, in both continuous and discrete diffusion settings, LCB sampling closely matches ideal rejection sampling while requiring substantially fewer model queries.
AI.conclusion: LCBs enable efficient, near-ideal reward-guided alignment for generative models in a model-agnostic framework, with tunable compute-accuracy trade-offs and strong theoretical guarantees.

title: The Use of AI Tools to Develop and Validate Q-Matrices
comment: An earlier version of this study was presented at the Psychometric Society Meeting held in July 2025 in Minneapolis, USA
summary: Constructing a Q-matrix is a critical but labor-intensive step in cognitive diagnostic modeling (CDM). This study investigates whether AI tools (i.e., general language models) can support Q-matrix development by comparing AI-generated Q-matrices with a validated Q-matrix from Li and Suen (2013) for a reading comprehension test. In May 2025, multiple AI models were provided with the same training materials as human experts. Agreement among AI-generated Q-matrices, the validated Q-matrix, and human raters' Q-matrices was assessed using Cohen's kappa. Results showed substantial variation across AI models, with Google Gemini 2.5 Pro achieving the highest agreement (Kappa = 0.63) with the validated Q-matrix, exceeding that of all human experts. A follow-up analysis in January 2026 using newer AI versions, however, revealed lower agreement with the validated Q-matrix. Implications and directions for future research are discussed.

AI.tldr: AI tools can assist Q-matrix construction in CDM with varying agreement to a validated standard; initial phase (May 2025) showed substantial agreement (kappa ~0.63) for the best model, surpassing human experts, but a follow-up with newer AI versions (Jan 2026) showed reduced agreement, signaling stability concerns and model drift.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether general language model AI tools can support Q-matrix development for cognitive diagnostic modeling and how their agreement with a validated standard and human raters compares.
AI.method: Provide the same training materials to multiple AI models and human experts; generate Q-matrices; assess agreement with a validated Q-matrix from Li and Suen (2013) and with human raters using Cohen's kappa; compare across AI models; conduct a follow-up analysis with newer AI versions.
AI.result: AI models show substantial variation; Google Gemini 2.5 Pro achieved the highest agreement with the validated Q-matrix (kappa = 0.63), exceeding all human experts. A subsequent analysis in January 2026 with newer AI versions revealed lower agreement with the validated Q-matrix.
AI.conclusion: AI tools can support Q-matrix construction but reliability is sensitive to model version and model architecture; benefits may be short-lived across updates. Rigorous, ongoing validation, stable versioning, and multi-source evaluation are needed before AI-generated Q-matrices can be trusted in practice.

title: Recovering 3D Shapes from Ultra-Fast Motion-Blurred Images
summary:
We consider the problem of 3D shape recovery from ultra-fast motion-blurred images. While 3D reconstruction from static images has been extensively studied, recovering geometry from extreme motion-blurred images remains challenging. Such scenarios frequently occur in both natural and industrial settings, such as fast-moving objects in sports (e.g., balls) or rotating machinery, where rapid motion distorts object appearance and makes traditional 3D reconstruction techniques like Multi-View Stereo (MVS) ineffective.
  In this paper, we propose a novel inverse rendering approach for shape recovery from ultra-fast motion-blurred images. While conventional rendering techniques typically synthesize blur by averaging across multiple frames, we identify a major computational bottleneck in the repeated computation of barycentric weights. To address this, we propose a fast barycentric coordinate solver, which significantly reduces computational overhead and achieves a speedup of up to 4.57x, enabling efficient and photorealistic simulation of high-speed motion. Crucially, our method is fully differentiable, allowing gradients to propagate from rendered images to the underlying 3D shape, thereby facilitating shape recovery through inverse rendering.
  We validate our approach on two representative motion types: rapid translation and rotation. Experimental results demonstrate that our method enables efficient and realistic modeling of ultra-fast moving objects in the forward simulation. Moreover, it successfully recovers 3D shapes from 2D imagery of objects undergoing extreme translational and rotational motion, advancing the boundaries of vision-based 3D reconstruction. Project page: https://maxmilite.github.io/rec-from-ultrafast-blur/

AI.tldr: A differentiable inverse-rendering framework recovers 3D shapes from ultra-fast motion blur by introducing a fast barycentric-coordinate solver that accelerates blur synthesis (up to 4.57x), enabling shape recovery for extreme translational and rotational motion.
AI.motivation: Extreme motion blur degrades geometry cues and defeats traditional MVS-based 3D reconstruction. There is a need for differentiable forward models that can simulate ultra-fast blur efficiently to enable inverse rendering-based shape recovery.
AI.method: Introduce a fast barycentric coordinate solver to reduce the computational bottleneck in blur synthesis. Build a differentiable rendering pipeline that supports ultra-fast motion (translation and rotation) and allows backpropagation from rendered blurred images to the underlying 3D shape for inverse rendering-based recovery.
AI.result: Achieves up to 4.57x speedup in blur synthesis. Demonstrates realistic forward simulation of high-speed motion and successful recovery of 3D shapes from 2D images under extreme translational and rotational motion.
AI.conclusion: The method provides a faster, differentiable path to model and recover geometry from ultra-fast blur, expanding the applicability of vision-based 3D reconstruction to high-speed scenarios.

title: Riemannian MeanFlow
summary: Diffusion and flow models have become the dominant paradigm for generative modeling on Riemannian manifolds, with successful applications in protein backbone generation and DNA sequence design. However, these methods require tens to hundreds of neural network evaluations at inference time, which can become a computational bottleneck in large-scale scientific sampling workflows. We introduce Riemannian MeanFlow~(RMF), a framework for learning flow maps directly on manifolds, enabling high-quality generations with as few as one forward pass. We derive three equivalent characterizations of the manifold average velocity (Eulerian, Lagrangian, and semigroup identities), and analyze parameterizations and stabilization techniques to improve training on high-dimensional manifolds. In promoter DNA design and protein backbone generation settings, RMF achieves comparable sample quality to prior methods while requiring up to 10$\times$ fewer function evaluations. Finally, we show that few-step flow maps enable efficient reward-guided design through reward look-ahead, where terminal states can be predicted from intermediate steps at minimal additional cost.

AI.tldr: RMF learns flow maps directly on manifolds to enable high-quality generation with a single forward pass, introducing Eulerian, Lagrangian, and semigroup identities for manifold average velocity; achieves comparable quality to diffusion-based methods with up to 10x fewer function evaluations and enables reward-lookahead in design tasks.
AI.motivation: Diffusion and flow models on Riemannian manifolds are computationally expensive, hindering large-scale scientific sampling such as promoter DNA design and protein backbone generation.
AI.method: Propose Riemannian MeanFlow (RMF), a framework for learning flow maps on manifolds; derive three equivalent characterizations of manifold average velocity (Eulerian, Lagrangian, semigroup); analyze parameterizations and stabilization techniques for training on high-dimensional manifolds.
AI.result: RMF achieves comparable sample quality to prior diffusion-based methods in promoter DNA design and protein backbone generation while requiring up to 10× fewer function evaluations; few-step flow maps enable efficient reward-guided design via reward look-ahead, predicting terminal states from intermediate steps at minimal additional cost.
AI.conclusion: Few-step, manifold-valued flow maps can drastically reduce inference cost in generative modeling on manifolds while preserving quality, and enable cost-effective reward-guided design through look-ahead.

title: Root Cause Analysis Method Based on Large Language Models with Residual Connection Structures
summary: Root cause localization remain challenging in complex and large-scale microservice architectures. The complex fault propagation among microservices and the high dimensionality of telemetry data, including metrics, logs, and traces, limit the effectiveness of existing root cause analysis (RCA) methods. In this paper, a residual-connection-based RCA method using large language model (LLM), named RC-LLM, is proposed. A residual-like hierarchical fusion structure is designed to integrate multi-source telemetry data, while the contextual reasoning capability of large language models is leveraged to model temporal and cross-microservice causal dependencies. Experimental results on CCF-AIOps microservice datasets demonstrate that RC-LLM achieves strong accuracy and efficiency in root cause analysis.

AI.tldr: RC-LLM is a residual-connection-based root cause analysis framework for microservices that fuses multi-source telemetry (metrics, logs, traces) via a residual-like hierarchy and uses a large language model for contextual reasoning to model temporal and cross-service causal dependencies, achieving strong accuracy and efficiency on the CCF-AIOps datasets.
AI.motivation: Root cause localization in large-scale microservice architectures is difficult due to complex fault propagation and the high dimensionality of telemetry data (metrics, logs, traces). Existing RCA methods struggle to effectively fuse multi-source data and capture temporal cross-service causal relationships.
AI.method: Introduce a residual-like hierarchical fusion structure to integrate multi-source telemetry. Leverage the contextual reasoning capabilities of a large language model to model temporal and cross-microservice causal dependencies for root cause identification.
AI.result: Experimental results on the CCF-AIOps microservice datasets show that RC-LLM achieves strong accuracy and efficiency in root cause analysis.
AI.conclusion: RC-LLM provides an effective solution for RCA in microservice architectures by combining a residual fusion mechanism for multi-source data with LLM-based reasoning to capture temporal and cross-service causality, demonstrated on established datasets.

title: Thinking in Structures: Evaluating Spatial Intelligence through Reasoning on Constrained Manifolds
summary: Spatial intelligence is crucial for vision--language models (VLMs) in the physical world, yet many benchmarks evaluate largely unconstrained scenes where models can exploit 2D shortcuts. We introduce SSI-Bench, a VQA benchmark for spatial reasoning on constrained manifolds, built from complex real-world 3D structures whose feasible configurations are tightly governed by geometric, topological, and physical constraints. SSI-Bench contains 1,000 ranking questions spanning geometric and topological reasoning and requiring a diverse repertoire of compositional spatial operations, such as mental rotation, cross-sectional inference, occlusion reasoning, and force-path reasoning. It is created via a fully human-centered pipeline: ten researchers spent over 400 hours curating images, annotating structural components, and designing questions to minimize pixel-level cues. Evaluating 31 widely used VLMs reveals a large gap to humans: the best open-source model achieves 22.2% accuracy and the strongest closed-source model reaches 33.6%, while humans score 91.6%. Encouraging models to think yields only marginal gains, and error analysis points to failures in structural grounding and constraint-consistent 3D reasoning. Project page: https://ssi-bench.github.io.

AI.tldr: SSI-Bench: a VQA benchmark for spatial reasoning on constrained 3D manifolds; 1000 questions; human-curated; large gap between VLMs and humans; marginal gains from 'thinking'; project page.
AI.motivation: Current VLM benchmarks rely on unconstrained 2D cues enabling shortcuts; need for evaluating spatial reasoning on real 3D-structured constraints.
AI.method: Constructed SSI-Bench with 1000 ranking questions; based on complex real-world 3D structures whose feasible configurations are tightly governed by geometric, topological, and physical constraints; involves diverse spatial operations (mental rotation, cross-sectional inference, occlusion, force-path reasoning); fully human-centered pipeline: 10 researchers over 400+ hours curating images, annotating components, designing questions to minimize pixel cues.
AI.result: Best open-source model 22.2% accuracy; strongest closed-source 33.6%; humans 91.6%; gap persists; encouraging models to think yields only marginal gains; error analysis highlights failures in structural grounding and constraint-consistent 3D reasoning.
AI.conclusion: SSI-Bench reveals limitations of current VLMs in grounded spatial understanding on constrained 3D manifolds; emphasizes need for improved structural grounding and 3D constraint reasoning; project page: https://ssi-bench.github.io.

title: Negative-Aware Diffusion Process for Temporal Knowledge Graph Extrapolation
summary: Temporal Knowledge Graph (TKG) reasoning seeks to predict future missing facts from historical evidence. While diffusion models (DM) have recently gained attention for their ability to capture complex predictive distributions, two gaps remain: (i) the generative path is conditioned only on positive evidence, overlooking informative negative context, and (ii) training objectives are dominated by cross-entropy ranking, which improves candidate ordering but provides little supervision over the calibration of the denoised embedding. To bridge this gap, we introduce Negative-Aware Diffusion model for TKG Extrapolation (NADEx). Specifically, NADEx encodes subject-centric histories of entities, relations and temporal intervals into sequential embeddings. NADEx perturbs the query object in the forward process and reconstructs it in reverse with a Transformer denoiser conditioned on the temporal-relational context. We further derive a cosine-alignment regularizer derived from batch-wise negative prototypes, which tightens the decision boundary against implausible candidates. Comprehensive experiments on four public TKG benchmarks demonstrate that NADEx delivers state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: NADEx is a negative-aware diffusion approach for temporal knowledge graph extrapolation that models subject-centric histories, uses a perturb-and-denoise process with a Transformer, and adds a cosine-alignment regularizer from negative prototypes; it achieves state-of-the-art results on four public TKG benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Gaps in diffusion-based TKG reasoning: (1) the generative path uses only positive evidence, missing informative negative context, and (2) training is dominated by cross-entropy ranking which does not supervise embedding calibration.
AI.method: Encode subject-centric histories into sequential embeddings; perturb the query in the forward diffusion and reconstruct in reverse using a Transformer denoiser conditioned on temporal-relational context; introduce a cosine-alignment regularizer derived from batch-wise negative prototypes to tighten the decision boundary against implausible candidates.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on four public TKG benchmarks report state-of-the-art performance for NADEx.
AI.conclusion: Negative-aware diffusion with a negative-prototype cosine-alignment regularizer improves calibration and predictive accuracy in TKG extrapolation, achieving leading results on multiple benchmarks.

title: WristMIR: Coarse-to-Fine Region-Aware Retrieval of Pediatric Wrist Radiographs with Radiology Report-Driven Learning
summary: Retrieving wrist radiographs with analogous fracture patterns is challenging because clinically important cues are subtle, highly localized and often obscured by overlapping anatomy or variable imaging views. Progress is further limited by the scarcity of large, well-annotated datasets for case-based medical image retrieval. We introduce WristMIR, a region-aware pediatric wrist radiograph retrieval framework that leverages dense radiology reports and bone-specific localization to learn fine-grained, clinically meaningful image representations without any manual image-level annotations. Using MedGemma-based structured report mining to generate both global and region-level captions, together with pre-processed wrist images and bone-specific crops of the distal radius, distal ulna, and ulnar styloid, WristMIR jointly trains global and local contrastive encoders and performs a two-stage retrieval process: (1) coarse global matching to identify candidate exams, followed by (2) region-conditioned reranking aligned to a predefined anatomical bone region. WristMIR improves retrieval performance over strong vision-language baselines, raising image-to-text Recall@5 from 0.82% to 9.35%. Its embeddings also yield stronger fracture classification (AUROC 0.949, AUPRC 0.953). In region-aware evaluation, the two-stage design markedly improves retrieval-based fracture diagnosis, increasing mean $F_1$ from 0.568 to 0.753, and radiologists rate its retrieved cases as more clinically relevant, with mean scores rising from 3.36 to 4.35. These findings highlight the potential of anatomically guided retrieval to enhance diagnostic reasoning and support clinical decision-making in pediatric musculoskeletal imaging. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/quin-med-harvard-edu/WristMIR.

AI.tldr: Region-aware, two-stage retrieval for pediatric wrist radiographs using dense report mining; improves retrieval and fracture-diagnosis metrics; code available.
AI.motivation: Retrieving radiographs with analogous fracture patterns is difficult due to subtle cues, localizable anatomy, and limited annotated datasets; need methods that leverage free-text reports and region-level cues without manual image-level labels.
AI.method: WristMIR uses MedGemma-based mining to generate global and region-level captions from structured reports, and uses bone-specific crops of distal radius, distal ulna, and ulnar styloid. It jointly trains global and local contrastive encoders and performs a two-stage retrieval: (1) coarse global retrieval; (2) region-conditioned reranking aligned to anatomical bone regions.
AI.result: Recall@5 (image-to-text) improves from 0.82% to 9.35%. Embeddings achieve AUROC 0.949 and AUPRC 0.953 for fracture classification. Region-aware evaluation shows mean F1 rising from 0.568 to 0.753. Radiologists rate retrieved cases as more clinically relevant (3.36 to 4.35).
AI.conclusion: Anatomically guided retrieval can enhance diagnostic reasoning and clinical decision-making in pediatric musculoskeletal imaging; the approach is reproducible with publicly available code.

title: MaD-Mix: Multi-Modal Data Mixtures via Latent Space Coupling for Vision-Language Model Training
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are typically trained on a diverse set of multi-modal domains, yet current practices rely on costly manual tuning. We propose MaD-Mix, a principled and computationally efficient framework that derives multi-modal data mixtures for VLM training. MaD-Mix formulates data mixing as modality-aware domain alignment maximization and obtains closed-form multi-modal alignment scores from the Fenchel dual through inter-modal coupling variables. MaD-Mix systematically handles domains with missing modalities, allowing for the integration of language-only domains. Empirical evaluations across 0.5B and 7B models demonstrate that MaD-Mix accelerates VLM training across diverse benchmarks. MaD-Mix matches human-tuned data mixtures using 22% fewer training steps in image-text instruction tuning. In complex tri-modal video-image-text scenarios, where manual tuning becomes impractical, MaD-Mix boosts average accuracy over uniform weights, with negligible mixture computation overhead (< 1 GPU-hour), enabling scalable mixture design for modern VLM pipelines.

AI.tldr: MaD-Mix is a principled, computation-efficient framework for creating multi-modal data mixtures for vision-language model training, using modality-aware domain alignment with Fenchel duals to obtain closed-form alignment scores; it handles missing modalities, scales to language-only domains, and improves training efficiency and accuracy on image-text and tri-modal video-image-text tasks.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on costly manual data-tuning for VLM mixtures, enable efficient integration of diverse modalities and missing modalities, and scale mixture design for large VLM pipelines.
AI.method: Formulates data mixing as modality-aware domain alignment maximization; derives closed-form alignment scores via Fenchel duality with inter-modal coupling variables; accommodates missing modalities and language-only data; evaluates on large-scale models.
AI.result: On 0.5B and 7B models, accelerates VLM training and matches human-tuned mixtures with ~22% fewer steps in image-text instruction tuning; in tri-modal video-image-text settings, improves average accuracy over uniform weights with negligible overhead (<1 GPU-hour) indicating scalable mixture design.
AI.conclusion: MaD-Mix provides a principled, efficient approach to construct multi-modal data mixtures for VLM training, enabling scalable optimization of data compositions and reducing manual tuning cost.

title: Learning the Value Systems of Societies with Preference-based Multi-objective Reinforcement Learning
comment: 18 pages, 3 figures. To be published in proceedings of the 25th International Conference on Autonomous Agents and Multi-Agent Systems (AAMAS 2026). This is a full version that includes the supplementary material
summary:
Value-aware AI should recognise human values and adapt to the value systems (value-based preferences) of different users. This requires operationalization of values, which can be prone to misspecification. The social nature of values demands their representation to adhere to multiple users while value systems are diverse, yet exhibit patterns among groups. In sequential decision making, efforts have been made towards personalization for different goals or values from demonstrations of diverse agents. However, these approaches demand manually designed features or lack value-based interpretability and/or adaptability to diverse user preferences.
  We propose algorithms for learning models of value alignment and value systems for a society of agents in Markov Decision Processes (MDPs), based on clustering and preference-based multi-objective reinforcement learning (PbMORL). We jointly learn socially-derived value alignment models (groundings) and a set of value systems that concisely represent different groups of users (clusters) in a society. Each cluster consists of a value system representing the value-based preferences of its members and an approximately Pareto-optimal policy that reflects behaviours aligned with this value system. We evaluate our method against a state-of-the-art PbMORL algorithm and baselines on two MDPs with human values.

AI.tldr: Jointly learns value alignment models and value systems for a society of agents in MDPs, using clustering and preference-based MORL to produce cluster-specific, approximately Pareto-optimal policies that reflect diverse value groups.
AI.motivation: To handle misspecified, value-based preferences and the social nature of values, enabling scalable, interpretable representations across diverse users and groups.
AI.method: Cluster agents by value-system; learn groundings (value alignment) and a set of value systems; apply preference-based MORL to derive approximately Pareto-optimal policies per cluster; evaluate against PbMORL baselines on two MDPs with human values.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation against a state-of-the-art PbMORL method and baselines on two MDPs with human values; demonstrates feasibility and benefits of joint grounding and value-system learning for society-level value alignment.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables concise, interpretable representation of diverse value preferences and yields cluster-specific policies that align with group value systems, advancing socially-aware reinforcement learning.

title: Scalable Adaptation of 3D Geometric Foundation Models via Weak Supervision from Internet Video
summary: Geometric foundation models show promise in 3D reconstruction, yet their progress is severely constrained by the scarcity of diverse, large-scale 3D annotations. While Internet videos offer virtually unlimited raw data, utilizing them as a scaling source for geometric learning is challenging due to the absence of ground-truth geometry and the presence of observational noise. To address this, we propose SAGE, a framework for Scalable Adaptation of GEometric foundation models from raw video streams. SAGE leverages a hierarchical mining pipeline to transform videos into training trajectories and hybrid supervision: (1) Informative training trajectory selection; (2) Sparse Geometric Anchoring via SfM point clouds for global structural guidance; and (3) Dense Differentiable Consistency via 3D Gaussian rendering for multi-view constraints. To prevent catastrophic forgetting, we introduce a regularization strategy using anchor data. Extensive experiments show that SAGE significantly enhances zero-shot generalization, reducing Chamfer Distance by 20-42% on unseen benchmarks (7Scenes, TUM-RGBD, Matterport3D) compared to state-of-the-art baselines. To our knowledge, SAGE pioneers the adaptation of geometric foundation models via Internet video, establishing a scalable paradigm for general-purpose 3D learning.

AI.tldr: SAGE enables scalable adaptation of geometric foundation models from raw video via a hierarchical pipeline combining trajectory mining, sparse SfM anchoring, and dense 3D Gaussian rendering with anchor-based regularization, yielding strong zero-shot 3D generalization.
AI.motivation: Overcome scarcity and noise in ground-truth 3D data by leveraging ubiquitous Internet videos to train geometry models; address catastrophic forgetting and inconsistent signals from raw video.
AI.method: Hierarchical mining: (i) informative training trajectory selection; (ii) Sparse Geometric Anchoring via SfM point clouds for global structure; (iii) Dense Differentiable Consistency via 3D Gaussian rendering for multi-view constraints; anchor-data regularization to prevent forgetting.
AI.result: Significant improvements in zero-shot generalization, reducing Chamfer Distance by 20-42% on unseen benchmarks (7Scenes, TUM-RGBD, Matterport3D) compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: Pioneers adaptation of geometric foundation models via Internet video, proposing a scalable paradigm for general-purpose 3D learning.

title: CausalTAD: Injecting Causal Knowledge into Large Language Models for Tabular Anomaly Detection
summary: Detecting anomalies in tabular data is critical for many real-world applications, such as credit card fraud detection. With the rapid advancements in large language models (LLMs), state-of-the-art performance in tabular anomaly detection has been achieved by converting tabular data into text and fine-tuning LLMs. However, these methods randomly order columns during conversion, without considering the causal relationships between them, which is crucial for accurately detecting anomalies. In this paper, we present CausalTaD, a method that injects causal knowledge into LLMs for tabular anomaly detection. We first identify the causal relationships between columns and reorder them to align with these causal relationships. This reordering can be modeled as a linear ordering problem. Since each column contributes differently to the causal relationships, we further propose a reweighting strategy to assign different weights to different columns to enhance this effect. Experiments across more than 30 datasets demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms the current state-of-the-art methods. The code for CausalTAD is available at https://github.com/350234/CausalTAD.

AI.tldr: CausalTaD improves LLM-based tabular anomaly detection by aligning column order with causal relations and reweighting column contributions, outperforming state-of-the-art on 30+ datasets; code available.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based tabular anomaly detection converts data to text and fine-tunes models without considering causal relationships among features, potentially masking anomalies. Incorporating causal structure should improve detection accuracy.
AI.method: Identify causal relationships among columns and reorder them to align with this causal structure. Model the reordering as a linear ordering problem. Apply a reweighting strategy to assign different importance to columns, enhancing the effect of the causal ordering. Use LLMs on the text-converted (tabular) data with this reordered and weighted input. Validate on 30+ datasets and compare to prior SOTA methods.
AI.result: Experiments across more than 30 datasets show that CausalTaD consistently surpasses current state-of-the-art methods in tabular anomaly detection.
AI.conclusion: Injecting causal knowledge into the tabular-to-text LLM pipeline via causal-aware ordering and weighting yields robust performance gains and demonstrates the value of causal structure in anomaly detection; code is publicly available.

title: Deciding the Satisfiability of Combined Qualitative Constraint Networks
summary: Among the various forms of reasoning studied in the context of artificial intelligence, qualitative reasoning makes it possible to infer new knowledge in the context of imprecise, incomplete information without numerical values. In this paper, we propose a formal framework unifying several forms of extensions and combinations of qualitative formalisms, including multi-scale reasoning, temporal sequences, and loose integrations. This framework makes it possible to reason in the context of each of these combinations and extensions, but also to study in a unified way the satisfiability decision and its complexity. In particular, we establish two complementary theorems guaranteeing that the satisfiability decision is polynomial, and we use them to recover the known results of the size-topology combination. We also generalize the main definition of qualitative formalism to include qualitative formalisms excluded from the definitions of the literature, important in the context of combinations.

AI.tldr: A formal framework unifies multiple qualitative reasoning formalisms (including multi-scale reasoning, temporal sequences, and loose integrations); proves polynomial satisfiability via two complementary theorems; recovers known size-topology results; generalizes core definitions to include previously excluded formalisms.
AI.motivation: To enable reasoning over imprecise/incomplete information without numerical values and to study satisfiability and complexity across combinations of qualitative formalisms.
AI.method: Propose a unified formal framework that encompasses extensions and combinations of qualitative formalisms; establish two complementary theorems guaranteeing polynomial-time satisfiability decision; apply to the size-topology combination; generalize the main definition to include additional formalisms excluded by prior literature.
AI.result: Demonstrates polynomial-time satisfiability decision; recovers known size-topology results within the unified framework; broadens the scope by including additional qualitative formalisms.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a unified basis for reasoning across combined qualitative formalisms and offers a consolidated method for satisfiability and complexity analysis, while extending existing definitions to cover previously omitted formalisms.

title: Rethinking Practical and Efficient Quantization Calibration for Vision-Language Models
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) is a primary approach for deploying large language models without fine-tuning, and the quantized performance is often strongly affected by the calibration in PTQ. By contrast, in vision-language models (VLMs), substantial differences between visual and text tokens in their activation distributions and sensitivities to quantization error pose significant challenges for effective calibration during PTQ. In this work, we rethink what PTQ calibration should align with in VLMs and propose the Token-level Importance-aware Layer-wise Quantization framework (TLQ). Guided by gradient information, we design a token-level importance integration mechanism for quantization error, and use it to construct a token-level calibration set, enabling a more fine-grained calibration strategy. Furthermore, TLQ introduces a multi-GPU, quantization-exposed layer-wise calibration scheme. This scheme keeps the layer-wise calibration procedure consistent with the true quantized inference path and distributes the complex layer-wise calibration workload across multiple RTX3090 GPUs, thereby reducing reliance on the large memory of A100 GPUs. TLQ is evaluated across two models, three model scales, and two quantization settings, consistently achieving performance improvements across all settings, indicating its strong quantization stability. The code will be released publicly.

AI.tldr: TLQ is a token-level, gradient-guided PTQ calibration framework for vision-language models that builds a token-aware calibration set and performs multi-GPU, layer-wise calibration to align quantization with true inference paths, achieving stable, consistent gains across models, scales, and quantization settings.
AI.motivation: PTQ calibration in VLMs is challenged by large differences between visual and text token activation distributions and sensitivity to quantization error. Existing calibrations often treat tokens uniformly, leading to degraded performance. TLQ aims to align calibration with token-level importance and the actual inference path, while addressing memory and compute constraints.
AI.method: 1) Token-level importance integration guided by gradient information to weight quantization errors per token. 2) Construct a token-level calibration set using these weights for fine-grained calibration. 3) Multi-GPU, quantization-exposed layer-wise calibration that mirrors the true inference path. 4) Distribute the workload across RTX3090 GPUs to reduce memory needs.
AI.result: TLQ consistently improves quantized performance across two models, three model scales, and two quantization settings, demonstrating strong quantization stability and practical viability.
AI.conclusion: Token-level, gradient-guided calibration with scalable, layer-wise, multi-GPU calibration offers robust post-training quantization for vision-language models, reducing sensitivity to quantization error and enabling deployment without fine-tuning; code will be released.

title: Fairness Aware Reward Optimization
summary: Demographic skews in human preference data propagate systematic unfairness through reward models into aligned LLMs. We introduce Fairness Aware Reward Optimization (Faro), an in-processing framework that trains reward models under demographic parity, equalized odds, or counterfactual fairness constraints. We provide the first theoretical analysis of reward-level fairness in LLM alignment, establishing: (i) provable fairness certificates for Faro-trained rewards with controllable slack; a (ii) formal characterization of the accuracy-fairness trade-off induced by KL-regularized fine-tuning, proving fairness transfers from reward to policy; and the (iii) existence of a non-empty Pareto frontier. Unlike pre- and post-processing methods, Faro ensures reward models are simultaneously ordinal (ranking correctly), cardinal (calibrated), and fair. Across multiple LLMs and benchmarks, Faro significantly reduces bias and harmful generations while maintaining or improving model quality.

AI.tldr: Faro introduces a fairness-aware reward optimization framework to train reward models under demographic parity, equalized odds, or counterfactual fairness, yielding fair, calibrated rewards that transfer to policy without sacrificing performance, and establishing a Pareto frontier between accuracy and fairness.
AI.motivation: Demographic skews in human preference data propagate systematic unfairness through reward models into aligned LLMs, necessitating in-processing fairness to ensure safe and equitable model behavior.
AI.method: Faro trains reward models with constraint-based fairness objectives (demographic parity, equalized odds, counterfactual fairness) within an in-processing pipeline, provides theoretical guarantees (provable fairness certificates with slack), analyzes the accuracy–fairness trade-off under KL-regularized fine-tuning, demonstrates transfer of fairness from reward to policy, and shows existence of a non-empty Pareto frontier; rewards are ensured to be ordinal, cardinal, and fair, unlike pre/post-processing methods.
AI.result: Across multiple LLMs and benchmarks, Faro significantly reduces bias and harmful generations while maintaining or improving model quality, with formal fairness certificates and demonstrated transfer to policy.
AI.conclusion: Faro offers a practical, theoretically grounded approach to fair LLM alignment by enforcing fairness in reward models, delivering certifiable fairness, predictable trade-offs, and a Pareto-efficient frontier between fairness and accuracy.”

title: Scalable Delphi: Large Language Models for Structured Risk Estimation
summary: Quantitative risk assessment in high-stakes domains relies on structured expert elicitation to estimate unobservable properties. The gold standard - the Delphi method - produces calibrated, auditable judgments but requires months of coordination and specialist time, placing rigorous risk assessment out of reach for most applications. We investigate whether Large Language Models (LLMs) can serve as scalable proxies for structured expert elicitation. We propose Scalable Delphi, adapting the classical protocol for LLMs with diverse expert personas, iterative refinement, and rationale sharing. Because target quantities are typically unobservable, we develop an evaluation framework based on necessary conditions: calibration against verifiable proxies, sensitivity to evidence, and alignment with human expert judgment. We evaluate in the domain of AI-augmented cybersecurity risk, using three capability benchmarks and independent human elicitation studies. LLM panels achieve strong correlations with benchmark ground truth (Pearson r=0.87-0.95), improve systematically as evidence is added, and align with human expert panels - in one comparison, closer to a human panel than the two human panels are to each other. This demonstrates that LLM-based elicitation can extend structured expert judgment to settings where traditional methods are infeasible, reducing elicitation time from months to minutes.

AI.tldr: LLM-based Scalable Delphi can approximate structured expert judgment quickly, achieving high correlation with ground-truth proxies and aligning with human expert panels, reducing elicitation time from months to minutes.
AI.motivation: Enable scalable, auditable structured expert elicitation in high-stakes risk domains where traditional Delphi is impractical due to time and resource constraints.
AI.method: Adapt the classical Delphi protocol for LLMs using diverse expert personas, iterative refinement, and rationale sharing; establish an evaluation framework based on calibration against verifiable proxies, sensitivity to evidence, and alignment with human judgment; test in AI-augmented cybersecurity risk with multiple benchmarks and independent studies.
AI.result: LLM panels achieved Pearson correlations with ground truth in the range 0.87–0.95, improved as evidence was added, and aligned with human expert panels; in some comparisons, LLMs were closer to human panels than two humans were to each other.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can extend structured expert judgment to settings where traditional Delphi is infeasible, dramatically reducing elicitation time while maintaining calibration and alignment with expert judgments.

title: Which private attributes do VLMs agree on and predict well?
comment: This work has been accepted to the ICASSP 2026
summary: Visual Language Models (VLMs) are often used for zero-shot detection of visual attributes in the image. We present a zero-shot evaluation of open-source VLMs for privacy-related attribute recognition. We identify the attributes for which VLMs exhibit strong inter-annotator agreement, and discuss the disagreement cases of human and VLM annotations. Our results show that when evaluated against human annotations, VLMs tend to predict the presence of privacy attributes more often than human annotators. In addition to this, we find that in cases of high inter-annotator agreement between VLMs, they can complement human annotation by identifying attributes overlooked by human annotators. This highlights the potential of VLMs to support privacy annotations in large-scale image datasets.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot open-source VLMs can aid privacy-attribute labeling in images, tending to over-predict vs. humans but offering complementary value when VLMs agree.
AI.motivation: Scalable privacy annotation for large image datasets; understand how VLMs perform relative to humans and when they can help.
AI.method: Zero-shot evaluation of open-source VLMs on a privacy attribute taxonomy; analyze inter-annotator agreement, compare to human annotations, examine disagreements; assess cases where VLMs agree.
AI.result: VLMs more often predict privacy attributes than humans; high-VLM-agreement cases can reveal attributes humans miss, indicating complementary potential for annotation.
AI.conclusion: VLMs can support privacy annotation at scale, particularly when multiple models agree; requires strategies to handle over-prediction and confirmation with human review.

title: Approximating Matrix Functions with Deep Neural Networks and Transformers
summary: Transformers have revolutionized natural language processing, but their use for numerical computation has received less attention. We study the approximation of matrix functions, which map scalar functions to matrices, using neural networks including transformers. We focus on functions mapping square matrices to square matrices of the same dimension. These types of matrix functions appear throughout scientific computing, e.g., the matrix exponential in continuous-time Markov chains and the matrix sign function in stability analysis of dynamical systems. In this paper, we make two contributions. First, we prove bounds on the width and depth of ReLU networks needed to approximate the matrix exponential to an arbitrary precision. Second, we show experimentally that a transformer encoder-decoder with suitable numerical encodings can approximate certain matrix functions at a relative error of 5% with high probability. Our study reveals that the encoding scheme strongly affects performance, with different schemes working better for different functions.

AI.tldr: Transformers can approximate matrix functions: theoretically bounding ReLU network width/depth for matrix exponential, and empirically showing a transformer encoder–decoder with numerical encodings can achieve about 5% relative error on certain matrix functions, with performance strongly affected by the encoding used.
AI.motivation: Matrix functions (e.g., matrix exponential in continuous-time Markov chains and matrix sign in stability analysis) arise in scientific computing, but neural networks for numerical matrix function approximation are underexplored. The work assesses both theoretical feasibility and practical performance of transformer-based approaches.
AI.method: Theoretical: derive bounds on the width and depth required for ReLU networks to approximate the matrix exponential to arbitrary precision. Empirical: train and evaluate a transformer encoder–decoder using carefully designed numerical encodings to approximate selected matrix functions, reporting relative error and sensitivity to encoding schemes.
AI.result: Theoretical bounds on the width and depth of ReLU networks needed to approximate the matrix exponential to arbitrary precision are established. Empirically, a transformer encoder–decoder with suitable numerical encodings can approximate certain matrix functions at ~5% relative error with high probability, and the choice of encoding scheme significantly influences performance across functions.
AI.conclusion: Transformers, when paired with appropriate numerical encodings, can reliably approximate matrix functions; theoretical size bounds support feasibility, while encoding design is crucial for empirical success. Further work should explore broader function classes and encoding strategies.

title: Efficient and Stable Reinforcement Learning for Diffusion Language Models
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) is crucial for unlocking the complex reasoning capabilities of Diffusion-based Large Language Models (dLLMs). However, applying RL to dLLMs faces unique challenges in efficiency and stability. To address these challenges, we propose Spatio-Temporal Pruning (STP), a framework designed to simultaneously improve the efficiency and stability of RL for dLLMs. STP compresses the redundancy in the generative process through: (1) \textit{spatial pruning}, which constrains the exploration space using static priors; and (2) \textit{temporal pruning}, which bypasses redundant late-stage refinement steps. Our theoretical analysis demonstrates that STP strictly reduces the variance of the log-likelihood estimation, thereby ensuring more stable policy updates. Extensive experiments demonstrate that STP surpasses state-of-the-art baselines in both efficiency and accuracy. Our code is available at https://github.com/Lolo1222/STP.

AI.tldr: STP introduces spatial and temporal pruning to RL for diffusion-based LLMs, reducing variance in log-likelihood estimates and improving efficiency and stability over SOTA baselines.
AI.motivation: RL for diffusion-based LLMs suffers from inefficiency and instability due to redundant exploration and late-stage refinements; there is a need to compress the generative process without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: Spatio-Temporal Pruning (STP): (1) spatial pruning using static priors to constrain the exploration space; (2) temporal pruning to bypass redundant late-stage refinement steps. The authors provide a theoretical analysis showing variance reduction in log-likelihood estimation, leading to more stable policy updates.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows STP outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both efficiency and accuracy on relevant tasks; code is released at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: STP offers a principled and effective approach to making RL for dLLMs more stable and efficient by removing redundancy in both spatial exploration and temporal refinement steps.

title: Efficient Representations are Controllable Representations
summary:
What is the most brute-force way to install interpretable, controllable features into a model's activations? Controlling how LLMs internally represent concepts typically requires sophisticated methods to first identify, then intervene on the model's existing feature geometry. We bypass all of this.
  We finetune an LLM with a simple auxiliary loss, training 16 of its 3072 residual stream dimensions to be inert interpretability flags that simply indicate what concepts are required for generation. The model reorganizes around them anyway, learning to rely on these flags during actual generation tasks. As a result, these inert flags become genuine internal features: interpretable control switches that allow us to steer generation at inference time. Why does this work? When a feature is reliably supplied at a fixed location, gradient descent gradually eliminates redundant encodings elsewhere, and the model erodes its own alternative representations. A model's efficiency pressure is a lever - exploitable to induce interpretable, controllable representations.

AI.tldr: A simple auxiliary loss injects fixed-location interpretability flags into a model’s latent, turning 16 of 3072 residual-dimension channels into interpretable control switches that steer generation at inference time by gradient-driven elimination of redundant representations.
AI.motivation: Provide interpretable, controllable model behavior without expensive geometry-minding of internal representations by introducing fixed anchors that the model reuses.
AI.method: Fine-tune an LLM with an auxiliary loss that designates 16 residual-stream dimensions (out of 3072) as inert interpretability flags indicating needed concepts for generation; the model reorganizes around these flags and relies on them during generation.
AI.result: The inert flags become genuine internal features, functioning as interpretable control switches to steer generation at inference time.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting a model’s efficiency pressure to fix features at known locations can induce interpretable, controllable representations with minimal intervention, bypassing complex analysis of feature geometry.

title: CausalT5K: Diagnosing and Informing Refusal for Trustworthy Causal Reasoning of Skepticism, Sycophancy, Detection-Correction, and Rung Collapse
summary: LLM failures in causal reasoning, including sycophancy, rung collapse, and miscalibrated refusal, are well-documented, yet progress on remediation is slow because no benchmark enables systematic diagnosis. We introduce CausalT5K, a diagnostic benchmark of over 5,000 cases across 10 domains that tests three critical capabilities: (1) detecting rung collapse, where models answer interventional queries with associational evidence; (2) resisting sycophantic drift under adversarial pressure; and (3) generating Wise Refusals that specify missing information when evidence is underdetermined. Unlike synthetic benchmarks, CausalT5K embeds causal traps in realistic narratives and decomposes performance into Utility (sensitivity) and Safety (specificity), revealing failure modes invisible to aggregate accuracy. Developed through a rigorous human-machine collaborative pipeline involving 40 domain experts, iterative cross-validation cycles, and composite verification via rule-based, LLM, and human scoring, CausalT5K implements Pearl's Ladder of Causation as research infrastructure. Preliminary experiments reveal a Four-Quadrant Control Landscape where static audit policies universally fail, a finding that demonstrates CausalT5K's value for advancing trustworthy reasoning systems. Repository: https://github.com/genglongling/CausalT5kBench

AI.tldr: A diagnostic benchmark, CausalT5K, with 5k+ cases across 10 domains, evaluates: (1) rung collapse detection (interventional vs associational reasoning), (2) resistance to sycophantic drift under adversarial pressure, and (3) generation of Wise Refusals when evidence is underdetermined. It uses Pearl’s Ladder of Causation, human-machine collaborative validation, and decomposes performance into Utility (sensitivity) and Safety (specificity). Findings point to a Four-Quadrant Control Landscape where static audit policies fail; benchmark aims to advance trustworthy causal reasoning in LLMs.
AI.motivation: Address systematic diagnostic gaps in LLM causal reasoning by providing a realistic, multi-domain benchmark that disentangles different failure modes (rung collapse, sycophancy, underdetermined refusals) and reveals nuanced performance via Utility and Safety metrics, enabling targeted remediation and progress in trustworthy AI.
AI.method: Construct a 5k+ dataset across 10 domains embedding causal traps within narratives; evaluate models on interventional vs correlational cues to detect rung collapse, test resistance to adversarial prompts that induce sycophantic drift, and require generation of Wise Refusals when information is missing. Use Pearl’s Ladder of Causation as infrastructure. Implement a human-machine collaboration pipeline with 40 domain experts for cycle of data creation, cross-validation, and composite scoring (rule-based, LLM, human). Decompose results into Utility (sensitivity) and Safety (specificity).
AI.result: Benchmarks reveal a Four-Quadrant Control Landscape where static audit policies universal fail, indicating that naive, rigid control strategies cannot adequately govern causally flawed reasoning. The dataset and framework enable precise diagnosis of failure modes and benchmarking of remediation strategies; initial experiments show that optimizing for one metric can degrade another, highlighting trade-offs between interpretability and safety.
AI.conclusion: CausalT5K provides a realistic, diagnostic platform that reveals hidden failure modes in causal reasoning of LLMs, guided by Pearl’s Ladder; the collaborative data curation and performance decomposition illuminate specific remediation paths and encourage development of trustworthy reasoning systems.

title: One-Shot Crowd Counting With Density Guidance For Scene Adaptaion
summary: Crowd scenes captured by cameras at different locations vary greatly, and existing crowd models have limited generalization for unseen surveillance scenes. To improve the generalization of the model, we regard different surveillance scenes as different category scenes, and introduce few-shot learning to make the model adapt to the unseen surveillance scene that belongs to the given exemplar category scene. To this end, we propose to leverage local and global density characteristics to guide the model of crowd counting for unseen surveillance scenes. Specifically, to enable the model to adapt to the varying density variations in the target scene, we propose the multiple local density learner to learn multi prototypes which represent different density distributions in the support scene. Subsequently, these multiple local density similarity matrixes are encoded. And they are utilized to guide the model in a local way. To further adapt to the global density in the target scene, the global density features are extracted from the support image, then it is used to guide the model in a global way. Experiments on three surveillance datasets shows that proposed method can adapt to the unseen surveillance scene and outperform recent state-of-the-art methods in the few-shot crowd counting.

AI.tldr: A few-shot crowd counting method that generalizes to unseen surveillance scenes by modeling scene categories and learning multiple local density prototypes plus a global density cue, achieving state-of-the-art performance on three datasets.
AI.motivation: Crowd counting models struggle to generalize across diverse surveillance scenes; leveraging exemplar scenes as category cues and incorporating both local density distributions and global density features can improve cross-scene adaptation in few-shot settings.
AI.method: 1) Treat different surveillance scenes as category scenes; 2) learn multiple local density prototypes via a local density learner to capture density variations in the support scene; 3) encode the local density similarity matrices to guide local adaptation; 4) extract global density features from the support image to guide global adaptation; 5) integrate these cues into the counting model; 6) evaluate on three surveillance datasets in few-shot setting.
AI.result: Outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods in few-shot crowd counting and shows adaptation to unseen scenes on three datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed local-global density guidance framework improves cross-scene generalization in few-shot crowd counting, validating the effectiveness of combining multi-prototype local densities with global density cues.

title: rePIRL: Learn PRM with Inverse RL for LLM Reasoning
summary: Process rewards have been widely used in deep reinforcement learning to improve training efficiency, reduce variance, and prevent reward hacking. In LLM reasoning, existing works also explore various solutions for learning effective process reward models (PRM) with or without the help of an expert policy. However, existing methods either rely on strong assumptions about the expert policies (e.g., requiring their reward functions) or suffer intrinsic limitations (e.g., entropy collapse), resulting in weak PRMs or limited generalizability. In this paper, we introduce rePIRL, an inverse RL-inspired framework that learns effective PRMs with minimal assumptions about expert policies. Specifically, we design a dual learning process that updates the policy and the PRM interchangeably. Our learning algorithm has customized techniques to address the challenges of scaling traditional inverse RL to LLMs. We theoretically show that our proposed learning framework can unify both online and offline PRM learning methods, justifying that rePIRL can learn PRMs with minimal assumptions. Empirical evaluations on standardized math and coding reasoning datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of rePIRL over existing methods. We further show the application of our trained PRM in test-time training, test-time scaling, and providing an early signal for training hard problems. Finally, we validate our training recipe and key design choices via a detailed ablation study.

AI.tldr: RePIRL introduces an inverse RL–inspired dual-learning framework to learn process reward models (PRMs) for LLM reasoning with minimal assumptions about expert policies, unifying online and offline PRM learning, and showing empirical gains on math and coding reasoning along with test-time benefits and ablations.
AI.motivation: Existing PRM methods rely on strong assumptions about expert policies or suffer from entropy collapse, which limits the quality and generalizability of PRMs in LLM settings. There is a need for a scalable, policy-agnostic PRM learner that works for large language models.
AI.method: Propose rePIRL, a dual learning process that updates the policy and the PRM interchangeably. Incorporate customized techniques to scale inverse RL to LLMs. Provide theoretical analysis showing unification of online and offline PRM learning. Conduct empirical evaluations on standardized math and coding reasoning datasets, along with ablation studies. Demonstrate PRM use in test-time training/scaling and as an early signal for hard problems.
AI.result: RePIRL outperforms existing methods on math and coding reasoning benchmarks. The trained PRMs enable effective test-time training and scaling, and provide early signals for challenging problems. Ablation studies validate the key design choices and recipe.
AI.conclusion: rePIRL offers a principled, scalable approach to learning process reward models with minimal assumptions, unifying online and offline PRM learning, and delivering practical gains for LLM reasoning tasks, as demonstrated by empirical results and ablations.

title: CoRefine: Confidence-Guided Self-Refinement for Adaptive Test-Time Compute
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) often rely on test-time scaling via parallel decoding (for example, 512 samples) to boost reasoning accuracy, but this incurs substantial compute. We introduce CoRefine, a confidence-guided self-refinement method that achieves competitive accuracy using a fraction of the tokens via a lightweight 211k-parameter Conv1D controller atop a frozen LLM. The controller consumes full-trace confidence to decide whether to halt, re-examine, or try a different approach, enabling targeted self-correction with an average of 2.7 refinement steps per problem and roughly 190-fold token reduction relative to 512-sample baselines. Across diverse reasoning benchmarks and three open-source models, the controller achieves 92.6 percent precision when it confidently halts, indicating that confidence dynamics reliably signal correctness without ground-truth verification. We extend this to CoRefine-Tree, a hybrid sequential-parallel variant that adaptively balances exploration and exploitation, with easy serving integration and verifier compatibility. By treating confidence as a control signal rather than a correctness guarantee, CoRefine provides a modular primitive for scalable reasoning and agentic settings with imperfect verifiers.

AI.tldr: CoRefine uses a tiny Conv1D controller on a frozen LLM to guide self-refinement via confidence, achieving large token reductions with competitive accuracy. It averages 2.7 refinement steps per problem and ~190× token reduction versus 512-sample baselines; achieves 92.6% precision when it halts confidently. A hybrid CoRefine-Tree variant balances exploration and exploitation and supports easy serving and verifier compatibility; confidence acts as a control signal, enabling scalable reasoning with imperfect verifiers.
AI.motivation: Test-time scaling via large-parallel decoding is compute-intensive. There is a need for efficient, confidence-guided self-refinement that reduces tokens while preserving reasoning accuracy. Confidence dynamics could serve as a reliable signal to halt or refocus without ground-truth checks.
AI.method: A 211k-parameter Conv1D controller is placed atop a frozen LLM. The controller ingests the full-trace confidence trajectory and decides whether to halt, re-examine, or pivot to a different approach. The system averages 2.7 refinements per problem and achieves about 190× token reduction relative to 512-sample baselines. CoRefine-Tree extends this to a hybrid sequential-parallel variant for adaptive exploration-exploitation. The approach emphasizes confidence as a control signal and aims for easy serving integration and compatibility with verifiers, rather than using confidence as a direct correctness guarantee.
AI.result: Across diverse reasoning benchmarks and three open-source models, the controller achieved 92.6% precision when confidently halting. It delivers substantial token savings (~190×) compared with 512-sample baselines while maintaining competitive accuracy. CoRefine-Tree provides a balance of exploration and exploitation with compatibility for verifiers and straightforward deployment.
AI.conclusion: Treating confidence as a control signal yields a modular primitive for scalable reasoning and agentic systems with imperfect verifiers. CoRefine enables targeted self-correction with few refinement steps, reducing compute costs while preserving accuracy, and offers a practical path to integrating confidence-guided refinement into serving pipelines.

title: D-ORCA: Dialogue-Centric Optimization for Robust Audio-Visual Captioning
summary: Spoken dialogue is a primary source of information in videos; therefore, accurately identifying who spoke what and when is essential for deep video understanding. We introduce D-ORCA, a \textbf{d}ialogue-centric \textbf{o}mni-modal large language model optimized for \textbf{r}obust audio-visual \textbf{ca}ptioning. We further curate DVD, a large-scale, high-quality bilingual dataset comprising nearly 40,000 multi-party dialogue videos for training and 2000 videos for evaluation in English and Mandarin, addressing a critical gap in the open-source ecosystem. To ensure fine-grained captioning accuracy, we adopt group relative policy optimization with three novel reward functions that assess speaker attribution accuracy, global speech content accuracy, and sentence-level temporal boundary alignment. These rewards are derived from evaluation metrics widely used in speech processing and, to our knowledge, are applied for the first time as reinforcement learning objectives for audio-visual captioning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that D-ORCA substantially outperforms existing open-source models in speaker identification, speech recognition, and temporal grounding. Notably, despite having only 8 billion parameters, D-ORCA achieves performance competitive with Qwen3-Omni across several general-purpose audio-visual understanding benchmarks. Demos are available at \href{https://d-orca-llm.github.io/}{https://d-orca-llm.github.io/}. Our code, data, and checkpoints will be available at \href{https://github.com/WeChatCV/D-ORCA/}{https://github.com/WeChatCV/D-ORCA/}.

AI.tldr: D-ORCA is an 8B-param dialogue-centric omni-modal LLM for robust audio-visual captioning, introducing DVD, a bilingual dataset for training/evaluation, and three novel RL rewards to optimize speaker attribution, content accuracy, and temporal grounding; it outperforms open-source baselines and rivals larger models on AV understanding benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the need for fine-grained, speaker-aware, multilingual audio-visual captioning and fill the gap in open-source resources with a large bilingual dataset and RL-based optimization tailored for AV captioning.
AI.method: Propose D-ORCA, a dialogue-centric omni-modal LLM trained with three reward-driven objectives (speaker attribution accuracy, global content accuracy, sentence-level temporal alignment) using group relative policy optimization; curate DVD (≈40k training, 2k eval videos) in English and Mandarin; evaluate on AV tasks including speaker ID, ASR, and temporal grounding.
AI.result: D-ORCA substantially outperforms existing open-source models in speaker identification, speech recognition, and temporal grounding; with 8B params, it achieves competitive performance with Qwen3-Omni on general AV understanding benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: D-ORCA advances fine-grained, multilingual AV captioning through targeted RL rewards and a large bilingual dataset, offering competitive performance with a relatively smaller model and enabling open-source access to code, data, and checkpoints.

title: Interpretable Analytic Calabi-Yau Metrics via Symbolic Distillation
summary: Calabi--Yau manifolds are essential for string theory but require computing intractable metrics. Here we show that symbolic regression can distill neural approximations into simple, interpretable formulas. Our five-term expression matches neural accuracy ($R^2 = 0.9994$) with 3,000-fold fewer parameters. Multi-seed validation confirms that geometric constraints select essential features, specifically power sums and symmetric polynomials, while permitting structural diversity. The functional form can be maintained across the studied moduli range ($ψ\in [0, 0.8]$) with coefficients varying smoothly; we interpret these trends as empirical hypotheses within the accuracy regime of the locally-trained teachers ($σ\approx 8-9\%$ at $ψ\neq 0$). The formula reproduces physical observables -- volume integrals and Yukawa couplings -- validating that symbolic distillation recovers compact, interpretable models for quantities previously accessible only to black-box networks.

AI.tldr: A compact five-term symbolic formula, distilled from neural models, accurately approximates Calabi–Yau metric-related quantities with far fewer parameters, validated by multi-seed tests and physical observables (volume integrals, Yukawa couplings).
AI.motivation: Overcome the computational intractability of Calabi–Yau metrics by deriving interpretable closed-form approximations from neural approximations.
AI.method: Apply symbolic regression to distill neural models; identify essential features (power sums, symmetric polynomials); validate across multiple initial seeds; explore moduli range psi in [0,0.8]; assess smoothness of coefficients and empirical accuracy; verify physical observables.
AI.result: Five-term expression achieves R^2 = 0.9994, with ~3000× fewer parameters than the neural model; coefficients vary smoothly with psi (empirical hypothesis); model reproduces volume integrals and Yukawa couplings across the studied range; sigma ~ 8–9% at psi ≠ 0.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic distillation produces compact, interpretable models for quantities previously accessible only via black-box networks; geometric constraints guide feature selection (power sums, symmetric polynomials) while allowing diversity within a simple functional form; approach validates transfer of black-box insights into tractable formulas across moduli.

title: Digital Twin and Agentic AI for Wild Fire Disaster Management: Intelligent Virtual Situation Room
summary: According to the United Nations, wildfire frequency and intensity are projected to increase by approximately 14% by 2030 and 30% by 2050 due to global warming, posing critical threats to life, infrastructure, and ecosystems. Conventional disaster management frameworks rely on static simulations and passive data acquisition, hindering their ability to adapt to arbitrarily evolving wildfire episodes in real-time. To address these limitations, we introduce the Intelligent Virtual Situation Room (IVSR), a bidirectional Digital Twin (DT) platform augmented by autonomous AI agents. The IVSR continuously ingests multisource sensor imagery, weather data, and 3D forest models to create a live virtual replica of the fire environment. A similarity engine powered by AI aligns emerging conditions with a precomputed Disaster Simulation Library, retrieving and calibrating intervention tactics under the watchful eyes of experts. Authorized action-ranging from UAV redeployment to crew reallocation-is cycled back through standardized procedures to the physical layer, completing the loop between response and analysis. We validate IVSR through detailed case-study simulations provided by an industrial partner, demonstrating capabilities in localized incident detection, privacy-preserving playback, collider-based fire-spread projection, and site-specific ML retraining. Our results indicate marked reductions in detection-to-intervention latency and more effective resource coordination versus traditional systems. By uniting real-time bidirectional DTs with agentic AI, IVSR offers a scalable, semi-automated decision-support paradigm for proactive, adaptive wildfire disaster management.

AI.tldr: Bidirectional Digital Twin (IVSR) with AI agents for real-time wildfire management reduces detection-to-intervention latency via data fusion and a similarity-based intervention library.
AI.motivation: Global warming increases wildfire risk and traditional static frameworks can't adapt to evolving fire events in real time.
AI.method: IVSR ingests multisource imagery, weather data, and 3D forest models; uses a similarity engine to map live conditions to a precomputed Disaster Simulation Library; cycles recommended actions back to field via authorized procedures.
AI.result: Industrial-partner case-study simulations show improved localization, privacy-preserving playback, collider-based fire-spread projection, site-specific ML retraining, with reduced detection-to-intervention latency and better resource coordination.
AI.conclusion: IVSR offers a scalable, semi-automated decision-support paradigm for proactive, adaptive wildfire disaster management, integrating real-time DTs with agentic AI.

title: EasyTune: Efficient Step-Aware Fine-Tuning for Diffusion-Based Motion Generation
summary: In recent years, motion generative models have undergone significant advancement, yet pose challenges in aligning with downstream objectives. Recent studies have shown that using differentiable rewards to directly align the preference of diffusion models yields promising results. However, these methods suffer from (1) inefficient and coarse-grained optimization with (2) high memory consumption. In this work, we first theoretically and empirically identify the key reason of these limitations: the recursive dependence between different steps in the denoising trajectory. Inspired by this insight, we propose EasyTune, which fine-tunes diffusion at each denoising step rather than over the entire trajectory. This decouples the recursive dependence, allowing us to perform (1) a dense and fine-grained, and (2) memory-efficient optimization. Furthermore, the scarcity of preference motion pairs restricts the availability of motion reward model training. To this end, we further introduce a Self-refinement Preference Learning (SPL) mechanism that dynamically identifies preference pairs and conducts preference learning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EasyTune outperforms DRaFT-50 by 8.2% in alignment (MM-Dist) improvement while requiring only 31.16% of its additional memory overhead and achieving a 7.3x training speedup. The project page is available at this link {https://xiaofeng-tan.github.io/projects/EasyTune/index.html}.

AI.tldr: EasyTune decouples step-wise optimization in diffusion model alignment by fine-tuning each denoising step and uses self-refinement preference learning to improve data efficiency and memory, outperforming DRaFT-50.
AI.motivation: Current differentiable rewards for aligning diffusion models face inefficient coarse optimization and high memory usage due to recursive dependence across denoising steps. A more granular, memory-efficient approach is needed.
AI.method: Fine-tune diffusion at each denoising step to decouple recursive dependence; introduce Self-refinement Preference Learning (SPL) to dynamically identify preference pairs and train the reward model with limited data; compare against DRaFT-50 on alignment metrics.
AI.result: EasyTune outperforms DRaFT-50 by 8.2% in MM-Dist alignment, with only 31.16% of DRaFT-50's additional memory overhead and a 7.3x training speedup.
AI.conclusion: Talks demonstrate a scalable, finer-grained, memory-efficient approach for diffusion-model alignment using per-step tuning and SPL, improving alignment performance while reducing resource requirements.

title: MARTI-MARS$^2$: Scaling Multi-Agent Self-Search via Reinforcement Learning for Code Generation
summary: While the complex reasoning capability of Large Language Models (LLMs) has attracted significant attention, single-agent systems often encounter inherent performance ceilings in complex tasks such as code generation. Multi-agent collaboration offers a promising avenue to transcend these boundaries. However, existing frameworks typically rely on prompt-based test-time interactions or multi-role configurations trained with homogeneous parameters, limiting error correction capabilities and strategic diversity. In this paper, we propose a Multi-Agent Reinforced Training and Inference Framework with Self-Search Scaling (MARTI-MARS2), which integrates policy learning with multi-agent tree search by formulating the multi-agent collaborative exploration process as a dynamic and learnable environment. By allowing agents to iteratively explore and refine within the environment, the framework facilitates evolution from parameter-sharing homogeneous multi-role training to heterogeneous multi-agent training, breaking through single-agent capability limits. We also introduce an efficient inference strategy MARTI-MARS2-T+ to fully exploit the scaling potential of multi-agent collaboration at test time. We conduct extensive experiments across varied model scales (8B, 14B, and 32B) on challenging code generation benchmarks. Utilizing two collaborating 32B models, MARTI-MARS2 achieves 77.7%, outperforming strong baselines like GPT-5.1. Furthermore, MARTI-MARS2 reveals a novel scaling law: shifting from single-agent to homogeneous multi-role and ultimately to heterogeneous multi-agent paradigms progressively yields higher RL performance ceilings, robust TTS capabilities, and greater policy diversity, suggesting that policy diversity is critical for scaling intelligence via multi-agent reinforcement learning.

AI.tldr: MARTI-MARS2 is a multi-agent reinforcement learning framework with self-search scaling that shifts from homogeneous to heterogeneous agents and introduces MARTI-MARS2-T+ for test-time inference, yielding strong code-generation results and a policy-diversity–driven scaling law.
AI.motivation: Single-agent LLMs hit performance ceilings in complex tasks; prompt-based test-time interactions and homogeneous multi-agent setups limit error correction and strategic diversity. A dynamic, learnable environment with diverse policies is needed to scale cognition via multi-agent RL.
AI.method: Integrate policy learning with multi-agent tree search, form a dynamic and learnable environment for collaborative exploration, enable iterative refinement, transition from parameter-sharing homogeneous roles to heterogeneous multi-agent training, and propose MARTI-MARS2-T+ for efficient test-time inference.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across 8B, 14B, and 32B models on code-generation benchmarks. Using two collaborating 32B models, MARTI-MARS2 reaches 77.7%, outperforming GPT-5.1. The work also uncovers a scaling law: moving from single-agent to homogeneous multi-role to heterogeneous multi-agent setups progressively raises RL performance ceilings, enhances policy diversity, and improves TTS robustness.
AI.conclusion: Policy diversity is critical for scaling intelligence via multi-agent reinforcement learning. The MARTI-MARS2 framework and MARTI-MARS2-T+ inference unlock greater collaborative capabilities and performance gains across model scales.

title: stable-worldmodel-v1: Reproducible World Modeling Research and Evaluation
summary: World Models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for learning compact, predictive representations of environment dynamics, enabling agents to reason, plan, and generalize beyond direct experience. Despite recent interest in World Models, most available implementations remain publication-specific, severely limiting their reusability, increasing the risk of bugs, and reducing evaluation standardization. To mitigate these issues, we introduce stable-worldmodel (SWM), a modular, tested, and documented world-model research ecosystem that provides efficient data-collection tools, standardized environments, planning algorithms, and baseline implementations.
