We present a comparative evaluation of four classification models: Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), Gradient Boosting, TabNet, and SAINT, using a benchmark dataset from the UCI Machine Learning Repository consisting of biomedical voice measurements from PD patients and healthy controls.
  Experimental results show that SAINT consistently outperformed all baseline models across multiple evaluation metrics, achieving a weighted precision of 0.98, weighted recall of 0.97, weighted F1-score of 0.97, a Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) of 0.9990, and the highest Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC-ROC). TabNet and MLP demonstrated competitive performance, while Gradient Boosting yielded the lowest overall scores. The superior performance of SAINT is attributed to its dual attention mechanism, which effectively models feature interactions within and across samples.
  These findings demonstrate the diagnostic potential of attention-based deep learning architectures for early Parkinson's disease detection and highlight the importance of dynamic feature representation in clinical prediction tasks.

AI.tldr: SAINT-based attention model achieves state-of-the-art performance for early Parkinson's disease detection on a tabular UCI dataset of biomedical voice measurements, outperforming MLP, TabNet, and Gradient Boosting with near-perfect metrics; Tabs: strong feature-interaction modeling via dual attention.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of traditional ML in capturing complex feature interactions in tabular biomedical data for early PD detection; evaluate attention-based architectures (SAINT, TabNet) against conventional baselines.
AI.method: Comparative evaluation of four classifiers (MLP, Gradient Boosting, TabNet, SAINT) on a benchmark PD dataset from UCI (biomedical voice measurements). Use weighted metrics (precision, recall, F1, MCC, AUC-ROC) to assess performance; emphasize SAINT's dual attention mechanism modeling intra- and inter-sample feature interactions.
AI.result: SAINT consistently outperforms all baselines across evaluated metrics (weighted precision 0.98, weighted recall 0.97, weighted F1 0.97, MCC 0.9990, highest AUC-ROC). TabNet and MLP competitive; Gradient Boosting underperforms.
AI.conclusion: Attention-based deep learning architectures, particularly SAINT with dual attention, effectively model feature interactions in tabular biomedical data for PD detection, demonstrating strong potential for early clinical prediction tasks.

title: A Thermodynamic Theory of Learning Part II: Critical Period Closure and Continual Learning Failure
comment: Part II of a series entitled "A Thermodynamic Theory of Learning."
summary:
Learning performed over finite time is necessarily irreversible. In Part~I of this series, we modeled learning as a transport process in the space of parameter distributions and derived the Epistemic Speed Limit, which lower-bounds entropy production under finite-time learning.
  In this work (Part~II), we study the consequences of this irreversibility for continual learning from a trajectory-level perspective. We show that finite dissipation constrains not only which solutions are reachable, but which learning paths remain dynamically accessible.
  Although a continuum of task-equivalent realizations can achieve identical task performance, finite-time learning irreversibly selects among these realizations. This selection occurs through the progressive elimination of degrees of freedom that would otherwise enable structural reconfiguration. We refer to this phenomenon as \emph{critical period closure}: beyond a certain stage of learning, transitions between compatible representations become dynamically inaccessible under any finite dissipation budget.
  As a result, continual learning failure arises not from the absence of solutions satisfying multiple tasks, but from an irreversible loss of representational freedom induced by prior learning. This reframes catastrophic forgetting as a dynamical constraint imposed by finite-time dissipation, rather than direct task interference.

AI.tldr: Finite-time learning irreversibly constrains continual learning by dissipative dynamics, narrowing accessible representations and causing a critical period closure that drives forgetting.
AI.motivation: To understand how irreversibility from finite-time learning affects trajectory-level continual learning and why forgetting arises beyond solvable constraints.
AI.method: Trajectory-level analysis of learning as a dissipative transport in parameter space; derive epistatic speed limit; examine how finite dissipation eliminates degrees of freedom and renders some representations dynamically inaccessible.
AI.result: Finite dissipation selects among task-equivalent realizations, eliminates degrees of freedom, and causes a critical period closure; forgetting is an irreversible loss of representational freedom, not just task interference.
AI.conclusion: For continual learning, address irreversibility by preserving representation flexibility or managing dissipation; reinterpret forgetting as a dynamical constraint and explore interventions to maintain access to alternative representations.

title: DICE: Disentangling Artist Style from Content via Contrastive Subspace Decomposition in Diffusion Models
summary: The recent proliferation of diffusion models has made style mimicry effortless, enabling users to imitate unique artistic styles without authorization. In deployed platforms, this raises copyright and intellectual-property risks and calls for reliable protection. However, existing countermeasures either require costly weight editing as new styles emerge or rely on an explicitly specified editing style, limiting their practicality for deployment-side safety. To address this challenge, we propose DICE (Disentanglement of artist Style from Content via Contrastive Subspace Decomposition), a training-free framework for on-the-fly artist style erasure. Unlike style editing that require an explicitly specified replacement style, DICE performs style purification, removing the artist's characteristics while preserving the user-intended content. Our core insight is that a model cannot truly comprehend the artist style from a single text or image alone. Consequently, we abandon the traditional paradigm of identifying style from isolated samples. Instead, we construct contrastive triplets to compel the model to distinguish between style and non-style features in the latent space. By formalizing this disentanglement process as a solvable generalized eigenvalue problem, we achieve precise identification of the style subspace. Furthermore, we introduce an Adaptive Attention Decoupling Editing strategy dynamically assesses the style concentration of each token and performs differential suppression and content enhancement on the QKV vectors. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DICE achieves a superior balance between the thoroughness of style erasure and the preservation of content integrity. DICE introduces an additional overhead of only 3 seconds to disentangle style, providing a practical and efficient technique for curbing style mimicry.

AI.tldr: DICE is a training-free framework that disentangles artist style from content in diffusion models via contrastive subspace decomposition and adaptive QKV editing to enable on-the-fly style erasure with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Mitigate copyright and IP risks from diffusion-model style mimicry by enabling safe deployment without costly retraining or explicit replacement styles.
AI.method: Construct contrastive triplets to separate style and content in latent space; cast disentanglement as a generalized eigenvalue problem to identify the style subspace; apply Adaptive Attention Decoupling Editing to dynamically suppress style components in QKV tokens and enhance content; training-free and fast (≈3 seconds).
AI.result: Extensive experiments show DICE achieves a favorable balance between thorough style erasure and preservation of content, with only about 3 seconds overhead to disentangle style, making it practical for deployment-side safety.
AI.conclusion: DICE offers a practical, scalable, training-free method to curb piracy of artistic style in diffusion models by purifying style from content without requiring a specified replacement style.

title: An Explainable Multi-Task Similarity Measure: Integrating Accumulated Local Effects and Weighted Fréchet Distance
summary:
In many machine learning contexts, tasks are often treated as interconnected components with the goal of leveraging knowledge transfer between them, which is the central aim of Multi-Task Learning (MTL). Consequently, this multi-task scenario requires addressing critical questions: which tasks are similar, and how and why do they exhibit similarity? In this work, we propose a multi-task similarity measure based on Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) techniques, specifically Accumulated Local Effects (ALE) curves.
  ALE curves are compared using the Fréchet distance, weighted by the data distribution, and the resulting similarity measure incorporates the importance of each feature. The measure is applicable in both single-task learning scenarios, where each task is trained separately, and multi-task learning scenarios, where all tasks are learned simultaneously. The measure is model-agnostic, allowing the use of different machine learning models across tasks. A scaling factor is introduced to account for differences in predictive performance across tasks, and several recommendations are provided for applying the measure in complex scenarios.
  We validate this measure using four datasets, one synthetic dataset and three real-world datasets. The real-world datasets include a well-known Parkinson's dataset and a bike-sharing usage dataset -- both structured in tabular format -- as well as the CelebA dataset, which is used to evaluate the application of concept bottleneck encoders in a multitask learning setting. The results demonstrate that the measure aligns with intuitive expectations of task similarity across both tabular and non-tabular data, making it a valuable tool for exploring relationships between tasks and supporting informed decision-making.

AI.tldr: ALE-based Fréchet-distance similarity for multi-task learning (MTL); model-agnostic, distribution-weighted; validated on synthetic, Parkinson's, bike-sharing, and CelebA; aligns with intuitive task similarity.
AI.motivation: In MTL, identifying which tasks are similar informs transfer learning strategies, shared representations, and architecture choices. Existing similarity notions are often model-specific or limited in scope. A model-agnostic, data-distribution-aware measure using explainable AI can provide a robust basis for task relationship assessment.
AI.method: Compute Accumulated Local Effects (ALE) curves for each task (and model, if applicable). Compare ALE curves via Fréchet distance, weighted by the data distribution to emphasize informative regions. Incorporate a scaling factor to account for cross-task performance differences. The approach is model-agnostic and applicable to both single-task (tasks learned independently) and multi-task (jointly learned) settings. Applicable to tabular and non-tabular data; validated with diverse models and datasets. Include recommendations for applying in complex scenarios.
AI.result: The proposed similarity measure yields task relationships that align with intuitive expectations across both tabular and non-tabular data. It is shown to be practical for exploring task relationships and supporting informed decision-making in MT contexts. The study also provides concrete recommendations for applying the measure in complex scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The ALE-based Fréchet-distance similarity measure offers a practical, model-agnostic tool for quantifying task similarity in MT learning, enabling more informed decisions about transfer, sharing of representations, and task selection. Its validation across diverse datasets supports broad applicability, with potential future work targeting refinements in weighting, scalability, and integration with MT architecture design.

title: ReRoPE: Repurposing RoPE for Relative Camera Control
summary: Video generation with controllable camera viewpoints is essential for applications such as interactive content creation, gaming, and simulation. Existing methods typically adapt pre-trained video models using camera poses relative to a fixed reference, e.g., the first frame. However, these encodings lack shift-invariance, often leading to poor generalization and accumulated drift. While relative camera pose embeddings defined between arbitrary view pairs offer a more robust alternative, integrating them into pre-trained video diffusion models without prohibitive training costs or architectural changes remains challenging. We introduce ReRoPE, a plug-and-play framework that incorporates relative camera information into pre-trained video diffusion models without compromising their generation capability. Our approach is based on the insight that Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) in existing models underutilize their full spectral bandwidth, particularly in the low-frequency components. By seamlessly injecting relative camera pose information into these underutilized bands, ReRoPE achieves precise control while preserving strong pre-trained generative priors. We evaluate our method on both image-to-video (I2V) and video-to-video (V2V) tasks in terms of camera control accuracy and visual fidelity. Our results demonstrate that ReRoPE offers a training-efficient path toward controllable, high-fidelity video generation. See project page for more results: https://sisyphe-lee.github.io/ReRoPE/

AI.tldr: ReRoPE is a plug‑and‑play method that injects relative camera pose information into pre-trained video diffusion models via Rotary Positional Embeddings, enabling controllable video generation with minimal retraining and preserved generative priors.
AI.motivation: Current camera-control approaches rely on fixed-reference pose encodings, which lack shift-invariance and generalization and require costly retraining. There is a need for a robust, training-efficient way to incorporate relative camera poses into pre-trained video generators without heavy architectural changes.
AI.method: ReRoPE identifies that RoPE in existing models underutilizes spectral bandwidth, especially in low-frequency components. It seamlessly injects relative camera pose information into these underutilized bands, effectively encoding pose differences without altering the model’s core architecture or training regime.
AI.result: On image-to-video (I2V) and video-to-video (V2V) tasks, ReRoPE yields improved camera control accuracy and visual fidelity while preserving pre-trained priors, demonstrating a training-efficient path toward controllable, high-quality video generation.
AI.conclusion: ReRoPE offers a practical, plug-and-play solution for controllable video generation by exploiting underutilized RoPE bandwidth to encode relative camera poses, achieving precise control with minimal training requirements and maintaining strong generative priors.

title: On Improving Neurosymbolic Learning by Exploiting the Representation Space
summary: We study the problem of learning neural classifiers in a neurosymbolic setting where the hidden gold labels of input instances must satisfy a logical formula. Learning in this setting proceeds by first computing (a subset of) the possible combinations of labels that satisfy the formula and then computing a loss using those combinations and the classifiers' scores. One challenge is that the space of label combinations can grow exponentially, making learning difficult. We propose a technique that prunes this space by exploiting the intuition that instances with similar latent representations are likely to share the same label. While this intuition has been widely used in weakly supervised learning, its application in our setting is challenging due to label dependencies imposed by logical constraints. We formulate the pruning process as an integer linear program that discards inconsistent label combinations while respecting logical structure. Our approach, CLIPPER, is orthogonal to existing training algorithms and can be seamlessly integrated with them. Across 16 benchmarks over complex neurosymbolic tasks, we demonstrate that CLIPPER boosts the performance of state-of-the-art neurosymbolic engines like Scallop, Dolphin, and ISED by up to 48%, 53%, and 8%, leading to state-of-the-art accuracies.

AI.tldr: CLIPPER is an ILP-based pruning technique for neurosymbolic learning with logical constraints that reduces the exponential label-space and improves state-of-the-art across 16 benchmarks, boosting Scallop, Dolphin, and ISED by up to 48%, 53%, and 8%.
AI.motivation: Addresses the exponential growth of label combinations under logical constraints; leverages the intuition that similar latent representations imply identical labels, while respecting label dependencies.
AI.method: Formulate pruning as an integer linear program that discards inconsistent label combinations while preserving logical structure; orthogonal to training algorithms and can be integrated with them.
AI.result: Empirically demonstrates significant gains across 16 benchmarks on neurosymbolic tasks; improves state-of-the-art accuracies for Scallop, Dolphin, ISED by up to 48%, 53%, 8%.
AI.conclusion: CLIPPER provides effective combinatorial pruning compatible with existing engines, enabling scalable neurosymbolic learning under logical constraints.

title: ViT-5: Vision Transformers for The Mid-2020s
summary: This work presents a systematic investigation into modernizing Vision Transformer backbones by leveraging architectural advancements from the past five years. While preserving the canonical Attention-FFN structure, we conduct a component-wise refinement involving normalization, activation functions, positional encoding, gating mechanisms, and learnable tokens. These updates form a new generation of Vision Transformers, which we call ViT-5. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ViT-5 consistently outperforms state-of-the-art plain Vision Transformers across both understanding and generation benchmarks. On ImageNet-1k classification, ViT-5-Base reaches 84.2\% top-1 accuracy under comparable compute, exceeding DeiT-III-Base at 83.8\%. ViT-5 also serves as a stronger backbone for generative modeling: when plugged into an SiT diffusion framework, it achieves 1.84 FID versus 2.06 with a vanilla ViT backbone. Beyond headline metrics, ViT-5 exhibits improved representation learning and favorable spatial reasoning behavior, and transfers reliably across tasks. With a design aligned with contemporary foundation-model practices, ViT-5 offers a simple drop-in upgrade over vanilla ViT for mid-2020s vision backbones.

AI.tldr: A ViT-5 backbone updates Vision Transformers with core advances (normalization, activation, positional encoding, gating, learnable tokens) while preserving Attention-FFN, achieving superior accuracy and generative performance; a practical drop-in upgrade.
AI.motivation: To modernize Vision Transformer backbones by integrating contemporary architectural advances from the last five years, while retaining the canonical Attention-FFN structure for broad applicability and simplicity.
AI.method: Systematic, component-wise refinements to normalization, activation functions, positional encoding, gating mechanisms, and learnable tokens within the standard Attention-FFN ViT framework, yielding a new ViT-5 backbone; extensive experiments on classification (ImageNet-1k) and generative tasks (SiT diffusion) to compare against vanilla ViT and DeiT baselines.
AI.result: ViT-5-Base achieves 84.2% top-1 on ImageNet-1k under comparable compute, surpassing DeiT-III-Base at 83.8%; in generative use within SiT diffusion, reaches 1.84 FID versus 2.06 with vanilla ViT; demonstrates improved representation learning, spatial reasoning, and reliable cross-task transfer.
AI.conclusion: ViT-5 offers a simple, effective drop-in upgrade for vanilla ViT backbones suitable for mid-2020s vision tasks, delivering consistent gains on understanding and generation benchmarks and aligning with contemporary foundation-model practices.

title: Beyond Optimization: Intelligence as Metric-Topology Factorization under Geometric Incompleteness
summary: Contemporary ML often equates intelligence with optimization: searching for solutions within a fixed representational geometry. This works in static regimes but breaks under distributional shift, task permutation, and continual learning, where even mild topological changes can invalidate learned solutions and trigger catastrophic forgetting. We propose Metric-Topology Factorization (MTF) as a unifying geometric principle: intelligence is not navigation through a fixed maze, but the ability to reshape representational geometry so desired behaviors become stable attractors. Learning corresponds to metric contraction (a controlled deformation of Riemannian structure), while task identity and environmental variation are encoded topologically and stored separately in memory. We show any fixed metric is geometrically incomplete: for any local metric representation, some topological transformations make it singular or incoherent, implying an unavoidable stability-plasticity tradeoff for weight-based systems. MTF resolves this by factorizing stable topology from plastic metric warps, enabling rapid adaptation via geometric switching rather than re-optimization. Building on this, we introduce the Topological Urysohn Machine (TUM), implementing MTF through memory-amortized metric inference (MAMI): spectral task signatures index amortized metric transformations, letting a single learned geometry be reused across permuted, reflected, or parity-altered environments. This explains robustness to task reordering, resistance to catastrophic forgetting, and generalization across transformations that defeat conventional continual learning methods (e.g., EWC).

AI.tldr: MTF reframes intelligence as adaptable geometric reshaping: learning contracts metric, while topology encodes task/environment; TUM/MAMI enable rapid geometric switching, improving robustness to shifts and forgetting.
AI.motivation: Address brittleness and forgetting under distributional shift; fixed metrics fail when topology changes; seeks separation of stable topology from plastic metric deformations to support continual learning.
AI.method: Propose Metric-Topology Factorization (MTF) with metric contraction of a Riemannian structure; encode task identity/environment topology in memory; prove fixed metric representations are topologically fragile; introduce Topological Urysohn Machine (TUM) implemented via memory-amortized metric inference (MAMI) using spectral task signatures to index metric transformations.
AI.result: Theoretically identifies a stability-plasticity tradeoff under topology changes; MTF enables rapid adaptation without re-optimization; TUM/MAMI framework yields robustness to task reordering, reduced forgetting, and generalization across transformations that defeat standard continual learning methods like EWC.
AI.conclusion: A geometry-factorized view provides a unified explanation for robustness under non-stationarity; practical instantiation (TUM/MAMI) shows promise for continual learning by reusing a fixed geometry across transformed environments.

title: Lagged backward-compatible physics-informed neural networks for unsaturated soil consolidation analysis
summary:
This study develops a Lagged Backward-Compatible Physics-Informed Neural Network (LBC-PINN) for simulating and inverting one-dimensional unsaturated soil consolidation under long-term loading. To address the challenges of coupled air and water pressure dissipation across multi-scale time domains, the framework integrates logarithmic time segmentation, lagged compatibility loss enforcement, and segment-wise transfer learning.
  In forward analysis, the LBC-PINN with recommended segmentation schemes accurately predicts pore air and pore water pressure evolution. Model predictions are validated against finite element method (FEM) results, with mean absolute errors below 1e-2 for time durations up to 1e10 seconds. A simplified segmentation strategy based on the characteristic air-phase dissipation time improves computational efficiency while preserving predictive accuracy. Sensitivity analyses confirm the robustness of the framework across air-to-water permeability ratios ranging from 1e-3 to 1e3.

AI.tldr: Proposes Lagged Backward-Compatible Physics-Informed Neural Network (LBC-PINN) for 1D unsaturated soil consolidation under long-term loading, using logarithmic time segmentation, lagged compatibility loss, and segment-wise transfer learning to handle multi-scale air-water pressure dissipation. Validated against FEM with MAE < 1e-2 up to 1e10 s; simplified segmentation boosts efficiency; robust to air–water permeability ratios 1e-3 to 1e3.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of accurate, efficient forward/inverse modeling of coupled air–water pressure dissipation in unsaturated soils over very long time horizons under multi-scale loading, where conventional numerical methods are computationally expensive.
AI.method: A Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) framework (LBC-PINN) incorporating: (1) logarithmic time segmentation to capture multi-scale dynamics; (2) lagged compatibility loss to enforce continuity across segments; (3) segment-wise transfer learning to reuse learned representations between segments; (4) validation against finite element method (FEM) results; sensitivity analysis across varying air-to-water permeability ratios.
AI.result: Forward predictions of pore air and pore water pressures show mean absolute error below 1e-2 for time intervals up to 1e10 s; a simplified segmentation strategy based on characteristic air-phase dissipation time maintains accuracy while improving computational efficiency; framework demonstrates robustness across permeability ratios from 1e-3 to 1e3.
AI.conclusion: LBC-PINN with segmentation, lagged compatibility, and transfer learning enables accurate, efficient forward simulations (and potential inversion) of long-duration unsaturated soil consolidation under multi-scale loading, with strong cross-domain robustness.

title: VidVec: Unlocking Video MLLM Embeddings for Video-Text Retrieval
summary: Recent studies have adapted generative Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) into embedding extractors for vision tasks, typically through fine-tuning to produce universal representations. However, their performance on video remains inferior to Video Foundation Models (VFMs). In this paper, we focus on leveraging MLLMs for video-text embedding and retrieval. We first conduct a systematic layer-wise analysis, showing that intermediate (pre-trained) MLLM layers already encode substantial task-relevant information. Leveraging this insight, we demonstrate that combining intermediate-layer embeddings with a calibrated MLLM head yields strong zero-shot retrieval performance without any training. Building on these findings, we introduce a lightweight text-based alignment strategy which maps dense video captions to short summaries and enables task-related video-text embedding learning without visual supervision. Remarkably, without any fine-tuning beyond text, our method outperforms current methods, often by a substantial margin, achieving state-of-the-art results across common video retrieval benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Pretrained Multimodal LLMs can yield competitive video-text embeddings by exploiting intermediate layers and text-based alignment, enabling zero-shot retrieval without fine-tuning and achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Video-text alignment tasks in video retrieval are lagging behind specialized Video Foundation Models; it is desirable to leverage existing MLLMs for embedding extraction with minimal or no fine-tuning.
AI.method: 1) Conduct layer-wise analysis showing intermediate (pre-trained) MLLM features encode task-relevant information. 2) Combine intermediate-layer embeddings with a calibrated MLLM head to achieve strong zero-shot retrieval without training. 3) Introduce a lightweight text-based alignment that maps dense video captions to short summaries, enabling task-related video-text embedding learning without visual supervision.
AI.result: Without any training beyond text, the method outperforms existing approaches by a substantial margin and achieves state-of-the-art results on standard video retrieval benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that intermediate-layer representations in MLLMs, when paired with a calibrated head and lightweight text-based alignment, can realize strong zero-shot video-text embeddings and retrieval without visual supervision.

title: When Is Compositional Reasoning Learnable from Verifiable Rewards?
summary: The emergence of compositional reasoning in large language models through reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has been a key driver of recent empirical successes. Despite this progress, it remains unclear which compositional problems are learnable in this setting using outcome-level feedback alone. In this work, we theoretically study the learnability of compositional problems in autoregressive models under RLVR training. We identify a quantity that we call the task-advantage ratio, a joint property of the compositional problem and the base model, that characterizes which tasks and compositions are learnable from outcome-level feedback. On the positive side, using this characterization, we show that compositional problems where correct intermediate steps provide a clear advantage are efficiently learnable with RLVR. We also analyze how such an advantage naturally arises in different problems. On the negative side, when the structural advantage is not present, RLVR may converge to suboptimal compositions. We prove that, in some cases, the quality of the base model determines if such an advantage exists and whether RLVR will converge to a suboptimal solution. We hope our analysis can provide a principled theoretical understanding of when and why RLVR succeeds and when it does not.

AI.tldr: Proposes a theoretical framework to determine which compositional tasks are learnable under RLVR with outcome-level feedback by introducing a task-advantage ratio; shows positive learnability when correct intermediate steps provide a clear advantage and negatives otherwise; links learnability to base model quality.
AI.motivation: Address the gap: RLVR's ability to learn compositional reasoning is not fully understood; outcomes-level feedback may be insufficient for certain structures; a single ratio can characterize learnability across tasks and models.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis introducing the task-advantage ratio as a joint property of task and base model; derive conditions for efficient learnability; prove sufficiency for tasks with clear intermediate-step advantage; analyze convergence to suboptimal compositions when structural advantage is absent; explore dependence on base model quality.
AI.result: Characterization via task-advantage ratio; efficient learnability for problems where intermediate steps offer clear advantage; naturalization of such advantages across different problems; potential convergence to suboptimal compositions when no advantage; base model quality determines existence of advantage and RLVR outcome.
AI.conclusion: Provides principled theoretical understanding of RLVR success/failure in compositional tasks and implications for designing tasks and models.

title: MMLSv2: A Multimodal Dataset for Martian Landslide Detection in Remote Sensing Imagery
summary: We present MMLSv2, a dataset for landslide segmentation on Martian surfaces. MMLSv2 consists of multimodal imagery with seven bands: RGB, digital elevation model, slope, thermal inertia, and grayscale channels. MMLSv2 comprises 664 images distributed across training, validation, and test splits. In addition, an isolated test set of 276 images from a geographically disjoint region from the base dataset is released to evaluate spatial generalization. Experiments conducted with multiple segmentation models show that the dataset supports stable training and achieves competitive performance, while still posing challenges in fragmented, elongated, and small-scale landslide regions. Evaluation on the isolated test set leads to a noticeable performance drop, indicating increased difficulty and highlighting its value for assessing model robustness and generalization beyond standard in-distribution settings. Dataset will be available at: https://github.com/MAIN-Lab/MMLS_v2

AI.tldr: A multimodal Martian landslide segmentation dataset (MMLSv2) with 7-band imagery and an isolated test set for spatial generalization, enabling stable training and benchmarking across segmentation models while exposing generalization gaps.
AI.motivation: The need for robust Martian landslide mapping and model robustness evaluation across spatially disjoint regions.
AI.method: Construct dataset with 664 images across train/val/test plus 276 isolated-test; seven modalities (RGB, DEM, slope, thermal inertia, grayscale); run baseline segmentation models; assess training stability and generalization.
AI.result: Models train stably and achieve competitive performance on standard splits; fragmentation, elongated, and small-scale landslides remain challenging; isolated-test reveals significant performance drop, highlighting generalization gap.
AI.conclusion: MMLSv2 provides a valuable benchmark for landslide segmentation on Mars and a testbed for robustness and spatial generalization; data available at GitHub.

title: Regret Analysis of Unichain Average Reward Constrained MDPs with General Parameterization
summary: We study infinite-horizon average-reward constrained Markov decision processes (CMDPs) under the unichain assumption and general policy parameterizations. Existing regret analyses for constrained reinforcement learning largely rely on ergodicity or strong mixing-time assumptions, which fail to hold in the presence of transient states. We propose a primal--dual natural actor--critic algorithm that leverages multi-level Monte Carlo (MLMC) estimators and an explicit burn-in mechanism to handle unichain dynamics without requiring mixing-time oracles. Our analysis establishes finite-time regret and cumulative constraint violation bounds that scale as $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$, up to approximation errors arising from policy and critic parameterization, thereby extending order-optimal guarantees to a significantly broader class of CMDPs.

AI.tldr: Primal–dual natural actor–critic for infinite-horizon average-reward CMDPs under unichain; uses MLMC estimators and burn-in to handle transient states; achieves O~(sqrt(T)) regret and cumulative constraint violation without mixing-time assumptions, up to parameterization errors.
AI.motivation: Existing CMDP regret analyses rely on ergodicity or strong mixing; they fail in transient/unichain settings. A method enabling regret bounds without these assumptions broadens CMDP applicability.
AI.method: Proposes primal–dual natural actor–critic, with multi-level Monte Carlo estimators and an explicit burn-in mechanism; avoids mixing-time oracles; accommodates unichain dynamics and general policy parameterizations.
AI.result: Finite-time regret and cumulative constraint-violation bounds that scale as ~O(sqrt(T)), up to errors from policy/critic parameterization; extends order-optimal guarantees to a broader CMDP class.
AI.conclusion: Shows that near-optimal performance is achievable in CMDPs without strong ergodicity, via MLMC-based estimation and burn-in in a primal–dual actor–critic framework.

title: Building Damage Detection using Satellite Images and Patch-Based Transformer Methods
summary:
Rapid building damage assessment is critical for post-disaster response. Damage classification models built on satellite imagery provide a scalable means of obtaining situational awareness. However, label noise and severe class imbalance in satellite data create major challenges. The xBD dataset offers a standardized benchmark for building-level damage across diverse geographic regions. In this study, we evaluate Vision Transformer (ViT) model performance on the xBD dataset, specifically investigating how these models distinguish between types of structural damage when training on noisy, imbalanced data.
  In this study, we specifically evaluate DINOv2-small and DeiT for multi-class damage classification. We propose a targeted patch-based pre-processing pipeline to isolate structural features and minimize background noise in training. We adopt a frozen-head fine-tuning strategy to keep computational requirements manageable. Model performance is evaluated through accuracy, precision, recall, and macro-averaged F1 scores. We show that small ViT architectures with our novel training method achieves competitive macro-averaged F1 relative to prior CNN baselines for disaster classification.

AI.tldr: Small ViT models with patch-based pre-processing and frozen-head fine-tuning achieve competitive macro-F1 on xBD multi-class building damage classification under noisy, imbalanced labels.
AI.motivation: Rapid disaster response requires scalable, robust damage assessment from satellite imagery; label noise and class imbalance hinder learning; xBD provides a standardized benchmark.
AI.method: Evaluate DINOv2-small and DeiT in multi-class damage classification on xBD; introduce a targeted patch-based pre-processing pipeline to reduce background noise; employ frozen-head fine-tuning to limit compute; measure accuracy, precision, recall, macro-F1.
AI.result: Small ViT architectures with the proposed training strategy achieve competitive macro-F1 scores relative to prior CNN baselines for disaster classification.
AI.conclusion: ViT-based approaches with targeted patch processing and frozen-head fine-tuning offer effective, computation-friendly options for multi-class building damage assessment on noisy, imbalanced satellite data.

title: Don't Always Pick the Highest-Performing Model: An Information Theoretic View of LLM Ensemble Selection
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are often ensembled together to improve overall reliability and robustness, but in practice models are strongly correlated. This raises a fundamental question: which models should be selected when forming an LLM ensemble? We formulate budgeted ensemble selection as maximizing the mutual information between the true label and predictions of the selected models. Furthermore, to explain why performance can saturate even with many models, we model the correlated errors of the models using Gaussian-copula and show an information-theoretic error floor for the performance of the ensemble. Motivated by these, we propose a simple greedy mutual-information selection algorithm that estimates the required information terms directly from data and iteratively builds an ensemble under a query budget. We test our approach in two question answering datasets and one binary sentiment classification dataset: MEDMCQA, MMLU, and IMDB movie reviews. Across all datasets, we observe that our method consistently outperforms strong baselines under the same query budget.

AI.tldr: A budgeted ensemble-selection method for LLMs that maximizes mutual information between true labels and model predictions, accounting for correlated errors via Gaussian copula; proposes a greedy MI-based algorithm under a query budget and validates on MEDMCQA, MMLU, and IMDB, achieving improvements over baselines.
AI.motivation: Ensembling LLMs can be hampered by strong model correlations, which reduces gains from adding models. Understanding how to select models efficiently under a query budget and explaining performance ceilings due to correlated errors is crucial for reliable ensemble design.
AI.method: Formulate subset selection as maximizing mutual information between the true label and predictions of the selected models. Model inter-model error dependency with a Gaussian copula and derive an information-theoretic performance floor. Propose a greedy algorithm that estimates mutual information terms directly from data and builds the ensemble within a fixed query budget.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three datasets (MEDMCQA, MMLU, IMDB) shows the proposed MI-based greedy selection consistently outperforms strong baselines at the same query budget.
AI.conclusion: Mutual-information based selection is effective for budgeted ensemble construction in LLMs, with an information-theoretic justification for diminishing returns due to correlated errors. The approach is data-driven and scalable to different tasks; future work could improve MI estimation and explore other dependency models.

title: From $O(mn)$ to $O(r^2)$: Two-Sided Low-Rank Communication for Adam in Distributed Training with Memory Efficiency
summary: As foundation models continue to scale, pretraining increasingly relies on data-parallel distributed optimization, making bandwidth-limited gradient synchronization a key bottleneck. Orthogonally, projection-based low-rank optimizers were mainly designed for memory efficiency, but remain suboptimal for communication-limited training: one-sided synchronization still transmits an $O(rn)$ object for an $m\times n$ matrix gradient and refresh steps can dominate peak communicated bytes. We propose TSR, which brings two-sided low-rank communication to Adam-family updates (TSR-Adam) by synchronizing a compact core $U^\top G V\in\mathbb{R}^{r\times r}$, reducing the dominant per-step payload from $O(mn)$ to $O(r^2)$ while keeping moment states in low-dimensional cores. To further reduce the peak communication from subspace refresh, TSR-Adam adopts a randomized SVD-based refresh that avoids full-gradient synchronization. We additionally extend low-rank communication to embedding gradients with embedding-specific ranks and refresh schedules, yielding additional communication and memory savings over keeping embeddings dense. Across pretraining from 60M to 1B model scales, TSR-Adam reduces average communicated bytes per step by $13\times$, and on GLUE fine-tuning it reduces communication by $25\times$, while achieving comparable performance; we further provide a theoretical stationarity analysis for the proposed update. Code is available at https://github.com/DKmiyan/TSR-Adam.

AI.tldr: TSR-Adam introduces two-sided low-rank communication for Adam-family updates by synchronizing a compact core U^T G V in R^{r×r}, cutting per-step data from O(mn) to O(r^2); adds randomized SVD-based refresh to avoid heavy gradient synchronization and extends to embedding gradients with per-embedding ranks. Empirically, it achieves substantial bandwidth savings with comparable performance and provides a theoretical stationarity analysis.
AI.motivation: As model scale grows, data-parallel training becomes bandwidth-bound due to gradient synchronization. Existing one-sided projections reduce memory but still transmit large O(mn) gradients and suffer refresh overhead. A two-sided, low-rank scheme can dramatically reduce communication while preserving optimizer state via compact cores.
AI.method: Apply two-sided low-rank factorization within Adam-like updates: represent gradients through a small core U^T G V ∈ R^{r×r}, communicate this core, and keep moment statistics in low-dimensional cores. Use randomized SVD-based refresh to avoid synchronizing full gradients. Extend the scheme to embedding layers with per-embedding ranks and refresh schedules to gain additional savings.
AI.result: On pretraining from 60M to 1B parameters, average per-step communication drops ~13×; on GLUE fine-tuning, ~25× reduction; performance remains comparable to baselines. The approach includes a theoretical stationarity analysis.
AI.conclusion: Two-sided low-rank communication with compact gradient cores and randomized refresh offers substantial bandwidth savings for Adam-family optimizers, scales to embedding layers, and preserves optimization quality. Code is available.

title: Fields of The World: A Field Guide for Extracting Agricultural Field Boundaries
summary: Field boundary maps are a building block for agricultural data products and support crop monitoring, yield estimation, and disease estimation. This tutorial presents the Fields of The World (FTW) ecosystem: a benchmark of 1.6M field polygons across 24 countries, pre-trained segmentation models, and command-line inference tools. We provide two notebooks that cover (1) local-scale field boundary extraction with crop classification and forest loss attribution, and (2) country-scale inference using cloud-optimized data. We use MOSAIKS random convolutional features and FTW derived field boundaries to map crop type at the field level and report macro F1 scores of 0.65--0.75 for crop type classification with limited labels. Finally, we show how to explore pre-computed predictions over five countries (4.76M km\textsuperscript{2}), with median predicted field areas from 0.06 ha (Rwanda) to 0.28 ha (Switzerland).

AI.tldr: Introduces the Fields of The World (FTW) ecosystem: a 1.6M-field benchmark across 24 countries with pre-trained segmentation models and CLI inference tools, enabling local- and country-scale field boundary extraction and crop-type mapping with macro F1 0.65–0.75; includes exploration of pre-computed predictions over 5 countries covering 4.76M km^2 and median field sizes of 0.06–0.28 ha.
AI.motivation: Field boundary maps are essential for agricultural data products and crop monitoring; there is a need for scalable, global field delineation and crop-type mapping with limited labels.
AI.method: FTW benchmark comprising 1.6M field polygons across 24 countries, pre-trained segmentation models, and command-line inference tools. Two notebooks: (1) local-scale field boundary extraction with crop classification and forest loss attribution; (2) country-scale inference using cloud-optimized data. Uses MOSAIKS random convolutional features with FTW boundaries to map crop type at the field level.
AI.result: Macro F1 scores of 0.65–0.75 for crop type classification with limited labels. Demonstrates exploration of pre-computed predictions over five countries (4.76M km^2), with median predicted field areas from 0.06 ha (Rwanda) to 0.28 ha (Switzerland).
AI.conclusion: The FTW ecosystem offers a scalable, open benchmark and tools for field boundary delineation and crop-type mapping, enabling cross-country inference and practical agricultural analytics.

title: A Unified Density Operator View of Flow Control and Merging
summary: Recent progress in large-scale flow and diffusion models raised two fundamental algorithmic challenges: (i) control-based reward adaptation of pre-trained flows, and (ii) integration of multiple models, i.e., flow merging. While current approaches address them separately, we introduce a unifying probability-space framework that subsumes both as limit cases, and enables reward-guided flow merging, allowing principled, task-aware combination of multiple pre-trained flows (e.g., merging priors while maximizing drug-discovery utilities). Our formulation renders possible to express a rich family of operators over generative models densities, including intersection (e.g., to enforce safety), union (e.g., to compose diverse models), interpolation (e.g., for discovery), their reward-guided counterparts, as well as complex logical expressions via generative circuits. Next, we introduce Reward-Guided Flow Merging (RFM), a mirror-descent scheme that reduces reward-guided flow merging to a sequence of standard fine-tuning problems. Then, we provide first-of-their-kind theoretical guarantees for reward-guided and pure flow merging via RFM. Ultimately, we showcase the capabilities of the proposed method on illustrative settings providing visually interpretable insights, and apply our method to high-dimensional de-novo molecular design and low-energy conformer generation.

AI.tldr: A unified probability-space framework for reward-guided flow merging (RFM) that unifies control-based reward adaptation and model merging, enabling principled, task-aware combination of pre-trained flows and operators on generative densities.
AI.motivation: Addresses two fundamental challenges in large-scale flow and diffusion models: (i) adapting rewards for pre-trained flows (reward-based control), and (ii) merging multiple models (flow merging). A unified framework is desired to combine priors and objectives in a principled way.
AI.method: Defines a unifying probability-space framework that subsumes reward-guided control and flow merging as limit cases. Introduces operators over generative model densities (intersection, union, interpolation) and their reward-guided variants, enabling complex logical expressions via generative circuits. Proposes Reward-Guided Flow Merging (RFM) as a mirror-descent scheme that reduces reward-guided merging to a sequence of standard fine-tuning problems. Provides theoretical guarantees for both reward-guided and pure flow merging via RFM.
AI.result: The framework yields theoretical guarantees for reward-guided and pure flow merging via RFM. Demonstrations include interpretable illustrative settings and applications to high-dimensional de-novo molecular design and low-energy conformer generation, showing practical utility and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a principled, flexible method to compose and control pre-trained generative flows, enabling task-aware merging and safety/constrained design through a rich set of operators over densities. It opens avenues for reward-guided model composition in complex domains like chemistry and molecular design.

title: Robustness of Vision Language Models Against Split-Image Harmful Input Attacks
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are now a core part of modern AI. Recent work proposed several visual jailbreak attacks using single/ holistic images. However, contemporary VLMs demonstrate strong robustness against such attacks due to extensive safety alignment through preference optimization (e.g., RLHF). In this work, we identify a new vulnerability: while VLM pretraining and instruction tuning generalize well to split-image inputs, safety alignment is typically performed only on holistic images and does not account for harmful semantics distributed across multiple image fragments. Consequently, VLMs often fail to detect and refuse harmful split-image inputs, where unsafe cues emerge only after combining images. We introduce novel split-image visual jailbreak attacks (SIVA) that exploit this misalignment. Unlike prior optimization-based attacks, which exhibit poor black-box transferability due to architectural and prior mismatches across models, our attacks evolve in progressive phases from naive splitting to an adaptive white-box attack, culminating in a black-box transfer attack. Our strongest strategy leverages a novel adversarial knowledge distillation (Adv-KD) algorithm to substantially improve cross-model transferability. Evaluations on three state-of-the-art modern VLMs and three jailbreak datasets demonstrate that our strongest attack achieves up to 60% higher transfer success than existing baselines. Lastly, we propose efficient ways to address this critical vulnerability in the current VLM safety alignment.

AI.tldr: Split-image visual jailbreak (SIVA) reveals safety-alignment gaps in VLMs by exploiting harmful cues distributed across image fragments; uses adversarial knowledge distillation (Adv-KD) to boost cross-model transfer; yields up to ~60% higher transfer success than baselines; defense strategies proposed.
AI.motivation: Safety alignment in VLMs is typically learned on holistic images. This creates a vulnerability: harmful semantics distributed across split image fragments can evade detection and refusal, since the alignment doesn’t account for multi-fragment cues.
AI.method: Develop SIVA attacks that progressively evolve from naive image splitting to an adaptive white-box attack and finally to a black-box transfer attack. Introduce Adv-KD to transfer adversarial knowledge across models, enhancing cross-model effectiveness.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three state-of-the-art VLMs and three jailbreak datasets shows the strongest SIVA attack achieves up to 60% higher transfer success compared to existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: Highlight a critical vulnerability in current VLM safety alignment; propose efficient defense strategies to mitigate split-image jailbreaks and improve safety robustness.

title: The Rise of Sparse Mixture-of-Experts: A Survey from Algorithmic Foundations to Decentralized Architectures and Vertical Domain Applications
summary: The sparse Mixture of Experts(MoE) architecture has evolved as a powerful approach for scaling deep learning models to more parameters with comparable computation cost. As an important branch of large language model(LLM), MoE model only activate a subset of experts based on a routing network. This sparse conditional computation mechanism significantly improves computational efficiency, paving a promising path for greater scalability and cost-efficiency. It not only enhance downstream applications such as natural language processing, computer vision, and multimodal in various horizontal domains, but also exhibit broad applicability across vertical domains. Despite the growing popularity and application of MoE models across various domains, there lacks a systematic exploration of recent advancements of MoE in many important fields. Existing surveys on MoE suffer from limitations such as lack coverage or none extensively exploration of key areas. This survey seeks to fill these gaps. In this paper, Firstly, we examine the foundational principles of MoE, with an in-depth exploration of its core components-the routing network and expert network. Subsequently, we extend beyond the centralized paradigm to the decentralized paradigm, which unlocks the immense untapped potential of decentralized infrastructure, enables democratization of MoE development for broader communities, and delivers greater scalability and cost-efficiency. Furthermore we focus on exploring its vertical domain applications. Finally, we also identify key challenges and promising future research directions. To the best of our knowledge, this survey is currently the most comprehensive review in the field of MoE. We aim for this article to serve as a valuable resource for both researchers and practitioners, enabling them to navigate and stay up-to-date with the latest advancements.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey of sparse MoE models, detailing routing and expert components, centralized vs decentralized paradigms, vertical-domain applications, and future directions; asserts to be the most comprehensive MoE review to date.
AI.motivation: MoE scaling is impactful for parametric efficiency in LLMs across domains, but existing surveys lack systematic, broad coverage—especially on decentralized MoE and vertical-domain applications.
AI.method: Literature review and synthesis of MoE fundamentals (routing and expert networks), taxonomy of centralized vs decentralized MoE, survey of vertical-domain deployments, and identification of key challenges and future research directions.
AI.result: Clarifies MoE architecture, highlights decentralized MoE as a path to scalability and democratization, maps vertical-domain applications, and inventories challenges and opportunities for future work.
AI.conclusion: Provides a timely, up-to-date resource for researchers and practitioners to understand MoE advances and guide future research.

title: DAS-SK: An Adaptive Model Integrating Dual Atrous Separable and Selective Kernel CNN for Agriculture Semantic Segmentation
summary: Semantic segmentation in high-resolution agricultural imagery demands models that strike a careful balance between accuracy and computational efficiency to enable deployment in practical systems. In this work, we propose DAS-SK, a novel lightweight architecture that retrofits selective kernel convolution (SK-Conv) into the dual atrous separable convolution (DAS-Conv) module to strengthen multi-scale feature learning. The model further enhances the atrous spatial pyramid pooling (ASPP) module, enabling the capture of fine-grained local structures alongside global contextual information. Built upon a modified DeepLabV3 framework with two complementary backbones - MobileNetV3-Large and EfficientNet-B3, the DAS-SK model mitigates limitations associated with large dataset requirements, limited spectral generalization, and the high computational cost that typically restricts deployment on UAVs and other edge devices. Comprehensive experiments across three benchmarks: LandCover.ai, VDD, and PhenoBench, demonstrate that DAS-SK consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, while being more efficient than CNN-, transformer-, and hybrid-based competitors. Notably, DAS-SK requires up to 21x fewer parameters and 19x fewer GFLOPs than top-performing transformer models. These findings establish DAS-SK as a robust, efficient, and scalable solution for real-time agricultural robotics and high-resolution remote sensing, with strong potential for broader deployment in other vision domains.

AI.tldr: A lightweight DAS-SK architecture integrates selective kernel convolution (SK-Conv) into the dual atrous separable convolution (DAS-Conv) within a DeepLabV3 framework, enhanced by ASPP for fine-grained local and global context. It uses MobileNetV3-Large and EfficientNet-B3 backbones and delivers state-of-the-art semantic segmentation on high-resolution agricultural imagery with far fewer parameters and FLOPs than transformer-based models.
AI.motivation: Address the need for accurate yet computationally efficient semantic segmentation of high-resolution agricultural imagery, enabling edge deployment on UAVs and robots, while coping with limited data, spectral generalization challenges, and high computational costs.
AI.method: Incorporates SK-Conv into the DAS-Conv module of a modified DeepLabV3, augments ASPP for better local structure capture, and evaluates on two lightweight backbones (MobileNetV3-Large and EfficientNet-B3). Training and evaluation are conducted on three benchmarks (LandCover.ai, VDD, PhenoBench) to compare against CNN-, transformer-, and hybrid-based models.
AI.result: DAS-SK achieves state-of-the-art performance on LandCover.ai, VDD, and PhenoBench, while requiring substantially fewer parameters (up to 21x fewer) and fewer GFLOPs (up to 19x fewer) than top transformer models, demonstrating superior efficiency without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.conclusion: DAS-SK is a robust, efficient, and scalable solution for real-time high-resolution semantic segmentation in agricultural robotics and remote sensing, with potential applicability to broader vision domains.

title: Sharp analysis of linear ensemble sampling
summary: We analyse linear ensemble sampling (ES) with standard Gaussian perturbations in stochastic linear bandits. We show that for ensemble size $m=Θ(d\log n)$, ES attains $\tilde O(d^{3/2}\sqrt n)$ high-probability regret, closing the gap to the Thompson sampling benchmark while keeping computation comparable. The proof brings a new perspective on randomized exploration in linear bandits by reducing the analysis to a time-uniform exceedance problem for $m$ independent Brownian motions. Intriguingly, this continuous-time lens is not forced; it appears natural--and perhaps necessary: the discrete-time problem seems to be asking for a continuous-time solution, and we know of no other way to obtain a sharp ES bound.

AI.tldr: Linear ensemble sampling with Gaussian perturbations in stochastic linear bandits achieves near-optimal regret with ensemble size m = Θ(d log n); a time-uniform exceedance analysis of m Brownian motions yields high-probability bounds tilde O(d^{3/2} sqrt n), close to Thompson sampling.
AI.motivation: Provide regret guarantees and computationally efficient exploration for ES in stochastic linear bandits, aiming to close the gap to Thompson sampling while keeping similar computation.
AI.method: Reduce the analysis to a time-uniform exceedance problem for m independent Brownian motions under Gaussian perturbations; adopt a continuous-time viewpoint that naturally yields sharp ES bounds.
AI.result: With ensemble size m = Θ(d log n), ensemble sampling attains tilde O(d^{3/2} sqrt n) high-probability regret in stochastic linear bandits.
AI.conclusion: The continuous-time lens is natural and perhaps necessary for sharp ES bounds; a discrete-time analysis appears to benefit from this continuous-time perspective, yielding a near-optimal regret bound for ES.

title: PEGAsus: 3D Personalization of Geometry and Appearance
summary: We present PEGAsus, a new framework capable of generating Personalized 3D shapes by learning shape concepts at both Geometry and Appearance levels. First, we formulate 3D shape personalization as extracting reusable, category-agnostic geometric and appearance attributes from reference shapes, and composing these attributes with text to generate novel shapes. Second, we design a progressive optimization strategy to learn shape concepts at both the geometry and appearance levels, decoupling the shape concept learning process. Third, we extend our approach to region-wise concept learning, enabling flexible concept extraction, with context-aware and context-free losses. Extensive experimental results show that PEGAsus is able to effectively extract attributes from a wide range of reference shapes and then flexibly compose these concepts with text to synthesize new shapes. This enables fine-grained control over shape generation and supports the creation of diverse, personalized results, even in challenging cross-category scenarios. Both quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate that our approach outperforms existing state-of-the-art solutions.

AI.tldr: PEGAsus is a framework for personalized 3D shape generation that learns geometry and appearance concepts, uses progressive optimization and region-wise concept learning to compose shapes with text, enabling fine-grained control and cross-category diversity.
AI.motivation: There is a need for controllable, cross-category, personalized 3D shape generation. Decoupling geometry and appearance concept learning and enabling flexible region-wise extraction can improve flexibility and quality.
AI.method: Extract reusable geometric and appearance attributes from reference shapes; employ progressive optimization to decouple geometry and appearance concept learning; extend to region-wise concept learning with context-aware and context-free losses; compose concepts with text prompts to synthesize new shapes.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate effective attribute extraction, flexible text-based composition, fine-grained control, and diverse personalized results, including cross-category scenarios; both quantitative and qualitative metrics show superiority over state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: PEGAsus provides a modular, scalable framework for personalized 3D shape synthesis via geometry/appearance concept learning and region-wise extraction, achieving high-quality, diverse results and strong cross-category generalization.

title: Horizon Imagination: Efficient On-Policy Training in Diffusion World Models
comment: This paper will be published in the ICLR 2026 proceedings
summary: We study diffusion-based world models for reinforcement learning, which offer high generative fidelity but face critical efficiency challenges in control. Current methods either require heavyweight models at inference or rely on highly sequential imagination, both of which impose prohibitive computational costs. We propose Horizon Imagination (HI), an on-policy imagination process for discrete stochastic policies that denoises multiple future observations in parallel. HI incorporates a stabilization mechanism and a novel sampling schedule that decouples the denoising budget from the effective horizon over which denoising is applied while also supporting sub-frame budgets. Experiments on Atari 100K and Craftium show that our approach maintains control performance with a sub-frame budget of half the denoising steps and achieves superior generation quality under varied schedules. Code is available at https://github.com/leor-c/horizon-imagination.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based world models for RL are made practical by Horizon Imagination (HI): an on-policy, parallel denoising method for discrete stochastic policies that decouples the denoising budget from the effective horizon and supports sub-frame budgets, yielding efficient control with maintained performance on Atari 100K and Craftium; code available.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based world models offer high generative fidelity but are computationally costly due to heavyweight inference or sequential imagination. There is a need for efficient, on-policy imagination that can run with reduced budgets.
AI.method: HI introduces an on-policy imagination process for discrete stochastic policies that denoises multiple future observations in parallel. It includes a stabilization mechanism and a novel sampling schedule that decouples the denoising budget from the horizon and supports sub-frame budgets.
AI.result: Empirical results show maintained control performance with a sub-frame budget of half the denoising steps and superior generation quality under varied schedules on Atari 100K and Craftium.
AI.conclusion: HI makes diffusion-based world models more computationally efficient for RL by enabling parallel denoising with decoupled budgets, potentially broadening practical applicability of diffusion-based planning in RL.

title: Generative Regression for Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction Estimation from Echocardiography Video
comment: 11 pages, 5 tables, 10 figures. Under peer review
summary: Estimating Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction (LVEF) from echocardiograms constitutes an ill-posed inverse problem. Inherent noise, artifacts, and limited viewing angles introduce ambiguity, where a single video sequence may map not to a unique ground truth, but rather to a distribution of plausible physiological values. Prevailing deep learning approaches typically formulate this task as a standard regression problem that minimizes the Mean Squared Error (MSE). However, this paradigm compels the model to learn the conditional expectation, which may yield misleading predictions when the underlying posterior distribution is multimodal or heavy-tailed -- a common phenomenon in pathological scenarios. In this paper, we investigate the paradigm shift from deterministic regression toward generative regression. We propose the Multimodal Conditional Score-based Diffusion model for Regression (MCSDR), a probabilistic framework designed to model the continuous posterior distribution of LVEF conditioned on echocardiogram videos and patient demographic attribute priors. Extensive experiments conducted on the EchoNet-Dynamic, EchoNet-Pediatric, and CAMUS datasets demonstrate that MCSDR achieves state-of-the-art performance. Notably, qualitative analysis reveals that the generation trajectories of our model exhibit distinct behaviors in cases characterized by high noise or significant physiological variability, thereby offering a novel layer of interpretability for AI-aided diagnosis.

AI.tldr: Introduces MCSDR, a multimodal conditional score-based diffusion model for regression to estimate LVEF from echocardiograms, modeling the full posterior rather than a point estimate; achieves state-of-the-art on multiple datasets and offers interpretability through generation trajectories.
AI.motivation: LVEF estimation from echocardiograms is ill-posed due to noise, artifacts, and limited viewing angles. Standard regression with MSE yields the conditional mean, which can be misleading when the posterior is multimodal or heavy-tailed, especially in pathological cases. A probabilistic, multimodal regression approach can capture uncertainty and improve interpretability.
AI.method: Proposes MCSDR, a Multimodal Conditional Score-based Diffusion model for Regression. It models the continuous posterior distribution of LVEF conditioned on echocardiogram videos and patient demographic priors, using a diffusion-based generative regression framework to produce plausible LVEF values and associated uncertainties. Evaluated on EchoNet-Dynamic, EchoNet-Pediatric, and CAMUS datasets.
AI.result: MCSDR achieves state-of-the-art performance across the tested datasets. Qualitative analyses show that generated LVEF trajectories differ across cases with high noise or substantial physiological variability, providing a new layer of interpretability for AI-assisted diagnosis.
AI.conclusion: Modeling the posterior distribution via a multimodal diffusion-based regression framework yields superior performance and interpretable uncertainty in LVEF estimation from echocardiograms, addressing the ill-posedness of the problem and suggesting broader applicability to medical inverse problems.

title: The Benefits of Diversity: Combining Comparisons and Ratings for Efficient Scoring
summary: Should humans be asked to evaluate entities individually or comparatively? This question has been the subject of long debates. In this work, we show that, interestingly, combining both forms of preference elicitation can outperform the focus on a single kind. More specifically, we introduce SCoRa (Scoring from Comparisons and Ratings), a unified probabilistic model that allows to learn from both signals. We prove that the MAP estimator of SCoRa is well-behaved. It verifies monotonicity and robustness guarantees. We then empirically show that SCoRa recovers accurate scores, even under model mismatch. Most interestingly, we identify a realistic setting where combining comparisons and ratings outperforms using either one alone, and when the accurate ordering of top entities is critical. Given the de facto availability of signals of multiple forms, SCoRa additionally offers a versatile foundation for preference learning.

AI.tldr: A unified probabilistic model, SCoRa, learns from both pairwise comparisons and ratings to improve preference learning, outperforming single-signal approaches, with a well-behaved MAP estimator (monotonicity and robustness) and empirical validation including robustness to model mismatch.
AI.motivation: Preference signals come in multiple forms (comparisons and ratings). A framework that integrates these signals can exploit complementary information and improve accuracy and ranking, motivating a unified probabilistic approach.
AI.method: Introduce SCoRa (Scoring from Comparisons and Ratings), a probabilistic model that jointly models ratings and comparisons for items. Derive the MAP estimator and prove monotonicity and robustness guarantees. Empirically evaluate recovery of true scores and sensitivity to model mismatch, and identify scenarios where combining signals outperforms single-signal methods, particularly for accurate top-item ordering.
AI.result: SCoRa yields accurate score recovery and remains robust under model mismatch. In a realistic setting, combining comparisons and ratings outperforms using either form alone, especially when precise top-item ordering is crucial.
AI.conclusion: SCoRa provides a versatile foundation for preference learning by leveraging multi-form signals, enabling improved scoring and ranking in practical scenarios where both comparisons and ratings are available.

title: Geospatial-Reasoning-Driven Vocabulary-Agnostic Remote Sensing Semantic Segmentation
summary: Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation has emerged as a promising research direction in remote sensing, enabling the recognition of diverse land-cover types beyond pre-defined category sets. However, existing methods predominantly rely on the passive mapping of visual features and textual embeddings. This ``appearance-based" paradigm lacks geospatial contextual awareness, leading to severe semantic ambiguity and misclassification when encountering land-cover classes with similar spectral features but distinct semantic attributes. To address this, we propose a Geospatial Reasoning Chain-of-Thought (GR-CoT) framework designed to enhance the scene understanding capabilities of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), thereby guiding open-vocabulary segmentation models toward precise mapping. The framework comprises two collaborative components: an offline knowledge distillation stream and an online instance reasoning stream. The offline stream establishes fine-grained category interpretation standards to resolve semantic conflicts between similar land-cover types. During online inference, the framework executes a sequential reasoning process involving macro-scenario anchoring, visual feature decoupling, and knowledge-driven decision synthesis. This process generates an image-adaptive vocabulary that guides downstream models to achieve pixel-level alignment with correct geographical semantics. Extensive experiments on the LoveDA and GID5 benchmarks demonstrate the superiority of our approach.

AI.tldr: GR-CoT leverages geospatial reasoning to improve open-vocabulary remote sensing segmentation with offline distillation and online reasoning, achieving state-of-the-art on LoveDA and GID5.
AI.motivation: Open-vocabulary segmentation relies on visual-text alignment but ignores geospatial context, causing semantic ambiguity between similar land-cover types.
AI.method: Two-stream GR-CoT: offline knowledge distillation to create fine-grained category interpretations; online instance reasoning with macro-scenario anchoring, visual feature decoupling, and knowledge-driven decision synthesis to generate an image-adaptive vocabulary guiding pixel-level geo-semantics.
AI.result: Demonstrates superiority on LoveDA and GID5 benchmarks; improved pixel-level alignment with geographical semantics.
AI.conclusion: Geospatial reasoning integrated with MLLMs can resolve semantic conflicts in open-vocabulary remote sensing segmentation and generalize to geospatially aware scene understanding.

title: Chain-of-Caption: Training-free improvement of multimodal large language model on referring expression comprehension
summary: Given a textual description, the task of referring expression comprehension (REC) involves the localisation of the referred object in an image. Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved high accuracy on REC benchmarks through scaling up the model size and training data. Moreover, the performance of MLLMs can be further improved using techniques such as Chain-of-Thought and tool use, which provides additional visual or textual context to the model. In this paper, we analyse the effect of various techniques for providing additional visual and textual context via tool use to the MLLM and its effect on the REC task. Furthermore, we propose a training-free framework named Chain-of-Caption to improve the REC performance of MLLMs. We perform experiments on RefCOCO/RefCOCOg/RefCOCO+ and Ref-L4 datasets and show that individual textual or visual context can improve the REC performance without any fine-tuning. By combining multiple contexts, our training-free framework shows between 5% to 30% performance gain over the baseline model on accuracy at various Intersection over Union (IoU) thresholds.

AI.tldr: Introduce Chain-of-Caption, a training-free framework that leverages textual and visual context via tool use to boost referring expression comprehension (REC) in multimodal LLMs; yields 5–30% accuracy gains over baselines on RefCOCO/RefCOCOg/RefCOCO+/Ref-L4 without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: REC models using multimodal LLMs benefit from extra context but typically rely on fine-tuning or specialized modules. The paper investigates the impact of various context-provision techniques (textual and visual) via tool use and proposes a training-free method to exploit such cues to improve REC.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of context-provision techniques; propose Chain-of-Caption as a training-free framework; combine multiple textual and visual contexts provided through prompting/tool use; evaluate on standard REC benchmarks (RefCOCO, RefCOCOg, RefCOCO+, Ref-L4) without fine-tuning.
AI.result: Individual textual or visual contexts improve REC without fine-tuning; combining multiple contexts yields 5–30% absolute improvement in accuracy across IoU thresholds compared to baseline LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Training-free context augmentation is effective for REC in MLLMs; Chain-of-Caption demonstrates notable gains and suggests broader potential for context-based prompting in multimodal tasks without training.

title: FIRE: Frobenius-Isometry Reinitialization for Balancing the Stability-Plasticity Tradeoff
summary: Deep neural networks trained on nonstationary data must balance stability (i.e., retaining prior knowledge) and plasticity (i.e., adapting to new tasks). Standard reinitialization methods, which reinitialize weights toward their original values, are widely used but difficult to tune: conservative reinitializations fail to restore plasticity, while aggressive ones erase useful knowledge. We propose FIRE, a principled reinitialization method that explicitly balances the stability-plasticity tradeoff. FIRE quantifies stability through Squared Frobenius Error (SFE), measuring proximity to past weights, and plasticity through Deviation from Isometry (DfI), reflecting weight isotropy. The reinitialization point is obtained by solving a constrained optimization problem, minimizing SFE subject to DfI being zero, which is efficiently approximated by Newton-Schulz iteration. FIRE is evaluated on continual visual learning (CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18), language modeling (OpenWebText with GPT-0.1B), and reinforcement learning (HumanoidBench with SAC and Atari games with DQN). Across all domains, FIRE consistently outperforms both naive training without intervention and standard reinitialization methods, demonstrating effective balancing of the stability-plasticity tradeoff.

AI.tldr: FIRE is a principled reinitialization method that balances stability (retaining past knowledge) and plasticity (adapting to new tasks) by optimizing a trade-off between proximity to past weights (Squared Frobenius Error, SFE) and weight isotropy (Deviation from Isometry, DfI). The reinitialization point solves a constrained optimization (minimize SFE with DfI = 0), efficiently approximated via Newton-Schulz iteration, and validated across vision, language, and reinforcement learning tasks.
AI.motivation: Nonstationary data in continual learning settings causes catastrophic forgetting if stability is overemphasized and prevents adaptation if plasticity is prioritized too much. Standard reinitialization methods are sensitive to tuning and may either erode useful knowledge or fail to restore plasticity.
AI.method: Define SFE to quantify closeness to past weights and DfI to quantify weight isotropy. Find the reinitialization point by solving a constrained optimization that minimizes SFE subject to DfI = 0; approximate the constraint with Newton-Schulz iterations for efficiency. Evaluate FIRE on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18, OpenWebText with GPT-0.1B, and HumanoidBench with SAC and Atari games with DQN.
