AI.result: FIRE consistently outperforms both naive training and standard reinitialization methods across all domains, demonstrating effective control of the stability–plasticity tradeoff and generalizing to vision, language, and reinforcement learning tasks.
AI.conclusion: FIRE provides a principled, efficient reinitialization mechanism that explicitly balances retaining past knowledge with adapting to new tasks, improving continual learning performance across multiple domains.

title: Implicit Strategic Optimization: Rethinking Long-Horizon Decision-Making in Adversarial Poker Environments
summary: Training large language model (LLM) agents for adversarial games is often driven by episodic objectives such as win rate. In long-horizon settings, however, payoffs are shaped by latent strategic externalities that evolve over time, so myopic optimization and variation-based regret analyses can become vacuous even when the dynamics are predictable. To solve this problem, we introduce Implicit Strategic Optimization (ISO), a prediction-aware framework in which each agent forecasts the current strategic context and uses it to update its policy online. ISO combines a Strategic Reward Model (SRM) that estimates the long-run strategic value of actions with iso-grpo, a context-conditioned optimistic learning rule. We prove sublinear contextual regret and equilibrium convergence guarantees whose dominant terms scale with the number of context mispredictions; when prediction errors are bounded, our bounds recover the static-game rates obtained when strategic externalities are known. Experiments in 6-player No-Limit Texas Hold'em and competitive Pokemon show consistent improvements in long-term return over strong LLM and RL baselines, and graceful degradation under controlled prediction noise.

AI.tldr: Introduce Implicit Strategic Optimization (ISO) for LLM agents in long-horizon adversarial games; combines SRM and iso-grpo to achieve prediction-aware learning with sublinear contextual regret and equilibrium convergence; demonstrates empirical gains in multi-player domains and robustness to noise.
AI.motivation: Myopic objective optimization fails in long-horizon strategic settings due to evolving externalities; need prediction-aware methods that account for changing strategic context to improve long-run payoff.
AI.method: Propose ISO framework with Strategic Reward Model (SRM) estimating long-run value of actions; iso-grpo, a context-conditioned optimistic learning rule; theoretical analysis yields sublinear contextual regret and equilibrium convergence; regret depends on context mispredictions; bounded errors recover static-game rates.
AI.result: Theoretically, sublinear contextual regret and convergence to equilibria; empirically, improved long-term return vs strong LLM and RL baselines in 6-player No-Limit Hold'em and competitive Pokemon; performance degrades gracefully under prediction noise.
AI.conclusion: ISO provides a principled, prediction-aware approach to long-horizon strategic optimization for LLM agents; link between prediction accuracy and regret; practical performance gains with robustness to noise.

title: Generating Adversarial Events: A Motion-Aware Point Cloud Framework
summary: Event cameras have been widely adopted in safety-critical domains such as autonomous driving, robotics, and human-computer interaction. A pressing challenge arises from the vulnerability of deep neural networks to adversarial examples, which poses a significant threat to the reliability of event-based systems. Nevertheless, research into adversarial attacks on events is scarce. This is primarily due to the non-differentiable nature of mainstream event representations, which hinders the extension of gradient-based attack methods. In this paper, we propose MA-ADV, a novel \textbf{M}otion-\textbf{A}ware \textbf{Adv}ersarial framework. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to generate adversarial events by leveraging point cloud representations. MA-ADV accounts for high-frequency noise in events and employs a diffusion-based approach to smooth perturbations, while fully leveraging the spatial and temporal relationships among events. Finally, MA-ADV identifies the minimal-cost perturbation through a combination of sample-wise Adam optimization, iterative refinement, and binary search. Extensive experimental results validate that MA-ADV ensures a 100\% attack success rate with minimal perturbation cost, and also demonstrate enhanced robustness against defenses, underscoring the critical security challenges facing future event-based perception systems.

AI.tldr: MA-ADV is a motion-aware adversarial framework for event cameras that uses point-cloud representations and diffusion-based smoothing to craft minimal perturbations, achieving 100% attack success and robustness against defenses.
AI.motivation: Event-based deep networks are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, but attacks on events are underexplored due to non-differentiable representations; there is a need to develop differentiable, effective attack methods that respect the temporal/spatial structure of events.
AI.method: Introduce MA-ADV, a framework that generates adversarial events via point-cloud representations. It accounts for high-frequency noise, uses diffusion-based smoothing to perturbations, and leverages spatial-temporal relationships. Perturbations are minimized via sample-wise Adam optimization, iterative refinement, and binary search.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation reports a 100% attack success rate with minimal perturbation cost and improved robustness against defenses.
AI.conclusion: MA-ADV uncovers critical security risks in event-based perception systems and represents the first work to generate adversarial events through point-cloud-based methods, underscoring the need for robust defenses in event-based DL.

title: V-ABFT: Variance-Based Adaptive Threshold for Fault-Tolerant Matrix Multiplication in Mixed-Precision Deep Learning
summary: Algorithm-Based Fault Tolerance (ABFT) is widely adopted to detect silent data corruptions (SDCs) in matrix multiplication, a cornerstone operation in deep learning systems. However, existing threshold determination methods face critical challenges: analytical bounds are overly conservative, while probabilistic approaches like A-ABFT yield thresholds $160$--$4200\times$ larger than actual rounding errors. We present V-ABFT, a variance-based adaptive threshold algorithm that achieves tighter error bounds by directly modeling the verification difference. By leveraging statistical variance estimation, V-ABFT reduces the threshold-to-actual-error ratio to approximately $7$--$20\times$ for FP32/FP64 and $48$--$158\times$ for BF16, representing a \textbf{6--48$\times$ improvement} over A-ABFT while maintaining zero false positive rate across BF16, FP16, FP32, and FP64 precisions. Furthermore, we demonstrate that for fused-kernel ABFT implementations that verify before output quantization, low-precision GEMM can use FP32-level thresholds ($e_{\max} \approx 10^{-6}$), enabling \textbf{$\sim$1000$\times$ finer detection granularity} compared to offline verification with low-precision output ($e_{\max} \approx 10^{-3}$). We reproduce A-ABFT's experimental setup and validate our implementation against the original paper's results. Our method requires only $O(n)$ complexity using max/min/mean statistics, compared to A-ABFT's $O(pn)$ for finding $p$ largest values. Extensive experiments on synthetic data and real model weights (LLaMA-7B, GPT-2, ViT) demonstrate V-ABFT's effectiveness across diverse distributions. V-ABFT is platform-agnostic and has been integrated into fault-tolerant GEMM implementations on both NPUs and GPUs.

AI.tldr: V-ABFT is a variance-based adaptive threshold for ABFT-based silent data corruption detection in GEMM, achieving significantly tighter thresholds than A-ABFT with zero false positives, reduced computational complexity, and robust performance across FP16/32/64 and BF16, including fused-kernel ABFT and low-precision deployments.
AI.motivation: Improve ABFT threshold accuracy to detect SDCs more reliably without sacrificing false-positive safety, especially for low-precision and fused-kernel GEMM used in deep learning, where current analytical bounds are too conservative and probabilistic methods overestimate thresholds.
AI.method: Model verification difference with variance estimation (statistical approach). Compute thresholds using max/min/mean statistics with O(n) complexity, instead of O(pn) to extract p largest deviations. Apply to FP16/32/64 and BF16; validate on synthetic data and real weights (LLaMA-7B, GPT-2, ViT); test across NPUs and GPUs; demonstrate zero FP across precisions; show improved granularity for fused-kernel ABFT with FP32 thresholds.
AI.result: Threshold-to-actual-error ratio reduced to ~7–20× (FP32/FP64) and ~48–158× (BF16); ~6–48× improvement over A-ABFT; enables ~1000× finer detection granularity in fused-kernel ABFT with pre-quantization verification; zero false positives across tested precisions; O(n) detectable using simple statistics; validated on synthetic data and real models; platform-agnostic and integrated into GEMM on NPUs/GPUs.
AI.conclusion: V-ABFT substantially tightens ABFT verification thresholds via a variance-based estimator, delivering strong detection granularity with low computational cost and zero false positives across widely-used precisions, and is ready for practical deployment in fault-tolerant GEMM.)

title: When and How Much to Imagine: Adaptive Test-Time Scaling with World Models for Visual Spatial Reasoning
comment: the first two authors are equally contributed. Project page: https://adaptive-visual-tts.github.io/
summary: Despite rapid progress in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), visual spatial reasoning remains unreliable when correct answers depend on how a scene would appear under unseen or alternative viewpoints. Recent work addresses this by augmenting reasoning with world models for visual imagination, but questions such as when imagination is actually necessary, how much of it is beneficial, and when it becomes harmful, remain poorly understood. In practice, indiscriminate imagination can increase computation and even degrade performance by introducing misleading evidence. In this work, we present an in-depth analysis of test-time visual imagination as a controllable resource for spatial reasoning. We study when static visual evidence is sufficient, when imagination improves reasoning, and how excessive or unnecessary imagination affects accuracy and efficiency. To support this analysis, we introduce AVIC, an adaptive test-time framework with world models that explicitly reasons about the sufficiency of current visual evidence before selectively invoking and scaling visual imagination. Across spatial reasoning benchmarks (SAT, MMSI) and an embodied navigation benchmark (R2R), our results reveal clear scenarios where imagination is critical, marginal, or detrimental, and show that selective control can match or outperform fixed imagination strategies with substantially fewer world-model calls and language tokens. Overall, our findings highlight the importance of analyzing and controlling test-time imagination for efficient and reliable spatial reasoning.

AI.tldr: Adaptive test-time visual imagination via AVIC for spatial reasoning in MLLMs; selective imagination matches or exceeds fixed strategies with fewer world-model calls and tokens across SAT, MMSI, and R2R.
AI.motivation: Visual spatial reasoning with unseen viewpoints is brittle; imagination through world models can help but is costly and sometimes harmful. Understanding when and how much imagination is beneficial enables more reliable and efficient MLLMs.
AI.method: Introduce AVIC, an adaptive framework that explicitly assesses the sufficiency of current visual evidence before invoking and scaling visual imagination. Evaluated on spatial reasoning benchmarks (SAT, MMSI) and embodied navigation (R2R); compares adaptive control with static imagination and baseline no-imagination.
AI.result: Imagination is not universally beneficial: it is critical in certain scenarios, marginal in others, and detrimental in some cases. The adaptive, selective control approach matches or outperforms fixed imagination strategies while reducing world-model calls and language tokens, improving efficiency and reliability.
AI.conclusion: Controlling test-time imagination is essential for efficient and reliable spatial reasoning in MLLMs. AVIC demonstrates how explicit sufficiency checks and adaptive scaling can optimize the trade-off between evidence, computation, and performance.

title: Interpretable Fuzzy Systems For Forward Osmosis Desalination
summary: Preserving interpretability in fuzzy rule-based systems (FRBS) is vital for water treatment, where decisions impact public health. While structural interpretability has been addressed using multi-objective algorithms, semantic interpretability often suffers due to fuzzy sets with low distinguishability. We propose a human-in-the-loop approach for developing interpretable FRBS to predict forward osmosis desalination productivity. Our method integrates expert-driven grid partitioning for distinguishable membership functions, domain-guided feature engineering to reduce redundancy, and rule pruning based on firing strength. This approach achieved comparable predictive performance to cluster-based FRBS while maintaining semantic interpretability and meeting structural complexity constraints, providing an explainable solution for water treatment applications.

AI.tldr: A human-in-the-loop framework for interpretable FRBS in water treatment, combining expert-guided grid partitioning, domain-guided feature engineering, and firing-strength-based pruning to predict forward osmosis productivity; achieves competitive accuracy with cluster-based FRBS while preserving semantic interpretability under structural constraints.
AI.motivation: Interpretability issues in fuzzy rule-based systems (FRBS) for water treatment, where semantic interpretability is compromised by indistinguishable fuzzy sets, and decisions impact public health. A human-in-the-loop approach aims to enhance both semantic and structural interpretability without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: 1) Expert-driven grid partitioning to create distinguishable membership functions. 2) Domain-guided feature engineering to reduce redundancy and align features with domain knowledge. 3) Rule pruning based on firing strength to simplify the rule base. 4) Integrate human insight into FRBS design to balance interpretability and predictive power.
AI.result: The proposed approach achieved predictive performance comparable to cluster-based FRBS while preserving semantic interpretability and meeting structural complexity constraints, yielding an explainable solution suitable for water treatment applications.
AI.conclusion: A human-in-the-loop FRBS design can deliver interpretable and accurate desalination productivity predictions, addressing semantic and structural interpretability requirements in water treatment contexts.

title: Moving Beyond Functional Connectivity: Time-Series Modeling for fMRI-Based Brain Disorder Classification
comment: This paper has been accepted by IEEE Transactions on Medical Imaging
summary: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) enables non-invasive brain disorder classification by capturing blood-oxygen-level-dependent (BOLD) signals. However, most existing methods rely on functional connectivity (FC) via Pearson correlation, which reduces 4D BOLD signals to static 2D matrices, discarding temporal dynamics and capturing only linear inter-regional relationships. In this work, we benchmark state-of-the-art temporal models (e.g., time-series models such as PatchTST, TimesNet, and TimeMixer) on raw BOLD signals across five public datasets. Results show these models consistently outperform traditional FC-based approaches, highlighting the value of directly modeling temporal information such as cycle-like oscillatory fluctuations and drift-like slow baseline trends. Building on this insight, we propose DeCI, a simple yet effective framework that integrates two key principles: (i) Cycle and Drift Decomposition to disentangle cycle and drift within each ROI (Region of Interest); and (ii) Channel-Independence to model each ROI separately, improving robustness and reducing overfitting. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DeCI achieves superior classification accuracy and generalization compared to both FC-based and temporal baselines. Our findings advocate for a shift toward end-to-end temporal modeling in fMRI analysis to better capture complex brain dynamics. The code is available at https://github.com/Levi-Ackman/DeCI.

AI.tldr: Temporal models applied to raw BOLD signals surpass traditional FC-based approaches for fMRI disorder classification; a novel DeCI framework with cycle/drift decomposition and channel-independence improves accuracy and generalization.
AI.motivation: Functional connectivity via Pearson correlation reduces 4D BOLD data to static 2D matrices, losing temporal dynamics and non-linear interactions. There is a need to evaluate end-to-end temporal models on raw fMRI signals across datasets to assess potential performance gains.
AI.method: Benchmark state-of-the-art time-series models (PatchTST, TimesNet, TimeMixer) on raw BOLD signals across five public datasets. Propose DeCI: (i) Cycle and Drift Decomposition within each ROI to separate cycle-like (oscillatory) and drift-like (baseline) components; (ii) Channel-Independence by modeling each ROI separately to reduce overfitting and improve robustness.
AI.result: Temporal models consistently outperform FC-based baselines. DeCI achieves superior classification accuracy and generalization compared to both FC-based and temporal baselines, with code available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end temporal modeling is advantageous for fMRI analysis, capturing complex brain dynamics beyond static connectivity. DeCI offers a simple, effective framework with strong empirical performance and potential for broader adoption.

title: Epigraph-Guided Flow Matching for Safe and Performant Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) provides a compelling paradigm for training autonomous systems without the risks of online exploration, particularly in safety-critical domains. However, jointly achieving strong safety and performance from fixed datasets remains challenging. Existing safe offline RL methods often rely on soft constraints that allow violations, introduce excessive conservatism, or struggle to balance safety, reward optimization, and adherence to the data distribution. To address this, we propose Epigraph-Guided Flow Matching (EpiFlow), a framework that formulates safe offline RL as a state-constrained optimal control problem to co-optimize safety and performance. We learn a feasibility value function derived from an epigraph reformulation of the optimal control problem, thereby avoiding the decoupled objectives or post-hoc filtering common in prior work. Policies are synthesized by reweighting the behavior distribution based on this epigraph value function and fitting a generative policy via flow matching, enabling efficient, distribution-consistent sampling. Across various safety-critical tasks, including Safety-Gymnasium benchmarks, EpiFlow achieves competitive returns with near-zero empirical safety violations, demonstrating the effectiveness of epigraph-guided policy synthesis.

AI.tldr: Proposes EpiFlow, an offline RL framework that uses epigraph-guided flow matching to jointly optimize safety and performance via a state-constrained optimal control formulation; achieves competitive returns with near-zero safety violations on Safety-Gymnasium.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of safe offline RL methods that rely on soft constraints, excessive conservatism, or decoupled safety/reward objectives that trade off performance and safety or violate the data distribution.
AI.method: Formulate safe offline RL as a state-constrained optimal control problem. Learn a feasibility value function from an epigraph reformulation to avoid decoupled objectives or post-hoc filtering. Synthesize policies by reweighting the behavior distribution using the epigraph value and fit a generative policy via flow matching to ensure distributional consistency.
AI.result: Across safety-critical tasks, including Safety-Gymnasium benchmarks, EpiFlow achieves competitive returns with near-zero empirical safety violations.
AI.conclusion: Epigraph-guided policy synthesis is effective for jointly balancing safety and performance in offline RL, addressing limitations of prior approaches by integrating feasibility via an epigraph formulation and distribution-consistent policy generation.

title: PISCO: Precise Video Instance Insertion with Sparse Control
summary: The landscape of AI video generation is undergoing a pivotal shift: moving beyond general generation - which relies on exhaustive prompt-engineering and "cherry-picking" - towards fine-grained, controllable generation and high-fidelity post-processing. In professional AI-assisted filmmaking, it is crucial to perform precise, targeted modifications. A cornerstone of this transition is video instance insertion, which requires inserting a specific instance into existing footage while maintaining scene integrity. Unlike traditional video editing, this task demands several requirements: precise spatial-temporal placement, physically consistent scene interaction, and the faithful preservation of original dynamics - all achieved under minimal user effort. In this paper, we propose PISCO, a video diffusion model for precise video instance insertion with arbitrary sparse keyframe control. PISCO allows users to specify a single keyframe, start-and-end keyframes, or sparse keyframes at arbitrary timestamps, and automatically propagates object appearance, motion, and interaction. To address the severe distribution shift induced by sparse conditioning in pretrained video diffusion models, we introduce Variable-Information Guidance for robust conditioning and Distribution-Preserving Temporal Masking to stabilize temporal generation, together with geometry-aware conditioning for realistic scene adaptation. We further construct PISCO-Bench, a benchmark with verified instance annotations and paired clean background videos, and evaluate performance using both reference-based and reference-free perceptual metrics. Experiments demonstrate that PISCO consistently outperforms strong inpainting and video editing baselines under sparse control, and exhibits clear, monotonic performance improvements as additional control signals are provided. Project page: xiangbogaobarry.github.io/PISCO.

AI.tldr: PISCO is a video diffusion model enabling precise video instance insertion with arbitrary sparse keyframe control, addressing distribution shift and temporal coherence via Variable-Information Guidance, Distribution-Preserving Temporal Masking, and geometry-aware conditioning; it introduces PISCO-Bench and outperforms baselines with increasing control signals.
AI.motivation: There is a shift in AI video generation toward fine-grained, controllable generation for professional workflows. Inserting an explicit instance into existing footage requires precise spatial-temporal placement, realistic interactions, and minimal user effort, which current methods struggle to achieve under sparse conditioning.
AI.method: PISCO uses a video diffusion framework allowing single keyframe, start-end keyframes, or sparse keyframes for instance insertion. It propagates appearance, motion, and interactions. It introduces Variable-Information Guidance for robust conditioning, Distribution-Preserving Temporal Masking for temporal stability, and geometry-aware conditioning for scene realism. It also provides PISCO-Bench with verified instance annotations and paired clean backgrounds for evaluation using both reference-based and reference-free metrics.
AI.result: Empirical results show PISCO outperforms strong inpainting and video editing baselines under sparse control. Performance improves monotonically as additional control signals are provided.
AI.conclusion: PISCO provides an effective, controllable solution for precise video instance insertion with sparse cues, supported by targeted conditioning strategies and a dedicated benchmark to evaluate realism and consistency.

title: Compiler-Assisted Speculative Sampling for Accelerated LLM Inference on Heterogeneous Edge Devices
summary: LLM deployment on resource-constrained edge devices faces severe latency constraints, particularly in real-time applications where delayed responses can compromise safety or usability. Among many approaches to mitigate the inefficiencies of sequential token-by-token generation, Speculative Decoding (SD) has emerged as a promising technique. However, SD at the edge is hindered by two major challenges: (1) integrating SD into a compiler-based workflow without sacrificing performance or programmability, and (2) exploiting the heterogeneous compute resources of modern SoCs through carefully designed partitioning strategies. This work addresses these challenges by using an analytical cost model that explores heterogeneous hardware configurations and guides coarse-grained partitioning of LLM subgraphs, particularly with edge-typical short input sequence lengths. The cost model predicts when speculative sampling and heterogeneous execution are jointly beneficial and is validated on an edge device featuring a hexacore Cortex-A CPU and a Mali GPU, revealing up to 1.68$\times$ speedup for translation tasks, closely matching analytic expectations.

AI.tldr: A cost-model-guided approach for edge LLM inference using speculative decoding and coarse-grained partitioning, validated on a Cortex-A/Mali platform, achieving up to 1.68x speedup for translation.
AI.motivation: Real-time, latency-constrained LLM deployment on edge devices; challenge: integrate speculative decoding into compiler workflows and exploit heterogeneous compute resources through effective partitioning.
AI.method: Develop an analytical cost model over heterogeneous hardware configurations to guide coarse-grained partitioning of LLM subgraphs for edge-typical short inputs; validate on a hexacore Cortex-A CPU with a Mali GPU; assess when speculative sampling and heterogeneous execution are jointly advantageous.
AI.result: The cost model accurately predicts when the combination of speculative sampling and heterogeneous execution is beneficial and yields up to 1.68× speedup for translation tasks on the edge device, aligning closely with analytical expectations.
AI.conclusion: A predictive cost model can guide SD deployment and hardware-aware partitioning for edge LLM inference, enabling substantial latency reductions under edge-typical workloads.

title: Tighnari v2: Mitigating Label Noise and Distribution Shift in Multimodal Plant Distribution Prediction via Mixture of Experts and Weakly Supervised Learning
summary: Large-scale, cross-species plant distribution prediction plays a crucial role in biodiversity conservation, yet modeling efforts in this area still face significant challenges due to the sparsity and bias of observational data. Presence-Absence (PA) data provide accurate and noise-free labels, but are costly to obtain and limited in quantity; Presence-Only (PO) data, by contrast, offer broad spatial coverage and rich spatiotemporal distribution, but suffer from severe label noise in negative samples. To address these real-world constraints, this paper proposes a multimodal fusion framework that fully leverages the strengths of both PA and PO data. We introduce an innovative pseudo-label aggregation strategy for PO data based on the geographic coverage of satellite imagery, enabling geographic alignment between the label space and remote sensing feature space. In terms of model architecture, we adopt Swin Transformer Base as the backbone for satellite imagery, utilize the TabM network for tabular feature extraction, retain the Temporal Swin Transformer for time-series modeling, and employ a stackable serial tri-modal cross-attention mechanism to optimize the fusion of heterogeneous modalities. Furthermore, empirical analysis reveals significant geographic distribution shifts between PA training and test samples, and models trained by directly mixing PO and PA data tend to experience performance degradation due to label noise in PO data. To address this, we draw on the mixture-of-experts paradigm: test samples are partitioned according to their spatial proximity to PA samples, and different models trained on distinct datasets are used for inference and post-processing within each partition. Experiments on the GeoLifeCLEF 2025 dataset demonstrate that our approach achieves superior predictive performance in scenarios with limited PA coverage and pronounced distribution shifts.

AI.tldr: A multimodal framework that fuses Presence-Absence (PA) and Presence-Only (PO) data for cross-species plant distribution, introducing PO pseudo-labels via satellite imagery geographic coverage, a Swin/TabM/Temporal Swin backbone, and a stackable serial tri-modal cross-attention fusion with mixture-of-experts to handle distribution shifts; validated on GeoLifeCLEF 2025 with limited PA coverage.
AI.motivation: Tackle sparse, bias-prone PA data and noisy PO labels, together with geographic distribution shifts, by aligning PO-derived signals with remote-sensing features and by using partitioned expert models to adapt to regional data characteristics.
AI.method: Backbone: Swin Transformer Base for satellite imagery; TabM for tabular features; Temporal Swin Transformer for time-series. Fusion via a stackable serial tri-modal cross-attention mechanism. PO data are pseudo-labeled using a novel aggregation strategy based on satellite imagery geographic coverage to align label space with RS feature space. Test-time inference uses a mixture-of-experts approach: partition test samples by spatial proximity to PA samples and apply models trained on corresponding data subsets, with post-processing within each partition.
AI.result: Experiments on GeoLifeCLEF 2025 show superior predictive performance under conditions of limited PA coverage and pronounced distribution shifts, compared with baselines that directly mix PO and PA data.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that integrating PA and PO data with geometry-aware pseudo-labels and partitioned mixture-of-experts can mitigate PO label noise and distribution shift, offering a practical, scalable framework for cross-species plant distribution prediction.

title: Efficient and Adaptable Detection of Malicious LLM Prompts via Bootstrap Aggregation
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in natural language understanding, reasoning, and generation. However, these systems remain susceptible to malicious prompts that induce unsafe or policy-violating behavior through harmful requests, jailbreak techniques, and prompt injection attacks. Existing defenses face fundamental limitations: black-box moderation APIs offer limited transparency and adapt poorly to evolving threats, while white-box approaches using large LLM judges impose prohibitive computational costs and require expensive retraining for new attacks. Current systems force designers to choose between performance, efficiency, and adaptability.
  To address these challenges, we present BAGEL (Bootstrap AGgregated Ensemble Layer), a modular, lightweight, and incrementally updatable framework for malicious prompt detection. BAGEL employs a bootstrap aggregation and mixture of expert inspired ensemble of fine-tuned models, each specialized on a different attack dataset. At inference, BAGEL uses a random forest router to identify the most suitable ensemble member, then applies stochastic selection to sample additional members for prediction aggregation. When new attacks emerge, BAGEL updates incrementally by fine-tuning a small prompt-safety classifier (86M parameters) and adding the resulting model to the ensemble. BAGEL achieves an F1 score of 0.92 by selecting just 5 ensemble members (430M parameters), outperforming OpenAI Moderation API and ShieldGemma which require billions of parameters. Performance remains robust after nine incremental updates, and BAGEL provides interpretability through its router's structural features. Our results show ensembles of small finetuned classifiers can match or exceed billion-parameter guardrails while offering the adaptability and efficiency required for production systems.

AI.tldr: A modular, lightweight ensemble framework (BAGEL) for malicious prompt detection that uses bootstrap aggregation and a mixture of expert classifiers, with a random-forest router to pick ensemble members and stochastic aggregation. It updates incrementally via small fine-tuned prompt-safety classifiers.
AI.motivation: Current defenses trade off transparency, cost, or adaptability (black-box moderation APIs lack transparency; large white-box judges are computationally expensive and hard to retrain for new attacks). There is a need for a scalable, efficient, and adaptable defense against evolving malicious prompts.
AI.method: BAGEL constructs an ensemble of small, fine-tuned classifiers specialized on different attack datasets. At inference, a random-forest router selects the most suitable ensemble member, and stochastic sampling adds more members to aggregate predictions. New attacks are handled by incrementally fine-tuning a small prompt-safety classifier (86M) and adding it to the ensemble.
AI.result: Achieves an F1 score of 0.92 using only 5 ensemble members (≈430M parameters), outperforming billion-parameter guards (OpenAI Moderation API, ShieldGemma). Demonstrates robustness over nine incremental updates and offers interpretability via the router's structural features.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that ensembles of small, fine-tuned classifiers can match or exceed performance of large guardrails while delivering better adaptability and efficiency for production deployment.

title: CAE-AV: Improving Audio-Visual Learning via Cross-modal Interactive Enrichment
summary: Audio-visual learning suffers from modality misalignment caused by off-screen sources and background clutter, and current methods usually amplify irrelevant regions or moments, leading to unstable training and degraded representation quality. To address this challenge, we proposed a novel Caption-aligned and Agreement-guided Enhancement framework (CAE-AV) for audio-visual learning, which used two complementary modules: Cross-modal Agreement-guided Spatio-Temporal Enrichment (CASTE) and Caption-Aligned Saliency-guided Enrichment (CASE) to relieve audio-visual misalignment. CASTE dynamically balances spatial and temporal relations by evaluating frame-level audio-visual agreement, ensuring that key information is captured from both preceding and subsequent frames under misalignment. CASE injects cross-modal semantic guidance into selected spatio-temporal positions, leveraging high-level semantic cues to further alleviate misalignment. In addition, we design lightweight objectives, caption-to-modality InfoNCE, visual-audio consistency, and entropy regularization to guide token selection and strengthen cross-modal semantic alignment. With frozen backbones, CAE-AV achieves state-of-the-art performance on AVE, AVVP, AVS, and AVQA benchmarks, and qualitative analyses further validate its robustness against audio-visual misalignment.

AI.tldr: Proposes CAE-AV with CASTE and CASE to address audio-visual misalignment; employs caption-aligned and agreement-guided enrichment; achieves state-of-the-art results on AVE, AVVP, AVS, and AVQA with frozen backbones; introduces lightweight cross-modal objectives.
AI.motivation: Modality misalignment from off-screen sources and background clutter degrades AV representations; existing methods amplify irrelevant regions, causing unstable training and poorer representations; a robust approach should align modalities and leverage semantic cues.
AI.method: CASTE dynamically balances spatial and temporal relations by frame-level audio-visual agreement to capture key information around misaligned frames; CASE injects cross-modal semantic guidance into selected spatio-temporal positions using high-level cues; introduces lightweight objectives: caption-to-modality InfoNCE, visual-audio consistency, and entropy regularization; trained with frozen backbones to maintain stability.
AI.result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on AVE, AVVP, AVS, and AVQA benchmarks; qualitative analyses support robustness to audio-visual misalignment.
AI.conclusion: CAE-AV effectively mitigates misalignment via two complementary modules (CASTE and CASE) and lightweight objectives, delivering robust cross-modal alignment with frozen backbones and strong empirical performance.

title: Efficient Distribution Learning with Error Bounds in Wasserstein Distance
summary: The Wasserstein distance has emerged as a key metric to quantify distances between probability distributions, with applications in various fields, including machine learning, control theory, decision theory, and biological systems. Consequently, learning an unknown distribution with non-asymptotic and easy-to-compute error bounds in Wasserstein distance has become a fundamental problem in many fields. In this paper, we devise a novel algorithmic and theoretical framework to approximate an unknown probability distribution $\mathbb{P}$ from a finite set of samples by an approximate discrete distribution $\widehat{\mathbb{P}}$ while bounding the Wasserstein distance between $\mathbb{P}$ and $\widehat{\mathbb{P}}$. Our framework leverages optimal transport, nonlinear optimization, and concentration inequalities. In particular, we show that, even if $\mathbb{P}$ is unknown, the Wasserstein distance between $\mathbb{P}$ and $\widehat{\mathbb{P}}$ can be efficiently bounded with high confidence by solving a tractable optimization problem (a mixed integer linear program) of a size that only depends on the size of the support of $\widehat{\mathbb{P}}$. This enables us to develop intelligent clustering algorithms to optimally find the support of $\widehat{\mathbb{P}}$ while minimizing the Wasserstein distance error. On a set of benchmarks, we demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art comparable methods by generally returning approximating distributions with substantially smaller support and tighter error bounds.

AI.tldr: A framework to learn a distribution from finite samples by a discrete approximation with non-asymptotic Wasserstein guarantees, using MILP-based bounds and clustering to optimize the support size for tight error control.
AI.motivation: Wasserstein distance is a natural metric for distributional distance; practical learning demands non-asymptotic, computable error bounds from finite data to justify approximations.
AI.method: Integrates optimal transport, nonlinear optimization, and concentration inequalities. Constructs a tractable mixed-integer linear program whose solution bounds the Wasserstein distance between the unknown P and its discrete estimate P̂. Uses intelligent clustering to determine the support of P̂, with complexity depending on the support size.
AI.result: Provides efficient, high-confidence bounds on W(P, P̂) and produces discrete approximations with smaller, tighter supports than competing methods. Empirically outperforms state-of-the-art methods on benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The framework yields practical, scalable non-asymptotic guarantees for distribution learning via discrete approximations, combining optimization and probabilistic concentration to tighten Wasserstein-distance bounds and improve support economy.

title: Language-Guided Transformer Tokenizer for Human Motion Generation
summary: In this paper, we focus on motion discrete tokenization, which converts raw motion into compact discrete tokens--a process proven crucial for efficient motion generation. In this paradigm, increasing the number of tokens is a common approach to improving motion reconstruction quality, but more tokens make it more difficult for generative models to learn. To maintain high reconstruction quality while reducing generation complexity, we propose leveraging language to achieve efficient motion tokenization, which we term Language-Guided Tokenization (LG-Tok). LG-Tok aligns natural language with motion at the tokenization stage, yielding compact, high-level semantic representations. This approach not only strengthens both tokenization and detokenization but also simplifies the learning of generative models. Furthermore, existing tokenizers predominantly adopt convolutional architectures, whose local receptive fields struggle to support global language guidance. To this end, we propose a Transformer-based Tokenizer that leverages attention mechanisms to enable effective alignment between language and motion. Additionally, we design a language-drop scheme, in which language conditions are randomly removed during training, enabling the detokenizer to support language-free guidance during generation. On the HumanML3D and Motion-X generation benchmarks, LG-Tok achieves Top-1 scores of 0.542 and 0.582, outperforming state-of-the-art methods (MARDM: 0.500 and 0.528), and with FID scores of 0.057 and 0.088, respectively, versus 0.114 and 0.147. LG-Tok-mini uses only half the tokens while maintaining competitive performance (Top-1: 0.521/0.588, FID: 0.085/0.071), validating the efficiency of our semantic representations.

AI.tldr: LG-Tok introduces Language-Guided Tokenization for motion data, using natural language to produce compact, high-level semantic tokens via a Transformer-based tokenizer; a language-drop training scheme enables language-free detokenization; achieves state-of-the-art results with fewer tokens, and LG-Tok-mini demonstrates token efficiency with competitive performance.
AI.motivation: The need to balance high motion reconstruction quality with manageable generation complexity. Traditional tokenizers (often conv-based) require many tokens for quality and struggle with global language guidance. Language-guided tokenization aims to inject semantic guidance at the tokenization stage to produce compact representations that are easier to learn.
AI.method: Propose LG-Tok, a Transformer-based tokenizer that aligns motion and natural language at tokenization to produce compact semantic tokens. Introduce a language-drop scheme so the detokenizer can support language-free generation during inference. Train and evaluate on HumanML3D and Motion-X, comparing against MARDM. Demonstrate that LG-Tok can use fewer tokens (LG-Tok-mini) while preserving performance.
AI.result: LG-Tok achieves Top-1 accuracies of 0.542 (HumanML3D) and 0.582 (Motion-X) with FID scores 0.057 and 0.088, respectively—outperforming MARDM (0.500, 0.528; FID 0.114, 0.147). LG-Tok-mini uses half the tokens and attains Top-1 0.521 and 0.588, with FID 0.085 and 0.071.
AI.conclusion: Language-guided tokenization with a Transformer-based tokenizer and a language-drop training regime yields compact, semantically rich motion tokens, improves both tokenization/detokenization, and reduces generation complexity while maintaining or improving reconstruction quality; the approach demonstrates efficacy on standard motion-generation benchmarks and offers a pathway to efficiency via semantic token representations.

title: SiameseNorm: Breaking the Barrier to Reconciling Pre/Post-Norm
summary: Modern Transformers predominantly adopt the Pre-Norm paradigm for its optimization stability, foregoing the superior potential of the unstable Post-Norm architecture. Prior attempts to combine their strengths typically lead to a stability-performance trade-off. We attribute this phenomenon to a structural incompatibility within a single-stream design: Any application of the Post-Norm operation inevitably obstructs the clean identity gradient preserved by Pre-Norm. To fundamentally reconcile these paradigms, we propose SiameseNorm, a two-stream architecture that couples Pre-Norm-like and Post-Norm-like streams with shared parameters. This design decouples the optimization dynamics of the two streams, retaining the distinct characteristics of both Pre-Norm and Post-Norm by enabling all residual blocks to receive combined gradients inherited from both paradigms, where one stream secures stability while the other enhances expressivity. Extensive pre-training experiments on 1.3B-parameter models demonstrate that SiameseNorm exhibits exceptional optimization robustness and consistently outperforms strong baselines. Code is available at https://github.com/Qwen-Applications/SiameseNorm.

AI.tldr: SiameseNorm: a two-stream, shared-parameter Transformer that fuses Pre-Norm-like and Post-Norm-like dynamics to decouple optimization and enhance stability and expressivity, achieving robust optimization and outperforming baselines on 1.3B-parameter models.
AI.motivation: Pre-Norm is favored for stability, Post-Norm offers expressivity but often destabilizes optimization when combined. The two paradigms are structurally incompatible in a single-stream design, causing a stability-performance trade-off.
AI.method: Propose SiameseNorm, a two-stream architecture with shared parameters: one stream behaves like Pre-Norm and the other like Post-Norm. The streams decouple optimization dynamics; residual blocks receive gradients from both streams, preserving stability from one and expressivity from the other. Training involves large-scale pretraining of 1.3B-parameter models.
AI.result: Empirical pretraining experiments show SiameseNorm with enhanced optimization robustness and consistent improvements over strong baselines on 1.3B-parameter models.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling Pre-Norm and Post-Norm dynamics via a Siamese two-stream design enables the benefits of both paradigms without the typical trade-off, offering a robust and expressive Transformer architecture.

title: UrbanGraphEmbeddings: Learning and Evaluating Spatially Grounded Multimodal Embeddings for Urban Science
summary: Learning transferable multimodal embeddings for urban environments is challenging because urban understanding is inherently spatial, yet existing datasets and benchmarks lack explicit alignment between street-view images and urban structure. We introduce UGData, a spatially grounded dataset that anchors street-view images to structured spatial graphs and provides graph-aligned supervision via spatial reasoning paths and spatial context captions, exposing distance, directionality, connectivity, and neighborhood context beyond image content. Building on UGData, we propose UGE, a two-stage training strategy that progressively and stably aligns images, text, and spatial structures by combining instruction-guided contrastive learning with graph-based spatial encoding. We finally introduce UGBench, a comprehensive benchmark to evaluate how spatially grounded embeddings support diverse urban understanding tasks -- including geolocation ranking, image retrieval, urban perception, and spatial grounding. We develop UGE on multiple state-of-the-art VLM backbones, including Qwen2-VL, Qwen2.5-VL, Phi-3-Vision, and LLaVA1.6-Mistral, and train fixed-dimensional spatial embeddings with LoRA tuning. UGE built upon Qwen2.5-VL-7B backbone achieves up to 44% improvement in image retrieval and 30% in geolocation ranking on training cities, and over 30% and 22% gains respectively on held-out cities, demonstrating the effectiveness of explicit spatial grounding for spatially intensive urban tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces UGData for spatial grounding of street-view to spatial graphs, UGE for two-stage alignment of images, text, and spatial structure, and UGBench for evaluating spatially grounded urban understanding; demonstrates substantial gains across multiple backbones and tasks.
AI.motivation: Urban understanding is intrinsically spatial, but existing data lack explicit alignment between street-view imagery and urban spatial structure. A spatially grounded dataset and training framework are needed to improve transferable multimodal embeddings for cities.
AI.method: UGData anchors street-view images to structured spatial graphs and provides graph-aligned supervision via spatial reasoning paths and neighborhood-context captions. UGE uses a two-stage training: instruction-guided contrastive learning plus graph-based spatial encoding to align images, text, and spatial structures. Spatial embeddings are fixed-dimensional and LoRA-tuned. UGBench evaluates geolocation ranking, image retrieval, urban perception, and spatial grounding across backbones (Qwen2-VL, Qwen2.5-VL, Phi-3-Vision, LLaVA1.6-Mistral).
AI.result: UGE with Qwen2.5-VL-7B backone achieves up to ~44% improvement in image retrieval and ~30% in geolocation ranking on training cities; improvements exceed 30% and 22% on held-out cities for these tasks.
AI.conclusion: Explicit spatial grounding substantially improves spatially intensive urban tasks and is effective across diverse VLM backbones; UGData/UGE/UGBench provide a coherent framework and benchmark for spatially grounded urban understanding.

title: Enhancing Bandit Algorithms with LLMs for Time-varying User Preferences in Streaming Recommendations
summary: In real-world streaming recommender systems, user preferences evolve dynamically over time. Existing bandit-based methods treat time merely as a timestamp, neglecting its explicit relationship with user preferences and leading to suboptimal performance. Moreover, online learning methods often suffer from inefficient exploration-exploitation during the early online phase. To address these issues, we propose HyperBandit+, a novel contextual bandit policy that integrates a time-aware hypernetwork to adapt to time-varying user preferences and employs a large language model-assisted warm-start mechanism (LLM Start) to enhance exploration-exploitation efficiency in the early online phase. Specifically, HyperBandit+ leverages a neural network that takes time features as input and generates parameters for estimating time-varying rewards by capturing the correlation between time and user preferences. Additionally, the LLM Start mechanism employs multi-step data augmentation to simulate realistic interaction data for effective offline learning, providing warm-start parameters for the bandit policy in the early online phase. To meet real-time streaming recommendation demands, we adopt low-rank factorization to reduce hypernetwork training complexity. Theoretically, we rigorously establish a sublinear regret upper bound that accounts for both the hypernetwork and the LLM warm-start mechanism. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that HyperBandit+ consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of accumulated rewards.

AI.tldr: Time-aware hypernetwork contextual bandit with LLM-assisted warm-start improves exploration and adapts to evolving user preferences in streaming recommendations, with low-rank efficiency and sublinear regret.
AI.motivation: In real-world streaming recommender systems, user preferences evolve over time and treating time as a mere timestamp leads to suboptimal rewards. Early online exploration is inefficient in online learning settings.
AI.method: Introduce HyperBandit+: a contextual bandit policy using a time-aware hypernetwork that ingests time features to generate parameters for time-varying reward estimation, plus an LLM-assisted warm-start (multi-step data augmentation) to improve offline learning and early online exploration. To ensure scalability in real-time settings, apply low-rank factorization to reduce hypernetwork complexity.
AI.result: The approach yields sublinear regret and empirical results on real-world datasets showing HyperBandit+ outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in accumulated rewards.
AI.conclusion: HyperBandit+ integrates time-aware modeling and LLM-assisted warm-start with efficient computation, providing theoretical guarantees and practical improvements for streaming recommender systems; potential limitations may include dependence on LLM quality and hypernetwork design choices.

title: What, Whether and How? Unveiling Process Reward Models for Thinking with Images Reasoning
summary: The rapid advancement of Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) has demonstrated excellent abilities in various visual tasks. Building upon these developments, the thinking with images paradigm has emerged, enabling models to dynamically edit and re-encode visual information at each reasoning step, mirroring human visual processing. However, this paradigm introduces significant challenges as diverse errors may occur during reasoning processes. This necessitates Process Reward Models (PRMs) for distinguishing positive and negative reasoning steps, yet existing benchmarks for PRMs are predominantly text-centric and lack comprehensive assessment under this paradigm. To address these gaps, this work introduces the first comprehensive benchmark specifically designed for evaluating PRMs under the thinking with images paradigm. Our main contributions are: (1) Through extensive analysis of reasoning trajectories and guided search experiments with PRMs, we define 7 fine-grained error types and demonstrate both the necessity for specialized PRMs and the potential for improvement. (2) We construct a comprehensive benchmark comprising 1,206 manually annotated thinking with images reasoning trajectories spanning 4 categories and 16 subcategories for fine-grained evaluation of PRMs. (3) Our experimental analysis reveals that current LVLMs fall short as effective PRMs, exhibiting limited capabilities in visual reasoning process evaluation with significant performance disparities across error types, positive evaluation bias, and sensitivity to reasoning step positions. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of our benchmark and establish crucial foundations for advancing PRMs in LVLMs.

AI.tldr: A new, comprehensive benchmark for evaluating Process Reward Models (PRMs) in the thinking-with-images paradigm for LVLMs. It identifies 7 fine-grained error types, builds a dataset of 1,206 reasoning trajectories (4 categories, 16 subcategories), and shows current LVLMs underperform as PRMs with biases and step-position sensitivity, establishing foundations for future PRM improvements.
AI.motivation: Existing PRM benchmarks are text-centric and do not adequately assess PRMs under the thinking-with-images paradigm where models iteratively edit/re-encode visual information. There is a need to quantify and categorize reasoning errors, and to evaluate PRMs in this setting to guide model improvements.
AI.method: 1) Analyze reasoning trajectories and perform guided search experiments with PRMs to identify error patterns. 2) Define 7 fine-grained error types. 3) Construct a benchmark with 1,206 manually annotated thinking-with-images reasoning trajectories across 4 categories and 16 subcategories for granular PRM evaluation. 4) Conduct experimental analysis to compare LVLMs’ ability as PRMs, examining performance across error types, bias in positive evaluation, and sensitivity to the position of reasoning steps.
AI.result: The study demonstrates the necessity for specialized PRMs and shows their potential for improvement. The benchmark is comprehensive and reveals that current LVLMs exhibit limited capability in evaluating visual reasoning processes, with notable disparities across error types, a positive-evaluation bias, and sensitivity to when steps occur in the sequence.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark effectively evaluates PRMs in the thinking-with-images paradigm and provides a foundational resource to advance PRMs in LVLMs, enabling targeted improvements across error types and reasoning stages.

title: Multimodal normative modeling in Alzheimers Disease with introspective variational autoencoders
comment: Conference on Health, Inference, and Learning (CHIL)
summary: Normative modeling learns a healthy reference distribution and quantifies subject-specific deviations to capture heterogeneous disease effects. In Alzheimers disease (AD), multimodal neuroimaging offers complementary signals but VAE-based normative models often (i) fit the healthy reference distribution imperfectly, inflating false positives, and (ii) use posterior aggregation (e.g., PoE/MoE) that can yield weak multimodal fusion in the shared latent space. We propose mmSIVAE, a multimodal soft-introspective variational autoencoder combined with Mixture-of-Product-of-Experts (MOPOE) aggregation to improve reference fidelity and multimodal integration. We compute deviation scores in latent space and feature space as distances from the learned healthy distributions, and map statistically significant latent deviations to regional abnormalities for interpretability. On ADNI MRI regional volumes and amyloid PET SUVR, mmSIVAE improves reconstruction on held-out controls and produces more discriminative deviation scores for outlier detection than VAE baselines, with higher likelihood ratios and clearer separation between control and AD-spectrum cohorts. Deviation maps highlight region-level patterns aligned with established AD-related changes. More broadly, our results highlight the importance of training objectives that prioritize reference-distribution fidelity and robust multimodal posterior aggregation for normative modeling, with implications for deviation-based analysis across multimodal clinical data.

AI.tldr: mmSIVAE, a multimodal soft-introspective VAE with MOPOE fusion, improves normative modeling for Alzheimer's disease by better estimating the healthy reference distribution and integrating multimodal signals, yielding stronger deviation scores and interpretable regional maps.
AI.motivation: VAE-based normative models often misfit the healthy reference and suffer from weak multimodal fusion when aggregating posteriors (PoE/MoE), leading to false positives and less informative deviation analyses.
AI.method: Propose mmSIVAE: a multimodal soft-introspective variational autoencoder combined with Mixture-of-Product-of-Experts (MOPOE) aggregation. Train on multimodal neuroimaging (MRI regional volumes and amyloid PET SUVR) to model healthy distributions, compute latent- and feature-space deviation scores from learned healthy distributions, and map significant latent deviations to regional abnormalities. Evaluate on ADNI data against VAE baselines, focusing on reconstruction, deviation discriminability, and interpretability of maps.
AI.result: mmSIVAE improves reconstruction on held-out controls and yields more discriminative deviation scores for outlier detection than VAE baselines, with higher likelihood ratios and clearer separation between control and AD-spectrum cohorts. Deviation maps show region-level patterns aligned with established AD-related changes.
AI.conclusion: Prioritize reference-distribution fidelity and robust multimodal posterior aggregation in normative modeling; mmSIVAE demonstrates tangible benefits for deviation-based analyses across multimodal clinical data and supports interpretable, region-level inferences of disease effects.

title: E-VAds: An E-commerce Short Videos Understanding Benchmark for MLLMs
summary: E-commerce short videos represent a high-revenue segment of the online video industry characterized by a goal-driven format and dense multi-modal signals. Current models often struggle with these videos because existing benchmarks focus primarily on general-purpose tasks and neglect the reasoning of commercial intent. In this work, we first propose a multi-modal information density assessment framework to quantify the complexity of this domain. Our evaluation reveals that e-commerce content exhibits substantially higher density across visual, audio, and textual modalities compared to mainstream datasets, establishing a more challenging frontier for video understanding. To address this gap, we introduce E-commerce Video Ads Benchmark (E-VAds), which is the first benchmark specifically designed for e-commerce short video understanding. We curated 3,961 high-quality videos from Taobao covering a wide range of product categories and used a multi-agent system to generate 19,785 open-ended Q&A pairs. These questions are organized into two primary dimensions, namely Perception and Cognition and Reasoning, which consist of five distinct tasks. Finally, we develop E-VAds-R1, an RL-based reasoning model featuring a multi-grained reward design called MG-GRPO. This strategy provides smooth guidance for early exploration while creating a non-linear incentive for expert-level precision. Experimental results demonstrate that E-VAds-R1 achieves a 109.2% performance gain in commercial intent reasoning with only a few hundred training samples.

AI.tldr: Proposes a framework to quantify information density in e-commerce short videos and introduces the E-VAds benchmark with 3,961 Taobao videos and 19,785 Q&A pairs across Perception, Cognition, and Reasoning tasks. Presents MG-GRPO, an RL-based reasoning model, achieving a 109.2% gain in commercial intent reasoning with a few hundred training samples. First benchmark tailored for e-commerce short-video understanding.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks emphasize general-purpose video tasks and overlook commercial intent reasoning. E-commerce content exhibits higher multi-modal information density across visual, audio, and text modalities, making understanding more challenging and business-relevant. A dedicated benchmark and baselines are needed to evaluate and advance commercial-intent video understanding.
AI.method: 1) Multi-modal information density assessment framework to quantify domain complexity. 2) Data collection of 3,961 high-quality Taobao videos across categories; generation of 19,785 open-ended Q&A pairs using a multi-agent system. 3) Organization of questions into two dimensions (Perception and Cognition, and Reasoning) with five tasks total. 4) E-VAds-R1: an RL-based reasoning model with a multi-grained reward design (MG-GRPO) that guides exploration and incentivizes precise, expert-level responses.
AI.result: Density analysis shows e-commerce videos have substantially higher density across visual, audio, and textual modalities than mainstream datasets. E-VAds is the first benchmark specifically for e-commerce short-video understanding. E-VAds-R1 achieves a 109.2% performance gain in commercial intent reasoning with only a few hundred training samples.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a new frontier for e-commerce short-video understanding by providing a dedicated benchmark and an RL-based reasoning model. The E-VAds dataset and MG-GRPO approach offer a practical and scalable path to improve commercial-intent reasoning with limited labeled data.

title: Spectral Guardrails for Agents in the Wild: Detecting Tool Use Hallucinations via Attention Topology
summary: Deploying autonomous agents in the wild requires reliable safeguards against tool use failures. We propose a training free guardrail based on spectral analysis of attention topology that complements supervised approaches. On Llama 3.1 8B, our method achieves 97.7\% recall with multi-feature detection and 86.1\% recall with 81.0\% precision for balanced deployment, without requiring any labeled training data. Most remarkably, we discover that single layer spectral features act as near-perfect hallucination detectors: Llama L26 Smoothness achieves 98.2\% recall (213/217 hallucinations caught) with a single threshold, and Mistral L3 Entropy achieves 94.7\% recall. This suggests hallucination is not merely a wrong token but a thermodynamic state change: the model's attention becomes noise when it errs. Through controlled cross-model evaluation on matched domains ($N=1000$, $T=0.3$, same General domain, hallucination rates 20--22\%), we reveal the ``Loud Liar'' phenomenon: Llama 3.1 8B's failures are spectrally catastrophic and dramatically easier to detect, while Mistral 7B achieves the best discrimination (AUC 0.900). These findings establish spectral analysis as a principled, efficient framework for agent safety.

AI.tldr: A training-free guardrail for autonomous agents using spectral analysis of attention topology to detect tool-use failures and hallucinations. It achieves high recall/precision without labeled data, with single-layer spectral features (L26 Smoothness, L3 Entropy) acting as strong detectors, and reveals cross-model differences (Loud Liar) while promoting spectral analysis as a principled safety framework.
AI.motivation: Safeguard autonomous agents deployed in the wild without reliance on labeled training data, addressing hallucinations and tool-use failures by exploiting intrinsic model dynamics.
AI.method: Spectral analysis of attention topology to derive features; multi-feature detection for balanced deployment; evaluation on Llama 3.1 8B and Mistral 7B; single-layer spectral detectors (L26 Smoothness, L3 Entropy); cross-model matched-domain evaluation (N=1000, T=0.3, hallucination rates ~20-22%); threshold-based detection; report of AUC and recall/precision metrics.
AI.result: High performance without training data: 97.7% recall with multi-feature detection and 81.0% precision; 86.1% recall with 81.0% precision for balanced deployment; single-layer detectors achieve 98.2% recall (213/217) for Llama and 94.7% recall for Mistral; cross-model AUC up to 0.900; identification of 'Loud Liar' phenomenon highlighting model-specific spectral detectability differences.
AI.conclusion: Spectral analysis provides a principled, efficient, training-free framework for agent safety that supplements supervised safeguards and reveals insights into model thermodynamics of errors; single-layer spectral features offer strong, robust hallucinatory detection and cross-model discrimination.

title: Geometric Image Editing via Effects-Sensitive In-Context Inpainting with Diffusion Transformers
summary: Recent advances in diffusion models have significantly improved image editing. However, challenges persist in handling geometric transformations, such as translation, rotation, and scaling, particularly in complex scenes. Existing approaches suffer from two main limitations: (1) difficulty in achieving accurate geometric editing of object translation, rotation, and scaling; (2) inadequate modeling of intricate lighting and shadow effects, leading to unrealistic results. To address these issues, we propose GeoEdit, a framework that leverages in-context generation through a diffusion transformer module, which integrates geometric transformations for precise object edits. Moreover, we introduce Effects-Sensitive Attention, which enhances the modeling of intricate lighting and shadow effects for improved realism. To further support training, we construct RS-Objects, a large-scale geometric editing dataset containing over 120,000 high-quality image pairs, enabling the model to learn precise geometric editing while generating realistic lighting and shadows. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks demonstrate that GeoEdit consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of visual quality, geometric accuracy, and realism.

AI.tldr: GeoEdit uses a diffusion-transformer with in-context editing and an effects-sensitive attention mechanism to achieve precise geometric edits (translation/rotation/scaling) and realistic lighting/shadows, backed by a large RS-Objects dataset; it outperforms state-of-the-art methods on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based image editing struggles to correctly handle geometric transformations and complex lighting in scenes. There is a need for a framework that jointly addresses accurate geometry and realistic illumination, supported by a scalable dataset for training.
AI.method: Propose GeoEdit: a diffusion-transformer module enabling in-context geometric edits; introduce Effects-Sensitive Attention to better model lighting and shadows; build RS-Objects, a >120k image-pair dataset for geometric editing; validate on public benchmarks.
AI.result: GeoEdit achieves consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods in visual quality, geometric accuracy, and realism across standard benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: GeoEdit advances geometric-aware diffusion editing and lighting realism, supported by a large dataset, and establishes a new direction for high-fidelity editing in complex scenes.

title: Probability Hacking and the Design of Trustworthy ML for Signal Processing in C-UAS: A Scenario Based Method
comment: 6 pages, Pre-publication. Copyright 2026 IEEE. Peer Reviewed. Accepted at ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP), scheduled for 3-8 May 2026 in Barcelona, Spain
summary: In order to counter the various threats manifested by Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS) adequately, specialized Counter Unmanned Aircraft Systems (C-UAS) are required. Enhancing C-UAS with Emerging and Disruptive Technologies (EDTs) such as Artificial Intelligence (AI) can lead to more effective countermeasures. In this paper a scenario-based method is applied to C-UAS augmented with Machine Learning (ML), a subset of AI, that can enhance signal processing capabilities. Via the scenarios-based method we frame in this paper probability hacking as a challenge and identify requirements which can be implemented in existing Rule of Law mechanisms to prevent probability hacking. These requirements strengthen the trustworthiness of the C-UAS, which feed into justified trust - a key to successful Human-Autonomy Teaming, in civil and military contexts. Index Terms: C-UAS, Scenario-based method, Emerging and Disruptive Technologies, Probability hacking, Trustworthiness.

AI.tldr: A scenario-based analysis of ML-enhanced C-UAS addressing probability hacking, identifying governance requirements to strengthen trust and justify human-autonomy teaming in civil and military use.
AI.motivation: Counter-UAS require advanced technologies; AI/ML can enhance sensing, processing, and decision-making. Ensuring trustworthy operation within existing legal frameworks is essential for civil and military deployment.
AI.method: Apply a scenario-based method to C-UAS augmented with ML; frame probability hacking as a challenge; derive actionable requirements that can be implemented in current Rule of Law mechanisms to safeguard trustworthiness and support justified trust in human–autonomy teaming.
AI.result: Identified governance and technical requirements to prevent probability hacking; strengthened trustworthiness of C-UAS and reinforced its role in enabling justified trust within human–autonomy teams.
AI.conclusion: ML-enabled C-UAS analyzed through scenarios can improve effectiveness while aligning with legal/ethical frameworks; provides governance guidance to prevent probability hacking and promote safe civil/military adoption.

title: D$^2$-VR: Degradation-Robust and Distilled Video Restoration with Synergistic Optimization Strategy
summary: The integration of diffusion priors with temporal alignment has emerged as a transformative paradigm for video restoration, delivering fantastic perceptual quality, yet the practical deployment of such frameworks is severely constrained by prohibitive inference latency and temporal instability when confronted with complex real-world degradations. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{D$^2$-VR}, a single-image diffusion-based video-restoration framework with low-step inference. To obtain precise temporal guidance under severe degradation, we first design a Degradation-Robust Flow Alignment (DRFA) module that leverages confidence-aware attention to filter unreliable motion cues. We then incorporate an adversarial distillation paradigm to compress the diffusion sampling trajectory into a rapid few-step regime. Finally, a synergistic optimization strategy is devised to harmonize perceptual quality with rigorous temporal consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that D$^2$-VR achieves state-of-the-art performance while accelerating the sampling process by \textbf{12$\times$}

AI.tldr: D^2-VR is a diffusion-based video restoration framework that achieves high perceptual quality with low inference latency by (1) robustly aligning temporal information using a degradation-robust flow alignment (DRFA) and (2) compressing the diffusion process into a few-step regime via adversarial distillation, followed by a optimization strategy that balances perceptual quality and temporal consistency, achieving state-of-the-art results with 12x faster sampling.
AI.motivation: Video restoration with diffusion priors offers high perceptual quality but suffers from prohibitive latency and temporal instability under real-world degradations. A practical solution requires robust temporal guidance and efficient sampling that preserves consistency across frames.
AI.method: Introduce D^2-VR, a single-image diffusion-based video restoration framework with low-step inference. Key components include (1) DRFA module that uses confidence-aware attention to filter unreliable motion cues during temporal alignment, (2) adversarial distillation to condense the diffusion sampling trajectory into a rapid few-step regime, and (3) a synergistic optimization strategy to harmonize perceptual quality with temporal consistency.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that D^2-VR achieves state-of-the-art performance while accelerating the sampling process by 12x compared to conventional diffusion-based video restoration methods.
AI.conclusion: D^2-VR demonstrates that diffusion priors for video restoration can be made practically deployable by combining degradation-robust temporal guidance with aggressive sampling compression and careful optimization, delivering superior quality at a fraction of the latency.

title: Online Domain-aware LLM Decoding for Continual Domain Evolution
summary: LLMs are typically fine-tuned offline on domain-specific data, assuming a static domain. In practice, domain knowledge evolves continuously through new regulations, products, services, and interaction patterns. Retraining or fine-tuning LLMs for every new instance is computationally infeasible. Additionally, real-world environments also exhibit temporal dynamics with shifting data distributions. Disregarding this phenomenon, commonly referred to as concept drift, can significantly diminish a model's predictive accuracy. This mismatch between evolving domains and static adaptation pipelines highlights the need for efficient, real-time adaptation without costly retraining. In response, we introduce Online Domain-aware Decoding framework (ODD). ODD performs probability-level fusion between a base LLM and a prefix-tree prior, guided by adaptive confidence modulation using disagreement and continuity signals. Empirical evaluation under diverse drift scenarios demonstrates that ODD consistently surpasses LLM-Greedy and LLM-Temp Scaled across all syntactic and semantic NLG metrics. It yields an absolute ROUGE-L gain of 0.065 and a 13.6% relative improvement in Cosine Similarity over the best baseline. These results demonstrate ODD 's robustness to evolving lexical and contextual patterns, making it suitable for dynamic LLM applications.

AI.tldr: Online Domain-aware Decoding (ODD) for real-time adaptation of LLM outputs to evolving domains via probability-level fusion with a prefix-tree prior and adaptive confidence signals; outperforms baselines under concept drift.
AI.motivation: Domain knowledge evolves continuously; retraining/finetuning is costly; require efficient, real-time adaptation to distribution shifts.
AI.method: Fusion of base LLM probabilities with a prefix-tree prior; adaptive confidence modulation using disagreement and continuity signals; evaluate under drift scenarios against LLM-Greedy and LLM-Temp Scaled.
AI.result: ODD yields improvements across syntactic and semantic NLG metrics; absolute ROUGE-L gain 0.065; 13.6% relative improvement in Cosine Similarity over best baseline.
AI.conclusion: ODD enables robust, efficient non-retraining adaptation to evolving lexical/contextual patterns, suitable for dynamic LLM deployments.

title: RealSynCol: a high-fidelity synthetic colon dataset for 3D reconstruction applications
summary: Deep learning has the potential to improve colonoscopy by enabling 3D reconstruction of the colon, providing a comprehensive view of mucosal surfaces and lesions, and facilitating the identification of unexplored areas. However, the development of robust methods is limited by the scarcity of large-scale ground truth data. We propose RealSynCol, a highly realistic synthetic dataset designed to replicate the endoscopic environment. Colon geometries extracted from 10 CT scans were imported into a virtual environment that closely mimics intraoperative conditions and rendered with realistic vascular textures. The resulting dataset comprises 28\,130 frames, paired with ground truth depth maps, optical flow, 3D meshes, and camera trajectories. A benchmark study was conducted to evaluate the available synthetic colon datasets for the tasks of depth and pose estimation. Results demonstrate that the high realism and variability of RealSynCol significantly enhance generalization performance on clinical images, proving it to be a powerful tool for developing deep learning algorithms to support endoscopic diagnosis.

AI.tldr: RealSynCol is a highly realistic synthetic colonoscopy dataset (28,130 frames) derived from 10 CT-based colon geometries, offering ground-truth depth maps, optical flow, 3D meshes, and camera trajectories; benchmark shows improved generalization to clinical images for depth and pose estimation.
AI.motivation: Addresses scarcity of large-scale ground-truth data in endoscopy, enabling robust DL for 3D reconstruction, lesion identification, and exploration of unexplored mucosal regions.
AI.method: Extract colon geometries from 10 CT scans; import into a virtual intraoperative-like environment; render with realistic vascular textures; produce frames with ground-truth depth, optical flow, 3D meshes, and camera trajectories; perform a benchmark comparing synthetic datasets for depth and pose estimation.
AI.result: High realism and variability in RealSynCol enhance generalization performance on clinical images, demonstrating its effectiveness for developing DL algorithms to support endoscopic diagnosis.
AI.conclusion: RealSynCol is a powerful tool to advance endoscopic DL for accurate 3D reconstruction and lesion assessment; encourages broader adoption and future expansion to cover more variations, modalities, and dynamic tissue behaviors.

title: Mutual information and task-relevant latent dimensionality
summary: Estimating the dimensionality of the latent representation needed for prediction -- the task-relevant dimension -- is a difficult, largely unsolved problem with broad scientific applications. We cast it as an Information Bottleneck question: what embedding bottleneck dimension is sufficient to compress predictor and predicted views while preserving their mutual information (MI). This repurposes neural MI estimators for dimensionality estimation.
