We show that standard neural estimators with separable/bilinear critics systematically inflate the inferred dimension, and we address this by introducing a hybrid critic that retains an explicit dimensional bottleneck while allowing flexible nonlinear cross-view interactions, thereby preserving the latent geometry. We further propose a one-shot protocol that reads off the effective dimension from a single over-parameterized hybrid model, without sweeping over bottleneck sizes. We validate the approach on synthetic problems with known task-relevant dimension. We extend the approach to intrinsic dimensionality by constructing paired views of a single dataset, enabling comparison with classical geometric dimension estimators. In noisy regimes where those estimators degrade, our approach remains reliable. Finally, we demonstrate the utility of the method on multiple physics datasets.

AI.tldr: An Information Bottleneck–based framework estimates the task-relevant latent dimension, addressing inflated dimensions from standard MI estimators by using a hybrid cross-view critic with an explicit bottleneck, plus a one-shot protocol to read the effective dimension; demonstrated on synthetic tasks, intrinsic dimensionality via paired views, and physics datasets with robustness to noise.
AI.motivation: Determining the minimal latent dimension necessary for accurate prediction is a central, difficult problem with broad scientific relevance. Standard neural MI estimators (with separable/bilinear critics) tend to inflate the inferred dimension and misrepresent latent geometry, motivating a more faithful, robust approach.
AI.method: Formulate dimension estimation as an Information Bottleneck task. diagnose biases of separable/bilinear MI estimators. introduce a hybrid critic that preserves an explicit bottleneck while enabling flexible nonlinear cross-view interactions to retain latent geometry. develop a one-shot protocol to infer the effective dimension from a single over-parameterized model. extend to intrinsic dimensionality using paired views of a dataset. compare with classical geometric estimators and validate on synthetic problems, noisy regimes, and physics datasets.
AI.result: The hybrid critic preserves latent geometry and reduces dimension inflation. The one-shot protocol successfully extracts the effective dimension without exhaustive bottleneck sweeps. The method remains reliable in noisy conditions and aligns with classical estimators on intrinsic dimensionality when applicable. Demonstrated utility across synthetic tasks and multiple physics datasets.
AI.conclusion: An Information Bottleneck–driven, hybrid-critic approach provides a reliable, scalable framework for estimating task-relevant and intrinsic dimensionality, enabling one-shot dimension reading, robustness to noise, and broad applicability to scientific data.

title: Understanding and Optimizing Attention-Based Sparse Matching for Diverse Local Features
summary: We revisit the problem of training attention-based sparse image matching models for various local features. We first identify one critical design choice that has been previously overlooked, which significantly impacts the performance of the LightGlue model. We then investigate the role of detectors and descriptors within the transformer-based matching framework, finding that detectors, rather than descriptors, are often the primary cause for performance difference. Finally, we propose a novel approach to fine-tune existing image matching models using keypoints from a diverse set of detectors, resulting in a universal, detector-agnostic model. When deployed as a zero-shot matcher for novel detectors, the resulting model achieves or exceeds the accuracy of models specifically trained for those features. Our findings offer valuable insights for the deployment of transformer-based matching models and the future design of local features.

AI.tldr: The paper reexamines attention-based sparse image matching, showing detectors (not descriptors) largely drive performance differences in transformer-based models, and introduces a detector-agnostic fine-tuning approach using diverse keypoints to yield a universal zero-shot matcher.
AI.motivation: Determine what components most affect performance in transformer-based local feature matching and achieve robustness across detectors by creating a universal model.
AI.method: 1) Critically re-evaluate the LightGlue design. 2) Empirically compare the roles of detectors vs descriptors within the transformer-based framework. 3) Develop a detector-agnostic fine-tuning strategy using keypoints from a diverse set of detectors to train a universal matcher. 4) Test zero-shot deployment on novel detectors.
AI.result: Detectors are the primary source of performance variation, surpassing descriptors. The detector-agnostic fine-tuning yields a universal model that achieves or exceeds the accuracy of models trained for specific detectors when used in zero-shot settings.
AI.conclusion: Detector choice is pivotal for transformer-based local-feature matching. A detector-agnostic fine-tuning approach enables robust, zero-shot performance across detectors, informing future design and deployment of local features.

title: Online Bayesian Imbalanced Learning with Bregman-Calibrated Deep Networks
summary: Class imbalance remains a fundamental challenge in machine learning, where standard classifiers exhibit severe performance degradation in minority classes. Although existing approaches address imbalance through resampling or cost-sensitive learning during training, they require retraining or access to labeled target data when class distributions shift at deployment time, a common occurrence in real-world applications such as fraud detection, medical diagnosis, and anomaly detection. We present \textit{Online Bayesian Imbalanced Learning} (OBIL), a principled framework that decouples likelihood-ratio estimation from class-prior assumptions, enabling real-time adaptation to distribution shifts without model retraining. Our approach builds on the established connection between Bregman divergences and proper scoring rules to show that deep networks trained with such losses produce posterior probability estimates from which prior-invariant likelihood ratios can be extracted. We prove that these likelihood-ratio estimates remain valid under arbitrary changes in class priors and cost structures, requiring only a threshold adjustment for optimal Bayes decisions. We derive finite-sample regret bounds demonstrating that OBIL achieves $O(\sqrt{T \log T})$ regret against an oracle with perfect prior knowledge. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets and medical diagnosis benchmarks under simulated deployment shifts demonstrate that OBIL maintains robust performance under severe distribution shifts, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in F1 Score when test distributions deviate significantly from the training conditions.

AI.tldr: Online Bayesian Imbalanced Learning (OBIL) decouples likelihood-ratio estimation from class priors, enabling real-time adaptation to deployment shifts without retraining; provides regret guarantees and robust F1 under distribution shift.
AI.motivation: Class-imbalanced data and deployment-time distribution shifts degrade performance; existing resampling/cost-sensitive methods require retraining or target labels.
AI.method: Leverages Bregman divergences and proper scoring rules to obtain prior-invariant likelihood ratios from posterior estimates; thresholding yields Bayes-optimal decisions under changing priors; establishes O(sqrt(T log T)) regret against an oracle.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees; extensive experiments on benchmarks and medical datasets show OBIL robust to shifts and superior F1 under significant test distribution deviation.
AI.conclusion: OBIL offers principled, online adaptation to prior shifts without retraining, with strong theoretical guarantees and empirical robustness; promising for fraud, medical, anomaly detection.

title: Demo-ICL: In-Context Learning for Procedural Video Knowledge Acquisition
summary: Despite the growing video understanding capabilities of recent Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), existing video benchmarks primarily assess understanding based on models' static, internal knowledge, rather than their ability to learn and adapt from dynamic, novel contexts from few examples. To bridge this gap, we present Demo-driven Video In-Context Learning, a novel task focused on learning from in-context demonstrations to answer questions about the target videos. Alongside this, we propose Demo-ICL-Bench, a challenging benchmark designed to evaluate demo-driven video in-context learning capabilities. Demo-ICL-Bench is constructed from 1200 instructional YouTube videos with associated questions, from which two types of demonstrations are derived: (i) summarizing video subtitles for text demonstration; and (ii) corresponding instructional videos as video demonstrations. To effectively tackle this new challenge, we develop Demo-ICL, an MLLM with a two-stage training strategy: video-supervised fine-tuning and information-assisted direct preference optimization, jointly enhancing the model's ability to learn from in-context examples. Extensive experiments with state-of-the-art MLLMs confirm the difficulty of Demo-ICL-Bench, demonstrate the effectiveness of Demo-ICL, and thereby unveil future research directions.

AI.tldr: Proposes Demo-ICL for video in-context learning, introducing a new benchmark Demo-ICL-Bench and a two-stage training method Demo-ICL to enable learning from demonstrations (text subtitles or video demonstrations) in YouTube videos.
AI.motivation: Current MLLMs' video benchmarks emphasize static, internal knowledge rather than adaptability to dynamic, few-shot contexts from demonstrations. There is a need to evaluate and improve in-context learning for video understanding.
AI.method: Construct Demo-ICL-Bench from 1200 instructional YouTube videos and associated questions. Derive two demonstration types: (i) text demonstrations by summarizing video subtitles; (ii) video demonstrations as instructional videos. Develop Demo-ICL with a two-stage training: (a) video-supervised fine-tuning and (b) information-assisted direct preference optimization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show Demo-ICL-Bench is challenging for state-of-the-art MLLMs. The proposed Demo-ICL improves performance on the benchmark, and reveals future research directions in demo-driven video in-context learning.
AI.conclusion: Demo-ICL establishes a new paradigm for evaluating and enhancing video in-context learning in MLLMs, providing a benchmark and a training strategy that enable learning from demonstrations and highlight remaining challenges.

title: Variance-Gated Ensembles: An Epistemic-Aware Framework for Uncertainty Estimation
summary: Machine learning applications require fast and reliable per-sample uncertainty estimation. A common approach is to use predictive distributions from Bayesian or approximation methods and additively decompose uncertainty into aleatoric (i.e., data-related) and epistemic (i.e., model-related) components. However, additive decomposition has recently been questioned, with evidence that it breaks down when using finite-ensemble sampling and/or mismatched predictive distributions. This paper introduces Variance-Gated Ensembles (VGE), an intuitive, differentiable framework that injects epistemic sensitivity via a signal-to-noise gate computed from ensemble statistics. VGE provides: (i) a Variance-Gated Margin Uncertainty (VGMU) score that couples decision margins with ensemble predictive variance; and (ii) a Variance-Gated Normalization (VGN) layer that generalizes the variance-gated uncertainty mechanism to training via per-class, learnable normalization of ensemble member probabilities. We derive closed-form vector-Jacobian products enabling end-to-end training through ensemble sample mean and variance. VGE matches or exceeds state-of-the-art information-theoretic baselines while remaining computationally efficient. As a result, VGE provides a practical and scalable approach to epistemic-aware uncertainty estimation in ensemble models. An open-source implementation is available at: https://github.com/nextdevai/vge.

AI.tldr: Variance-Gated Ensembles (VGE) introduce a differentiable, ensemble-based uncertainty framework that injects epistemic sensitivity via a signal-to-noise gate, yielding VGMU and VGN with end-to-end training and competitive performance.
AI.motivation: Additive decomposition of uncertainty into aleatoric/epistemic can fail with finite ensembles and distribution mismatch; a scalable, differentiable method for epistemic-aware uncertainty in ensembles is needed.
AI.method: Compute a signal-to-noise gate from ensemble mean and variance to modulate predictions; define VGMU score coupling decision margins with ensemble predictive variance; introduce VGN layer for per-class learnable normalization of ensemble probabilities; derive closed-form vector-Jacobian products enabling backprop through ensemble mean/variance; provide end-to-end training and open-source implementation.
AI.result: VGE matches or exceeds state-of-the-art information-theoretic baselines and is computationally efficient.
AI.conclusion: VGE provides a practical, scalable approach to epistemic-aware uncertainty estimation in ensemble models; open-source implementation available.

title: Vista: Scene-Aware Optimization for Streaming Video Question Answering under Post-Hoc Queries
summary: Streaming video question answering (Streaming Video QA) poses distinct challenges for multimodal large language models (MLLMs), as video frames arrive sequentially and user queries can be issued at arbitrary time points. Existing solutions relying on fixed-size memory or naive compression often suffer from context loss or memory overflow, limiting their effectiveness in long-form, real-time scenarios. We present Vista, a novel framework for scene-aware streaming video QA that enables efficient and scalable reasoning over continuous video streams. The innovation of Vista can be summarized in three aspects: (1) scene-aware segmentation, where Vista dynamically clusters incoming frames into temporally and visually coherent scene units; (2) scene-aware compression, where each scene is compressed into a compact token representation and stored in GPU memory for efficient index-based retrieval, while full-resolution frames are offloaded to CPU memory; and (3) scene-aware recall, where relevant scenes are selectively recalled and reintegrated into the model input upon receiving a query, enabling both efficiency and completeness. Vista is model-agnostic and integrates seamlessly with a variety of vision-language backbones, enabling long-context reasoning without compromising latency or memory efficiency. Extensive experiments on StreamingBench demonstrate that Vista achieves state-of-the-art performance, establishing a strong baseline for real-world streaming video understanding.

AI.tldr: Vista introduces scene-aware streaming video QA via dynamic scene segmentation, scene-aware compression into compact GPU tokens with CPU offload for high-res frames, and selective scene recall for query-driven reasoning; achieves state-of-the-art on StreamingBench while preserving latency and memory efficiency, and is model-agnostic.
AI.motivation: In streaming video QA, sequential frame arrival and arbitrary query times create challenges for memory management and long-context reasoning. Fixed-size memory and naive compression cause context loss or memory overflow, limiting real-time performance on long-form events.
AI.method: Three components: (1) scene-aware segmentation that dynamically clusters incoming frames into temporally and visually coherent scenes; (2) scene-aware compression that encodes each scene into a compact token representation stored in GPU memory, while full-resolution frames are offloaded to CPU; (3) scene-aware recall that selectively retrieves relevant scenes and reintegrates them into the model input upon a query, enabling efficient yet complete reasoning. The framework is model-agnostic and compatible with various vision-language backbones, using index-based retrieval.
AI.result: On StreamingBench, Vista achieves state-of-the-art results, demonstrating strong performance in long-form streaming video QA while maintaining favorable latency and memory usage due to its selective recall and compression strategy.
AI.conclusion: Vista offers a scalable, efficient, and scene-aware approach to streaming video QA that preserves context over long horizons, is compatible with multiple backbones, and establishes a new baseline for real-world streaming video understanding.

title: Reliable and Responsible Foundation Models: A Comprehensive Survey
summary: Foundation models, including Large Language Models (LLMs), Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), Image Generative Models (i.e, Text-to-Image Models and Image-Editing Models), and Video Generative Models, have become essential tools with broad applications across various domains such as law, medicine, education, finance, science, and beyond. As these models see increasing real-world deployment, ensuring their reliability and responsibility has become critical for academia, industry, and government. This survey addresses the reliable and responsible development of foundation models. We explore critical issues, including bias and fairness, security and privacy, uncertainty, explainability, and distribution shift. Our research also covers model limitations, such as hallucinations, as well as methods like alignment and Artificial Intelligence-Generated Content (AIGC) detection. For each area, we review the current state of the field and outline concrete future research directions. Additionally, we discuss the intersections between these areas, highlighting their connections and shared challenges. We hope our survey fosters the development of foundation models that are not only powerful but also ethical, trustworthy, reliable, and socially responsible.

AI.tldr: Survey of reliable and responsible development of foundation models (LLMs, MLLMs, image/video generative models), covering bias/fairness, security/privacy, uncertainty, explainability, and distribution shift, as well as hallucinations, alignment, and AIGC detection; reviews current state and outlines concrete future research directions and cross-cutting connections.
AI.motivation: Foundation models are increasingly deployed in critical domains, making reliability, safety, and social responsibility essential. The survey identifies core issues, gaps, and the need for integrated, forward-looking research.
AI.method: Comprehensive literature review and synthesis across core topics, with a taxonomy of issues, discussion of intersections, and recommendations for future work.
AI.result: Summarizes state-of-the-art for each issue, identifies gaps and challenges, proposes concrete future directions, and highlights interconnections among issues to guide responsible development.
AI.conclusion: Aims to advance powerful foundation models that are ethical, trustworthy, reliable, and socially responsible through structured research and alignment with societal values.

title: TriC-Motion: Tri-Domain Causal Modeling Grounded Text-to-Motion Generation
summary: Text-to-motion generation, a rapidly evolving field in computer vision, aims to produce realistic and text-aligned motion sequences. Current methods primarily focus on spatial-temporal modeling or independent frequency domain analysis, lacking a unified framework for joint optimization across spatial, temporal, and frequency domains. This limitation hinders the model's ability to leverage information from all domains simultaneously, leading to suboptimal generation quality. Additionally, in motion generation frameworks, motion-irrelevant cues caused by noise are often entangled with features that contribute positively to generation, thereby leading to motion distortion. To address these issues, we propose Tri-Domain Causal Text-to-Motion Generation (TriC-Motion), a novel diffusion-based framework integrating spatial-temporal-frequency-domain modeling with causal intervention. TriC-Motion includes three core modeling modules for domain-specific modeling, namely Temporal Motion Encoding, Spatial Topology Modeling, and Hybrid Frequency Analysis. After comprehensive modeling, a Score-guided Tri-domain Fusion module integrates valuable information from the triple domains, simultaneously ensuring temporal consistency, spatial topology, motion trends, and dynamics. Moreover, the Causality-based Counterfactual Motion Disentangler is meticulously designed to expose motion-irrelevant cues to eliminate noise, disentangling the real modeling contributions of each domain for superior generation. Extensive experimental results validate that TriC-Motion achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods, attaining an outstanding R@1 of 0.612 on the HumanML3D dataset. These results demonstrate its capability to generate high-fidelity, coherent, diverse, and text-aligned motion sequences. Code is available at: https://caoyiyang1105.github.io/TriC-Motion/.

AI.tldr: TriC-Motion proposes a diffusion-based framework that jointly models spatial, temporal, and frequency domains with causal intervention to improve text-to-motion generation; attains R@1 0.612 on HumanML3D, outperforming SOTA, with code available.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-motion methods optimize across separate domains (spatial-temporal or frequency analyses) and often suffer from motion-irrelevant noise leaking into generated motions. A unified, multi-domain, causally informed framework could leverage information across domains and suppress noisy cues to improve fidelity and alignment.
AI.method: Three domain-specific modules: Temporal Motion Encoding (temporal dynamics), Spatial Topology Modeling (spatial structure), and Hybrid Frequency Analysis (frequency content). A Score-guided Tri-domain Fusion module integrates information across domains to ensure temporal consistency, spatial topology, and motion dynamics. A Causality-based Counterfactual Motion Disentangler reveals motion-irrelevant cues to disentangle each domain's true contribution, guiding diffusion-based generation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows state-of-the-art performance with R@1 = 0.612 on the HumanML3D dataset, indicating high-fidelity, coherent, diverse, and text-aligned motion generation. Code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: TriC-Motion demonstrates that joint tri-domain modeling combined with causal disentangling can surpass existing approaches in text-to-motion generation, validating the benefit of cross-domain information fusion and causal interventions. The work invites further analysis of ablations, efficiency, and generalization to other datasets.

title: A second order regret bound for NormalHedge
summary: We consider the problem of prediction with expert advice for ``easy'' sequences. We show that a variant of NormalHedge enjoys a second-order $ε$-quantile regret bound of $O\big(\sqrt{V_T \log(V_T/ε)}\big) $ when $V_T > \log N$, where $V_T$ is the cumulative second moment of instantaneous per-expert regret averaged with respect to a natural distribution determined by the algorithm. The algorithm is motivated by a continuous time limit using Stochastic Differential Equations. The discrete time analysis uses self-concordance techniques.

AI.tldr: A variant of NormalHedge achieves a data-dependent second-order ε-quantile regret bound of O(√(V_T log(V_T/ε))) for easy sequences, provided V_T > log N; V_T is the cumulative second moment of per-expert regret under the algorithm's weighting; the analysis uses a continuous-time SDE limit and discrete-time self-concordance techniques.
AI.motivation: To obtain tighter, data-dependent regret bounds for predicting with expert advice on easy sequences, leveraging second-moment variability and drawing intuition from continuous-time models.
AI.method: Introduce a NormalHedge variant whose analysis follows from a continuous-time limit via stochastic differential equations. In discrete time, employ self-concordant potential/techniques to derive a bound in terms of the cumulative second moment V_T and the quantile parameter ε. Define V_T as the cumulative second moment of instantaneous per-expert regret averaged over the algorithm-derived distribution; show the bound holds when V_T > log N.
AI.result: Proves a second-order ε-quantile regret bound: Regret ≤ O(√(V_T log(V_T/ε))) for V_T > log N, with the bound depending on ε-quantile and the observed second moment rather than worst-case quantities.
AI.conclusion: The work bridges continuous-time intuition (SDEs) with discrete-time analysis (self-concordance) to achieve tighter, data-dependent regret bounds for easy sequences, advancing the theory of NormalHedge and its second-order guarantees.

title: Gesture Matters: Pedestrian Gesture Recognition for AVs Through Skeleton Pose Evaluation
comment: 9th International Conference on Instrumentation, Control, and Automation (ICA)
summary: Gestures are a key component of non-verbal communication in traffic, often helping pedestrian-to-driver interactions when formal traffic rules may be insufficient. This problem becomes more apparent when autonomous vehicles (AVs) struggle to interpret such gestures. In this study, we present a gesture classification framework using 2D pose estimation applied to real-world video sequences from the WIVW dataset. We categorise gestures into four primary classes (Stop, Go, Thank & Greet, and No Gesture) and extract 76 static and dynamic features from normalised keypoints. Our analysis demonstrates that hand position and movement velocity are especially discriminative in distinguishing between gesture classes, achieving a classification accuracy score of 87%. These findings not only improve the perceptual capabilities of AV systems but also contribute to the broader understanding of pedestrian behaviour in traffic contexts.

AI.tldr: Gesture classification for pedestrian–AV interaction using 2D pose estimation on real-world sequences (WIVW). Four classes (Stop, Go, Thank & Greet, No Gesture) with 76 features; 87% accuracy; hand position and velocity are most discriminative; aids AV perception and understanding of pedestrian behavior.
AI.motivation: Pedestrian gestures convey intent in traffic and can be misinterpreted by autonomous vehicles; improving gesture perception is essential for safe human–AV interaction.
AI.method: 2D pose estimation on real-world video from the WIVW dataset; categorize gestures into four classes; extract 76 static and dynamic features from normalised keypoints; analyze discriminative power of features.
AI.result: Achieves 87% classification accuracy; identifies hand position and movement velocity as particularly discriminative for distinguishing gesture classes.
AI.conclusion: The framework enhances the perceptual capabilities of AV systems for pedestrian gestures and contributes to understanding pedestrian behavior in traffic contexts.

title: The Confidence Manifold: Geometric Structure of Correctness Representations in Language Models
summary: When a language model asserts that "the capital of Australia is Sydney," does it know this is wrong? We characterize the geometry of correctness representations across 9 models from 5 architecture families. The structure is simple: the discriminative signal occupies 3-8 dimensions, performance degrades with additional dimensions, and no nonlinear classifier improves over linear separation. Centroid distance in the low-dimensional subspace matches trained probe performance (0.90 AUC), enabling few-shot detection: on GPT-2, 25 labeled examples achieve 89% of full-data accuracy. We validate causally through activation steering: the learned direction produces 10.9 percentage point changes in error rates while random directions show no effect. Internal probes achieve 0.80-0.97 AUC; output-based methods (P(True), semantic entropy) achieve only 0.44-0.64 AUC. The correctness signal exists internally but is not expressed in outputs. That centroid distance matches probe performance indicates class separation is a mean shift, making detection geometric rather than learned.

AI.tldr: Correctness signals in language models are low-dimensional and linearly separable, residing in a compact internal subspace that does not manifest in outputs; performance of detection is explainable by mean-shift geometry and can be achieved with few-shot probes, while causal activation steering confirms a real internal direction influencing error rates.
AI.motivation: To determine whether models encode a veracity/falsehood signal internally, how it is structured geometrically, and whether it can be detected with internal probes or via outputs, with implications for interpretability and safety.
AI.method: Cross-model analysis of 9 models from 5 architectures. Investigated dimensionality of the correctness signal (3–8 dimensions). Compared linear vs nonlinear classifiers; used centroid distance in low-dimensional subspace as a detection metric; evaluated few-shot detection (GPT-2, 25 labeled examples); conducted activation steering to test causality; evaluated internal probes (0.80–0.97 AUC) versus output-based signals (P(True), semantic entropy, 0.44–0.64 AUC).
AI.result: Correctness representations are compact and linearly separable; centroid distance aligns with probe performance (~0.90 AUC). Few-shot detection is effective (GPT-2: 25 examples reach 89% of full-data accuracy). Activation steering yields ~10.9 percentage point error changes; random directions have no effect. Internal probes perform well; output signals are weaker. The correctness signal exists internally but is not reflected in model outputs, and the separation is essentially a mean-shift, implying a geometric rather than learned detection.
AI.conclusion: Models encode an internal, low-dimensional correctness signal that is not surfaced in outputs. Detection relies on geometric (mean-shift) properties rather than learned decision boundaries, enabling simple, few-shot probing and causal manipulation, with implications for interpretability and safe deployment.

title: Understanding Image2Video Domain Shift in Food Segmentation: An Instance-level Analysis on Apples
summary: Food segmentation models trained on static images have achieved strong performance on benchmark datasets; however, their reliability in video settings remains poorly understood. In real-world applications such as food monitoring and instance counting, segmentation outputs must be temporally consistent, yet image-trained models often break down when deployed on videos. In this work, we analyze this failure through an instance segmentation and tracking perspective, focusing on apples as a representative food category. Models are trained solely on image-level food segmentation data and evaluated on video sequences using an instance segmentation with tracking-by-matching framework, enabling object-level temporal analysis. Our results reveal that high frame-wise segmentation accuracy does not translate to stable instance identities over time. Temporal appearance variations, particularly illumination changes, specular reflections, and texture ambiguity, lead to mask flickering and identity fragmentation, resulting in significant errors in apple counting. These failures are largely overlooked by conventional image-based metrics, which substantially overestimate real-world video performance. Beyond diagnosing the problem, we examine practical remedies that do not require full video supervision, including post-hoc temporal regularization and self-supervised temporal consistency objectives. Our findings suggest that the root cause of failure lies in image-centric training objectives that ignore temporal coherence, rather than model capacity. This study highlights a critical evaluation gap in food segmentation research and motivates temporally-aware learning and evaluation protocols for video-based food analysis.

AI.tldr: Image-trained food segmentation achieves high frame-wise accuracy but lacks temporal consistency in video; temporal coherence is ignored by training objectives, leading to identity fragmentation and counting errors; remedies include post-hoc temporal regularization and self-supervised temporal objectives; calls for temporally-aware evaluation protocols.
AI.motivation: Assess reliability of static-image segmentation models in video for food analysis, using apples as a representative case, by evaluating instance-level temporal stability and its impact on counting; reveal gaps between image metrics and video performance.
AI.method: Train models on image-level segmentation data and evaluate on video sequences with an instance segmentation and tracking-by-matching framework to analyze object-level temporal consistency; diagnose failure modes due to illumination, reflections, and texture ambiguity; investigate remedies such as post-hoc temporal regularization and self-supervised temporal consistency objectives that do not require full video supervision.
AI.result: Frame-wise segmentation accuracy does not guarantee stable instance identities over time; temporal appearance changes cause mask flickering and identity fragmentation, leading to counting errors; image-based metrics overestimate real-world video performance; temporal remedies provide partial improvements but do not fully mitigate the problem; root cause is image-centric training objectives that ignore temporal coherence.
AI.conclusion: There is an evaluation gap for video-based food analysis; need temporally-aware learning and evaluation protocols; develop methods that incorporate temporal coherence without requiring full video supervision.

title: Spherical Steering: Geometry-Aware Activation Rotation for Language Models
summary: Inference-time steering has emerged as a promising paradigm for controlling language models (LMs) without the cost of retraining. However, standard approaches typically rely on activation addition, a geometric operation that inevitably alters the magnitude of hidden representations. This raises concerns about representation collapse and degradation of open-ended generation capabilities. In this work, we explore Spherical Steering, a training-free primitive that resolves this trade-off through activation rotation. Rather than shifting activations with a fixed vector, our method rotates them along a geodesic toward a target direction, guiding the activation toward the target concept while preserving the integrity of the signal. To further enhance adaptivity, we incorporate a confidence gate that dynamically modulates steering strength based on input uncertainty. Extensive experiments across multiple-choice benchmarks demonstrate that Spherical Steering significantly outperforms addition-based baselines (notably by +10% on TruthfulQA, COPA, and Storycloze), while simultaneously maintaining the model's general open-ended generation quality. This work highlights the value of geometric consistency, suggesting that norm-preserving rotation is a robust and effective primitive for precise inference-time control.

AI.tldr: Spherical Steering uses norm-preserving activation rotation with a confidence gate for inference-time control, outperforming activation-addition baselines by ~10% on several benchmarks while preserving open-ended generation quality.
AI.motivation: Activation addition changes magnitudes and can degrade representations and generative abilities; a training-free, geometry-consistent primitive is desirable for safe, reliable steering of LMs at inference.
AI.method: Rotate activations along a geodesic toward a target direction (instead of adding a fixed vector), preserving signal magnitude; incorporate a confidence gate to modulate steering by input uncertainty; training-free.
AI.result: Empirical gains of about +10% on TruthfulQA, COPA, Storycloze compared with addition-based baselines; no noticeable loss in open-ended generation quality; evidence for geometric-consistency benefits.
AI.conclusion: Norm-preserving rotation is a robust, effective inference-time control primitive with better trade-offs than magnitude-altering additive methods; suggests geometric operations can enable precise steering without retraining.

title: Learning Self-Correction in Vision-Language Models via Rollout Augmentation
summary: Self-correction is essential for solving complex reasoning problems in vision-language models (VLMs). However, existing reinforcement learning (RL) methods struggle to learn it, as effective self-correction behaviors emerge only rarely, making learning signals extremely sparse. To address this challenge, we propose correction-specific rollouts (Octopus), an RL rollout augmentation framework that synthesizes dense self-correction examples by recombining existing rollouts. This augmentation simultaneously improves sample efficiency due to rollout reuse and stabilizes RL optimization through balanced supervision. Furthermore, we introduce a response-masking strategy that decouples self-correction from direct reasoning, avoiding signal conflicts and enabling both behaviors to be learned effectively. Building on this, we introduce Octopus-8B, a reasoning VLM with controllable self-correction capability. Across 7 benchmarks, it achieves SoTA performance among open-source VLMs, outperforming the best RLVR baseline by 1.0 score while requiring only $0.72\times$ training time per step.

AI.tldr: Proposes Octopus: a correction-specific RL augmentation for self-correction in vision-language models, using rollout recombination and response masking; achieves state-of-the-art open-source VLMs (Octopus-8B) across 7 benchmarks with improved sample efficiency.
AI.motivation: Self-correction in VLMs is sparsely signaled, causing learning signals in RL to be extremely sparse. There is a need for dense supervision and stable RL optimization; current RL methods struggle to learn self-correction effectively.
AI.method: Introduce correction-specific rollouts (Octopus) to synthesize dense self-correction examples by recombining existing rollouts; rollout reuse improves sample efficiency; a response-masking strategy decouples self-correction from direct reasoning to avoid signal conflicts; build Octopus-8B with controllable self-correction capability.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source VLMs across 7 benchmarks; outperforms the best RLVR baseline by 1.0 score while using only 0.72x training time per step.
AI.conclusion: The proposed RL augmentation framework effectively enables self-correction learning in vision-language models, improving performance and training efficiency; Octopus-8B demonstrates controllable self-correction as a practical capability.

title: A Causal Machine Learning Framework for Treatment Personalization in Clinical Trials: Application to Ulcerative Colitis
summary: Randomized controlled trials estimate average treatment effects, but treatment response heterogeneity motivates personalized approaches. A critical question is whether statistically detectable heterogeneity translates into improved treatment decisions -- these are distinct questions that can yield contradictory answers. We present a modular causal machine learning framework that evaluates each question separately: permutation importance identifies which features predict heterogeneity, best linear predictor (BLP) testing assesses statistical significance, and doubly robust policy evaluation measures whether acting on the heterogeneity improves patient outcomes. We apply this framework to patient-level data from the UNIFI maintenance trial of ustekinumab in ulcerative colitis, comparing placebo, standard-dose ustekinumab every 12 weeks, and dose-intensified ustekinumab every 8 weeks, using cross-fitted X-learner models with baseline demographics, medication history, week-8 clinical scores, laboratory biomarkers, and video-derived endoscopic features. BLP testing identified strong associations between endoscopic features and treatment effect heterogeneity for ustekinumab versus placebo, yet doubly robust policy evaluation showed no improvement in expected remission from incorporating endoscopic features, and out-of-fold multi-arm evaluation showed worse performance. Diagnostic comparison of prognostic contribution against policy value revealed that endoscopic scores behaved as disease severity markers -- improving outcome prediction in untreated patients but adding noise to treatment selection -- while clinical variables (fecal calprotectin, age, CRP) captured the decision-relevant variation. These results demonstrate that causal machine learning applications to clinical trials should include policy-level evaluation alongside heterogeneity testing.

AI.tldr: Endoscopic features are prognostic but not predictive for treatment decisions; a modular causal ML approach separates heterogeneity detection from policy impact evaluation, revealing no clinical gain from using endoscopy features to guide ustekinumab dosing in UC within UNIFI.
AI.motivation: To distinguish statistical heterogeneity in treatment effects from actual improvements in clinical decision-making, using a modular framework that separately tests heterogeneity and policy value.
AI.method: Permutation importance to identify predictors of heterogeneity; best linear predictor (BLP) testing for statistical significance; doubly robust off-policy policy evaluation to assess outcome gains from acting on detected heterogeneity; cross-fitted X-learner with baseline demographics, medical history, week-8 scores, biomarkers, and video-derived endoscopic features; applied to three-arm UNIFI trial (placebo, standard-dose q12w, dose-intensified q8w).
AI.result: BLP found strong associations between endoscopic features and heterogeneity in treatment effect for ustekinumab vs placebo. However, doubly robust policy evaluation showed no improvement in remission by using endoscopic features to guide decisions; out-of-fold multi-arm evaluation performed worse when incorporating endoscopy. Prognostic–predictive diagnostic comparison indicated endoscopic scores primarily captured disease severity (prognostic in untreated patients) but added noise for treatment selection, whereas clinical variables (fecal calprotectin, age, CRP) captured decision-relevant variation.
AI.conclusion: Policy-level evaluation should accompany heterogeneity testing in causal ML analyses of clinical trials to properly assess whether detected heterogeneity translates into actionable improvements in patient outcomes.

title: Are Vision Foundation Models Foundational for Electron Microscopy Image Segmentation?
summary: Although vision foundation models (VFMs) are increasingly reused for biomedical image analysis, it remains unclear whether the latent representations they provide are general enough to support effective transfer and reuse across heterogeneous microscopy image datasets. Here, we study this question for the problem of mitochondria segmentation in electron microscopy (EM) images, using two popular public EM datasets (Lucchi++ and VNC) and three recent representative VFMs (DINOv2, DINOv3, and OpenCLIP). We evaluate two practical model adaptation regimes: a frozen-backbone setting in which only a lightweight segmentation head is trained on top of the VFM, and parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) via Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) in which the VFM is fine-tuned in a targeted manner to a specific dataset. Across all backbones, we observe that training on a single EM dataset yields good segmentation performance (quantified as foreground Intersection-over-Union), and that LoRA consistently improves in-domain performance. In contrast, training on multiple EM datasets leads to severe performance degradation for all models considered, with only marginal gains from PEFT. Exploration of the latent representation space through various techniques (PCA, Fréchet Dinov2 distance, and linear probes) reveals a pronounced and persistent domain mismatch between the two considered EM datasets in spite of their visual similarity, which is consistent with the observed failure of paired training. These results suggest that, while VFMs can deliver competitive results for EM segmentation within a single domain under lightweight adaptation, current PEFT strategies are insufficient to obtain a single robust model across heterogeneous EM datasets without additional domain-alignment mechanisms.

AI.tldr: VFMs can achieve good segmentation within a single EM domain with lightweight adaptation; cross-domain transfer across Lucchi++ and VNC is hampered; LoRA improves in-domain but not cross-domain; latent-space analysis shows persistent domain mismatch; domain alignment is needed for robust cross-dataset models.
AI.motivation: Assess how general the latent representations from vision foundation models are for transfer and reuse across heterogeneous electron microscopy datasets in mitochondria segmentation.
AI.method: Evaluate DINOv2, DINOv3, and OpenCLIP backbones on two public EM datasets (Lucchi++ and VNC). Compare two adaptation regimes: (1) frozen-backbone with a lightweight segmentation head; (2) parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA). Measure segmentation performance (foreground IoU) and analyze latent spaces using PCA, Fréchet Dinov2 distance, and linear probes to probe domain alignment.
AI.result: Training on a single EM dataset yields strong segmentation performance across backbones; LoRA improves in-domain performance. Training on multiple EM datasets causes severe performance degradation for all models, with only marginal gains from PEFT. Latent-space analyses reveal pronounced and persistent domain mismatch between Lucchi++ and VNC despite visual similarity, consistent with failed paired training.
AI.conclusion: VFMs provide competitive EM segmentation within a single domain under lightweight adaptation, but current PEFT strategies do not yield a single robust model across heterogeneous EM datasets without explicit domain-alignment mechanisms.

title: Nansde-net: A neural sde framework for generating time series with memory
summary: Modeling time series with long- or short-memory characteristics is a fundamental challenge in many scientific and engineering domains. While fractional Brownian motion has been widely used as a noise source to capture such memory effects, its incompatibility with Itô calculus limits its applicability in neural stochastic differential equation~(SDE) frameworks. In this paper, we propose a novel class of noise, termed Neural Network-kernel ARMA-type noise~(NA-noise), which is an Itô-process-based alternative capable of capturing both long- and short-memory behaviors. The kernel function defining the noise structure is parameterized via neural networks and decomposed into a product form to preserve the Markov property. Based on this noise process, we develop NANSDE-Net, a generative model that extends Neural SDEs by incorporating NA-noise. We prove the theoretical existence and uniqueness of the solution under mild conditions and derive an efficient backpropagation scheme for training. Empirical results on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that NANSDE-Net matches or outperforms existing models, including fractional SDE-Net, in reproducing long- and short-memory features of the data, while maintaining computational tractability within the Itô calculus framework.

AI.tldr: Introduces NA-noise, an Itô-process-based, neural network–parameterized kernel ARMA–type noise for neural SDEs to capture long- and short-memory effects, enabling NANSDE-Net with theoretical guarantees and competitive empirical performance vs fractional SDE-Net.
AI.motivation: Overcome the incompatibility of fractional Brownian motion with Itô calculus in Neural SDE frameworks; provide a memory model that preserves the Itô/Markov structure for tractable training and simulation while capturing both long- and short-range dependencies.
AI.method: Define a neural-network–parametrized kernel for the noise, arranged in a product form to maintain Markov property; integrate this NA-noise into a Neural SDE framework to form NANSDE-Net; prove existence and uniqueness under mild conditions; derive an efficient backpropagation/training scheme.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets shows NANSDE-Net reproduces and often surpasses other models (notably fractional SDE-Net) in capturing long- and short-memory behavior, with tractable computation within Itô calculus.
AI.conclusion: NA-noise provides a flexible, Itô-compatible memory model for stochastic dynamics; NANSDE-Net demonstrates strong empirical performance and theoretical soundness, expanding the toolkit for memory-aware neural SDE modeling.

title: GeoFocus: Blending Efficient Global-to-Local Perception for Multimodal Geometry Problem-Solving
summary: Geometry problem-solving remains a significant challenge for Large Multimodal Models (LMMs), requiring not only global shape recognition but also attention to intricate local relationships related to geometric theory. To address this, we propose GeoFocus, a novel framework comprising two core modules. 1) Critical Local Perceptor, which automatically identifies and emphasizes critical local structure (e.g., angles, parallel lines, comparative distances) through thirteen theory-based perception templates, boosting critical local feature coverage by 61% compared to previous methods. 2) VertexLang, a compact topology formal language, encodes global figures through vertex coordinates and connectivity relations. By replacing bulky code-based encodings, VertexLang reduces global perception training time by 20% while improving topology recognition accuracy. When evaluated in Geo3K, GeoQA, and FormalGeo7K, GeoFocus achieves a 4.7% accuracy improvement over leading specialized models and demonstrates superior robustness in MATHVERSE under diverse visual conditions. Project Page -- https://github.com/dle666/GeoFocus

AI.tldr: GeoFocus introduces Critical Local Perceptor and VertexLang to enhance geometry reasoning in LMMs, achieving 4.7% accuracy gains and faster training.
AI.motivation: Geometry problem solving in LMMs requires both global shape understanding and fine-grained local geometric relations; existing solutions struggle with robust local reasoning and topology encoding.
AI.method: Critical Local Perceptor uses thirteen theory-based perception templates to highlight critical local structures (angles, parallel lines, distances) and boosts local feature coverage by 61%. VertexLang is a compact topology language encoding vertex coordinates and connectivity, replacing bulky code-based encodings and reducing global perception training time by 20%.
AI.result: GeoFocus achieves a 4.7% accuracy improvement over leading specialized models on Geo3K, GeoQA, and FormalGeo7K, and shows superior robustness on MATHVERSE under diverse visual conditions; also reports 61% gain in critical local feature coverage and 20% training-time reduction.
AI.conclusion: GeoFocus advances geometry reasoning in LMMs by coupling targeted local-structure perception with a compact topology encoding language, yielding improved accuracy and robustness; project page available.

title: Dreaming in Code for Curriculum Learning in Open-Ended Worlds
comment: 11 pages (main text), 90 pages total. Project page: https://konstantinosmitsides.github.io/dreaming-in-code
summary: Open-ended learning frames intelligence as emerging from continual interaction with an ever-expanding space of environments. While recent advances have utilized foundation models to programmatically generate diverse environments, these approaches often focus on discovering isolated behaviors rather than orchestrating sustained progression. In complex open-ended worlds, the large combinatorial space of possible challenges makes it difficult for agents to discover sequences of experiences that remain consistently learnable. To address this, we propose Dreaming in Code (DiCode), a framework in which foundation models synthesize executable environment code to scaffold learning toward increasing competence. In DiCode, "dreaming" takes the form of materializing code-level variations of the world. We instantiate DiCode in Craftax, a challenging open-ended benchmark characterized by rich mechanics and long-horizon progression. Empirically, DiCode enables agents to acquire long-horizon skills, achieving a $16\%$ improvement in mean return over the strongest baseline and non-zero success on late-game combat tasks where prior methods fail. Our results suggest that code-level environment design provides a practical mechanism for curriculum control, enabling the construction of intermediate environments that bridge competence gaps in open-ended worlds. Project page and source code are available at https://konstantinosmitsides.github.io/dreaming-in-code and https://github.com/konstantinosmitsides/dreaming-in-code.

AI.tldr: DiCode uses foundation-model–generated executable code to scaffold learning by gradually constructing code-level environment variants, enabling long-horizon skill acquisition and improved performance on open-ended tasks.
AI.motivation: Open-ended learning suffers from a combinatorial environment space; prior methods generate diverse environments but struggle to orchestrate sustained progression; there's a need for curriculum-like progression to keep agents learning.
AI.method: Generate executable environment code via foundation models; 'dreaming' materializes code-level variations of the world; implement and evaluate in the Craftax open-ended benchmark to test long-horizon progression.
AI.result: Achieves a 16% mean return improvement over the strongest baseline; demonstrates non-zero success on late-game combat tasks where prior methods fail.
AI.conclusion: Code-level environment design offers a practical mechanism for curriculum control in open-ended worlds, enabling intermediate environments that bridge competence gaps and support longer-horizon skill acquisition.

title: Automatic regularization parameter choice for tomography using a double model approach
summary: Image reconstruction in X-ray tomography is an ill-posed inverse problem, particularly with limited available data. Regularization is thus essential, but its effectiveness hinges on the choice of a regularization parameter that balances data fidelity against a priori information. We present a novel method for automatic parameter selection based on the use of two distinct computational discretizations of the same problem. A feedback control algorithm dynamically adjusts the regularization strength, driving an iterative reconstruction toward the smallest parameter that yields sufficient similarity between reconstructions on the two grids. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is demonstrated using real tomographic data.

AI.tldr: Automatic parameter selection for regularization in X-ray tomography using two discretizations and a feedback controller to enforce grid-consistency, selecting the smallest regularization parameter that achieves sufficient similarity.
AI.motivation: Regularization parameters critically balance data fidelity and priors in ill-posed tomography; manual tuning is tedious and dataset-specific.
AI.method: Run reconstructions on two distinct discretizations of the forward model; iteratively adjust the regularization strength via a feedback control law to drive the two reconstructions toward similarity; terminate when similarity is achieved with the smallest parameter.
AI.result: Validated on real tomographic data; demonstrates effectiveness of automatic parameter selection.
AI.conclusion: Grid-consistency based parameter control offers a principled, data-driven way to choose regularization strength in ill-posed tomography and may generalize to similar inverse problems.

title: Interpretable Dynamic Network Modeling of Tensor Time Series via Kronecker Time-Varying Graphical Lasso
summary: With the rapid development of web services, large amounts of time series data are generated and accumulated across various domains such as finance, healthcare, and online platforms. As such data often co-evolves with multiple variables interacting with each other, estimating the time-varying dependencies between variables (i.e., the dynamic network structure) has become crucial for accurate modeling. However, real-world data is often represented as tensor time series with multiple modes, resulting in large, entangled networks that are hard to interpret and computationally intensive to estimate. In this paper, we propose Kronecker Time-Varying Graphical Lasso (KTVGL), a method designed for modeling tensor time series. Our approach estimates mode-specific dynamic networks in a Kronecker product form, thereby avoiding overly complex entangled structures and producing interpretable modeling results. Moreover, the partitioned network structure prevents the exponential growth of computational time with data dimension. In addition, our method can be extended to stream algorithms, making the computational time independent of the sequence length. Experiments on synthetic data show that the proposed method achieves higher edge estimation accuracy than existing methods while requiring less computation time. To further demonstrate its practical value, we also present a case study using real-world data. Our source code and datasets are available at https://github.com/Higashiguchi-Shingo/KTVGL.

AI.tldr: We present KTVGL, a Kronecker-structured time-varying graphical Lasso for tensor time series that yields scalable, interpretable dynamic networks with improved edge recovery and lower computation, with streaming extensions.
AI.motivation: Time series data across multiple domains often form tensor structures with evolving, interdependent variables. Conventional graphical models produce tangled networks that are hard to interpret and computationally expensive, especially for high-dimensional tensor data.
AI.method: Propose Kronecker Time-Varying Graphical Lasso (KTVGL) that estimates mode-specific dynamic networks in a Kronecker product form, yielding partitioned, interpretable networks and reduced computational complexity; extendable to streaming settings to make time complexity independent of sequence length.
AI.result: On synthetic data, KTVGL achieves higher edge estimation accuracy than existing methods while requiring less computation time; a real-world case study demonstrates practical value; source code and data are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: KTVGL offers a scalable, interpretable framework for tensor time series with dynamic networks, mitigating entanglement and exponential scaling; it supports streaming implementations and improves edge recovery with lower computational cost.

title: Thegra: Graph-based SLAM for Thermal Imagery
summary: Thermal imaging provides a practical sensing modality for visual SLAM in visually degraded environments such as low illumination, smoke, or adverse weather. However, thermal imagery often exhibits low texture, low contrast, and high noise, complicating feature-based SLAM. In this work, we propose a sparse monocular graph-based SLAM system for thermal imagery that leverages general-purpose learned features -- the SuperPoint detector and LightGlue matcher, trained on large-scale visible-spectrum data to improve cross-domain generalization. To adapt these components to thermal data, we introduce a preprocessing pipeline to enhance input suitability and modify core SLAM modules to handle sparse and outlier-prone feature matches. We further incorporate keypoint confidence scores from SuperPoint into a confidence-weighted factor graph to improve estimation robustness. Evaluations on public thermal datasets demonstrate that the proposed system achieves reliable performance without requiring dataset-specific training or fine-tuning a desired feature detector, given the scarcity of quality thermal data. Code will be made available upon publication.

AI.tldr: Sparse monocular graph-based SLAM for thermal imagery uses cross-domain general-purpose learned features (SuperPoint detector and LightGlue matcher) trained on visible data, with a preprocessing pipeline and confidence-weighted factor graph; achieves reliable performance without thermal-specific training; code to be released.
AI.motivation: Thermal SLAM suffers from low texture, low contrast, and high noise, making feature-based SLAM challenging. Reusing general-purpose features trained on large-scale visible data could improve cross-domain generalization in thermal imagery, especially given scarce high-quality thermal data.
AI.method: A preprocessing pipeline enhances input suitability for thermal data; integrates SuperPoint detector and LightGlue matcher; modifies core SLAM modules to handle sparse and outlier-prone feature matches; incorporates keypoint confidence scores from SuperPoint into a confidence-weighted factor graph for robust estimation.
AI.result: Evaluations on public thermal datasets show reliable performance without dataset-specific training or fine-tuning of the feature detectors; demonstrates robustness to sparse/outlier matches; code will be available upon publication.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates the viability of cross-domain general-purpose features for thermal SLAM, avoiding detector-specific thermal training and paving the way for zero/few-shot domain adaptation in SLAM; release of code will facilitate reproducibility.

title: CADO: From Imitation to Cost Minimization for Heatmap-based Solvers in Combinatorial Optimization
summary: Heatmap-based solvers have emerged as a promising paradigm for Combinatorial Optimization (CO). However, we argue that the dominant Supervised Learning (SL) training paradigm suffers from a fundamental objective mismatch: minimizing imitation loss (e.g., cross-entropy) does not guarantee solution cost minimization. We dissect this mismatch into two deficiencies: Decoder-Blindness (being oblivious to the non-differentiable decoding process) and Cost-Blindness (prioritizing structural imitation over solution quality). We empirically demonstrate that these intrinsic flaws impose a hard performance ceiling. To overcome this limitation, we propose CADO (Cost-Aware Diffusion models for Optimization), a streamlined Reinforcement Learning fine-tuning framework that formulates the diffusion denoising process as an MDP to directly optimize the post-decoded solution cost. We introduce Label-Centered Reward, which repurposes ground-truth labels as unbiased baselines rather than imitation targets, and Hybrid Fine-Tuning for parameter-efficient adaptation. CADO achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse benchmarks, validating that objective alignment is essential for unlocking the full potential of heatmap-based solvers.

AI.tldr: Objective-aligned, cost-aware learning for heatmap-based solvers via diffusion-RL (CADO) improves optimization performance and overcomes SL-imitation drawbacks.
AI.motivation: SL training for heatmap-based combinatorial solvers suffers from objective mismatch: imitation losses do not guarantee low solution cost due to non-differentiable decoding and mismatch between structural imitation and actual objective (cost).
AI.method: Reinforcement Learning fine-tuning of a diffusion denoising process framed as an MDP; introduce Label-Centered Reward using ground-truth labels as unbiased baselines; Hybrid Fine-Tuning for parameter-efficient adaptation.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple CO benchmarks, demonstrating that objective-aligned cost minimization can surpass traditional imitation-based training and overcome the identified deficiencies.
AI.conclusion: Aligning optimization objective with final solution cost is essential for leveraging heatmap-based solvers; cost-aware diffusion-RL (CADO) provides a scalable, effective framework.

title: TIBR4D: Tracing-Guided Iterative Boundary Refinement for Efficient 4D Gaussian Segmentation
summary: Object-level segmentation in dynamic 4D Gaussian scenes remains challenging due to complex motion, occlusions, and ambiguous boundaries. In this paper, we present an efficient learning-free 4D Gaussian segmentation framework that lifts video segmentation masks to 4D spaces, whose core is a two-stage iterative boundary refinement, TIBR4D. The first stage is an Iterative Gaussian Instance Tracing (IGIT) at the temporal segment level. It progressively refines Gaussian-to-instance probabilities through iterative tracing, and extracts corresponding Gaussian point clouds that better handle occlusions and preserve completeness of object structures compared to existing one-shot threshold-based methods. The second stage is a frame-wise Gaussian Rendering Range Control (RCC) via suppressing highly uncertain Gaussians near object boundaries while retaining their core contributions for more accurate boundaries. Furthermore, a temporal segmentation merging strategy is proposed for IGIT to balance identity consistency and dynamic awareness. Longer segments enforce stronger multi-frame constraints for stable identities, while shorter segments allow identity changes to be captured promptly. Experiments on HyperNeRF and Neu3D demonstrate that our method produces accurate object Gaussian point clouds with clearer boundaries and higher efficiency compared to SOTA methods.

AI.tldr: A learning-free 4D Gaussian segmentation framework (TIBR4D) using iterative boundary refinement (IGIT) and rendering range control (RCC) with temporal segment merging, achieving clearer object boundaries and higher efficiency than SOTA on HyperNeRF and Neu3D.
AI.motivation: Object-level segmentation in dynamic 4D Gaussian scenes is challenging due to complex motion, occlusions, and ambiguous boundaries. A learning-free, two-stage approach that refines Gaussian-to-instance associations and boundary confidence is proposed to lift segmentation masks into 4D Gaussian representations.
AI.method: Two-stage boundary refinement: (1) Iterative Gaussian Instance Tracing (IGIT) at temporal segment level to iteratively refine Gaussian-to-instance probabilities and extract Gaussian point clouds; (2) frame-wise Gaussian Rendering Range Control (RCC) to suppress highly uncertain Gaussians near boundaries while preserving core contributions. A temporal segmentation merging strategy balances identity consistency and dynamic awareness, using longer segments for multi-frame consistency and shorter segments for prompt identity changes.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on HyperNeRF and Neu3D shows the proposed method yields accurate object Gaussian point clouds with clearer boundaries and higher efficiency compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: TIBR4D provides an effective, learning-free framework for 4D Gaussian segmentation, enhancing boundary sharpness and occlusion handling while delivering efficiency gains; validated on standard benchmarks.

title: DrugR: Optimizing Molecular Drugs through LLM-based Explicit Reasoning
summary: Molecule generation and optimization is a fundamental task in chemical domain. The rapid development of intelligent tools, especially large language models (LLMs) with powerful knowledge reserves and interactive capabilities, has provided new paradigms for it. Nevertheless, the intrinsic challenge for LLMs lies in the complex implicit relationship between molecular structure and pharmacological properties and the lack of corresponding labeled data. To bridge this gap, we propose DrugR, an LLM-based method that introduces explicit, step-by-step pharmacological reasoning into the optimization process. Our approach integrates domain-specific continual pretraining, supervised fine-tuning via reverse data engineering, and self-balanced multi-granular reinforcement learning. This framework enables DrugR to effectively improve key ADMET properties while preserving the original molecule's core efficacy. Experimental results demonstrate that DrugR achieves comprehensive enhancement across multiple properties without compromising structural similarity or target binding affinity. Importantly, its explicit reasoning process provides clear, interpretable rationales for each optimization step, yielding actionable design insights and advancing toward automated, knowledge-driven scientific discovery. Our code and model checkpoints are open-sourced to foster future research.

AI.tldr: Introduces DrugR, an LLM-based framework that performs explicit, step-by-step pharmacological reasoning to optimize molecular properties (ADMET) while preserving efficacy, using domain-pretraining, reverse data engineering fine-tuning, and balanced multi-granular reinforcement learning; yields interpretable, property-enhanced molecules with preserved similarity and binding.
AI.motivation: Addresses the core challenge in molecule design: the complex, implicit mapping from structure to pharmacological properties and the scarcity of labeled data; seeks interpretable, knowledge-driven optimization using large language models.
AI.method: Domain-specific continual pretraining on chemical knowledge; supervised fine-tuning via reverse data engineering; self-balanced multi-granular reinforcement learning; enables explicit stepwise pharmacological reasoning to guide molecular optimization while preserving core activity.
AI.result: DrugR achieves comprehensive improvements across multiple ADMET properties without sacrificing structural similarity or target binding affinity; the explicit reasoning provides transparent rationales for each optimization step.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, interpretable reasoning in LLM-based drug design facilitates actionable design insights and moves toward automated, knowledge-driven scientific discovery; code and model checkpoints are open-sourced to support future work.

title: GOT-Edit: Geometry-Aware Generic Object Tracking via Online Model Editing
comment: ICLR 2026. This is a preprint version. The camera-ready version will be updated soon
summary: Human perception for effective object tracking in a 2D video stream arises from the implicit use of prior 3D knowledge combined with semantic reasoning. In contrast, most generic object tracking (GOT) methods primarily rely on 2D features of the target and its surroundings while neglecting 3D geometric cues, which makes them susceptible to partial occlusion, distractors, and variations in geometry and appearance. To address this limitation, we introduce GOT-Edit, an online cross-modality model editing approach that integrates geometry-aware cues into a generic object tracker from a 2D video stream. Our approach leverages features from a pre-trained Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer to enable geometric cue inference from only a few 2D images. To tackle the challenge of seamlessly combining geometry and semantics, GOT-Edit performs online model editing with null-space constrained updates that incorporate geometric information while preserving semantic discrimination, yielding consistently better performance across diverse scenarios. Extensive experiments on multiple GOT benchmarks demonstrate that GOT-Edit achieves superior robustness and accuracy, particularly under occlusion and clutter, establishing a new paradigm for combining 2D semantics with 3D geometric reasoning for generic object tracking.

AI.tldr: GOT-Edit is an online cross-modality model-editing framework that injects 3D geometric cues into 2D generic object tracking via a Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer, improving robustness under occlusion and clutter.
AI.motivation: Generic object trackers rely mainly on 2D features and struggle with occlusion, distractors, and geometry/appearance variations. There is a need to leverage 3D geometric cues inferred from limited 2D information to enhance tracking performance.
AI.method: Leverages a pre-trained Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer to infer geometry cues from only a few 2D images. Performs online model editing with null-space constrained updates to fuse geometric information with semantic discrimination in a 2D tracker, enabling a geometry-aware, cross-modality extension.
AI.result: GOT-Edit demonstrates superior robustness and accuracy across multiple GOT benchmarks, especially under occlusion and clutter, outperforming baselines and establishing a new paradigm for integrating 2D semantics with 3D geometry in generic object tracking.
AI.conclusion: Online cross-modality model editing that incorporates 3D geometric reasoning into 2D GOT is effective, yielding robust tracking across diverse scenarios and enabling a shift from purely 2D semantics to geometry-informed tracking.

title: Distribution-Free Robust Functional Predict-Then-Optimize
summary: The solution of PDEs in decision-making tasks is increasingly being undertaken with the help of neural operator surrogate models due to the need for repeated evaluation. Such methods, while significantly more computationally favorable compared to their numerical counterparts, fail to provide any calibrated notions of uncertainty in their predictions. Current methods approach this deficiency typically with ensembling or Bayesian posterior estimation. However, these approaches either require distributional assumptions that fail to hold in practice or lack practical scalability, limiting their applications in practice. We, therefore, propose a novel application of conformal prediction to produce distribution-free uncertainty quantification over the function spaces mapped by neural operators. We then demonstrate how such prediction regions enable a formal regret characterization if leveraged in downstream robust decision-making tasks. We further demonstrate how such posited robust decision-making tasks can be efficiently solved using an infinite-dimensional generalization of Danskin's Theorem and calculus of variations and empirically demonstrate the superior performance of our proposed method over more restrictive modeling paradigms, such as Gaussian Processes, across several engineering tasks.

AI.tldr: Proposes distribution-free uncertainty quantification for neural operator PDE surrogates via conformal prediction, and an infinite-dimensional Danskin-based optimization framework for robust decision-making, showing superior performance to Gaussian Processes in engineering tasks.
AI.motivation: Need calibrated, scalable uncertainty estimates for neural operators used in PDE-based decision tasks; existing ensembles/Bayes approaches rely on assumptions or scale poorly.
AI.method: Apply conformal prediction to function-space outputs of neural operators; derive robust decision-making via infinite-dimensional Danskin's theorem and calculus of variations; enable efficient solution of downstream optimization with predicted regions.
AI.result: Prediction regions yield regret guarantees and robust decisions; empirical results show improved performance over Gaussian Processes on multiple engineering tasks.
AI.conclusion: Conformal prediction for neural operators offers calibrated uncertainty and a tractable infinite-dimensional optimization framework, outperforming restrictive priors in practical PDE-related decision tasks.

title: FLAG-4D: Flow-Guided Local-Global Dual-Deformation Model for 4D Reconstruction
summary: We introduce FLAG-4D, a novel framework for generating novel views of dynamic scenes by reconstructing how 3D Gaussian primitives evolve through space and time. Existing methods typically rely on a single Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) to model temporal deformations, and they often struggle to capture complex point motions and fine-grained dynamic details consistently over time, especially from sparse input views. Our approach, FLAG-4D, overcomes this by employing a dual-deformation network that dynamically warps a canonical set of 3D Gaussians over time into new positions and anisotropic shapes. This dual-deformation network consists of an Instantaneous Deformation Network (IDN) for modeling fine-grained, local deformations and a Global Motion Network (GMN) for capturing long-range dynamics, refined through mutual learning. To ensure these deformations are both accurate and temporally smooth, FLAG-4D incorporates dense motion features from a pretrained optical flow backbone. We fuse these motion cues from adjacent timeframes and use a deformation-guided attention mechanism to align this flow information with the current state of each evolving 3D Gaussian. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FLAG-4D achieves higher-fidelity and more temporally coherent reconstructions with finer detail preservation than state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: FLAG-4D uses a dual-deformation network to warp 3D Gaussian primitives over time for dynamic scene novel-view synthesis, achieving improved fidelity and temporal coherence from sparse views.
AI.motivation: Existing 4D dynamic scene methods largely rely on a single MLP to model temporal deformations, which struggles to capture complex, fine-grained motion and maintain consistency across time when input views are sparse.
AI.method: Propose Instantaneous Deformation Network (IDN) for fine local deformations and Global Motion Network (GMN) for long-range dynamics, mutually refined. A canonical set of 3D Gaussians is deformed across time. Dense motion features from a pretrained optical flow backbone are fused via a deformation-guided attention mechanism to align flow with the evolving Gaussians.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show higher fidelity and more temporally coherent reconstructions with better detail preservation than state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: FLAG-4D offers a robust 4D dynamic scene representation by dual deformation of 3D Gaussian primitives, improving reconstruction quality from sparse views and ensuring temporal smoothness.

title: Thermodynamic Isomorphism of Transformers: A Lagrangian Approach to Attention Dynamics
comment: 9 pages, 1 figure. Based on a thermodynamic framework for Transformer architectures. Derives the equation of state from first principles
summary: Although the Transformer architecture has revolutionized artificial intelligence, its underlying mechanisms remain largely heuristic and lack a unified physical theory. In this work, we propose a first-principles framework for information dynamics, treating the attention mechanism as a physical system governed by the principle of least action rather than as an algorithmic optimization. By mapping information states to a Riemannian manifold with the Fisher information metric, we derive the intelligence Lagrangian. We show that the softmax function corresponds to the unique thermodynamic equilibrium state that minimizes the Helmholtz free energy of the information gas. In addition, we identify the query-key interaction as an electrodynamic coupling between an external field and an intrinsic dipole moment. This theory establishes the first law of information thermodynamics, unifying inference (mechanical work) and learning (chemical evolution). It also explains emergent phenomena, such as scaling laws and grokking, as phase transitions characterized by the divergence of specific heat. Finally, we discuss how rotational symmetry breaking in the attention manifold generates massless Goldstone bosons, providing a field-theoretic perspective on rotary positional embeddings (RoPE). Our work connects Statistical Physics and Deep Learning, laying the groundwork for a general theory of physics-based intelligence.
