AI.tldr: A physics-based, first-principles framework for Transformer attention using information dynamics and thermodynamics, mapping states to a Fisher metric, and explaining phenomena (scaling, grokking, RoPE) via phase transitions and symmetry breaking.
AI.motivation: Move beyond heuristic, algorithmic views of attention to a unified physical theory of intelligence that ties inference and learning to thermodynamics and field theory.
AI.method: Develop a Lagrangian on a Riemannian information manifold using the Fisher metric; derive an information Lagrangian; prove softmax minimizes Helmholtz free energy of an information gas; model query-key interaction as electrodynamic coupling; define a first law of information thermodynamics; characterize scaling laws and grokking as phase transitions via divergent specific heat; analyze rotational symmetry breaking leading to Goldstone modes and relate to RoPE.
AI.result: A theoretical framework linking inference and learning through information thermodynamics; provides explanations for observed phenomena (scaling, grokking) as phase transitions; offers a field-theoretic interpretation of rotary embeddings and a pathway to a physics-based theory of intelligence.
AI.conclusion: Bridges Statistical Physics and Deep Learning to establish a foundational, physics-driven perspective on Transformer intelligence and to generalize to a universal theory of information processing.

title: SemiNFT: Learning to Transfer Presets from Imitation to Appreciation via Hybrid-Sample Reinforcement Learning
summary: Photorealistic color retouching plays a vital role in visual content creation, yet manual retouching remains inaccessible to non-experts due to its reliance on specialized expertise. Reference-based methods offer a promising alternative by transferring the preset color of a reference image to a source image. However, these approaches often operate as novice learners, performing global color mappings derived from pixel-level statistics, without a true understanding of semantic context or human aesthetics. To address this issue, we propose SemiNFT, a Diffusion Transformer (DiT)-based retouching framework that mirrors the trajectory of human artistic training: beginning with rigid imitation and evolving into intuitive creation. Specifically, SemiNFT is first taught with paired triplets to acquire basic structural preservation and color mapping skills, and then advanced to reinforcement learning (RL) on unpaired data to cultivate nuanced aesthetic perception. Crucially, during the RL stage, to prevent catastrophic forgetting of old skills, we design a hybrid online-offline reward mechanism that anchors aesthetic exploration with structural review. % experiments Extensive experiments show that SemiNFT not only outperforms state-of-the-art methods on standard preset transfer benchmarks but also demonstrates remarkable intelligence in zero-shot tasks, such as black-and-white photo colorization and cross-domain (anime-to-photo) preset transfer. These results confirm that SemiNFT transcends simple statistical matching and achieves a sophisticated level of aesthetic comprehension. Our project can be found at https://melanyyang.github.io/SemiNFT/.

AI.tldr: SemiNFT introduces a diffusion-transformer-based retouching framework trained first via paired triplets and then via reinforcement learning on unpaired data, with a hybrid online-offline reward to maintain previously learned skills; achieves superior results on preset transfer benchmarks and zero-shot tasks, indicating semantic aesthetic understanding beyond simple color mappings.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in reference-based color transfer methods that rely on global statistics and lack semantic or aesthetic understanding; enable non-experts to achieve photorealistic retouching by learning high-level aesthetics and structure-preserving mappings.
AI.method: Architecture: Diffusion Transformer (DiT) named SemiNFT. Training in two stages: supervised phase with paired triplets to enforce structure preservation and basic color mapping; RL phase on unpaired data to refine aesthetics. A hybrid online-offline reward mechanism anchors old skills during RL to combat catastrophic forgetting. Evaluations on standard preset transfer benchmarks and zero-shot tasks (e.g., black-and-white colorization, cross-domain anime-to-photo).
AI.result: Reported state-of-the-art performance on preset transfer benchmarks; strong zero-shot capabilities; demonstrates learned semantic aesthetic comprehension beyond pixel-statistics.
AI.conclusion: SemiNFT demonstrates a progression from rigid imitation to intuitive creation, integrating structure-preserving color mapping with aesthetic refinement via RL, advancing the field of perceptual-based image retouching and cross-domain transfer.

title: Sparsity-Aware Evolution for Model Merging
summary: We propose a sparsity-aware evolutionary (SAE) framework for model merging that involves iterative pruning-merging cycles to act as a novel mutation operator. We incorporate the sparsity constraints into the score function, which steers the evolutionary process to favor more sparse models, in addition to other conventional performance scores. Interestingly, the by-product of \textit{competition} for sparsity introduces an extra local \textit{attraction} and interplay into the evolutionary process: if one competitor has more zero elements, the other competitor's non-zero elements will occupy those positions, even though the less sparse competitor loses to the more sparse competitor in other positions. The proposed pipeline is evaluated on a variety of large-scale LLM benchmarks. Experiments demonstrate that our approach can improve model merging reliability across multiple benchmarks, and is easy to incorporate due to its simplicity and being orthogonal to most existing approaches.

AI.tldr: SAE is a sparsity-aware evolutionary framework for model merging that uses iterative pruning-merge cycles as a mutation operator, with sparsity embedded in the scoring function; it reveals a sparsity-competition effect and improves reliability on large LLM benchmarks, while remaining simple and orthogonal to existing methods.
AI.motivation: To improve reliability and scalability of model merging for large models by integrating sparsity considerations into the evolutionary search, and by introducing a pruning-merge mutation operator that exploits sparsity dynamics.
AI.method: Propose an iterative pruning-merging cycle as a mutation operator within an evolutionary framework; formulate a sparsity-aware score that combines traditional performance metrics with a sparsity constraint; leverage competition among candidates such that sparser models influence others to fill zeros, creating an attraction effect; evaluate on large-scale LLM benchmarks.
AI.result: Experiments show improved model merging reliability across multiple large-scale benchmarks; the approach is simple to implement, and its sparsity-centric objective is orthogonal to most existing methods, facilitating integration.
AI.conclusion: SAE offers a generalizable, easy-to-incorporate framework that embeds sparsity into evolutionary model merging, using pruning-merge mutations and competition-driven dynamics to enhance reliability on large models.

title: Overview and Comparison of AVS Point Cloud Compression Standard
summary: Point cloud is a prevalent 3D data representation format with significant application values in immersive media, autonomous driving, digital heritage protection, etc. However, the large data size of point clouds poses challenges to transmission and storage, which influences the wide deployments. Therefore, point cloud compression plays a crucial role in practical applications for both human and machine perception optimization. To this end, the Moving Picture Experts Group (MPEG) has established two standards for point cloud compression, including Geometry-based Point Cloud Compression (G-PCC) and Video-based Point Cloud Compression (V-PCC). In the meantime, the Audio Video coding Standard (AVS) Workgroup of China also have launched and completed the development for its first generation point cloud compression standard, namely AVS PCC. This new standardization effort has adopted many new coding tools and techniques, which are different from the other counterpart standards. This paper reviews the AVS PCC standard from two perspectives, i.e., the related technologies and performance comparisons.

AI.tldr: Review of AVS PCC for point cloud compression, with comparative analysis against MPEG G-PCC and V-PCC, focusing on technologies and performance.
AI.motivation: Point clouds are data-intensive; efficient compression is essential for transmission/storage and for human/machine perception, motivating comparison of AVS PCC with established MPEG standards.
AI.method: Literature/standards review analyzing AVS PCC’s coding tools and its performance relative to MPEG G-PCC and V-PCC.
AI.result: The paper provides an overview of AVS PCC’s technologies and presents performance comparisons with MPEG standards.
AI.conclusion: AVS PCC introduces novel coding tools; the paper analyzes its technology and performance, highlighting potential advantages and trade-offs within the point cloud compression landscape.

title: SkillRL: Evolving Agents via Recursive Skill-Augmented Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have shown stunning results in complex tasks, yet they often operate in isolation, failing to learn from past experiences. Existing memory-based methods primarily store raw trajectories, which are often redundant and noise-heavy. This prevents agents from extracting high-level, reusable behavioral patterns that are essential for generalization. In this paper, we propose SkillRL, a framework that bridges the gap between raw experience and policy improvement through automatic skill discovery and recursive evolution. Our approach introduces an experience-based distillation mechanism to build a hierarchical skill library SkillBank, an adaptive retrieval strategy for general and task-specific heuristics, and a recursive evolution mechanism that allows the skill library to co-evolve with the agent's policy during reinforcement learning. These innovations significantly reduce the token footprint while enhancing reasoning utility. Experimental results on ALFWorld, WebShop and seven search-augmented tasks demonstrate that SkillRL achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming strong baselines over 15.3% and maintaining robustness as task complexity increases. Code is available at this https://github.com/aiming-lab/SkillRL.

AI.tldr: SkillRL enables automatic skill discovery and recursive evolution to create a hierarchical SkillBank, enabling adaptive retrieval and policy co-evolution for LLM-based agents, yielding state-of-the-art results and around 15.3% gains over baselines across diverse tasks with improved robustness.
AI.motivation: Current memory-based approaches store raw trajectories, which are redundant and noise-heavy, hindering the extraction of high-level, reusable behavioral patterns needed for generalization in LLM agents. There is a need to bridge raw experience and policy improvement by learning reusable skills.
AI.method: Introduce an experience-based distillation mechanism to build a hierarchical skill library (SkillBank), an adaptive retrieval strategy for general and task-specific heuristics, and a recursive evolution mechanism that co-evolves the SkillBank with the agent's policy during reinforcement learning; results in reduced token footprint and improved reasoning utility.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on ALFWorld, WebShop, and seven search-augmented tasks shows SkillRL achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming strong baselines by about 15.3%, and maintains robustness as task complexity increases.
AI.conclusion: SkillRL effectively bridges raw experience and policy improvement through hierarchical, evolvable skill representations. It reduces token usage while boosting reasoning utility, demonstrates strong generalization across tasks, and provides code for reproducibility.

title: Inspiration Seeds: Learning Non-Literal Visual Combinations for Generative Exploration
summary: While generative models have become powerful tools for image synthesis, they are typically optimized for executing carefully crafted textual prompts, offering limited support for the open-ended visual exploration that often precedes idea formation. In contrast, designers frequently draw inspiration from loosely connected visual references, seeking emergent connections that spark new ideas. We propose Inspiration Seeds, a generative framework that shifts image generation from final execution to exploratory ideation. Given two input images, our model produces diverse, visually coherent compositions that reveal latent relationships between inputs, without relying on user-specified text prompts. Our approach is feed-forward, trained on synthetic triplets of decomposed visual aspects derived entirely through visual means: we use CLIP Sparse Autoencoders to extract editing directions in CLIP latent space and isolate concept pairs. By removing the reliance on language and enabling fast, intuitive recombination, our method supports visual ideation at the early and ambiguous stages of creative work.

AI.tldr: Inspiration Seeds enables prompt-free ideation by generating diverse, coherent compositions from two input images, revealing latent cross-input relationships using a feed-forward model trained on visually derived triplets and CLIP-based editing directions.
AI.motivation: Designers want open-ended visual exploration without language prompts; traditional generative models optimize for final execution and prompt-driven synthesis, limiting ideation.
AI.method: A feed-forward model trained on synthetic triplets of decomposed visual aspects. It uses CLIP Sparse Autoencoders to extract editing directions in CLIP latent space and isolate concept pairs, generating diverse compositions from two input images without text prompts.
AI.result: The approach yields diverse, visually coherent compositions that reveal latent relationships between the two inputs, enabling fast, intuitive recombination for early-stage visual ideation without language.
AI.conclusion: Removing language dependence and enabling prompt-free recombination accelerates visual ideation and supports exploration of latent relationships during the early, ambiguous stages of creative work.

title: Linearization Explains Fine-Tuning in Large Language Models
summary: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) is a popular class of techniques that strive to adapt large models in a scalable and resource-efficient manner. Yet, the mechanisms underlying their training performance and generalization remain underexplored. In this paper, we provide several insights into such fine-tuning through the lens of linearization. Fine-tuned models are often implicitly encouraged to remain close to the pretrained model. By making this explicit, using an Euclidean distance inductive bias in parameter space, we show that fine-tuning dynamics become equivalent to learning with the positive-definite neural tangent kernel (NTK). We specifically analyze how close the fully linear and the linearized fine-tuning optimizations are, based on the strength of the regularization. This allows us to be pragmatic about how good a model linearization is when fine-tuning large language models (LLMs). When linearization is a good model, our findings reveal a strong correlation between the eigenvalue spectrum of the NTK and the performance of model adaptation. Motivated by this, we give spectral perturbation bounds on the NTK induced by the choice of layers selected for fine-tuning. We empirically validate our theory on Low Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on LLMs. These insights not only characterize fine-tuning but also have the potential to enhance PEFT techniques, paving the way to better informed and more nimble adaptation in LLMs.

AI.tldr: PEFT's training dynamics can be understood via linearization: keeping models close to pretrained parameters makes fine-tuning equivalent to learning with a positive-definite NTK; the NTK spectrum correlates with adaptation performance, with spectral perturbation bounds for layer choices and empirical validation on LoRA in LLMs.
AI.motivation: To demystify why PEFT methods work and how their training dynamics and generalization relate to staying near a pretrained model, by connecting fine-tuning to NTK theory and linearization assumptions.
AI.method: Theoretically analyze fine-tuning under an Euclidean distance bias in parameter space, showing equivalence to positive-definite NTK learning when linearized; compare fully linear vs linearized regimes under regularization; derive spectral perturbation bounds on how NTK changes with layer selection for fine-tuning; empirically validate on LoRA applied to large language models.
AI.result: When linearization holds, there is a strong correlation between the NTK eigen-spectrum and adaptation performance; the paper provides spectral perturbation bounds showing how layer choices affect NTK; empirical experiments on LoRA with LLMs corroborate the theoretical claims.
AI.conclusion: The work clarifies the mechanisms of PEFT through NTK and linearization, offering principled guidance for selecting layers and regularization to improve adaptation efficiency and performance in LLMs.

title: Improving Reconstruction of Representation Autoencoder
summary: Recent work leverages Vision Foundation Models as image encoders to boost the generative performance of latent diffusion models (LDMs), as their semantic feature distributions are easy to learn. However, such semantic features often lack low-level information (\eg, color and texture), leading to degraded reconstruction fidelity, which has emerged as a primary bottleneck in further scaling LDMs. To address this limitation, we propose LV-RAE, a representation autoencoder that augments semantic features with missing low-level information, enabling high-fidelity reconstruction while remaining highly aligned with the semantic distribution. We further observe that the resulting high-dimensional, information-rich latent make decoders sensitive to latent perturbations, causing severe artifacts when decoding generated latent and consequently degrading generation quality. Our analysis suggests that this sensitivity primarily stems from excessive decoder responses along directions off the data manifold. Building on these insights, we propose fine-tuning the decoder to increase its robustness and smoothing the generated latent via controlled noise injection, thereby enhancing generation quality. Experiments demonstrate that LV-RAE significantly improves reconstruction fidelity while preserving the semantic abstraction and achieving strong generative quality. Our code is available at https://github.com/modyu-liu/LVRAE.

AI.tldr: LV-RAE augments semantic latent features from vision foundation models with missing low-level information via a representation autoencoder, improving reconstruction fidelity while preserving semantic distribution; it also debuts decoder fine-tuning and controlled noise injection to enhance robustness and generative quality.
AI.motivation: Semantic features from vision foundation models are semantically rich but lack low-level cues (color/texture), causing poor reconstruction fidelity and bottleneck for scaling latent diffusion models.
AI.method: Introduce LV-RAE: a representation autoencoder that supplements semantic features with low-level details; analyze decoder sensitivity to latent perturbations; fine-tune the decoder for robustness; apply controlled noise injection to smooth latent during generation.
AI.result: LV-RAE yields higher reconstruction fidelity without sacrificing semantic alignment and achieves improved generation quality; experiments demonstrate notable gains; code available at provided repository.
AI.conclusion: Augmenting semantic latent representations with controlled low-level information via LV-RAE resolves the fidelity gap in LDMs and, with a robust decoder and noise-based latent smoothing, enhances generative performance while maintaining semantic abstraction.

title: Learning in Context, Guided by Choice: A Reward-Free Paradigm for Reinforcement Learning with Transformers
summary: In-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) leverages the in-context learning capabilities of transformer models (TMs) to efficiently generalize to unseen sequential decision-making tasks without parameter updates. However, existing ICRL methods rely on explicit reward signals during pretraining, which limits their applicability when rewards are ambiguous, hard to specify, or costly to obtain. To overcome this limitation, we propose a new learning paradigm, In-Context Preference-based Reinforcement Learning (ICPRL), in which both pretraining and deployment rely solely on preference feedback, eliminating the need for reward supervision. We study two variants that differ in the granularity of feedback: Immediate Preference-based RL (I-PRL) with per-step preferences, and Trajectory Preference-based RL (T-PRL) with trajectory-level comparisons. We first show that supervised pretraining, a standard approach in ICRL, remains effective under preference-only context datasets, demonstrating the feasibility of in-context reinforcement learning using only preference signals. To further improve data efficiency, we introduce alternative preference-native frameworks for I-PRL and T-PRL that directly optimize TM policies from preference data without requiring reward signals nor optimal action labels.Experiments on dueling bandits, navigation, and continuous control tasks demonstrate that ICPRL enables strong in-context generalization to unseen tasks, achieving performance comparable to ICRL methods trained with full reward supervision.

AI.tldr: ICPRL enables in-context reinforcement learning using only preference feedback (no rewards), showing competitive performance to reward-supervised ICRL across tasks; introduces two variants (I-PRL, per-step; T-PRL, trajectory-level) and preference-native optimization methods.
AI.motivation: ICRL typically relies on explicit reward signals during pretraining, which limits applicability when rewards are ambiguous, hard to specify, or costly to obtain. The paper seeks a reward-free alternative by leveraging preference feedback for both pretraining and deployment.
AI.method: Study two variants: Immediate Preference-based RL (I-PRL) with per-step preferences and Trajectory Preference-based RL (T-PRL) with trajectory-level comparisons. First demonstrate that supervised pretraining remains effective with preference-only context datasets. Then introduce preference-native frameworks that optimize transformer policies directly from preference data without rewards or optimal action labels. Evaluate on dueling bandits, navigation, and continuous control tasks to test in-context generalization to unseen tasks.
AI.result: ICPRL enables strong in-context generalization to unseen tasks with performance comparable to ICRL methods trained with full reward supervision. Preference-only pretraining is feasible, and the proposed preference-native approaches further data efficiency, achieving competitive results across domains (dueling bandits, navigation, continuous control).
AI.conclusion: Preference-based in-context reinforcement learning is a viable, data-efficient paradigm, demonstrating that reward supervision can be eliminated from both pretraining and deployment while maintaining strong task generalization. The work proposes practical variants and methods for learning from preferences alone.

title: Revisiting [CLS] and Patch Token Interaction in Vision Transformers
comment: To be published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: Vision Transformers have emerged as powerful, scalable and versatile representation learners. To capture both global and local features, a learnable [CLS] class token is typically prepended to the input sequence of patch tokens. Despite their distinct nature, both token types are processed identically throughout the model. In this work, we investigate the friction between global and local feature learning under different pre-training strategies by analyzing the interactions between class and patch tokens. Our analysis reveals that standard normalization layers introduce an implicit differentiation between these token types. Building on this insight, we propose specialized processing paths that selectively disentangle the computational flow of class and patch tokens, particularly within normalization layers and early query-key-value projections. This targeted specialization leads to significantly improved patch representation quality for dense prediction tasks. Our experiments demonstrate segmentation performance gains of over 2 mIoU points on standard benchmarks, while maintaining strong classification accuracy. The proposed modifications introduce only an 8% increase in parameters, with no additional computational overhead. Through comprehensive ablations, we provide insights into which architectural components benefit most from specialization and how our approach generalizes across model scales and learning frameworks.

AI.tldr: Disentangle class and patch token flows in Vision Transformers through specialized normalization and early QKV projections, yielding over 2 mIoU segmentation gains with ~8% more parameters and no extra compute.
AI.motivation: Address the friction between global (CLS) and local (patch) feature learning in ViTs, where standard normalization homogenizes token types and hinders dense-prediction performance.
AI.method: Introduce specialized processing paths that selectively disentangle the computational flow of class and patch tokens, focusing on normalization layers and early query-key-value projections to separate their flows.
AI.result: Achieves segmentation gains >2 mIoU on standard benchmarks while maintaining classification accuracy; parameters increase by ~8% with no additional compute overhead; ablations show which components benefit most and results generalize across model scales and learning frameworks.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling token flows via targeted architectural specialization improves patch representation quality for dense prediction tasks with modest parameter overhead and robust generalization.

title: Constraint-Aware Generative Auto-bidding via Pareto-Prioritized Regret Optimization
summary: Auto-bidding systems aim to maximize marketing value while satisfying strict efficiency constraints such as Target Cost-Per-Action (CPA). Although Decision Transformers provide powerful sequence modeling capabilities, applying them to this constrained setting encounters two challenges: 1) standard Return-to-Go conditioning causes state aliasing by neglecting the cost dimension, preventing precise resource pacing; and 2) standard regression forces the policy to mimic average historical behaviors, thereby limiting the capacity to optimize performance toward the constraint boundary. To address these challenges, we propose PRO-Bid, a constraint-aware generative auto-bidding framework based on two synergistic mechanisms: 1) Constraint-Decoupled Pareto Representation (CDPR) decomposes global constraints into recursive cost and value contexts to restore resource perception, while reweighting trajectories based on the Pareto frontier to focus on high-efficiency data; and 2) Counterfactual Regret Optimization (CRO) facilitates active improvement by utilizing a global outcome predictor to identify superior counterfactual actions. By treating these high-utility outcomes as weighted regression targets, the model transcends historical averages to approach the optimal constraint boundary. Extensive experiments on two public benchmarks and online A/B tests demonstrate that PRO-Bid achieves superior constraint satisfaction and value acquisition compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: PRO-Bid is a constraint-aware generative auto-bidding framework that uses Constraint-Decoupled Pareto Representation (CDPR) to decompose global constraints into recursive cost and value contexts and reweight trajectories by the Pareto frontier, and Counterfactual Regret Optimization (CRO) with a global outcome predictor to identify superior counterfactual actions, achieving better constraint satisfaction and value on benchmarks and online tests.
AI.motivation: Auto-bidding under strict efficiency constraints (e.g., Target CPA) suffers from state aliasing (cost not modeled in decision process) and regression to historical averages, hindering resource pacing and optimization toward the constraint boundary.
AI.method: Integrate two mechanisms: CDPR to decouple constraints into recursive cost/value contexts and reweighting toward high-efficiency data; CRO guided by a global outcome predictor to find better counterfactual actions, treating high-utility outcomes as weighted regression targets.
AI.result: Empirical results on two public benchmarks and online A/B tests show improved constraint satisfaction and value acquisition relative to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: PRO-Bid provides a principled framework for constraint-aware auto-bidding, enabling closer adherence to constraints while maximizing value, and demonstrates the effectiveness of combining constraint decomposition with counterfactual regret optimization.

title: Deep Learning-Based Fixation Type Prediction for Quality Assurance in Digital Pathology
summary:
Accurate annotation of fixation type is a critical step in slide preparation for pathology laboratories. However, this manual process is prone to
  errors, impacting downstream analyses and diagnostic accuracy. Existing methods for verifying formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE), and frozen
  section (FS) fixation types typically require full-resolution whole-slide images (WSIs), limiting scalability for high-throughput quality control.
  We propose a deep-learning model to predict fixation types using low-resolution, pre-scan thumbnail images. The model was trained on WSIs from
  the TUM Institute of Pathology (n=1,200, Leica GT450DX) and evaluated on a class-balanced subset of The Cancer Genome Atlas dataset (TCGA, n=8,800,
  Leica AT2), as well as on class-balanced datasets from Augsburg (n=695 [392 FFPE, 303 FS], Philips UFS) and Regensburg (n=202, 3DHISTECH P1000).
  Our model achieves an AUROC of 0.88 on TCGA, outperforming comparable pre-scan methods by 4.8%. It also achieves AUROCs of 0.72 on Regensburg and
  Augsburg slides, underscoring challenges related to scanner-induced domain shifts. Furthermore, the model processes each slide in 21 ms, $400\times$
  faster than existing high-magnification, full-resolution methods, enabling rapid, high-throughput processing.
  This approach provides an efficient solution for detecting labelling errors without relying on high-magnification scans, offering a valuable tool for
  quality control in high-throughput pathology workflows. Future work will improve and evaluate the model's generalisation to additional scanner
  types. Our findings suggest that this method can increase accuracy and efficiency in digital pathology workflows and may be extended to other
  low-resolution slide annotations.

AI.tldr: Low-resolution thumbnail-based deep learning model predicts fixation type (FFPE/FS) in pathology slides, achieving AUROC 0.88 on TCGA and 0.72 on Augsburg/Regensburg, with 21 ms per slide (≈400x faster than full-resolution methods), enabling high-throughput QC but facing scanner-domain shifts.
AI.motivation: Address errors in manual fixation-type annotation and scale QC without requiring full-resolution WSIs, enabling rapid, high-throughput quality control in digital pathology.
AI.method: Train a deep-learning model on low-resolution pre-scan thumbnail images from WSIs (training on TUM dataset, n=1,200; Leica GT450DX) and evaluate on TCGA (n=8,800; Leica AT2) and on Augsburg (n=695; 392 FFPE, 303 FS) and Regensburg (n=202; 3DHISTECH P1000) datasets. Compare to pre-scan baselines; report AUROC and processing speed; assess domain shift effects across scanners.
AI.result: AUROC 0.88 on TCGA, outperforming comparable pre-scan methods by 4.8%. AUROCs of 0.72 on Regensburg and Augsburg. Processing speed: 21 ms per slide, ~400× faster than high-magnification, full-resolution methods.
AI.conclusion: Low-resolution thumbnail-based analysis provides an efficient tool for detecting labeling errors in high-throughput pathology workflows; generalization to additional scanner types remains an area for future work; potential to extend to other low-resolution slide annotations.

title: Inverting Data Transformations via Diffusion Sampling
summary: We study the problem of transformation inversion on general Lie groups: a datum is transformed by an unknown group element, and the goal is to recover an inverse transformation that maps it back to the original data distribution. Such unknown transformations arise widely in machine learning and scientific modeling, where they can significantly distort observations. We take a probabilistic view and model the posterior over transformations as a Boltzmann distribution defined by an energy function on data space. To sample from this posterior, we introduce a diffusion process on Lie groups that keeps all updates on-manifold and only requires computations in the associated Lie algebra. Our method, Transformation-Inverting Energy Diffusion (TIED), relies on a new trivialized target-score identity that enables efficient score-based sampling of the transformation posterior. As a key application, we focus on test-time equivariance, where the objective is to improve the robustness of pretrained neural networks to input transformations. Experiments on image homographies and PDE symmetries demonstrate that TIED can restore transformed inputs to the training distribution at test time, showing improved performance over strong canonicalization and sampling baselines. Code is available at https://github.com/jw9730/tied.

AI.tldr: Introduces Transformation-Inverting Energy Diffusion (TIED), a diffusion-based method on Lie groups to infer and sample inverse transformations that map transformed data back to the training distribution. It uses a Boltzmann energy posterior and a trivialized target-score identity for efficient score-based sampling in the Lie algebra, enabling on-manifold updates. Demonstrates improved test-time equivariance on image homographies and PDE symmetries, outperforming canonicalization and sampling baselines. Code released.
AI.motivation: Unknown, potentially wide-ranging transformations on data lie in Lie groups and distort observations. A probabilistic framework is needed to infer inverse transformations that re-map data to the training distribution, with updates that respect the manifold structure and leverage algebraic computations for efficiency.
AI.method: Model the posterior over transformations as a Boltzmann distribution defined by an energy function on data space. Introduce a diffusion process on Lie groups that keeps all updates on-manifold and reduces computations to the Lie algebra. Propose Transformation-Inverting Energy Diffusion (TIED) and a new trivialized target-score identity to enable efficient score-based sampling of the transformation posterior.
AI.result: Empirical results show TIED can restore transformed inputs to the training distribution at test time and improve performance over strong canonicalization and sampling baselines.
AI.conclusion: TIED provides a principled and efficient framework for transformation inversion on Lie groups, enhancing model robustness to input transformations at test time; code is publicly available.

title: WiFlow: A Lightweight WiFi-based Continuous Human Pose Estimation Network with Spatio-Temporal Feature Decoupling
summary: Human pose estimation is fundamental to intelligent perception in the Internet of Things (IoT), enabling applications ranging from smart healthcare to human-computer interaction. While WiFi-based methods have gained traction, they often struggle with continuous motion and high computational overhead. This work presents WiFlow, a novel framework for continuous human pose estimation using WiFi signals. Unlike vision-based approaches such as two-dimensional deep residual networks that treat Channel State Information (CSI) as images, WiFlow employs an encoder-decoder architecture. The encoder captures spatio-temporal features of CSI using temporal and asymmetric convolutions, preserving the original sequential structure of signals. It then refines keypoint features of human bodies to be tracked and capture their structural dependencies via axial attention. The decoder subsequently maps the encoded high-dimensional features into keypoint coordinates. Trained on a self-collected dataset of 360,000 synchronized CSI-pose samples from 5 subjects performing continuous sequences of 8 daily activities, WiFlow achieves a Percentage of Correct Keypoints (PCK) of 97.00% at a threshold of 20% (PCK@20) and 99.48% at PCK@50, with a mean per-joint position error of 0.008m. With only 4.82M parameters, WiFlow significantly reduces model complexity and computational cost, establishing a new performance baseline for practical WiFi-based human pose estimation. Our code and datasets are available at https://github.com/DY2434/WiFlow-WiFi-Pose-Estimation-with-Spatio-Temporal-Decoupling.git.

AI.tldr: Efficient WiFi-based pose estimator using an encoder-decoder with spatio-temporal decoupling; achieves high PCK and low MPJPE on a self-collected dataset with 4.82M parameters.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of vision-based pose estimation and prior CSI methods, enabling continuous, low-cost, privacy-preserving pose tracking in IoT contexts.
AI.method: An encoder with temporal and asymmetric convolutions captures spatio-temporal CSI while preserving sequential structure. Keypoint features are refined and structured dependencies are modeled via axial attention. A decoder maps encoded features to 3D/2D keypoint coordinates. Trained on 360k CSI-pose samples from 5 subjects performing 8 activities; ~4.82M parameters.
AI.result: PCK@20 = 97.00%, PCK@50 = 99.48%, MPJPE = 0.008 m. Demonstrates strong accuracy with relatively small model, establishing a new practical baseline. Code and dataset released.
AI.conclusion: WiFlow provides an efficient, accurate WiFi-based solution for continuous pose estimation, suitable for IoT deployments; further validation across more subjects/environments would strengthen generalization.

title: When Do Multi-Agent Systems Outperform? Analysing the Learning Efficiency of Agentic Systems
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a crucial method for training or fine-tuning large language models (LLMs), enabling adaptive, task-specific optimizations through interactive feedback. Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL), in particular, offers a promising avenue by decomposing complex tasks into specialized subtasks learned by distinct interacting agents, potentially enhancing the ability and efficiency of LLM systems. However, theoretical insights regarding when and why MARL outperforms Single-Agent RL (SARL) remain limited, creating uncertainty in selecting the appropriate RL framework. In this paper, we address this critical gap by rigorously analyzing the comparative sample efficiency of MARL and SARL within the context of LLM. Leveraging the Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) framework, we formally define SARL and MARL setups for LLMs, derive explicit sample complexity bounds, and systematically characterize how task decomposition and alignment influence learning efficiency. Our results demonstrate that MARL improves sample complexity when tasks naturally decompose into independent subtasks, whereas dependent subtasks diminish MARL's comparative advantage. Additionally, we introduce and analyze the concept of task alignment, quantifying the trade-offs when enforcing independent task decomposition despite potential misalignments. These theoretical insights clarify empirical inconsistencies and provide practical criteria for deploying MARL strategies effectively in complex LLM scenarios.

AI.tldr: PAC-based analysis showing MARL can improve sample efficiency over SARL when tasks naturally decompose into independent subtasks; dependency among subtasks reduces MARL's advantage; introduces task alignment as a trade-off factor; provides criteria for deploying MARL in LLM contexts.
AI.motivation: There is limited theoretical understanding of when MARL outperforms SARL for large language models. A formal PAC-based analysis is needed to quantify sample efficiency and guide framework choice, taking into account task decomposition and alignment.
AI.method: Formalize SARL and MARL setups for LLMs within the Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) framework. Derive explicit sample complexity bounds under varying task-decomposition structures. Introduce and analyze 'task alignment' to quantify trade-offs when enforcing independent task decomposition. Evaluate conditions under which MARL yields better sample efficiency than SARL, and when it does not.
AI.result: MARL offers improved sample complexity when tasks decompose into independent subtasks; if subtasks are dependent, MARL loses its edge. The work introduces 'task alignment' to quantify trade-offs and provides theoretical criteria to decide when MARL is preferable in LLM scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Theoretical insights reconcile empirical inconsistencies and yield practical guidelines for deploying MARL with LLMs, highlighting the role of task decomposition and alignment in determining sample efficiency.

title: A Machine Learning accelerated geophysical fluid solver
summary: Machine learning methods have been successful in many areas, like image classification and natural language processing. However, it still needs to be determined how to apply ML to areas with mathematical constraints, like solving PDEs. Among various approaches to applying ML techniques to solving PDEs, the data-driven discretization method presents a promising way of accelerating and improving existing PDE solver on structured grids where it predicts the coefficients of quasi-linear stencils for computing values or derivatives of a function at given positions. It can improve the accuracy and stability of low-resolution simulation compared with using traditional finite difference or finite volume schemes. Meanwhile, it can also benefit from traditional numerical schemes like achieving conservation law by adapting finite volume type formulations. In this thesis, we have implemented the shallow water equation and Euler equation classic solver under a different framework. Experiments show that our classic solver performs much better than the Pyclaw solver. Then we propose four different deep neural networks for the ML-based solver. The results indicate that two of these approaches could output satisfactory solutions.

AI.tldr: A data-driven discretization framework using ML to predict quasi-linear stencil coefficients for PDE solvers, improving accuracy and stability at low resolution; applied to shallow water and Euler equations, outperforming PyClaw in experiments, with two of four DNN architectures yielding satisfactory results.
AI.motivation: To integrate machine learning into PDE solvers by learning discretization operators that respect the underlying physics (e.g., conservation) on structured grids, enabling faster and more accurate simulations.
AI.method: Develop data-driven discretization that predicts stencil coefficients for quasi-linear finite-difference/finite-volume schemes. Implement classic solvers for shallow water and Euler equations in a new framework; compare performance to PyClaw. Propose four DNN architectures for the ML-based solver and evaluate their outputs.
AI.result: The classic solver outperforms PyClaw; among four proposed DNN-based solvers, two architectures produce satisfactory solutions.
AI.conclusion: ML-based data-driven discretization shows promise for PDE solvers, potentially improving accuracy and stability at low resolutions while preserving conservation properties; performance depends on architecture, with some designs delivering viable solutions.

title: Noise Stability of Transformer Models
summary: Understanding simplicity biases in deep learning offers a promising path toward developing reliable AI. A common metric for this, inspired by Boolean function analysis, is average sensitivity, which captures a model's robustness to single-token perturbations. We argue that average sensitivity has two key limitations: it lacks a natural generalization to real-valued domains and fails to explain the "junta-like" input dependence we empirically observe in modern LLMs. To address these limitations, we propose noise stability as a more comprehensive simplicity metric. Noise stability expresses a model's robustness to correlated noise applied to all input coordinates simultaneously. We provide a theoretical analysis of noise stability for single-layer attention and ReLU MLP layers and tackle the multi-layer propagation problem with a covariance interval propagation approach. Building on this theory, we develop a practical noise stability regularization method. Experiments on algorithmic and next-token-prediction tasks show that our regularizer consistently catalyzes grokking and accelerates training by approximately $35\%$ and $75\%$ respectively. Our results sculpt a new connection between signal propagation in neural networks and interpretability, with noise stability emerging as a powerful tool for understanding and improving modern Transformers.

AI.tldr: Proposes noise stability as a universal simplicity metric for deep nets, addressing average sensitivity limitations; theory for single-layer attention and ReLU MLPs, covariance interval propagation for multi-layer nets; introduces a regularizer that speeds up grokking and training in experiments (~35% and ~75%); links signal propagation to interpretability in Transformers.
AI.motivation: Average sensitivity, inspired by Boolean function analysis, fails to generalize to real-valued inputs and cannot explain junta-like input dependence in modern LLMs; a more general metric capturing robustness to correlated input perturbations is needed.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of noise stability for single-layer attention and ReLU MLPs; covariance interval propagation to handle multi-layer propagation; development of a practical noise stability regularizer; empirical evaluation on algorithmic tasks and next-token prediction.
AI.result: Regularizer catalyzes grokking and accelerates training by ~35% and ~75% in their experiments; demonstrates that noise stability is a useful tool for understanding and improving Transformer's interpretability and training dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Noise stability provides a more comprehensive framework for assessing and improving simplicity/robustness in neural networks, with a concrete methodology to compute and regularize it, yielding practical gains and linking signal propagation to interpretability.

title: ALIVE: Animate Your World with Lifelike Audio-Video Generation
summary: Video generation is rapidly evolving towards unified audio-video generation. In this paper, we present ALIVE, a generation model that adapts a pretrained Text-to-Video (T2V) model to Sora-style audio-video generation and animation. In particular, the model unlocks the Text-to-Video&Audio (T2VA) and Reference-to-Video&Audio (animation) capabilities compared to the T2V foundation models. To support the audio-visual synchronization and reference animation, we augment the popular MMDiT architecture with a joint audio-video branch which includes TA-CrossAttn for temporally-aligned cross-modal fusion and UniTemp-RoPE for precise audio-visual alignment. Meanwhile, a comprehensive data pipeline consisting of audio-video captioning, quality control, etc., is carefully designed to collect high-quality finetuning data. Additionally, we introduce a new benchmark to perform a comprehensive model test and comparison. After continue pretraining and finetuning on million-level high-quality data, ALIVE demonstrates outstanding performance, consistently outperforming open-source models and matching or surpassing state-of-the-art commercial solutions. With detailed recipes and benchmarks, we hope ALIVE helps the community develop audio-video generation models more efficiently. Official page: https://github.com/FoundationVision/Alive.

AI.tldr: ALIVE adapts a pretrained Text-to-Video model to enable Text-to-Video&Audio (T2VA) and Reference-to-Video&Audio (animation) in Sora-style generation; achieves audio-visual synchronization and reference animation via a joint audio-video extension to MMDiT, adds TA-CrossAttn for temporal cross-modal fusion and UniTemp-RoPE for precise alignment; establishes a data pipeline for high-quality finetuning, introduces a new benchmark, and after large-scale finetuning outperforms open-source models and equals or exceeds some commercial SOTA solutions.
AI.motivation: Address the need for unified audio-video generation that combines video synthesis with synchronized audio and reference-based animation beyond existing Text-to-Video (T2V) models.
AI.method: Augment the MMDiT architecture with a joint audio-video branch incorporating TA-CrossAttn for temporally-aligned cross-modal fusion and UniTemp-RoPE for precise audio-visual alignment. Develop a comprehensive data pipeline including audio-video captioning and quality control to curate high-quality finetuning data. Perform continued pretraining and finetuning on million-scale data, followed by evaluation on a new benchmark.
AI.result: ALIVE consistently outperforms open-source models and matches or surpasses state-of-the-art commercial solutions on the proposed benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ALIVE provides concrete recipes and benchmarks to accelerate community development of audio-video generation models and demonstrates the feasibility of unified T2V+Audio and reference-animated generation.

title: Trust-Based Incentive Mechanisms in Semi-Decentralized Federated Learning Systems
comment: To appear in the ICBTA 2025 Conference Proceedings and published as a volume of Lecture Notes in Networks and Systems by Springer
summary: In federated learning (FL), decentralized model training allows multi-ple participants to collaboratively improve a shared machine learning model without exchanging raw data. However, ensuring the integrity and reliability of the system is challenging due to the presence of potentially malicious or faulty nodes that can degrade the model's performance. This paper proposes a novel trust-based incentive mechanism designed to evaluate and reward the quality of contributions in FL systems. By dynamically assessing trust scores based on fac-tors such as data quality, model accuracy, consistency, and contribution fre-quency, the system encourages honest participation and penalizes unreliable or malicious behavior. These trust scores form the basis of an incentive mechanism that rewards high-trust nodes with greater participation opportunities and penal-ties for low-trust participants. We further explore the integration of blockchain technology and smart contracts to automate the trust evaluation and incentive distribution processes, ensuring transparency and decentralization. Our proposed theoretical framework aims to create a more robust, fair, and transparent FL eco-system, reducing the risks posed by untrustworthy participants.

AI.tldr: A trust-based incentive mechanism for federated learning using trust scores and blockchain to reward high-trust participants and penalize low-trust ones; aims to boost robustness, fairness, and transparency.
AI.motivation: FL's decentralization makes it vulnerable to malicious/faulty nodes; need to align incentives, ensure data/model integrity, and enable transparent incentive distribution.
AI.method: Proposes dynamic trust scores based on data quality, model accuracy, consistency, and contribution frequency; uses blockchain and smart contracts to automate trust evaluation and incentive distribution; provides a theoretical framework rather than empirical evaluation.
AI.result: Theoretical framework and design; outlines architecture and expected benefits; no empirical validation in abstract.
AI.conclusion: Aims to create robust, fair, and transparent FL ecosystem and mitigate risks from untrustworthy participants.

title: OneVision-Encoder: Codec-Aligned Sparsity as a Foundational Principle for Multimodal Intelligence
summary:
Hypothesis. Artificial general intelligence is, at its core, a compression problem. Effective compression demands resonance: deep learning scales best when its architecture aligns with the fundamental structure of the data. These are the fundamental principles. Yet, modern vision architectures have strayed from these truths: visual signals are highly redundant, while discriminative information, the surprise, is sparse. Current models process dense pixel grids uniformly, wasting vast compute on static background rather than focusing on the predictive residuals that define motion and meaning. We argue that to solve visual understanding, we must align our architectures with the information-theoretic principles of video, i.e., Codecs.
  Method. OneVision-Encoder encodes video by compressing predictive visual structure into semantic meaning. By adopting Codec Patchification, OV-Encoder abandons uniform computation to focus exclusively on the 3.1%-25% of regions rich in signal entropy. To unify spatial and temporal reasoning under irregular token layouts, OneVision-Encoder employs a shared 3D RoPE and is trained with a large-scale cluster discrimination objective over more than one million semantic concepts, jointly capturing object permanence and motion dynamics.
  Evidence. The results validate our core hypothesis: efficiency and accuracy are not a trade-off; they are positively correlated. When integrated into LLM, it consistently outperforms strong vision backbones such as Qwen3-ViT and SigLIP2 across 16 image, video, and document understanding benchmarks, despite using substantially fewer visual tokens and pretraining data. Notably, on video understanding tasks, OV-Encoder achieves an average improvement of 4.1% over Qwen3-ViT. Codec-aligned, patch-level sparsity is a foundational principle, enabling OV-Encoder as a scalable engine for next-generation visual generalists.

AI.tldr: A codec-inspired vision model (OneVision-Encoder) achieves efficiency and accuracy via patch-level sparsity that focuses on high-entropy regions, integrating predictive visual structure into semantic meaning.
AI.motivation: Address the inefficiency of dense pixel processing by aligning vision architectures with information-theoretic principles of video/compression to enable scalable, generalizable visual understanding.
AI.method: OneVision-Encoder encodes video by compressing predictive structure into semantic meaning. Codec Patchification concentrates computation on 3.1%-25% of regions rich in signal entropy. It uses a shared 3D RoPE to handle irregular token layouts and is trained with a large-scale cluster discrimination objective over >1,000,000 semantic concepts to capture object permanence and motion dynamics.
AI.result: Empirical results show efficiency and accuracy can improve together: OV-Encoder outperforms strong backbones (e.g., Qwen3-ViT, SigLIP2) across 16 image/video/document benchmarks using fewer visual tokens and less pretraining data, with an average 4.1% improvement on video tasks.
AI.conclusion: Codec-aligned, patch-level sparsity is a foundational principle for next-generation visual generalists, enabling OV-Encoder as a scalable engine for visual general intelligence.

title: Grokking in Linear Models for Logistic Regression
summary: Grokking, the phenomenon of delayed generalization, is often attributed to the depth and compositional structure of deep neural networks. We study grokking in one of the simplest possible settings: the learning of a linear model with logistic loss for binary classification on data that are linearly (and max margin) separable about the origin. We investigate three testing regimes: (1) test data drawn from the same distribution as the training data, in which case grokking is not observed; (2) test data concentrated around the margin, in which case grokking is observed; and (3) adversarial test data generated via projected gradient descent (PGD) attacks, in which case grokking is also observed. We theoretically show that the implicit bias of gradient descent induces a three-phase learning process-population-dominated, support-vector-dominated unlearning, and support-vector-dominated generalization-during which delayed generalization can arise. Our analysis further relates the emergence of grokking to asymmetries in the data, both in the number of examples per class and in the distribution of support vectors across classes, and yields a characterization of the grokking time. We experimentally validate our theory by planting different distributions of population points and support vectors, and by analyzing accuracy curves and hyperplane dynamics. Overall, our results demonstrate that grokking does not require depth or representation learning, and can emerge even in linear models through the dynamics of the bias term.

AI.tldr: Grokking can occur in a simple linear model with logistic loss due to gradient descent bias, yielding a three-phase learning process and delayed generalization under certain test distributions; depth is not required.
AI.motivation: To determine whether grokking—delayed generalization—requires depth/representation learning, or can arise in simple linear models, and to identify the mechanisms and data conditions that produce it.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of gradient-descent bias for a linear classifier on origin-centered, linearly separable data under logistic loss; identification of three learning phases (population-dominated, unlearning of population points, and support-vector-dominated generalization); derivation of grokking time; experimental validation by engineering distributions of population points and support vectors and by evaluating accuracy under in-distribution, margin-proximate, and adversarial (PGD) test regimes.
AI.result: Grokking is observed in margin-proximate and adversarial test regimes but not under in-distribution testing; the three-phase dynamics explain delayed generalization; grokking time depends on asymmetries in class counts and the distribution of support vectors; experimental planting of distributions validates the theory; depth is unnecessary for grokking in this setting.
AI.conclusion: Grokking does not require depth or representation learning; linear models with gradient-descent bias can exhibit delayed generalization, driven by margin and support-vector dynamics and modulated by data asymmetries; this broadens the scope of grokking and informs how training dynamics shape generalization.

title: Low-Light Video Enhancement with An Effective Spatial-Temporal Decomposition Paradigm
summary: Low-Light Video Enhancement (LLVE) seeks to restore dynamic or static scenes plagued by severe invisibility and noise. In this paper, we present an innovative video decomposition strategy that incorporates view-independent and view-dependent components to enhance the performance of LLVE. The framework is called View-aware Low-light Video Enhancement (VLLVE). We leverage dynamic cross-frame correspondences for the view-independent term (which primarily captures intrinsic appearance) and impose a scene-level continuity constraint on the view-dependent term (which mainly describes the shading condition) to achieve consistent and satisfactory decomposition results. To further ensure consistent decomposition, we introduce a dual-structure enhancement network featuring a cross-frame interaction mechanism. By supervising different frames simultaneously, this network encourages them to exhibit matching decomposition features. This mechanism can seamlessly integrate with encoder-decoder single-frame networks, incurring minimal additional parameter costs. Building upon VLLVE, we propose a more comprehensive decomposition strategy by introducing an additive residual term, resulting in VLLVE++. This residual term can simulate scene-adaptive degradations, which are difficult to model using a decomposition formulation for common scenes, thereby further enhancing the ability to capture the overall content of videos. In addition, VLLVE++ enables bidirectional learning for both enhancement and degradation-aware correspondence refinement (end-to-end manner), effectively increasing reliable correspondences while filtering out incorrect ones. Notably, VLLVE++ demonstrates strong capability in handling challenging cases, such as real-world scenes and videos with high dynamics. Extensive experiments are conducted on widely recognized LLVE benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Proposes View-aware Low-light Video Enhancement (VLLVE) with a decomposition into view-independent (intrinsic appearance) and view-dependent (shading/degradation) terms, enforced by cross-frame cues and scene continuity; adds an additive residual term in VLLVE++ for better degradation modeling and bidirectional learning of enhancement and correspondence refinement, improving robustness on real-world high-dynamic videos.
AI.motivation: LLVE is hindered by severe invisibility and noise; decomposing video into components that capture intrinsic appearance and shading/degradation can enable robust enhancement and temporal consistency; leveraging cross-frame information and scene-level continuity can improve decomposition fidelity and generalization to challenging scenes.
AI.method: Introduce a dual-structure enhancement network with a cross-frame interaction mechanism; dynamic cross-frame correspondences to capture view-independent term; a scene-level continuity constraint on the view-dependent term; supervision across multiple frames to enforce consistent decomposition; integrates with encoder-decoder single-frame networks with minimal extra params; extend to VLLVE++ by adding an additive residual term to model scene-adaptive degradations and enable bidirectional learning for enhancement and correspondence refinement.
AI.result: Authors claim strong performance on widely recognized LLVE benchmarks, with improved handling of real-world scenes and high dynamics; reported improvements in decomposition consistency, robust correspondences, and minimal parameter overhead; validated through extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: VLLVE offers a principled, decomposition-based approach for LLVE that achieves robust enhancement and temporal consistency; VLLVE++ extends this with a residual-driven component and bidirectional learning to better capture content and degradations, potentially improving generalization to challenging real-world videos.

title: TextResNet: Decoupling and Routing Optimization Signals in Compound AI Systems via Deep Residual Tuning
summary: Textual Gradient-style optimizers (TextGrad) enable gradient-like feedback propagation through compound AI systems. However, they do not work well for deep chains. The root cause of this limitation stems from the Semantic Entanglement problem in these extended workflows. In standard textual backpropagation, feedback signals mix local critiques with upstream contexts, leading to Attribution Ambiguity. To address this challenge, we propose TextResNet, a framework that reformulates the optimization process to achieve precise signal routing via four key innovations. Firstly, in the forward pass, it enforces Additive Semantic Deltas to preserve an Identity Highway for gradient flow. Secondly, in the backward pass, it introduces Semantic Gradient Decomposition via a Semantic Projector to disentangle feedback into causally independent subspaces. Thirdly, it implements Causal Routing, which routes projected signals to their specific components. Finally, it performs Density-Aware Optimization Scheduling to leverage the disentangled signals to dynamically allocate resources to key system bottlenecks. Our results show that TextResNet not only achieves superior performance compared to TextGrad, but also exhibits remarkable stability for agentic tasks in compound AI systems where baselines collapse. Code is available at https://github.com/JeanDiable/TextResNet.

AI.tldr: TextResNet reframes gradient-based feedback through deep compound AI systems to address Semantic Entanglement and Attribution Ambiguity by four design pillars that route and allocate gradient signals precisely, outperforming TextGrad and stabilizing agentic tasks; code available.
AI.motivation: To tackle the Semantic Entanglement problem that degrades textual gradient backpropagation across long AI pipelines. Existing TextGrad signals mix local critiques with upstream contexts, causing Attribution Ambiguity and instability in deep chains.
AI.method: Four innovations: (1) Additive Semantic Deltas in the forward pass to preserve an Identity Highway for gradient flow; (2) Semantic Gradient Decomposition via a Semantic Projector in the backward pass to disentangle feedback into causally independent subspaces; (3) Causal Routing to direct projected signals to their specific components; (4) Density-Aware Optimization Scheduling to dynamically allocate resources to bottlenecks using the disentangled signals.
AI.result: Empirical results claim TextResNet outperforms TextGrad and exhibits strong stability for agentic tasks in compound AI systems where baselines collapse; code is available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: TextResNet offers a modular, signal-routing framework that improves gradient-like optimization in complex AI pipelines; the authors emphasize robustness and practical applicability, with a need for broader empirical validation across domains.

title: TimeChat-Captioner: Scripting Multi-Scene Videos with Time-Aware and Structural Audio-Visual Captions
summary: This paper proposes Omni Dense Captioning, a novel task designed to generate continuous, fine-grained, and structured audio-visual narratives with explicit timestamps. To ensure dense semantic coverage, we introduce a six-dimensional structural schema to create "script-like" captions, enabling readers to vividly imagine the video content scene by scene, akin to a cinematographic screenplay. To facilitate research, we construct OmniDCBench, a high-quality, human-annotated benchmark, and propose SodaM, a unified metric that evaluates time-aware detailed descriptions while mitigating scene boundary ambiguity. Furthermore, we construct a training dataset, TimeChatCap-42K, and present TimeChat-Captioner-7B, a strong baseline trained via SFT and GRPO with task-specific rewards. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TimeChat-Captioner-7B achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing Gemini-2.5-Pro, while its generated dense descriptions significantly boost downstream capabilities in audio-visual reasoning (DailyOmni and WorldSense) and temporal grounding (Charades-STA). All datasets, models, and code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/yaolinli/TimeChat-Captioner.

AI.tldr: Introduces Omni Dense Captioning for continuous, time-stamped, script-like captions; develops OmniDCBench and SodaM; builds TimeChatCap-42K and TimeChat-Captioner-7B (SFT + GRPO) achieving SOTA and improving audio-visual reasoning and temporal grounding; datasets/models to be released on GitHub.
AI.motivation: Address the need for dense, fine-grained, time-aware narratives that comprehensively cover video content and support fine-grained reasoning and temporal localization.
AI.method: Propose a six-dimensional structural schema to generate script-like captions; construct OmniDCBench benchmark; introduce SodaM unified metric to evaluate time-aware detailed descriptions and mitigate scene boundary ambiguity; assemble TimeChatCap-42K dataset; train TimeChat-Captioner-7B using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and GRPO with task-specific rewards.
AI.result: TimeChat-Captioner-7B achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing Gemini-2.5-Pro; the dense descriptions boost downstream tasks in audio-visual reasoning (DailyOmni, WorldSense) and temporal grounding (Charades-STA).
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the effectiveness of structured, time-aware dense captions for enhanced video understanding and reasoning; all datasets, models, and code will be publicly released.

title: Interaction-Grounded Learning for Contextual Markov Decision Processes with Personalized Feedback
summary: In this paper, we study Interaction-Grounded Learning (IGL) [Xie et al., 2021], a paradigm designed for realistic scenarios where the learner receives indirect feedback generated by an unknown mechanism, rather than explicit numerical rewards. While prior work on IGL provides efficient algorithms with provable guarantees, those results are confined to single-step settings, restricting their applicability to modern sequential decision-making systems such as multi-turn Large Language Model (LLM) deployments. To bridge this gap, we propose a computationally efficient algorithm that achieves a sublinear regret guarantee for contextual episodic Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) with personalized feedback. Technically, we extend the reward-estimator construction of Zhang et al. [2024a] from the single-step to the multi-step setting, addressing the unique challenges of decoding latent rewards under MDPs. Building on this estimator, we design an Inverse-Gap-Weighting (IGW) algorithm for policy optimization. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in learning personalized objectives from multi-turn interactions through experiments on both a synthetic episodic MDP and a real-world user booking dataset.

AI.tldr: Proposes a multi-turn Interaction-Grounded Learning (IGL) framework for contextual episodic MDPs with personalized feedback; extends single-step reward estimation to multi-step, introduces Inverse-Gap-Weighting (IGW) for policy optimization, achieving sublinear regret; validated on synthetic and real-world booking data.
AI.motivation: Overcome the limitation of existing IGL that handles only single-step feedback in realistic sequential decision-making; enable learning of personalized objectives in multi-turn interactions such as LLM deployments.
AI.method: Extend the reward-estimator construction from single-step IGL to multi-step episodic MDPs; address decoding latent rewards under MDPs; design and implement an Inverse-Gap-Weighting (IGW) algorithm for policy optimization with personalized feedback; ensure computational efficiency and provide regret guarantees.
AI.result: The method achieves sublinear regret in contextual episodic MDPs with personalized feedback; experiments on a synthetic episodic MDP and a real-world user booking dataset demonstrate effective learning of personalized objectives from multi-turn interactions.
AI.conclusion: This work closes the gap for IGL in sequential settings by providing a scalable estimator and IGW algorithm that learn personalized objectives from multi-turn interactions, with promising implications for adaptive systems like LLM deployments.

title: Towards Understanding Multimodal Fine-Tuning: Spatial Features
summary: Contemporary Vision-Language Models (VLMs) achieve strong performance on a wide range of tasks by pairing a vision encoder with a pre-trained language model, fine-tuned for visual-text inputs. Yet despite these gains, it remains unclear how language backbone representations adapt during multimodal training and when vision-specific capabilities emerge. In this work, we present the first mechanistic analysis of VLM adaptation. Using stage-wise model diffing, a technique that isolates representational changes introduced during multimodal fine-tuning, we reveal how a language model learns to "see". We first identify vision-preferring features that emerge or reorient during fine-tuning. We then show that a selective subset of these features reliably encodes spatial relations, revealed through controlled shifts to spatial prompts. Finally, we trace the causal activation of these features to a small group of attention heads. Our findings show that stage-wise model diffing reveals when and where spatially grounded multimodal features arise. It also provides a clearer view of modality fusion by showing how visual grounding reshapes features that were previously text-only. This methodology enhances the interpretability of multimodal training and provides a foundation for understanding and refining how pretrained language models acquire vision-grounded capabilities.

AI.tldr: Stage-wise model diffing reveals how vision grounding emerges in vision-language models: a subset of language features becomes vision-preferring, encodes spatial relations, and is causally driven by a small set of attention heads, reshaping text-only representations during multimodal fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Understand how pretrained language models adapt to multimodal training and when vision-specific capabilities emerge, to improve interpretability and guide refinement of VLMs.
AI.method: Apply stage-wise model diffing to isolate representational changes during multimodal fine-tuning; identify vision-preferring features; test spatial prompts to reveal spatial relation encoding; trace causal activation to a small group of attention heads.
AI.result: Found vision-preferring features that emerge or reorient during fine-tuning; a selective subset encodes spatial relations, revealed by spatial prompts; causal activation traced to a small set of attention heads; stage-wise diffing locates when/where these features arise and shows how visual grounding reshapes previous text-only features.
AI.conclusion: Stage-wise diffing provides a mechanistic view of multimodal adaptation, enabling interpretable analysis of how vision grounding emerges in language representations and guiding future refinements of VLMs.

title: Fast Flow Matching based Conditional Independence Tests for Causal Discovery
summary: Constraint-based causal discovery methods require a large number of conditional independence (CI) tests, which severely limits their practical applicability due to high computational complexity. Therefore, it is crucial to design an algorithm that accelerates each individual test. To this end, we propose the Flow Matching-based Conditional Independence Test (FMCIT). The proposed test leverages the high computational efficiency of flow matching and requires the model to be trained only once throughout the entire causal discovery procedure, substantially accelerating causal discovery. According to numerical experiments, FMCIT effectively controls type-I error and maintains high testing power under the alternative hypothesis, even in the presence of high-dimensional conditioning sets. In addition, we further integrate FMCIT into a two-stage guided PC skeleton learning framework, termed GPC-FMCIT, which combines fast screening with guided, budgeted refinement using FMCIT. This design yields explicit bounds on the number of CI queries while maintaining high statistical power. Experiments on synthetic and real-world causal discovery tasks demonstrate favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-offs over existing CI testing methods and PC variants.

AI.tldr: FMCIT introduces a single-train, flow-matching based conditional independence test to accelerate constraint-based causal discovery, paired with a two-stage skeleton learning framework (GPC-FMCIT) that provides explicit CI-query bounds. It achieves strong type-I error control and power, even with high-dimensional conditioning, and shows favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-offs on synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: Constraint-based causal discovery relies on many conditional independence tests, which are computationally expensive. There is a need for faster CI testing and budgeted CI-query strategies that maintain statistical power, especially with large conditioning sets.
AI.method: Develop FMCIT (Flow Matching-based Conditional Independence Test) that uses efficient flow-matching to perform CI testing while training a single model to be reused across the procedure. Integrate FMCIT into a two-stage guided PC skeleton learning framework, GPC-FMCIT, which first does fast screening and then budgeted, guided refinement using FMCIT. The framework provides explicit bounds on the number of CI queries.
AI.result: FMCIT controls Type-I error and preserves high power under the alternative, even with high-dimensional conditioning sets. GPC-FMCIT achieves efficient computation with explicit CI-query bounds and competitive accuracy compared to existing CI tests and PC variants. Experiments on synthetic and real-world data demonstrate favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: Flow-matching-based CI testing enables substantial acceleration of constraint-based causal discovery by reusing a single trained model and leveraging guided skeleton refinement, yielding efficient, powerful causal discovery with provable expectations on CI query usage.

title: Zero-shot System for Automatic Body Region Detection for Volumetric CT and MR Images
summary: Reliable identification of anatomical body regions is a prerequisite for many automated medical imaging workflows, yet existing solutions remain heavily dependent on unreliable DICOM metadata. Current solutions mainly use supervised learning, which limits their applicability in many real-world scenarios. In this work, we investigate whether body region detection in volumetric CT and MR images can be achieved in a fully zero-shot manner by using knowledge embedded in large pre-trained foundation models. We propose and systematically evaluate three training-free pipelines: (1) a segmentation-driven rule-based system leveraging pre-trained multi-organ segmentation models, (2) a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) guided by radiologist-defined rules, and (3) a segmentation-aware MLLM that combines visual input with explicit anatomical evidence. All methods are evaluated on 887 heterogeneous CT and MR scans with manually verified anatomical region labels. The segmentation-driven rule-based approach achieves the strongest and most consistent performance, with weighted F1-scores of 0.947 (CT) and 0.914 (MR), demonstrating robustness across modalities and atypical scan coverage. The MLLM performs competitively in visually distinctive regions, while the segmentation-aware MLLM reveals fundamental limitations.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot body region detection in CT/MR using three training-free pipelines; the segmentation-driven rule-based method outperforms others (CT F1 0.947, MR F1 0.914).
AI.motivation: Reduce dependence on unreliable DICOM metadata and supervised learning by leveraging large pre-trained foundation models for zero-shot anatomical localization.
AI.method: Three pipelines: (1) segmentation-driven rule-based using pre-trained multi-organ segmentation models; (2) Multimodal LLM guided by radiologist-defined rules; (3) segmentation-aware LLM combining visual inputs with explicit anatomical evidence. Evaluation on 887 heterogeneous CT/MR scans with manual region labels.
