AI.result: Segmentation-driven rule-based approach delivers strongest, most consistent performance (weighted F1: CT 0.947, MR 0.914), robust to modality and atypical scan coverage. MLLM is competitive in visually distinctive regions; segmentation-aware MLLM shows fundamental limitations.
AI.conclusion: Zero-shot body region detection is feasible, with segmentation-driven rules offering the best robustness across modalities; MLLMs hold potential but require addressing their limitations for reliable anatomy reasoning in medical imaging.

title: Towards Efficient Large Language Reasoning Models via Extreme-Ratio Chain-of-Thought Compression
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning successfully enhances the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet it incurs substantial computational overhead for inference. Existing CoT compression methods often suffer from a critical loss of logical fidelity at high compression ratios, resulting in significant performance degradation. To achieve high-fidelity, fast reasoning, we propose a novel EXTreme-RAtio Chain-of-Thought Compression framework, termed Extra-CoT, which aggressively reduces the token budget while preserving answer accuracy. To generate reliable, high-fidelity supervision, we first train a dedicated semantically-preserved compressor on mathematical CoT data with fine-grained annotations. An LLM is then fine-tuned on these compressed pairs via a mixed-ratio supervised fine-tuning (SFT), teaching it to follow a spectrum of compression budgets and providing a stable initialization for reinforcement learning (RL). We further propose Constrained and Hierarchical Ratio Policy Optimization (CHRPO) to explicitly incentivize question-solving ability under lower budgets by a hierarchical reward. Experiments on three mathematical reasoning benchmarks show the superiority of Extra-CoT. For example, on MATH-500 using Qwen3-1.7B, Extra-CoT achieves over 73\% token reduction with an accuracy improvement of 0.6\%, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods.

AI.tldr: Extra-CoT introduces a semantically-preserved compressor and CHRPO reinforcement learning to aggressively compress Chain-of-Thought (CoT) while maintaining or slightly improving accuracy, outperforming SOTA on three math benchmarks (e.g., ~73% token reduction with +0.6% accuracy on MATH-500 using Qwen3-1.7B).
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational cost and latency of CoT reasoning in LLMs without sacrificing logical fidelity. Existing compression methods degrade performance at high compression ratios; math reasoning is particularly sensitive to CoT loss.
AI.method: 1) Train a semantically-preserved compressor on mathematical CoT data with fine-grained annotations. 2) Fine-tune an LLM on compressed pairs via mixed-ratio supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to handle various budgets. 3) Propose Constrained and Hierarchical Ratio Policy Optimization (CHRPO), a hierarchical reward framework to encourage solving under tight budgets.
AI.result: Evaluated on three mathematical reasoning benchmarks; Extra-CoT outperforms SOTA methods in both efficiency and accuracy. On MATH-500 with Qwen3-1.7B, achieved ~73% token reduction with a 0.6% accuracy gain, indicating high-fidelity, fast reasoning under extreme compression.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that aggressive CoT compression can maintain or slightly improve accuracy when guided by semantically-preserving compression, budget-aware supervised fine-tuning, and hierarchical RL rewards. Potentially extendable to other domains, though effectiveness hinges on accurate semantics preservation and robust RL optimization.

title: Rotated Lights for Consistent and Efficient 2D Gaussians Inverse Rendering
summary: Inverse rendering aims to decompose a scene into its geometry, material properties and light conditions under a certain rendering model. It has wide applications like view synthesis, relighting, and scene editing. In recent years, inverse rendering methods have been inspired by view synthesis approaches like neural radiance fields and Gaussian splatting, which are capable of efficiently decomposing a scene into its geometry and radiance. They then further estimate the material and lighting that lead to the observed scene radiance. However, the latter step is highly ambiguous and prior works suffer from inaccurate color and baked shadows in their albedo estimation albeit their regularization. To this end, we propose RotLight, a simple capturing setup, to address the ambiguity. Compared to a usual capture, RotLight only requires the object to be rotated several times during the process. We show that as few as two rotations is effective in reducing artifacts. To further improve 2DGS-based inverse rendering, we additionally introduce a proxy mesh that not only allows accurate incident light tracing, but also enables a residual constraint and improves global illumination handling. We demonstrate with both synthetic and real world datasets that our method achieves superior albedo estimation while keeping efficient computation.

AI.tldr: RotLight introduces a simple rotating capture setup (two rotations suffice) to reduce ambiguity in inverse rendering albedo estimation; uses a proxy mesh to improve incident light tracing and residual constraints within a 2DGS-based pipeline, achieving better albedo while maintaining efficiency, validated on synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: Inverse rendering aims to decompose a scene into geometry, material, and lighting. While recent methods borrow view-synthesis techniques to recover geometry and radiance, estimating material and lighting remains highly ambiguous, often producing incorrect albedo and baked shadows. A simple, practical capture strategy and improved light handling are needed to reduce ambiguity and artifacting.
AI.method: Propose RotLight: a capture setup where the object is rotated during capture (as few as two rotations). Introduce a proxy mesh to enable accurate incident-light tracing and to provide a residual constraint, improving global illumination handling. Integrate with 2DGS-based inverse rendering to decompose scene into albedo and lighting with reduced ambiguity. Validate on synthetic and real datasets.
AI.result: RotLight reduces artifacts in albedo estimation and improves global illumination handling. A two-rotation capture suffices to mitigate ambiguity, and the proxy mesh enhances incident-light tracing and residual constraints, yielding superior albedo estimates while keeping computation efficient, demonstrated on both synthetic and real-world data.
AI.conclusion: RotLight offers a simple, effective augmentation to 2DGS-based inverse rendering, enabling more accurate albedo estimation under realistic lighting with modest additional capture effort, and the proxy mesh further strengthens light transport modeling and residual constraints.

title: FusionEdit: Semantic Fusion and Attention Modulation for Training-Free Image Editing
summary: Text-guided image editing aims to modify specific regions according to the target prompt while preserving the identity of the source image. Recent methods exploit explicit binary masks to constrain editing, but hard mask boundaries introduce artifacts and reduce editability. To address these issues, we propose FusionEdit, a training-free image editing framework that achieves precise and controllable edits. First, editing and preserved regions are automatically identified by measuring semantic discrepancies between source and target prompts. To mitigate boundary artifacts, FusionEdit performs distance-aware latent fusion along region boundaries to yield the soft and accurate mask, and employs a total variation loss to enforce smooth transitions, obtaining natural editing results. Second, FusionEdit leverages AdaIN-based modulation within DiT attention layers to perform a statistical attention fusion in the editing region, enhancing editability while preserving global consistency with the source image. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our FusionEdit significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/Yvan1001/FusionEdit}{https://github.com/Yvan1001/FusionEdit}.

AI.tldr: FusionEdit is a training-free framework for text-guided image editing that automatically identifies editing regions via semantic discrepancies between prompts, uses distance-aware latent fusion for soft masks and a total variation loss for smooth boundaries, and applies AdaIN-based modulation in DiT attention layers for statistical attention fusion, yielding precise edits and better global consistency without training.
AI.motivation: Hard mask boundaries in text-guided editing cause artifacts and limit editability; there is a need for precise, controllable edits without extra training.
AI.method: 1) Identify editing/preserved regions by semantic discrepancy between source and target prompts. 2) Use distance-aware latent fusion along region boundaries to produce soft, accurate masks. 3) Apply total variation loss to enforce smooth transitions. 4) Use AdaIN-based modulation within DiT attention layers for statistical attention fusion in the editing region. 5) Training-free framework with automatic prompts-based editing.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show FusionEdit significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods; code is provided, indicating reproducibility and practical applicability.
AI.conclusion: FusionEdit delivers precise, controllable, and natural edits in a training-free manner by combining soft boundary-aware masking with region-focused AdaIN-based attention fusion, improving editability and global consistency.

title: Regime Change Hypothesis: Foundations for Decoupled Dynamics in Neural Network Training
summary: Despite the empirical success of DNN, their internal training dynamics remain difficult to characterize. In ReLU-based models, the activation pattern induced by a given input determines the piecewise-linear region in which the network behaves affinely. Motivated by this geometry, we investigate whether training exhibits a two-timescale behavior: an early stage with substantial changes in activation patterns and a later stage where weight updates predominantly refine the model within largely stable activation regimes. We first prove a local stability property: outside measure-zero sets of parameters and inputs, sufficiently small parameter perturbations preserve the activation pattern of a fixed input, implying locally affine behavior within activation regions. We then empirically track per-iteration changes in weights and activation patterns across fully-connected and convolutional architectures, as well as Transformer-based models, where activation patterns are recorded in the ReLU feed-forward (MLP/FFN) submodules, using fixed validation subsets. Across the evaluated settings, activation-pattern changes decay 3 times earlier than weight-update magnitudes, showing that late-stage training often proceeds within relatively stable activation regimes. These findings provide a concrete, architecture-agnostic instrument for monitoring training dynamics and motivate further study of decoupled optimization strategies for piecewise-linear networks. For reproducibility, code and experiment configurations will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Two-timescale training dynamics in ReLU nets: early activation-pattern changes, later weight refinements; local stability of activation patterns; empirical decay of activation-pattern changes ~3x faster than weight updates across architectures; suggests decoupled optimization strategies.
AI.motivation: Characterize training dynamics of piecewise-linear DNNs and develop architecture-agnostic monitoring to guide optimization.
AI.method: Prove a local stability property: activation pattern preserved under small parameter perturbations for fixed input (except measure-zero). Empirically track per-iteration weight and activation-pattern changes in FC, CNN, Transformer models on fixed validation sets.
AI.result: Activation-pattern changes decay about three times faster than weight-update magnitudes; training proceeds within stable activation regimes in late stage; cross-architecture evidence; provides a practical monitoring instrument.
AI.conclusion: Supports a two-timescale perspective and motivates decoupled optimization for ReLU networks; reproducibility via released code.

title: SynSacc: A Blender-to-V2E Pipeline for Synthetic Neuromorphic Eye-Movement Data and Sim-to-Real Spiking Model Training
comment: Accepted to the 2nd Workshop on "Event-based Vision in the Era of Generative AI - Transforming Perception and Visual Innovation, IEEE Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV 2026)
summary: The study of eye movements, particularly saccades and fixations, are fundamental to understanding the mechanisms of human cognition and perception. Accurate classification of these movements requires sensing technologies capable of capturing rapid dynamics without distortion. Event cameras, also known as Dynamic Vision Sensors (DVS), provide asynchronous recordings of changes in light intensity, thereby eliminating motion blur inherent in conventional frame-based cameras and offering superior temporal resolution and data efficiency. In this study, we introduce a synthetic dataset generated with Blender to simulate saccades and fixations under controlled conditions. Leveraging Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs), we evaluate its robustness by training two architectures and finetuning on real event data. The proposed models achieve up to 0.83 accuracy and maintain consistent performance across varying temporal resolutions, demonstrating stability in eye movement classification. Moreover, the use of SNNs with synthetic event streams yields substantial computational efficiency gains over artificial neural network (ANN) counterparts, underscoring the utility of synthetic data augmentation in advancing event-based vision. All code and datasets associated with this work is available at https: //github.com/Ikhadija-5/SynSacc-Dataset.

AI.tldr: Synthetic Blender-generated event-camera dataset for saccade/fixation classification using Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs); achieves up to 0.83 accuracy and stable performance across temporal resolutions; SNNs outperform ANNs in computational efficiency on synthetic event streams; code available on GitHub.
AI.motivation: Address the need for distortion-free, high-temporal-resolution sensing of rapid eye movements and to explore the value of synthetic event data for training efficient, robust event-based vision models.
AI.method: Create a Blender-based synthetic dataset simulating saccades and fixations for an event camera; train two SNN architectures; finetune on real event data; evaluate robustness to varying temporal resolutions; compare computational efficiency with ANN baselines.
AI.result: Classification accuracy up to 0.83 with consistent performance across temporal resolutions; SNNs with synthetic event streams show computational efficiency gains over ANN counterparts; synthetic augmentation proves beneficial for event-based vision.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic data augmentation can effectively advance event-based vision; SNNs can exploit synthetic event streams for efficient eye-movement classification; repository of code and dataset provided.

title: ManifoldKV: Training-Free KV Cache Compression via Euclidean Outlier Detection
summary:
Long-context inference is constrained by KV-cache memory, which grows linearly with sequence length; KV-cache compression therefore hinges on reliably selecting which past tokens to retain. Most geometry-based eviction methods score keys by cosine similarity to a global centroid, but cosine is scale-invariant and can discard magnitude cues that distinguish semantically salient tokens. We propose ManifoldKV, a training-free scorer that ranks tokens by Euclidean distance to the key centroid, capturing both angular and radial deviations.
  On the RULER benchmark, ManifoldKV achieves 95.7% accuracy at 4K-16K contexts with 20% compression; matching the best geometric baseline while improving robustness in two regimes where cosine scoring fails. First, on multi-key retrieval, ManifoldKV reduces directional collisions, achieving 92.4% vs KeyDiff's 77.0% (+15.4 points) on 3-key NIAH at 50% compression. Second, to address dilution and performance collapse of global centroids at 64K context, we introduce WindowedManifoldKV, which restores accuracy to 84.3% at 25% compression, a 49-point recovery over global L2 and +3.2 points over KeyDiff. The method requires only 3 lines of code and works across 4 architectures without tuning.

AI.tldr: ManifoldKV introduces a training-freeToken-eviction scorer for KV-cache long-context models, ranking keys by Euclidean distance to the key centroid instead of cosine distance. It addresses magnitude cues and multi-key retrieval collisions, and extends to WindowedManifoldKV for very long contexts, achieving strong accuracy at compressed contexts with minimal code and architecture-agnostic applicability.
AI.motivation: Long-context inference is constrained by KV-cache memory which scales with sequence length. Cosine-based (scale-invariant) centroids can discard magnitude cues essential for semantic salience, leading to degraded performance under compression and multi-key retrieval. A more expressive, training-free scorer is needed to robustly retain informative tokens.
AI.method: Propose Euclidean-distance to the key centroid as the scoring function (ManifoldKV), training-free and simple to implement (3 lines of code). Evaluated on RULER benchmark across 4 architectures with 20% compression; extends to WindowedManifoldKV to mitigate centroid dilution at 64K contexts. WindowedManifoldKV uses a sliding/windowed centroid approach to preserve local structure and maintain accuracy under very long contexts.
AI.result: ManifoldKV achieves 95.7% accuracy at 4K-16K contexts with 20% compression, matching the best geometric baseline and improving robustness in two regimes where cosine scoring fails. In multi-key retrieval, 3-key NIAH at 50% compression shows 92.4% accuracy versus KeyDiff's 77.0% ( +15.4 points). WindowedManifoldKV recovers accuracy to 84.3% at 25% compression for 64K contexts, a 49-point gain over global L2 and +3.2 points over KeyDiff.
AI.conclusion: A simple, training-free Euclidean-distance scorer captures both angular and radial token deviations, overcoming limitations of cosine-based centroids. It demonstrates robustness across retrieval settings and very long contexts with minimal code, and is architecture-agnostic without tuning.

title: Artifact Reduction in Undersampled 3D Cone-Beam CTs using a Hybrid 2D-3D CNN Framework
summary: Undersampled CT volumes minimize acquisition time and radiation exposure but introduce artifacts degrading image quality and diagnostic utility. Reducing these artifacts is critical for high-quality imaging. We propose a computationally efficient hybrid deep-learning framework that combines the strengths of 2D and 3D models. First, a 2D U-Net operates on individual slices of undersampled CT volumes to extract feature maps. These slice-wise feature maps are then stacked across the volume and used as input to a 3D decoder, which utilizes contextual information across slices to predict an artifact-free 3D CT volume. The proposed two-stage approach balances the computational efficiency of 2D processing with the volumetric consistency provided by 3D modeling. The results show substantial improvements in inter-slice consistency in coronal and sagittal direction with low computational overhead. This hybrid framework presents a robust and efficient solution for high-quality 3D CT image post-processing. The code of this project can be found on github: https://github.com/J-3TO/2D-3DCNN_sparseview/.

AI.tldr: A two-stage hybrid 2D+3D CNN pipeline for artifact reduction in undersampled CT, using a 2D U‑Net on each slice to extract features, then a 3D decoder that aggregates slice features to produce a consistent artifact-free 3D volume, achieving improved inter-slice consistency with low overhead.
AI.motivation: Undersampling in CT reduces acquisition time and radiation but introduces artifacts that degrade image quality and diagnostic utility. Efficient post-processing methods that restore quality while preserving 3D consistency are needed.
AI.method: Stage 1: apply a 2D U‑Net to each CT slice to extract feature maps. Stage 2: stack the slice-wise features along the volume and input them to a 3D decoder to leverage cross-slice context and output an artifact-free 3D volume.
AI.result: The approach yields substantial improvements in inter-slice consistency (coronal and sagittal directions) with low computational overhead compared to fully 3D approaches.
AI.conclusion: A computationally efficient 2D+3D hybrid framework effectively denoises/de-artifacts undersampled CT volumes, balancing per-slice processing with volumetric coherence; code is available at the provided GitHub repository.

title: All ERMs Can Fail in Stochastic Convex Optimization Lower Bounds in Linear Dimension
summary:
We study the sample complexity of the best-case Empirical Risk Minimizer in the setting of stochastic convex optimization. We show that there exists an instance in which the sample size is linear in the dimension, learning is possible, but the Empirical Risk Minimizer is likely to be unique and to overfit. This resolves an open question by Feldman. We also extend this to approximate ERMs.
  Building on our construction we also show that (constrained) Gradient Descent potentially overfits when horizon and learning rate grow w.r.t sample size. Specifically we provide a novel generalization lower bound of $Ω\left(\sqrt{ηT/m^{1.5}}\right)$ for Gradient Descent, where $η$ is the learning rate, $T$ is the horizon and $m$ is the sample size. This narrows down, exponentially, the gap between the best known upper bound of $O(ηT/m)$ and existing lower bounds from previous constructions.

AI.tldr: Constructs a linear-in-dimension instance where the best-case ERM both learns and overfits, resolving Feldman’s open question and extending to approximate ERMs. It also shows that constrained gradient descent can overfit as horizon and learning rate grow with sample size, via a new generalization lower bound Ω(√(ηT/m^{1.5})), narrowing the gap to the upper bound O(ηT/m).
AI.motivation: Clarify the sample complexity and generalization behavior of ERM and gradient descent in stochastic convex optimization, addressing an open question and tightening known bounds.
AI.method: Explicit instance construction to separate learning from overfitting for ERM, extension to approximate ERMs, and derivation of a generalization lower bound for (constrained) gradient descent under scaling of horizon, learning rate, and sample size.
AI.result: Demonstrates that ERM can overfit even when learning is possible and sample size is linear in dimension; ERM may be unique; extends to approximate ERMs. Provides Ω(√(ηT/m^{1.5})) lower bound for GD generalization, tightening the gap to the known O(ηT/m) upper bound.
AI.conclusion: Highlights intrinsic overfitting risks for ERM and GD in stochastic convex optimization, resolves Feldman’s question, and tightens the theoretical bounds by linking sample size, horizon, and learning rate.

title: Closing the Confusion Loop: CLIP-Guided Alignment for Source-Free Domain Adaptation
summary: Source-Free Domain Adaptation (SFDA) tackles the problem of adapting a pre-trained source model to an unlabeled target domain without accessing any source data, which is quite suitable for the field of data security. Although recent advances have shown that pseudo-labeling strategies can be effective, they often fail in fine-grained scenarios due to subtle inter-class similarities. A critical but underexplored issue is the presence of asymmetric and dynamic class confusion, where visually similar classes are unequally and inconsistently misclassified by the source model. Existing methods typically ignore such confusion patterns, leading to noisy pseudo-labels and poor target discrimination. To address this, we propose CLIP-Guided Alignment(CGA), a novel framework that explicitly models and mitigates class confusion in SFDA. Generally, our method consists of three parts: (1) MCA: detects first directional confusion pairs by analyzing the predictions of the source model in the target domain; (2) MCC: leverages CLIP to construct confusion-aware textual prompts (e.g. a truck that looks like a bus), enabling more context-sensitive pseudo-labeling; and (3) FAM: builds confusion-guided feature banks for both CLIP and the source model and aligns them using contrastive learning to reduce ambiguity in the representation space. Extensive experiments on various datasets demonstrate that CGA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art SFDA methods, with especially notable gains in confusion-prone and fine-grained scenarios. Our results highlight the importance of explicitly modeling inter-class confusion for effective source-free adaptation. Our code can be find at https://github.com/soloiro/CGA

AI.tldr: We propose CGA, a source-free domain adaptation framework that models and mitigates asymmetric, dynamic class confusion using MCA to detect confusion pairs, MCC to use CLIP-based confusion-aware prompts, and FAM to build confusion-guided feature banks for CLIP and the source model, with contrastive alignment; it improves SFDA, especially in fine-grained/confusion-prone scenarios.
AI.motivation: In SFDA, pseudo-labels degrade when fine-grained similarities produce asymmetric and dynamic class confusion; current methods ignore confusion patterns, leading to noisy labels and poor target discrimination.
AI.method: Three components: MCA identifies directional confusion from source model predictions on the target; MCC uses CLIP to craft textual prompts that describe confusing visuals (e.g., 'a truck that looks like a bus') to guide pseudo-labeling; FAM builds confusion-guided feature banks and aligns CLIP and source-model features via contrastive learning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show CGA outperforms state-of-the-art SFDA methods, with notable gains in confusion-prone and fine-grained settings.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling inter-class confusion is beneficial for effective source-free domain adaptation; CLIP-guided confusion modeling enhances pseudo-labeling and representation learning in SFDA.

title: The Chicken and Egg Dilemma: Co-optimizing Data and Model Configurations for LLMs
summary: Co-optimizing data and model configurations for training LLMs presents a classic chicken-and-egg dilemma: The best training data configuration (e.g., data mixture) for a downstream task depends on the chosen model configuration (e.g., model architecture), and vice versa. However, jointly optimizing both data and model configurations is often deemed intractable, and existing methods focus on either data or model optimization without considering their interaction. We introduce JoBS, an approach that uses a scaling-law-inspired performance predictor to aid Bayesian optimization (BO) in jointly optimizing LLM training data and model configurations efficiently. JoBS allocates a portion of the optimization budget to learn an LLM performance predictor that predicts how promising a training configuration is from a small number of training steps. The remaining budget is used to perform BO entirely with the predictor, effectively amortizing the cost of running full-training runs. We study JoBS's average regret and devise the optimal budget allocation to minimize regret. JoBS outperforms existing multi-fidelity BO baselines, as well as data and model optimization approaches across diverse LLM tasks under the same optimization budget.

AI.tldr: JoBS uses a scaling-law-inspired predictor to accelerate joint data/model optimization for LLM training, achieving better performance within a fixed budget by amortizing predictor training and guiding Bayesian optimization over configurations.
AI.motivation: Addresses the intractable chicken‑and‑egg problem of jointly optimizing data configurations (e.g., data mix) and model configurations (e.g., architecture) for LLM training, where the best data setup depends on the model and vice versa.
AI.method: JoBS allocates part of the optimization budget to learn a low-cost LLM performance predictor (based on a scaling law) that forecasts promising configurations from few training steps. The remainder of the budget runs Bayesian optimization entirely with the predictor, amortizing full-training costs. The authors derive the optimal budget split by minimizing average regret.
AI.result: JoBS outperforms existing multi-fidelity BO baselines and separate data/model optimization approaches under the same budget across diverse LLM tasks.
AI.conclusion: Joint optimization of data and model configurations for LLMs can be made tractable by predictor-guided BO with budget amortization; JoBS provides a principled framework and demonstrates empirical gains.

title: From Correspondence to Actions: Human-Like Multi-Image Spatial Reasoning in Multi-modal Large Language Models
summary: While multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have made substantial progress in single-image spatial reasoning, multi-image spatial reasoning, which requires integration of information from multiple viewpoints, remains challenging. Cognitive studies suggest that humans address such tasks through two mechanisms: cross-view correspondence, which identifies regions across different views that correspond to the same physical locations, and stepwise viewpoint transformation, which composes relative viewpoint changes sequentially. However, existing studies incorporate these mechanisms only partially and often implicitly, without explicit supervision for both. We propose Human-Aware Training for Cross-view correspondence and viewpoint cHange (HATCH), a training framework with two complementary objectives: (1) Patch-Level Spatial Alignment, which encourages patch representations to align across views for spatially corresponding regions, and (2) Action-then-Answer Reasoning, which requires the model to generate explicit viewpoint transition actions before predicting the final answer. Experiments on three benchmarks demonstrate that HATCH consistently outperforms baselines of comparable size by a clear margin and achieves competitive results against much larger models, while preserving single-image reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: HATCH introduces patch-level spatial alignment and action-then-answer reasoning to train multimodal LLMs for cross-view multi-image spatial reasoning, yielding strong gains on three benchmarks while preserving single-image capabilities.
AI.motivation: Multi-image spatial reasoning requires cross-view correspondence and stepwise viewpoint transformation. Current MLLMs provide only partial, implicit support for these mechanisms, lacking explicit supervision for both cross-view alignment and viewpoint transitions.
AI.method: Two objectives: (1) Patch-Level Spatial Alignment to align patch representations across views for spatially corresponding regions; (2) Action-then-Answer Reasoning to require generating explicit viewpoint transition actions before predicting the final answer, enabling explicit, human-like reasoning over viewpoints.
AI.result: HATCH consistently outperforms baselines of comparable size and is competitive with much larger models on three benchmarks, while preserving single-image reasoning capability.
AI.conclusion: Explicit supervision for cross-view correspondence and viewpoint transformation effectively enhances multi-image spatial reasoning in MLLMs, with HATCH providing a practical, scalable training paradigm.

title: Dynamic Regret via Discounted-to-Dynamic Reduction with Applications to Curved Losses and Adam Optimizer
summary: We study dynamic regret minimization in non-stationary online learning, with a primary focus on follow-the-regularized-leader (FTRL) methods. FTRL is important for curved losses and for understanding adaptive optimizers such as Adam, yet existing dynamic regret analyses are less explored for FTRL. To address this, we build on the discounted-to-dynamic reduction and present a modular way to obtain dynamic regret bounds of FTRL-related problems. Specifically, we focus on two representative curved losses: linear regression and logistic regression. Our method not only simplifies existing proofs for the optimal dynamic regret of online linear regression, but also yields new dynamic regret guarantees for online logistic regression. Beyond online convex optimization, we apply the reduction to analyze the Adam optimizers, obtaining optimal convergence rates in stochastic, non-convex, and non-smooth settings. The reduction also enables a more detailed treatment of Adam with two discount parameters $(β_1,β_2)$, leading to new results for both clipped and clip-free variants of Adam optimizers.

AI.tldr: Modular reduction from discounted dynamic regret to dynamic regret for FTRL-based online learning, applied to curved losses (linear/logistic regression) and adaptive optimizers (Adam); yields simplified proofs and new guarantees, including clipped and clip-free Adam variants.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in dynamic regret analysis for FTRL and curved losses, and extend to adaptive optimizers like Adam in stochastic, non-convex, and non-smooth settings.
AI.method: Introduce a discounted-to-dynamic reduction and a modular framework to derive dynamic regret bounds for FTRL-related problems; apply to online linear and logistic regression; extend to Adam with two discount parameters (β1, β2) and both clipped/clip-free variants.
AI.result: Simplifies existing dynamic regret proofs for online linear regression; provides new dynamic regret guarantees for online logistic regression; obtains optimal convergence rates for Adam in stochastic, non-convex, and non-smooth settings; offers refined analysis for both clipped and clip-free Adam using two discount parameters.
AI.conclusion: The reduction offers a unified, modular approach to dynamic regret analysis in FTRL and adaptive optimizers, enabling simpler proofs and new guarantees across curved losses and non-convex/non-smooth regimes, with explicit treatment of discount parameters in Adam.

title: Shifting the Breaking Point of Flow Matching for Multi-Instance Editing
summary: Flow matching models have recently emerged as an efficient alternative to diffusion, especially for text-guided image generation and editing, offering faster inference through continuous-time dynamics. However, existing flow-based editors predominantly support global or single-instruction edits and struggle with multi-instance scenarios, where multiple parts of a reference input must be edited independently without semantic interference. We identify this limitation as a consequence of globally conditioned velocity fields and joint attention mechanisms, which entangle concurrent edits. To address this issue, we introduce Instance-Disentangled Attention, a mechanism that partitions joint attention operations, enforcing binding between instance-specific textual instructions and spatial regions during velocity field estimation. We evaluate our approach on both natural image editing and a newly introduced benchmark of text-dense infographics with region-level editing instructions. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach promotes edit disentanglement and locality while preserving global output coherence, enabling single-pass, instance-level editing.

AI.tldr: A new instance-disentangled attention mechanism for flow-based editors enables multi-instance, region-level, text-guided edits in a single pass by binding instance-specific instructions to spatial regions during velocity-field estimation, addressing interference from globally conditioned flows.
AI.motivation: Flow-based editors currently rely on global conditioning and joint attention, which entangles multiple concurrent edits and hinders region-specific modification in multi-instance scenarios.
AI.method: Introduce Instance-Disentangled Attention that partitions joint attention operations, ensuring instance-specific textual instructions bind to corresponding spatial regions during velocity field estimation, thus enabling localized, independent edits within a single forward pass.
AI.result: Empirical results show improved edit disentanglement and locality on natural image editing and a new benchmark of text-dense infographics with region-level editing instructions, achieving single-pass, instance-level edits while preserving global coherence.
AI.conclusion: Instance-Disentangled Attention effectively mitigates interference between concurrent edits in flow-based editors, enabling accurate, region-level, instance-specific edits without sacrificing overall image consistency.

title: OJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) is widely used to compress large language models without retraining. However, many existing weight-only methods rely on heuristic objectives and greedy rounding, thus leading to noticeable degradation under low-bit quantization. In this work, we introduce OJBKQ (Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization with K-Best Sampling), a layer-wise PTQ method that formulates weight quantization as a joint optimization problem over activations and weights. This formulation results in a multiple-right-hand-side box-constrained integer least squares (BILS) problem in each layer, which is NP-hard. For each column of the weight matrix, we apply an extended Babai nearest-plane algorithm and an extended version of Klein's randomized Babai algorithm to find the minimum-residual Babai-Klein point, a sub-optimal solution to the BILS problem. Experimental results on large language models show that OJBKQ achieves lower perplexity at 3-4 bits compared to existing PTQ approaches, while maintaining comparable computational cost.

AI.tldr: A layer-wise post-training quantization method (OJBKQ) jointly optimizes activations and weights, turning the problem into a multi-right-hand-side box-constrained integer least squares (BILS) problem. It solves each weight-column via extended Babai nearest-plane and Klein's randomized algorithm to obtain a minimum-residual Babai-Klein point (a sub-optimal BILS solution). Empirically, it yields lower perplexity at 3–4 bits with similar computational cost.
AI.motivation: PTQ for large language models often relies on weight-only quantization with heuristic objectives and greedy rounding, which degrades performance at low bit-widths. A principled joint optimization over activations and weights could reduce quantization error and preserve model quality without retraining.
AI.method: Formulate weight quantization in a layer as a joint optimization over activations and weights, creating a multiple-right-hand-side BILS problem. For each column of the weight matrix, apply an extended Babai nearest-plane algorithm and an extended Klein randomized Babai algorithm to locate the minimum-residual Babai-Klein point—a sub-optimal BILS solution. The approach is layer-wise and maintains comparable computational cost to existing PTQ methods.
AI.result: The method achieves lower perplexity than existing PTQ approaches at 3–4 bits on large language models, with similar computational cost, despite the problem’s NP-hardness.
AI.conclusion: OJBKQ provides a principled, tractable approximate solution to a multi-right-hand-side BILS arising in joint activations-weights quantization, improving low-bit PTQ performance without additional training cost. The approach is heuristic due to NP-hardness and depends on per-column solves and randomized searches.

title: MVAnimate: Enhancing Character Animation with Multi-View Optimization
summary: The demand for realistic and versatile character animation has surged, driven by its wide-ranging applications in various domains. However, the animation generation algorithms modeling human pose with 2D or 3D structures all face various problems, including low-quality output content and training data deficiency, preventing the related algorithms from generating high-quality animation videos. Therefore, we introduce MVAnimate, a novel framework that synthesizes both 2D and 3D information of dynamic figures based on multi-view prior information, to enhance the generated video quality. Our approach leverages multi-view prior information to produce temporally consistent and spatially coherent animation outputs, demonstrating improvements over existing animation methods. Our MVAnimate also optimizes the multi-view videos of the target character, enhancing the video quality from different views. Experimental results on diverse datasets highlight the robustness of our method in handling various motion patterns and appearances.

AI.tldr: MVAnimate: a multi-view priors-based framework for 2D/3D human animation that yields temporally coherent, spatially consistent videos and improves quality across views.
AI.motivation: Realistic and versatile character animation is increasingly demanded, but pose-based animation from 2D/3D structures often suffers from low-quality outputs and limited training data.
AI.method: A framework that synthesizes both 2D and 3D information of dynamic figures using multi-view prior information to enhance generated video quality, and additionally optimizes multi-view videos of the target character to improve cross-view quality, achieving temporal consistency and spatial coherence.
AI.result: Demonstrates improvements over existing animation methods and shows robustness across diverse motion patterns and appearances on diverse datasets.
AI.conclusion: MVAnimate effectively leverages multi-view priors to produce high-quality, consistent 2D/3D animations.

title: Reinforcement Learning with Backtracking Feedback
summary: Addressing the critical need for robust safety in Large Language Models (LLMs), particularly against adversarial attacks and in-distribution errors, we introduce Reinforcement Learning with Backtracking Feedback (RLBF). This framework advances upon prior methods, such as BSAFE, by primarily leveraging a Reinforcement Learning (RL) stage where models learn to dynamically correct their own generation errors. Through RL with critic feedback on the model's live outputs, LLMs are trained to identify and recover from their actual, emergent safety violations by emitting an efficient "backtrack by x tokens" signal, then continuing generation autoregressively. This RL process is crucial for instilling resilience against sophisticated adversarial strategies, including middle filling, Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) attacks, and decoding parameter manipulations. To further support the acquisition of this backtracking capability, we also propose an enhanced Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) data generation strategy (BSAFE+). This method improves upon previous data creation techniques by injecting violations into coherent, originally safe text, providing more effective initial training for the backtracking mechanism. Comprehensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that RLBF significantly reduces attack success rates across diverse benchmarks and model scales, achieving superior safety outcomes while critically preserving foundational model utility.

AI.tldr: Proposes Reinforcement Learning with Backtracking Feedback (RLBF), a backtracking-based safety framework for LLMs that uses live-output critic feedback and a 'backtrack by x tokens' signal to recover from safety violations, augmented by BSAFE+ data generation. Demonstrates reduced attack success across benchmarks and scales while preserving model utility.
AI.motivation: Address persistent safety gaps in large language models under sophisticated adversarial attacks and in-distribution errors; existing methods (e.g., BSAFE) are insufficient for robust recovery from emergent violations.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) RL with critic feedback on live model outputs enabling a backtracking signal (backtrack by x tokens) and autoregressive continuation to recover from violations; (2) BSAFE+ enhanced supervised fine-tuning data generation by injecting violations into coherent safe text to better train the backtracking mechanism; evaluation targets adversarial strategies such as middle filling, Greedy Coordinate Gradient attacks, and decoding parameter manipulations across multiple model scales.
AI.result: Empirical results show RLBF significantly reduces attack success rates across diverse benchmarks and model sizes, achieving stronger safety without materially sacrificing core model utility.
AI.conclusion: RLBF provides a robust safety mechanism for LLMs by leveraging a reinforcement learning stage to learn dynamic backtracking from live outputs, complemented by improved SFT data (BSAFE+). It demonstrates strong robustness against sophisticated adversarial attacks and maintains utility, suggesting broad applicability and possible extensions to broader threat models.

title: VedicTHG: Symbolic Vedic Computation for Low-Resource Talking-Head Generation in Educational Avatars
summary: Talking-head avatars are increasingly adopted in educational technology to deliver content with social presence and improved engagement. However, many recent talking-head generation (THG) methods rely on GPU-centric neural rendering, large training sets, or high-capacity diffusion models, which limits deployment in offline or resource-constrained learning environments. A deterministic and CPU-oriented THG framework is described, termed Symbolic Vedic Computation, that converts speech to a time-aligned phoneme stream, maps phonemes to a compact viseme inventory, and produces smooth viseme trajectories through symbolic coarticulation inspired by Vedic sutra Urdhva Tiryakbhyam. A lightweight 2D renderer performs region-of-interest (ROI) warping and mouth compositing with stabilization to support real-time synthesis on commodity CPUs. Experiments report synchronization accuracy, temporal stability, and identity consistency under CPU-only execution, alongside benchmarking against representative CPU-feasible baselines. Results indicate that acceptable lip-sync quality can be achieved while substantially reducing computational load and latency, supporting practical educational avatars on low-end hardware. GitHub: https://vineetkumarrakesh.github.io/vedicthg

AI.tldr: A CPU-friendly talking-head generation framework using symbolic phoneme-to-viseme processing and a lightweight 2D renderer achieves real-time lip-sync on commodity CPUs, enabling offline educational avatars; it reduces computation while maintaining synchronization and identity consistency compared with CPU baselines.
AI.motivation: GPU-centric neural rendering and large training data in current THG methods hinder deployment in offline or resource-constrained settings; a deterministic, CPU-oriented approach aims to enable accessible educational avatars on low-end hardware.
AI.method: 1) Convert speech to a time-aligned phoneme stream. 2) Map phonemes to a compact viseme inventory. 3) Produce smooth viseme trajectories via symbolic coarticulation inspired by the Vedic sutra Urdhva Tiryakbhyam. 4) Render via a lightweight 2D ROI-based pipeline for mouth compositing with stabilization. 5) Real-time synthesis on commodity CPUs and evaluation against CPU-feasible baselines.
AI.result: Experiments report synchronization accuracy, temporal stability, and identity consistency under CPU-only execution; benchmarking against representative CPU-feasible baselines. Results indicate acceptable lip-sync quality with substantially reduced computational load and latency.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates practical educational talking-head avatars on low-end hardware; an accessible CPU-focused THG pipeline; codebase likely available (GitHub).

title: Modalities, a PyTorch-native Framework For Large-scale LLM Training and Research
summary: Today's LLM (pre-) training and research workflows typically allocate a significant amount of compute to large-scale ablation studies. Despite the substantial compute costs of these ablations, existing open-source frameworks provide limited tooling for these experiments, often forcing researchers to write their own wrappers and scripts. We propose Modalities, an end-to-end PyTorch-native framework that integrates data-driven LLM research with large-scale model training from two angles. Firstly, by integrating state-of-the-art parallelization strategies, it enables both efficient pretraining and systematic ablations at trillion-token and billion-parameter scale. Secondly, Modalities adopts modular design with declarative, self-contained configuration, enabling reproducibility and extensibility levels that are difficult to achieve out-of-the-box with existing LLM training frameworks.

AI.tldr: Modalities is a PyTorch-native framework that enables scalable LLM pretraining and systematic ablations via advanced parallelism and a modular declarative config for reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Researchers incur massive compute for ablations, but existing open-source frameworks lack tooling for large-scale, reproducible experiments; there is a need for an integrated end-to-end solution that unifies data-driven LLM research with large-scale training.
AI.method: Integrates state-of-the-art parallelization strategies within a PyTorch-native framework. Adopts a modular design with declarative, self-contained configuration to enable reproducibility and extensibility. Supports ablations and pretraining at trillion-token and billion-parameter scales, respectively, linking data-driven research with large-scale model training.
AI.result: The abstract proposes Modalities and outlines its capabilities, including efficient pretraining and systematic ablations at large scales. No empirical results are reported in the abstract; the contribution is the framework design and claimed benefits over existing tooling.
AI.conclusion: Modalities aims to streamline LLM research workflows by providing an end-to-end, scalable, and reproducible framework that reduces bespoke scripting and facilitates comprehensive ablations and large-scale training.

title: Multimodal Learning for Arcing Detection in Pantograph-Catenary Systems
summary: The pantograph-catenary interface is essential for ensuring uninterrupted and reliable power delivery in electrified rail systems. However, electrical arcing at this interface poses serious risks, including accelerated wear of contact components, degraded system performance, and potential service disruptions. Detecting arcing events at the pantograph-catenary interface is challenging due to their transient nature, noisy operating environment, data scarcity, and the difficulty of distinguishing arcs from other similar transient phenomena. To address these challenges, we propose a novel multimodal framework that combines high-resolution image data with force measurements to more accurately and robustly detect arcing events. First, we construct two arcing detection datasets comprising synchronized visual and force measurements. One dataset is built from data provided by the Swiss Federal Railways (SBB), and the other is derived from publicly available videos of arcing events in different railway systems and synthetic force data that mimic the characteristics observed in the real dataset. Leveraging these datasets, we propose MultiDeepSAD, an extension of the DeepSAD algorithm for multiple modalities with a new loss formulation. Additionally, we introduce tailored pseudo-anomaly generation techniques specific to each data type, such as synthetic arc-like artifacts in images and simulated force irregularities, to augment training data and improve the discriminative ability of the model. Through extensive experiments and ablation studies, we demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms baseline approaches, exhibiting enhanced sensitivity to real arcing events even under domain shifts and limited availability of real arcing observations.

AI.tldr: A multimodal anomaly-detection framework (MultiDeepSAD) for arcing at pantograph-catenary using synchronized image and force data, with synthetic pseudo-anomalies and two real/synthetic datasets, outperforming baselines and robust under domain shifts.
AI.motivation: Arcing at the pantograph-catenary is a critical failure mode in electrified rail systems. Arcs are transient, occur in noisy environments, and real observations are scarce, making reliable detection challenging. A robust method leveraging multiple data modalities could improve detection accuracy and resilience to domain shifts.
AI.method: Construct two synchronized datasets—one from Swiss Federal Railways (SBB) data and another from publicly available arcing videos with synthetic force data—to enable multimodal learning. Propose MultiDeepSAD, a multimodal extension of the DeepSAD anomaly-detection framework with a novel loss formulation. Develop pseudo-anomaly generation tailored to each modality (image-like arc artifacts; simulated force irregularities) to augment training. Evaluate via extensive experiments and ablation studies, comparing to baselines and testing robustness under domain shifts and limited real data.
AI.result: The framework significantly outperforms baseline approaches, with enhanced sensitivity to real arcing events even under domain shifts and limited real arcing observations.
AI.conclusion: A multimodal, semi-supervised anomaly-detection approach with dataset augmentation and modality-specific pseudo-anomalies yields robust arcing detection at the pantograph-catenary interface, suggesting practical applicability and resilience to unavailable or shifting data distributions.

title: Drop the mask! GAMM-A Taxonomy for Graph Attributes Missing Mechanisms
summary: Exploring missing data in attributed graphs introduces unique challenges beyond those found in tabular datasets. In this work, we extend the taxonomy for missing data mechanisms to attributed graphs by proposing GAMM (Graph Attributes Missing Mechanisms), a framework that systematically links missingness probability to both node attributes and the underlying graph structure. Our taxonomy enriches the conventional definitions of masking mechanisms by introducing graph-specific dependencies. We empirically demonstrate that state-of-the-art imputation methods, while effective on traditional masks, significantly struggle when confronted with these more realistic graph-aware missingness scenarios.

AI.tldr: GAMM provides a graph-specific missingness framework linking missing data to node attributes and graph structure, revealing that current imputation methods struggle with graph-aware masking.
AI.motivation: Attributed graphs exhibit missingness patterns that depend on both node features and topology, which traditional missing-data taxonomies fail to capture.
AI.method: Introduce GAMM (Graph Attributes Missing Mechanisms), a taxonomy that extends conventional masking definitions with graph-dependent dependencies, and validate via experiments comparing standard imputers under graph-aware masks.
AI.result: Graph-aware missingness degrades performance of state-of-the-art imputers more than traditional masks; GAMM highlights dependencies that conventional masks miss.
AI.conclusion: Modeling missing data in graphs requires graph-aware mechanisms; GAMM provides a principled framework to reason about such missingness and signals the need for imputers that leverage graph structure.

title: MOVA: Towards Scalable and Synchronized Video-Audio Generation
comment: Technical report for MOVA (open-source video-audio generation model). 38 pages, 10 figures, 22 tables. Project page: https://mosi.cn/models/mova Code: https://github.com/OpenMOSS/MOVA Models: https://huggingface.co/collections/OpenMOSS-Team/mova. Qinyuan Cheng and Tianyi Liang are project leader. Xie Chen and Xipeng Qiu are corresponding authors
summary: Audio is indispensable for real-world video, yet generation models have largely overlooked audio components. Current approaches to producing audio-visual content often rely on cascaded pipelines, which increase cost, accumulate errors, and degrade overall quality. While systems such as Veo 3 and Sora 2 emphasize the value of simultaneous generation, joint multimodal modeling introduces unique challenges in architecture, data, and training. Moreover, the closed-source nature of existing systems limits progress in the field. In this work, we introduce MOVA (MOSS Video and Audio), an open-source model capable of generating high-quality, synchronized audio-visual content, including realistic lip-synced speech, environment-aware sound effects, and content-aligned music. MOVA employs a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, with a total of 32B parameters, of which 18B are active during inference. It supports IT2VA (Image-Text to Video-Audio) generation task. By releasing the model weights and code, we aim to advance research and foster a vibrant community of creators. The released codebase features comprehensive support for efficient inference, LoRA fine-tuning, and prompt enhancement.

AI.tldr: MOVA is an open-source 32B MoE model for synchronized audio-visual generation (lip-sync, environment sounds, music) with 18B active during inference and IT2VA support; includes LoRA fine-tuning and efficient inference.
AI.motivation: Address the under-emphasis of audio in video generation, avoid cascading pipelines, and enable open, reproducible research to advance joint audio-visual modeling.
AI.method: Mixture-of-Experts architecture totaling 32B parameters (18B active during inference); IT2VA (image-text-to-video-audio) generation; open-source release with efficient inference, LoRA fine-tuning, and prompt enhancement.
AI.result: Produces high-quality, synchronized audio-visual content including lip-synced speech, environment sounds, and content-aligned music; supports efficient inference and LoRA/prompt tooling; released openly to the community.
AI.conclusion: Open-source release aims to accelerate research and foster a community around joint audio-visual generation.

title: Radial Müntz-Szász Networks: Neural Architectures with Learnable Power Bases for Multidimensional Singularities
summary: Radial singular fields, such as $1/r$, $\log r$, and crack-tip profiles, are difficult to model for coordinate-separable neural architectures. We show that any $C^2$ function that is both radial and additively separable must be quadratic, establishing a fundamental obstruction for coordinate-wise power-law models. Motivated by this result, we introduce Radial Müntz-Szász Networks (RMN), which represent fields as linear combinations of learnable radial powers $r^μ$, including negative exponents, together with a limit-stable log-primitive for exact $\log r$ behavior. RMN admits closed-form spatial gradients and Laplacians, enabling physics-informed learning on punctured domains. Across ten 2D and 3D benchmarks, RMN achieves 1.5$\times$--51$\times$ lower RMSE than MLPs and 10$\times$--100$\times$ lower RMSE than SIREN while using 27 parameters, compared with 33,537 for MLPs and 8,577 for SIREN. We extend RMN to angular dependence (RMN-Angular) and to multiple sources with learnable centers (RMN-MC); when optimization converges, source-center recovery errors fall below $10^{-4}$. We also report controlled failures on smooth, strongly non-radial targets to delineate RMN's operating regime.

AI.tldr: Radial Müntz-Szász Networks (RMN) address a fundamental obstruction for coordinate-separable models by showing that any C^2 radial, additively separable function must be quadratic, and by introducing RMN, which model radial fields as linear combinations of learnable radial powers r^μ (including negative exponents) plus a log primitive. RMN achieves large RMSE gains over MLPs and SIREN across many benchmarks with far fewer parameters, and extensions to angular dependence (RMN-Angular) and multi-source centers (RMN-MC) yield accurate center recovery; the authors also discuss controlled failures on non-radial targets."
AI.motivation: Identify and overcome the fundamental limitation of coordinate-wise, power-law models in representing radial singular fields (e.g., 1/r, log r, crack-tip profiles). The paper provides a rigidity result for C^2 radial additively separable functions and proposes a flexible radial basis (r^μ with log) to enable accurate, efficient physics-informed learning on punctured domains, including gradient/Laplacian computations.
AI.method: Propose Radial Müntz-Szász Networks (RMN): model fields as linear combinations of radial powers r^μ (allowing negative μ) plus a limit-stable log primitive for log r. RMN admits closed-form spatial gradients and Laplacians, enabling physics-informed training on punctured domains. Extend to RMN-Angular (angular dependence) and RMN-MC (multiple sources with learnable centers). Evaluate on ten 2D/3D benchmarks; compare to MLPs and SIREN.
AI.result: RMN achieves 1.5×–51× RMSE reductions relative to MLPs and 10×–100× reductions relative to SIREN with only 27 parameters (vs 33,537 for MLPs and 8,577 for SIREN). Source-center recovery errors fall below 1e-4 with RMN-MC; RMN remains effective across radial targets, with controlled failures on smooth, strongly non-radial data delineating its operating regime.
AI.conclusion: RMN provides a compact, expressive radial basis for learning singular radial fields, enabling exact log and singular behavior with efficient gradients/Laplacians for physics-informed learning on punctured domains. The framework generalizes to angular and multi-source settings, and its limitations are demonstrated by failures on non-radial targets, clarifying the method’s regime of applicability.

title: Addressing data annotation scarcity in Brain Tumor Segmentation on 3D MRI scan Using a Semi-Supervised Teacher-Student Framework
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures. Submitted to IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics (JBHI)
summary: Accurate brain tumor segmentation from MRI is limited by expensive annotations and data heterogeneity across scanners and sites. We propose a semi-supervised teacher-student framework that combines an uncertainty-aware pseudo-labeling teacher with a progressive, confidence-based curriculum for the student. The teacher produces probabilistic masks and per-pixel uncertainty; unlabeled scans are ranked by image-level confidence and introduced in stages, while a dual-loss objective trains the student to learn from high-confidence regions and unlearn low-confidence ones. Agreement-based refinement further improves pseudo-label quality. On BraTS 2021, validation DSC increased from 0.393 (10% data) to 0.872 (100%), with the largest gains in early stages, demonstrating data efficiency. The teacher reached a validation DSC of 0.922, and the student surpassed the teacher on tumor subregions (e.g., NCR/NET 0.797 and Edema 0.980); notably, the student recovered the Enhancing class (DSC 0.620) where the teacher failed. These results show that confidence-driven curricula and selective unlearning provide robust segmentation under limited supervision and noisy pseudo-labels.

AI.tldr: Semi-supervised, uncertainty-aware teacher-student framework with a curriculum-based, confidence-driven pseudo-labeling approach improves brain tumor segmentation under limited annotations; achieves strong BraTS 2021 performance, with the student surpassing the teacher on key tumor subregions and recovering the Enhancing class.
AI.motivation: Overcome expensive annotations and data heterogeneity across scanners/sites by leveraging unlabeled data with a principled, uncertainty-aware semi-supervised strategy.
AI.method: A teacher generates probabilistic masks and per-pixel uncertainty. Unlabeled scans are ranked by image-level confidence and introduced progressively. The student is trained with a dual loss to learn from high-confidence regions while unlearning low-confidence regions; agreement-based refinement improves pseudo-labels.
AI.result: On BraTS 2021, validation DSC improves from 0.393 (10% data) to 0.872 (100%). The teacher reaches 0.922 DSC. The student surpasses the teacher on subregions (NCR/NET 0.797; Edema 0.980) and recovers the Enhancing class (0.620) where the teacher failed.
AI.conclusion: Confidence-driven curricula and selective unlearning enable robust segmentation with limited supervision and noisy pseudo-labels, demonstrating data efficiency and robustness to label noise.

title: The Connection between Kriging and Large Neural Networks
summary: AI has impacted many disciplines and is nowadays ubiquitous. In particular, spatial statistics is in a pivotal moment where it will increasingly intertwine with AI. In this scenario, a relevant question is what relationship spatial statistics models have with machine learning (ML) models, if any. In particular, in this paper, we explore the connections between Kriging and neural networks. At first glance, they may appear unrelated. Kriging - and its ML counterpart, Gaussian process regression - are grounded in probability theory and stochastic processes, whereas many ML models are extensively considered Black-Box models. Nevertheless, they are strongly related. We study their connections and revisit the relevant literature. The understanding of their relations and the combination of both perspectives may enhance ML techniques by making them more interpretable, reliable, and spatially aware.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the links between Kriging/Gaussian process regression and neural networks, arguing that their relationship can enhance interpretability, reliability, and spatial awareness in ML.
AI.motivation: Bridge spatial statistics and machine learning by clarifying how Kriging and neural-network models relate, to leverage probabilistic grounding for more interpretable and spatially aware ML.
AI.method: A literature-driven analysis that maps conceptual and technical connections between Kriging/GP regression and neural networks, and discusses implications for integration.
AI.result: It clarifies conceptual links between GP-based Kriging and neural networks, synthesizes relevant literature, and outlines potential integration strategies to improve ML with interpretability, reliability, and spatial awareness.
AI.conclusion: Understanding and combining Kriging and neural-network perspectives can enrich ML by incorporating probabilistic foundations and spatial awareness.

title: Omni-Video 2: Scaling MLLM-Conditioned Diffusion for Unified Video Generation and Editing
summary: We present Omni-Video 2, a scalable and computationally efficient model that connects pretrained multimodal large-language models (MLLMs) with video diffusion models for unified video generation and editing. Our key idea is to exploit the understanding and reasoning capabilities of MLLMs to produce explicit target captions to interpret user instructions. In this way, the rich contextual representations from the understanding model are directly used to guide the generative process, thereby improving performance on complex and compositional editing. Moreover, a lightweight adapter is developed to inject multimodal conditional tokens into pretrained text-to-video diffusion models, allowing maximum reuse of their powerful generative priors in a parameter-efficient manner. Benefiting from these designs, we scale up Omni-Video 2 to a 14B video diffusion model on meticulously curated training data with quality, supporting high quality text-to-video generation and various video editing tasks such as object removal, addition, background change, complex motion editing, \emph{etc.} We evaluate the performance of Omni-Video 2 on the FiVE benchmark for fine-grained video editing and the VBench benchmark for text-to-video generation. The results demonstrate its superior ability to follow complex compositional instructions in video editing, while also achieving competitive or superior quality in video generation tasks.

AI.tldr: Omni-Video 2 is a scalable framework that integrates pretrained multimodal LLMs with video diffusion via a lightweight adapter to enable unified text-to-video generation and editing at 14B scale, achieving strong compositional editing and competitive generation quality on FiVE and VBench.
AI.motivation: Leverage the reasoning and grounding capabilities of multimodal LLMs to translate complex user instructions into explicit target captions, which guide diffusion-based generation. Use a parameter-efficient adapter to reuse powerful pretrained video diffusion priors for diverse editing tasks.
AI.method: 1) Use an MLLM to generate explicit target captions from user instructions. 2) Introduce a lightweight adapter to inject multimodal conditional tokens into pretrained text-to-video diffusion models. 3) Scale to a 14B video diffusion model trained on high-quality curated data. 4) Evaluate on FiVE (fine-grained video editing) and VBench (text-to-video generation).
AI.result: Omni-Video 2 achieves superior ability to follow complex compositional instructions in video editing and delivers competitive or superior quality in video generation tasks. It supports diverse editing operations (object removal/addition, background changes, complex motion edits) while maintaining high-quality generation.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates a scalable, efficient approach to unified video generation and editing by bridging MLLMs and diffusion models through a parameter-efficient adapter, with strong empirical performance on standard benchmarks and capabilities for complex editing.

title: USBD: Universal Structural Basis Distillation for Source-Free Graph Domain Adaptation
summary: SF-GDA is pivotal for privacy-preserving knowledge transfer across graph datasets. Although recent works incorporate structural information, they implicitly condition adaptation on the smoothness priors of sourcetrained GNNs, thereby limiting their generalization to structurally distinct targets. This dependency becomes a critical bottleneck under significant topological shifts, where the source model misinterprets distinct topological patterns unseen in the source domain as noise, rendering pseudo-label-based adaptation unreliable. To overcome this limitation, we propose the Universal Structural Basis Distillation, a framework that shifts the paradigm from adapting a biased model to learning a universal structural basis for SF-GDA. Instead of adapting a biased source model to a specific target, our core idea is to construct a structure-agnostic basis that proactively covers the full spectrum of potential topological patterns. Specifically, USBD employs a bi-level optimization framework to distill the source dataset into a compact structural basis. By enforcing the prototypes to span the full Dirichlet energy spectrum, the learned basis explicitly captures diverse topological motifs, ranging from low-frequency clusters to high-frequency chains, beyond those present in the source. This ensures that the learned basis creates a comprehensive structural covering capable of handling targets with disparate structures. For inference, we introduce a spectral-aware ensemble mechanism that dynamically activates the optimal prototype combination based on the spectral fingerprint of the target graph. Extensive experiments on benchmarks demonstrate that USBD significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, particularly in scenarios with severe structural shifts, while achieving superior computational efficiency by decoupling the adaptation cost from the target data scale.

AI.tldr: USBD learns a universal, structure-agnostic basis for SF-GDA to cover the full Dirichlet energy spectrum, avoiding reliance on source-model priors and enabling robust generalization under topological shifts via a spectral-aware ensemble.
AI.motivation: Current SF-GDA methods rely on smoothness priors derived from the source-trained GNN, which hampers generalization when target graphs have different topologies. A structure-agnostic basis could provide universal support across diverse graphs.
AI.method: Bi-level optimization distills the source data into a compact structural basis (prototypes) whose spectral components span the full Dirichlet energy spectrum. Inference uses a spectral-aware ensemble that selects prototype combinations based on the target's spectral fingerprint.
AI.result: Empirical results show USBD outperforms state-of-the-art SF-GDA methods, especially under severe structural shifts, while decoupling adaptation cost from target data size to improve computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Learning a universal structural basis enables robust, efficient SF-GDA across structurally diverse graphs, mitigating biases from source-model priors and improving generalization.

title: Any-to-All MRI Synthesis: A Unified Foundation Model for Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma and Its Downstream Applications
summary: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is essential for nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC) radiotherapy (RT), but practical constraints, such as patient discomfort, long scan times, and high costs often lead to incomplete modalities in clinical practice, compromising RT planning accuracy. Traditional MRI synthesis methods are modality-specific, limited in anatomical adaptability, and lack clinical interpretability-failing to meet NPC's RT needs. Here, we developed a unified foundation model integrating contrastive visual representation learning and vision-language alignment (VLA) to enable any-to-all MRI synthesis. The model uses a contrastive encoder for modality-invariant representations and a CLIP-based text-informed decoder for semantically consistent synthesis, supporting any-to-all MRI synthesis via one unified foundation model. Trained on 40,825 images from 13 institutions, it achieves consistently high performance (average SSIM 0.90, PSNR 27) across 26 internal/external validation sites (15,748 images), with superior synthesis fidelity and robustness to noise and domain shifts. Meanwhile, its unified representation enhances downstream RT-relevant tasks (e.g., segmentation). This work advances digital medicine solutions for NPC care by leveraging foundation models to bridge technical synthesis and clinical utility.

AI.tldr: A unified foundation model enables any-to-all MRI synthesis for nasopharyngeal carcinoma radiotherapy by aligning contrastive representations with a CLIP-based decoder, delivering high-fidelity synthesis across multiple sites and improving RT planning tasks.
AI.motivation: To address practical constraints in NPC MRI for radiotherapy (patient discomfort, long scan times, high costs) and overcome modality-specific, non-interpretable synthesis by enabling adaptable, clinically interpretable MRI synthesis that supports RT planning.
AI.method: Develop a single foundation model combining a contrastive encoder for modality-invariant representations with a CLIP-inspired text-informed decoder for semantically consistent synthesis; enables any-to-all MRI synthesis; trained on 40,825 images from 13 institutions; evaluated across 26 validation sites and 15,748 images; robust to noise and domain shifts.
AI.result: Average SSIM ≈ 0.90 and PSNR ≈ 27 across 26 validation sites; superior synthesis fidelity; robust to noise and domain shifts; improved downstream RT-relevant tasks (e.g., segmentation) due to unified representations.
AI.conclusion: Foundation models can bridge technical MRI synthesis and clinical utility in NPC care, enabling robust, adaptable MRI synthesis for radiotherapy planning and enhancing downstream tasks, ultimately advancing digital medicine solutions for NPC care.

title: VideoVeritas: AI-Generated Video Detection via Perception Pretext Reinforcement Learning
summary: The growing capability of video generation poses escalating security risks, making reliable detection increasingly essential. In this paper, we introduce VideoVeritas, a framework that integrates fine-grained perception and fact-based reasoning. We observe that while current multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) exhibit strong reasoning capacity, their granular perception ability remains limited. To mitigate this, we introduce Joint Preference Alignment and Perception Pretext Reinforcement Learning (PPRL). Specifically, rather than directly optimizing for detection task, we adopt general spatiotemporal grounding and self-supervised object counting in the RL stage, enhancing detection performance with simple perception pretext tasks. To facilitate robust evaluation, we further introduce MintVid, a light yet high-quality dataset containing 3K videos from 9 state-of-the-art generators, along with a real-world collected subset that has factual errors in content. Experimental results demonstrate that existing methods tend to bias towards either superficial reasoning or mechanical analysis, while VideoVeritas achieves more balanced performance across diverse benchmarks.

AI.tldr: VideoVeritas presents a framework that fuses fine-grained perception with fact-based reasoning via PPRL to enhance video deception detection, complemented by MintVid, a new dataset for robust evaluation; results claim balanced performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current multi-modal large language models have strong reasoning but limited granular perception, which hampers reliable detection of manipulated videos. A robust detector should combine both high-level reasoning and precise perception, and evaluate across varied, realistic data.
AI.method: Propose Joint Preference Alignment and Perception Pretext Reinforcement Learning (PPRL). In the RL stage, optimize for general spatiotemporal grounding and self-supervised object counting as perception pretext tasks rather than the detection task itself, to improve downstream detection. Introduce MintVid, a dataset with 3k videos from 9 generation methods plus real-world subset with factual errors.
AI.result: Empirical results show existing methods biased toward either superficial reasoning or mechanical analysis, while VideoVeritas achieves more balanced performance across diverse benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: VideoVeritas advances robust video deception detection by integrating fine-grained perception and fact-based reasoning, aided by MintVid for robust evaluation. Potential limitations include generalization to unseen generators and reliance on synthetic data biases; further work could enhance real-world generalization and address potential annotation noise.

title: Estimating Aleatoric Uncertainty in the Causal Treatment Effect
summary: Previous work on causal inference has primarily focused on averages and conditional averages of treatment effects, with significantly less attention on variability and uncertainty in individual treatment responses. In this paper, we introduce the variance of the treatment effect (VTE) and conditional variance of treatment effect (CVTE) as the natural measure of aleatoric uncertainty inherent in treatment responses, and we demonstrate that these quantities are identifiable from observed data under mild assumptions, even in the presence of unobserved confounders. We further propose nonparametric kernel-based estimators for VTE and CVTE, and our theoretical analysis establishes their convergence. We also test the performance of our method through extensive empirical experiments on both synthetic and semi-simulated datasets, where it demonstrates superior or comparable performance to naive baselines.
