AI.result: Empirical results show up to 66% performance improvements over state-of-the-art benchmarks across regular and irregular environments, with faster convergence and improved computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: DGRL enables scalable, stable RL in extremely large and hybrid action spaces, offering provable properties (local support, monotonic improvement) and broad applicability without reliance on hierarchical action decompositions.

title: ERIS: Enhancing Privacy and Communication Efficiency in Serverless Federated Learning
summary: Scaling federated learning (FL) to billion-parameter models introduces critical trade-offs between communication efficiency, model accuracy, and privacy guarantees. Existing solutions often tackle these challenges in isolation, sacrificing accuracy or relying on costly cryptographic tools. We propose ERIS, a serverless FL framework that balances privacy and accuracy while eliminating the server bottleneck and distributing the communication load. ERIS combines a model partitioning strategy, distributing aggregation across multiple client-side aggregators, with a distributed shifted gradient compression mechanism. We theoretically prove that ERIS (i) converges at the same rate as FedAvg under standard assumptions, and (ii) bounds mutual information leakage inversely with the number of aggregators, enabling strong privacy guarantees with no accuracy degradation. Experiments across image and text tasks, including large language models, confirm that ERIS achieves FedAvg-level accuracy while substantially reducing communication cost and improving robustness to membership inference and reconstruction attacks, without relying on heavy cryptography or noise injection.

AI.tldr: ERIS is a serverless federated learning framework that partitions models and distributes aggregation across multiple client-side aggregators using distributed shifted gradient compression, achieving FedAvg-level accuracy with substantially reduced communication and improved privacy without heavy cryptography.
AI.motivation: Scaling FL to billion-parameter models creates bottlenecks in communication, privacy, and server load. Existing approaches trade off accuracy, rely on costly cryptography, or fail to remove central bottlenecks. ERIS targets accuracy, privacy, and scalability together in a serverless setting.
AI.method: ERIS employs a model-partitioning strategy distributing aggregation across multiple client-side aggregators and a distributed shifted gradient compression mechanism. Theoretical analysis shows convergence at the FedAvg rate under standard assumptions, and mutual information leakage is bounded inversely by the number of aggregators. Empirical evaluation on image and text tasks, including large language models, demonstrates FedAvg-level accuracy with reduced communication and enhanced robustness to membership inference and reconstruction attacks without heavy cryptography or noise injection.
AI.result: Convergence at the same rate as FedAvg; mutual information leakage decreases with more aggregators; substantial communication cost reductions; improved robustness to privacy attacks; maintained accuracy across tasks including LLMs; no reliance on heavy cryptography or noise injection.
AI.conclusion: ERIS delivers a scalable, serverless FL framework that preserves accuracy while improving privacy and reducing communication, by combining model partitioning with distributed aggregation and gradient compression. It removes the server bottleneck and shows practical viability for large models and diverse tasks.

title: Sparse Models, Sparse Safety: Unsafe Routes in Mixture-of-Experts LLMs
summary: By introducing routers to selectively activate experts in Transformer layers, the mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture significantly reduces computational costs in large language models (LLMs) while maintaining competitive performance, especially for models with massive parameters. However, prior work has largely focused on utility and efficiency, leaving the safety risks associated with this sparse architecture underexplored. In this work, we show that the safety of MoE LLMs is as sparse as their architecture by discovering unsafe routes: routing configurations that, once activated, convert safe outputs into harmful ones. Specifically, we first introduce the Router Safety importance score (RoSais) to quantify the safety criticality of each layer's router. Manipulation of only the high-RoSais router(s) can flip the default route into an unsafe one. For instance, on JailbreakBench, masking 5 routers in DeepSeek-V2-Lite increases attack success rate (ASR) by over 4$\times$ to 0.79, highlighting an inherent risk that router manipulation may naturally occur in MoE LLMs. We further propose a Fine-grained token-layer-wise Stochastic Optimization framework to discover more concrete Unsafe Routes (F-SOUR), which explicitly considers the sequentiality and dynamics of input tokens. Across four representative MoE LLM families, F-SOUR achieves an average ASR of 0.90 and 0.98 on JailbreakBench and AdvBench, respectively. Finally, we outline defensive perspectives, including safety-aware route disabling and router training, as promising directions to safeguard MoE LLMs. We hope our work can inform future red-teaming and safeguarding of MoE LLMs. Our code is provided in https://github.com/TrustAIRLab/UnsafeMoE.

AI.tldr: MoE LLMs harbor safety vulnerabilities via sparse router routing. The paper introduces RoSais to quantify the safety criticality of each router, shows that manipulating high-RoSais routers can flip safe outputs into unsafe ones, and introduces F-SOUR to discover Unsafe Routes considering token dynamics. Empirical results demonstrate substantial increases in attack success rate (ASR) on JailbreakBench and AdvBench across four MoE LLM families, with ASR reaching 0.90 and 0.98 respectively; code is released.
AI.motivation: Address overlooked safety risks in sparse mixture-of-experts (MoE) transformers, where router sparsity may create critical routes to produce harmful outputs.
AI.method: Define Router Safety importance score (RoSais) to measure safety criticality of each router. Demonstrate that manipulating high-RoSais routers can flip a model into unsafe behavior. Develop Fine-grained token-layer-wise Stochastic Optimization framework (F-SOUR) to locate Unsafe Routes by considering token sequentiality/dynamics. Evaluate on four representative MoE LLM families; report ASR results. Propose defense ideas such as safety-aware route disabling and router training.
AI.result: Masking just five routers can raise ASR to 0.79 on JailbreakBench; average ASR achieved by F-SOUR is 0.90 on JailbreakBench and 0.98 on AdvBench across four MoE LLM families. Code provided.
AI.conclusion: Router-level safety in MoE LLMs is a real concern; must pursue safeguarding strategies (e.g., safety-aware routing controls and targeted router training) and engage in red-teaming to improve MoE safety.

title: CauScale: Neural Causal Discovery at Scale
summary: Causal discovery is essential for advancing data-driven fields such as scientific AI and data analysis, yet existing approaches face significant time- and space-efficiency bottlenecks when scaling to large graphs. To address this challenge, we present CauScale, a neural architecture designed for efficient causal discovery that scales inference to graphs with up to 1000 nodes. CauScale improves time efficiency via a reduction unit that compresses data embeddings and improves space efficiency by adopting tied attention weights to avoid maintaining axis-specific attention maps. To keep high causal discovery accuracy, CauScale adopts a two-stream design: a data stream extracts relational evidence from high-dimensional observations, while a graph stream integrates statistical graph priors and preserves key structural signals. CauScale successfully scales to 500-node graphs during training, where prior work fails due to space limitations. Across testing data with varying graph scales and causal mechanisms, CauScale achieves 99.6% mAP on in-distribution data and 84.4% on out-of-distribution data, while delivering 4-13,000 times inference speedups over prior methods. Our project page is at https://github.com/OpenCausaLab/CauScale.

AI.tldr: CauScale is a scalable neural architecture for causal discovery that handles graphs up to 1000 nodes with high speed and competitive accuracy, using a data compression unit and tied attention, plus a two-stream framework.
AI.motivation: To overcome time- and space-efficiency bottlenecks in causal discovery on large graphs, enabling inference on larger graphs and faster performance without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: A reduction unit compresses data embeddings; tied attention weights avoid axis-specific attention maps to save memory; a two-stream design with a data stream extracting relational evidence from high-dimensional observations and a graph stream integrating statistical graph priors to preserve structural signals.
AI.result: CauScale scales to 500-node graphs during training (previous methods fail due to space limitations). In testing across varied graph scales and causal mechanisms, it achieves 99.6% mAP on in-distribution data and 84.4% on out-of-distribution data, with 4–13,000x inference speedups over prior methods; project page: https://github.com/OpenCausaLab/CauScale.
AI.conclusion: CauScale demonstrates strong scalability and robustness for causal discovery, balancing accuracy and efficiency, and enabling scalable inference on large graphs (up to ~1000 nodes) with substantial speedups.

title: LEFT: Learnable Fusion of Tri-view Tokens for Unsupervised Time Series Anomaly Detection
summary: As a fundamental data mining task, unsupervised time series anomaly detection (TSAD) aims to build a model for identifying abnormal timestamps without assuming the availability of annotations. A key challenge in unsupervised TSAD is that many anomalies are too subtle to exhibit detectable deviation in any single view (e.g., time domain), and instead manifest as inconsistencies across multiple views like time, frequency, and a mixture of resolutions. However, most cross-view methods rely on feature or score fusion and do not enforce analysis-synthesis consistency, meaning the frequency branch is not required to reconstruct the time signal through an inverse transform, and vice versa. In this paper, we present Learnable Fusion of Tri-view Tokens (LEFT), a unified unsupervised TSAD framework that models anomalies as inconsistencies across complementary representations. LEFT learns feature tokens from three views of the same input time series: frequency-domain tokens that embed periodicity information, time-domain tokens that capture local dynamics, and multi-scale tokens that learns abnormal patterns at varying time series granularities. By learning a set of adaptive Nyquist-constrained spectral filters, the original time series is rescaled into multiple resolutions and then encoded, allowing these multi-scale tokens to complement the extracted frequency- and time-domain information. When generating the fused representation, we introduce a novel objective that reconstructs fine-grained targets from coarser multi-scale structure, and put forward an innovative time-frequency cycle consistency constraint to explicitly regularize cross-view agreement. Experiments on real-world benchmarks show that LEFT yields the best detection accuracy against SOTA baselines, while achieving a 5x reduction on FLOPs and 8x speed-up for training.

AI.tldr: LEFT presents a tri-view unsupervised TSAD framework that learns time-domain, frequency-domain, and multi-scale tokens with cross-view reconstruction and cycle-consistency constraints, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with substantial efficiency gains.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised TSAD struggles with anomalies that are subtle in any single view but become apparent through cross-view inconsistencies. Existing cross-view methods often rely on feature/score fusion without enforcing analysis-synthesis consistency between views.
AI.method: LEFT learns tokens from three input views (frequency-domain, time-domain, and multi-scale representations). It uses adaptive Nyquist-constrained spectral filters to obtain multiple resolutions, enabling multi-scale tokens. A reconstruction objective transfers information from coarse to fine scales, and a time-frequency cycle-consistency constraint enforces cross-view agreement. All components operate in an unsupervised setting.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on real-world benchmarks shows state-of-the-art detection accuracy compared to SOTA baselines, with ~5x reduction in FLOPs and ~8x faster training.
AI.conclusion: Tri-view token fusion with reconstruction and cycle-consistency enhances unsupervised TSAD by leveraging complementary views and multi-scale representations while delivering significant computational efficiency.

title: Projected Gradient Ascent for Efficient Reward-Guided Updates with One-Step Generative Models
summary: We propose a constrained latent optimization method for reward-guided generation that preserves white Gaussian noise characteristics with negligible overhead. Test-time latent optimization can unlock substantially better reward-guided generations from pretrained generative models, but it is prone to reward hacking that degrades quality and also too slow for practical use. In this work, we make test-time optimization both efficient and reliable by replacing soft regularization with hard white Gaussian noise constraints enforced via projected gradient ascent. Our method applies a closed-form projection after each update to keep the latent vector explicitly noise-like throughout optimization, preventing the drift that leads to unrealistic artifacts. This enforcement adds minimal cost: the projection matches the $O(N \log N)$ complexity of standard algorithms such as sorting or FFT and does not practically increase wall-clock time. In experiments, our approach reaches a comparable Aesthetic Score using only 30% of the wall-clock time required by the SOTA regularization-based method, while preventing reward hacking.

AI.tldr: Constrained latent optimization using hard white Gaussian noise constraints via projected gradient ascent for efficient, reliable test-time reward-guided generation without reward hacking.
AI.motivation: Test-time latent optimization can improve reward-guided generation but often suffers from reward hacking and high computational overhead; a method that preserves the noise characteristics of latent space could mitigate artifacts and maintain efficiency.
AI.method: Replace soft regularization with hard noise constraints enforced by a projected gradient ascent step. After each update, apply a closed-form projection to the latent vector to maintain explicit noise-like properties, keeping the latent vector aligned with white Gaussian noise. The projection has O(N log N) complexity (via sorting or FFT) and does not meaningfully increase wall-clock time.
AI.result: Achieves comparable Aesthetic Score with only ~30% of the wall-clock time of the state-of-the-art regularization-based method, while eliminating reward hacking.
AI.conclusion: Hard white Gaussian noise constraint projection during test-time optimization yields efficient, reliable reward-guided generation, preserving latent space noise characteristics and reducing artifacts without sacrificing quality.

title: From Robotics to Sepsis Treatment: Offline RL via Geometric Pessimism
summary: Offline Reinforcement Learning (RL) promises the recovery of optimal policies from static datasets, yet it remains susceptible to the overestimation of out-of-distribution (OOD) actions, particularly in fractured and sparse data manifolds.Current solutions necessitates a trade off between computational efficiency and performance. Methods like CQL offers rigorous conservatism but require tremendous compute power while efficient expectile-based methods like IQL often fail to correct OOD errors on pathological datasets, collapsing to Behavioural Cloning. In this work, we propose Geometric Pessimism, a modular, compute-efficient framework that augments standard IQL with density-based penalty derived from k-nearest-neighbour distances in the state-action embedding space. By pre-computing the penalties applied to each state-action pair our method injects OOD conservatism via reward shaping with a O(1) training overhead. Evaluated on the D4Rl MuJoCo benchmark, our method, Geo-IQL outperforms standard IQL on sensitive and unstable medium-replay tasks by over 18 points, while reducing inter-seed variance by 4x. Furthermore, Geo-IQL does not degrade performance on stable manifolds. Crucially, we validate our algorithm on the MIMIC-III Sepsis critical care dataset. While standard IQL collapses to behaviour cloning, Geo-IQL demonstrates active policy improvement. Maintaining safety constraints, achieving 86.4% terminal agreement with clinicians compared to IQL's 75%. Our results suggest that geometric pessimism provides the necessary regularisation to safely overcome local optima in critical, real-world decision systems.

AI.tldr: Geometric Pessimism (Geo-IQL) augments IQL with a density-based OOD penalty derived from k-nearest-neighbor distances in the state-action embedding space. The penalty is precomputed to enable O(1) training overhead, providing a compute-efficient form of conservatism that improves performance on sensitive/mid-replay offline RL tasks and demonstrates safer policy improvement on a real-world clinical dataset (MIMIC-III Sepsis).
AI.motivation: Offline RL often overestimates OOD actions due to sparse and fractured data manifolds. While conservative methods like CQL are compute-intensive, fast expectile-based methods like IQL can collapse on pathological data. There is a need for a scalable, regularizing approach that enforces OOD conservatism without heavy computation.
AI.method: Introduce Geometric Pessimism by adding a density-based penalty computed from kNN distances in a learned state-action embedding space. Precompute the penalty for each state-action pair to incorporate as reward shaping, achieving O(1) training overhead. Integrate with standard IQL (Geo-IQL). Evaluate on D4RL MuJoCo benchmarks and the MIMIC-III Sepsis dataset, comparing against standard IQL and discussing upstream conservatism.
AI.result: Geo-IQL outperforms standard IQL on unstable, medium-replay MuJoCo tasks by >18 points and reduces inter-seed variance by ~4x, while preserving performance on stable manifolds. On MIMIC-III Sepsis data, Geo-IQL avoids collapse to behavior cloning and achieves higher safety-aligned outcomes (86.4% terminal agreement vs 75%). The approach is computationally efficient and provides regularization to overcome local optima in critical decision systems.
AI.conclusion: Geometric pessimism provides a scalable, regularizing form of OOD conservatism for offline RL, offering practical performance gains and safety improvements in high-stakes domains without sacrificing efficiency.

title: Two-Stage Data Synthesization: A Statistics-Driven Restricted Trade-off between Privacy and Prediction
summary: Synthetic data have gained increasing attention across various domains, with a growing emphasis on their performance in downstream prediction tasks. However, most existing synthesis strategies focus on maintaining statistical information. Although some studies address prediction performance guarantees, their single-stage synthesis designs make it challenging to balance the privacy requirements that necessitate significant perturbations and the prediction performance that is sensitive to such perturbations. We propose a two-stage synthesis strategy. In the first stage, we introduce a synthesis-then-hybrid strategy, which involves a synthesis operation to generate pure synthetic data, followed by a hybrid operation that fuses the synthetic data with the original data. In the second stage, we present a kernel ridge regression (KRR)-based synthesis strategy, where a KRR model is first trained on the original data and then used to generate synthetic outputs based on the synthetic inputs produced in the first stage. By leveraging the theoretical strengths of KRR and the covariant distribution retention achieved in the first stage, our proposed two-stage synthesis strategy enables a statistics-driven restricted privacy--prediction trade-off and guarantee optimal prediction performance. We validate our approach and demonstrate its characteristics of being statistics-driven and restricted in achieving the privacy--prediction trade-off both theoretically and numerically. Additionally, we showcase its generalizability through applications to a marketing problem and five real-world datasets.

AI.tldr: Two-stage synthetic data framework to balance privacy and prediction: stage 1 synthesize pure data then hybridize with real data; stage 2 use kernel ridge regression trained on real data to generate outputs for stage-1 inputs. The approach claims theoretical and numerical guarantees of a restricted privacy–prediction trade-off, with good prediction performance and generalizability to marketing and five real datasets.
AI.motivation: To address the privacy-perturbation vs. prediction-performance trade-off in synthetic data for downstream tasks; existing single-stage schemes struggle to balance these competing objectives.
AI.method: Stage 1: synthesis-then-hybrid — generate pure synthetic data and fuse with original data. Stage 2: KRR-based synthesis — train a kernel ridge regression model on original data and generate synthetic outputs for the synthetic inputs from stage 1. Leverages properties of KRR and distribution retention to achieve a statistics-driven restricted privacy–prediction trade-off and near-optimal prediction performance.
AI.result: The approach is validated theoretically and numerically, demonstrating a statistics-driven restricted privacy–prediction trade-off and near-optimal prediction performance. Generalizable to a marketing problem and five real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: A two-stage synthesis framework effectively balances privacy and predictive accuracy, with theoretical guarantees and empirical support, and shows strong generalizability across domains.

title: Equalized Generative Treatment: Matching f-divergences for Fairness in Generative Models
summary: Fairness is a crucial concern for generative models, which not only reflect but can also amplify societal and cultural biases. Existing fairness notions for generative models are largely adapted from classification and focus on balancing the probability of generating samples from each sensitive group. We show that such criteria are brittle, as they can be met even when different sensitive groups are modeled with widely varying quality. To address this limitation, we introduce a new fairness definition for generative models, termed as equalized generative treatment (EGT), which requires comparable generation quality across all sensitive groups, with quality measured via a reference f-divergence. We further analyze the trade-offs induced by EGT, demonstrating that enforcing fairness constraints necessarily couples the overall model quality to that of the most challenging group to approximate. This indicates that a simple yet efficient min-max fine-tuning method should be able to balance f-divergences across sensitive groups to satisfy EGT. We validate this theoretical insight through a set of experiments on both image and text generation tasks. We demonstrate that min-max methods consistently achieve fairer outcomes compared to other approaches from the literature, while maintaining competitive overall performance for both tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces equalized generative treatment (EGT) fairness for generative models, analyzes trade-offs, and shows min-max fine-tuning balances f-divergences across sensitive groups, yielding fairer yet competitive performance in image and text generation.
AI.motivation: Existing fairness notions for generative models borrow from classification and focus on equalizing generation probability across sensitive groups. These criteria can be brittle, as they may be satisfied even when generation quality varies widely between groups. A fairness notion that ensures comparable generation quality across groups is needed.
AI.method: Propose EGT defined via a reference f-divergence to measure generation quality across sensitive groups. Provide theoretical analysis showing that enforcing EGT couples overall model quality to the hardest-to-approximate group. Introduce a simple min-max fine-tuning method to balance f-divergences across groups.
AI.result: Empirical results on image and text generation show that min-max methods achieve fairer outcomes than prior approaches while maintaining competitive overall performance.
AI.conclusion: EGT offers a robust fairness criterion for generative models. Balancing per-group f-divergences via min-max optimization can achieve comparable generation quality across groups without sacrificing overall performance.

title: LLaDA2.1: Speeding Up Text Diffusion via Token Editing
summary: While LLaDA2.0 showcased the scaling potential of 100B-level block-diffusion models and their inherent parallelization, the delicate equilibrium between decoding speed and generation quality has remained an elusive frontier. Today, we unveil LLaDA2.1, a paradigm shift designed to transcend this trade-off. By seamlessly weaving Token-to-Token (T2T) editing into the conventional Mask-to-Token (M2T) scheme, we introduce a joint, configurable threshold-decoding scheme. This structural innovation gives rise to two distinct personas: the Speedy Mode (S Mode), which audaciously lowers the M2T threshold to bypass traditional constraints while relying on T2T to refine the output; and the Quality Mode (Q Mode), which leans into conservative thresholds to secure superior benchmark performances with manageable efficiency degrade. Furthering this evolution, underpinned by an expansive context window, we implement the first large-scale Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework specifically tailored for dLLMs, anchored by specialized techniques for stable gradient estimation. This alignment not only sharpens reasoning precision but also elevates instruction-following fidelity, bridging the chasm between diffusion dynamics and complex human intent. We culminate this work by releasing LLaDA2.1-Mini (16B) and LLaDA2.1-Flash (100B). Across 33 rigorous benchmarks, LLaDA2.1 delivers strong task performance and lightning-fast decoding speed. Despite its 100B volume, on coding tasks it attains an astounding 892 TPS on HumanEval+, 801 TPS on BigCodeBench, and 663 TPS on LiveCodeBench.

AI.tldr: LLaDA2.1 combines Token-to-Token (T2T) editing with Mask-to-Token (M2T) diffusion decoding via a configurable threshold scheme to trade speed for quality. It introduces two modes (Speedy and Quality), adds a large-context RL framework for diffusion-based LLMs, and releases LLaDA2.1-Mini (16B) and LLaDA2.1-Flash (100B). It achieves very high decoding throughput on coding benchmarks while maintaining strong task performance.
AI.motivation: Address the persistent speed–quality trade-off in block-diffusion LLMs as model size scales, and push toward practical, high-throughput inference. Extend diffusion-based generation with learning-based alignment (RL) and longer context to improve reasoning and instruction-following.
AI.method: Integrates Token-to-Token editing into the conventional Mask-to-Token scheme to form a joint, configurable threshold-decoding pipeline. Defines two personas: Speedy Mode (lower M2T threshold, relies on T2T for refinement) and Quality Mode (conservative thresholds for higher quality). Develops an RL framework tailored for large diffusion-driven LLMs with stable gradient estimation and a larger context window. Releases two models: LLaDA2.1-Mini (16B) and LLaDA2.1-Flash (100B).
AI.result: Across 33 benchmarks, achieves strong task performance and very fast decoding: up to 892 TPS on HumanEval+ (coding tasks), 801 TPS on BigCodeBench, and 663 TPS on LiveCodeBench for the 100B variant.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that a joint T2T/M2T threshold-decoding strategy, combined with mode-assisted inference and RL-based alignment over extended context, can substantially improve decoding speed while preserving or enhancing task performance in diffusion-based LLMs. The released Mini and Flash variants offer practical deployment options and strong streaming capabilities for coding tasks.

title: Dashed Line Defense: Plug-And-Play Defense Against Adaptive Score-Based Query Attacks
summary: Score-based query attacks pose a serious threat to deep learning models by crafting adversarial examples (AEs) using only black-box access to model output scores, iteratively optimizing inputs based on observed loss values. While recent runtime defenses attempt to disrupt this process via output perturbation, most either require access to model parameters or fail when attackers adapt their tactics. In this paper, we first reveal that even the state-of-the-art plug-and-play defense can be bypassed by adaptive attacks, exposing a critical limitation of existing runtime defenses. We then propose Dashed Line Defense (DLD), a plug-and-play post-processing method specifically designed to withstand adaptive query strategies. By introducing ambiguity in how the observed loss reflects the true adversarial strength of candidate examples, DLD prevents attackers from reliably analyzing and adapting their queries, effectively disrupting the AE generation process. We provide theoretical guarantees of DLD's defense capability and validate its effectiveness through experiments on ImageNet, demonstrating that DLD consistently outperforms prior defenses--even under worst-case adaptive attacks--while preserving the model's predicted labels.

AI.tldr: DLD is a plug-and-play post-processing defense against adaptive score-based black-box attacks, introducing ambiguity in the observed loss to disrupt AE generation; claims theoretical guarantees and ImageNet-level empirical superiority over prior defenses while preserving labels.
AI.motivation: Score-based query attacks exploit black-box access to model outputs to craft adversarial examples (AEs). Existing runtime defenses, especially plug-and-play output perturbations, can be bypassed by adaptive attackers or require model parameters.
AI.method: Dashed Line Defense (DLD) is a plug-and-play post-processing method that adds ambiguity to how the observed loss reflects an input's true adversarial strength, preventing attackers from reliably analyzing and adapting their queries; it comes with theoretical guarantees.
AI.result: Experiments on ImageNet show DLD consistently outperforms prior defenses under worst-case adaptive attacks while preserving the model's predicted labels.
AI.conclusion: DLD provides robust defense against adaptive query strategies with theoretical backing and strong empirical validation, representing a practical, parameter-free addition to runtime defenses.

title: The Theory and Practice of MAP Inference over Non-Convex Constraints
summary: In many safety-critical settings, probabilistic ML systems have to make predictions subject to algebraic constraints, e.g., predicting the most likely trajectory that does not cross obstacles. These real-world constraints are rarely convex, nor the densities considered are (log-)concave. This makes computing this constrained maximum a posteriori (MAP) prediction efficiently and reliably extremely challenging. In this paper, we first investigate under which conditions we can perform constrained MAP inference over continuous variables exactly and efficiently and devise a scalable message-passing algorithm for this tractable fragment. Then, we devise a general constrained MAP strategy that interleaves partitioning the domain into convex feasible regions with numerical constrained optimization. We evaluate both methods on synthetic and real-world benchmarks, showing our approaches outperform constraint-agnostic baselines, and scale to complex densities intractable for SoTA exact solvers.

AI.tldr: Two methods for constrained MAP inference with non-convex, non-log-concave constraints: (i) a tractable fragment with a scalable message-passing algorithm under exact conditions, and (ii) a general partition-and-optimize strategy that convexifies regions. Empirically, they beat unconstrained baselines and scale beyond existing exact solvers.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical ML settings require predictions that respect algebraic constraints; real-world constraints are often non-convex and densities non-log-concave, making constrained MAP hard.
AI.method: 1) Identify conditions under which constrained MAP over continuous variables is exactly and efficiently solvable, and develop a scalable message-passing algorithm for this tractable fragment. 2) Propose a general constrained MAP strategy that partitions the domain into convex feasible regions and applies numerical constrained optimization within regions, interleaving the two steps.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world benchmarks shows the proposed methods outperform constraint-agnostic baselines and scale to densities that are intractable for state-of-the-art exact solvers.
AI.conclusion: The work provides scalable, reliable constrained MAP inference methods for non-convex algebraic constraints, combining a tractable fragment with a partition-and-optimize framework that extends applicability beyond existing exact solvers.

title: CompilerKV: Risk-Adaptive KV Compression via Offline Experience Compilation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) in long-context scenarios are severely constrained by the linear growth of Key-Value (KV) cache memory. Existing KV compression methods rely either on static thresholds and attention-only heuristics or on coarse memory budget allocation. Under tight memory budgets, these methods overlook two key factors: prompt-dependent variation in compression risk and functional heterogeneity across attention heads, which destabilize token selection and lead to tail failures. To address these challenges, we propose CompilerKV, a risk-adaptive and head-aware compression framework that compiles offline experience into reusable decision tables for prefill-only deployment. CompilerKV integrates two key synergistic components: (i) a Head Heterogeneity Table, learned via offline contextual bandits, which assigns head-specific reliability weights to govern functional differences across attention heads explicitly; and (ii) a Risk-Adaptive Threshold Gating mechanism that jointly models attention entropy and local perplexity, transforming prompt-level risk into deployable retention thresholds. Experiments on LongBench show CompilerKV dominates SOTA methods under a 512-token budget, recovering 97.7\% of FullKV performance while achieving up to +5.2 points gain over the strongest competitor.

AI.tldr: A risk-adaptive, head-aware KV compression framework for long-context LLMs that compiles offline decision tables for prefill-only deployment, achieving near FullKV performance at tight budgets.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLMs suffer from linear KV cache growth; under tight memory budgets, static thresholds and coarse budgeting fail to capture prompt-dependent compression risk and head heterogeneity, causing instability and tail failures.
AI.method: Two components: (i) Head Heterogeneity Table learned via offline contextual bandits to assign head-specific reliability weights; (ii) Risk-Adaptive Threshold Gating that jointly models attention entropy and local perplexity to convert prompt-level risk into deployable retention thresholds; results are compiled into decision tables for prefill-only deployment.
AI.result: On LongBench with a 512-token budget, CompilerKV recovers 97.7% of FullKV performance and outperforms the strongest competitor by up to 5.2 points.
AI.conclusion: CompilerKV effectively combines head-aware reliability with risk-adaptive gating, enabling offline-deployable KV compression that preserves long-context quality under tight memory budgets.

title: Learning To Sample From Diffusion Models Via Inverse Reinforcement Learning
summary: Diffusion models generate samples through an iterative denoising process, guided by a neural network. While training the denoiser on real-world data is computationally demanding, the sampling procedure itself is more flexible. This adaptability serves as a key lever in practice, enabling improvements in both the quality of generated samples and the efficiency of the sampling process. In this work, we introduce an inverse reinforcement learning framework for learning sampling strategies without retraining the denoiser. We formulate the diffusion sampling procedure as a discrete-time finite-horizon Markov Decision Process, where actions correspond to optional modifications of the sampling dynamics. To optimize action scheduling, we avoid defining an explicit reward function. Instead, we directly match the target behavior expected from the sampler using policy gradient techniques. We provide experimental evidence that this approach can improve the quality of samples generated by pretrained diffusion models and automatically tune sampling hyperparameters.

AI.tldr: Inverse reinforcement learning to learn diffusion-sampling strategies without retraining the denoiser; frames sampling as a discrete-time finite-horizon MDP with actions that modify sampling dynamics; uses policy gradient to directly match target sampler behavior (no explicit reward); improves sample quality and automatically tunes sampling hyperparameters.
AI.motivation: Enable higher-quality and more efficient sampling for pretrained diffusion models without expensive retraining of the denoiser.
AI.method: Formulate diffusion sampling as a finite-horizon MDP where actions correspond to optional modifications of the sampling dynamics. Eschew an explicit reward function; optimize by policy gradient to match the target sampler behavior, effectively performing inverse reinforcement learning to schedule sampling steps.
AI.result: Experimental evidence that the approach improves the quality of samples from pretrained diffusion models and can automatically tune sampling hyperparameters.
AI.conclusion: IRL-based sampling scheduling is a viable, model-agnostic way to enhance diffusion model sampling without retraining the denoiser, yielding better samples and automatic hyperparameter tuning.

title: SoK: The Pitfalls of Deep Reinforcement Learning for Cybersecurity
summary: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has achieved remarkable success in domains requiring sequential decision-making, motivating its application to cybersecurity problems. However, transitioning DRL from laboratory simulations to bespoke cyber environments can introduce numerous issues. This is further exacerbated by the often adversarial, non-stationary, and partially-observable nature of most cybersecurity tasks. In this paper, we identify and systematize 11 methodological pitfalls that frequently occur in DRL for cybersecurity (DRL4Sec) literature across the stages of environment modeling, agent training, performance evaluation, and system deployment. By analyzing 66 significant DRL4Sec papers (2018-2025), we quantify the prevalence of each pitfall and find an average of over five pitfalls per paper. We demonstrate the practical impact of these pitfalls using controlled experiments in (i) autonomous cyber defense, (ii) adversarial malware creation, and (iii) web security testing environments. Finally, we provide actionable recommendations for each pitfall to support the development of more rigorous and deployable DRL-based security systems.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically catalogs 11 methodological pitfalls in applying deep reinforcement learning to cybersecurity (DRL4Sec), analyzes 66 papers (2018–2025) showing frequent pitfalls (avg >5 per paper), demonstrates their practical impact via controlled experiments in autonomous cyber defense, adversarial malware creation, and web security testing, and offers actionable recommendations to improve rigor and deployability of DRL-based security systems.
AI.motivation: DRL has strong potential for cybersecurity but faces challenges when moving from simulations to real or bespoke cyber environments due to adversarial, non-stationary, and partial observability aspects. There is a risk of methodological shortcomings in environment modeling, training, evaluation, and deployment that undermine rigor and deployment viability.
AI.method: Identify and enumerate 11 DRL-for-cybersecurity pitfalls across four stages (environment modeling, agent training, performance evaluation, system deployment); perform a meta-analysis of 66 significant DRL4Sec papers (2018–2025) to quantify pitfall prevalence; conduct controlled experiments in three domains (autonomous cyber defense, adversarial malware creation, web security testing) to demonstrate practical impact; propose principled recommendations for each pitfall.
AI.result: Findings indicate an average of over five pitfalls per paper, confirming widespread methodological shortcomings. Controlled experiments illustrate the tangible negative effects of these pitfalls on DRL-based cybersecurity systems and surface concrete recommendations aimed at improving rigor and deployability.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a structured, actionable framework to recognize, quantify, and mitigate common DRL pitfalls in cybersecurity research, facilitating more rigorous evaluation and smoother deployment of DRL-based security solutions.

title: Reasoning aligns language models to human cognition
comment: 38 pages, 4 main figures, multiple appendix figures
summary: Do language models make decisions under uncertainty like humans do, and what role does chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning play in the underlying decision process? We introduce an active probabilistic reasoning task that cleanly separates sampling (actively acquiring evidence) from inference (integrating evidence toward a decision). Benchmarking humans and a broad set of contemporary large language models against near-optimal reference policies reveals a consistent pattern: extended reasoning is the key determinant of strong performance, driving large gains in inference and producing belief trajectories that become strikingly human-like, while yielding only modest improvements in active sampling. To explain these differences, we fit a mechanistic model that captures systematic deviations from optimal behavior via four interpretable latent variables: memory, strategy, choice bias, and occlusion awareness. This model places humans and models in a shared low-dimensional cognitive space, reproduces behavioral signatures across agents, and shows how chain-of-thought shifts language models toward human-like regimes of evidence accumulation and belief-to-choice mapping, tightening alignment in inference while leaving a persistent gap in information acquisition.

AI.tldr: Extended reasoning (Chain-of-Thought) is the main driver of strong inference performance in active probabilistic reasoning; sampling (evidence acquisition) gains are modest. CoT aligns language-model behavior with human-like belief trajectories and evidence accumulation, but leaves a gap in information acquisition. A four-parameter latent-variable model (memory, strategy, choice bias, occlusion awareness) captures cross-agent deviations and places humans and models in a shared cognitive space.
AI.motivation: To disentangle sampling from inference in decision-making under uncertainty, compare humans and large language models (LLMs) across an active probabilistic task, and quantify the role of chain-of-thought in decision processes using a low-dimensional cognitive model.
AI.method: Design an active probabilistic reasoning task that cleanly separates sampling (evidence acquisition) from inference (evidence integration). Benchmark humans and a broad set of contemporary LLMs against near-optimal reference policies. Fit a mechanistic, low-dimensional latent-variable model with four factors—memory, strategy, choice bias, occlusion awareness—to reproduce observed behaviors and map agents into shared cognitive space. Analyze belief trajectories, evidence accumulation, and belief-to-choice mapping with and without CoT prompts.
AI.result: Across agents, extended reasoning substantially boosts inference performance and yields human-like belief trajectories; active sampling shows only modest gains from added reasoning. The latent-variable model reproduces cross-agent patterns and reveals that CoT shifts LLMs toward human-like evidence accumulation and belief-to-choice mapping, tightening inference alignment while leaving information acquisition relatively impaired.
AI.conclusion: Chain-of-Thought principally enhances inference processing and belief–to–choice mapping, bringing LLMs closer to humans in decision dynamics; however, it does not fully close the gap in information acquisition. A four-parameter latent-space provides a compact, interpretable account of systematic deviations from optimal behavior across humans and models.

title: Trapped by simplicity: When Transformers fail to learn from noisy features
summary: Noise is ubiquitous in data used to train large language models, but it is not well understood whether these models are able to correctly generalize to inputs generated without noise. Here, we study noise-robust learning: are transformers trained on data with noisy features able to find a target function that correctly predicts labels for noiseless features? We show that transformers succeed at noise-robust learning for a selection of $k$-sparse parity and majority functions, compared to LSTMs which fail at this task for even modest feature noise. However, we find that transformers typically fail at noise-robust learning of random $k$-juntas, especially when the boolean sensitivity of the optimal solution is smaller than that of the target function. We argue that this failure is due to a combination of two factors: transformers' bias toward simpler functions, combined with an observation that the optimal function for noise-robust learning typically has lower sensitivity than the target function for random boolean functions. We test this hypothesis by exploiting transformers' simplicity bias to trap them in an incorrect solution, but show that transformers can escape this trap by training with an additional loss term penalizing high-sensitivity solutions. Overall, we find that transformers are particularly ineffective for learning boolean functions in the presence of feature noise.

AI.tldr: Transformers show mixed robustness to feature noise on boolean functions: they succeed on structured tasks (k-sparse parity, majority) but fail on random k-juntas; LSTMs fail earlier; boosting with a sensitivity-penalizing loss helps; overall transformers are not robust for boolean functions under noise.
AI.motivation: Understand how transformer models generalize when input features are corrupted, and explain why they succeed on some function classes but fail on others; examine the role of simplicity bias and function sensitivity.
AI.method: Theoretical and empirical evaluation on boolean function tasks: k-sparse parity, majority, random k-juntas; compare transformers vs LSTMs; analyze sensitivity; propose and test an additional loss term penalizing high-sensitivity solutions to escape traps.
AI.result: Transformers succeed on noise-robust learning for certain function classes (k-sparse parity, majority) but fail for random k-juntas under feature noise; LSTMs fail earlier; the failure correlates with lower sensitivity of the optimal solution vs target; adding a loss term reduces this problem; overall transformers are notably ineffective for learning boolean functions with feature noise.
AI.conclusion: The noise-robust generalization of transformers is function-class dependent; reliance on simplicity bias can lead to incorrect solutions without proper regularization; improving robustness requires sensitivity-aware training objectives.

title: QUOKA: Query-Oriented KV Selection For Efficient LLM Prefill
summary: We present QUOKA: Query-oriented KV selection for efficient attention, a training-free and hardware agnostic sparse attention algorithm for accelerating transformer inference under chunked prefill. While many queries focus on a smaller group of keys in the attention operator, we observe that queries with low cosine similarity with respect to the mean query interact more strongly with more keys and have the greatest contribution to final attention logits. By prioritizing these low cosine similarity queries, the behavior of full attention during the prefill stage can be closely approximated. QUOKA leverages this observation, accelerating attention by (1) first retaining a small set of representative queries and (2) then subselectin the keys most aligned with those queries. Through experiments on Needle-In-A-Haystack, LongBench, RULER, and Math500, we show that, while realizing a 3x reduction in time-to-first-token, 5x speedup in attention on Nvidia GPUs and up to nearly a 7x speedup on Intel Xeon CPUs, QUOKA achieves near-baseline accuracy, utilizing 88% fewer key-value pairs per attention evaluation.

AI.tldr: A training-free, hardware-agnostic sparse attention method (QUOKA) that selects representative queries and then subselects keys to approximate full attention, achieving substantial speedups with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Full attention is computationally expensive in transformers; many queries interact with many keys, and low-cosine queries interact with more keys. By focusing on these queries during prefill, we can approximate full attention with much less work.
AI.method: Identify a small set of representative queries to capture the diversity of queries; for each, select the keys most aligned with these queries (based on cosine similarity) to form a sparse attention approximation during prefill. The approach is training-free and hardware-agnostic, designed for chunked prefill.
AI.result: QUOKA achieves about 3x reduction in time-to-first-token, ~5x speedup in attention on Nvidia GPUs, and up to ~7x on Intel Xeon CPUs, while using 88% fewer key-value pairs and maintaining near-baseline accuracy across benchmarks (Needle-In-A-Haystack, LongBench, RULER, Math500).
AI.conclusion: QUOKA offers a practical, training-free sparse attention strategy that substantially accelerates inference with minimal accuracy loss, suitable for chunked prefill and heterogeneous hardware.

title: Data Reconstruction: Identifiability and Optimization with Sample Splitting
summary: Training data reconstruction from KKT conditions has shown striking empirical success, yet it remains unclear when the resulting KKT equations have unique solutions and, even in identifiable regimes, how to reliably recover solutions by optimization. This work hereby focuses on these two complementary questions: identifiability and optimization. On the identifiability side, we discuss the sufficient conditions for KKT system of two-layer networks with polynomial activations to uniquely determine the training data, providing a theoretical explanation of when and why reconstruction is possible. On the optimization side, we introduce sample splitting, a curvature-aware refinement step applicable to general reconstruction objectives (not limited to KKT-based formulations): it creates additional descent directions to escape poor stationary points and refine solutions. Experiments demonstrate that augmenting several existing reconstruction methods with sample splitting consistently improves reconstruction performance.

AI.tldr: Identifiability conditions for KKT-based data reconstruction in two-layer networks with polynomial activations; introduces sample splitting as a curvature-aware optimization refinement to improve reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Understand when KKT-based reconstruction uniquely recovers training data and how to reliably optimize to that solution, addressing identifiability and optimization challenges.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis for identifiability in two-layer networks with polynomial activations; propose sample splitting as a general refinement technique; test on reconstruction objectives; show additional descent directions to escape poor stationary points.
AI.result: Sufficient conditions for unique recovery of training data under KKT system; empirical improvements in reconstruction when applying sample splitting to existing reconstruction methods.
AI.conclusion: Identifiability can be achieved under certain conditions for polynomial-activated two-layer networks; sample splitting enhances reconstruction performance by providing curvature-aware descent directions; applicable beyond KKT-based objectives.

title: Foundation Inference Models for Ordinary Differential Equations
summary: Ordinary differential equations (ODEs) are central to scientific modelling, but inferring their vector fields from noisy trajectories remains challenging. Current approaches such as symbolic regression, Gaussian process (GP) regression, and Neural ODEs often require complex training pipelines and substantial machine learning expertise, or they depend strongly on system-specific prior knowledge. We propose FIM-ODE, a pretrained Foundation Inference Model that amortises low-dimensional ODE inference by predicting the vector field directly from noisy trajectory data in a single forward pass. We pretrain FIM-ODE on a prior distribution over ODEs with low-degree polynomial vector fields and represent the target field with neural operators. FIM-ODE achieves strong zero-shot performance, matching and often improving upon ODEFormer, a recent pretrained symbolic baseline, across a range of regimes despite using a simpler pretraining prior distribution. Pretraining also provides a strong initialisation for finetuning, enabling fast and stable adaptation that outperforms modern neural and GP baselines without requiring machine learning expertise.

AI.tldr: FIM-ODE is a pretrained foundation model that directly infers ODE vector fields from noisy trajectories in one pass, enabling zero-shot and fine-tuned performance without heavy ML expertise.
AI.motivation: Inferring vector fields from noisy trajectory data is difficult and current methods rely on complex pipelines or strong priors. A pretrained foundation model could amortize this process and generalize across ODEs.
AI.method: Pretrain FIM-ODE on a prior distribution of low-degree polynomial ODEs; represent the vector field with neural operators; perform zero-shot inference on unseen data and enable fast/adaptive fine-tuning.
AI.result: FIM-ODE achieves strong zero-shot performance, matching or exceeding ODEFormer across regimes; pretraining provides robust initialization for fast, stable fine-tuning that outperforms neural and GP baselines without ML expertise.
AI.conclusion: Foundation inference models with neural-operator representations can effectively and efficiently infer ODE vector fields from noisy data, offering strong zero-shot performance and easy adaptation without extensive ML knowledge.

title: On the Expressive Power of GNNs for Boolean Satisfiability
summary: Machine learning approaches to solving Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) aim to replace handcrafted heuristics with learning-based models. Graph Neural Networks have emerged as the main architecture for SAT solving, due to the natural graph representation of Boolean formulas. We analyze the expressive power of GNNs for SAT solving through the lens of the Weisfeiler-Leman (WL) test. As our main result, we prove that the full WL hierarchy cannot, in general, distinguish between satisfiable and unsatisfiable instances. We show that indistinguishability under higher-order WL carries over to practical limitations for WL-bounded solvers that set variables sequentially. We further study the expressivity required for several important families of SAT instances, including regular, random and planar instances. To quantify expressivity needs in practice, we conduct experiments on random instances from the G4SAT benchmark and industrial instances from the International SAT Competition. Our results suggest that while random instances are largely distinguishable, industrial instances often require more expressivity to predict a satisfying assignment.

AI.tldr: WL-based GNNs cannot generally distinguish SAT from UNSAT; higher-order WL indistinguishability limits WL-bounded solvers; results vary by instance type, with industrial problems requiring more expressivity than random ones.
AI.motivation: To understand the theoretical and practical expressivity limits of Graph Neural Networks for SAT solving, using the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy as a lens and connecting it to practical solver behavior.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of the WL hierarchy and its implications for GNN-based SAT solvers, including the translation of higher-order WL indistinguishability to sequential, WL-bounded solvers. Empirical evaluation on random (G4SAT) and industrial SAT instances to assess actual expressivity needs across instance families.
AI.result: Proves that the full WL hierarchy cannot in general distinguish between satisfiable and unsatisfiable SAT instances. Shows that indistinguishability under higher-order WL carries over to practical WL-bounded sequential solvers. Experiments indicate random instances are largely distinguishable by WL, whereas industrial instances require greater expressivity to predict satisfiability.
AI.conclusion: The work reveals fundamental limits of WL-based GNNs for SAT, suggesting that achieving strong performance on industrial problems may require more expressive architectures or additional inductive biases beyond WL-equivalence. The results provide guidance on the expressivity needs for different SAT instance families and can inform solver design and evaluation.

title: Central Dogma Transformer II: An AI Microscope for Understanding Cellular Regulatory Mechanisms
summary:
Current biological AI models lack interpretability -- their internal representations do not correspond to biological relationships that
  researchers can examine. Here we present CDT-II, an "AI microscope" whose attention maps are directly interpretable as regulatory structure.
  By mirroring the central dogma in its architecture, each attention mechanism corresponds to a specific biological relationship: DNA
  self-attention for genomic relationships, RNA self-attention for gene co-regulation, and DNA-to-RNA cross-attention for transcriptional
  control. Using only genomic embeddings and raw per-cell expression, CDT-II enables experimental biologists to observe regulatory networks in
  their own data. Applied to K562 CRISPRi data, CDT-II predicts perturbation effects (per-gene mean $r = 0.84$) and recovers the GFI1B
  regulatory network without supervision (6.6-fold enrichment, $P = 3.5 \times 10^{-17}$). Two distinct attention mechanisms converge on an RNA
  processing module ($P = 1 \times 10^{-16}$). CDT-II establishes mechanism-oriented AI as an alternative to task-oriented approaches, revealing
  regulatory structure rather than merely optimizing predictions.

AI.tldr: CDT-II is an interpretable AI model that maps neural attention to biological regulatory relationships, mirroring the central dogma to make mechanisms visible in single-cell data. It yields strong, unsupervised recovery of regulatory networks and high predictive correlation on CRISPRi perturbations.
AI.motivation: To address the interpretability gap in biological AI by aligning model components with known biological relationships, enabling mechanism-level insights rather than purely predictive performance.
AI.method: A transformer-like architecture whose attention maps correspond to DNA self-attention (genomic relationships), RNA self-attention (gene co-regulation), and DNA-to-RNA cross-attention (transcriptional control). Trained on genomic embeddings and per-cell expression without supervision, evaluated on K562 CRISPRi data.
AI.result: Per-gene mean r = 0.84 for perturbation effect predictions; successful, unsupervised recovery of the GFI1B regulatory network with 6.6-fold enrichment (P = 3.5×10^-17). Two distinct attention components converge on an RNA processing module (P = 1×10^-16).
AI.conclusion: Mechanism-oriented AI can reveal regulatory structure and relationships in biological data, offering interpretability beyond task-focused predictive benchmarks.

title: Redundancy-Free View Alignment for Multimodal Human Activity Recognition with Arbitrarily Missing Views
summary: Multimodal multiview learning seeks to integrate information from diverse sources to enhance task performance. Existing approaches often struggle with flexible view configurations, including arbitrary view combinations, numbers of views, and heterogeneous modalities. Focusing on the context of human activity recognition, we propose RALIS, a model that combines multiview contrastive learning with a mixture-of-experts module to support arbitrary view availability during both training and inference. Instead of trying to reconstruct missing views, an adjusted center contrastive loss is used for self-supervised representation learning and view alignment, mitigating the impact of missing views on multiview fusion. This loss formulation allows for the integration of view weights to account for view quality. Additionally, it reduces computational complexity from $O(V^2)$ to $O(V)$, where $V$ is the number of views. To address residual discrepancies not captured by contrastive learning, we employ a mixture-of-experts module with a specialized load balancing strategy, tasked with adapting to arbitrary view combinations. We highlight the geometric relationship among components in our model and how they combine well in the latent space. RALIS is validated on four datasets encompassing inertial and human pose modalities, with the number of views ranging from three to nine, demonstrating its performance and flexibility.

AI.tldr: RALIS: a flexible multiview learning framework for HAR that supports arbitrary view combinations using adjusted center contrastive loss and a mixture-of-experts, achieving O(V) complexity and robust fusion across 3–9 views.
AI.motivation: To address flexible view configurations and missing modalities in multiview HAR, enabling robust and efficient fusion without view reconstruction.
AI.method: Combines multiview contrastive learning with an adjusted center contrastive loss that aligns views, attaches view-quality weights, reduces pairwise computation to O(V), and uses a mixture-of-experts with load-balancing to adapt to arbitrary view combos.
AI.result: Validated on four HAR datasets with inertial and pose data, showing strong performance and flexibility across 3–9 views, with improved robustness to missing views and reduced computational cost.
AI.conclusion: RALIS enables flexible, efficient multiview fusion for HAR under arbitrary view availability, via a view-weighted center contrastive objective and a mixture-of-experts module that handles residual discrepancies.

title: HoGS: Homophily-Oriented Graph Synthesis for Local Differentially Private GNN Training
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) have demonstrated remarkable performance in various graph-based machine learning tasks by effectively modeling high-order interactions between nodes. However, training GNNs without protection may leak sensitive personal information in graph data, including links and node features. Local differential privacy (LDP) is an advanced technique for protecting data privacy in decentralized networks. Unfortunately, existing local differentially private GNNs either only preserve link privacy or suffer significant utility loss in the process of preserving link and node feature privacy. In this paper, we propose an effective LDP framework, called HoGS, which trains GNNs with link and feature protection by generating a synthetic graph. Concretely, HoGS first collects the link and feature information of the graph under LDP, and then utilizes the phenomenon of homophily in graph data to reconstruct the graph structure and node features separately, thereby effectively mitigating the negative impact of LDP on the downstream GNN training. We theoretically analyze the privacy guarantee of HoGS and conduct experiments using the generated synthetic graph as input to various state-of-the-art GNN architectures. Experimental results on three real-world datasets show that HoGS significantly outperforms baseline methods in the accuracy of training GNNs.

AI.tldr: HoGS provides a local-DP framework for GNNs that protects both links and features by generating a synthetic graph, reconstructed via homophily, to enable accurate downstream GNN training.
AI.motivation: Protect privacy in decentralized GNN training while preserving utility for both link structure and node features under local differential privacy constraints.
AI.method: Collects link and feature information under LDP, then separately reconstructs the graph structure and node features using homophily to generate a synthetic graph used for GNN training; includes theoretical privacy guarantees and evaluation with SOTA GNNs on three real datasets.
AI.result: HoGS significantly improves GNN accuracy on three real-world datasets compared with baseline LDP methods when trained on the generated synthetic graphs.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic graph generation under LDP can mitigate utility loss for GNNs, enabling effective private training; the approach and guarantees invite exploration of broader settings and potential improvements in privacy-utility tradeoffs.

title: FreqLens: Interpretable Frequency Attribution for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Time series forecasting models often lack interpretability, limiting their adoption in domains requiring explainable predictions. We propose \textsc{FreqLens}, an interpretable forecasting framework that discovers and attributes predictions to learnable frequency components. \textsc{FreqLens} introduces two key innovations: (1) \emph{learnable frequency discovery} -- frequency bases are parameterized via sigmoid mapping and learned from data with diversity regularization, enabling automatic discovery of dominant periodic patterns without domain knowledge; and (2) \emph{axiomatic frequency attribution} -- a theoretically grounded framework that provably satisfies Completeness, Faithfulness, Null-Frequency, and Symmetry axioms, with per-frequency attributions equivalent to Shapley values. On Traffic and Weather datasets, \textsc{FreqLens} achieves competitive or superior performance while discovering physically meaningful frequencies: all 5 independent runs discover the 24-hour daily cycle ($24.6 \pm 0.1$h, 2.5\% error) and 12-hour half-daily cycle ($11.8 \pm 0.1$h, 1.6\% error) on Traffic, and weekly cycles ($10\times$ longer than the input window) on Weather. These results demonstrate genuine frequency-level knowledge discovery with formal theoretical guarantees on attribution quality.

AI.tldr: FreqLens presents an interpretable forecasting framework with learnable frequency bases and axiomatic attribution, enabling automatic discovery of dominant periodic patterns and Shapley-like per-frequency attributions with guarantees.
AI.motivation: Time-series forecasting models often lack interpretability; need principled, frequency-level explanations with theoretical attribution guarantees to improve trust and adoption across domains.
AI.method: Two main contributions: (1) learnable frequency discovery where frequency bases are parameterized via sigmoid mapping and learned with diversity regularization to capture dominant periodic patterns without prior domain knowledge; (2) axiomatic frequency attribution that satisfies Completeness, Faithfulness, Null-Frequency, and Symmetry, with per-frequency attributions equivalent to Shapley values; empirical evaluation on Traffic and Weather datasets showing discovery of meaningful frequencies (e.g., 24h and 12h cycles on Traffic; weekly cycles on Weather) and competitive/superior forecasting performance.
AI.result: FreqLens achieves competitive or superior forecasting accuracy while uncovering physically meaningful frequencies. Across five independent runs, it discovers the 24-hour daily cycle (24.6 ± 0.1 h, 2.5% error) and the 12-hour half-daily cycle (11.8 ± 0.1 h, 1.6% error) on Traffic; on Weather, it discovers weekly cycles that are ~10× longer than the input window. The attribution framework provides theoretically grounded, frequency-level explanations with guarantees.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates genuine, data-driven frequency discovery with formal attribution guarantees, achieving interpretable forecasts that align with known physical cycles and offering robust theoretical properties for attribution quality.

title: Default Machine Learning Hyperparameters Do Not Provide Informative Initialization for Bayesian Optimization
summary: Bayesian Optimization (BO) is a standard tool for hyperparameter tuning thanks to its sample efficiency on expensive black-box functions. While most BO pipelines begin with uniform random initialization, default hyperparameter values shipped with popular ML libraries such as scikit-learn encode implicit expert knowledge and could serve as informative starting points that accelerate convergence. This hypothesis, despite its intuitive appeal, has remained largely unexamined. We formalize the idea by initializing BO with points drawn from truncated Gaussian distributions centered at library defaults and compare the resulting trajectories against a uniform-random baseline. We conduct an extensive empirical evaluation spanning three BO back-ends (BoTorch, Optuna, Scikit-Optimize), three model families (Random Forests, Support Vector Machines, Multilayer Perceptrons), and five benchmark datasets covering classification and regression tasks. Performance is assessed through convergence speed and final predictive quality, and statistical significance is determined via one-sided binomial tests. Across all conditions, default-informed initialization yields no statistically significant advantage over purely random sampling, with p-values ranging from 0.141 to 0.908. A sensitivity analysis on the prior variance confirms that, while tighter concentration around the defaults improves early evaluations, this transient benefit vanishes as optimization progresses, leaving final performance unchanged. Our results provide no evidence that default hyperparameters encode useful directional information for optimization. We therefore recommend that practitioners treat hyperparameter tuning as an integral part of model development and favor principled, data-driven search strategies over heuristic reliance on library defaults.

AI.tldr: Initializing Bayesian Optimization with library-default hyperparameters via truncated Gaussian around the defaults yields no significant advantage over uniform random initialization across multiple back-ends and model families; early gains from tighter priors disappear and final performance is unchanged.
AI.motivation: Test whether hyperparameters shipped as library defaults carry informative directional priors that could accelerate BO, reducing search space effectively.
AI.method: Compare BO runs initialized from truncated Gaussian distributions centered at library defaults against uniform-random initialization across three BO back-ends (BoTorch, Optuna, Scikit-Optimize), three model families (RF, SVM, MLP), and five datasets. Evaluate convergence speed and final predictive quality; use one-sided binomial tests for significance; perform a sensitivity analysis on prior variance.
AI.result: No statistically significant advantage observed (p-values 0.141–0.908). Sensitivity analysis shows tighter concentration around defaults improves early evaluations but the benefit vanishes as optimization proceeds; final performance unchanged.
AI.conclusion: Default hyperparameters do not encode useful directional information for BO. Hyperparameter tuning should be treated as integral to model development, favoring principled, data-driven search strategies over heuristics based on library defaults.

title: A Graphop Analysis of Graph Neural Networks on Sparse Graphs: Generalization and Universal Approximation
summary: Generalization and approximation capabilities of message passing graph neural networks (MPNNs) are often studied by defining a compact metric on a space of input graphs under which MPNNs are Hölder continuous. Such analyses are of two varieties: 1) when the metric space includes graphs of unbounded sizes, the theory is only appropriate for dense graphs, and, 2) when studying sparse graphs, the metric space only includes graphs of uniformly bounded size. In this work, we present a unified approach, defining a compact metric on the space of graphs of all sizes, both sparse and dense, under which MPNNs are Hölder continuous. This leads to more powerful universal approximation theorems and generalization bounds than previous works. The theory is based on, and extends, a recent approach to graph limit theory called graphop analysis.

AI.tldr: A unified compact metric on graphs of all sizes makes MPNNs Hölder continuous, enabling stronger universal approximation and generalization results for both sparse and dense graphs, via graphop analysis.
AI.motivation: Existing analyses either handle unbounded dense graphs or sparsely bounded graphs, leaving a gap for a theory that covers all graph sizes within a single compact metric space.
AI.method: Define a compact metric on the space of graphs of all sizes (sparse and dense) using graphop analysis; prove Hölder continuity of MPNNs w.r.t. this metric; derive universal approximation and generalization bounds.
AI.result: MPNNs are Hölder continuous in the new metric; universal approximation theorems and generalization bounds become stronger and apply uniformly across sparse and dense graphs; extends graphop-based graph limit theory to learning with MPNNs.
AI.conclusion: A unified graph size-agnostic metric framework enables robust MPNN generalization and approximation results, advancing graphop-based analysis in learning on graphs of varying sizes.

title: How2Everything: Mining the Web for How-To Procedures to Evaluate and Improve LLMs
summary: Generating step-by-step "how-to" procedures is a key LLM capability: how-to advice is commonly requested in chatbots, and step-by-step planning is critical for reasoning over complex tasks. Yet, measuring and improving procedural validity at scale on real-world tasks remains challenging and understudied. To address this, we introduce How2Everything, a scalable framework to evaluate and improve goal-conditioned procedure generation. Our framework includes How2Mine, which mines 351K procedures from 980K web pages across 14 topics and readily scales to larger corpora. From this pool we build How2Bench, a 7K-example evaluation set balanced across topics. To reliably score model outputs, we develop How2Score, an evaluation protocol that uses an LLM judge to detect whether a generation contains any critical failure that would prevent achieving the goal. For low-cost, reproducible evaluation, we distill a frontier model into an open 8B model, achieving 80.5% agreement with human annotators. How2Bench reveals clear scaling trends across model sizes and training stages, providing signal early in pretraining. Finally, RL using How2Score as a reward improves performance on How2Bench by >10 points across three models without systematic regressions on standard benchmarks, with gains robust to superficial source-document memorization or format compliance. Taken together, How2Everything shows how pretraining web data can support a closed loop of capability evaluation and improvement at scale.

AI.tldr: Proposes How2Everything, a scalable pipeline to mine, evaluate, and improve goal-directed procedural generation using an LLM-judged metric and RL, including an open 8B frontier model; demonstrates scaling benefits and >10-point improvements on How2Bench.
AI.motivation: Procedural validity in step-by-step instructions is critical for real-world tasks but difficult to measure and improve at scale. Existing evaluation methods are limited; a scalable, closed-loop framework that leverages web data for ongoing improvement is needed.
AI.method: How2Mine: mined 351k procedures from 980k web pages across 14 topics. How2Bench: 7k-example evaluation set. How2Score: LLM-based judge to detect critical failures in generation. Distillation to an open 8B frontier model achieving 80.5% agreement with humans. RL using How2Score as reward to improve performance on How2Bench. Demonstrates scaling trends and early signals in pretraining.
AI.result: Found clear scaling trends across model sizes and training stages; RL with How2Score yields >10-point gains on How2Bench across three models with no systematic regression on standard benchmarks; gains are robust to superficial memorization or format compliance.
AI.conclusion: Pretraining on large-scale web data can support a closed-loop framework for evaluating and improving procedural generation at scale, enabling scalable measurement and ongoing enhancement of LLMs' step-by-step capabilities.

title: Efficient Deep Learning for Biometrics: Overview, Challenges and Trends in Ear of Frugal AI
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures, accepted at the 2025 IEEE SDS conference
summary: Recent advances in deep learning, whether on discriminative or generative tasks have been beneficial for various applications, among which security and defense. However, their increasing computational demands during training and deployment translates directly into high energy consumption. As a consequence, this induces a heavy carbon footprint which hinders their widespread use and scalability, but also a limitation when deployed on resource-constrained edge devices for real-time use. In this paper, we briefly survey efficient deep learning methods for biometric applications. Specifically, we tackle the challenges one might incur when training and deploying deep learning approaches, and provide a taxonomy of the various efficient deep learning families. Additionally, we discuss complementary metrics for evaluating the efficiency of these models such as memory, computation, latency, throughput, and advocate for universal and reproducible metrics for better comparison. Last, we give future research directions to consider.

AI.tldr: A survey of efficient deep learning methods for biometric applications, focusing on reducing training and inference energy use, presenting a taxonomy of efficient DL families, discussing evaluation metrics, and outlining future research directions.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models are becoming computationally and energy-intensive, which increases carbon footprint and limits deployment on resource-constrained devices, particularly for biometric security/defense tasks.
AI.method: Literature survey that organizes efficient deep learning approaches into a taxonomy, analyzes metrics for efficiency (memory, computation, latency, throughput), and proposes universal, reproducible evaluation standards, plus a discussion of challenges and future directions.
