AI.result: Empirical results show up to 29x speedups versus CondTSC and up to 10,000x over naive shapelet-based condensation on Sleep dataset (3k timesteps). Downstream classification accuracy improves and ShapeCond outperforms all prior time-series condensation methods across extensive experiments. Code released.
AI.conclusion: ShapeCond effectively preserves key local patterns in time series, enabling scalable, accurate condensation and setting a new state-of-the-art for time-series dataset condensation.

title: ANCRe: Adaptive Neural Connection Reassignment for Efficient Depth Scaling
summary: Scaling network depth has been a central driver behind the success of modern foundation models, yet recent investigations suggest that deep layers are often underutilized. This paper revisits the default mechanism for deepening neural networks, namely residual connections, from an optimization perspective. Rigorous analysis proves that the layout of residual connections can fundamentally shape convergence behavior, and even induces an exponential gap in convergence rates. Prompted by this insight, we introduce adaptive neural connection reassignment (ANCRe), a principled and lightweight framework that parameterizes and learns residual connectivities from the data. ANCRe adaptively reassigns residual connections with negligible computational and memory overhead ($<1\%$), while enabling more effective utilization of network depth. Extensive numerical tests across pre-training of large language models, diffusion models, and deep ResNets demonstrate consistently accelerated convergence, boosted performance, and enhanced depth efficiency over conventional residual connections.

AI.tldr: Residual layouts shape optimization and convergence; adaptive residual reassignment (ANCRe) learns skip patterns with minimal overhead to improve depth efficiency and convergence across LLMs, diffusion models, and ResNets.
AI.motivation: Deep networks with residual connections can be underutilized due to suboptimal skip layouts. Understanding and optimizing residual connectivity can unlock better depth usage and faster convergence.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis showing how residual connection layouts affect convergence rates, revealing an exponential gap. Proposes ANCRe—a lightweight framework that parameterizes and learns residual connectivities from data, with negligible overhead (<1%).
AI.result: ANCRe yields consistently faster convergence, better performance, and enhanced depth efficiency across large-scale pretraining of language models, diffusion models, and deep ResNets compared to conventional residual connections.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive learning of residual connectivities enables more effective use of network depth with minimal cost, offering a new design principle for deep architectures and optimization.

title: Next-Gen CAPTCHAs: Leveraging the Cognitive Gap for Scalable and Diverse GUI-Agent Defense
summary: The rapid evolution of GUI-enabled agents has rendered traditional CAPTCHAs obsolete. While previous benchmarks like OpenCaptchaWorld established a baseline for evaluating multimodal agents, recent advancements in reasoning-heavy models, such as Gemini3-Pro-High and GPT-5.2-Xhigh have effectively collapsed this security barrier, achieving pass rates as high as 90% on complex logic puzzles like "Bingo". In response, we introduce Next-Gen CAPTCHAs, a scalable defense framework designed to secure the next-generation web against the advanced agents. Unlike static datasets, our benchmark is built upon a robust data generation pipeline, allowing for large-scale and easily scalable evaluations, notably, for backend-supported types, our system is capable of generating effectively unbounded CAPTCHA instances. We exploit the persistent human-agent "Cognitive Gap" in interactive perception, memory, decision-making, and action. By engineering dynamic tasks that require adaptive intuition rather than granular planning, we re-establish a robust distinction between biological users and artificial agents, offering a scalable and diverse defense mechanism for the agentic era.

AI.tldr: Next-Gen CAPTCHAs: a scalable, dynamic defense framework that leverages the human–AI cognitive gap with a data-generation pipeline to create effectively unbounded CAPTCHA instances, countering high performance by modern GUI-enabled agents.
AI.motivation: Traditional CAPTCHAs are increasingly ineffective as reasoning-heavy agents surpass static benchmarks; there is a need for scalable, robust defenses that can adapt to evolving AI capabilities in the web ecosystem.
AI.method: Proposes a scalable data-generation pipeline to produce large-scale CAPTCHA instances, including backend-supported types. Designs dynamic tasks that emphasize interactive perception, memory, decision-making, and action, forcing adaptive intuition rather than granular planning to distinguish humans from agents.
AI.result: A framework enabling effectively unbounded CAPTCHA instances for backend-supported types, exploiting the persistent human–agent cognitive gap to re-establish a robust human–agent distinction in interactive tasks.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, adaptive CAPTCHA tasks offer a scalable and diverse defense for the agentic era, restoring the biological user distinction against advanced agents.

title: NLP Sampling: Combining MCMC and NLP Methods for Diverse Constrained Sampling
summary: Generating diverse samples under hard constraints is a core challenge in many areas. With this work we aim to provide an integrative view and framework to combine methods from the fields of MCMC, constrained optimization, as well as robotics, and gain insights in their strengths from empirical evaluations. We propose NLP Sampling as a general problem formulation, propose a family of restarting two-phase methods as a framework to integrated methods from across the fields, and evaluate them on analytical and robotic manipulation planning problems. Complementary to this, we provide several conceptual discussions, e.g. on the role of Lagrange parameters, global sampling, and the idea of a Diffused NLP and a corresponding model-based denoising sampler.

AI.tldr: Proposes NLP Sampling: a general framework to generate diverse samples under hard constraints by integrating MCMC, constrained optimization, and robotics. Introduces a restarting two-phase method to unify methods across fields; evaluated on analytical and robotic manipulation planning tasks; discusses Lagrange parameters, global sampling, Diffused NLP, and model-based denoising samplers.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of producing diverse, feasible samples under hard constraints across disciplines (MCMC, optimization, robotics) and to leverage complementary strengths through an integrative framework.
AI.method: Formulates NLP Sampling as a general problem; introduces a restarting two-phase framework to integrate methods from MCMC, constrained optimization, and robotics; conducts empirical evaluations on analytical and robotic manipulation planning problems; includes conceptual discussions on Lagrange parameters, global sampling, and diffusion-based denoising approaches.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on analytical and robotic manipulation planning problems demonstrate the framework’s viability and the utility of the restarting two-phase approach for generating diverse, constrained samples across domains.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a unified framework and multiple conceptual insights (Lagrange parameters, global sampling, Diffused NLP, model-based denoising). It highlights cross-field strengths and lays groundwork for further integration and diffusion-based approaches in constrained sampling.

title: Speech Emotion Recognition Leveraging OpenAI's Whisper Representations and Attentive Pooling Methods
summary: Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) research has faced limitations due to the lack of standard and sufficiently large datasets. Recent studies have leveraged pre-trained models to extract features for downstream tasks such as SER. This work explores the capabilities of Whisper, a pre-trained ASR system, in speech emotion recognition by proposing two attention-based pooling methods, Multi-head Attentive Average Pooling and QKV Pooling, designed to efficiently reduce the dimensionality of Whisper representations while preserving emotional features. We experiment on English and Persian, using the IEMOCAP and ShEMO datasets respectively, with Whisper Tiny and Small. Our multi-head QKV architecture achieves state-of-the-art results on the ShEMO dataset, with a 2.47% improvement in unweighted accuracy. We further compare the performance of different Whisper encoder layers and find that intermediate layers often perform better for SER on the Persian dataset, providing a lightweight and efficient alternative to much larger models such as HuBERT X-Large. Our findings highlight the potential of Whisper as a representation extractor for SER and demonstrate the effectiveness of attention-based pooling for dimension reduction.

AI.tldr: Whisper, a pre-trained ASR model, is explored as a feature extractor for Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) using two attention-based pooling methods (Multi-head Attentive Average Pooling and QKV Pooling). The approach yields state-of-the-art performance on Persian ShEMO and competitive results on English IEMOCAP, with intermediate Whisper layers often performing better for Persian. This demonstrates Whisper's potential for SER with lightweight pooling strategies.
AI.motivation: Address limitations from small/uneven SER datasets and the need for robust feature extractors. Leverage large pre-trained models (Whisper) to improve SER performance across languages with efficient pooling to reduce dimensionality.
AI.method: Propose two attention-based pooling schemes—Multi-head Attentive Average Pooling and QKV Pooling—to compress Whisper encoder outputs into emotion-relevant representations. Evaluate on English (IEMOCAP) and Persian (ShEMO) using Whisper Tiny and Small, and analyze different Whisper encoder layers.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art unweighted accuracy on ShEMO with a 2.47% improvement. Intermediate Whisper encoder layers can outperform deeper layers for Persian SER, providing a lightweight alternative to large models such as HuBERT X-Large. Overall, Whisper shows strong potential as a SER representation extractor, with attention-based pooling effectively preserving emotional cues during dimensionality reduction.
AI.conclusion: Whisper can serve as an effective representation extractor for SER across languages when coupled with efficient attention-based pooling. The approach enables light, high-performing SER systems and highlights the benefit of selecting suitable encoder depths for target languages (e.g., Persian).

title: Graph-Based Nearest-Neighbor Search without the Spread
summary: $\renewcommand{\Re}{\mathbb{R}}$Recent work showed how to construct nearest-neighbor graphs of linear size, on a given set $P$ of $n$ points in $\Re^d$, such that one can answer approximate nearest-neighbor queries in logarithmic time in the spread. Unfortunately, the spread might be unbounded in $n$, and an interesting theoretical question is how to remove the dependency on the spread. Here, we show how to construct an external linear-size data structure that, combined with the linear-size graph, allows us to answer ANN queries in logarithmic time in $n$.

AI.tldr: A linear-size external data structure, paired with a linear-size nearest-neighbor graph, yields approximate nearest-neighbor queries in O(log n) time while removing dependence on spread.
AI.motivation: Remove spread dependence in approximate nearest-neighbor search while maintaining linear storage, improving query efficiency for arbitrary point sets.
AI.method: Construct an external linear-size data structure that, when combined with the existing linear-size NN graph, enables ANN queries to be answered in logarithmic time in the number of points n.
AI.result: Queries can be answered in O(log n) time in n, with the spread no longer affecting the bound, using linear storage.
AI.conclusion: The combination of a linear-size NN graph with an external linear-size DS achieves spread-independent, logarithmic-time ANN queries, enhancing practical scalability for large datasets.

title: Scalable spatial point process models for forensic footwear analysis
summary: Shoe print evidence recovered from crime scenes plays a key role in forensic investigations. By examining shoe prints, investigators can determine details of the footwear worn by suspects. However, establishing that a suspect's shoes match the make and model of a crime scene print may not be sufficient. Typically, thousands of shoes of the same size, make, and model are manufactured, any of which could be responsible for the print. Accordingly, a popular approach used by investigators is to examine the print for signs of ``accidentals,'' i.e., cuts, scrapes, and other features that accumulate on shoe soles after purchase due to wear. While some patterns of accidentals are common on certain types of shoes, others are highly distinctive, potentially distinguishing the suspect's shoe from all others. Quantifying the rarity of a pattern is thus essential to accurately measuring the strength of forensic evidence. In this study, we address this task by developing a hierarchical Bayesian model. Our improvement over existing methods primarily stems from two advancements. First, we frame our approach in terms of a latent Gaussian model, thus enabling inference to be efficiently scaled to large collections of annotated shoe prints via integrated nested Laplace approximations. Second, we incorporate spatially varying coefficients to model the relationship between shoes' tread patterns and accidental locations. We demonstrate these improvements through superior performance on held-out data, which enhances accuracy and reliability in forensic shoe print analysis.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical Bayesian latent Gaussian model for forensic shoe print analysis using INLA for scalable inference and spatially varying coefficients to link tread patterns to accidentals, improving accuracy on held-out data.
AI.motivation: Quantify the rarity of accidental features on shoe soles to strengthen forensic evidence; address the abundance of similar shoes and the need for scalable, spatially aware inference.
AI.method: Frame the problem as a latent Gaussian model and perform inference with integrated nested Laplace approximations (INLA). Incorporate spatially varying coefficients to model how tread patterns relate to the locations of accidentals across shoe prints within a hierarchical Bayesian framework.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior performance on held-out data, with enhanced accuracy and reliability in forensic shoe print analysis compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: A scalable, spatially aware Bayesian approach improves quantification of pattern rarity in shoe prints, enabling more robust forensic assessments and potentially benefiting large annotated shoe print collections.

title: Where Not to Learn: Prior-Aligned Training with Subset-based Attribution Constraints for Reliable Decision-Making
summary: Reliable models should not only predict correctly, but also justify decisions with acceptable evidence. Yet conventional supervised learning typically provides only class-level labels, allowing models to achieve high accuracy through shortcut correlations rather than the intended evidence. Human priors can help constrain such behavior, but aligning models to these priors remains challenging because learned representations often diverge from human perception. To address this challenge, we propose an attribution-based human prior alignment method. We encode human priors as input regions that the model is expected to rely on (e.g., bounding boxes), and leverage a highly faithful subset-selection-based attribution approach to expose the model's decision evidence during training. When the attribution region deviates substantially from the prior regions, we penalize reliance on off-prior evidence, encouraging the model to shift its attribution toward the intended regions. This is achieved through a training objective that imposes attribution constraints induced by the human prior. We validate our method on both image classification and click decision tasks in MLLM-based GUI agent models. Across conventional classification and autoregressive generation settings, human prior alignment consistently improves task accuracy while also enhancing the model's decision reasonability.

AI.tldr: Proposes attribution-based human-prior alignment by constraining model attributions to user-specified regions to reduce reliance on spurious evidence; shows improvements in accuracy and explainability in image classification and MLLM-driven GUI tasks.
AI.motivation: Conventional supervised learning often relies on shortcut correlations and coarse class labels, yielding models whose internal evidence diverges from human perception. There is a need to align model explanations and decision evidence with human priors to improve robustness and interpretability across tasks.
AI.method: Encode human priors as input attribution regions (e.g., bounding boxes). Use a faithful subset-selection attribution method to reveal decision evidence during training. Penalize reliance on off-prior evidence when attribution deviates from priors, enforcing an attribution-constrained objective.
AI.result: The approach yields improvements in task accuracy and decision reasonability across image classification and autoregressive generation tasks in MLLM-based GUI agent models.
AI.conclusion: Attribution-based human-prior alignment effectively guides model explanations toward human-aligned regions, improving both performance and interpretability across diverse tasks.

title: DLLM-Searcher: Adapting Diffusion Large Language Model for Search Agents
summary: Recently, Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) have demonstrated unique efficiency advantages, enabled by their inherently parallel decoding mechanism and flexible generation paradigm. Meanwhile, despite the rapid advancement of Search Agents, their practical deployment is constrained by a fundamental limitation, termed as 1) Latency Challenge: the serial execution of multi-round reasoning, tool calling, and tool response waiting under the ReAct agent paradigm induces severe end-to-end latency. Intuitively, dLLMs can leverage their distinctive strengths to optimize the operational efficiency of agents under the ReAct agent paradigm. Practically, existing dLLM backbones face the 2) Agent Ability Challenge. That is, existing dLLMs exhibit remarkably weak reasoning and tool-calling capabilities, preventing these advantages from being effectively realized in practice. In this paper, we propose DLLM-Searcher, an optimization framework for dLLM-based Search Agents. To solve the Agent Ability Challenge, we design a two-stage post-training pipeline encompassing Agentic Supervised Fine-Tuning (Agentic SFT) and Agentic Variance-Reduced Preference Optimization Agentic VRPO, which enhances the backbone dLLM's information seeking and reasoning capabilities. To mitigate the Latency Challenge, we leverage the flexible generation mechanism of dLLMs and propose a novel agent paradigm termed Parallel-Reasoning and Acting P-ReAct. P-ReAct guides the model to prioritize decoding tool_call instructions, thereby allowing the model to keep thinking while waiting for the tool's return. Experimental results demonstrate that DLLM-Searcher achieves performance comparable to mainstream LLM-based search agents and P-ReAct delivers approximately 15% inference acceleration. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/DLLM-Searcher-553C

AI.tldr: Proposes DLLM-Searcher, a two-stage post-training pipeline for dLLMs (Agentic SFT + Agentic VRPO) to boost information seeking and reasoning in search agents, plus a P-ReAct paradigm to parallelize reasoning and tool calling to reduce latency; achieves competitive performance with LLM-based agents and ~15% inference speedup.
AI.motivation: Address two core challenges in dLLM-based search agents: (1) Latency from serial multi-round reasoning and tool calls under ReAct, and (2) weak reasoning/tool-calling abilities of current dLLMs that hinder practical deployment.
AI.method: Two-stage post-training: Agentic SFT to enhance information-seeking/reasoning; Agentic VRPO to reduce variance in preference optimization for agent actions. Introduces P-ReAct, a parallel reasoning/acting paradigm that prioritizes decoding tool_call instructions while awaiting tool responses.
AI.result: DLLM-Searcher achieves performance comparable to mainstream LLM-based search agents. P-ReAct provides approximately 15% inference acceleration.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that dLLMs can rival LLM-based agents with targeted post-training and a parallelized agent paradigm, and that P-ReAct can meaningfully reduce latency in real-world deployments.
